topics.title: Basic Principles of Capital Budgeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Capital Budgeting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital Budgeting** is the strategic financial planning process employed by corporate enterprises to evaluate and select **long-term investment projects** that are expected to generate cash inflows over multiple accounting periods (typically exceeding **1 year**). It involves the systematic allocation of a firm's scarce capital resources among competing investment proposals to maximize shareholder wealth.

The term "Capital Budgeting" is derived from two words:
- **Capital** → Long-term funds invested in fixed/physical assets (machinery, land, buildings, technology).
- **Budgeting** → Planning, allocating, and controlling financial resources.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is also referred to as **"Capital Expenditure (CapEx) Decision-Making"** or **"Investment Decision Appraisal."**

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Farmer and the Mango Orchard":**
>
> Imagine a farmer named *Ramesh* who has saved ₹10,00,000 over five years. He has three options:
> 1. **Option A:** Buy a small piece of land and grow seasonal vegetables (returns in 6 months, but recurring).
> 2. **Option B:** Plant a mango orchard (initial investment today, no returns for 3 years, but ₹3,00,000 every year for the next 20 years).
> 3. **Option C:** Deposit the money in a bank FD at 7% (safe, predictable, but low returns).
>
> The farmer's decision is **NOT** about choosing the safest option — it's about comparing which investment adds the **most real value** to his wealth after considering the **time value of money**, **risk**, and **duration**.
>
> This is precisely what *Capital Budgeting* does for corporations — it answers: *"Which big project should we spend our long-term money on, and will it actually make the company wealthier?"*

## 1.3 Why Capital Budgeting is Critical for Corporations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Capital budgeting decisions are **irreversible** (sunk costs cannot be easily recovered).
> - These decisions involve **substantial capital outlays** (often millions or crores).
> - They directly impact the firm's **long-term profitability, growth trajectory, and competitive positioning**.
> - Errors in capital budgeting lead to **destruction of shareholder wealth**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Capital Budgeting

> [!NOTE]
> The four fundamental principles of capital budgeting revolve around these **bold** standard metrics:
> 1. **Cash Flows** (not accounting profits)
> 2. **Time Value of Money (TVM)** — discount rate ≈ **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)**, typically between **8%–15%** for Indian corporates.
> 3. **Risk-Return Trade-off**
> 4. **Opportunity Cost of Capital**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Basic Principles of Capital Budgeting

### Principle 1: Principle of Cash Flow Consideration (Cash, Not Profit)
Capital budgeting decisions are based on **actual cash inflows and outflows**, NOT on accounting profits. This is because:
- Accounting profits include non-cash items (depreciation, amortization).
- Only cash can be reinvested or distributed to shareholders.
- Cash flows are objective and verifiable.

### Principle 2: Principle of Time Value of Money (TVM)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A rupee today is worth more than a rupee received a year from now.** This is the foundational premise of TVM.

A project that yields ₹1,00,000 today is preferred over ₹1,00,000 after 5 years, because:
- Today's rupee can be invested and earn returns.
- Inflation erodes the future rupee's purchasing power.
- Future cash flows carry uncertainty.

### Principle 3: Principle of Risk and Return (Higher Risk → Higher Required Return)
Projects with **higher risk profiles** must earn a **proportionally higher rate of return** to compensate investors. The discount rate used in appraisal is adjusted upward for riskier projects.

### Principle 4: Principle of Profitability (Acceptance Criterion)
A project must generate returns **greater than the firm's cost of capital**. Otherwise, it destroys value.

## 2.2 The Five Standard Capital Budgeting Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Broad Categories:**
> 1. **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods** — Time-value-aware
> 2. **Non-Discounted (Traditional) Methods** — Ignore TVM

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method | Formula | Acceptance Rule | Decision Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $\text{NPV} \geq 0$ | Accept if NPV > 0; Reject if NPV < 0 |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | $IRR \geq r$ (cost of capital) | Accept if IRR > r |
| **Profitability Index (PI)** | $PI = \dfrac{\text{PV of Future Cash Inflows}}{\text{Initial Investment}}$ | $PI \geq 1$ | Accept if PI > 1 |
| **Payback Period (PP)** | $\text{PP} = \text{Years before full recovery} + \dfrac{\text{Unrecovered cost at start of year}}{\text{Cash flow during year}}$ | Compare with target | Accept if PP < Target |
| **Average Rate of Return (ARR)** | $ARR = \dfrac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100$ | Compare with target rate | Accept if ARR > Target |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Note:** In all mathematical expressions above, the **vertical bar symbol** $\vert$ should be used for "absolute value" or "modulus" — NEVER the keyboard pipe character, as it breaks markdown table parsing.

## 2.4 Engineering / Real-World Utility

In real corporate finance practice (relevance for B.Tech students entering industry):
- **NPV** is the gold standard — directly measures wealth creation in monetary terms (₹).
- **IRR** is intuitively appealing but suffers from multiple-IRR problems in non-conventional cash flows.
- **PI** is essential when capital is **rationed** (limited funds available).
- **Payback Period** is preferred by **small-scale industries and startups** due to liquidity concerns.
- **ARR** is largely obsolete but still tested in KTU exams for completeness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Net Present Value (NPV) Formula

### Step 1: Define Future Cash Flow
Let $CF_t$ represent the net cash flow at the end of period $t$.

### Step 2: Discount Each Future Cash Flow to Present
According to the **Principle of Time Value of Money**, a cash flow $CF_t$ received $t$ periods from now is worth less today. The present value (PV) is calculated by dividing by the discount factor $(1+r)^t$, where $r$ is the discount rate.

$$
PV_t = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

### Step 3: Sum All Discounted Cash Flows
The total present value of all future cash inflows from year 1 to year $n$ is:

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

### Step 4: Subtract Initial Investment
The initial investment $CF_0$ occurs at time $t = 0$ and is already at present value (no discounting required).

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PV_{\text{inflows}} - \text{Initial Investment} \\
NPV &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - CF_0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Unified Single-Sum Form (KTU Standard Notation)
Combining the initial investment into the summation (where $CF_0$ is typically negative):

$$
\boxed{NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical interpretation:** NPV is the **net monetary gain** to the firm from undertaking the project, expressed in today's rupees.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A company is evaluating a project requiring an initial investment of **₹5,00,000**. Expected cash inflows are:
- Year 1: ₹2,00,000
- Year 2: ₹2,50,000
- Year 3: ₹3,00,000

Cost of capital (discount rate) **r = 10%**.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

Step 1: Compute discount factors.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^1} = 0.9091 \\
DF_2 &= \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^2} = 0.8264 \\
DF_3 &= \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^3} = 0.7513
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Multiply each cash flow by its discount factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 1{,}81{,}818 \\
PV_2 &= 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 2{,}06{,}612 \\
PV_3 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 2{,}25{,}394
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3: Sum the discounted cash flows.

$$
PV_{\text{total}} = 1{,}81{,}818 + 2{,}06{,}612 + 2{,}25{,}394 = 6{,}13{,}824
$$

Step 4: Compute NPV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PV_{\text{total}} - \text{Initial Investment} \\
NPV &= 6{,}13{,}824 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}13{,}824
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since NPV = **₹1,13,824 > 0**, the project should be **ACCEPTED**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of All Techniques

| Parameter | NPV | IRR | PI | Payback | ARR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Considers Time Value of Money | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Considers All Cash Flows | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (truncated) | ❌ No |
| Measures Profitability in Absolute ₹ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (gives %) | ❌ No (gives ratio) | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Useful for Mutually Exclusive Projects | ✅ Best | ⚠️ May mislead | ⚠️ May mislead | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Useful under Capital Rationing | ⚠️ Moderate | ❌ No | ✅ Best | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Subjectivity | Low | Moderate | Moderate | High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capital Budgeting Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Investment Proposal] --> B[Estimate Cash Flows]
    B --> C[Determine Cost of Capital r]
    C --> D[Apply Evaluation Techniques]
    D --> E1[NPV Calculation]
    D --> E2[IRR Calculation]
    D --> E3[PI Calculation]
    D --> E4[Payback Period]
    D --> E5[ARR Calculation]
    E1 --> F[Compare Results]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G{Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Approve Project for Implementation]
    G -- No --> I[Reject Project or Re-evaluate]
    H --> J[Post-Audit and Performance Review]
    J --> K[Feedback for Future Capital Budgeting]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Initial Investment CF0] --> CORE[Capital Budgeting Engine]
    INPUT2[Future Cash Inflows CFt] --> CORE
    INPUT3[Discount Rate r or WACC] --> CORE
    INPUT4[Project Risk Profile] --> CORE
    CORE --> OUT1[NPV Decision]
    CORE --> OUT2[IRR Decision]
    CORE --> OUT3[PI Decision]
    CORE --> OUT4[Payback Decision]
    CORE --> OUT5[ARR Decision]
    OUT1 --> MERGE[Integrated Ranking Engine]
    OUT2 --> MERGE
    OUT3 --> MERGE
    OUT4 --> MERGE
    OUT5 --> MERGE
    MERGE --> FINAL[Final Recommendation: Accept or Reject]
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree of Capital Budgeting Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TECH[Capital Budgeting Techniques]
    TECH --> DCF[Discounted Cash Flow Methods]
    TECH --> NONDCF[Non-Discounted Methods]
    DCF --> NPV[Net Present Value]
    DCF --> IRR[Internal Rate of Return]
    DCF --> PI[Profitability Index]
    NONDCF --> PP[Payback Period]
    NONDCF --> ARR[Average Rate of Return]
    PP --> PPN[Payback Period Normal]
    PP --> PPD[Discounted Payback Period]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Capital Budgeting. List any four features/principles of capital budgeting.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Capital Budgeting is the process of planning, evaluating, and selecting long-term investment projects that involve significant capital outlays and generate cash flows over multiple years, with the objective of maximizing shareholder wealth.

**Four Features/Principles:**
1. It involves **long-term investment decisions** (impact spanning multiple years).
2. It involves **large capital outlays** (substantial financial commitment).
3. It is based on **cash flows, not accounting profits**.
4. It is guided by the **Time Value of Money** principle.
5. It aims at **maximizing shareholder wealth / firm value**.
6. It involves **irreversible decisions** with significant risk.

*(Any four well-explained points = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Distinguish between Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR).**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | NPV | IRR |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Absolute monetary gain in ₹ | Percentage return earned by the project |
| Unit | Rupees (₹) | Percentage (%) |
| Formula | $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Discount rate where NPV = 0 |
| Decision Rule | NPV > 0 → Accept | IRR > r → Accept |
| Mutually Exclusive Projects | Reliable | May give contradictory rankings |
| Reinvestment Assumption | At cost of capital (r) | At IRR itself |

*(Tabular comparison with 5 valid points = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects, X and Y. Both require an initial investment of ₹10,00,000. The cost of capital is 12%. The expected cash inflows are:**

| Year | Project X (₹) | Project Y (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4,00,000 | 2,00,000 |
| 2 | 5,00,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 3 | 6,00,000 | 8,00,000 |

**Required:**
**(a)** Calculate NPV, PI, and IRR for both projects. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Which project should be selected? Justify using NPV and PI criteria. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Discount factors at 12%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= \dfrac{1}{1.12} = 0.8929 \\
DF_2 &= \dfrac{1}{(1.12)^2} = 0.7972 \\
DF_3 &= \dfrac{1}{(1.12)^3} = 0.7118
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — NPV of Project X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929 = 3{,}57{,}160 \\
PV_2 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972 = 3{,}98{,}600 \\
PV_3 &= 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118 = 4{,}27{,}080 \\
\\
PV_{\text{total}} &= 3{,}57{,}160 + 3{,}98{,}600 + 4{,}27{,}080 = 11{,}82{,}840 \\
\\
NPV_X &= 11{,}82{,}840 - 10{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}82{,}840
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Profitability Index of Project X:**

$$
PI_X = \dfrac{11{,}82{,}840}{10{,}00{,}000} = 1.183
$$

**Step 4 — IRR of Project X (trial-and-error interpolation):**

Test at r = 18%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8475 = 3{,}39{,}000 \\
PV_2 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7182 = 3{,}59{,}100 \\
PV_3 &= 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6086 = 3{,}65{,}160 \\
\\
PV_{\text{total at 18\%}} &= 10{,}63{,}260 \\
NPV \text{ at } 18\% &= +63{,}260
\end{aligned}
$$

Test at r = 20%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8333 = 3{,}33{,}320 \\
PV_2 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6944 = 3{,}47{,}200 \\
PV_3 &= 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5787 = 3{,}47{,}220 \\
\\
PV_{\text{total at 20\%}} &= 10{,}27{,}740 \\
NPV \text{ at } 20\% &= +27{,}740
\end{aligned}
$$

Test at r = 22%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8197 = 3{,}27{,}880 \\
PV_2 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6719 = 3{,}35{,}950 \\
PV_3 &= 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5507 = 3{,}30{,}420 \\
\\
PV_{\text{total at 22\%}} &= 9{,}94{,}250 \\
NPV \text{ at } 22\% &= -5{,}750
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply linear interpolation between 20% and 22%:

$$
IRR_X = 20\% + \left( \dfrac{27{,}740}{27{,}740 + 5{,}750} \right) \times 2\%
$$

$$
IRR_X = 20\% + 0.828 \times 2\% = 21.66\%
$$

**Step 5 — NPV of Project Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929 = 1{,}78{,}580 \\
PV_2 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972 = 3{,}18{,}880 \\
PV_3 &= 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118 = 5{,}69{,}440 \\
\\
PV_{\text{total}} &= 1{,}78{,}580 + 3{,}18{,}880 + 5{,}69{,}440 = 10{,}66{,}900 \\
\\
NPV_Y &= 10{,}66{,}900 - 10{,}00{,}000 = 66{,}900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — PI of Project Y:**

$$
PI_Y = \dfrac{10{,}66{,}900}{10{,}00{,}000} = 1.067
$$

**Step 7 — IRR of Project Y (trial at 14% and 16%):**

At 14%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= 2{,}00{,}000(0.8772) + 4{,}00{,}000(0.7695) + 8{,}00{,}000(0.6750) \\
&= 1{,}75{,}440 + 3{,}07{,}800 + 5{,}40{,}000 = 10{,}23{,}240 \\
NPV &= +23{,}240
\end{aligned}
$$

At 16%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= 2{,}00{,}000(0.8621) + 4{,}00{,}000(0.7432) + 8{,}00{,}000(0.6407) \\
&= 1{,}72{,}420 + 2{,}97{,}280 + 5{,}12{,}560 = 9{,}82{,}260 \\
NPV &= -17{,}740
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpolation:

$$
IRR_Y = 14\% + \left( \dfrac{23{,}240}{23{,}240 + 17{,}740} \right) \times 2\% = 14\% + 1.134\% = 15.13\%
$$

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Tabulated Summary:**

| Metric | Project X | Project Y |
|---|---|---|
| NPV | ₹1,82,840 | ₹66,900 |
| PI | 1.183 | 1.067 |
| IRR | 21.66% | 15.13% |

**Decision:**
- **NPV Criterion:** Project X (₹1,82,840) > Project Y (₹66,900) → **Select Project X.**
- **PI Criterion:** Project X (1.183) > Project Y (1.067) → **Select Project X.**
- **IRR Criterion:** Project X (21.66%) > Project Y (15.13%) → **Select Project X.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Recommendation:** All three criteria unanimously recommend **Project X** because it creates greater shareholder wealth, provides better returns per rupee invested, and yields a higher percentage return.

---

### **Question B** *(Internal Choice Alternative)* `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Payback Period method** with its merits and demerits. Compute the payback period for a project with the following cash flows: Initial Investment = ₹6,00,000; Year 1: ₹2,00,000; Year 2: ₹3,00,000; Year 3: ₹3,50,000. The target payback period is 2.5 years. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Average Rate of Return (ARR)** method. How is it different from IRR? Compute ARR for a project with an initial investment of ₹5,00,000, salvage value ₹1,00,000, life = 5 years, and average annual profit (after depreciation) of ₹80,000. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Payback Period Method:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept:** Payback Period is the time required for the cumulative cash inflows from a project to recover the initial investment.

**Merits:**
1. Simple and easy to compute.
2. Useful for assessing liquidity and risk of investment.
3. Preferred by small businesses and in capital-rationing situations.

**Demerits:**
1. Ignores the **Time Value of Money**.
2. Ignores cash flows occurring **after** the payback period.
3. Biases towards **short-term** projects.
4. Subjective target period.

**Computation:**

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) | Cumulative Cash Inflow (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2,00,000 | 2,00,000 |
| 2 | 3,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| 3 | 3,50,000 | 8,50,000 |

The initial investment of ₹6,00,000 is recovered during **Year 3**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payback Period} &= 2 \text{ years} + \dfrac{(6{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000)}{3{,}50{,}000} \\
&= 2 + 0.286 = 2.29 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Since **2.29 years < 2.5 years (target)**, the project is **ACCEPTED**.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Average Rate of Return (ARR):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept:** ARR expresses the average annual accounting profit as a percentage of the average investment over the project's life.

**Difference from IRR:**

| Basis | ARR | IRR |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Accounting profit | Cash flow |
| Time Value of Money | ❌ Ignored | ✅ Considered |
| Discount Rate | Not used | Discount rate at which NPV = 0 |
| Use | Managerial/financial reporting | Investment decision-making |

**Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Investment} &= \dfrac{\text{Initial Cost} + \text{Salvage Value}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000}{2} = 3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
ARR &= \dfrac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{80{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 26.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project earns an average return of **26.67% per year** on the average book value of investment. If the target ARR exceeds this, accept; otherwise, reject.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Using Accounting Profit Instead of Cash Flows** — A 2-mark penalty for confusing depreciation adjustments.
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Initial Investment** — When computing NPV or PI, students often forget to subtract the Year 0 outflow, leading to wrong decisions.
> 3. **IRR Trial-and-Error Not Shown** — Examiners demand at least **two trial rates** (one positive NPV, one negative NPV) before interpolation.
> 4. **Decimal Precision** — Use **4 decimal places** for discount factors; round only at the final answer.
> 5. **Decision Rule Confusion** — "Accept if NPV > 0" (strict inequality). Saying "Accept if NPV = 0" loses 0.5 marks.
> 6. **Payback Formula Error** — Writing only the year without adding the fractional recovery component loses full marks.
> 7. **Mixing up ARR with IRR** — Both are percentages but differ fundamentally in calculation base.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> ✅ **Capital Budgeting** = Long-term investment decision-making using cash flows.
>
> ✅ **Four Principles:** Cash flow basis, TVM, Risk-Return, Profitability.
>
> ✅ **NPV** is the **GOLD STANDARD** method — accept if NPV > 0.
>
> ✅ **Formula Mastery:** $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$; $IRR$ solves NPV = 0; $PI = \dfrac{PV_{\text{inflows}}}{CF_0}$.
>
> ✅ **Payback Period** ignores TVM — pure liquidity measure.
>
> ✅ **ARR** uses accounting profit — obsolete but tested.
>
> ✅ **Mutually Exclusive Projects** → Always rely on **NPV**, not IRR (size discrepancy issue).
>
> ✅ **Capital Rationing** → Use **Profitability Index (PI)** for ranking.
>
> ✅ **Discount Rate** = Cost of Capital = WACC for the firm.
>
> ✅ **Decision Hierarchy:** NPV > IRR > PI > Payback > ARR (in terms of theoretical superiority).
>
> ✅ **KTU 14-mark questions** typically combine **2 techniques** (e.g., NPV + IRR) plus a decision.
>
> ✅ **Always show discount factor table** at the start of any numerical solution.
>
> ✅ **Units:** NPV in ₹; IRR/ARR in %; PI as ratio (no units).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Investment Decision Criteria - NPV

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# INVESTMENT DECISION CRITERIA — NET PRESENT VALUE (NPV)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Present Value (NPV)** is the algebraic sum of the present values of all expected cash inflows and outflows associated with a capital investment proposal, discounted at the firm's cost of capital (or required rate of return). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, NPV is classified as a **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** technique and is the most theoretically sound investment decision criterion taught in PEIET744.

Mathematically, the formal definition is expressed as the difference between the **Present Value of Cash Inflows (PVCI)** and the **Present Value of Cash Outflows (PVCO)**:

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

Where the summation runs from $t = 0$ (decision point / initial outlay) to $t = n$ (terminal year of the project's life).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Net Present Value method is treated as the *primary* capital budgeting criterion because it directly measures the **dollar (₹) amount of wealth creation** for the firm's shareholders. Every other criterion (IRR, PI, Payback) is benchmarked against NPV in the KTU board valuation key.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Time-Value Bank Account"

Imagine you walk into a bank called **"Future Cashflow Bank"**. This bank has one strict rule:

> *"Any rupee you receive in the future must be 'discounted' before it is allowed inside today's wallet, because today's ₹100 is worth more than tomorrow's ₹100 (it can earn interest, fight inflation, and be invested elsewhere)."*

**How the analogy works:**

| Real-World Banking Concept | NPV Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Money you deposit today | Initial Investment (Cash Outflow at $t=0$) |
| Future money promised to you | Future Cash Inflows (CF₁, CF₂, ... CFₙ) |
| The bank's "discount interest rate" | Cost of Capital ($r$) — also called the discount rate, hurdle rate, or required rate of return |
| Net balance in your wallet after discounting everything | The **Net Present Value** |
| A positive balance (wallet heavier) | **NPV > 0 → Accept the project** |
| A negative balance (wallet lighter / you owe the bank) | **NPV < 0 → Reject the project** |
| Balance is exactly zero | **NPV = 0 → Indifferent** (project just recovers cost of capital) |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** NPV literally answers one crisp question — *"If I undertake this project today, how much extra value (in today's rupees) will my shareholders end up with?"* A positive NPV means the project earns *more* than the cost of financing it; a negative NPV means the project *destroys* shareholder wealth.

---

## 1.3 Why NPV is the "Gold Standard"

The KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes for PEIET744 (specifically **CO1** — *Apply time-value-of-money concepts to corporate investment decisions*) place NPV at the centre because it satisfies **all four** desirable properties of a sound investment criterion:

1. **Considers the Time Value of Money** — every cash flow is discounted to $t=0$.
2. **Considers All Cash Flows** — including the terminal/scrap value at year $n$.
3. **Uses the True Cost of Capital** — the discount rate is the opportunity cost of funds.
4. **Maximises Shareholder Wealth** — the ultimate goal of financial management (per the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned outcome framework).

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How NPV is Computed

The calculation follows a strict, sequential logic that KTU examiners expect students to articulate in the 14-mark ESE questions:

- **Step 1 — Identify the initial outlay ($C_0$ or $CF_0$):** This is the cash outflow at $t=0$. It includes the purchase price of the asset, installation cost, working capital requirement, and any pre-operative expenses. By convention, it is treated as a *negative* value in the summation.
- **Step 2 — Forecast the after-tax operating cash flows ($CF_t$) for $t = 1, 2, \dots, n$:** These are *incremental* cash flows — meaning only the additional cash generated by accepting the project. They are after-tax, not before-tax.
- **Step 3 — Identify the terminal/terminal cash flow:** This includes the salvage value of the asset, recovery of working capital, and any terminal tax effects (e.g., tax on capital gain).
- **Step 4 — Determine the appropriate discount rate ($r$):** For KTU problems, this is usually given directly. Conceptually, $r$ equals the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** for an average-risk project.
- **Step 5 — Discount every future cash flow to its present value (PV):** Each $CF_t$ is divided by $(1+r)^t$.
- **Step 6 — Sum all the present values:** The algebraic sum gives the NPV.
- **Step 7 — Apply the decision rule:** $\text{NPV} \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ Accept; $\text{NPV} < 0 \Rightarrow$ Reject.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Discounting:** A rupee tomorrow is worth less than a rupee today for three concrete reasons — (a) **inflation** erodes purchasing power, (b) **opportunity cost** — the rupee today could earn risk-free interest, and (c) **default/project risk** — the future cash flow is uncertain. The discount rate $r$ is essentially the *minimum* return investors demand to forgo current consumption.

---

## 2.2 The Core NPV Formula — Detailed Variants

### 2.2.1 General Discrete-Time NPV Formula

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t} \;=\; \frac{\text{CF}_0}{(1+r)^0} + \frac{\text{CF}_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{\text{CF}_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{\text{CF}_n}{(1+r)^n}$$

### 2.2.2 Conventional NPV Formula (Most Frequently Asked in KTU)

When initial investment $C_0$ occurs at $t=0$ and the project generates an **annuity** (equal cash flow $A$ for $n$ years):

$$\text{NPV} = -C_0 + A \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \right]$$

The bracketed term is the **Present Value Interest Factor of an Annuity (PVIFA)**.

### 2.2.3 Mixed-Stream NPV Formula

When cash flows are *unequal* across years:

$$\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \frac{\text{CF}_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{\text{CF}_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{\text{CF}_n}{(1+r)^n} + \frac{\text{SV}}{(1+r)^n}$$

where **SV** is the salvage value recovered at the end of year $n$.

### 2.2.4 Profitability Index Variant (NPV Edition)

$$\text{PI} = \frac{\text{PV of Future Cash Inflows}}{\text{Initial Investment}} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \text{CF}_t / (1+r)^t}{C_0}$$

The decision rule is: Accept if $\text{PI} > 1$, equivalent to $\text{NPV} > 0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Hall Quick Reference Table** — memorize these entries verbatim. The KTU valuation key awards full marks only when the correct factor table values (PVIF / PVIFA) are quoted.

| Concept | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Decision Rule | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Net Present Value | $\text{NPV}$ | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \text{CF}_t / (1+r)^t$ | $\text{NPV} \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ Accept | $r > -1$ (for real $PV$) |
| Present Value Factor (single CF) | $\text{PVIF}(r,t)$ | $1 / (1+r)^t$ | Used in mixed-stream NPV | $t \geq 0$ integer |
| Annuity Factor (equal CFs) | $\text{PVIFA}(r,n)$ | $\bigl[1 - (1+r)^{-n}\bigr] / r$ | Used in annuity NPV | $r \neq 0$ |
| Profitability Index | $\text{PI}$ | $\sum \text{PV of inflows} / C_0$ | $\text{PI} > 1 \Rightarrow$ Accept | Same as NPV |
| NPV Profile Slope | $\dfrac{d\,\text{NPV}}{dr}$ | $- \sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot \text{CF}_t / (1+r)^{t+1}$ | Slope is always negative for positive CFs | NPV always falls as $r$ rises |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** The KTU answer sheet evaluation is automated for digital submissions. When you write "PVIFA" or "PVIF" with the *vertical bar* (e.g., $\vert 10\%, 5 \text{ yrs} \vert$), always use `\;` for spacing or write it in words to avoid PDF rendering errors.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although this course sits in the Management basket of the KTU 2024 curriculum, NPV is the *de facto* language of **engineering economics**:

- **Capital-intensive industries** (manufacturing plants, power projects, infrastructure): Every ₹100 crore of capex is justified or rejected based on NPV.
- **R&D project selection** in tech firms (Intel, Tata Consultancy, ISRO): Software/hardware development cycles are evaluated by NPV.
- **Public-Private Partnership (PPP) projects**: Government bodies (NHAI, Kochi Metro) require mandatory NPV analysis for viability-gap funding decisions.
- **Replacement decisions** in industrial engineering: Whether to replace an old CNC machine with a new one is decided by the *incremental* NPV of the replacement.
- **Energy efficiency upgrades**: Solar panel installations, wind farms, and LED retrofits are routinely evaluated using NPV against the discount rate (often the company's WACC or government bond yield).

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From First Principles to the NPV Formula

Let us derive the NPV formula from the fundamental **time-value-of-money** identity.

### 3.1.1 The Single Cash-Flow Present Value

A rupee received at the end of year $t$, when the risk-adjusted discount rate is $r$, is equivalent to how many rupees today?

If $PV$ rupees are invested today at rate $r$ (compounded annually), after one year it becomes $PV(1+r)$, after two years $PV(1+r)^2$, and after $t$ years $PV(1+r)^t$.

Setting the future value equal to the future cash flow $\text{CF}_t$:

$$PV \cdot (1+r)^t = \text{CF}_t$$

Solving for $PV$ (the present value today):

$$PV = \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

### 3.1.2 Summing All Cash Flows

The NPV is defined as the *sum of present values* of every cash flow occurring over the project's life, from $t=0$ to $t=n$:

\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\text{CF}_0}{(1+r)^0} + \frac{\text{CF}_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{\text{CF}_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{\text{CF}_n}{(1+r)^n}
\end{aligned}

Since $(1+r)^0 = 1$, the $t=0$ term simplifies to $\text{CF}_0$, which is the initial outlay (a negative number by convention).

### 3.1.3 Specialisation to an Annuity (Equal Cash Flows)

When $\text{CF}_1 = \text{CF}_2 = \cdots = \text{CF}_n = A$, factor out $A$:

\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -C_0 + A \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+r)^n} \right] \\[4pt]
&= -C_0 + A \cdot \left[ \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^n} \right] \\[4pt]
&= -C_0 + A \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \right]
\end{aligned}

The closed-form expression in the square brackets is the **PVIFA(r, n)** factor.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Exam Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a *fully solved* exemplar. In the KTU valuation key, every numerical step must be shown with the corresponding factor value quoted. A blank factor table is *not* allowed in the ESE answer sheet.

### Problem Statement

> *M/s Kerala Engineering Ltd. is evaluating a machine costing **₹5,00,000**. The machine generates net cash inflows of **₹1,50,000 per year for 5 years**. At the end of year 5, the machine has a salvage value of **₹50,000**. The firm's cost of capital is **12% p.a.**. Determine the NPV and advise whether to accept the project.*

### Step 1 — Identify Variables

- $C_0 = ₹5,00,000$ (initial investment at $t=0$)
- $A = ₹1,50,000$ (annual equal cash inflow, years 1–5)
- $SV = ₹50,000$ (salvage value at the end of year 5)
- $r = 0.12$ (12% discount rate)
- $n = 5$ (project life in years)

### Step 2 — Discount the Annual Cash Flows (Annuity Portion)

Using $\text{PVIFA}(12\%, 5) = \dfrac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12} = 3.6048$

$$\text{PV of Annuity} = A \times \text{PVIFA} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048 = ₹5{,}40{,}720$$

### Step 3 — Discount the Salvage Value (Single CF at $t=5$)

Using $\text{PVIF}(12\%, 5) = (1.12)^{-5} = 0.5674$

$$\text{PV of Salvage} = SV \times \text{PVIF} = 50{,}000 \times 0.5674 = ₹28{,}370$$

### Step 4 — Compute the NPV

\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -C_0 + \text{PV of Annuity} + \text{PV of Salvage} \\[4pt]
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}40{,}720 + 28{,}370 \\[4pt]
&= ₹69{,}090
\end{aligned}

### Step 5 — Decision

Since $\text{NPV} = +₹69{,}090 > 0$, the project is **financially acceptable**. The investment will increase shareholder wealth by ₹69,090 in present-value terms.

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for the Valuation Key:** The project earns a *return greater than 12% p.a.* because NPV is positive. Had the cost of capital been, say, 20%, the NPV would turn negative — the project would be destroying value at that hurdle rate.

---

## 3.3 NPV Decision Matrix (For Two Mutually Exclusive Projects)

| Project | Initial Cost (₹) | Annual CF (₹) | Life (yrs) | Discount Rate (%) | NPV (₹) | Accept / Reject | Ranking |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P | 4,00,000 | 1,20,000 | 5 | 10 | +69,815 | Accept | **First** |
| Q | 6,00,000 | 1,65,000 | 5 | 10 | +20,265 | Accept | Second |
| R | 8,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 5 | 10 | -14,640 | Reject | — |

For **mutually exclusive** projects (only one can be chosen), KTU examiners specifically check: *the project with the highest positive NPV is selected*, not the one with the highest IRR or PI (this is a common confusion in valuation scripts).

---

## 3.4 The NPV Profile — Graphical Representation

The NPV profile plots NPV on the Y-axis against the discount rate ($r$) on the X-axis. Key properties:

- The curve is **downward-sloping** (as $r$ increases, NPV decreases).
- The point where the curve crosses the X-axis is the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — i.e., the $r$ at which $\text{NPV} = 0$.
- A *steeper* profile indicates a project whose NPV is highly sensitive to discount-rate changes (high risk).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** The NPV profile is the natural bridge to the next topic — IRR. The intersection of two projects' NPV profiles is called the **Fisher intersection** (or crossover rate), and it is a frequently asked concept in Part A questions.

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The NPV Decision Flow — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *decision logic* the finance manager follows when an NPV analysis is undertaken. This is a high-fidelity block diagram that captures the inputs, the computational engine, the comparison test, and the terminal decision nodes — exactly the structure expected in KTU ESE descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step A: Identify Project Proposal] --> nodeB[Step B: Forecast Initial Outlay CF0 at t equals 0]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step C: Forecast Operating Cash Flows CF1 to CFn for n years]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step D: Identify Salvage Value SV and Terminal Cash Flow]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step E: Determine Discount Rate r equals WACC or Hurdle Rate]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step F: Compute Present Value of each CFt using PVIF r t]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step G: Sum all PVs to obtain NPV]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Decision Test: Is NPV greater than or equal to zero}
    nodeH --> nodeI[Branch 1: NPV greater than zero — Accept the Project]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Branch 2: NPV equals zero — Indifferent Marginal]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Branch 3: NPV less than zero — Reject the Project]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Final Output: Project adds Value to Shareholders]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Final Output: Project Earns exactly Cost of Capital]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Final Output: Project destroys Value avoid]
```

---

## 4.2 NPV Profile Schematic (Two Project Comparison)

The NPV profile visually compares two projects at varying discount rates. Below is a Mermaid XY-Chart schematic that mimics the classical NPV profile taught in PEIET744.

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "NPV Profile Comparison of Project A versus Project B"
    x-axis "Discount Rate (percent)" [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30]
    y-axis "Net Present Value (Lakhs INR)" -200 to 800
    line "Project A NPV" [780, 620, 480, 360, 250, 155, 70]
    line "Project B NPV" [550, 480, 410, 340, 270, 200, 130]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this profile for KTU exams:**
> - Both NPVs are positive at the firm's WACC (say 12%) → both projects are *acceptable* on a stand-alone basis.
> - The **crossover point** (where the two lines intersect) is the discount rate at which both projects have equal NPV. Below this rate, the steeper project is preferred; above it, the flatter project is preferred.
> - In a *mutually exclusive* comparison, KTU expects students to choose the project with the higher NPV at the **relevant cost of capital**, *not* the project with the higher IRR.

---

## 4.3 Cash-Flow Timeline Diagram (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid timeline shows the typical *cash-flow structure* of a 5-year capital project, with the initial outlay at $t=0$ and the inflows at $t=1$ to $t=5$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0[Time t equals 0 — Initial Outflow CF0 negative] --> t1[Time t equals 1 — Inflow CF1 positive]
    t1 --> t2[Time t equals 2 — Inflow CF2 positive]
    t2 --> t3[Time t equals 3 — Inflow CF3 positive]
    t3 --> t4[Time t equals 4 — Inflow CF4 positive]
    t4 --> t5[Time t equals 5 — Inflow CF5 plus Salvage Value]
    t0 -.Discount at rate r.-> t1
    t1 -.Discount at rate r.-> t2
    t2 -.Discount at rate r.-> t3
    t3 -.Discount at rate r.-> t4
    t4 -.Discount at rate r.-> t5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted "discount" arrows symbolise the discounting operation — every future cash flow is mathematically pulled back (discounted) to the present ($t=0$) before summation. This is the visual essence of the time-value-of-money concept.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

> *"Define Net Present Value (NPV). State the decision rule based on NPV for accepting or rejecting a capital budgeting proposal."*

**Model Answer:**

> **Net Present Value (NPV)** is the difference between the present value of cash inflows and the present value of cash outflows of a project over its entire economic life, discounted at the firm's cost of capital.
>
> $$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t}$$
>
> **Decision Rule:**
> - If $\text{NPV} > 0$ → **Accept** the project (it adds value).
> - If $\text{NPV} < 0$ → **Reject** the project (it destroys value).
> - If $\text{NPV} = 0$ → **Indifference** (project just meets the cost of capital).

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

> *"Distinguish between NPV and Profitability Index (PI) as capital budgeting decision tools."*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | NPV | Profitability Index (PI) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Absolute measure: present value of net cash flows | Relative measure: ratio of PV of inflows to initial cost |
| Unit | Rupees (₹) | Dimensionless number / ratio |
| Formula | $\sum \text{CF}_t / (1+r)^t$ | $\sum \text{PV of inflows} / C_0$ |
| Decision Rule | $\text{NPV} \geq 0$ | $\text{PI} \geq 1$ |
| Use | Mutually exclusive projects (choose highest NPV) | Capital-rationed situations (rank by PI) |
| Scale Consideration | Ignores scale issues | Useful when projects differ in scale |

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Repeated Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects, A and B, with the following cash flows. The cost of capital is **10% p.a.**

| Year | Project A Cash Flow (₹) | Project B Cash Flow (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | (50,000) | (70,000) |
| 1 | 20,000 | 25,000 |
| 2 | 22,000 | 28,000 |
| 3 | 24,000 | 30,000 |
| 4 | 26,000 | 32,000 |
| 5 | 28,000 | 35,000 |

*Calculate the NPV of each project using 10% discount rate. State which project should be selected and why.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *"NPV is the most superior investment criterion among all capital budgeting techniques."* Critically discuss this statement, highlighting its advantages and limitations.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Given:** $r = 10\% = 0.10$, $n = 5$ years.

**Discount Factors (PVIF table at 10%):**

$$\text{PVIF}(10\%, t) = (1.10)^{-t}$$

| Year $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PVIF | 0.9091 | 0.8264 | 0.7513 | 0.6830 | 0.6209 |

**Project A — NPV Calculation:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_A &= -50{,}000 + \frac{20{,}000}{1.10} + \frac{22{,}000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{24{,}000}{(1.10)^3} + \frac{26{,}000}{(1.10)^4} + \frac{28{,}000}{(1.10)^5} \\[4pt]
&= -50{,}000 + (20{,}000 \times 0.9091) + (22{,}000 \times 0.8264) + (24{,}000 \times 0.7513) + (26{,}000 \times 0.6830) + (28{,}000 \times 0.6209) \\[4pt]
&= -50{,}000 + 18{,}182 + 18{,}181 + 18{,}031 + 17{,}758 + 17{,}385 \\[4pt]
&= ₹39{,}537
\end{aligned}

**Project B — NPV Calculation:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_B &= -70{,}000 + \frac{25{,}000}{1.10} + \frac{28{,}000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{30{,}000}{(1.10)^3} + \frac{32{,}000}{(1.10)^4} + \frac{35{,}000}{(1.10)^5} \\[4pt]
&= -70{,}000 + (25{,}000 \times 0.9091) + (28{,}000 \times 0.8264) + (30{,}000 \times 0.7513) + (32{,}000 \times 0.6830) + (35{,}000 \times 0.6209) \\[4pt]
&= -70{,}000 + 22{,}727 + 23{,}139 + 22{,}539 + 21{,}856 + 21{,}731 \\[4pt]
&= ₹41{,}992
\end{aligned}

**Decision:**

- $\text{NPV}_A = +₹39{,}537 > 0 \Rightarrow$ Project A is acceptable.
- $\text{NPV}_B = +₹41{,}992 > 0 \Rightarrow$ Project B is acceptable.
- Since the projects are **mutually exclusive**, select **Project B** (higher NPV).

#### Part (b) Solution — Critical Discussion

**Advantages of NPV:**

1. **Considers Time Value of Money** — every cash flow is discounted, reflecting the true economic value.
2. **Considers All Cash Flows** — over the entire project life, not just up to payback.
3. **Uses the True Cost of Capital** — discount rate equals opportunity cost of funds.
4. **Absolute Measure of Value Addition** — NPV directly quantifies the *rupee amount* of wealth created.
5. **Objective Decision Rule** — accept if NPV > 0; no ambiguity.

**Limitations of NPV:**

1. **Requires Accurate Forecasts** — NPV is highly sensitive to estimates of future cash flows, which are inherently uncertain.
2. **Difficulty in Determining Discount Rate** — the appropriate cost of capital is not always easy to compute (especially for new product lines).
3. **Not Useful in Capital Rationing** — when funds are limited, NPV alone cannot rank projects; Profitability Index is better.
4. **Lacks Comparative Ratio** — NPV is an absolute value, so a small project with high NPV may be preferred over a large project with marginally lower NPV (sometimes irrational).
5. **Ignores Project Size** — NPV can be misleading when comparing projects of vastly different scale.

**Conclusion:** Despite its limitations, NPV remains the **most theoretically sound** capital budgeting technique because it is the only method that directly aligns with the primary financial objective — *maximisation of shareholder wealth*. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (CO1 & CO2 of PEIET744), NPV is the *benchmark* against which IRR, PI, and Payback Period are evaluated.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a) [7 Marks]** A firm is considering the purchase of a new machine. The machine costs **₹3,00,000** and is expected to generate cash inflows of **₹90,000, ₹1,00,000, ₹1,10,000, and ₹1,20,000** at the end of years 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively. The salvage value at the end of year 4 is **₹20,000**. The cost of capital is **14% p.a.**

*Compute the NPV of the project and advise the firm on its acceptance.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a suitable diagram the concept of the **NPV Profile**. How does it help a financial manager in making investment decisions when the cost of capital is uncertain?

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Given Data:**
- $C_0 = ₹3,00,000$ (initial outlay)
- $\text{CF}_1 = ₹90,000$, $\text{CF}_2 = ₹1,00,000$, $\text{CF}_3 = ₹1,10,000$, $\text{CF}_4 = ₹1,20,000$
- $SV = ₹20,000$ (at $t = 4$)
- $r = 14\% = 0.14$, $n = 4$ years

**Discount Factors at 14%:**

| Year $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{PVIF} = (1.14)^{-t}$ | 0.8772 | 0.7695 | 0.6750 | 0.5921 |

**Step 1: PV of each operating cash flow**

- $\text{PV}(\text{CF}_1) = 90{,}000 \times 0.8772 = ₹78{,}948$
- $\text{PV}(\text{CF}_2) = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7695 = ₹76{,}950$
- $\text{PV}(\text{CF}_3) = 1{,}10{,}000 \times 0.6750 = ₹74{,}250$
- $\text{PV}(\text{CF}_4) = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.5921 = ₹71{,}052$

**Step 2: PV of salvage value**

$$\text{PV}(SV) = 20{,}000 \times 0.5921 = ₹11{,}842$$

**Step 3: Total PV of inflows**

$$\text{Total PV} = 78{,}948 + 76{,}950 + 74{,}250 + 71{,}052 + 11{,}842 = ₹3{,}13{,}042$$

**Step 4: NPV**

\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \text{Total PV} - C_0 \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}13{,}042 - 3{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= +₹13{,}042
\end{aligned}

**Decision:** Since $\text{NPV} = +₹13{,}042 > 0$, the project is **financially viable** and should be **accepted**. The investment will yield a return greater than 14% p.a. and create shareholder wealth of ₹13,042 in present-value terms.

#### Part (b) Solution — NPV Profile

**Definition:**
The NPV profile is a graphical representation that plots the **Net Present Value** of a project on the Y-axis against the **discount rate (cost of capital)** on the X-axis. It shows how the project's NPV changes as the discount rate varies.

**Diagram (described in words for the answer sheet):**

- **X-axis:** Discount Rate ($r$%) — typically ranging from 0% to 30% or beyond.
- **Y-axis:** NPV in rupees (₹).
- **Curve:** Downward-sloping (often convex).
- **X-intercept:** The point where the curve crosses the X-axis; the value of $r$ at this point is the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** (where NPV = 0).
- **Y-intercept:** The NPV when $r = 0\%$ (i.e., simple undiscounted sum of cash flows).

**Usefulness in Decision-Making:**

1. **Visualises Risk:** A steeply sloping NPV profile indicates a project that is highly sensitive to changes in the discount rate — i.e., a *risky* project.

2. **Compares Multiple Projects:** When NPV profiles of two projects intersect, the point of intersection is called the **crossover (or Fisher) rate**. Below this rate, the project with the steeper slope is preferred; above it, the flatter one is preferred.

3. **Decision Under Uncertainty of Cost of Capital:** If the manager is uncertain about the exact WACC, the NPV profile helps identify the *range* of discount rates over which the project remains acceptable. For example, a project with NPV > 0 for $r$ between 0% and 18% is robust to a wide band of WACC assumptions.

4. **Direct Visual Link to IRR:** The X-intercept of the NPV profile is the IRR — providing a graphical cross-check of the algebraic IRR calculation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Many students wrongly add the initial outlay ($-3,00,000$) to the PV of inflows *without* the negative sign, yielding an inflated NPV. Always maintain the convention: $\text{NPV} = \text{PV of inflows} - C_0$.
> 2. **Mis-quoted Factor Values:** Writing the factor as $\text{PVIF}(14\%, 4) = 0.5921$ is *correct*. Writing $1.14^4 = 1.688$ but using it in the denominator incorrectly is a common error. Use $\text{PVIF} = 1 / (1+r)^t$, **not** $(1+r)^t$.
> 3. **Ignoring Salvage Value:** Students often forget to add the PV of the salvage value at the end of year $n$, losing 2 marks in the 7-mark part (a).
> 4. **Confusing PI with NPV:** A 3-mark question on "Distinguish NPV from PI" requires a *tabular comparison* — students who write a paragraph without a table lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Mutually Exclusive Project Trap:** When two projects are mutually exclusive, *never* pick the one with higher IRR or higher PI if NPV contradicts. KTU's valuation key explicitly deducts 2 marks if the rationale for project selection is given in terms of IRR/PI instead of NPV.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin this on your wall before the ESE.**

- **Definition:** NPV is the *discounted sum* of all cash flows (inflows and outflows) over the project's life, expressed in present-value (₹) terms.
- **Formula:** $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \text{CF}_t / (1+r)^t$.
- **Decision Rule:** $\text{NPV} \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ Accept. $\text{NPV} < 0 \Rightarrow$ Reject.
- **Annuity Shortcut:** $\text{NPV} = -C_0 + A \cdot \text{PVIFA}(r, n)$.
- **Single CF Shortcut:** $\text{PV} = \text{CF} \cdot \text{PVIF}(r, t) = \text{CF} / (1+r)^t$.
- **PVIF value at $t=0$** is always 1.0000.
- **PVIFA value at $r=0$** is always equal to $n$.
- **The discount rate $r$** = WACC for an average-risk project; higher for riskier projects, lower for safer ones.
- **NPV is in absolute ₹** — directly measures *wealth creation* in today's money.
- **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Choose the one with the *highest positive NPV* (not highest IRR/PI).
- **Independent Projects:** Accept all projects with NPV > 0 (subject to capital rationing).
- **NPV Profile:** NPV vs $r$ graph. The X-intercept is the IRR. Slope is *always negative* for projects with positive cash flows.
- **Crossover Rate (Fisher Intersection):** Discount rate at which two NPV profiles intersect — important when NPV and IRR rankings conflict.
- **Terminal Cash Flow** = Salvage Value + Recovery of Working Capital + Tax effects on capital gain.
- **NPV is preferred over IRR** in mutually exclusive situations because of *scale* and *reinvestment-rate* assumptions.
- **Sensitivity Analysis:** Always conduct a "what-if" on key variables (selling price, cost, discount rate) — NPV is sensitive to *all* of them.
- **KTU Board Convention:** Use 5 decimal places for PVIF/PVIFA values quoted in the answer sheet. Round only the *final* NPV to the nearest rupee.

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Investment Decision Criteria — NPV**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, Course PEIET744 — Corporate Finance and Portfolio Management.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Payback Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Payback Period

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Payback Period (PBP)** is defined as the exact length of time (typically expressed in years) required for an investment project's cumulative net cash inflows to fully recover the original initial investment (capital outlay) from the project. It is the most elementary and widely used **non-discounted (traditional) capital budgeting technique** taught at the undergraduate level.

In formal KTU finance notation, if $CF_0$ is the initial investment and $CF_t$ represents the cash inflow in year $t$, then the Payback Period is the smallest integer $n$ such that:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} CF_t \geq CF_0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Note:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this concept is covered under the umbrella of *Traditional (or Unsophisticated) Capital Budgeting Techniques*, alongside the **Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)**. Unlike sophisticated techniques (NPV, IRR), it ignores the **time value of money** in its basic form and disregards cash flows occurring *after* the payback threshold is reached.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "EMI / Loan Recovery" Intuition

Imagine you borrow **₹5,00,000** from a friend to start a small business, and you promise to return the principal from the profits your business generates. Suppose your business earns a steady net profit of **₹1,00,000 per year** (after all expenses). How long will it take to return the borrowed money to your friend?

- Year 1: ₹1,00,000 returned → **Remaining debt: ₹4,00,000**
- Year 2: ₹1,00,000 returned → **Remaining debt: ₹3,00,000**
- Year 3: ₹1,00,000 returned → **Remaining debt: ₹2,00,000**
- Year 4: ₹1,00,000 returned → **Remaining debt: ₹1,00,000**
- Year 5: ₹1,00,000 returned → **Remaining debt: ₹0** ✅

So, the **Payback Period = 5 years**. The moment cumulative profits equal the original loan is your "payback moment." In corporate finance, your **initial investment plays the role of the loan**, and the **annual project cash flows play the role of yearly profits**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why do managers love this method?** It is brutally simple, easily understood by non-finance executives, and directly answers the question every risk-averse manager asks: *"How soon do I get my money back?"*

---

### 1.3 Types of Payback Period

| Type | Time Value of Money Considered? | Cash Flow Assumption | KTU Status |
|------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------|
| **Simple (Traditional) Payback Period** | ❌ No | Uses raw, undiscounted cash flows | Core syllabus topic |
| **Discounted Payback Period** | ✅ Yes (at cost of capital $k$) | Uses PV of cash flows | Advanced variant; sometimes asked as a Part B twist |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How the Method Works

The Payback Period method follows a strict, sequential logic:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Initial Investment ($CF_0$):** This is the total capital outlay at Year 0, typically a negative cash flow representing the cost of machinery, setup, R&D, etc.
- **Step 2 — Project the Annual Cash Inflows ($CF_1, CF_2, \dots, CF_n$):** These are the **after-tax operating cash flows** the project is expected to generate.
- **Step 3 — Build the Cumulative Cash Flow Schedule:** Sum the inflows year by year until the cumulative total is greater than or equal to the initial investment.
- **Step 4 — Identify the Payback Year:** The year in which cumulative cash flow first equals or exceeds the initial investment.
- **Step 5 — Compare with Cut-off Period:** If PBP $\leq$ Target/Maximum acceptable period, **Accept**; otherwise, **Reject**.

### 2.2 The Underlying Philosophy

The method is rooted in the **liquidity preference theory** and the **risk-aversion principle**:

1. **Risk Reduction:** Projects with shorter payback periods are exposed to less forecasting uncertainty. A project that pays back in 2 years is far less risky than one that pays back in 8 years.
2. **Liquidity Emphasis:** Firms facing cash crunches prefer short payback projects to preserve operational liquidity.
3. **Conservative Bias:** It heavily penalises long-gestation projects (e.g., infrastructure, R&D) that may be highly profitable in the long run.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table.** Direct formula application and computation questions are a 2024 Scheme favourite.

| # | Situation | Formula / Method | Variables |
|---|-----------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Even (Constant) Annual Cash Flows** | $PBP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}} = \dfrac{CF_0}{CF}$ | $CF_0$ = Initial outlay; $CF$ = Equal annual inflow |
| 2 | **Uneven Cash Flows (Whole Year Payback)** | Use **Cumulative Cash Flow Table** and read off the year when cumulative $\geq CF_0$ | All $CF_t$ values |
| 3 | **Uneven Cash Flows (Fractional Payback — Interpolation)** | $PBP = A + \dfrac{B}{C}$ | $A$ = Year *before* full recovery; $B$ = Unrecovered amount at end of year $A$; $C$ = Cash inflow during year $A+1$ |
| 4 | **Discounted Payback Period** | $PBP_d = A_d + \dfrac{B_d}{C_d}$ where all inflows are first discounted at cost of capital $k$ using $PV = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}$ | $k$ = Discount rate (cost of capital) |
| 5 | **Decision Rule** | Accept if $PBP \leq$ Target Payback; Reject if $PBP >$ Target Payback | Target set by management |
| 6 | **Mathematical Condition for Acceptance** | $\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t} \geq CF_0$ | Discounted variant |

> **Important:** In the interpolation formula, we use absolute values; mathematically written as $\left\lvert \frac{B}{C} \right\rvert$. Inside tables, render as `\vert B / C \vert` to avoid markdown pipe conflicts.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Corporate Utility

In engineering project appraisal (where this course has industrial applications), the Payback Period is used in:

- **Capital Equipment Purchase Decisions** (e.g., buying a CNC machine vs. a manual lathe).
- **Energy Efficiency Upgrades** (e.g., payback of solar panel installation in a factory).
- **Process Automation Projects** (e.g., ROI on robotics in a manufacturing line).
- **R&D Project Screening** as a *first-level filter* before sophisticated NPV/IRR analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common KTU 2024 question framing is: *"Critically evaluate the Payback Period as a capital budgeting technique."* The answer must always cover **Advantages**, **Limitations**, and a **comparative note** with NPV/IRR.

### 2.5 Merits and Demerits — KTU Two-Mark Favourite

| Merits (Why Used) | Demerits (Why Criticised) |
|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Simple to compute and understand | Ignores Time Value of Money (basic version) |
| Emphasises liquidity and early recovery | Ignores cash flows after payback period |
| Useful as a risk indicator for volatile projects | Biased against long-term, strategic projects |
| Easy communication to non-financial managers | No objective cutoff — based on subjective benchmark |
| Useful for projects with obsolescence risk (e.g., tech) | Cannot rank mutually exclusive projects correctly |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation of the Interpolation Formula

Suppose cumulative cash flow at the end of year $A$ is less than the initial investment $CF_0$, but by the end of year $A+1$, it exceeds $CF_0$. The payback clearly lies **within year $A+1$**.

**Setup:**
- Amount unrecovered at the *end* of year $A$ = $CF_0 - \text{Cumulative Cash Flow at end of Year } A$
- Cash flow generated during year $A+1$ = $CF_{A+1}$
- Fraction of year $A+1$ required to recover the remaining unrecovered amount = $\dfrac{\text{Unrecovered Amount}}{CF_{A+1}}$

**Mathematical Assembly:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PBP &= A + \frac{\text{Unrecovered amount at end of Year } A}{\text{Cash inflow during Year } (A+1)} \\
&= A + \frac{CF_0 - \left(\sum_{t=1}^{A} CF_t\right)}{CF_{A+1}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **interpolation formula** that is universally applied for fractional-year paybacks and is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU ESE.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Even Cash Flows (Model Question)

**Problem:** A company is evaluating a machine costing **₹5,00,000**. It generates a constant after-tax cash inflow of **₹1,25,000 per year** for 5 years. The management has set a target payback of **4 years**. Should the project be accepted?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify variables.
- $CF_0 = 5{,}00{,}000$
- $CF = 1{,}25{,}000$ (constant)
- Target PBP = 4 years

**Step 2:** Apply the even-cash-flow formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
PBP &= \frac{CF_0}{CF} \\
&= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}25{,}000} \\
&= 4 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the decision rule.

$$PBP_{\text{actual}} = 4 \text{ years} \leq PBP_{\text{target}} = 4 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion. ✅ **Accept the project** (the project exactly meets the target; many boards would marginally accept since PBP equals the cutoff).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Uneven Cash Flows with Interpolation

**Problem:** A project requires an initial investment of **₹10,00,000** and generates the following cash inflows. Calculate the Payback Period.

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) | Cumulative Cash Flow (₹) |
|------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| 1    | 2,00,000        | 2,00,000                  |
| 2    | 3,00,000        | 5,00,000                  |
| 3    | 3,00,000        | 8,00,000                  |
| 4    | 3,00,000        | **11,00,000**             |
| 5    | 2,00,000        | 13,00,000                 |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Locate the year in which cumulative cash flow first exceeds or equals the initial investment.
- End of Year 3: ₹8,00,000 < ₹10,00,000 (not yet recovered)
- End of Year 4: ₹11,00,000 ≥ ₹10,00,000 ✅ (recovered during Year 4)

**Step 2:** Identify the interpolation parameters.
- $A$ = 3 (the year *before* full recovery)
- Unrecovered amount at end of Year 3 = $CF_0 - \text{Cumulative}_3 = 10{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000$
- Cash flow during Year 4 = ₹3,00,000

**Step 3:** Apply the interpolation formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
PBP &= A + \frac{\text{Unrecovered Amount}}{CF_{A+1}} \\
&= 3 + \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 3 + 0.6667 \\
&= 3.67 \text{ years (approximately)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion. The Payback Period is **3 years and 8 months** (since $0.67 \times 12 \approx 8$ months).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Discounted Payback Period

**Problem:** A project costs **₹6,00,000** and yields cash inflows of ₹2,00,000 per year for 5 years. The cost of capital is **10% p.a.** Compute the Discounted Payback Period.

**Step 1:** Discount each cash flow using $PV = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1 + k)^t}$ with $k = 0.10$.

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10^1} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10} = 1{,}81{,}818.18 \\
PV_2 &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10^2} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.21} = 1{,}65{,}289.26 \\
PV_3 &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10^3} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.331} = 1{,}50{,}262.96 \\
PV_4 &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10^4} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.4641} = 1{,}36{,}602.69 \\
PV_5 &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10^5} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.61051} = 1{,}24{,}184.26
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Build the cumulative discounted cash flow table.

| Year | Discounted CF (₹) | Cumulative Discounted CF (₹) |
|------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1    | 1,81,818          | 1,81,818                     |
| 2    | 1,65,289          | 3,47,107                     |
| 3    | 1,50,263          | 4,97,370                     |
| 4    | 1,36,603          | 6,33,973                     |
| 5    | 1,24,184          | 7,58,157                     |

**Step 3:** Identify crossover.
- End of Year 3: ₹4,97,370 < ₹6,00,000 (not recovered)
- End of Year 4: ₹6,33,973 ≥ ₹6,00,000 ✅

**Step 4:** Apply interpolation.

$$\begin{aligned}
PBP_d &= 3 + \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}97{,}370}{1{,}36{,}603} \\
&= 3 + \frac{1{,}02{,}630}{1{,}36{,}603} \\
&= 3 + 0.7512 \\
&= 3.75 \text{ years (approximately)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation. Notice that the Discounted Payback (3.75 years) is **longer** than the Simple Payback ($6{,}00{,}000 \div 2{,}00{,}000 = 3$ years). This is **always** the case because discounting reduces the present value of future cash flows.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow — How the Payback Method is Applied in Practice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode1[Identify Project and Initial Investment CF0] --> nodeStep2[Estimate Annual Cash Inflows CFt]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3{Cash Flows Even or Uneven?}
    nodeStep3 -->|Even| nodeStep4A[Use Formula PBP = CF0 / CF]
    nodeStep3 -->|Uneven| nodeStep4B[Construct Cumulative Cash Flow Table]
    nodeStep4A --> nodeStep5A{Cash Flow Exactly Divisible?}
    nodeStep4A -->|Yes| nodeStep5A
    nodeStep4B --> nodeStep5B{Locate Year of Full Recovery}
    nodeStep5A -->|No| nodeStep6A[Apply Interpolation A + B / C]
    nodeStep5A -->|Yes| nodeStep7[Compute Final PBP]
    nodeStep5B -->|Recovery Falls Mid Year| nodeStep6B[Apply Interpolation A + B / C]
    nodeStep5B -->|Recovery at Year End| nodeStep7
    nodeStep6A --> nodeStep7
    nodeStep6B --> nodeStep7
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8{Compare PBP with Target Cutoff}
    nodeStep8 -->|PBP Less or Equal| nodeAccept[ACCEPT the Project]
    nodeStep8 -->|PBP Greater| nodeReject[REJECT the Project]
```

### 4.2 Functional Comparison — Simple vs. Discounted Payback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawInput[Raw Project Cash Flow Series CF0 to CFn] --> branchA
    branchA[Branch 1 Simple Payback] --> cumRaw[Cumulative Sum of Raw CFs]
    cumRaw --> resultA[PBP Simple]
    branchB[Branch 2 Discounted Payback] --> discountOp[Discount Each CFt at Cost of Capital k]
    discountOp --> cumDisc[Cumulative Sum of Discounted CFs]
    cumDisc --> resultB[PBP Discounted]
    resultA --> compareNode[Compare PBP Simple vs PBP Discounted]
    resultB --> compareNode
    compareNode --> note1[Note PBP Discounted is Always Greater than or Equal to PBP Simple]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Inputs, Process, and Outputs of the Payback Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputsBlock [INPUT LAYER]
        inA[Initial Investment CF0]
        inB[Annual Cash Inflow Estimates CF1 to CFn]
        inC[Target Payback Period Management Set Cutoff]
        inD[Optional Cost of Capital k for Discounted Variant]
    end
    subgraph processBlock [PROCESS LAYER]
        pA[Cash Flow Classification Even or Uneven]
        pB[Cumulative Cash Flow Accumulation]
        pC[Interpolation if Fractional Year Required]
        pD[Discounting Engine if Discounted Payback]
    end
    subgraph outputBlock [OUTPUT LAYER]
        outA[Computed Payback Period in Years]
        outB[Decision Accept or Reject]
        outC[Comparison with NPV or IRR for Sophisticated Review]
    end
    inputsBlock --> processBlock
    processBlock --> outputBlock
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

---

**Q1. Define the term 'Payback Period'. Mention its main limitation.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Payback Period is the time required for a project's cumulative cash inflows to recover the original initial investment. It is computed as $PBP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ for even cash flows, or via cumulative cash flow tables for uneven cash flows.
**Main limitation:** It ignores the time value of money (in the simple version) and disregards all cash flows occurring after the payback period, which can lead to the rejection of highly profitable long-term projects. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. Distinguish between 'Simple Payback Period' and 'Discounted Payback Period'.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Simple Payback | Discounted Payback |
|-------|----------------|---------------------|
| Time Value of Money | Ignored | Considered (cash flows discounted at cost of capital $k$) |
| Computation | Based on raw cash flows | Based on $PV = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}$ |
| Result | Always shorter or equal | Always longer or equal |
| Conservatism | Less conservative | More conservative |

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern, Module 1]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**A project of M/s Kerala Engineering Ltd. involves an initial investment of ₹8,00,000. The project is expected to generate cash inflows of ₹2,50,000, ₹3,00,000, ₹3,50,000, and ₹2,00,000 in Years 1 to 4 respectively.**

**(a)** Compute the Payback Period for the project.
**(b)** If the management has a target payback of 3 years, advise whether the project should be accepted. Comment on the major limitations of this method.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Construct the cumulative cash flow table.**

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) | Cumulative Cash Flow (₹) |
|------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| 1    | 2,50,000        | 2,50,000                  |
| 2    | 3,00,000        | 5,50,000                  |
| 3    | 3,50,000        | 9,00,000                  |
| 4    | 2,00,000        | 11,00,000                 |

**Step 2: Identify the crossover year.**

- End of Year 2: ₹5,50,000 < ₹8,00,000 (not recovered)
- End of Year 3: ₹9,00,000 ≥ ₹8,00,000 ✅ (recovered in Year 3)

**Step 3: Interpolate for fractional year.**

- $A$ = 2 (year before recovery)
- Unrecovered amount = $8{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000$
- Cash flow in Year 3 = ₹3,50,000

$$\begin{aligned}
PBP &= 2 + \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} \\
&= 2 + 0.7143 \\
&= 2.71 \text{ years (approximately)} \\
&\approx 2 \text{ years and } 9 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer (a): The Payback Period is approximately 2.71 years (2 years and 9 months).** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compare with target.**

$$PBP_{\text{actual}} = 2.71 \text{ years} \leq PBP_{\text{target}} = 3 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2: Decision.** ✅ **The project should be ACCEPTED** since the actual payback is less than the target.

**Step 3: Major limitations of the Payback Period method (write any three for full marks):**

1. **Ignores Time Value of Money:** The simple version treats ₹1 received in Year 1 and ₹1 received in Year 5 as equal, which is theoretically incorrect.
2. **Ignores Post-Payback Cash Flows:** A project that pays back in 3 years but yields nothing thereafter is rated equally to one that pays back in 3 years and yields ₹50 lakhs more over the next 5 years.
3. **Subjective Cutoff Period:** The target payback is set arbitrarily by management, leading to inconsistency in decision-making.
4. **No Profitability Measure:** It tells us *when* money is recovered, not *whether* the project is profitable in absolute or relative terms.

*(7 marks)*

---

### ❓ Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks)

**A firm is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects with the following cash flows. The cost of capital is 12% p.a. and the target payback is 4 years.**

| Year | Project X (₹) | Project Y (₹) |
|------|---------------|---------------|
| 0 (Initial Investment) | (6,00,000) | (6,00,000) |
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 2,00,000 |
| 2 | 2,00,000 | 2,00,000 |
| 3 | 2,50,000 | 2,00,000 |
| 4 | 2,00,000 | 1,50,000 |
| 5 | 1,00,000 | 1,50,000 |

**(a)** Calculate the Payback Period for both projects and identify which is preferable on this basis.
**(b)** Briefly discuss why the Payback Period may give a misleading ranking between mutually exclusive projects.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Project X — Cumulative Cash Flow Table:**

| Year | CF (₹) | Cumulative (₹) |
|------|--------|----------------|
| 1    | 1,50,000 | 1,50,000    |
| 2    | 2,00,000 | 3,50,000    |
| 3    | 2,50,000 | 6,00,000    |
| 4    | 2,00,000 | 8,00,000    |
| 5    | 1,00,000 | 9,00,000    |

- Project X recovers ₹6,00,000 *exactly* at the **end of Year 3**.
- **PBP (Project X) = 3 years**

**Project Y — Cumulative Cash Flow Table:**

| Year | CF (₹) | Cumulative (₹) |
|------|--------|----------------|
| 1    | 2,00,000 | 2,00,000    |
| 2    | 2,00,000 | 4,00,000    |
| 3    | 2,00,000 | 6,00,000    |
| 4    | 1,50,000 | 7,50,000    |
| 5    | 1,50,000 | 9,00,000    |

- Project Y recovers ₹6,00,000 *exactly* at the **end of Year 3**.
- **PBP (Project Y) = 3 years**

**Conclusion (a):** Both projects have an identical Payback Period of 3 years, which is less than the 4-year target. **Both are acceptable on payback criteria**, and the method fails to differentiate between them. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Why the Payback Period gives a misleading ranking between mutually exclusive projects:**

1. **Equal PBP, Unequal Total Profitability:** Both projects have PBP = 3 years, but Project Y yields a total of ₹9,00,000 vs. Project X's ₹9,00,000 (in this case identical) — however, in general, the time-distribution of cash flows differs, and the method cannot capture the difference in profitability after payback.

2. **Ignores Cash Flow Magnitude Post-Payback:** Suppose Project X yields ₹1,00,000 in Year 5 and Project Y yields ₹5,00,000 in Year 5. The Payback Period treats them identically because both have already recovered the initial investment. The huge additional benefit of Y is **completely ignored**.

3. **No Consideration of Risk-Return Trade-off:** A project that pays back quickly but yields little may be ranked equal to one that pays back in the same time but yields significantly more wealth. Payback cannot account for this trade-off.

4. **Conflict with NPV/IRR:** Sophisticated techniques like NPV may clearly favour one project, but Payback may reject both or accept both without differentiation. This makes Payback unsuitable as a *primary* decision tool for mutually exclusive choices.

5. **Time Value of Money is Ignored:** ₹1 in Year 1 is valued identically to ₹1 in Year 3 in the simple version, leading to distorted rankings.

**Conclusion (b):** The Payback Period should be used only as a **secondary, supplementary risk filter**, not as the primary criterion for choosing among mutually exclusive projects. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the "Year Before" as $A$** in the interpolation formula. Students often mistakenly take $A$ as the year *of* recovery, not the year *before*. This costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up "Unrecovered Amount" calculation** — it is $CF_0 - \text{Cumulative CF at end of Year } A$, not the cash flow of Year $A$ itself.
> 3. **Writing the formula without identifying $A$, $B$, and $C$ explicitly** in the answer sheet. Examiners award method marks only when the variables are clearly labelled.
> 4. **Omitting units** ("years") in the final answer. Always write "X years" or "X years and Y months."
> 5. **Ignoring the decision rule** — never end a payback problem without explicitly comparing to the cutoff and stating Accept/Reject.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Payback Period = time to recover initial investment from project cash inflows.**
- ✅ **Simple Payback** uses raw cash flows; **Discounted Payback** uses PV-discounted cash flows.
- ✅ **Even cash flow formula:** $PBP = \dfrac{CF_0}{CF}$.
- ✅ **Uneven cash flow formula (interpolation):** $PBP = A + \dfrac{\vert B \vert}{C}$, where $A$ = year before full recovery, $B$ = unrecovered amount, $C$ = next year's cash flow.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** Accept if $PBP \leq$ Target Cutoff; Reject if $PBP >$ Target Cutoff.
- ✅ **Discounted Payback is always $\geq$ Simple Payback** (because discounting reduces future cash flows).
- ✅ **Biggest limitation:** Ignores time value of money (in simple version) and post-payback cash flows.
- ✅ **Biggest advantage:** Simplicity, liquidity emphasis, and risk indication.
- ✅ **Use as a screening tool, not a primary decision tool**, especially for mutually exclusive projects.
- ✅ **Always label $A$, $B$, $C$ explicitly** in interpolation problems for method marks.
- ✅ **Always state the decision** (Accept/Reject) explicitly — examiners deduct marks for ambiguous conclusions.
- ✅ **Discounted Payback formula** requires the discount factor table or computation of $PV = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}$.
- ✅ **Comparison with NPV/IRR:** Payback is a *traditional/unsophisticated* method; NPV/IRR are *sophisticated/discounted* methods. Always make this distinction in theory answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Average Accounting Rate of Return
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Average Accounting Rate of Return

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Average Accounting Rate of Return (AARR)**, also referred to in KTU textbooks as the **Average Rate of Return (ARR)** or the **Accounting Return on Investment (AROI)**, is a non-discounted capital budgeting technique that evaluates the profitability of a proposed investment by comparing the **average annual accounting profit** earned from the project to the **book value of the investment** (either initial or average).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *Corporate Finance and Portfolio Management* (PEIET744), AARR is positioned as an **accounting-based profitability measure** — meaning it relies on accrual-based accounting profits (i.e., profits *after* deducting depreciation) rather than on actual cash inflows. The decision rule under the KTU valuation key is binary: **Accept** a project if its AARR is greater than or equal to the firm's **hurdle rate** (target/minimum required rate of return); **Reject** if AARR falls below the hurdle rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style for Board Answers):**
> *"The Average Accounting Rate of Return is the ratio of average annual accounting profit (after depreciation) to the average book value of investment, expressed as a percentage. It measures the earning power of a project on a per-annum accounting basis."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you start a small coconut-trading business in Kerala by investing **₹5,00,000** of your own savings.
