> **Standard Metric:** A project's cost of capital is denoted as **k** or **r**, and the IRR is the discount rate that drives NPV to **exactly zero**. When no such *r* > 0 exists, the project is said to suffer from the "No IRR" problem.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🛣️ The "Hilly Road" Analogy for Multiple IRR
Imagine you are driving from City A (initial investment) to City B (final salvage) across a hilly landscape. The *sea level* represents **NPV = 0**. If the road is flat, you cross sea level only once. But if the road dips into a valley, climbs over a mountain, and then descends again, you could cross sea level **multiple times** — yet you still began and ended your journey. The "discount rate" at sea level is the IRR, and just like that road, the project has **two or more IRRs** even though there is only one true financial destination.

### 🌊 The "Tunnel" Analogy for No IRR
Now consider a tunnel bored entirely *underneath* the sea. As you walk through it, you never touch sea level — no matter how fast or slow you walk. Similarly, a project whose NPV is **always positive** (or always negative) for every discount rate has **no IRR**, because the NPV curve never intersects the horizontal axis.

---

## 1.4 Why These Problems Matter in Corporate Finance

| Phenomenon | Mathematical Symptom | Decision-Making Symptom |
|---|---|---|
| **Multiple IRR** | Polynomial has ≥ 2 real positive roots | IRR rule gives conflicting accept/reject signals |
| **No IRR** | Polynomial has zero real positive roots | IRR rule cannot be applied at all |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, blindly applying the IRR criterion to non-conventional cash flows is a **classic mark-deduction trap**. Examiners explicitly test whether the student has identified the structural flaw and recommended an alternative (NPV or MIRR).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

### A. The NPV Polynomial Equation

The NPV of a project with cash flows $CF_0, CF_1, \ldots, CF_n$ is:

$$NPV(r) = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

When we set $NPV(r) = 0$ and multiply both sides by $(1+r)^n$, we obtain a polynomial of degree *n*:

$$CF_0(1+r)^n + CF_1(1+r)^{n-1} + \cdots + CF_n = 0$$

### B. Descartes' Rule of Signs (The Root Cause)

- The **number of sign changes** in the cash flow stream equals the **maximum possible number of positive real roots** of this polynomial.
- A single sign change (conventional cash flow) → at most **one** positive IRR (unambiguous).
- Two sign changes → up to **two** positive IRRs (Multiple IRR Problem).
- Three sign changes → up to **three** positive IRRs, and so on.

### C. NPV Profile Behavior

The NPV profile is a plot of $NPV(r)$ versus *r*. For conventional projects, it is a monotonically decreasing curve crossing the *x*-axis exactly once. For non-conventional projects, the profile is **non-monotonic** — it dips, rises, and dips again — yielding multiple crossings or none at all.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the cash flow stream and count sign changes.**
- Zero sign changes → mathematically impossible to solve for IRR (No IRR).
- One sign change → unique IRR (normal case).
- Two or more sign changes → multiple IRRs possible.

**Step 2 — Apply Descartes' Rule to confirm theoretical possibility.**

**Step 3 — Solve the polynomial using algebraic methods** (quadratic formula for degree 2, numerical methods for higher degrees).

**Step 4 — Examine the NPV profile** to determine *which* IRR is economically meaningful, if any.

**Step 5 — If the IRR criterion fails, fall back to NPV or MIRR.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **high-yield formula bank** for this topic. Memorize the notation and the structural form of each equation.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Boundary Condition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NPV Equation** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $r > -1$ | Foundation of all DCF analysis |
| 2 | **IRR Definition** | $NPV(r^{\ast}) = 0$ | $r^{\ast} > 0$ | Rate that zeroes NPV |
| 3 | **Descartes' Rule** | $\text{Max positive roots} = \text{Number of sign changes}$ | Cash flow vector | Predicts Multiple IRR |
| 4 | **Quadratic IRR** | $r = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ | $b^2 - 4ac \geq 0$ | Two-IRR project |
| 5 | **No-IRR Discriminant** | $b^2 - 4ac < 0$ | Negative discriminant | No real solution exists |
| 6 | **MIRR (Remedy)** | $MIRR = \left( \frac{FV_{inflows}}{-PV_{outflows}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$ | $n =$ project life | Unique, valid IRR substitute |
| 7 | **NPV Decision Rule** | Accept if $NPV > 0$ at cost of capital *k* | Always valid | Universal remedy |
| 8 | **Pure Investment Cash Flow** | All $CF_t \geq 0$ (or all $\leq 0$) | Zero sign changes | Impossible IRR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the MIRR formula, $-PV_{outflows}$ is rendered in **absolute value terms** using $\vert PV_{outflows} \vert$ to keep the denominator positive. In the formula table above, the negation sign is used implicitly to express magnitude only.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Corporate Utility

- **Oil & Gas Industry**: Exploration projects often have non-conventional cash flows (large upfront investment → initial revenues → closure/remediation costs), making them classic **Multiple IRR** candidates.
- **Mining & Infrastructure**: Strip-mining or toll-road projects involve huge end-of-life restoration costs after the revenue phase — a textbook source of Multiple IRR.
- **Pharmaceutical R&D**: Projects with patent expiries and litigation payouts can have **No IRR** situations if NPV remains positive across all reasonable discount rates.
- **Mutually Exclusive Project Selection**: Even with conventional cash flows, IRR can mis-rank projects of different scale. The **incremental IRR analysis** (Fisherian crossover) is the engineered remedy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Multiple IRR Problem

**Project X Cash Flows:**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | $-1{,}600$ |
| 1 | $+10{,}000$ |
| 2 | $-10{,}000$ |

**Sign change analysis:** Negative → Positive → Negative = **2 sign changes** → Multiple IRR expected.

### Derivation

The NPV equation is:

$$NPV = -1600 + \frac{10000}{(1+r)} - \frac{10000}{(1+r)^2}$$

Setting $NPV = 0$ and multiplying throughout by $(1+r)^2$:

$$-1600(1+r)^2 + 10000(1+r) - 10000 = 0$$

Expanding $(1+r)^2 = 1 + 2r + r^2$:

$$-1600(1 + 2r + r^2) + 10000(1+r) - 10000 = 0$$

$$-1600 - 3200r - 1600r^2 + 10000 + 10000r - 10000 = 0$$

**Explanation:** We are simply distributing the $-1600$ coefficient across the squared binomial and adding the remaining linear terms. Notice the $+10000$ and $-10000$ from the numerator terms cancel out.

Combining like terms:

$$-1600r^2 + 6800r - 1600 = 0$$

Dividing the entire equation by $-1600$ to obtain a monic polynomial:

$$r^2 - 4.25r + 1 = 0$$

**Explanation:** Dividing by a negative flips the signs to produce a clean standard quadratic form $ar^2 + br + c = 0$ with $a = 1$.

Applying the **Quadratic Formula** with $a = 1$, $b = -4.25$, $c = 1$:

$$r = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$

$$r = \frac{4.25 \pm \sqrt{(-4.25)^2 - 4(1)(1)}}{2(1)}$$

$$r = \frac{4.25 \pm \sqrt{18.0625 - 4}}{2}$$

$$r = \frac{4.25 \pm \sqrt{14.0625}}{2}$$

$$r = \frac{4.25 \pm 3.75}{2}$$

**Two distinct roots emerge:**

$$r_1 = \frac{4.25 - 3.75}{2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad IRR_1 = 25\%$$

$$r_2 = \frac{4.25 + 3.75}{2} = \frac{8.0}{2} = 4.00 \quad \Rightarrow \quad IRR_2 = 400\%$$

**Interpretation:** Both 25% and 400% make NPV mathematically zero. If the firm's cost of capital is 10%, the IRR rule would say "Accept" (using 25%). But if the manager picks 400% and the cost of capital is 350%, the rule would say "Reject" — yet 25% says "Accept". The criterion **collapses**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Trap:** In KTU valuation, students often pick *one* IRR and proceed. Always state **both roots** and discuss why IRR is unreliable.

### Verification by NPV at Cost of Capital = 10%

$$NPV = -1600 + \frac{10000}{1.10} - \frac{10000}{(1.10)^2}$$

$$NPV = -1600 + 9090.91 - 8264.46 = -773.55$$

The NPV is negative → **Reject** the project. The IRR rule fails to give a consistent answer; NPV succeeds.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — No IRR Problem

**Project Y Cash Flows:**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | $+500$ |
| 1 | $-800$ |
| 2 | $+400$ |

**Sign change analysis:** Positive → Negative → Positive = **2 sign changes**, so Multiple or No IRR is possible.

### Derivation

The NPV equation:

$$NPV = 500 - \frac{800}{(1+r)} + \frac{400}{(1+r)^2}$$

Setting $NPV = 0$ and multiplying by $(1+r)^2$:

$$500(1+r)^2 - 800(1+r) + 400 = 0$$

Expanding:

$$500 + 1000r + 500r^2 - 800 - 800r + 400 = 0$$

Combining like terms:

$$500r^2 + 200r + 100 = 0$$

Dividing by 100:

$$5r^2 + 2r + 1 = 0$$

**Computing the discriminant:**

$$\Delta = b^2 - 4ac = (2)^2 - 4(5)(1) = 4 - 20 = -16$$

Since $\Delta = -16 < 0$, the polynomial has **no real roots**. Hence, **No IRR exists** for this project.

**Interpretation:** The NPV curve never touches the x-axis for any real discount rate. The IRR criterion simply **cannot be applied**.

### NPV Profile Verification

At $r = 0\%$:

$$NPV = 500 - 800 + 400 = +100 \quad (\text{Positive})$$

At $r = 100\%$:

$$NPV = 500 - 400 + 100 = +200 \quad (\text{Positive})$$

At $r = -50\%$ (mathematical, just for profile study):

$$NPV = 500 - \frac{800}{0.5} + \frac{400}{0.25} = 500 - 1600 + 1600 = +500 \quad (\text{Positive})$$

The NPV is **always positive** — confirming the **No IRR** scenario.

## 3.3 Remedies — The Modified IRR (MIRR) Demonstration

To address the Multiple IRR case (Project X), let us compute **MIRR** assuming a finance rate of 8% and reinvestment rate of 12%.

**Step 1 — Discount all negative cash flows to present value (at finance rate):**

$$PV_{outflows} = 1600 + \frac{10000}{(1.08)^2} = 1600 + 8573.39 = 10{,}173.39$$

**Step 2 — Compound all positive cash flows to future value (at reinvestment rate) at year 2:**

$$FV_{inflows} = 10000(1.12)^1 = 11{,}200$$

**Step 3 — Apply the MIRR formula:**

$$MIRR = \left( \frac{FV_{inflows}}{-PV_{outflows}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$

$$MIRR = \left( \frac{11200}{10173.39} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}} - 1$$

$$MIRR = (1.1010)^{0.5} - 1 = 1.0493 - 1 = 0.0493$$

$$MIRR \approx 4.93\%$$

**Conclusion:** A single, unambiguous MIRR of ~4.93% is obtained. If the cost of capital exceeds 4.93%, reject the project. The MIRR resolves the Multiple IRR ambiguity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table

| Project | Cash Flow Pattern | Sign Changes | Discriminant | Result | Remedy |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|---|---|
| **Project X** | $-, +, -$ | 2 | $\Delta > 0$ | **Two IRRs (25%, 400%)** | Use **MIRR** or **NPV** |
| **Project Y** | $+, -, +$ | 2 | $\Delta < 0$ | **No IRR** | Use **NPV** directly |
| **Pure Investment** | $-, +, +$ (or $-, +, -, +$) | 1 | $\Delta \geq 0$ | Unique IRR | IRR rule is fine |
| **Pure Financing** | $+, -, -$ | 1 | $\Delta \geq 0$ | Unique IRR | IRR rule is fine |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for IRR Anomalies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Cash Flow Stream] --> B[Count Sign Changes in CF Stream]
    B --> C{Sign Changes = 0?}
    C -- Yes --> C1[No IRR Possible: Use NPV Rule]
    C -- No --> D{Sign Changes = 1?}
    D -- Yes --> D1[Unique IRR: Apply Standard IRR Rule]
    D -- No --> E{Multiple Sign Changes >= 2?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Compute NPV Polynomial of Degree n]
    F --> G{Discriminant of Polynomial}
    G -- Two or More Positive Roots --> H[Multiple IRR Problem Detected]
    G -- No Real Positive Root --> I[No IRR Problem Detected]
    G -- Exactly One Positive Root --> J[Unique IRR: Proceed Normally]
    H --> K[Remedy: Use MIRR or NPV]
    I --> L[Remedy: Use NPV Only]
    K --> M[Final Decision: Accept or Reject]
    L --> M
    J --> M
    D1 --> M
    C1 --> M
    M[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — NPV Profile Under Different Cash Flow Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG1[Conventional Cash Flow: One Sign Change]
        A1[CF0 Negative] --> A2[Subsequent CFs Positive]
        A2 --> A3[NPV Curve Decreases Monotonically]
        A3 --> A4[Crosses X Axis Once: Unique IRR]
    end
    subgraph SG2[Non-Conventional Cash Flow: Multiple Sign Changes]
        B1[CF0 Negative] --> B2[Middle CF Positive]
        B2 --> B3[Terminal CF Negative]
        B3 --> B4[NPV Curve Oscillates]
        B4 --> B5[Crosses X Axis Multiple Times: Multiple IRR]
    end
    subgraph SG3[Pure Investment or Negative Discriminant]
        C1[All CFs Same Sign] --> C2[NPV Curve Stays Above or Below X Axis]
        C2 --> C3[Never Crosses X Axis: No IRR]
    end
    A4 --> DEC[Decision Step]
    B5 --> DEC
    C3 --> DEC
    DEC --> REM[Apply Remedy: NPV or MIRR]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Process Flow for Handling IRR Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Tabulate Cash Flows] --> P2[Step 2: Apply Descartes Rule of Signs]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Construct NPV Equation as Polynomial]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Solve Polynomial for Real Positive Roots]
    P4 --> P5{Root Count Check}
    P5 -- Exactly One Root --> P6[Standard IRR: Compare with Cost of Capital]
    P5 -- Two or More Roots --> P7[Multiple IRR: Switch to MIRR Computation]
    P5 -- Zero Real Roots --> P8[No IRR: Compute NPV Directly]
    P6 --> P9[Final Recommendation]
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9[Decision Communicated to Management]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic flow above mirrors the step-by-step decision protocol expected in KTU valuation scripts. Examiners award full credit when the student demonstrates **systematic reasoning** rather than ad-hoc computation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"What is the Multiple IRR Problem? Under what cash flow condition does it arise?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Multiple IRR Problem** is a situation in capital budgeting where a project yields **two or more mathematically valid Internal Rates of Return**, making the IRR acceptance rule ambiguous.

It arises when the project's cash flow stream contains **more than one sign change** (i.e., non-conventional or non-normal cash flows). By **Descartes' Rule of Signs**, the number of positive real roots of the NPV equation cannot exceed the number of sign changes, so multiple sign changes can yield multiple IRRs.

**Example:** A project with cash flows $-1000, +2500, -2000$ has two sign changes and therefore two potential IRRs.

*(Allocation: Definition — 1 mark; Condition — 1 mark; Example — 1 mark)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Explain the 'No IRR' problem with an example."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **No IRR Problem** occurs when the NPV equation $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$ has **no real positive solution** for *r*. The project therefore has **no IRR** at which NPV equals zero.

**Conditions leading to No IRR:**
- Cash flows all have the **same sign** (pure investment or pure financing).
- The **discriminant** of the NPV polynomial is negative.

**Example:** A project with cash flows $+500, -800, +400$ gives the polynomial $5r^2 + 2r + 1 = 0$, whose discriminant is $4 - 20 = -16 < 0$, yielding **no real IRR**. The remedy is to apply the **NPV criterion** directly.

*(Allocation: Definition — 1 mark; Causes — 1 mark; Example with discriminant — 1 mark)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Note:** Students must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A project requires an initial investment of ₹ 1,600. It generates ₹ 10,000 at the end of Year 1 and requires a terminal outflow of ₹ 10,000 at the end of Year 2 (e.g., restoration cost). The cost of capital is 10%.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compute the IRR(s) and demonstrate the Multiple IRR problem.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Tabulate the cash flows.**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | $-1{,}600$ |
| 1 | $+10{,}000$ |
| 2 | $-10{,}000$ |

**Step 2: Identify sign changes.**

$(-)$ → $(+)$ → $(-)$ = **2 sign changes**. By Descartes' Rule, **up to 2 positive real IRRs are possible**.

**Step 3: Set up the NPV equation.**

$$NPV = -1600 + \frac{10000}{(1+r)} - \frac{10000}{(1+r)^2} = 0$$

**Step 4: Multiply through by $(1+r)^2$ to obtain a polynomial.**

$$-1600(1+r)^2 + 10000(1+r) - 10000 = 0$$

**Step 5: Expand and simplify.**

$$-1600 - 3200r - 1600r^2 + 10000 + 10000r - 10000 = 0$$

$$-1600r^2 + 6800r - 1600 = 0$$

**Step 6: Divide by $-1600$ to get the monic quadratic.**

$$r^2 - 4.25r + 1 = 0$$

**Step 7: Apply the quadratic formula.**

$$r = \frac{4.25 \pm \sqrt{(4.25)^2 - 4(1)(1)}}{2} = \frac{4.25 \pm \sqrt{14.0625}}{2} = \frac{4.25 \pm 3.75}{2}$$

$$r_1 = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad IRR_1 = 25\%$$

$$r_2 = \frac{8.0}{2} = 4.00 \quad \Rightarrow \quad IRR_2 = 400\%$$

**Step 8: Decision-making dilemma.**

Since the cost of capital is 10%:
- Using $IRR_1 = 25\%$ → Accept (since 25% > 10%).
- Using $IRR_2 = 400\%$ → Accept (since 400% > 10%).
- However, NPV at 10% is negative ($-773.55$), so the project **should be rejected**. This contradiction confirms the **Multiple IRR ambiguity**.

*(7 marks: Setup — 2; Algebra — 2; Roots — 2; Interpretation — 1)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Compute the Modified IRR (MIRR) and recommend whether to accept or reject the project. Assume finance rate = reinvestment rate = 10%.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify all negative and positive cash flows.**

- Negative CFs (outflows): $CF_0 = -1600$, $CF_2 = -10000$.
- Positive CFs (inflows): $CF_1 = +10000$.

**Step 2: Compute the Present Value of all outflows at the finance rate (10%).**

$$PV_{outflows} = -1600 + \frac{-10000}{(1.10)^2} = -1600 - 8264.46 = -9864.46$$

$$\vert PV_{outflows} \vert = 9864.46$$

**Step 3: Compute the Future Value of all inflows at the reinvestment rate (10%) at the end of Year 2.**

$$FV_{inflows} = 10000 \times (1.10)^{(2-1)} = 10000 \times 1.10 = 11000$$

**Step 4: Apply the MIRR formula with $n = 2$.**

$$MIRR = \left( \frac{FV_{inflows}}{\vert PV_{outflows} \vert} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$

$$MIRR = \left( \frac{11000}{9864.46} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}} - 1$$

$$MIRR = (1.1152)^{0.5} - 1 = 1.0560 - 1 = 0.0560$$

$$\boxed{MIRR \approx 5.60\%}$$

**Step 5: Decision.**

Since $MIRR = 5.60\% < k = 10\%$ (cost of capital), the project should be **REJECTED**.

This single, unambiguous MIRR resolves the Multiple IRR ambiguity and gives a clear decision consistent with the NPV method.

*(7 marks: Identification of flows — 1; PV of outflows — 2; FV of inflows — 2; MIRR calculation — 1; Decision — 1)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Consider a project with the following cash flows:**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | $+500$ |
| 1 | $-800$ |
| 2 | $+400$ |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Show that this project has No IRR. Compute the NPV at cost of capital = 10%.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify sign changes.**

$(+)$ → $(-)$ → $(+)$ = **2 sign changes**. By Descartes' Rule, up to 2 real positive roots are mathematically possible.

**Step 2: Set up the NPV equation.**

$$NPV = 500 - \frac{800}{(1+r)} + \frac{400}{(1+r)^2} = 0$$

**Step 3: Multiply through by $(1+r)^2$ to obtain a polynomial.**

$$500(1+r)^2 - 800(1+r) + 400 = 0$$

**Step 4: Expand and simplify.**

$$500 + 1000r + 500r^2 - 800 - 800r + 400 = 0$$

$$500r^2 + 200r + 100 = 0$$

Dividing by 100:

$$5r^2 + 2r + 1 = 0$$

**Step 5: Compute the discriminant.**

$$\Delta = b^2 - 4ac = (2)^2 - 4(5)(1) = 4 - 20 = -16$$

Since $\Delta = -16 < 0$, the quadratic has **no real roots**. Therefore, **No IRR exists** for this project.

**Step 6: Compute NPV at $r = 10\%$.**

$$NPV = 500 - \frac{800}{1.10} + \frac{400}{(1.10)^2}$$

$$NPV = 500 - 727.27 + 330.58 = 103.31$$

$$\boxed{NPV = ₹ 103.31 > 0}$$

**Step 7: Decision.** Since NPV is positive at the cost of capital, **ACCEPT** the project.

*(7 marks: Polynomial setup — 2; Discriminant — 2; No IRR conclusion — 1; NPV at 10% — 1; Decision — 1)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Discuss the various remedies available when faced with the Multiple IRR and No IRR problems.

**Solution:**

### **Remedies for Multiple IRR Problem:**

1. **Use the NPV Criterion:** The NPV method is unaffected by cash flow patterns and gives an unambiguous accept/reject signal. Always compute NPV at the firm's cost of capital.

2. **Use Modified Internal Rate of Return (MIRR):** MIRR assumes a realistic finance rate (for outflows) and reinvestment rate (for inflows), collapsing the multiple IRRs into a **single, economically meaningful rate**.

3. **Incremental IRR Analysis (for mutually exclusive projects):** Rank projects by size and apply IRR to the incremental cash flows between projects.

4. **Profitability Index (PI):** $PI = \frac{PV \text{ of future cash flows}}{Initial Investment}$. A PI > 1 indicates acceptance.

### **Remedies for No IRR Problem:**

1. **Use the NPV Criterion:** The most reliable remedy. Compute NPV directly at the cost of capital — if positive, accept.

2. **Use Profitability Index:** For projects of different scales, PI normalizes the metric.

3. **Use Payback Period or Discounted Payback Period:** As secondary screening tools, though they have limitations.

4. **Restructure Cash Flows:** If possible, re-negotiate the timing of inflows/outflows to produce a more conventional stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The **NPV method is the universally valid remedy** for both the Multiple IRR and No IRR problems. MIRR is a strong secondary alternative when the IRR criterion is desired but cannot be unambiguously applied.

*(7 marks: Multiple IRR remedies — 4 marks; No IRR remedies — 3 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for failing to identify the **number of sign changes** before attempting IRR computation.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** for stating only one IRR root when two exist. Always quote **both** roots.
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for not computing the **NPV at cost of capital** as the final tie-breaker.
> - For "No IRR" problems, students **lose 3 marks** if they do not compute the **discriminant** explicitly.
> - Avoid using the IRR rule for **mutually exclusive** projects — use **Incremental IRR** or **NPV**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

- ⭐ **Multiple IRR** arises when cash flows have **≥ 2 sign changes**. The NPV equation yields a polynomial of degree *n* with potentially multiple real roots.

- ⭐ **No IRR** arises when the NPV polynomial has **no real positive roots** (negative discriminant) OR when cash flows are **all of one sign**.

- ⭐ **Descartes' Rule of Signs** is the foundational diagnostic: number of positive real roots ≤ number of sign changes in the cash flow stream.

- ⭐ **Always begin** by tabulating cash flows and counting sign changes **before** attempting any IRR computation.

- ⭐ For a quadratic cash flow stream (degree 2), use the **quadratic formula**:
  $$r = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$

- ⭐ The **NPV method is the universal remedy** — it never fails regardless of cash flow pattern.

- ⭐ **MIRR (Modified IRR)** is the engineered alternative that combines the simplicity of IRR with the rigor of NPV:
  $$MIRR = \left( \frac{FV_{inflows}}{\vert PV_{outflows} \vert} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$

- ⭐ **Real-world contexts** where these problems occur: mining restoration, oil & gas decommissioning, R&D with patent cliffs, environmental cleanup projects.

- ⭐ In KTU 14-mark questions, the **valuation pattern** is: (i) tabulate cash flows, (ii) count sign changes, (iii) set up polynomial, (iv) solve, (v) compute NPV at cost of capital, (vi) recommend decision with justification.

- ⭐ **Avoid the IRR rule for mutually exclusive projects** — always use **Incremental IRR** or **NPV**.

- ⭐ **Decision rule when MIRR is computed:** Accept if $MIRR > k$ (cost of capital); Reject if $MIRR < k$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cash Flow Projections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cash Flow Projections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cash Flow Projections** refer to the systematic, forward-looking estimation of all anticipated monetary inflows (receipts) and outflows (payments) that a firm, project, or investment is expected to generate over a defined future horizon, typically aligned with the project's economic life or a strategic planning window (usually **3 to 10 years**). 

In the precise terminology of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Corporate Finance and Portfolio Management**, cash flow projection is the analytical process of translating qualitative business decisions, operating assumptions, capital expenditure schedules, and tax regulations into a quantitative stream of periodic (annual, semi-annual, or quarterly) cash movements that can subsequently be discounted to derive present value, Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), and other capital budgeting decision metrics.

A cash flow projection is fundamentally distinct from a **profit projection** because it adheres strictly to the **cash basis of accounting** rather than the accrual basis. This means:
- Non-cash expenses (such as **depreciation**, **amortization**, and **provisions**) are added back.
- Non-cash revenues (such as **credit sales not yet collected**) are excluded.
- Capital expenditures are treated as cash outflows at the time of occurrence, not as depreciation spread over years.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** Cash Flow Projection is the cornerstone input for all capital budgeting techniques covered in this module. Every discounted cash flow (DCF) valuation in PEIET744 begins with a correctly constructed projected cash flow schedule. A one-mark deduction is applied in valuation for confusing cash flow with accounting profit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **three-year road trip across India** in a second-hand car. Before you leave, you don't just calculate your *expected profit* (income from side gigs minus costs on paper) — you actually count the *money leaving your wallet* and the *money entering it*:

- **Outflows (Cash going OUT):** Buying the car, fuel, tolls, hotel stays, food, emergency repairs, EMI on a car loan.
- **Inflows (Cash coming IN):** Salary credited, side-business earnings, money from selling old belongings, refunds.
- **Non-cash items to IGNORE:** The *decline in value* of the car (depreciation) — your wallet doesn't feel this every day; it's not a real cash outflow until you actually sell.
- **Timing matters:** A ₹1 lakh repair bill in **Year 1** hurts more (in present value) than the same bill in **Year 3**, because money today is worth more than the same money tomorrow.

A **Cash Flow Projection** is exactly this — but for a business or a capital project. It forecasts, year by year, *when* and *how much* real money will move in and out, and then uses this to judge whether the trip (the project) is worth taking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** In infrastructure project finance (e.g., the Kochi Metro or GIFT City projects), cash flow projections are legally mandated by lenders before loan sanction. Banks will not release funds without a verified 25–30 year cash flow model showing debt-service capability. This is the same concept at industrial scale.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Corporate Tax Rate (India, AY 2024–25):** **25.17%** for domestic companies with turnover ≤ ₹400 crore (under Section 115BAA); **30%** for others (Section 115BAB optional rate is **22%** plus surcharge, effective rate ≈ **25.17%**).
- **Standard Project Horizon for Capital Budgeting:** **5 to 10 years**; infrastructure projects extend to **20–30 years**.
- **Working Capital:** Typically **15% to 25%** of annual sales for manufacturing firms; service firms may run **5% to 10%**.
- **Depreciation rates (Companies Act, 2013, Schedule II):** Buildings (non-residential) **10%**, Plant & Machinery **15%**, Computers & Software **40%**, Vehicles **15%**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Pattern):** When a numerical problem does not specify a tax rate, assume **30%**. When depreciation rate is not given, use the **Written Down Value (WDV) method** at the rates above. Always state both assumptions in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Three Pillars of Cash Flow

Cash flow projections are built on three hierarchical pillars. Every KTU numerical problem in this module will require you to identify and compute each one correctly.

### Pillar 1: Initial Cash Outflow (Year 0 Investment)
- Cost of new asset (purchase price + installation + freight + trial-run costs).
- Add: Increase in **Net Working Capital (NWC)** required to support operations.
- Subtract: Tax savings from sale of old asset (if replacement decision).
- This is a **negative cash flow** occurring at **t = 0**.

### Pillar 2: Operating Cash Flows (Years 1 to n)
- These are the **after-tax cash flows generated by the project's day-to-day operations**.
- The standard KTU formula is derived from the income statement:
  - Start with Sales, subtract Operating Costs (cash costs only), arrive at **EBITDA** (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation & Amortization).
  - Subtract Depreciation (non-cash) to get **EBIT** (Earnings Before Interest & Taxes).
  - Pay Taxes on EBIT to arrive at **Net Income**.
  - **Add back Depreciation** (since it was a non-cash deduction) to get the operating cash flow.

### Pillar 3: Terminal Cash Flow (Year n, end of project)
- **Salvage Value** of the asset at the end of its useful life.
- Tax effect on salvage: if sold above book value → taxable gain; if below → tax shield on loss.
- **Recovery of Net Working Capital** originally invested in Year 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind Each Step:** Operating cash flow must include depreciation's tax shield because depreciation is a *legal* deduction from taxable income even though no cash leaves the firm. The firm effectively "saves" tax = Depreciation × Tax Rate. This is called the **Depreciation Tax Shield** and is one of the most frequently tested KTU concepts.

## 2.2 The Critical Distinction: Accounting Profit vs. Cash Flow

| **Item** | **Accounting Profit (P&L)** | **Cash Flow (CF)** |
|---|---|---|
| Depreciation | Subtract as expense | **Add back** (non-cash) |
| Credit Sales | Recognized as revenue | **Excluded** until collected |
| Credit Purchases | Excluded as cost | **Excluded** until paid (but WC is tracked) |
| Provision for Doubtful Debts | Subtract | **Add back** (non-cash) |
| Capital Expenditure on New Machine | Capitalized (depreciated over years) | **Treated as full outflow in the year incurred** |

## 2.3 Principles Guiding Cash Flow Projection (KTU Module 1 Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates that students must apply the following four principles while constructing a cash flow projection:

1. **Principle of Incremental Cash Flows:** Only cash flows that *change* as a result of accepting the project should be included. Sunk costs, allocated overheads, and unaffected cash flows are excluded.
2. **Principle of After-Tax Basis:** All flows are measured on an after-tax basis to reflect the true wealth impact on shareholders.
3. **Principle of Timing Consistency:** Cash flows must be matched to the exact period they occur — typically end-of-year for KTU problems.
4. **Principle of Cash (Not Accrual):** The focus is the movement of *cash*, not recognition of revenue/expense.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Units / Boundary** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | After-Tax Operating Cash Flow (Tax-Shield Form) | $OCF = (S - C)(1 - t) + D \cdot t$ | S, C, D in ₹; t is decimal tax rate |
| 2 | After-Tax OCF (Net-Income Form) | $OCF = NI + D$ | NI = Net Income; D = Depreciation |
| 3 | Operating Cash Flow (EBIT Form) | $OCF = EBIT(1 - t) + D$ | EBIT = Earnings Before Interest & Tax |
| 4 | Free Cash Flow to Firm (FCFF) | $FCFF = EBIT(1-t) + D - CapEx - \Delta WC$ | All in ₹; $\Delta WC$ is change in WC |
| 5 | Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE) | $FCFE = NI + D - CapEx - \Delta WC + Net\ Borrowing$ | All in ₹; for equity-holders |
| 6 | Initial Investment | $I_0 = P_0 + Inst + \Delta WC_0$ | P₀ = Purchase price; Inst = Installation |
| 7 | Terminal Cash Flow | $TCF = SV(1-t) + Book\ Value \cdot t + \Delta WC_n$ | SV = Salvage Value |
| 8 | Depreciation Tax Shield | $DTS = D \cdot t$ | Annual cash equivalent benefit |
| 9 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | r = discount rate (WACC) |
| 10 | Net Cash Flow per Period | $CF_t = OCF_t - CapEx_t - \Delta WC_t$ | End-of-year t convention |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** The examiner will deliberately test whether you confuse $\Delta WC$ (change in working capital) with the *level* of WC. Always remember: an **increase** in current assets (or decrease in current liabilities) is a **cash OUTFLOW** (use of cash). A **decrease** in current assets is a cash INFLOW.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Corporate Fields

- **Capital Budgeting Decisions:** Accept/Reject decisions for new machinery, R&D, plant expansion.
- **Valuation Models:** DCF valuation of firms for M&A, IPO pricing, fairness opinions.
- **Loan Syndication:** Project finance models for roads, power, real estate (debt-service coverage ratio depends on projected cash flows).
- **Strategic Planning:** Rolling 5-year financial plans for corporate boards.
- **Bankruptcy Prediction:** Altman Z-score and other models use cash flow patterns to predict distress.
- **Engineering Economics:** Replacement analysis, lease-vs-buy, and make-vs-buy decisions all rely on incremental cash flow projections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the After-Tax Operating Cash Flow Formula

This is the **single most important derivation in Module 1** of PEIET744. Every KTU numerical problem in capital budgeting will use one of its forms. We will derive it from first principles using the income statement, step by step.

### Step 1: Start with the Operating Income Statement (Cash + Non-Cash)

We define the variables as follows for a single operating year of a project:

- $S$ = Sales Revenue (cash received, end of year, for simplicity)
- $C$ = Cash Operating Costs (variable + fixed, excluding depreciation)
- $D$ = Depreciation (a non-cash expense charged by tax law)
- $t$ = Corporate tax rate (decimal, e.g., 0.30 for 30%)

### Step 2: Compute Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT)

EBIT is the operating profit before considering the cost of debt (interest) and the government (tax). The full deduction of operating costs (both cash and non-cash) gives us:

$$EBIT = S - C - D$$

The reasoning: from total sales revenue, we first subtract the actual cash we paid out for operations (C), and then we also deduct the non-cash depreciation charge (D) to arrive at the taxable operating profit. This is what the income tax department will assess tax on.

### Step 3: Compute Tax Liability

The tax authority taxes EBIT at the corporate tax rate t. So the tax payable is:

$$Tax = EBIT \cdot t = (S - C - D) \cdot t$$

This is a real cash outflow — the firm must actually pay this amount to the government in cash.

### Step 4: Compute Net Income (Accounting Profit)

Net Income is what remains for shareholders after all operating costs, depreciation, and taxes:

$$NI = EBIT - Tax = (S - C - D) - (S - C - D) \cdot t$$

Factor out the common term (S − C − D):

$$NI = (S - C - D)(1 - t)$$

This is the standard accounting identity. The factor (1 − t) is called the **after-tax retention factor**.

### Step 5: Adjust for Non-Cash Items to Get Cash Flow

Here is the **critical conceptual leap**. Net Income, as computed above, is an *accrual* number — it deducted depreciation D, which never actually left the cash account. To convert accounting profit to actual cash flow, we add back the non-cash depreciation:

$$OCF = NI + D$$

Substitute the expression for NI from Step 4:

$$OCF = (S - C - D)(1 - t) + D$$

### Step 6: Expand and Simplify Algebraically

Expand the first term:

$$OCF = (S - C - D) - (S - C - D) \cdot t + D$$

$$OCF = S - C - D - (S - C - D) \cdot t + D$$

Observe that $-D$ and $+D$ **cancel each other out**:

$$OCF = S - C - (S - C) \cdot t + D \cdot t$$

Factor (S − C) from the first two surviving terms:

$$OCF = (S - C)(1 - t) + D \cdot t$$

This is the **canonical KTU tax-shield form** of the operating cash flow equation. The interpretation is elegant:

- $(S - C)(1 - t)$: After-tax operating profit *ignoring* depreciation.
- $D \cdot t$: The tax saving generated by the depreciation deduction (the **Depreciation Tax Shield**).

### Step 7: The Equivalent EBIT Form

Starting from the original EBIT identity, we can re-express OCF as:

$$OCF = EBIT(1 - t) + D$$

This follows directly from Step 4, since $NI = EBIT(1 - t)$, and $OCF = NI + D$. This form is most useful when the problem gives you EBIT directly.

### Step 8: Complete Derivation Summary

| **Step** | **Result** | **Economic Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $EBIT = S - C - D$ | Taxable operating profit |
| 2 | $Tax = EBIT \cdot t$ | Cash outflow to government |
| 3 | $NI = (S - C - D)(1 - t)$ | Accounting profit (accrual) |
| 4 | $OCF = NI + D$ | Add back non-cash D |
| 5 | $OCF = (S - C)(1 - t) + D \cdot t$ | Final tax-shield form |
| 6 | $OCF = EBIT(1 - t) + D$ | EBIT-based form |

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement (Module 1 Practice):**
A Kerala-based spice processing firm is evaluating a new turmeric drying machine. The relevant data are:

- Purchase price of machine: **₹20,00,000**
- Installation & freight: **₹2,00,000**
- Expected increase in annual sales: **₹18,00,000**
- Expected annual cash operating costs: **₹10,00,000**
- Useful life: **5 years**
- Depreciation method: **Straight Line** (assume zero salvage for simplicity)
- Corporate tax rate: **30%**
- Required increase in Working Capital at start: **₹3,00,000** (recovered at end)
- Salvage value at end of Year 5: **₹4,00,000**

**Required:** Construct the complete cash flow projection and compute NPV at 12% discount rate.

### Solution Step-by-Step

**Step A — Compute Initial Investment (Year 0):**

$$I_0 = Purchase + Installation + \Delta WC_0$$

$$I_0 = 20{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000$$

$$I_0 = 25{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR (Cash Outflow)}$$

**Step B — Compute Annual Depreciation (Straight Line):**

$$D = \frac{Cost - Salvage}{Life} = \frac{22{,}00{,}000 - 0}{5}$$

$$D = 4{,}40{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

Note: We depreciate only the **depreciable base** of ₹22,00,000 (excluding the working capital, which is a current asset, not a fixed asset). The terminal salvage we are using for tax purposes at the end of Year 5 will be the actual sale price (₹4,00,000), not zero.

**Step C — Compute Annual Operating Cash Flow (Years 1 to 5):**

Using the tax-shield form:

$$OCF = (S - C)(1 - t) + D \cdot t$$

$$OCF = (18{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000)(1 - 0.30) + 4{,}40{,}000 \cdot 0.30$$

$$OCF = 8{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.70 + 1{,}32{,}000$$

$$OCF = 5{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}32{,}000$$

$$OCF = 6{,}92{,}000 \text{ INR per year}$$

**Step D — Compute Terminal Cash Flow at End of Year 5:**

The terminal cash flow has two components:

(i) **Tax-adjusted Salvage Value:**

$$SV_{after-tax} = SV - Tax\ on\ Gain$$

The book value at end of Year 5 (with zero salvage assumed in SLM) is ₹0, so the entire ₹4,00,000 is a taxable gain:

$$Tax\ on\ Gain = 4{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.30 = 1{,}20{,}000$$

$$SV_{after-tax} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 2{,}80{,}000$$

(ii) **Recovery of Working Capital:**

$$WC\ Recovery = 3{,}00{,}000$$

**Total Terminal Cash Flow:**

$$TCF = 2{,}80{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}80{,}000$$

**Step E — Build the Cash Flow Timeline:**

| **Year** | **OCF (₹)** | **CapEx (₹)** | **ΔWC (₹)** | **Terminal (₹)** | **Net CF (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | −22,00,000 | −3,00,000 | 0 | **−25,00,000** |
| 1 | 6,92,000 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **+6,92,000** |
| 2 | 6,92,000 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **+6,92,000** |
| 3 | 6,92,000 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **+6,92,000** |
| 4 | 6,92,000 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **+6,92,000** |
| 5 | 6,92,000 | 0 | 0 | 5,80,000 | **+12,72,000** |

**Step F — Compute NPV at r = 12%:**

$$NPV = -25{,}00{,}000 + \sum_{t=1}^{4} \frac{6{,}92{,}000}{(1.12)^t} + \frac{12{,}72{,}000}{(1.12)^5}$$

First, compute the discount factors:

| **Year (t)** | **CF (₹)** | **DF @ 12%** | **PV (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6,92,000 | 0.8929 | 6,17,857 |
| 2 | 6,92,000 | 0.7972 | 5,51,658 |
| 3 | 6,92,000 | 0.7118 | 4,92,553 |
| 4 | 6,92,000 | 0.6355 | 4,39,779 |
| 5 | 12,72,000 | 0.5674 | 7,21,798 |
| **Total PV of Inflows** | | | **28,23,645** |
| Less: Initial Outflow | | | **25,00,000** |
| **NPV** | | | **+3,23,645** |

**Decision:** Since NPV > 0, **ACCEPT the project**. The spice firm should buy the turmeric drying machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation for Valuation:** Notice how the tax-shield term ($D \cdot t = \text{₹}1{,}32{,}000$) increased the annual cash flow by 23% over the pure operating profit. Ignoring depreciation's tax shield is one of the most common errors KTU examiners penalize.

## 3.3 Derivation: Free Cash Flow to Firm (FCFF) vs. Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE)

This distinction is important for portfolio management and is a high-yield KTU topic.

**Starting Point:** Operating Cash Flow = $EBIT(1 - t) + D$

**FCFF (Cash available to ALL capital providers — equity + debt):**

To get from OCF to FCFF, we must subtract the cash flows needed to *maintain and grow* the asset base:

- Subtract **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** — needed to replace depreciating assets and grow.
- Subtract **Change in Net Working Capital (ΔWC)** — needed to support sales growth.

$$FCFF = EBIT(1 - t) + D - CapEx - \Delta WC$$

**FCFE (Cash available to EQUITY holders only):**

Starting from Net Income (the equity holders' bottom line), we add back non-cash depreciation, subtract investment needs, and add net new borrowing (since debt is a source of cash for equity):

$$FCFE = NI + D - CapEx - \Delta WC + Net\ Borrowing$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** FCFF is what the **firm** gets (used for WACC discounting). FCFE is what the **equity shareholder** gets (used for Cost of Equity discounting). The bridge between them is interest, tax, and debt flow.

## 3.4 The Incremental Cash Flow Decision Tree

When KTU problems give a "replacement" or "expansion" scenario, use this decision tree:

```
                Project Cash Flow Projection
                          |
        +-----------------+-----------------+
        |                                    |
   Initial Year 0                    Operating Years 1 to n
        |                                    |
   +----+----+                          +-----+-----+
   |         |                          |           |
 Asset    ΔWC                        After-tax   CapEx
 Cost   Investment                   Operating  (mid-life
         (released                   Cash Flow   replace-
         at end)                                 ment)
                                                       |
                                                  ΔWC change
                                                  (each year)
                                                       |
                                                  Terminal
                                                  Year n:
                                                  Salvage +
                                                  WC Recovery
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Note:** Incremental cash flows mean we ignore costs that the firm will incur *anyway* (e.g., CEO salary of an existing firm), and we include opportunity costs (e.g., rent foregone on idle land). This is the **with-vs-without** principle.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Note

Per the V10 protocol, all Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters), and labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Diagram 1: The Cash Flow Projection Pipeline (Process Architecture)

This diagram maps the sequential engineering process of converting a business decision into a usable discounted cash flow model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Business Decision or Capital Request] --> A2[Identify Project Boundary and Time Horizon]
    A2 --> A3[Gather Operating Assumptions: Sales, Costs, Tax, Life]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Depreciation Schedule per Companies Act 2013]
    A4 --> A5[Compute Initial Investment: Asset Cost plus Working Capital]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Year 1 to n Operating Cash Flow using OCF Formula]
    A6 --> A7[Insert Mid Life Capital Expenditure if any]
    A7 --> A8[Track Year on Year Change in Working Capital]
    A8 --> A9[Compute Terminal Cash Flow: Salvage plus WC Recovery]
    A9 --> A10[Apply Tax Adjustment to Salvage Gain or Loss]
    A10 --> A11[Construct Cash Flow Timeline Table]
    A11 --> A12[Apply Discount Rate: WACC or Cost of Equity]
    A12 --> A13[Compute NPV and IRR for Decision]
    A13 --> A14[End: Accept or Reject Recommendation]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 2: The Three Cash Flow Categories (Functional Architecture Block Diagram)

This shows how every cash flow projection in a corporate finance context is decomposed into the three classical categories, mirroring the Cash Flow Statement format prescribed under Ind AS 7 / IFRS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Total Project Cash Flow Universe] --> B1[Operating Activities Section]
    B0 --> B2[Investing Activities Section]
    B0 --> B3[Financing Activities Section]

    B1 --> B1a[Cash Sales Receipts]
    B1 --> B1b[Cash Operating Costs Paid]
    B1 --> B1c[Tax Paid to Government]
    B1 --> B1d[Net Operating Cash Flow]

    B2 --> B2a[Purchase of Fixed Assets]
    B2 --> B2b[Sale of Old Assets]
    B2 --> B2c[Change in Working Capital]
    B2 --> B2d[Net Investing Cash Flow]

    B3 --> B3a[Equity Capital Raised]
    B3 --> B3b[Debt Borrowed]
    B3 --> B3c[Interest and Principal Repaid]
    B3 --> B3d[Dividends Paid]
    B3 --> B3e[Net Financing Cash Flow]

    B1d --> B4[Free Cash Flow to Firm FCFF]
    B2d --> B4
    B3e --> B5[Free Cash Flow to Equity FCFE]
    B1d --> B5
```

## 4.4 Diagram 3: Cash Flow vs. Accounting Profit Reconciliation (Educational Schematic)

This diagram is the most critical visualization for KTU students — it illustrates the conceptual bridge between the income statement and the cash flow statement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Starting Point: Sales Revenue S] --> C1[Subtract Cash Operating Costs C]
    C1 --> C2[Equals Cash Operating Profit before Tax]
    C2 --> C3[Add Depreciation Tax Shield equals D times t]
    C3 --> C4[Equals Operating Cash Flow OCF]
    C2 --> C5[Subtract Depreciation D non cash]
    C5 --> C6[Equals EBIT taxable operating profit]
    C6 --> C7[Subtract Tax at rate t]
    C7 --> C8[Equals Net Income NI accounting profit]
    C8 --> C9[Add back Depreciation D]
    C9 --> C4
    C4 --> C10[Final Result: Actual Cash Generated in Period]
    C8 --> C11[Parallel Result: Accounting Profit Recognized]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **left branch** (S → C → OCF) is the **cash basis** path. The **right branch** (S → C → D → NI → OCF) is the **accrual basis** path. Both must converge at the same OCF figure — this is the fundamental accounting identity. KTU problems often test whether students can navigate between these two representations correctly.

## 4.5 Diagram 4: Capital Budgeting Decision Tree (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Project Proposal Received] --> D1{Identify Initial Investment I0}
    D1 --> D2[Compute Annual Depreciation Schedule]
    D2 --> D3[Compute Year 1 to n OCF for each year]
    D3 --> D4[Compute Terminal Cash Flow at end of Year n]
    D4 --> D5[Construct Full Cash Flow Timeline]
    D5 --> D6{Select Discount Rate: WACC or Cost of Equity}
    D6 --> D7[Apply Present Value Discounting]
    D7 --> D8[Compute NPV and IRR]
    D8 --> D9{NPV greater than Zero?}
    D9 -->|Yes| D10[Accept Project: Adds Shareholder Value]
    D9 -->|No| D11[Reject Project: Destroys Shareholder Value]
    D10 --> D12[Proceed to Sensitivity and Scenario Analysis]
    D11 --> D12
    D12 --> D13[End of Capital Budgeting Evaluation]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (Conceptual) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Distinguish between accounting profit and cash flow. Why is the cash flow concept preferred for capital budgeting decisions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Accounting profit is calculated on the accrual basis, recognizing revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, while cash flow records the actual movement of cash into and out of the firm.

**Key Differences (1 mark):** Depreciation is a non-cash expense that reduces accounting profit but is added back in cash flow calculations. Similarly, credit sales increase accounting profit immediately but are not cash inflows until collected.

**Why Preferred (1 mark):** Capital budgeting aims to measure the actual increase in shareholder wealth, which is a function of cash generated, not paper profit. Discounted cash flow techniques (NPV, IRR) require accurate, time-matched cash flow data to be valid. Hence, cash flow is the correct basis for investment decisions.

---

### Question A2 (Definition) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define "Depreciation Tax Shield" and state its formula. Why is it relevant in cash flow projection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Depreciation Tax Shield (DTS) is the tax saving a firm obtains by claiming depreciation as a deductible expense on its taxable income, even though depreciation is a non-cash charge.

**Formula (1 mark):** $DTS = D \times t$, where D is the annual depreciation amount and t is the applicable corporate tax rate.

**Relevance (1 mark):** In a cash flow projection, the depreciation tax shield is *added back* to operating cash flow because, although it reduces taxable income, no cash actually leaves the firm. Including it ensures the cash flow reflects the firm's true cash-generating capacity and the tax benefit of asset ownership.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**A firm is considering the purchase of a new machine for ₹15,00,000 with installation charges of ₹1,00,000. The machine has a useful life of 5 years and zero salvage value. Straight-line depreciation is applicable. The machine is expected to generate additional sales of ₹12,00,000 per annum, with cash operating costs of ₹6,00,000 per annum. The corporate tax rate is 30%. An additional working capital of ₹2,00,000 is required at the beginning, which will be fully recovered at the end of the project.**

**Required:**
**(a) Compute the annual operating cash flow for the project. (7 marks)**
**(b) Compute the Net Present Value (NPV) of the project at a discount rate of 10% and advise whether the project should be accepted. (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — Annual Operating Cash Flow [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compute Depreciable Base and Annual Depreciation (2 marks)**

Depreciable base = Purchase + Installation = ₹15,00,000 + ₹1,00,000 = **₹16,00,000**

Salvage value = ₹0 (given)

$$D = \frac{16{,}00{,}000 - 0}{5} = 3{,}20{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2: Identify Inputs for the OCF Formula (1 mark)**

S = ₹12,00,000 ; C = ₹6,00,000 ; D = ₹3,20,000 ; t = 0.30

**Step 3: Apply the Tax-Shield Formula (3 marks)**

$$OCF = (S - C)(1 - t) + D \cdot t$$

$$OCF = (12{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000)(1 - 0.30) + 3{,}20{,}000 \cdot 0.30$$

$$OCF = 6{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.70 + 96{,}000$$

$$OCF = 4{,}20{,}000 + 96{,}000$$

$$\boxed{OCF = 5{,}16{,}000 \text{ per year}}$$

**Step 4: Verification Using the EBIT Form (1 mark)**

EBIT = S − C − D = 12,00,000 − 6,00,000 − 3,20,000 = 2,80,000

OCF = EBIT(1 − t) + D = 2,80,000 × 0.70 + 3,20,000 = 1,96,000 + 3,20,000 = 5,16,000 ✓

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — NPV Calculation [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compute Initial Cash Outflow at Year 0 (1 mark)**

$$I_0 = \text{Asset Cost} + \Delta WC = 16{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Identify Terminal Cash Flow at End of Year 5 (1 mark)**

Since salvage value is zero, no tax adjustment is needed. Only working capital recovery:

$$TCF = 2{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: Build the Cash Flow Timeline (1 mark)**

| **Year** | **CF (₹)** |
|---|---|
| 0 | −18,00,000 |
| 1 to 4 | +5,16,000 each |
| 5 | +5,16,000 + 2,00,000 = +7,16,000 |

**Step 4: Compute Present Values at 10% (3 marks)**

Discount factors:
- Year 1: 0.9091
- Year 2: 0.8264
- Year 3: 0.7513
- Year 4: 0.6830
- Year 5: 0.6209

PV calculations:
- Year 1: 5,16,000 × 0.9091 = 4,69,096
- Year 2: 5,16,000 × 0.8264 = 4,26,422
- Year 3: 5,16,000 × 0.7513 = 3,87,671
- Year 4: 5,16,000 × 0.6830 = 3,52,428
- Year 5: 7,16,000 × 0.6209 = 4,44,564

**Total PV of inflows = ₹20,80,181**

**Step 5: Compute NPV and Conclude (1 mark)**

$$NPV = 20{,}80{,}181 - 18{,}00{,}000 = +2{,}80{,}181$$

**Decision:** Since NPV > 0, the project should be **ACCEPTED**. It adds approximately ₹2,80,181 in present value to shareholder wealth.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects, A and B, with the following cash flow profiles. The cost of capital is 12% and the corporate tax rate is 30%. Both projects require an initial working capital of ₹1,00,000, which is fully recovered at the end. Straight-line depreciation is used, and the salvage value of each asset is ₹50,000 at the end of its life.**

| **Particulars (₹)** | **Project A** | **Project B** |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment | 10,00,000 | 12,00,000 |
| Life (years) | 4 | 4 |
| Annual Sales | 8,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| Annual Cash Costs | 4,00,000 | 5,50,000 |

**Required:**
**(a) Construct the complete cash flow projection table for both projects. (7 marks)**
**(b) Compute the NPV of each project at 12% and state which project should be selected. (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — Cash Flow Projection Table [7 marks]

**Project A: (2.5 marks)**

Depreciation per year = (10,00,000 − 50,000) / 4 = 2,37,500

$$OCF_A = (8{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000)(0.70) + 2{,}37{,}500 \cdot 0.30$$

$$OCF_A = 2{,}80{,}000 + 71{,}250 = 3{,}51{,}250$$

Terminal Cash Flow A:
- Salvage tax: Gain on sale = 50,000 − Book value (0) = 50,000; Tax = 50,000 × 0.30 = 15,000; After-tax salvage = 35,000
- WC recovery = 1,00,000
- Total TCF_A = 35,000 + 1,00,000 = 1,35,000

**Project B: (2.5 marks)**

Depreciation per year = (12,00,000 − 50,000) / 4 = 2,37,500

$$OCF_B = (10{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}50{,}000)(0.70) + 2{,}37{,}500 \cdot 0.30$$

$$OCF_B = 3{,}15{,}000 + 71{,}250 = 3{,}86{,}250$$

Terminal Cash Flow B:
- Same logic: After-tax salvage = 35,000; WC recovery = 1,00,000
- Total TCF_B = 1,35,000

**Consolidated Cash Flow Table: (2 marks)**

| **Year** | **CF Project A (₹)** | **CF Project B (₹)** |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | −11,00,000 | −13,00,000 |
| 1 | +3,51,250 | +3,86,250 |
| 2 | +3,51,250 | +3,86,250 |
| 3 | +3,51,250 | +3,86,250 |
| 4 | +4,86,250 (3,51,250 + 1,35,000) | +5,21,250 (3,86,250 + 1,35,000) |

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — NPV Comparison and Selection [7 marks]

**Discount factors at 12%:** Y1 = 0.8929, Y2 = 0.7972, Y3 = 0.7118, Y4 = 0.6355 (2 marks)

**PV Calculation Project A (2 marks):**
- Y1: 3,51,250 × 0.8929 = 3,13,581
- Y2: 3,51,250 × 0.7972 = 2,80,000
- Y3: 3,51,250 × 0.7118 = 2,50,000
- Y4: 4,86,250 × 0.6355 = 3,09,012
- Total PV inflows A = 11,52,593

NPV_A = 11,52,593 − 11,00,000 = **₹52,593**

**PV Calculation Project B (2 marks):**
- Y1: 3,86,250 × 0.8929 = 3,44,832
- Y2: 3,86,250 × 0.7972 = 3,07,886
- Y3: 3,86,250 × 0.7118 = 2,74,898
- Y4: 5,21,250 × 0.6355 = 3,31,239
- Total PV inflows B = 12,58,855

NPV_B = 12,58,855 − 13,00,000 = **−₹41,145**

**Decision (1 mark):** Project A has positive NPV (₹52,593) and should be selected. Project B has negative NPV (₹41,145) and should be rejected, despite higher absolute cash flows, because it does not recover its higher initial investment at the 12% discount rate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Treating depreciation as a cash outflow in the year of asset purchase. Depreciation is non-cash; only the initial asset cost is the cash outflow. (*Lose 2 marks*)
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to add back working capital recovery to the final year's cash flow. (*Lose 1 mark*)
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to apply tax on the gain on sale of the asset at the end of life. The book value at end of SLM (with zero salvage assumption) is zero, so the entire salvage is taxable. (*Lose 2 marks*)
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Using the wrong depreciation base. Installation and freight are part of the asset cost and must be depreciated. Working capital is NOT depreciated. (*Lose 1 mark*)
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Conflating the depreciation tax shield term with the depreciation itself. The tax shield is $D \times t$, not D. (*Lose 1 mark*)
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** Not clearly labeling the timeline (Year 0, Year 1, ..., Year n). The KTU evaluator needs to see the convention. (*Lose 0.5 mark*)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Cash flow ≠ Accounting profit.** Depreciation, provisions, and credit accruals are non-cash and must be adjusted.
- [x] **Master the dual form of OCF:** $(S-C)(1-t) + D \cdot t$ and $EBIT(1-t) + D$ give identical results — pick the one matching the data given.
- [x] **The Depreciation Tax Shield** ($D \cdot t$) is the tax saved by claiming depreciation; it is real cash equivalent and must appear in OCF.
- [x] **Initial Investment** includes the full asset cost (purchase + installation + freight + trial run) AND the initial working capital injection.
- [x] **Terminal Cash Flow** = After-tax Salvage + Recovery of Working Capital.
- [x] **Tax on Salvage Gain** = (Salvage − Book Value at end) × Tax Rate. If salvage < book value, the loss creates a tax shield (after-tax salvage > salvage).
- [x] **Use Straight Line Depreciation** unless the problem specifies WDV. Always show the depreciation schedule.
- [x] **Default Tax Rate** is **30%** if not stated; default discount rate is given in the problem; default life is 5 years.
- [x] **Working Capital is recovered FULLY at the end of the project** unless stated otherwise. It is a *temporary* use of cash.
- [x] **Increase in Working Capital = Cash Outflow**; Decrease = Cash Inflow.
- [x] **Only incremental cash flows matter** — ignore sunk costs, include opportunity costs.
- [x] **Always construct a timeline table** before discounting — the KTU examiner awards step marks for a clear table.
- [x] **NPV > 0 → Accept; NPV < 0 → Reject.** This decision rule is inviolate under the 2024 Scheme.
- [x] **For mutually exclusive projects**, select the one with the highest positive NPV (not necessarily the highest absolute cash flow).
- [x] **FCFF vs FCFE:** FCFF is for the whole firm (discount at WACC); FCFE is for shareholders only (discount at cost of equity).
- [x] **Time convention:** KTU default is end-of-year cash flows; Year 0 is the present (today).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Project Analysis and Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Analysis and Evaluation** is the systematic, multi-disciplinary process of examining a proposed capital investment project to determine its viability, profitability, risk profile, and strategic alignment with the organization's objectives before a commitment of significant financial resources is made.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PEIET744 – Corporate Finance and Portfolio Management*, Project Analysis and Evaluation forms the bedrock of capital budgeting decisions. It is the bridge between **project identification** and **project sanction/implementation**. The evaluation framework integrates **financial appraisal** (quantifying returns), **technical appraisal** (engineering feasibility), **market appraisal** (demand assessment), **managerial appraisal** (organizational capability), and **social cost-benefit analysis** (welfare impact).

The principal objective is to answer four cardinal questions:

1. **Is the project technically feasible?**
2. **Is the project commercially viable?**
3. **Is the project financially attractive?**
4. **Is the project socially desirable?**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Buying a House**
>
> Imagine you have ₹50 lakhs saved up and you are considering buying a house for ₹1 crore. Before signing the dotted line, you will:
>
> - **Inspect the structure** (is the foundation strong? is the wiring safe?) — this is **Technical Analysis**.
> - **Survey the neighborhood** (are there schools, hospitals, transport nearby? what are future development plans?) — this is **Market & Demand Analysis**.
> - **Compute the EMIs, tax benefits, and resale value** — this is **Financial Analysis**.
> - **Check your legal rights, title deeds, and approvals** — this is **Legal/Managerial Analysis**.
> - **Consider the broader impact** (will it displace tenants? is it in a flood zone?) — this is **Social Cost-Benefit Analysis**.
>
> *Project Analysis and Evaluation is exactly this — but applied to multi-crore industrial or infrastructure projects, where the cost of a wrong decision is orders of magnitude higher.*

---

## 1.3 Types of Projects in Corporate Finance

| Project Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Expansion Project** | Increasing capacity of an existing product line | Adding a new assembly line at a Maruti plant |
| **Diversification Project** | Entering an entirely new product/market | Tata entering the electronics business |
| **Replacement Project** | Substituting obsolete assets | Replacing CNC machines with robotic arms |
| **Modernization Project** | Upgrading technology without capacity change | ERP system migration |
| **R&D / Strategic Project** | Long-gestation innovation bets | Tata's investment in Nano-electric platform |
| **Social / Welfare Project** | Public-good-oriented projects (Govt. PSUs) | NHAI highway, metro rail, rural electrification |

---

## 1.4 The Five Pillars of Project Feasibility Study (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UNESCO/UNIDO Standard Framework — also the de facto KTU evaluation checklist:**

1. **Market & Demand Analysis (M)** — *Will it sell?*
2. **Technical Analysis (T)** — *Can we build it?*
3. **Financial Analysis (F)** — *Is it profitable?*
4. **Economic / Social Cost-Benefit Analysis (E)** — *Is it welfare-enhancing?*
5. **Managerial / Organizational Analysis (O)** — *Can we manage it?*

These are often remembered through the mnemonic **"M-T-F-E-O"** or the **"Five-Finger Test"** of project feasibility.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used (KTU Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> - **NPV (Net Present Value)** — measured in **₹ / $** monetary units
> - **IRR (Internal Rate of Return)** — measured in **%** (compounded annual rate)
> - **Payback Period** — measured in **years**
> - **Profitability Index (PI)** — dimensionless ratio
> - **B-C Ratio (Benefit-Cost Ratio)** — dimensionless ratio
> - **ARR (Average Rate of Return)** — measured in **%**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Project Life Cycle & Evaluation Phases

A capital project is not a one-time event — it progresses through **three distinct phases**, each requiring a different analytical lens:

### Phase 1: Pre-Investment Phase (Planning & Analysis)
- **Identification of investment opportunities**
- **Feasibility study** (M-T-F-E-O framework)
- **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** preparation
- **Project appraisal** by financial institutions / board

### Phase 2: Investment Phase (Implementation)
- Sanction & disbursement of funds
- Procurement, construction, commissioning
- Trial runs and stabilization

### Phase 3: Operational Phase (Post-Implementation)
- Commercial production begins
- Cash inflows realized
- **Post-completion audit / Performance review**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Questions in Module-1 of PEIET744 typically focus on **Phase 1** (the analytical phase). Be prepared to discuss both **qualitative** (non-numeric) and **quantitative** (numeric) evaluation techniques.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Feasibility Study — Structured Logic

### A. Market & Demand Analysis

**Why?** — A technically perfect, financially profitable project is worthless if nobody buys the output. Demand analysis is the **gatekeeper** of project viability.

**Key Steps:**
1. **Define the product** and its end-use market
2. **Estimate the total market size** — using top-down or bottom-up approach
3. **Forecast demand** over the project's economic life using:
   - Time-series methods (trend, cyclical)
   - Regression analysis (income elasticity, price elasticity)
   - End-use method
   - Leading indicator method
4. **Conduct demand-supply gap analysis** — to identify the "demand pocket"
5. **Pricing strategy and elasticity** — price sensitivity assessment
6. **Marketing mix (4Ps)** — Product, Price, Place, Promotion

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Metric:** *Income Elasticity of Demand* — if E > 1, the good is a **luxury**; if E < 1, a **necessity**; if E < 0, a **Giffen/Veblen good**.

### B. Technical Analysis

**Why?** — Even the best demand forecast fails if the firm lacks the engineering capability to deliver the product at the right cost and quality.

**Key Components:**
1. **Location & site selection** — proximity to raw materials, market, labour, power, transport
2. **Technology selection** — indigenous vs. imported; level of automation
3. **Plant capacity** — optimum size (using *economies of scale* curves)
4. **Input requirements** — raw materials, utilities (water, power, steam)
5. **Manpower planning** — skilled, semi-skilled, managerial
6. **Pollution control & environmental clearance**
7. **Project schedule** — PERT/CPM critical path

### C. Financial Analysis

**Why?** — This is the **money view** of the project. Even the most socially beneficial, technically sound project is rejected by shareholders if it does not generate adequate returns.

**Inputs Required:**
- Capital cost (fixed + working capital)
- Revenue projections
- Operating cost structure
- Depreciation schedule
- Tax computation
- Financing pattern (debt-equity ratio)
- Discount rate (WACC or hurdle rate)

**Outputs (Decision Metrics):**
- **NPV, IRR, PI, B-C Ratio** — discounted cash flow methods
- **Payback Period, ARR** — non-discounted methods

### D. Economic / Social Cost-Benefit Analysis (SCBA)

**Why?** — From the *nation's* perspective, certain projects (roads, railways, irrigation) deserve investment even if their private NPV is negative. The **social discount rate** (typically lower than commercial rates, e.g., 8–10% in India) is used.

**Key Adjustments:**
- **Shadow prices** instead of market prices
- **Shadow wage rate** for unskilled labour
- **Conversion factors** for tradable vs. non-tradable goods
- **Externalities** — positive (employment generation) and negative (pollution)

### E. Managerial / Organizational Analysis

**Why?** — A project may look great on paper but the **people-side** can derail it. Evaluators check:
- Promoter's track record
- Management depth and experience
- Organizational structure
- HR policies and labour relations

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Formula | Decision Rule | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}} - I_{0}$ | Accept if $NPV \geq 0$ | **₹ / $** |
| 2 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $NPV = 0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+IRR)^{t}}$ | Accept if $IRR \geq \text{hurdle rate}$ | **%** |
| 3 | **Profitability Index (PI)** | $PI = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}}{I_{0}}$ | Accept if $PI \geq 1$ | **Ratio** |
| 4 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (B-C)** | $B/C = \frac{\sum \frac{B_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}}{\sum \frac{C_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}}$ | Accept if $B/C \geq 1$ | **Ratio** |
| 5 | **Payback Period (PBP)** | $PBP = a + \frac{b}{c}$ | Accept if PBP $\leq$ target | **Years** |
| 6 | **Discounted Payback** | Same as PBP but uses discounted $CF_{t}$ | Accept if DPB $\leq$ target | **Years** |
| 7 | **Average Rate of Return (ARR)** | $ARR = \frac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100$ | Accept if ARR $\geq$ target | **%** |
| 8 | **Modified IRR (MIRR)** | $MIRR = \left(\frac{FV_{pos}}{PV_{neg}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$ | Accept if MIRR $\geq r$ | **%** |
| 9 | **WACC (Discount Rate)** | $WACC = w_{e}k_{e} + w_{d}k_{d}(1-T)$ | Used as $r$ in NPV/PI | **%** |
| 10 | **EAA (Equivalent Annual Annuity)** | $EAA = \frac{NPV \times r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}$ | Used for unequal-life comparison | **₹ / year** |

Where:
- $CF_{t}$ = Net Cash Flow in year $t$
- $I_{0}$ = Initial Investment
- $r$ = Discount rate (cost of capital / WACC)
- $a$ = years before full recovery, $b$ = unrecovered amount, $c$ = cash flow in the recovery year
- $w_{e}, w_{d}$ = weights of equity and debt; $k_{e}, k_{d}$ = respective costs; $T$ = tax rate

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Safety Protocol:** All absolute-value or modulus expressions in the table above use the symbols `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown pipe syntax. **Do not** write `|x|` directly inside table cells.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

In actual engineering and management practice, project analysis and evaluation is used by:

- **Banks and Financial Institutions** (SBI, IDBI, IFCI) — to decide loan sanctions
- **Venture Capital / Private Equity firms** — to evaluate startup pitches
- **Public Sector Undertakings** (NTPC, BHEL, SAIL) — for capex approvals
- **Government bodies** (NITI Aayog, Planning Commission) — for Five-Year Plan projects
- **Multilateral institutions** (World Bank, ADB, IMF) — for development project financing

> [!NOTE]
> In India, **ONGC's Mumbai High redevelopment**, **Dedicated Freight Corridors**, and **Delhi Metro Phase-4** are all real-world projects that went through the full M-T-F-E-O evaluation cycle, with DPRs running into thousands of pages.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Net Present Value (NPV) Formula

### Starting from First Principles

Consider an investment of $I_{0}$ made at time $t = 0$, generating cash flows $CF_{1}, CF_{2}, \ldots, CF_{n}$ at the end of years 1 through $n$. The opportunity cost of capital is $r$ (a positive, time-invariant rate).

The **Present Value (PV)** of each future cash flow is obtained by *discounting* it back to the present, since a rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow (the *time value of money* principle).

$$
PV_{t} = \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}
$$

**Logic:** A sum $CF_{t}$ received $t$ years from now, if invested at rate $r$, would have grown to $CF_{t}$ at time $t$. Working backwards, the equivalent *today's* value is $CF_{t}$ divided by the compounding factor $(1+r)^{t}$.

The **Net Present Value** is the algebraic sum of all present values of inflows minus the initial outflow:

$$
NPV = -I_{0} + \frac{CF_{1}}{(1+r)^{1}} + \frac{CF_{2}}{(1+r)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{CF_{n}}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

In summation notation:

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}
$$

where $CF_{0} = -I_{0}$ (the initial investment, conventionally a negative cash flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** NPV represents the *wealth-maximization* measure. A positive NPV means the project creates value *above* the cost of capital; a negative NPV destroys value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of IRR from NPV

The IRR is defined as the discount rate $r^{*}$ at which the NPV equals zero:

$$
0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+r^{*})^{t}}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- We want a rate $r^{*}$ such that the present value of inflows exactly equals the present value of outflows.
- This is a polynomial equation of degree $n$ in $(1+r^{*})$.
- For $n \geq 2$, a closed-form analytical solution is generally **not available**.
- Therefore, IRR is solved numerically using:
  - **Trial-and-error (Interpolation method)**
  - **Newton-Raphson method**
  - **Financial calculator / Excel's `IRR()` function**

**Interpolation Method Formula:**

$$
IRR \approx r_{L} + \frac{NPV_{L}}{NPV_{L} - NPV_{H}} \times (r_{H} - r_{L})
$$

where:
- $r_{L}$ = lower rate (giving positive NPV)
- $r_{H}$ = higher rate (giving negative NPV)
- $NPV_{L}, NPV_{H}$ = NPVs at the lower and higher rates, respectively

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem (14-Mark, Part-B sub-question pattern)**

**Problem:** A firm is evaluating a project with the following data:
- Initial Investment: ₹5,00,000
- Annual Cash Inflow (Years 1–5): ₹1,50,000 each year
- Salvage Value at end of Year 5: ₹50,000
- Cost of Capital: 10%

**Required:** Compute NPV, PI, and the Payback Period.

### Step 1: Present Value of Cash Inflows (Annuity for 5 years)

We use the **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)** formula:

$$
PVAF(r, n) = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

Substituting $r = 0.10$ and $n = 5$:

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.6209}{0.10} = \frac{0.3791}{0.10} = 3.7908
$$

**PV of annuity of ₹1,50,000 for 5 years:**

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.7908 = 5{,}68{,}620
$$

### Step 2: Present Value of Salvage Value (Year 5)

$$
PV_{\text{salvage}} = \frac{50{,}000}{(1.10)^{5}} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.6105} = 31{,}046
$$

### Step 3: Total Present Value of Inflows

$$
PV_{\text{total}} = 5{,}68{,}620 + 31{,}046 = 5{,}99{,}666
$$

### Step 4: NPV Calculation

$$
NPV = PV_{\text{total}} - I_{0}
$$

$$
NPV = 5{,}99{,}666 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 99{,}666
$$

**Decision:** Since $NPV = 99{,}666 > 0$, the project is **ACCEPTED**.

### Step 5: Profitability Index (PI)

$$
PI = \frac{PV_{\text{total}}}{I_{0}} = \frac{5{,}99{,}666}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.1993
$$

**Decision:** Since $PI = 1.20 \geq 1$, the project is **ACCEPTED**.

### Step 6: Payback Period (PBP)

Cumulative cash flows:
- End of Year 1: ₹1,50,000
- End of Year 2: ₹3,00,000
- End of Year 3: ₹4,50,000
- End of Year 4: ₹6,00,000 (₹50,000 excess)

The full ₹5,00,000 is recovered between Year 3 and Year 4. Using the precise formula:

$$
PBP = a + \frac{b}{c}
$$

where $a = 3$ years, $b = 50{,}000$ (unrecovered balance at end of Year 3), $c = 1{,}50{,}000$ (Year 4 cash flow).

$$
PBP = 3 + \frac{50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} = 3 + 0.333 = 3.33 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Answer-Writing Tip:** Always present the *Decision Rule* explicitly, then the *Computation*, then the *Inference*. Examiners award step-marks for showing all transitional steps.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strengths & Weaknesses of Each Method

| Method | Strengths | Weaknesses | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NPV** | Considers time value of money; aligns with wealth-maximization goal | Requires accurate discount rate; hard to compute for uneven cash flows | **Default choice** for all corporate projects |
| **IRR** | Expressed as %; intuitive; language of return | Multiple IRR problem (non-conventional flows); mutually exclusive ranking conflict | Standalone project acceptance |
| **PI** | Useful under capital rationing | May conflict with NPV in mutually exclusive projects | **Best for ranking** under capital constraints |
| **PBP** | Simple; measures liquidity/risk | Ignores time value; ignores post-payback cash flows | Supplementary, for risky/liquidity-sensitive projects |
| **ARR** | Easy to compute from accounting data | Ignores time value; based on profits, not cash flows | Historical/replacement decisions only |
| **B-C Ratio** | Direct welfare measure for SCBA | Difficult to define benefits/costs for social projects | Public sector / Govt. projects |

---

## 3.5 Decision Hierarchy for KTU Problems

When KTU questions give a single project to evaluate, follow this decision hierarchy:

1. **Calculate NPV** → primary decision criterion
2. **Cross-verify with IRR** → consistency check
3. **Compute PI** → relevant only under capital rationing
4. **Compute PBP / ARR** → supplementary liquidity check
5. **Apply qualitative filters** (M-T-F-E-O viability) → final go/no-go

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** For *mutually exclusive* projects with different scales or lives, NPV and IRR may **disagree**. Always re-rank using **NPV (primary)** or **Equivalent Annual Annuity (EAA)** when scale/life differ. Do not blindly follow IRR.
