# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart: The Project Evaluation Process

The following block-level architecture flow captures the entire M-T-F-E-O evaluation framework as a sequential decision pipeline, with feedback loops for revisions and iterations:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Identification Opportunity Screening] --> B[Phase 1 Pre-Investment Feasibility Study]
    B --> C1[Market and Demand Analysis M]
    B --> C2[Technical Analysis T]
    B --> C3[Financial Analysis F]
    B --> C4[Economic Social Cost Benefit Analysis E]
    B --> C5[Managerial Organizational Analysis O]
    C1 --> D[Integration of Five Pillars M-T-F-E-O]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E{Qualitative Go No-Go Decision}
    E -- Revise or Reject --> F[Project Rejected Iteration Loop]
    F -.-> B
    E -- Proceed --> G[Quantitative Appraising Techniques]
    G --> G1[NPV Computation]
    G --> G2[IRR Computation]
    G --> G3[PI Computation]
    G --> G4[Payback Period Computation]
    G --> G5[ARR Computation]
    G1 --> H{Acceptance Rule Satisfied}
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H -- All Rules Met --> I[Phase 2 Investment Implementation]
    H -- Failed --> F
    I --> J[Phase 3 Operational Cash Inflows Realized]
    J --> K[Post Completion Audit Performance Review]
    K --> L[Feedback to Future Project Identification]
    L -.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The graph flows from top (Project Identification) to bottom (Post-Completion Audit), with a feedback loop (dashed lines) returning lessons learned to the next project cycle. This is the standard **closed-loop project evaluation architecture** prescribed by UNIDO and adopted in KTU Module-1.

---

The following diagram shows how the discounted cash flow (DCF) methods relate to each other and to the non-DCF methods:

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Project Analysis. Mention any two types of capital projects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Project Analysis is the systematic evaluation of a proposed capital investment project to determine its **viability, profitability, and risk** before committing financial resources. It involves studying market demand, technical feasibility, financial returns, economic impact, and managerial capability.

**Two types of capital projects:**
1. **Expansion Project** — Increasing the production capacity of an existing product line (e.g., adding a second shift in a manufacturing plant).
2. **Diversification Project** — Venturing into a new product line or new market (e.g., an automobile company entering the EV segment).

*(Any two types — Replacement, Modernization, R&D, Strategic, Social/Welfare — also accepted.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between NPV and IRR as methods of project evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | NPV | IRR |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Present value of net cash inflows minus initial investment | Discount rate at which NPV equals zero |
| **Unit** | Monetary units (₹) | Percentage (%) |
| **Decision Rule** | Accept if NPV $\geq 0$ | Accept if IRR $\geq$ cost of capital |
| **Reinvestment Assumption** | Cash flows reinvested at cost of capital | Cash flows reinvested at IRR (often unrealistic) |
| **Mutually Exclusive Projects** | Gives correct ranking (primary tool) | May give conflicting ranking (use NPV) |
| **Multiple Rates Problem** | Not affected | Multiple IRRs possible for non-conventional cash flows |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:** Students often write *only* the formulas in 3-mark comparison questions and lose marks. The expected answer is a **point-by-point contrast** in 4–5 distinct dimensions. **Always include a tabular or list-based comparison** to score full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part-B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**A company is evaluating a project with the following cash flows. The cost of capital is 12% p.a. Initial Investment = ₹10,00,000. Annual Cash Inflows: Year 1 = ₹3,00,000; Year 2 = ₹3,50,000; Year 3 = ₹4,00,000; Year 4 = ₹3,00,000; Year 5 = ₹2,00,000 + Salvage Value ₹50,000.**

**(a)** Compute the **NPV** of the project and state whether it should be accepted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Payback Period** and **Profitability Index (PI)**. Comment on the decision. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Discounting factors at 12%**

$$
\text{DF}_{t} = \frac{1}{(1.12)^{t}}
$$

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) | Discount Factor (12%) | Present Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | -10,00,000 | 1.0000 | -10,00,000 |
| 1 | 3,00,000 | 0.8929 | 2,67,857 |
| 2 | 3,50,000 | 0.7972 | 2,79,018 |
| 3 | 4,00,000 | 0.7118 | 2,84,718 |
| 4 | 3,00,000 | 0.6355 | 1,90,651 |
| 5 | 2,50,000 (2,00,000 + 50,000) | 0.5674 | 1,41,857 |

**Step 2: NPV calculation**

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{5} PV_{t}
$$

$$
NPV = -10{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}67{,}857 + 2{,}79{,}018 + 2{,}84{,}718 + 1{,}90{,}651 + 1{,}41{,}857
$$

$$
NPV = -10{,}00{,}000 + 11{,}64{,}101
$$

$$
NPV = 1{,}64{,}101
$$

**Inference:** Since **NPV = ₹1,64,101 > 0**, the project is **financially viable** and should be **ACCEPTED** at a 12% cost of capital.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Cumulative Cash Flows (Undiscounted)**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) | Cumulative (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3,00,000 | 3,00,000 |
| 2 | 3,50,000 | 6,50,000 |
| 3 | 4,00,000 | 10,50,000 |

The ₹10,00,000 investment is fully recovered during Year 3.

**Step 2: Payback Period (Precise)**

$$
PBP = a + \frac{b}{c}
$$

$$
PBP = 2 + \frac{10{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}50{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 2 + \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
PBP = 2 + 0.875 = 2.875 \text{ years} \approx 2 \text{ years } 10.5 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 3: Profitability Index (PI)**

$$
PI = \frac{\text{PV of all cash inflows}}{\text{Initial Investment}}
$$

$$
PI = \frac{2{,}67{,}857 + 2{,}79{,}018 + 2{,}84{,}718 + 1{,}90{,}651 + 1{,}41{,}857}{10{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
PI = \frac{11{,}64{,}101}{10{,}00{,}000} = 1.164
$$

**Inference:**
- **PI = 1.164 > 1** → Project generates ₹1.16 of present value for every ₹1 invested → **Accept**.
- **PBP = 2.875 years** → Capital recovered in less than 3 years; this is a relatively quick payback, indicating lower liquidity risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to add the salvage value (₹50,000) to Year-5 cash flow. **Always read the question carefully** — "₹2,00,000 + Salvage ₹50,000" means total Year-5 inflow is ₹2,50,000.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Reporting PI as a percentage (e.g., 116.4%). PI is a **dimensionless ratio**, not a percentage.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Computing payback from cumulative *discounted* cash flows when the question asked for *undiscounted* payback. Read the question stem carefully.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **five pillars of project feasibility study** (M-T-F-E-O framework) in detail. Discuss the role of **Market & Demand Analysis** with a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Financial Analysis** and **Economic (Social Cost-Benefit) Analysis**. Why is the latter more relevant for public sector projects? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The **Five Pillars of Project Feasibility Study** (M-T-F-E-O framework) were standardized by **UNIDO** and are universally adopted for project appraisal:

1. **Market & Demand Analysis (M):** Evaluates whether the product has a sustainable market, what is the demand-supply gap, and how the firm's market share will evolve. Uses techniques like demand forecasting (regression, time-series), elasticity estimation, and competitive analysis.

   *Real-world example:* Before launching the **Hyundai Creta**, Hyundai conducted extensive demand analysis using focus groups, dealer feedback, and competitor benchmarking. This drove decisions on engine variants and feature pricing.

2. **Technical Analysis (T):** Examines the engineering viability — plant location, technology choice, plant capacity, input requirements, manpower, and project schedule. Includes environmental impact and pollution control.

3. **Financial Analysis (F):** Quantifies monetary returns using **NPV, IRR, PI, B-C Ratio**, and Payback Period. Determines the financing pattern (debt-equity mix) and the cost of capital.

4. **Economic / Social Cost-Benefit Analysis (E):** Assesses the project's contribution to **national welfare** rather than private profit. Uses **shadow prices, social discount rate**, and accounts for **externalities** (positive: employment; negative: pollution).

5. **Managerial / Organizational Analysis (O):** Evaluates the promoter's track record, depth of management team, organizational culture, and HR systems.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

| Dimension | Financial Analysis | Economic (SCBA) Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Perspective** | Private / firm-level | National / societal |
| **Objective** | Profit maximization | Welfare maximization |
| **Prices Used** | Market prices | Shadow prices (adjusted for distortions) |
| **Externalities** | Ignored | Explicitly included (positive & negative) |
| **Discount Rate** | Commercial WACC (12–18%) | Social discount rate (8–10% in India) |
| **Taxes & Subsidies** | Actual amounts paid/received | Adjusted using conversion factors |
| **Typical User** | Private firms, banks | Govt., World Bank, ADB, NITI Aayog |

**Why SCBA is more relevant for public sector projects:**
- Public projects (highways, metros, irrigation, rural electrification) often have **negative private NPV** but **high social value** (employment, regional development, equity).
- Example: **Konkan Railway** had a low commercial IRR but extremely high social returns from connecting the western coast to mainland India, justifying the public investment.
- SCBA ensures that government capital is allocated to projects that maximize **aggregate national welfare**, not just revenue.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **WACC** (used in financial NPV) with the **social discount rate** (used in economic NPV). The social discount rate is *lower* than WACC because society values future consumption more patiently (intergenerational equity). Writing "WACC is used in SCBA" will cost you 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Project Analysis and Evaluation (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Project Analysis** = systematic pre-investment evaluation of a capital project.
- ☐ The **Three Phases** of a project: Pre-Investment → Investment → Operational.
- ☐ The **Five Pillars (M-T-F-E-O)**: Market, Technical, Financial, Economic, Managerial.
- ☐ **DCF Methods** (time-value-based): NPV, IRR, PI, B-C Ratio, Discounted Payback.
- ☐ **NDCF Methods** (non-time-value): Simple Payback, ARR.
- ☐ **Primary decision tool** = **NPV** (wealth maximization).
- ☐ **IRR** is the rate that makes NPV = 0; accept if IRR $\geq$ cost of capital.
- ☐ **PI** is the best tool for **capital rationing** (ranking competing projects).
- ☐ **Payback Period** = $a + \frac{b}{c}$; measure of liquidity, not profitability.
- ☐ **Social Cost-Benefit Analysis (SCBA)** uses **shadow prices** and **social discount rate**.
- ☐ **Mutually exclusive projects** with different lives/scale: use **EAA** or **NPV with common-life chain** to avoid IRR-vs-NPV conflict.
- ☐ **Multiple IRR problem** arises in non-conventional cash flows — use **MIRR** or **NPV** instead.
- ☐ The **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** consolidates all five pillars into a single sanction document.
- ☐ **Post-completion audit** is the feedback mechanism that closes the project evaluation loop.
- ☐ For KTU Part-B numericals: always show **formula → substitution → answer → decision rule**.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module-1 Notes — Project Analysis and Evaluation**
> *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEIET744. For full marks, remember to (a) state the formula, (b) substitute values with units, (c) show one intermediate step, and (d) conclude with an explicit decision rule.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Capital: Costs of the Different Sources of Capital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost of Capital: Costs of the Different Sources of Capital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Capital (CoC)** is the minimum rate of return that a firm must earn on its investments to maintain the market value of its shares and to satisfy the expectations of its investors (both equity and debt holders). It represents the **opportunity cost of funds** employed in the business and is also referred to as the **hurdle rate**, **required rate of return**, or **cut-off rate**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET744)**, the Cost of Capital is fundamentally broken down into the **component costs of each specific source of financing**, because different sources (equity, preference capital, debt, retained earnings) carry different risk-return profiles and therefore different required returns.

#### The Four Principal Sources of Capital

> [!NOTE]
> **Component Costs of Capital** — The Building Blocks of WACC
> 1. **Cost of Equity Capital ($K_e$)** — The required return demanded by ordinary shareholders.
> 2. **Cost of Preference Share Capital ($K_p$)** — The fixed dividend yield expected by preference shareholders.
> 3. **Cost of Debt ($K_d$ and $K_{d(1-t)}$)** — The effective borrowing rate, considered both before and after corporate tax shield.
> 4. **Cost of Retained Earnings ($K_r$)** — The opportunity cost of ploughing back profits instead of distributing them.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rental Property" Intuition

Imagine you own a **plot of land** and you want to construct a commercial building. You have **four possible sources of money** to fund the construction:

| Source | Real-World Analogy | Required Return |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Bank Loan | Rent paid to the bank (interest) | Interest rate |
| Friends' Money (Equity) | Share of profits promised | Higher, since riskier |
| Retained Profits | Money you already had | Opportunity cost (what it could earn elsewhere) |
| Preference Capital | Fixed monthly payout to silent partners | Fixed dividend % |

> **Each source of money is like a different "landlord"** — and each landlord demands a different rental yield (return). The **Cost of Capital** is simply the **weighted average rental yield** you must pay to all landlords combined so that **nobody walks away** from the deal. If the building (project) earns *less* than this blended rental yield, you are destroying value; if it earns *more*, you are creating wealth.

### 1.3 Why Different Sources Have Different Costs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk → Required Return Trade-off (Risk-Return Asymmetry)**
>
> **Debt Holders** are paid first (senior claim on assets & earnings) → **Lowest risk** → **Lowest required return**.
>
> **Preference Shareholders** are paid after debt but before equity → **Moderate risk** → **Moderate required return** (fixed dividend).
>
> **Equity Shareholders** are residual claimants (paid last, after all obligations) → **Highest risk** → **Highest required return**.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Priority Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Significance (KTU 2024 Scheme - PEIET744, Module 1):**
> - This is the **foundation topic** for nearly all capital budgeting decisions (Module 2/3 onward).
> - Every investment appraisal technique (NPV, IRR, MIRR) uses the Cost of Capital as the **discount rate**.
> - An incorrect cost of capital directly leads to **wrong accept/reject decisions**, capital misallocation, and shareholder wealth destruction.
> - Frequently tested in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) as 7-mark sub-questions and full 14-mark structured problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Cost of Equity Capital ($K_e$)

Equity capital is raised by issuing **ordinary (common) shares**. Unlike debt, equity does **not** carry a contractual obligation to pay — the return is contingent on the firm's performance. Therefore, $K_e$ is the most conceptually complex and theoretically rich component.

#### Method 1: Dividend Growth Model (Gordon's Model)

**Assumption:** Dividends grow at a constant rate ($g$) forever.

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

Where:
- $D_1 = D_0 (1 + g)$ = Expected dividend at end of Year 1
- $P_0$ = Current market price per share
- $g$ = Constant growth rate of dividends
- $D_0$ = Most recent dividend paid

**Logic Step-by-Step:**
1. Shareholders expect at least the **dividend yield** ($D_1 / P_0$) on their investment.
2. Additionally, they expect **capital appreciation** equal to the dividend growth rate ($g$), since the share price is assumed to grow in tandem with dividends.
3. Total required return = Dividend Yield + Capital Gains Yield.

#### Method 2: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

$$K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$$

Where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate of return (typically Government of India 10-Year G-Sec yield)
- $\beta$ = Systematic risk measure of the firm's equity (sensitivity to market movements)
- $R_m$ = Expected return on the market portfolio (e.g., Nifty 50 or BSE Sensex)
- $(R_m - R_f)$ = Market Risk Premium (MRP)

**Logic Step-by-Step:**
1. The risk-free rate is the **time value of money** component — compensation for waiting.
2. The term $\beta \times (R_m - R_f)$ is the **risk premium** for bearing systematic (non-diversifiable) risk.
3. $\beta = 1$: Firm moves in line with the market.
4. $\beta > 1$: Firm is more volatile than the market (aggressive).
5. $\beta < 1$: Firm is less volatile than the market (defensive).

#### Method 3: Earnings Yield Approach (Alternative Method)

$$K_e = \frac{EPS}{P_0}$$

Where:
- $EPS$ = Earnings Per Share
- $P_0$ = Current market price per share

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is used when **dividend data is unavailable or unreliable**, and when the firm has a stable earnings payout pattern.

#### Method 4: Realised Yield Approach (Past Performance)

$$K_e = \frac{D_0}{P_0} + \frac{(P_1 - P_0)}{P_0}$$

This measures the **historical actual return** earned by shareholders (dividend + price appreciation), useful for retrospective valuation but **less reliable** for forward-looking cost of capital estimation.

---

### 2.2 Cost of Preference Share Capital ($K_p$)

Preference shares carry a **fixed dividend** and have priority over equity for dividends and asset liquidation, but rank below debt. The cost is straightforward because the dividend is **contractually fixed**.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_0}$$

When preference shares are issued at a premium or discount, or with floatation costs:

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_0 - F}$$

Where:
- $D_p$ = Annual preference dividend (fixed)
- $P_0$ = Net proceeds per share (after issue expenses)
- $F$ = Floatation cost per share

> [!NOTE]
> **Tax Treatment:** Unlike debt, preference dividends are **NOT tax-deductible** — they are paid out of **after-tax profits**. Therefore, there is **no tax shield adjustment** for $K_p$.

---

### 2.3 Cost of Debt Capital ($K_d$)

Debt includes **debentures, bonds, term loans, and other borrowed funds**. Debt-holders receive **contractually fixed interest** and have a senior claim on assets and earnings.

#### Before-Tax Cost of Debt ($K_d$)

$$K_d = \frac{I(1 - t)}{NP}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Annual interest payment (coupon rate × face value)
- $t$ = Corporate tax rate
- $NP$ = Net proceeds from debt issue (after discount/premium and floatation costs)

#### Why the Tax Shield Matters — The $(1 - t)$ Multiplier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interest on debt is a tax-deductible expense** under the Indian Income Tax Act, 1961 (Section 30-37). This means the **effective cost to the firm** is lower than the contractual interest rate.
>
> Example: A firm paying **12% interest** at a **30% tax rate** effectively bears only **12% × (1 − 0.30) = 8.40%**.

#### Cost of Irredeemable (Perpetual) Debt

$$K_d = \frac{I}{NP}$$

#### Cost of Redeemable Debt

$$K_d = \frac{I(1 - t) + \frac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV + NP)}{2}}$$

Where:
- $RV$ = Redemption Value of debt
- $n$ = Number of years to maturity
- $NP$ = Net proceeds

---

### 2.4 Cost of Retained Earnings ($K_r$)

Retained earnings are **profits ploughed back** into the business instead of being distributed as dividends. They are an **internal source** of finance but are **NOT free** — they carry an **opportunity cost**.

$$K_r = K_e \times (1 - t)$$

OR (more commonly in Indian practice):

$$K_r = K_e$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** If retained earnings were distributed as dividends, shareholders could reinvest that money in equivalent-risk investments and earn $K_e$. Therefore, the firm must earn at least $K_e$ on retained earnings to keep shareholders indifferent. Some texts apply a small adjustment factor for personal tax on dividends, but at KTU level, **$K_r = K_e$** is the standard assumption.

---

### 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Cost of Capital Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme**

| # | Source | Formula | Key Variables | Tax Adjustable? |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Cost of Equity (Dividend Growth) | $K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $D_1$, $P_0$, $g$ | No |
| 2 | Cost of Equity (CAPM) | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$, $\beta$, $R_m$ | No |
| 3 | Cost of Equity (Earnings Yield) | $K_e = \frac{EPS}{P_0}$ | $EPS$, $P_0$ | No |
| 4 | Cost of Preference Shares | $K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_0}$ (or $P_0 - F$) | $D_p$, $P_0$ | **No** (dividend not tax-deductible) |
| 5 | Cost of Debt (Irredeemable) | $K_d = \frac{I}{NP}$ | $I$, $NP$ | Apply $(1-t)$ |
| 6 | Cost of Debt (Redeemable) | $K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{(RV-NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV+NP)}{2}}$ | $I$, $t$, $RV$, $NP$, $n$ | Built-in |
| 7 | After-Tax Cost of Debt | $K_{d(1-t)} = K_d \times (1 - t)$ | $K_d$, $t$ | Yes |
| 8 | Cost of Retained Earnings | $K_r = K_e$ | $K_e$ | No |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of Component Costs

| Engineering / Business Decision | How Component Cost is Used |
|-------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Capital Budgeting (NPV, IRR)** | Discount rate = Weighted Cost of Capital |
| **Project Appraisal** | Each project's hurdle rate reflects its risk-adjusted $K_e$ |
| **Working Capital Decisions** | Short-term debt cost ($K_d$) for short-term financing |
| **Dividend Policy** | $K_r$ vs $K_e$ comparison helps decide retain vs distribute |
| **Capital Structure Decisions** | Trade-off between cheap debt ($K_d$) and risky equity ($K_e$) |
| **M&A Valuation** | Discounted Cash Flow uses WACC as discount rate |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Cost of Equity using Gordon's Dividend Growth Model

**Starting Premise:** The current price of a share ($P_0$) equals the **present value of all future dividends** growing at a constant rate ($g$):

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \cdots$$

Since dividends grow at constant rate $g$: $D_2 = D_1(1+g)$, $D_3 = D_1(1+g)^2$, and so on.

Substituting:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_1(1+g)}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_1(1+g)^2}{(1+K_e)^3} + \cdots$$

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_1(1+g)^{t-1}}{(1+K_e)^t}$$

Recognizing this as an infinite geometric series with first term $a = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)}$ and common ratio $r = \frac{(1+g)}{(1+K_e)}$:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)} \times \frac{1}{1 - \frac{(1+g)}{(1+K_e)}}$$

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)} \times \frac{1}{\frac{(1+K_e) - (1+g)}{(1+K_e)}}$$

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)} \times \frac{(1+K_e)}{(K_e - g)}$$

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}$$

**Solving for $K_e$:**

$$K_e - g = \frac{D_1}{P_0}$$

$$\boxed{K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g}$$

**Validation:** When $g = 0$, $K_e = D_1/P_0$ (simple dividend yield on perpetuity) — consistent with a zero-growth stock.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Cost of Equity (Gordon Model)

> **Problem:** A company's equity share is currently trading at ₹120. The company has just paid a dividend of ₹4.50 per share. Investors expect dividends to grow at 8% per annum indefinitely. The company's marginal tax rate is 30%. Find the cost of equity capital.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $D_0 = ₹4.50$
- $P_0 = ₹120$
- $g = 8\% = 0.08$

Step 1: Compute expected dividend in Year 1.

$$D_1 = D_0 (1 + g) = 4.50 \times (1 + 0.08) = 4.50 \times 1.08$$

$$D_1 = ₹4.86$$

Step 2: Apply Gordon's formula.

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

$$K_e = \frac{4.86}{120} + 0.08$$

$$K_e = 0.0405 + 0.08$$

$$K_e = 0.1205$$

$$\boxed{K_e = 12.05\%}$$

**Note:** Tax rate is irrelevant for $K_e$ (dividends are not tax-deductible).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Cost of Equity (CAPM)

> **Problem:** The risk-free rate is 7%. The expected market return is 14%. The beta of stock ABC Ltd. is 1.2. Compute $K_e$ using CAPM.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $R_f = 7\%$
- $R_m = 14\%$
- $\beta = 1.2$

Step 1: Compute Market Risk Premium (MRP).

$$MRP = R_m - R_f = 14\% - 7\% = 7\%$$

Step 2: Compute Risk Premium for the stock.

$$\text{Stock Risk Premium} = \beta \times MRP = 1.2 \times 7\% = 8.4\%$$

Step 3: Apply CAPM.

$$K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$$

$$K_e = 7\% + 1.2 \times (14\% - 7\%)$$

$$K_e = 7\% + 8.4\%$$

$$\boxed{K_e = 15.4\%}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Cost of Preference Capital

> **Problem:** A company issues 10,000 12% preference shares of ₹100 each at a premium of 10%. Floatation costs are ₹5 per share. Compute $K_p$.

**Solution:**

Given:
- Face value = ₹100
- Premium = 10% × ₹100 = ₹10
- Floatation cost = ₹5
- Dividend rate = 12%

Step 1: Compute annual preference dividend.

$$D_p = 12\% \times ₹100 = ₹12$$

Step 2: Compute net proceeds per share.

$$NP = \text{Face Value} + \text{Premium} - \text{Floatation Cost}$$

$$NP = 100 + 10 - 5 = ₹105$$

Step 3: Compute $K_p$.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{NP} = \frac{12}{105}$$

$$K_p = 0.1143$$

$$\boxed{K_p = 11.43\%}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Cost of Debt (Before & After Tax)

> **Problem:** A company issues 10,000 13% debentures of ₹100 each redeemable at par after 10 years. Floatation costs are 2% of face value. Tax rate is 30%. Find (a) before-tax cost and (b) after-tax cost.

**Solution:**

Given:
- Face Value (FV) = ₹100
- Coupon Rate = 13%
- Redemption Value (RV) = ₹100 (redeemable at par)
- $n = 10$ years
- Floatation cost = 2% × ₹100 = ₹2
- $t = 30\%$

Step 1: Compute Net Proceeds.

$$NP = 100 - 2 = ₹98$$

Step 2: Compute Annual Interest.

$$I = 13\% \times 100 = ₹13$$

Step 3: Apply redeemable debt formula.

$$K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV + NP)}{2}}$$

Step 4: Compute numerator components.

$$I(1-t) = 13 \times (1 - 0.30) = 13 \times 0.70 = 9.10$$

$$\frac{RV - NP}{n} = \frac{100 - 98}{10} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 9.10 + 0.20 = 9.30$$

Step 5: Compute denominator.

$$\frac{RV + NP}{2} = \frac{100 + 98}{2} = \frac{198}{2} = 99$$

Step 6: Compute $K_d$ (after-tax, since formula already includes $(1-t)$).

$$K_d = \frac{9.30}{99} = 0.09394$$

$$\boxed{K_d \approx 9.39\% \text{ (after-tax)}}$$

Step 7: Compute before-tax $K_d$ for reference.

$$K_{d,\text{before tax}} = \frac{I + \frac{(RV-NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV+NP)}{2}} = \frac{13 + 0.20}{99} = \frac{13.20}{99} = 13.33\%$$

Verification: $9.39\% \approx 13.33\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 9.33\%$ ✓ (small difference due to amortization of discount included in the redeemable formula).

---

### 3.6 Worked Example — Combined Cost Computation (Synthesis Problem)

> **Problem:** A firm has the following capital structure. Compute the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)**.

| Source | Book Value (₹) | Specific Cost |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Equity Shares | 6,00,000 | 16% |
| Preference Shares | 2,00,000 | 11% |
| Debentures (13%) | 2,00,000 | 9% (after-tax) |

Tax rate = 30%.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute Total Capital.

$$\text{Total} = 6,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 2,00,000 = ₹10,00,000$$

Step 2: Compute Weights.

$$W_e = \frac{6,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.60$$

$$W_p = \frac{2,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.20$$

$$W_d = \frac{2,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.20$$

Step 3: Apply WACC Formula.

$$WACC = W_e \cdot K_e + W_p \cdot K_p + W_d \cdot K_d(1-t)$$

$$WACC = (0.60 \times 16\%) + (0.20 \times 11\%) + (0.20 \times 9\%)$$

$$WACC = 9.60\% + 2.20\% + 1.80\%$$

$$\boxed{WACC = 13.60\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The firm must earn **at least 13.60%** on its total investments to satisfy all capital providers. Any project yielding less than this threshold destroys shareholder value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Sources of Capital

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FIRM CAPITAL STRUCTURE"]:::root
    B["OWNERS FUNDS"]:::own
    C["BORROWED FUNDS"]:::debt
    
    A --> B
    A --> C
    
    B --> D["EQUITY SHARE CAPITAL"]
    B --> E["PREFERENCE SHARE CAPITAL"]
    B --> F["RETAINED EARNINGS"]
    B --> G["RESERVES AND SURPLUS"]
    
    C --> H["DEBENTURES AND BONDS"]
    C --> I["TERM LOANS FROM BANKS"]
    C --> J["PUBLIC DEPOSITS"]
    C --> K["SHORT TERM BORROWINGS"]
    
    D --> L["Cost Ke - Highest Risk"]:::eq
    E --> M["Cost Kp - Fixed Dividend"]:::pref
    F --> N["Cost Kr - Opportunity Cost"]:::eq
    H --> O["Cost Kd - Lowest with Tax Shield"]:::debtbox
    
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef own fill:#059669,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef debt fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef eq fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef pref fill:#ea580c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef debtbox fill:#0891b2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.2 Risk-Return Hierarchy — The "Pyramid of Capital Claims"

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["DEBT HOLDERS - Senior Claim - Lowest Risk - Lowest Required Return"]:::debtop
    L2["PREFERENCE SHAREHOLDERS - Mid Priority - Moderate Risk - Fixed Dividend"]:::prefop
    L3["EQUITY SHAREHOLDERS - Residual Claim - Highest Risk - Highest Required Return"]:::eqop
    
    L1 --> L2 --> L3
    
    classDef debtop fill:#0891b2,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef prefop fill:#ea580c,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef eqop fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.3 Computational Flowchart — Choosing the Right $K_e$ Method

```mermaid

    S["START: Compute Cost of Equity Ke"]:::start
    Q1{"Is dividend data stable?"}:::decide
    Q2{"Is beta available?"}:::decide
    P1["USE GORDON DIVIDEND GROWTH MODEL Ke = D1/P0 + g"]:::method1
    P2["USE CAPM Ke = Rf + Beta times Rm minus Rf"]:::method2
    P3["USE EARNINGS YIELD Ke = EPS / P0"]:::method3
    END["FINAL Ke VALUE"]:::result
    
    S --> Q1
    Q1 -- YES --> P1
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2
    Q2 -- YES --> P2
    Q2 -- NO --> P3
    P1 --> END
    P2 --> END
    P3 --> END
    
    classDef start fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decide fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef method1 fill:#059669,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef method2 fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef method3 fill:#ea580c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef result fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

    B["MULTIPLY BY 1 minus t"]:::process

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 📘 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

#### **Q1. Define "Cost of Capital" and explain why it is also known as the "Hurdle Rate" or "Cut-off Rate".**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Cost of Capital** is the minimum rate of return expected by the suppliers of capital (debt-holders, preference shareholders, and equity shareholders) for the funds invested in the firm. It is also called the **Hurdle Rate** or **Cut-off Rate** because any new investment project must "jump over" (i.e., earn at least) this rate of return to be acceptable.
>
> (1) It serves as a benchmark for evaluating investment proposals. (2) Projects yielding returns below the cost of capital result in erosion of shareholder wealth and must be rejected. (3) It is the discount rate used in NPV calculations. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for hurdle/cut-off explanation + 1 mark for application)*

---

#### **Q2. Why is the cost of debt lower than the cost of equity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The cost of debt is lower than the cost of equity due to **two key reasons**:
>
> 1. **Lower Risk:** Debt-holders have a **senior claim** on the firm's assets and earnings. They receive **fixed contractual interest** and are paid **before** equity shareholders. Lower risk implies a lower required return.
>
> 2. **Tax Deductibility of Interest:** Interest paid on debt is an **expense** under the Income Tax Act and is **tax-deductible**, while dividends on equity are paid out of **after-tax profits** and are **not tax-deductible**. This tax shield further reduces the effective cost of debt.
>
> *(1 mark for risk reason + 1 mark for tax reason + 1 mark for logical conclusion)*

---

### 📕 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — COMPONENT COST COMPUTATION**

**(a)** A company has the following capital structure:

| Source | Amount (₹) |
|--------|-----------|
| Equity Share Capital (10,000 shares of ₹100 each) | 10,00,000 |
| 12% Preference Shares (1,000 shares of ₹100 each) | 1,00,000 |
| 14% Debentures (1,000 debentures of ₹1,000 each) | 10,00,000 |
| **Total** | **21,00,000** |

Additional information:
- Current market price of equity share = ₹130
- Expected dividend per share next year = ₹8
- Expected growth rate of dividends = 6%
- Preference shares were issued at 10% premium; floatation cost ₹5 per share
- Debentures were issued at par; redeemable at par after 8 years
- Corporate tax rate = 30%

**Compute:** (i) Cost of Equity $K_e$ (ii) Cost of Preference Shares $K_p$ (iii) Cost of Debt $K_d$ (after-tax) (iv) Weighted Average Cost of Capital.

**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**. Discuss its assumptions, components ($R_f$, $\beta$, MRP), and limitations. How does CAPM improve upon the Dividend Growth Model for estimating the cost of equity?

**[7 Marks]**

---

##### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-A(a):**

**Given Data Extraction:**
- Equity: $P_0 = ₹130$, $D_1 = ₹8$, $g = 6\%$
- Preference: $D_p = 12\% \times ₹100 = ₹12$; Premium = ₹10; Floatation = ₹5
- Debt: Coupon = 14%, $RV = ₹1,000$, $n = 8$ years, Issued at par, $t = 30\%$
- Total Capital = ₹21,00,000

**(i) Cost of Equity $K_e$ — Gordon's Dividend Growth Model:**

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{8}{130} + 0.06$$

$$K_e = 0.06154 + 0.06 = 0.12154$$

$$\boxed{K_e = 12.15\%}$$

**(ii) Cost of Preference Shares $K_p$:**

Step 1: Net Proceeds per share.

$$NP = 100 + 10 - 5 = ₹105$$

Step 2: Apply $K_p$ formula.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{NP} = \frac{12}{105} = 0.11429$$

$$\boxed{K_p = 11.43\%}$$

**(iii) Cost of Debt $K_d$ (After-Tax, Redeemable):**

Step 1: Net Proceeds.

$$NP = ₹1,000 - 0 = ₹1,000 \text{ (issued at par, no floatation)}$$

Step 2: Annual Interest.

$$I = 14\% \times 1,000 = ₹140$$

Step 3: Apply redeemable debt formula.

$$K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{(RV-NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV+NP)}{2}}$$

$$K_d = \frac{140(1-0.30) + \frac{(1000-1000)}{8}}{\frac{(1000+1000)}{2}}$$

$$K_d = \frac{140 \times 0.70 + 0}{\frac{2000}{2}}$$

$$K_d = \frac{98}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{K_d = 9.80\%}$$

**(iv) Weighted Average Cost of Capital:**

Step 1: Weights.

$$W_e = \frac{10,00,000}{21,00,000} = 0.4762$$

$$W_p = \frac{1,00,000}{21,00,000} = 0.0476$$

$$W_d = \frac{10,00,000}{21,00,000} = 0.4762$$

Step 2: WACC Formula.

$$WACC = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d$$

$$WACC = (0.4762 \times 12.15\%) + (0.0476 \times 11.43\%) + (0.4762 \times 9.80\%)$$

$$WACC = 5.786\% + 0.544\% + 4.667\%$$

$$\boxed{WACC = 10.997\% \approx 11.00\%}$$

---

##### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-A(b): CAPM — Explanation, Assumptions, and Comparison**

**Definition (2 marks):**

The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM), developed by **William Sharpe (1964), John Lintner, and Jan Mossin**, expresses the expected return on a security as a linear function of its systematic risk (beta). It provides a theoretically grounded framework for estimating the cost of equity.

$$K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$$

**Components (2 marks):**

1. **$R_f$ (Risk-Free Rate):** The return on a default-free investment, typically proxied by the yield on long-term Government of India securities (10-Year G-Sec). It compensates for the time value of money.

2. **$\beta$ (Beta):** A statistical measure of a stock's sensitivity to market movements. $\beta = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$. $\beta > 1$ indicates higher volatility than the market.

3. **$(R_m - R_f)$ (Market Risk Premium):** The excess return demanded by investors for investing in the market portfolio over the risk-free asset.

**Key Assumptions (1.5 marks):**

- Investors are rational and risk-averse.
- Single-period investment horizon.
- Homogeneous expectations among investors.
- No taxes or transaction costs (in basic CAPM).
- Investors can borrow and lend at the risk-free rate.

**Limitations (1 mark):**

- Estimation of beta is unstable over time.
- Market portfolio is theoretically unobservable (Roll's Critique).
- Single-factor model may be insufficient to explain returns.
- Assumes normal distribution of returns.

**Comparison with Gordon's Model (0.5 mark):**

- Gordon's Model relies on **dividend stability** and **constant growth** — restrictive assumptions.
- CAPM is more theoretically robust as it explicitly accounts for **systematic (market) risk**.
- CAPM is preferred when **beta and market data are available**; Gordon's is used when **dividend data is reliable**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** What is the **Cost of Retained Earnings**? Why are retained earnings not considered "free" capital? Compute the cost of retained earnings for a firm with the following data:
- Risk-free rate = 6%
- Beta = 1.1
- Expected market return = 13%
- Dividend payout ratio = 40%
- Expected dividend per share = ₹5
- Current market price = ₹80

**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A firm is evaluating a project requiring an investment of ₹50,00,000. The project is expected to generate cash inflows of ₹15,00,000 per annum for 5 years. The firm's capital structure comprises:
- Equity: ₹30,00,000 at 15% cost
- 12% Debentures: ₹20,00,000 (after-tax cost 8.40%)

**Determine** whether the project should be accepted using the **Net Present Value (NPV)** method, with the WACC as the discount rate.

**[7 Marks]**

---

##### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-B(a):**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 Marks):**

> **Cost of Retained Earnings ($K_r$):** It is the opportunity cost of internal accruals. Although the firm does not pay any explicit cost on retained earnings, these funds belong to shareholders who could have received them as dividends. If the firm cannot earn at least as much as shareholders could earn elsewhere on equivalent-risk investments, it should distribute the earnings as dividends.

**Therefore:** $K_r = K_e$ (most common formulation).

**Computation (4 Marks):**

Step 1: Compute $K_e$ using CAPM.

$$K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 6\% + 1.1 \times (13\% - 6\%)$$

$$K_e = 6\% + 1.1 \times 7\% = 6\% + 7.7\% = 13.7\%$$

Step 2: Verify $K_e$ using Gordon's Model (cross-check).

Given Payout = 40%, Retention = 60%.

$$g = b \times r = 0.60 \times \text{ROE}$$

Wait — without ROE, we can only compute $K_e$ via Gordon as:

$$D_0 = ₹5 / \text{(already given as expected, so } D_1 = ₹5)$$

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

We need $g$. Since payout = 40%, retention $b = 60\%$. Without ROE, use growth = retention × ROE — but ROE is not given directly. Using the data provided, we focus on CAPM-based $K_e = 13.7\%$.

Step 3: State $K_r$.

$$\boxed{K_r = K_e = 13.7\%}$$

**Key Insight (1 mark):** Even though no cash leaves the firm, shareholders' opportunity cost must be matched — making retained earnings a **non-zero cost** source.

---

##### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-B(b):**

**Step 1: Compute WACC.**

$$W_e = \frac{30,00,000}{50,00,000} = 0.60$$

$$W_d = \frac{20,00,000}{50,00,000} = 0.40$$

$$WACC = (0.60 \times 15\%) + (0.40 \times 8.40\%)$$

$$WACC = 9.00\% + 3.36\%$$

$$\boxed{WACC = 12.36\%}$$

**Step 2: Compute NPV at WACC = 12.36%.**

Present Value of Annuity Factor (PVAF) for $n = 5$, $r = 12.36\%$:

$$PVAF = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} = \frac{1 - (1.1236)^{-5}}{0.1236}$$

Computing $(1.1236)^5$:

$$(1.1236)^1 = 1.1236$$
$$(1.1236)^2 = 1.2625$$
$$(1.1236)^3 = 1.4185$$
$$(1.1236)^4 = 1.5938$$
$$(1.1236)^5 = 1.7908$$

Therefore: $(1.1236)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.7908} = 0.5584$.

$$PVAF = \frac{1 - 0.5584}{0.1236} = \frac{0.4416}{0.1236} = 3.5728$$

Step 3: Total Present Value of Cash Inflows.

$$PV = 15,00,000 \times 3.5728 = ₹53,59,200$$

Step 4: Compute NPV.

$$NPV = PV - \text{Initial Investment}$$

$$NPV = 53,59,200 - 50,00,000$$

$$\boxed{NPV = ₹3,59,200 \text{ (Positive)}}$$

**Decision (1 mark):** Since NPV is **positive**, the project **should be ACCEPTED**. It generates value above the cost of capital.

---

### ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Cost of Capital Problems (As per KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Tax Shield on Debt:** Students frequently compute $K_d$ without multiplying by $(1-t)$, losing **2-3 marks**. **Always check** whether the formula explicitly contains $(1-t)$ or whether you must apply it separately.
>
> 2. **Using $D_0$ instead of $D_1$ in Gordon's Model:** A classic blunder. $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$. Using $D_0$ gives a lower (incorrect) $K_e$. **Examiner deduction: 1-2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Applying Tax Adjustment to Preference Dividends:** Preference dividends are **NOT** tax-deductible. Do **NOT** apply $(1-t)$ to $K_p$. **Common mistake, costs 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Confusing Book Value vs Market Value Weights:** At KTU level, **book values** are typically used unless the question specifies market values. Mixing them without justification costs **1-2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Floatation Costs:** Many students compute $K_p$ and $K_d$ using face value or gross proceeds. Always subtract **floatation expenses** from proceeds. **Examiner deduction: 1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Units & Percentage Mismatch:** Express $g$ as a decimal (0.08, not 8%) when computing $K_e$ via Gordon's Model. Mixing percentages and decimals is a frequent silent error. **Deduction: 1 mark.**
>
> 7. **Rounding Too Early:** Avoid rounding intermediate steps. Carry at least 4 decimal places until the final answer.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cost of Capital: Different Sources**

**📌 Core Conceptual Hierarchy:**
- ✅ Cost of Capital = Minimum required return to maintain firm value.
- ✅ Component costs: $K_e > K_p > K_d$ (in general, before tax).
- ✅ After-tax $K_d < K_p < K_e$ always (because of tax shield on debt only).

**📌 Cost of Equity — Master Formulas:**
- ✅ **Gordon's Model:** $K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ — use when dividends are stable.
- ✅ **CAPM:** $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — use when beta is available.
- ✅ **Earnings Yield:** $K_e = \frac{EPS}{P_0}$ — use when dividends are absent/unreliable.

**📌 Cost of Preference Shares:**
- ✅ $K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_0 - F}$ — **No tax adjustment.**
- ✅ Dividend is contractual and fixed; paid out of after-tax profits.

**📌 Cost of Debt:**
- ✅ **Irredeemable:** $K_d = \frac{I}{NP}$; After-tax: multiply by $(1-t)$.
- ✅ **Redeemable:** $K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{(RV-NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV+NP)}{2}}$.
- ✅ Tax shield is the **single most important reason** debt is cheaper than equity.

**📌 Cost of Retained Earnings:**
- ✅ $K_r = K_e$ (at KTU level).
- ✅ Not "free" — carries full opportunity cost equal to foregone dividend reinvestment.

**📌 Synthesis into WACC:**
- ✅ $WACC = \sum W_i \times K_i$ — weighted average of all component costs.
- ✅ Always use **after-tax cost** for debt; use **full cost** for equity and preference.

**📌 Practical Engineering Insight:**
- ✅ In capital budgeting (NPV/IRR), WACC is the **discount rate** — any error in component costs cascades into wrong investment decisions worth **crores** in real corporate finance.
- ✅ A higher proportion of debt (within tolerable limits) **lowers WACC** due to tax shield — this is the foundation of the **Trade-off Theory** of capital structure (covered in later KTU modules).

**📌 Examiner's Favourite Sub-Topics:**
- ✅ Gordon's Model application (most frequent 7-mark question).
- ✅ CAPM derivation and interpretation (high-yield theory question).
- ✅ Combined WACC computation (full 14-mark structured problem).
- ✅ Why debt is cheaper than equity — conceptual short note.
- ✅ Retained earnings opportunity cost reasoning.

---

topics.title: Cost of Debt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost of Debt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Debt (Kd)** is the effective rate of interest a firm must pay on its borrowed capital (debt financing). It represents the **required rate of return** demanded by lenders and bondholders for providing debt capital to the company, expressed as a percentage of the total debt raised. Crucially, because interest on debt is a **tax-deductible expense** under corporate tax law, the relevant figure for valuation is the **After-Tax Cost of Debt**: $K_d^{AT} = K_d^{BT} \times (1 - t)$, where **t** is the corporate tax rate.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Cost of Debt is one of the most heavily weighted components of the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)**. It is a *contractually obligated* return — unlike equity cost, it is not based on expectations but on **stipulated rate schedules** in bond indentures and loan agreements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Landlord Mortgage" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Cost of Debt as the "interest rate the bank charges YOU."**

Imagine you borrow ₹10,00,000 from a bank at 9% interest to buy a rental property. The bank wants **9% guaranteed** because the loan is secured and the bank is first in line for repayment. The cost of debt to YOU is that 9% rate — the price of using someone else's money. However, here's the powerful twist: the Income Tax Act allows you to **deduct that 9% interest expense** from your rental income before paying tax. If your tax bracket is 30%, the *true economic cost* of that debt drops to:

$$9\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 6.30\%$$

This 6.30% is your **After-Tax Cost of Debt**. This is precisely why debt is called a **"tax shield"** in finance — it legally reduces the firm's tax bill.

## 1.3 Tax-Deductibility — Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interest is the ONLY return paid by a company that is tax-deductible.** Dividends to equity holders are NOT tax-deductible. This single fact makes debt cheaper than equity after tax.

## 1.4 Categories of Debt Instruments (KTU Syllabus Scope)

| Instrument Type | Example | Typical Horizon |
|---|---|---|
| Irredeemable / Perpetual Debt | Perpetual Bonds (rare) | Infinite (n → ∞) |
| Redeemable Debt | Regular Bonds, Debentures | Fixed (n years) |
| Convertible Debt | Convertible Debentures | Fixed + Conversion option |
| Preference Shares | Cumulative/Non-cumulative Pref. Shares | Fixed dividend |

> [!NOTE]
> In Module 1 of PEIET744, the focus is on **Redeemable Debt** and **Irredeemable Debt** with constant coupon rates. Floating-rate and convertible debt valuation falls under later modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The cost of debt must be estimated differently depending on:
1. **Whether the debt is redeemable or irredeemable**
2. **Whether the company is using fresh issue or already issued debt**
3. **Whether the book value or market value of debt is used as the base**

### A. Irredeemable (Perpetual) Debt

A debt instrument that has **no maturity date**. The lender receives interest forever.

**Before-Tax Cost:**
$$K_d = \frac{I}{P_0}$$

**After-Tax Cost:**
$$K_d^{AT} = \frac{I \times (1 - t)}{P_0}$$

Where:
- **I** = Annual interest payment (in ₹)
- **P₀** = Net proceeds from debt issue (in ₹)
- **t** = Corporate tax rate

### B. Redeemable Debt

A debt instrument with a **fixed maturity** where the principal is repaid at the end. The lender's actual return is measured by the **Yield to Maturity (YTM)**.

**Conceptual Setup:**
An investor who buys a redeemable bond at price **P₀** receives:
- Annual interest **I** for **n** years
- Redemption value **F** at year **n**

The YTM is the discount rate that equates the present value of these inflows to the current market price.

**Method 1 — YTM / IRR Method (Exact)**
$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{I}{(1+K_d)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+K_d)^n}$$

This equation must be solved by **trial-and-error** or **interpolation**.

**Method 2 — Approximation Formula (Weighted Average Yield)**

$$K_d = \frac{I + \dfrac{(F - P_0)}{n}}{\dfrac{(F + P_0)}{2}}$$

Where:
- Numerator = Annual interest + Annual amortization of premium/discount
- Denominator = Average value of the bond over its life

**Method 3 — Simple Approximation (Annualized Yield on Premium/Discount)**

$$K_d = \frac{I + \dfrac{(F - P_0)}{n}}{P_0}$$

**Method 4 — Realized Yield / Book Value Approach (used when historical data exists)**

$$K_d = \frac{I + \dfrac{(F - NV)}{n}}{NV}$$

Where **NV** = Net proceeds / Book value.

## 2.2 The Tax Shield — Detailed Treatment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under Section 16 of the Income Tax Act (and corresponding KTU curriculum treatment), interest paid on borrowed capital is allowed as a deduction from business income. Therefore, the *effective* cost of debt to the firm is reduced.

**Tax Shield Value (per rupee of interest):**
$$\text{Tax Shield} = I \times t$$

The annual after-tax cash outflow on debt:
$$\text{After-tax Interest} = I - (I \times t) = I \times (1 - t)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table — it covers 90% of KTU ESE numerical problems on Cost of Debt.**

| # | Debt Type | Before-Tax Formula | After-Tax Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Irredeemable / Perpetual | $K_d = I/P_0$ | $K_d^{AT} = I(1-t)/P_0$ | No maturity date |
| 2 | Redeemable (YTM) | Solve $P_0 = \sum I/(1+K_d)^t + F/(1+K_d)^n$ | Multiply result by $(1-t)$ | Most accurate, requires iteration |
| 3 | Redeemable (Approx. 1) | $K_d = \dfrac{I + (F-P_0)/n}{(F+P_0)/2}$ | Multiply result by $(1-t)$ | Weighted average basis |
| 4 | Redeemable (Approx. 2) | $K_d = \dfrac{I + (F-P_0)/n}{P_0}$ | Multiply result by $(1-t)$ | On original issue price basis |
| 5 | Realized Yield | $K_d = \dfrac{I + (F-NV)/n}{NV}$ | Multiply result by $(1-t)$ | Past/Book value analysis |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Corporate Finance

- **Capital Budgeting (NPV/IRR Projects):** Cost of debt feeds directly into WACC, the discount rate used to evaluate all long-term projects.
- **Capital Structure Decisions:** A firm compares $K_d^{AT}$ with $K_e$ to decide the optimal debt-equity mix (Modigliani-Miller Theorem extensions).
- **Bond Pricing:** Reverse-engineering — given market price, solve for YTM to know the bond's yield.
- **Credit Risk Assessment:** Rising cost of debt signals worsening creditworthiness to investors.

## 2.5 Key Assumptions and Caveats

1. **Constant Coupon:** Interest rate **I** is assumed fixed over the bond's life.
2. **Lump-sum Redemption:** Principal **F** is paid only at maturity, not in installments.
3. **No Default:** Issuer is assumed to honor all obligations.
4. **Single Tax Rate:** A marginal corporate tax rate **t** is applied uniformly.
5. **Annual Compounding:** KTU standard assumes yearly coupon periods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cost of Irredeemable Debt

**Setup:**
A company issues perpetual bonds. Annual interest = **I**. Net proceeds = **P₀**. Market required rate of return = **Kd**.

**Step 1:** For an investor to be willing to pay **P₀** for a perpetual stream of interest payments, the present value of that infinite stream must equal the price.

$$P_0 = \frac{I}{1+K_d} + \frac{I}{(1+K_d)^2} + \frac{I}{(1+K_d)^3} + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Recognize this as an infinite geometric series with first term $a = I/(1+K_d)$ and common ratio $r = 1/(1+K_d)$.

$$\text{Sum} = \frac{a}{1 - r} = \frac{\dfrac{I}{1+K_d}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+K_d}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the denominator:

$$1 - \frac{1}{1+K_d} = \frac{(1+K_d) - 1}{1+K_d} = \frac{K_d}{1+K_d}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$P_0 = \frac{\dfrac{I}{1+K_d}}{\dfrac{K_d}{1+K_d}} = \frac{I}{K_d}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for Kd:

$$\boxed{K_d = \frac{I}{P_0}}$$

**Step 6:** Apply tax shield:

$$\boxed{K_d^{AT} = \frac{I \times (1 - t)}{P_0}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Gordon Growth Model for zero growth applied to debt** — the "dividend" (interest) never grows and never ends.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost of Redeemable Debt (Approximation Formula)

**Setup:** Bond issued at price **P₀**, redeemed at **F** after **n** years. Annual interest **I**.

**Step 1:** The investor's total return over **n** years consists of:
- Total interest earned: $n \times I$
- Capital gain or loss: $(F - P_0)$

**Step 2:** Total earnings over the life:

$$\text{Total Earnings} = nI + (F - P_0)$$

**Step 3:** Average annual earnings:

$$\text{Average Annual Earnings} = \frac{nI + (F - P_0)}{n} = I + \frac{F - P_0}{n}$$

**Step 4:** The investor's capital is "locked in" between **P₀** (initially) and **F** (finally). The logical average investment is:

$$\text{Average Investment} = \frac{P_0 + F}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Yield = Average Annual Earnings ÷ Average Investment:

$$\boxed{K_d = \frac{I + \dfrac{(F - P_0)}{n}}{\dfrac{(F + P_0)}{2}}}$$

**Step 6:** After-tax form:

$$\boxed{K_d^{AT} = \left[\frac{I + \dfrac{(F - P_0)}{n}}{\dfrac{(F + P_0)}{2}}\right] \times (1 - t)}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-mark style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A company issued 1,000 12% debentures of ₹100 each at a premium of 10%. The debentures are redeemable after 5 years at par. Tax rate = 30%. Compute the cost of debt using: (a) Approximation formula, (b) YTM method.

**Given Data:**
- Face Value (FV) = ₹100 each
- Premium = 10% → Issue Price **P₀** = ₹110
- Redemption Value **F** = ₹100 (at par)
- Annual Interest **I** = 12% of ₹100 = ₹12
- Tenure **n** = 5 years
- Tax rate **t** = 30%

---

### Part (a) — Approximation Method

**Step 1:** Compute premium amortization per year:

$$\frac{F - P_0}{n} = \frac{100 - 110}{5} = \frac{-10}{5} = -₹2$$

**Step 2:** Compute annual earnings per bond:

$$I + \frac{F - P_0}{n} = 12 + (-2) = ₹10$$

**Step 3:** Compute average investment:

$$\frac{F + P_0}{2} = \frac{100 + 110}{2} = ₹105$$

**Step 4:** Compute before-tax cost:

$$K_d^{BT} = \frac{10}{105} = 0.0952 = 9.52\%$$

**Step 5:** Apply tax shield:

$$K_d^{AT} = 9.52\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 9.52\% \times 0.70 = 6.67\%$$

**Answer (a):** $K_d^{BT} = 9.52\%$, $K_d^{AT} = 6.67\%$

---

### Part (b) — YTM Method (Trial and Error)

**Step 1:** Set up the equation:

$$110 = \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{12}{(1+K_d)^t} + \frac{100}{(1+K_d)^5}$$

**Step 2:** Try **Kd = 8%**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(8\%, 5) &= \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-5}}{0.08} = 3.9927 \\
\text{PVIF}(8\%, 5) &= (1.08)^{-5} = 0.6806 \\
\text{PV} &= 12 \times 3.9927 + 100 \times 0.6806 \\
&= 47.91 + 68.06 = 115.97
\end{aligned}$$

Price at 8% (₹115.97) > Issue price (₹110), so YTM > 8%.

**Step 3:** Try **Kd = 9%**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(9\%, 5) &= \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-5}}{0.09} = 3.8897 \\
\text{PVIF}(9\%, 5) &= (1.09)^{-5} = 0.6499 \\
\text{PV} &= 12 \times 3.8897 + 100 \times 0.6499 \\
&= 46.68 + 64.99 = 111.67
\end{aligned}$$

Price at 9% (₹111.67) > ₹110, still slightly above.

**Step 4:** Try **Kd = 9.30%**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(9.30\%, 5) &= \frac{1 - (1.093)^{-5}}{0.093} \approx 3.8440 \\
\text{PVIF}(9.30\%, 5) &= (1.093)^{-5} \approx 0.6374 \\
\text{PV} &= 12 \times 3.8440 + 100 \times 0.6374 \\
&= 46.13 + 63.74 = 109.87
\end{aligned}$$

Price at 9.30% (₹109.87) < ₹110.

**Step 5:** Interpolate between 9% and 9.30%:

$$K_d = 9\% + \frac{111.67 - 110.00}{111.67 - 109.87} \times (9.30\% - 9.00\%)$$

$$K_d = 9\% + \frac{1.67}{1.80} \times 0.30\% = 9\% + 0.278\% = 9.28\%$$

**Step 6:** Apply tax shield:

$$K_d^{AT} = 9.28\% \times 0.70 = 6.50\%$$

**Answer (b):** $K_d^{BT} \approx 9.28\%$, $K_d^{AT} \approx 6.50\%$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Use?

| Condition | Recommended Formula | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Bond has no maturity date | $K_d = I/P_0$ | Simplest case, perpetuity |
| Market price and FV differ significantly | Approximation 1: weighted avg | Smooths out premium/discount |
| Fresh issue at known price, standard maturity | Approximation 2: $K_d = [I + (F-P_0)/n] / P_0$ | Direct yield on proceeds |
| Existing bond with multiple cash flows | YTM / IRR method | Most accurate, captures time value |
| Historical analysis, book values available | Realized Yield | Used in ex-post studies |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Cost of Debt. Why is after-tax cost of debt considered more relevant than before-tax cost of debt for capital budgeting decisions?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost of Debt (Kd)** is the effective rate of interest a firm must pay on its borrowed capital. It is the required rate of return demanded by lenders for extending debt to the company.

The **After-Tax Cost of Debt** is more relevant than the Before-Tax Cost for the following reasons:

1. **Tax Deductibility:** Interest on debt is an allowable deduction under the Income Tax Act, reducing the firm's taxable income. The actual economic outflow from the firm is the interest *after* tax savings.
2. **Accurate WACC Computation:** WACC must reflect the *true* cost of each source of capital to the firm. Using before-tax cost would overstate the cost of debt.
3. **Capital Budgeting Consistency:** Project cash flows in NPV analysis are computed on an after-tax basis. Discounting them with a pre-tax rate creates a mismatch.
4. **Formula:** $K_d^{AT} = K_d^{BT} \times (1 - t)$, where **t** is the marginal tax rate.

**(3 marks — definition 1 + three reasons 1+1+1 OR definition 1 + explanation 2)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between the cost of irredeemable debt and the cost of redeemable debt. State the formula for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Irredeemable Debt | Redeemable Debt |
|---|---|---|
| Maturity | No fixed maturity date | Fixed maturity date |
| Cash Flows | Perpetual interest stream | Interest + Principal at maturity |
| Complexity | Simple — single-period formula | Complex — multi-period valuation |
| Before-Tax Formula | $K_d = I/P_0$ | $K_d = \dfrac{I + (F-P_0)/n}{(F+P_0)/2}$ or YTM equation |
| After-Tax Formula | $K_d^{AT} = I(1-t)/P_0$ | Multiply before-tax by $(1-t)$ |
| Typical Example | Perpetual bonds (rare) | Regular debentures, bonds |

**(3 marks — distinction table with formulas)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** A company issued 2,000 11% debentures of ₹500 each at a discount of 4%. The debentures are redeemable at a premium of 5% after 6 years. The company is in the 30% tax bracket. Calculate the cost of debt using:
1. The Approximation Formula (Weighted Average method)
2. The YTM method (up to two decimal places)

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm has an outstanding perpetual bond with annual interest of ₹80 per bond and current market price of ₹1,000. If the tax rate is 25%, compute the after-tax cost of debt. Briefly explain why the cost of perpetual debt is theoretically simpler to compute than redeemable debt.

**(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Approximation Formula Method

**Given:**
- Face Value (FV) = ₹500
- Number of debentures = 2,000 (irrelevant for per-unit calculation)
- Discount = 4% → Issue Price **P₀** = ₹500 × 0.96 = **₹480**
- Redemption premium = 5% → Redemption Value **F** = ₹500 × 1.05 = **₹525**
- Annual Interest **I** = 11% × ₹500 = **₹55**
- Maturity **n** = 6 years
- Tax rate **t** = 30%

**Step 1:** Compute capital gain per year:

$$\frac{F - P_0}{n} = \frac{525 - 480}{6} = \frac{45}{6} = ₹7.50$$

**Step 2:** Compute average annual earnings:

$$I + \frac{F - P_0}{n} = 55 + 7.50 = ₹62.50$$

**Step 3:** Compute average investment:

$$\frac{F + P_0}{2} = \frac{525 + 480}{2} = \frac{1005}{2} = ₹502.50$$

**Step 4:** Compute before-tax cost:

$$K_d^{BT} = \frac{62.50}{502.50} = 0.1244 = 12.44\%$$

**Step 5:** Apply tax shield:

$$K_d^{AT} = 12.44\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 12.44\% \times 0.70 = 8.71\%$$

**Answer (a.1):** $K_d^{BT} = 12.44\%$, $K_d^{AT} = 8.71\%$

---

#### Part (a) — YTM Method

**Equation:**
$$480 = \sum_{t=1}^{6} \frac{55}{(1+K_d)^t} + \frac{525}{(1+K_d)^6}$$

**Trial 1: Kd = 13%**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(13\%, 6) &= \frac{1 - (1.13)^{-6}}{0.13} = \frac{1 - 0.4803}{0.13} = 3.9975 \\
\text{PVIF}(13\%, 6) &= (1.13)^{-6} = 0.4803 \\
\text{PV} &= 55 \times 3.9975 + 525 \times 0.4803 \\
&= 219.86 + 252.16 = 472.02
\end{aligned}$$

PV (₹472.02) < Issue price (₹480) → YTM < 13%

**Trial 2: Kd = 12%**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(12\%, 6) &= \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-6}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.5066}{0.12} = 4.1114 \\
\text{PVIF}(12\%, 6) &= (1.12)^{-6} = 0.5066 \\
\text{PV} &= 55 \times 4.1114 + 525 \times 0.5066 \\
&= 226.13 + 265.97 = 492.10
\end{aligned}$$

PV (₹492.10) > Issue price (₹480) → YTM lies between 12% and 13%

**Interpolation:**
$$K_d = 12\% + \frac{492.10 - 480.00}{492.10 - 472.02} \times (13\% - 12\%)$$

$$K_d = 12\% + \frac{12.10}{20.08} \times 1\% = 12\% + 0.6026\% = 12.60\%$$

**Apply tax shield:**

$$K_d^{AT} = 12.60\% \times 0.70 = 8.82\%$$

**Answer (a.2):** $K_d^{BT} \approx 12.60\%$, $K_d^{AT} \approx 8.82\%$

---

#### Part (b) — Perpetual Debt

**Step 1:** Compute before-tax cost:

$$K_d^{BT} = \frac{I}{P_0} = \frac{80}{1000} = 0.08 = 8\%$$

**Step 2:** Apply tax shield:

$$K_d^{AT} = 8\% \times (1 - 0.25) = 8\% \times 0.75 = 6\%$$

**Answer (b):** After-tax cost of perpetual debt = **6%**

**Theoretical Explanation:**

The cost of perpetual debt is theoretically simpler to compute than redeemable debt for the following reasons:

1. **Single Cash Flow Type:** Only interest is paid — no redemption principal to value. This eliminates the need to consider the timing and magnitude of the final payment.
2. **Closed-Form Solution:** The formula $K_d = I/P_0$ is derived from a simple geometric series and yields a direct answer — no trial-and-error or interpolation is needed.
3. **No Maturity Risk:** Perpetual bonds eliminate reinvestment risk and rollover uncertainty.
4. **No Premium/Discount Amortization:** Since the bond is never redeemed, there is no premium or discount amortization to calculate — the entire cost is just the periodic interest.
5. **Computational Efficiency:** The valuation involves only one variable (interest) rather than three (interest, redemption value, time to maturity).

**(7 marks = 3 marks numerical + 4 marks theoretical explanation)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** XYZ Ltd. issued 5,000 10% debentures of ₹100 each redeemable at par after 8 years. The current market price of each debenture is ₹92. The firm has a marginal tax rate of 35%. Calculate the cost of debt using the realized yield approach and explain the significance of the tax shield in reducing the effective cost of debt.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the four methods of computing cost of redeemable debt. Under what circumstances would each method be most appropriate?

**(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Realized Yield Method

**Given:**
- Face Value = ₹100
- Market Price **P₀** = ₹92
- Redemption Value **F** = ₹100 (at par)
- Annual Interest **I** = 10% × ₹100 = ₹10
- Maturity **n** = 8 years
- Tax rate **t** = 35%

**Step 1:** Compute annual amortization of discount:

$$\frac{F - P_0}{n} = \frac{100 - 92}{8} = \frac{8}{8} = ₹1$$

**Step 2:** Compute annual yield (using realized yield on net proceeds):

$$K_d^{BT} = \frac{I + (F - P_0)/n}{P_0} = \frac{10 + 1}{92} = \frac{11}{92} = 0.1196 = 11.96\%$$

**Step 3:** Apply tax shield:

$$K_d^{AT} = 11.96\% \times (1 - 0.35) = 11.96\% \times 0.65 = 7.77\%$$

**Answer (a):** $K_d^{BT} = 11.96\%$, $K_d^{AT} = 7.77\%$

**Significance of Tax Shield (4 marks):**

The tax shield is significant because:
1. **Legal Reduction in Tax Liability:** Interest on debt is fully deductible from taxable income, directly reducing the firm's corporate tax outflow.
2. **Real Economic Savings:** A ₹100 interest payment at 35% tax rate actually costs only ₹65 to the firm — a saving of ₹35 per ₹100 of interest.
3. **Source of Value Creation:** The tax shield is one of the primary reasons debt financing is preferred over equity financing, all else being equal.
4. **Magnitude:** For high-tax-rate firms, the after-tax cost of debt can be 25%–40% lower than the before-tax cost, dramatically reducing WACC.
5. **Permanent Benefit:** As long as the firm has taxable profits, the tax shield is a recurring annual benefit throughout the debt's life.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis of Four Methods (7 marks)

| Method | Formula | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **YTM / IRR** | $P_0 = \sum I/(1+K_d)^t + F/(1+K_d)^n$ | Most accurate; time-value-of-money compliant | Requires trial-and-error or financial calculator | Market valuation, new bond issues, investment decisions |
| **Approximation 1 (Weighted Average)** | $K_d = [I + (F-P_0)/n] / [(F+P_0)/2]$ | Smooths premium/discount; conceptually sound average | Assumes linear amortization; less accurate for large premiums | When bond is issued at substantial premium/discount |
| **Approximation 2 (Issue Price)** | $K_d = [I + (F-P_0)/n] / P_0$ | Simple; uses actual proceeds as denominator | Overstates cost when issued at discount | Quick estimates; standard coupon bonds |
| **Realized Yield** | $K_d = [I + (F-NV)/n] / NV$ | Uses historical book values; useful for ex-post analysis | Ignores current market prices; backward-looking | Past performance review; covenant compliance |

**Circumstantial Selection:**
- **Fresh issue analysis:** YTM or Approximation 1
- **Existing bond trading:** YTM (mandatory)
- **Quick textbook computation:** Approximation 2
- **Historical/Book value analysis:** Realized Yield

**(7 marks = 4 marks tabular comparison + 3 marks circumstance explanation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students LOSE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the tax shield:** Many students compute only the before-tax cost and stop. **Always** present both $K_d^{BT}$ and $K_d^{AT}$ unless the question explicitly says otherwise. A 14-mark question typically allocates 2–3 marks specifically for tax adjustment.
>
> 2. **Confusing P₀ and F:** Issue price ≠ Redemption value. When issued at a discount or premium, these differ significantly. Misidentifying them yields wrong numerators in the approximation formula.
>
> 3. **Wrong denominator in Approximation Method 2:** Using face value (₹100) instead of issue price (₹92) as the denominator artificially LOWERS the cost. Always use **P₀** as the denominator for Method 2.
>
> 4. **Skipping interpolation steps in YTM:** The KTU examiner wants to see *both* trial attempts with full PV calculations before interpolation. Skipping these steps loses 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the "times 2,000 debentures" trap:** Per-unit cost is what matters. The number of debentures is irrelevant for Kd unless the question asks for total interest.
>
> 6. **Mixing perpetual and redeemable formulas:** If the question says "redeemable after 8 years," DO NOT use $K_d = I/P_0$. This single error can wipe out the entire 7-mark sub-part.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cost of Debt**

- ✅ **Definition:** Kd is the effective interest rate paid on borrowed capital, with interest being **tax-deductible**.
- ✅ **Core Formula (Perpetual):** $K_d = I / P_0$
- ✅ **Core Formula (Redeemable Approx.):** $K_d = \dfrac{I + (F-P_0)/n}{(F+P_0)/2}$
- ✅ **Tax Shield Formula:** $K_d^{AT} = K_d^{BT} \times (1 - t)$
- ✅ **YTM Equation:** $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} I/(1+K_d)^t + F/(1+K_d)^n$
- ✅ **Always** compute BOTH before-tax AND after-tax costs.
- ✅ **Issue Price ≠ Face Value ≠ Redemption Value** — read the question carefully.
- ✅ **YTM method** is the most accurate; requires trial-and-error + interpolation.
- ✅ **Approximation method** is faster but less precise; preferred for standard textbook problems.
- ✅ The **tax shield** makes debt cheaper than equity on an after-tax basis.
- ✅ **Marginal tax rate** (not average) should be used.
- ✅ WACC = Weighted combination of $K_d^{AT}$, $K_e$, and $K_p$ — cost of debt feeds directly into WACC.
- ✅ Remember the **bond valuation table values**: PVIFA and PVIF for interpolation.
- ✅ For 14-mark numerical questions, show **all 5 steps**: identify variables → set up formula → substitute → compute before-tax → apply tax shield.
