topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preferred Stock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Preferred Stock** (also called *Preference Shares*) is a hybrid security that combines features of both equity and debt. It represents ownership in a corporation but carries no voting rights (typically). Holders of preferred stock are entitled to receive a **fixed dividend**, paid out before any dividends are distributed to common stockholders. In the event of liquidation, preferred stockholders have a **higher claim on assets** than common shareholders, though they rank below bondholders (debt holders).

From a corporate finance perspective, preferred stock is classified as part of the **equity capital** of a firm, yet it behaves much like a perpetuity (an infinite-life annuity) for valuation purposes because of its fixed contractual dividend.

### 1.1.1 Position in the Capital Structure

The order of claim in a company, from highest priority to lowest priority, is:

1. **Secured Creditors** (Banks, Bondholders)
2. **Unsecured Creditors**
3. **Preferred Stockholders** (Dividend in arrears + Principal)
4. **Common Stockholders** (Residual claim)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: The "VIP Investor Pass"**
> Imagine a theme park (the company) with two types of visitors:
> - **Common Stockholders** = Regular guests. They enjoy all rides, vote on park rules, but they get food coupons *only after* the park has paid all its staff, suppliers, and VIP guests.
> - **Preferred Stockholders** = VIP Pass holders. They don't vote on the roller-coaster design (no voting rights), but they are guaranteed a fixed snack allowance (dividend) every month **before** regular guests get anything. If the park cannot pay them in a bad month, the unpaid allowance **accumulates** and must be paid later before regular guests get anything.
> - **Bondholders** = The park's landlord. They are paid fixed rent **before** even the VIP guests.

This analogy makes it instantly clear: Preferred stock is a *middle ground* — safer than common stock, but riskier than bonds.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Preferred Stock

| # | Characteristic | Description |
|---|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Fixed Dividend** | A stated annual dividend (e.g., ₹8 per share) is paid at regular intervals. |
| 2 | **Preference in Dividends** | Paid **before** common stockholders. |
| 3 | **Preference in Liquidation** | Higher claim than equity on company assets. |
| 4 | **No Voting Rights** | Usually no say in corporate governance (unlike common stock). |
| 5 | **Perpetual Life** | Most preferred stocks have **no maturity date**. |
| 6 | **Cumulative Feature** | Unpaid dividends accumulate and are paid in arrears. |
| 7 | **Callability** | Issuer can buy back the stock at a pre-set call price. |
| 8 | **Convertibility** | Can be converted into a fixed number of common shares. |
| 9 | **Participating Feature** | Allows additional dividend above the fixed rate. |

## 1.4 Types of Preferred Stock

> [!NOTE] **KTU High-Yield Classification**

1. **Cumulative Preferred Stock** – Unpaid dividends accumulate and must be paid before common shareholders receive anything.
2. **Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock** – Unpaid dividends are *lost forever* if the firm skips a payment.
3. **Callable Preferred Stock** – The company can *retire* the shares at a predetermined call price.
4. **Convertible Preferred Stock** – Investors can *swap* their preferred shares for common shares.
5. **Participating Preferred Stock** – After receiving the fixed dividend, holders may share in extra profits with common shareholders.
6. **Adjustable-Rate (Floating-Rate) Preferred Stock** – Dividend varies with a benchmark like the T-Bill rate.
7. **Straight Preferred Stock** – Plain fixed dividend, no exotic features.

---

**End of Section 1 — Conceptual Foundation Established**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Preferred Stock: The Need for Hybrid Capital

Companies need capital. They have three broad sources:
- **Debt** (Loans, Bonds) → Cheap but risky for the company; legally mandatory payments.
- **Common Equity** → Expensive (high required return) but flexible.
- **Preferred Stock** → A middle path: more flexible than debt (dividends are not legally mandatory in most jurisdictions), but less risky than common equity (fixed dividend, priority claim).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Insight for KTU**
> Preferred stock is essentially a **perpetual dividend-paying instrument** from the perspective of valuation. This is why its valuation formula mirrors the perpetuity formula from time value of money.

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis: Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1 — Understanding the Cash Flow Stream
- A preferred stockholder receives a **constant periodic dividend (D)** at fixed intervals (quarterly/annually).
- The stream continues **indefinitely** (perpetuity), as most preferred stocks have no maturity.
- The valuation thus reduces to finding the **present value of a perpetuity**.

### Step 2 — Applying the Perpetuity Principle
The present value of a perpetuity is given by the general formula:

$$PV = \frac{C}{r}$$

Where $C$ is the constant periodic cash flow and $r$ is the discount rate (required rate of return).

### Step 3 — Mapping to Preferred Stock
- $C$ → Annual Preferred Dividend ($D_p$)
- $r$ → Required Rate of Return on Preferred Stock ($r_p$)
- $PV$ → Current Market Price of Preferred Stock ($P_p$)

This yields the master valuation equation:

$$P_p = \frac{D_p}{r_p}$$

### Step 4 — Re-arranging for Yield
If the price and dividend are known, the required rate of return (cost of preferred stock) is:

$$r_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p}$$

This is also called the **dividend yield** of the preferred stock.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield Formula Reference — Master This Table**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Price of Preferred Stock** | $P_p = \dfrac{D_p}{r_p}$ | $D_p$ = Annual Dividend, $r_p$ = Required Return | Currency per share; Return as decimal |
| 2 | **Cost of Preferred Stock (Yield)** | $r_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_p}$ | Same as above | Expressed as **%** in answers |
| 3 | **Net Proceeds Price** | $P_n = P_p - F$ | $F$ = Flotation Cost per share | Used for *flotation-adjusted* cost |
| 4 | **Flotation-Adjusted Cost** | $r_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_p - F}$ | $P_p$ = Issue Price, $F$ = Flotation Cost | More accurate for new issues |
| 5 | **Cumulative Dividend in Arrears** | $\text{Arrears} = D_p \times n$ | $n$ = Number of missed years | Must be cleared before common dividends |
| 6 | **Current Yield (Bond-style)** | $CY = \dfrac{D_p}{P_0}$ | $P_0$ = Current Market Price | Always positive for non-defaulted stock |
| 7 | **Convertible Stock Value** | $\text{Conversion Value} = CR \times P_c$ | $CR$ = Conversion Ratio, $P_c$ = Common Stock Price | Holder compares with $P_p$ |

## 2.4 Engineering & Finance Utility

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Utility**
> - **Corporate Issuers** use preferred stock to fund expansion without diluting voting control (since preferred shares usually have no voting rights).
> - **Banks and Regulated Utilities** issue preferred stock because it qualifies as **Tier-1 Capital** under Basel norms.
> - **Investors** use preferred stock for stable income streams, similar to bonds, but with potentially tax-advantaged dividend treatment (depending on jurisdiction).
> - In **Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A)**, convertible preferred stock is often used as acquisition currency.

---

**End of Section 2 — Theoretical & Formula Foundation Built**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation: The Preferred Stock Valuation Model

We will derive the formula $P_p = D_p / r_p$ rigorously from the **Perpetuity Discounting Principle**.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let a preferred stock pay a constant annual dividend $D_p$ forever. Let the required rate of return of investors be $r_p$ (per year, constant). Let $P_0$ be the current market price.

### 3.1.2 Cash Flow Representation

The expected cash flows to the holder at the end of years $1, 2, 3, \ldots, \infty$ are:

$$CF_1 = D_p, \quad CF_2 = D_p, \quad CF_3 = D_p, \quad \ldots, \quad CF_t = D_p \ \forall \ t \geq 1$$

### 3.1.3 Present Value of Each Cash Flow

Each future dividend is discounted back to the present at rate $r_p$:

$$PV_1 = \frac{D_p}{(1+r_p)^1}, \quad PV_2 = \frac{D_p}{(1+r_p)^2}, \quad \ldots, \quad PV_t = \frac{D_p}{(1+r_p)^t}$$

### 3.1.4 Summing to Infinity

The total price is the infinite sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_p}{(1+r_p)^t} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{D_p}{1+r_p} + \frac{D_p}{(1+r_p)^2} + \frac{D_p}{(1+r_p)^3} + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Applying the Geometric Series Formula

For a geometric series with first term $a$ and common ratio $R$ (where $\vert R \vert < 1$), the infinite sum is $S = a / (1 - R)$.

- Here, $a = \dfrac{D_p}{1+r_p}$ and $R = \dfrac{1}{1+r_p}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= \frac{\dfrac{D_p}{1+r_p}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r_p}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{D_p}{1+r_p}}{\dfrac{(1+r_p) - 1}{1+r_p}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{D_p}{1+r_p}}{\dfrac{r_p}{1+r_p}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{D_p}{r_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Result: $P_0 = D_p / r_p$** — The market price of a preferred stock equals its annual dividend divided by the required rate of return.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cost of Preferred Stock (With Flotation Costs)

When a company issues *new* preferred stock, it incurs **flotation costs** (underwriting fees, legal fees, registration). The net proceeds per share are:

$$\text{Net Proceeds} = P_p - F$$

Where $F$ is the flotation cost per share. The cost of capital, adjusted for these costs, is derived by recognizing that investors still require the dividend $D_p$ on a *smaller* effective investment.

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_p &= \frac{D_p}{P_p - F} \\[4pt]
\text{or equivalently,} \quad r_p &= \frac{D_p}{P_p(1 - f)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $f$ is the flotation cost expressed as a **percentage** of the issue price.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1: Basic Valuation

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Typical 7-Mark Problem**
> A company's preferred stock pays an annual dividend of **₹10 per share**. An investor requires a return of **12%** on this stock. What is the maximum price the investor should pay?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_p &= \frac{D_p}{r_p} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{10}{0.12} \\[4pt]
&= ₹83.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** At a price of ₹83.33, the investor earns exactly 12%. If the market price is *lower*, the investor earns *more* than 12% (a good buy). If *higher*, the investor earns *less* than 12% (avoid).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2: Cost of Preferred Stock with Flotation

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Typical 7-Mark Problem**
> A company issues preferred stock with a face value of ₹100, an annual dividend of **₹9**, and a market price of ₹90. Flotation costs are **₹5 per share**. Compute the cost of preferred stock.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_p &= \frac{D_p}{P_p - F} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{9}{90 - 5} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{9}{85} \\[4pt]
&= 0.1059 \\[4pt]
&= 10.59\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3: Cumulative Dividends in Arrears

A company has **not paid dividends for 3 years** on its 8% cumulative preferred stock (face value ₹100). In Year 4, the board declares a total dividend of ₹50 lakh on 1 lakh preferred shares.

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Dividend per Share:**

$$D_p = 8\% \times 100 = ₹8$$

**Step 2 — Compute Arrears (3 years):**

$$\text{Arrears} = 8 \times 3 = ₹24 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 3 — Total Claim per Share in Year 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 4 Dividend} &= ₹8 \\[2pt]
\text{Arrears} &= ₹24 \\[2pt]
\text{Total Preferred Claim} &= ₹32 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Preferred Dividend Pool:**

$$1,00,000 \times 32 = ₹32,00,000$$

**Step 5 — Common Stockholders Receive:**

$$50,00,000 - 32,00,000 = ₹18,00,000$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table

> [!NOTE] **Master Comparison — Frequently Asked in KTU**

| Feature | Common Stock | Preferred Stock | Bonds (Debt) |
|---------|--------------|------------------|--------------|
| Ownership | Yes | Yes (technically) | No (creditor) |
| Voting Rights | Yes | Usually No | No |
| Dividend / Interest | Variable, not guaranteed | Fixed, not legally mandatory | Fixed, **legally mandatory** |
| Tax-Deductibility of Payments | No (dividend) | No (dividend) | Yes (interest) |
| Priority in Liquidation | Lowest | Middle | Highest |
| Maturity | None | Usually None | Yes (fixed) |
| Convertibility | N/A | Often convertible | Sometimes convertible |

---

**End of Section 3 — Derivations & Worked Examples Complete**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capital Structure Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This Mermaid diagram maps the **claim priority** and **cash-flow direction** in a corporation's capital structure, showing where preferred stock sits between debt and common equity.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Secured Creditors and Bondholders"]:::top
    nodeB["Unsecured Creditors"]:::layer2
    nodeC["Preferred Stockholders Fixed Dividend and Principal Priority"]:::layer3
    nodeD["Common Stockholders Residual Claim and Voting Rights"]:::layer4
    nodeE["Retained Earnings Internal Accruals"]:::layer5

    nodeA -->|"Paid First"| nodeB
    nodeB -->|"Paid Second"| nodeC
    nodeC -->|"Paid Third Dividend Before Common"| nodeD
    nodeD -->|"Residual Profits"| nodeE

    classDef top fill="#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff"
    classDef layer2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef layer3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer5 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Preferred Stock Feature Decision Tree

This diagram maps the **classification logic** of different preferred stock variants based on their features.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Preferred Stock Base Security"]
    nodeCum{"Unpaid Dividends Accumulate?"}
    nodeCumYes["Cumulative Preferred Stock"]
    nodeCumNo["Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock"]
    nodeCall{"Issuer Can Buy Back?"}
    nodeCallYes["Callable Preferred Stock"]
    nodeCallNo["Non-Callable Preferred Stock"]
    nodeConv{"Convertible to Common?"}
    nodeConvYes["Convertible Preferred Stock"]
    nodeConvNo["Straight Non-Convertible Preferred Stock"]
    nodePart{"Share Extra Profits?"}
    nodePartYes["Participating Preferred Stock"]
    nodePartNo["Non-Participating Straight"]

    nodeStart --> nodeCum
    nodeCum -->|Yes| nodeCumYes
    nodeCum -->|No| nodeCumNo
    nodeStart --> nodeCall
    nodeCall -->|Yes| nodeCallYes
    nodeCall -->|No| nodeCallNo
    nodeStart --> nodeConv
    nodeConv -->|Yes| nodeConvYes
    nodeConv -->|No| nodeConvNo
    nodeStart --> nodePart
    nodePart -->|Yes| nodePartYes
    nodePart -->|No| nodePartNo
```

## 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline for a Perpetual Preferred Stock

This block diagram illustrates the infinite, uniform cash flow stream received by a preferred stockholder, justifying the perpetuity valuation model.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["Time 0 Price P_p Paid Today"]:::outflow
    t1["Year 1 Dividend D_p"]:::inflow
    t2["Year 2 Dividend D_p"]:::inflow
    t3["Year 3 Dividend D_p"]:::inflow
    t4["Year 4 Dividend D_p"]:::inflow
    tInf["Year Infinity D_p Continues Forever"]:::inflow

    t0 -->|Invest| t1
    t1 --> t2
    t2 --> t3
    t3 --> t4
    t4 --> tInf

    classDef inflow fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outflow fill:#f4b084,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**End of Section 4 — Visual Schematics Mapped**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Preferred Stock and list any four of its key features.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Preferred stock is a hybrid security that combines features of both equity and debt. It represents a form of ownership in a company but typically carries no voting rights. Holders are entitled to a **fixed dividend** and have a **priority claim** over common stockholders for both dividends and asset distribution upon liquidation.

**Four Key Features (any four):**
1. **Fixed Dividend Rate** – The dividend amount is pre-determined at issuance.
2. **Priority in Dividend Payment** – Paid before common stockholders.
3. **Priority in Liquidation** – Higher claim on assets than common equity.
4. **No Voting Rights** – Usually excluded from corporate voting decisions.
5. **Cumulative Option** – Unpaid dividends can accumulate.
6. **Callability** – The company can buy back the shares at a call price.

*(Marks: 2 for definition + ½ × 4 = 2 for features, totaling 3 marks — no extra explanation required at this depth.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Cumulative and Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Cumulative Preferred Stock | Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock |
|-------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Arrears Treatment** | Unpaid dividends accumulate and must be paid in future years | Unpaid dividends are lost forever |
| **Risk to Investor** | Lower risk | Higher risk |
| **Common Stockholder Protection** | Common dividend is blocked until arrears are cleared | Common dividend may be paid even if preferred is skipped (subject to board) |
| **Investor Preference** | More popular in India and global markets | Less commonly issued |

*(Marks: 3 for clear distinction. Board expects at least 3 valid points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** A company has issued 10,000 9% cumulative preferred shares of ₹100 each. The company has not declared any dividend for the last 2 years due to losses. In the third year, the company earned a profit and the board declared a total dividend of ₹5,00,000. Show the distribution of dividends between preferred and common shareholders. State clearly the arrears cleared.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the cost of preferred stock? A firm issues preferred stock with a face value of ₹100, paying an annual dividend of ₹8, at a market price of ₹96. Flotation costs are 5% of the issue price. Calculate the cost of preferred stock.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Annual Dividend per Share:**

$$D_p = 9\% \times 100 = ₹9 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 2 — Arrears Calculation (2 years):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Arrears} &= D_p \times \text{Years Missed} \\[4pt]
&= 9 \times 2 = ₹18 \text{ per share in arrears}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Preferred Claim in Year 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Year Dividend} &= ₹9 \\
\text{Arrears} &= ₹18 \\
\text{Total Preferred Claim per Share} &= ₹27
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Dividend Pool for Preferred Shareholders:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Preferred Dividend} &= 10,000 \times 27 \\[4pt]
&= ₹2,70,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Common Stockholder Distribution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Declared} &= ₹5,00,000 \\
\text{Less: Preferred} &= ₹2,70,000 \\
\text{Common Stockholders Receive} &= ₹2,30,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Preferred shareholders receive ₹2,70,000 (clearing ₹1,80,000 in arrears and ₹90,000 current year). Common shareholders receive ₹2,30,000.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Flotation Cost per Share:**

$$F = 5\% \times 96 = ₹4.80$$

**Step 2 — Net Proceeds:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_p - F \\[4pt]
&= 96 - 4.80 = ₹91.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost of Preferred Stock Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_p &= \frac{D_p}{P_n} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8}{91.20} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0877 \\[4pt]
&= 8.77\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The cost of preferred stock is **8.77%**.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using the *market price* (₹96) directly in the denominator **without subtracting flotation cost** — leads to an incorrect 8.33% instead of 8.77%. **Always deduct flotation cost** for *new issue* problems.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In part (a), students often **forget to clear arrears** before paying current-year common dividend. The 2-year backlog **must be cleared first** for cumulative preferred stock.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **arrears per share** with total arrears on all shares. Multiply by the **number of preferred shares** at the end.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to express the final cost of capital as a **percentage** (multiplied by 100). Examiners deduct ½ mark for this.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain any four features of Preferred Stock. Why is it considered a *hybrid* security?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company has 50,000 8% cumulative preferred shares of ₹50 each. The dividend has been in arrears for 2 years. The current market price of the preferred share is ₹42. If the required rate of return of an investor is 14%, calculate the **value of the share** and decide whether it is a worthwhile investment.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A preferred stock is called a *hybrid* security because it borrows features from both **equity** and **debt**.

**Four Key Features:**

1. **Fixed Dividend (Debt-like):** Like a bond's coupon, the dividend is a pre-specified amount (e.g., 8% of face value), giving investors predictable income.

2. **Perpetual Life (Debt-like):** Most preferred stocks have no maturity date, behaving like perpetuity bonds from a valuation standpoint.

3. **No Voting Rights (Unlike Common Equity):** While technically an ownership instrument, preferred shareholders usually cannot vote on corporate matters, similar to bondholders.

4. **Priority Claim (Debt-like but not as strong):** Preferred shareholders are paid dividends **before** common shareholders and have a higher claim on assets in liquidation, but they are still **below bondholders**.

5. **Non-Mandatory Dividend (Equity-like):** Unlike bond interest, dividends are **not legally compulsory** — the company may skip them in lean years, behaving like equity.

6. **Callability & Convertibility:** Many preferred stocks can be called back or converted to common stock, making them flexible (debt-like optionality).

**Why Hybrid?**
- **Debt-like traits:** Fixed dividend, perpetual cash flow, priority over equity.
- **Equity-like traits:** Ownership, dividends not legally mandatory, ranks after creditors.

Hence, preferred stock is a true *hybrid* instrument, sitting between pure debt and pure equity.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Annual Dividend per Share:**

$$D_p = 8\% \times 50 = ₹4 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical (Fair) Value of the Share:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_p &= \frac{D_p}{r_p} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4}{0.14} \\[4pt]
&= ₹28.57
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with Market Price:**

- Theoretical Value = ₹28.57
- Market Price = ₹42.00

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Market Price} - \text{Theoretical Value} &= 42 - 28.57 \\[4pt]
&= ₹13.43 \text{ (Premium)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the market price (₹42) is **greater than** the fair value (₹28.57), the stock is **overvalued** at the given required return of 14%. An investor should **NOT buy** the share at ₹42 because it would yield less than 14% (specifically, only $4/42 = 9.52\%$).

> [!NOTE] **Conclusion:** The investor requires 14% but would actually earn 9.52% at the current price. Hence, the share is **not a worthwhile investment** at ₹42.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Question B)**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Many students quote the *fair value* and stop, ignoring the **investment decision** — KTU awards 2 marks specifically for the "should you buy" reasoning.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Do not confuse *arrears* (a separate concern) with the **current annual dividend** in the valuation formula — the perpetuity formula only uses the **current annual dividend**, not cumulative arrears.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Always state the decision in clear **YES/NO** form. Vague answers like "maybe" lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- **Definition:** Preferred stock = hybrid security = fixed dividend + priority over common + usually no voting.
- **Capital Structure Order (Top to Bottom):** Secured Creditors → Unsecured Creditors → **Preferred Stock** → Common Stock → Retained Earnings.
- **Master Formula:** $P_p = D_p / r_p$ — same as the **perpetuity** formula. Memorize it cold.
- **Cost of Preferred Stock:** $r_p = D_p / (P_p - F)$ when flotation costs are involved. Otherwise $r_p = D_p / P_p$.
- **Cumulative vs Non-Cumulative:** Cumulative = arrears accumulate. Non-cumulative = arrears are forfeited.
- **Callable:** Company can buy back; investor faces reinvestment risk.
- **Convertible:** Investor can convert to common stock; gains optionality.
- **Participating:** Holder gets fixed dividend *plus* a share of extra profits.
- **Valuation Rule of Thumb:** If market price < fair value → BUY. If market price > fair value → AVOID.
- **Cumulative Arrears Rule:** Always clear arrears *before* paying current-year common dividend.
- **Decision Rule in Numerical Problems:** Always end with a clear **YES/NO** buy recommendation and supporting yield calculation.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Don't confuse preferred stock dividends with bond interest — dividends are *not* tax-deductible for the issuing company, but bond interest *is*.
- **Exam Tip:** For 7-mark valuation problems, always show **3 steps** — Formula → Substitution → Final numerical answer with units (₹ or %).

---

**End of Section 5 — Question Bank & Recap Complete**
