topics.title: Common Equity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Common Equity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Common Equity** (also known as **Common Stock**, **Ordinary Shares**, or **Equity Capital**) is the most basic form of corporate ownership securities that represents a residual claim on a firm's assets and earnings. It is the permanent capital base of a company that carries no fixed dividend obligation, no maturity date, and grants the holder voting rights proportional to their shareholding.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard Text):**
> *Common equity is the ownership interest of common shareholders in a corporation. It represents the residual claim on income and assets after the claims of creditors and preferred shareholders have been satisfied. It is a permanent source of finance and forms the core risk-bearing capital of a firm.*

Mathematically, the **book value of common equity** is expressed as:

$$B_{ce} = \text{Total Assets} - \text{Total Liabilities} - \text{Preferred Stock Equity}$$

Where:
- $B_{ce}$ = Book value of common equity
- Total Liabilities include both current and long-term debt obligations
- Preferred stock equity is deducted first due to its prior claim

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Apartment Building Analogy:**
> Imagine a company as a 100-floor luxury apartment building. The **bondholders** are like the bank that gave the construction loan — they have the first right to receive monthly EMI payments and get their principal back first. The **preferred shareholders** are like the high-floor penthouse leaseholders — they get a fixed "rent" (dividend) before others. The **common shareholders** are like the **building owners** — they get whatever rent is left after everyone else is paid (residual income), they have voting rights in the owners' association meetings, and if the building value goes up, they enjoy capital appreciation. However, if the building burns down, they are the **last to be compensated** after the bank and leaseholders are fully paid.

This analogy crystallizes three core attributes:
1. **Residual Claim** — paid last in liquidation
2. **Voting Rights** — control over corporate decisions
3. **Unlimited Upside Potential** — participate in all growth

## 1.3 Key Characteristics (High-Yield Pointers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Characteristics (Memorize These):**
> - **Residual Claim on Earnings** — equity holders receive dividends only after bondholders and preferred shareholders are paid
> - **Residual Claim on Assets** — in liquidation, common shareholders are paid last after all creditors
> - **Voting Rights** — usually one vote per share for board elections and major corporate decisions
> - **Limited Liability** — maximum loss is limited to the amount invested (purchase price of shares)
> - **No Fixed Dividend** — dividends are not legally mandatory; declared at the discretion of the Board of Directors
> - **No Maturity Date** — equity is permanent capital; it never has to be "repaid" like debt
> - **Pre-emptive Rights** — existing shareholders have the first right to purchase new shares to maintain their proportional ownership (Anti-dilution clause)
> - **Right to Information** — access to annual reports, financial statements, and corporate disclosures

## 1.4 Authorized vs. Issued vs. Outstanding Shares

| Category | Definition | Trading Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Authorized Shares** | Maximum number of shares a company is legally permitted to issue (per its charter/Articles of Association) | Cannot trade |
| **Issued Shares** | Total shares that have been sold to investors (public + private + promoters) | May or may not trade |
| **Outstanding Shares** | Issued shares currently held by all shareholders **excluding treasury stock** (shares bought back by the company) | Actively trade in the market |
| **Treasury Stock** | Shares issued but later repurchased by the company; held in its own treasury | Does not trade / No voting rights / No dividends |

**Formula:**

$$N_{out} = N_{issued} - N_{treasury}$$

Where $N_{out}$ = Outstanding shares, $N_{issued}$ = Issued shares, $N_{treasury}$ = Treasury stock.

## 1.5 Forms of Common Equity

Common equity can be raised through several mechanisms:

1. **IPOs (Initial Public Offerings)** — first-time sale of shares to the public
2. **Rights Issues** — existing shareholders offered new shares at a discount
3. **Private Placements** — shares sold to a select group of institutional investors
4. **ESOPs / Stock Options** — shares issued to employees as compensation
5. **Bonus / Stock Splits** — additional shares issued from accumulated reserves

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Common Equity

### 2.1.1 The Three Pillars of Common Equity Theory

**Pillar 1 — The Net Present Value (NPV) Framework**
Common equity represents a perpetual claim on future cash flows. Its value is derived from the discounted value of expected future dividends and capital gains.

**Pillar 2 — The Risk-Return Tradeoff**
Since common equity is the most junior claim, it carries the **highest risk** and therefore demands the **highest required rate of return** among all capital sources.

**Pillar 3 — The Modigliani-Miller Theorem (MM Proposition I, with taxes)**
In a perfect market, the value of a firm is independent of its capital structure. However, with corporate taxes, debt creates a tax shield that influences the relative cost of common equity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Common Equity Exists in the Capital Structure:**
> - Provides a cushion for creditors (loss-absorbing capacity)
> - Aligns management incentives with shareholders (agency theory)
> - Avoids the rigid obligations of debt covenants
> - Provides permanent, non-redeemable capital

## 2.2 Cost of Common Equity (Cost of Equity Capital, $K_e$)

The **cost of common equity** is the rate of return required by the company's common shareholders. It is the **most critical input** in capital budgeting and valuation.

### 2.2.1 Three Primary Estimation Methods

**Method 1: Dividend Discount Model (DDM) / Gordon Growth Model**

Assumes dividends grow at a constant rate forever.

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

Where:
- $K_e$ = Cost of common equity (decimal)
- $D_1$ = Expected dividend at end of Year 1 ($= D_0 \times (1+g)$)
- $P_0$ = Current market price per share
- $g$ = Constant growth rate of dividends

**Method 2: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**

$$K_e = R_f + \beta_i \times (R_m - R_f)$$

Where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate (typically 10-year Government of India bond yield)
- $\beta_i$ = Beta of the stock (systematic risk)
- $R_m$ = Expected return on the market portfolio (e.g., Nifty 50)
- $(R_m - R_f)$ = Market risk premium

**Method 3: Bond Yield Plus Risk Premium Approach**

$$K_e = K_d + RP$$

Where:
- $K_d$ = Cost of debt (after-tax or pre-tax depending on context)
- $RP$ = Risk premium for common equity (typically **3% to 5%** for India)

## 2.3 Market Value, Book Value & Intrinsic Value

| Value Type | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Book Value per Share (BVPS)** | $BVPS = \frac{\text{Common Stockholders' Equity}}{N_{out}}$ | Accounting value; per-share net assets |
| **Market Value per Share** | Equals current trading price $P_0$ | Determined by market forces (supply/demand) |
| **Intrinsic Value** | $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_t}{(1+K_e)^t}$ | The "true" economic worth of a share |
| **Market Capitalization** | $MC = P_0 \times N_{out}$ | Total market value of common equity |
| **Market-to-Book Ratio** | $M/B = \frac{MC}{\text{Book Value of Common Equity}}$ | Indicates growth/intangible value |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Book Value of Common Equity | $B_{ce} = \text{TA} - \text{TL} - \text{PS}$ | Currency units (₹, $, etc.) |
| 2 | Outstanding Shares | $N_{out} = N_{issued} - N_{treasury}$ | Number of shares |
| 3 | Book Value per Share | $BVPS = \frac{B_{ce}}{N_{out}}$ | Currency per share |
| 4 | Market Capitalization | $MC = P_0 \times N_{out}$ | Currency units |
| 5 | Market-to-Book Ratio | $M/B = \frac{P_0 \times N_{out}}{B_{ce}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 6 | Cost of Equity (DDM) | $K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | Decimal or % |
| 7 | Cost of Equity (CAPM) | $K_e = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f)$ | Decimal or % |
| 8 | Cost of Equity (Risk Premium) | $K_e = K_d + RP$ | Decimal or % |
| 9 | Required Return (CAPM) | $r_i = R_f + \beta_i \times MRP$ | Decimal or % |
| 10 | EPS | $EPS = \frac{\text{Net Income} - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{N_{out}}$ | Currency per share |
| 11 | P/E Ratio | $P/E = \frac{P_0}{EPS}$ | Times (multiple) |
| 12 | Dividend Payout Ratio | $b = \frac{DPS}{EPS}$ | Decimal (0 to 1) |
| 13 | Retention Ratio | $r = 1 - b$ | Decimal (0 to 1) |
| 14 | Sustainable Growth Rate | $g = r \times ROE$ | Decimal or % |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility & Engineering Applications

While common equity is a finance concept, it is **critically applied in**:
- **Start-up Valuation** — venture capital firms use equity-based NPV models
- **Infrastructure Project Finance** — PPP (Public-Private Partnership) models rely on equity IRR calculations
- **Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A)** — stock-for-stock swaps are valued using equity models
- **Employee Stock Ownership Plans (ESOPs)** in tech and engineering firms
- **Corporate Capital Budgeting** — every engineering project's NPV is discounted at $K_e$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Connection:** As future engineers, you may found a company, raise Series A/B venture capital, or sit on a board evaluating capex. Understanding cost of common equity is essential for **accept-reject decisions on long-gestation projects** (e.g., a new semiconductor fab, hydroelectric dam, or satellite launch system).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cost of Common Equity using the Gordon Growth Model (DDM)

### 3.1.1 Starting Premise
The price of a share today equals the present value of all future dividends, assuming a constant growth rate $g$ and discount rate $K_e$.

### 3.1.2 Full Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the perpetuity with growth:**

The intrinsic value of a share is the present value of a growing perpetuity:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots + \frac{D_\infty}{(1+K_e)^\infty}$$

**Step 2 — Assume constant growth $g$:**

If $D_t = D_{t-1} \times (1+g)$, then $D_2 = D_1(1+g)$, $D_3 = D_1(1+g)^2$, and so on.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)} + \frac{D_1(1+g)}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_1(1+g)^2}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots \\
P_0 &= \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_1(1+g)^{t-1}}{(1+K_e)^t} \\
P_0 &= \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)} \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1+g}{1+K_e}\right)^{t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the geometric series formula:**

For $\vert \frac{1+g}{1+K_e} \vert < 1$, the sum converges to:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} x^{t} = \frac{1}{1-x} \quad \text{where } x = \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)} \times \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}} \\
P_0 &= \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)} \times \frac{(1+K_e)}{(1+K_e) - (1+g)} \\
P_0 &= \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $K_e$ (the cost of common equity):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_0 \times (K_e - g) &= D_1 \\
K_e - g &= \frac{D_1}{P_0} \\
K_e &= \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Condition:** This derivation holds ONLY when $K_e > g$. If $g \geq K_e$, the model breaks down (denominator becomes zero or negative).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost of Common Equity using CAPM

### 3.2.1 CAPM Assumptions
- Investors are rational and risk-averse
- Single-period investment horizon
- Homogeneous expectations
- No taxes or transaction costs
- Many small investors (price-takers)

### 3.2.2 Logical Flow

**Step 1 — Decompose total risk:**

Any stock's total risk = Systematic risk (market) + Unsystematic risk (firm-specific).

**Step 2 — By diversification, unsystematic risk is eliminated**, leaving only systematic risk, measured by beta ($\beta$).

**Step 3 — Build the Security Market Line (SML):**

$$K_e = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f)$$

**Step 4 — Derive $\beta$ from regression:**

$$\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$$

Where:
- $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)$ = Covariance of stock $i$'s returns with the market
- $\text{Var}(R_m)$ = Variance of market returns

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation of Beta:**
> - $\beta = 1.0$ → Stock moves exactly with the market
> - $\beta > 1.0$ → Aggressive stock (more volatile than market)
> - $\beta < 1.0$ → Defensive stock (less volatile than market)
> - $\beta < 0$ → Inverse relationship (rare; e.g., gold stocks)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Question:** A company has the following data:
- Current market price per share ($P_0$): ₹120
- Current annual dividend ($D_0$): ₹6
- Expected constant growth rate ($g$): 8% per annum
- Risk-free rate ($R_f$): 7%
- Beta ($\beta$): 1.2
- Expected market return ($R_m$): 14%

**Find:** The cost of common equity using (a) DDM and (b) CAPM.

### 3.3.1 Solution (a) — DDM Method

**Step 1:** Calculate expected next-year dividend $D_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= D_0 \times (1+g) \\
D_1 &= 6 \times (1 + 0.08) \\
D_1 &= 6 \times 1.08 \\
D_1 &= 6.48
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the DDM formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_e^{DDM} &= \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g \\
K_e^{DDM} &= \frac{6.48}{120} + 0.08 \\
K_e^{DDM} &= 0.054 + 0.08 \\
K_e^{DDM} &= 0.134 \text{ or } 13.4\%
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.2 Solution (b) — CAPM Method

**Step 1:** Calculate Market Risk Premium (MRP):

$$MRP = R_m - R_f = 14\% - 7\% = 7\%$$

**Step 2:** Apply the CAPM formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_e^{CAPM} &= R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f) \\
K_e^{CAPM} &= 7\% + 1.2 \times 7\% \\
K_e^{CAPM} &= 7\% + 8.4\% \\
K_e^{CAPM} &= 15.4\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The two methods give different values because DDM relies on actual dividend behavior, while CAPM is based on market risk perception. In KTU answers, **always show both methods** and briefly justify which is more appropriate for the given firm.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Method?

| Method | Best Suited For | Limitations |
|---|---|---|
| **DDM (Gordon Growth)** | Mature, stable firms paying regular dividends | Useless for non-dividend-paying firms; sensitive to $g$ |
| **CAPM** | All firms; works with market data | Beta is unstable; assumes market efficiency |
| **Bond + Risk Premium** | Private firms without public beta | Risk premium is judgmental (3-5%) |
| **Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)** | Multi-factor risk environments | Complex to estimate; rarely asked in KTU |

## 3.5 Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR) — Detailed Derivation

SGR is the maximum growth rate achievable **without external equity financing**, given constant financial policies.

**Step 1 — Start with the standard growth formula:**

$$g = ROE \times \text{Retention Ratio}$$

**Step 2 — Expand ROE (DuPont Identity):**

$$ROE = \frac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Common Equity}} = \frac{NI}{Sales} \times \frac{Sales}{Assets} \times \frac{Assets}{Equity}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute retention ratio $r = (1 - \text{Payout})$:**

$$g = r \times ROE = (1 - b) \times \frac{NI}{E_{ce}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:**

If ROE = 18% and dividend payout = 40%, then retention $r = 60\%$:

$$g = 0.60 \times 0.18 = 0.108 = 10.8\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map of Common Equity

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the structural hierarchy and key components of common equity within a firm's capital structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FIRM CAPITAL STRUCTURE"] --> B["DEBT CAPITAL"]
    A --> C["PREFERRED EQUITY"]
    A --> D["COMMON EQUITY"]

    B --> B1["Senior Secured Debt"]
    B --> B2["Unsecured Debentures"]
    B --> B3["Convertible Bonds"]

    C --> C1["Cumulative Preference Shares"]
    C --> C2["Redeemable Preference Shares"]
    C --> C3["Participating Preference Shares"]

    D --> D1["Authorized Capital"]
    D --> D2["Issued Capital"]
    D --> D3["Outstanding Capital"]

    D3 --> D4["Promoter Holdings"]
    D3 --> D5["Institutional Investors"]
    D3 --> D6["Retail Public Shareholders"]
    D3 --> D7["ESOP Pool"]

    D1 -.->|less than or equal| D2
    D2 -.->|less than or equal| D1
    D2 -->|minus Treasury| D3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The arrows show the relationship "is composed of"
> - The dashed arrows show the inclusion constraints (Issued ≤ Authorized, Outstanding = Issued − Treasury)
> - The claim priority is top-down: Debt > Preference > Common

## 4.2 Process Flow — Cost of Common Equity Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["START: Select Estimation Method"] --> S2{"Does the firm pay regular dividends?"}
    S2 -->|YES| S3["Use Gordon Growth DDM"]
    S2 -->|NO| S4{"Is the firm publicly listed with computable beta?"}
    S4 -->|YES| S5["Use CAPM"]
    S4 -->|NO| S6["Use Bond Yield + Risk Premium"]

    S3 --> S7["Ke = D1 by P0 plus g"]
    S5 --> S8["Ke = Rf plus Beta times MRP"]
    S6 --> S9["Ke = Kd plus RP"]

    S7 --> S10["Apply in WACC and NPV calculations"]
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11["END: Capital Budgeting Decision"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Sources of Common Equity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC["SOURCES OF COMMON EQUITY"] --> SRC1["Internal Sources"]
    SRC --> SRC2["External Sources"]

    SRC1 --> I1["Retained Earnings"]
    SRC1 --> I2["Reserves and Surplus Conversion"]

    SRC2 --> E1["Initial Public Offer IPO"]
    SRC2 --> E2["Follow-on Public Offer FPO"]
    SRC2 --> E3["Rights Issue to Existing Shareholders"]
    SRC2 --> E4["Private Placement QIBs HNIs"]
    SRC2 --> E5["ADR GDR for Foreign Capital"]
    SRC2 --> E6["ESOP and Sweat Equity"]
```

## 4.4 Risk-Return Position of Common Equity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LO["LOW RISK LOW RETURN"] --> SEC1["Government Bonds"]
    SEC1 --> SEC2["Corporate Bonds Senior Debt"]
    SEC2 --> SEC3["Subordinated Debt"]
    SEC3 --> SEC4["Preferred Stock"]
    SEC4 --> SEC5["COMMON EQUITY HIGHEST RISK HIGHEST RETURN"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Diagram Matters for KTU:**
> Examiners often ask "Why is the cost of common equity the highest?" The answer lies in the risk-return hierarchy shown above. Common equity holders bear the **maximum residual risk** and therefore demand the **highest required return**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines, Part A questions test direct recall, definitions, and short conceptual clarity. Answer length: 3-5 sentences (or 1-2 short paragraphs). Marks awarded for **accuracy, completeness, and terminology**.

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Similar to Past Pattern]**

**"Define Common Equity. List any four distinguishing features of common stock."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Common equity is the ownership capital of a company representing the residual claim of common shareholders on the firm's assets and earnings after all creditors and preference shareholders have been paid. It is a permanent source of finance that carries no fixed dividend obligation and grants voting rights proportionate to shareholding.

**Four Distinguishing Features:**
1. **Residual Claim on Earnings and Assets** — Common shareholders are paid only after debt and preference shareholders receive their dues.
2. **Voting Rights** — Each share typically carries one vote, enabling shareholders to influence corporate decisions including election of the board.
3. **Limited Liability** — The maximum loss of a shareholder is restricted to the amount invested in purchasing the shares.
4. **No Fixed Maturity or Mandatory Dividend** — Equity is perpetual capital; dividends are declared at the discretion of the Board of Directors and are not legally binding obligations.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**"Distinguish between Authorized Capital, Issued Capital, and Outstanding Capital with a suitable example."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Authorized Capital | Issued Capital | Outstanding Capital |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Maximum shares a company can issue per its Memorandum of Association | Shares actually sold to investors | Issued shares excluding treasury stock |
| **Example** | If a company's AoA permits 10,00,000 shares, that is authorized | If 7,00,000 are sold, issued = 7,00,000 | If 50,000 are bought back as treasury, outstanding = 6,50,000 |
| **Flexibility** | Requires shareholder resolution to alter | Set by the Board | Reduces on buyback |

**Numerical Example:** A company XYZ Ltd. is authorized to issue 10 lakh shares. It issues 7 lakh shares to the public and promoters. Later, it buys back 50,000 shares as treasury stock. Then, authorized = 10,00,000; issued = 7,00,000; outstanding = 6,50,000.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice within the module).

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Internal Choice | Adapted Past Pattern]**

**"Discuss in detail the various methods of estimating the cost of common equity. Illustrate with examples. State the relative merits and limitations of each method."** (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Discuss the theoretical basis and formulae of all major methods (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The cost of common equity ($K_e$) is the minimum rate of return that a company must earn on its equity-financed investments to maintain its market value. It is the **most important** component in the weighted average cost of capital (WACC) and represents the opportunity cost for shareholders. There are three primary methods of estimation:

**Method 1: Dividend Discount Model (DDM) / Gordon Growth Model**

Theoretically, the intrinsic value of a share equals the present value of all future dividends. Assuming a constant growth rate $g$:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

**Method 2: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**

Based on the principle that investors are compensated for time value of money and systematic risk:

$$K_e = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f)$$

**Method 3: Bond Yield Plus Risk Premium Approach**

$$K_e = K_d + RP$$

Where $RP$ is the equity risk premium over debt cost.

**Method 4: Earnings Yield Approach (Newer / Less Common)**

$$K_e = \frac{EPS}{P_0}$$

**Method 5: Realized / Historical Returns Approach**

Using historical average stock returns over a long period, adjusted for inflation.

#### Part (b) — Comparative analysis, examples, and limitations (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Comparative Table:**

| Method | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| DDM | Mature dividend-paying firms | Fails for zero-dividend firms; sensitive to $g$ |
| CAPM | All listed firms | Beta instability; market efficiency assumption |
| Bond + RP | Private/unlisted firms | Subjective risk premium estimate |
| Earnings Yield | Growth companies (e.g., Amazon) | Ignores dividend policy |

**Illustrative Example:**

A firm has $D_0 = ₹4$, $P_0 = ₹50$, $g = 6\%$, $R_f = 7\%$, $\beta = 1.25$, $R_m = 13\%$.

DDM gives: $D_1 = 4 \times 1.06 = ₹4.24$, hence $K_e = 4.24/50 + 0.06 = 14.48\%$.

CAPM gives: $K_e = 7\% + 1.25 \times (13\% - 7\%) = 7\% + 7.5\% = 14.5\%$.

**Conclusion:** Both methods yield similar results (~14.5%), which validates the estimates. In practice, **CAPM is the most widely used** by KTU examiners because it directly incorporates market risk.

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Similar Pattern]**

**"Explain the concept of Book Value and Market Value of common equity. How is the cost of common equity determined using the CAPM approach? Work out a numerical example."** (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Concept of Book Value vs. Market Value (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Book Value of Common Equity** is the value of shareholders' equity as shown in the company's balance sheet. It is an accounting measure derived as:

$$B_{ce} = \text{Total Assets} - \text{Total Liabilities} - \text{Preferred Stock}$$

Book Value Per Share (BVPS):

$$BVPS = \frac{B_{ce}}{N_{out}}$$

**Market Value of Common Equity** is the total market capitalization of the company, determined by multiplying the current market price by the number of outstanding shares:

$$MC = P_0 \times N_{out}$$

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Book Value | Market Value |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Historical cost accounting | Forward-looking market expectations |
| Source | Balance sheet | Stock exchange |
| Reflects | Past transactions | Future growth potential |
| Volatility | Stable | Highly volatile |

**Market-to-Book Ratio:**

$$M/B = \frac{MC}{B_{ce}} = \frac{P_0 \times N_{out}}{B_{ce}}$$

A ratio > 1 indicates that the market values the company above its book value, implying intangible value like brand, growth prospects, and goodwill.

#### Part (b) — CAPM Approach with Numerical (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Capital Asset Pricing Model was developed by **William Sharpe (1964)**, building on Harry Markowitz's portfolio theory. It states that the expected return on a security is a linear function of its systematic risk (beta).

**Formula:**

$$K_e = R_f + \beta_i \times (R_m - R_f)$$

**Components:**
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate (proxied by 10-year G-Sec yield in India)
- $\beta_i$ = Sensitivity of stock's returns to market returns
- $(R_m - R_f)$ = Market Risk Premium (MRP)

**Numerical Example:**

Given: $R_f = 6.5\%$, $\beta = 1.3$, $R_m = 13\%$, current share price $P_0 = ₹200$, expected dividend $D_1 = ₹10$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute MRP:**

$$MRP = R_m - R_f = 13\% - 6.5\% = 6.5\%$$

**Step 2 — Apply CAPM:**

$$K_e = 6.5\% + 1.3 \times 6.5\%$$
$$K_e = 6.5\% + 8.45\%$$
$$K_e = 14.95\%$$

**Step 3 — Verification using DDM:**

$$K_e^{DDM} = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{10}{200} + 0.05 = 0.05 + 0.05 = 10\%$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

CAPM (14.95%) > DDM (10%) because CAPM reflects market-perceived risk, while DDM reflects only dividend yield expectations. In **valuation and capital budgeting**, the higher of the two is generally used as a conservative estimate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to use $D_1$ (next-year dividend) instead of $D_0$ in the DDM formula. This costs 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** For CAPM, students write "Beta = Correlation × Std Dev" incorrectly. The correct formula is $\beta = \text{Cov}(R_i, R_m) / \text{Var}(R_m)$.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Not specifying units (decimal vs. percentage) in final answers. Always state "$K_e = 14.95\%$" or "0.1495".
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **Book Value** with **Market Value**. KTU examiners frequently test this. Book Value is backward-looking; Market Value is forward-looking.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to validate the condition $K_e > g$ in DDM. If $g \geq K_e$, the model is invalid.
> 6. **Common Mistake 6:** Not stating assumptions when using CAPM (e.g., market efficiency, single-period horizon, no transaction costs).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY QUICK REVISION (Use this for last-minute exam prep):**

### Conceptual Recap
- Common equity is the **ownership capital** representing the residual claim of shareholders after all other claims are settled.
- It is the **most junior** form of capital but offers the **highest return potential** in successful firms.
- It is **permanent capital** with no maturity, no mandatory dividend, and voting rights.

### Key Definitions
- **Authorized Capital:** Maximum shares allowed by charter
- **Issued Capital:** Shares actually sold
- **Outstanding Capital:** Issued − Treasury Stock
- **Treasury Stock:** Shares bought back by the company

### Key Formulae
- **DDM:** $K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$
- **CAPM:** $K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$
- **Bond + RP:** $K_e = K_d + RP$
- **BVPS:** $\frac{B_{ce}}{N_{out}}$
- **Market Cap:** $P_0 \times N_{out}$
- **Sustainable Growth Rate:** $g = r \times ROE$

### Key Characteristics (Memorize)
1. Residual claim on earnings ✓
2. Residual claim on assets ✓
3. Voting rights ✓
4. Limited liability ✓
5. No fixed dividend ✓
6. No maturity date ✓
7. Pre-emptive rights ✓
8. Right to information ✓

### Critical Conditions to Remember
- **DDM validity:** $K_e > g$ (mandatory for the formula to yield positive value)
- **CAPM assumption:** Single-period horizon, market efficiency, homogeneous expectations
- **Beta interpretation:** $\beta = 1$ (market), $\beta > 1$ (aggressive), $\beta < 1$ (defensive)

### Common KTU Trap Topics
- Distinguishing Book Value vs. Market Value
- Outstanding vs. Issued vs. Authorized shares
- Numerical calculation of $K_e$ using DDM and CAPM
- Sustainable growth rate derivation
- Why common equity is the most expensive source of finance

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Capital Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cost of Capital Estimation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cost of Capital (CoC)** is the **minimum rate of return** that a firm must earn on its investment projects to maintain the **market value of its shares** and satisfy the expectations of its investors (both equity holders and debt holders). It is also known as the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** when expressed as a composite, weighted rate across all sources of long-term finance. In the **CAPM framework** (Sharpe, 1964), the cost of capital represents the **opportunity cost of funds** invested in a firm — i.e., the return investors could earn elsewhere for taking equivalent risk. ### Components of Cost of Capital (KTU Syllabus Mapping) | Source | Symbol | Nature | |---|---|---| | Cost of Equity Capital | $K_e$ | Required return on ordinary shares | | Cost of Retained Earnings | $K_r$ | Opportunity cost of internal accruals | | Cost of Preference Share Capital | $K_p$ | Fixed dividend yield | | Cost of Debt (Debentures/Bonds) | $K_d$ | After-tax cost of borrowings | | Weighted Average Cost of Capital | **WACC / $K_o$** | Composite discount rate | --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Rent on Money" Concept > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Hostel Building** > > Imagine you are a business owner who needs **₹25,00,000** to build a hostel. You don't have all the money, so you raise it from three sources: > - **Friends (Equity Investors)** — contribute ₹10,00,000. They expect a share of profits AND capital appreciation. > - **A Bank Loan (Debt)** — contributes ₹10,00,000 at **8% interest**. Interest is **tax-deductible**. > - **Relatives (Preference Shareholders)** — contribute ₹5,00,000 expecting a **fixed 10% dividend**. > > The hostel business must earn **enough rent revenue** to: > 1. Pay the bank interest (₹80,000 gross, less tax shield). > 2. Pay relatives their fixed dividend (₹50,000). > 3. Give friends a return at least equal to what they could have earned in the next-best alternative investment (say, mutual funds). > > The **weighted average** of all these required returns is the **Cost of Capital** — the "rent" the firm must pay for every rupee of capital employed. If the hostel earns BELOW this blended rate, the business is **destroying value**. --- ## 1.3 Why is Cost of Capital Important? (KTU Board Favourite) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Primary Purposes in KTU Board Examinations:** > > 1. **Capital Budgeting Acceptance Criterion:** A project is accepted **IF** its **Internal Rate of Return (IRR) > Cost of Capital**. > 2. **Performance Evaluation:** Used in **EVA (Economic Value Added)** and **Residual Income** models. > 3. **Valuation of Firm:** $V = \sum \dfrac{FCF_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$ — the entire DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) framework pivots on WACC. --- ## 1.4 Key Terminology (Must Memorise) > [!NOTE] > - **Explicit Cost:** Outflow payments (interest, dividends) — *visible in books*. > - **Implicit Cost:** Opportunity cost of equity — *not visible in books* but **must be included** in CoC. > - **Marginal Cost of Capital:** Cost of an **additional** rupee of capital — relevant for new project decisions. > - **Specific Cost:** Cost of a **single** source of finance. > - **Composite/Overall Cost:** The weighted average — used as the **hurdle rate**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Pillars of Cost Estimation ### Pillar 1: Cost of Equity ($K_e$) — The Equity Investors' Required Return Equity has **no contractual obligation**; the cost must be **estimated** using one of three models. #### Model A: Dividend Discount Model (Gordon Growth Model) **Logic:** The shareholder's return = Expected dividend yield + Capital gain yield. - **Assumption:** Dividends grow at a constant rate $g$ in perpetuity. - **Derivation basis:** $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g}$ #### Model B: CAPM (Capital Asset Pricing Model) **Logic:** A rational investor demands: **Risk-free return + Premium for systematic (market) risk.** - **Beta ($\beta$)** measures the stock's co-movement with the market. #### Model C: Earning Yield Approach / Realised Yield Approach **Logic:** Historical average of $(E/P)$ ratio adjusted for growth. --- ### Pillar 2: Cost of Retained Earnings ($K_r$) - Identical to $K_e$ in concept, **but no flotation costs** and **no personal tax disadvantage** to shareholders. - $K_r = K_e$ (generally) but may be slightly less because of **no issue expenses**. --- ### Pillar 3: Cost of Preference Share Capital ($K_p$) - Like equity, **dividend is not tax-deductible** (unlike interest). - Generally **irredeemable** in Indian practice. --- ### Pillar 4: Cost of Debt ($K_d$) - Interest is **tax-deductible** under the Income Tax Act — therefore we use the **after-tax cost**. - Two sub-cases: **Irredeemable** and **Redeemable**. #### Sub-case A: Irredeemable Debt (Perpetual) - $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t)}{NP}$ where NP = Net Proceeds #### Sub-case B: Redeemable Debt - Average (interpolation) method: $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t) + \dfrac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\dfrac{(RV + NP)}{2}}$ #### Sub-case C: Yield-to-Maturity (YTM) Approach (Market-based) - $K_d = \dfrac{I + \dfrac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\dfrac{(RV + NP)}{2}}$ (pre-tax), then multiply by $(1-t)$. --- ### Pillar 5: Weights — Book Value vs Market Value vs Marginal | Weighting Basis | Symbol | KTU Exam Verdict | |---|---|---| | **Book Value Weights** | $W_{BV}$ | Easy to compute from Balance Sheet; **commonly tested** | | **Market Value Weights** | $W_{MV}$ | Theoretically superior; reflects investor's opportunity cost | | **Marginal/Target Weights** | $W_{M}$ | Used for new project decisions | | **Historical Weights** | $W_{H}$ | Rare in exams | --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **SAVE THIS TABLE — Covers 90% of Numerical Questions in KTU Board Exams.** | # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cost of Equity (DDM) | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $D_1$ = Next year dividend; $P_0$ = Market price; $g$ = Growth | If floatation cost $F$ exists: $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g$ | | 2 | Cost of Equity (CAPM) | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = Risk-free rate; $R_m$ = Market return | $(R_m - R_f)$ = **Market Risk Premium** | | 3 | Cost of Retained Earnings | $K_r = K_e$ (or $-F$) | Adjust for opportunity cost | No floatation, no personal tax | | 4 | Cost of Pref. Shares | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_0}$ | $D_p$ = Annual pref. dividend | **Not tax-deductible** | | 5 | Cost of Irredeemable Debt | $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t)}{NP}$ | $I$ = Annual interest; $t$ = Tax rate | After-tax cost | | 6 | Cost of Redeemable Debt (Approx.) | $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t) + \dfrac{(RV-NP)}{n}}{\dfrac{(RV+NP)}{2}}$ | $RV$ = Redemption Value; $n$ = Years to maturity | Most-tested formula | | 7 | **WACC / Overall CoC** | $K_o = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d$ | $W_i$ = Weight of each source | **The Master Formula** | | 8 | Marginal CoC | $K_m = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Total Cost}}{\Delta \text{Total Capital}}$ | Used in capital raising plans | $K_m > WACC$ ⇒ Halt issue | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Project Appraisal in Infrastructure:** NHAI uses WACC to evaluate PPP (Public-Private Partnership) bids. > - **Valuation of Tech Start-ups:** VCs use cost of equity (CAPM) + cost of venture debt to compute discount rates. > - **M&A (Mergers & Acquisitions):** WACC determines the fair bid premium. > - **Working Capital Decisions:** Marginal cost of capital informs short-term loan pricing. > - **Dividend Policy:** Cost of retained earnings vs cost of new equity → payout ratio decision. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Dividend Discount Model for $K_e$ **Starting Premise:** The market price of a share equals the present value of all future dividends, assuming they grow at a constant rate $g$. **Step 1 — Write the present value of the infinite dividend stream:** $$ P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the growth relationship $D_t = D_1(1+g)^{t-1}$:** $$ P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)} + \frac{D_1(1+g)}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_1(1+g)^2}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots $$ **Step 3 — Recognise this as a Geometric Progression with common ratio $r = \frac{(1+g)}{(1+K_e)}$:** For an infinite GP with first term $a$ and common ratio $r < 1$, sum = $\frac{a}{1-r}$. **Step 4 — Apply the GP sum formula and solve for $K_e$:** $$ P_0 = \frac{D_1}{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+K_e}\right)} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+K_e)} $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+K_e)$: $$ P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e) - (1+g)} = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g} $$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the cost of equity:** $$ K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g $$ > **Interpretation:** The required return = **Dividend Yield + Capital Appreciation Rate**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) **Starting Premise:** The firm's total cost of capital is the **weighted average** of the costs of individual sources, where the weights are the proportions of each source in the total capital structure. **Step 1 — Define the three primary sources and their costs:** - Equity: cost = $K_e$, weight = $W_e = \dfrac{E}{E+D+P}$ - Debt: cost = $K_d$, weight = $W_d = \dfrac{D}{E+D+P}$ - Preference: cost = $K_p$, weight = $W_p = \dfrac{P}{E+D+P}$ **Step 2 — Combine the costs using the weighted average principle:** For every ₹1 of capital raised, the firm pays: - $W_e$ proportion at cost $K_e$ - $W_d$ proportion at cost $K_d$ - $W_p$ proportion at cost $K_p$ $$ K_o = W_e \cdot K_e + W_d \cdot K_d + W_p \cdot K_p $$ **Step 3 — Impose the constraint that weights sum to 1:** $$ W_e + W_d + W_p = 1 $$ This constraint ensures the WACC is a true average. **Step 4 — Final WACC expression (book value basis):** $$ K_o = \left(\frac{E}{V}\right) K_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right) K_d + \left(\frac{P}{V}\right) K_p $$ where $V = E + D + P$ is the **total capitalisation**. --- ## 3.3 Comprehensive Numerical Example — A Complete KTU Board Question > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** > *ABC Ltd. has the following capital structure as on 31-Mar-2025:* > - *Equity: 50,000 shares of ₹10 each (current market price: ₹25 per share)* > - *8% Preference Shares: 10,000 shares of ₹100 each (current market price: ₹90 per share)* > - *10% Debentures: ₹5,00,000 face value (redeemable at par in 5 years, current market price: ₹96)* > > *Additional Information:* > - *Expected dividend next year: ₹3 per share; growth rate: 6% p.a.* > - *Tax rate: 30%* > > ***Compute the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) using Market Value Weights.*** ### Step 1 — Compute Market Values of Each Component $$ E_{MV} = 50{,}000 \times 25 = 12{,}50{,}000 $$ $$ P_{MV} = 10{,}000 \times 90 = 9{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ D_{MV} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{96}{100} = 4{,}80{,}000 $$ $$ V_{MV} = 12{,}50{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 = 26{,}30{,}000 $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Specific Cost of Each Source **(a) Cost of Equity ($K_e$) using DDM:** $$ K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{3}{25} + 0.06 = 0.12 + 0.06 = 0.18 = 18\% $$ **(b) Cost of Preference Shares ($K_p$):** $$ K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_0} = \frac{8}{90} = 0.0889 = 8.89\% $$ **(c) Cost of Debt ($K_d$) — Redeemable, after-tax:** Annual interest: $I = 10\% \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000$ Net Proceeds: $NP = 4{,}80{,}000$ Redemption Value: $RV = 5{,}00{,}000$ Years to maturity: $n = 5$ $$ K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV + NP)}{2}} $$ $$ K_d = \frac{50{,}000(1 - 0.30) + \frac{(5{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000)}{5}}{\frac{(5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000)}{2}} $$ $$ K_d = \frac{35{,}000 + 4{,}000}{4{,}90{,}000} = \frac{39{,}000}{4{,}90{,}000} = 0.0796 = 7.96\% $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Market Value Weights $$ W_e = \frac{12{,}50{,}000}{26{,}30{,}000} = 0.4753 $$ $$ W_p = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{26{,}30{,}000} = 0.3422 $$ $$ W_d = \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{26{,}30{,}000} = 0.1825 $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the WACC Master Formula $$ K_o = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d $$ $$ K_o = (0.4753 \times 18) + (0.3422 \times 8.89) + (0.1825 \times 7.96) $$ $$ K_o = 8.5554 + 3.0422 + 1.4527 $$ $$ K_o = 13.05\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** $K_o = 13.05\%$ > *Any project proposal with IRR > 13.05% should be accepted; otherwise, rejected.* --- ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Book Value vs Market Value WACC > [!NOTE] > **Why KTU loves this comparison question (2 marks in Part A, 7 marks in Part B):** | Criterion | Book Value WACC | Market Value WACC | |---|---|---| | **Data Source** | Balance Sheet | Stock Exchange / Market quotes | | **Reflects current investor expectations?** | ❌ No (historical cost) | ✅ Yes (current opportunity cost) | | **Use in Capital Budgeting** | Inferior | **Theoretically superior** | | **Use in Valuation** | Not preferred | **Industry standard** | | **Volatility** | Stable | Fluctuates with share prices | | **KTU Exam Frequency** | Medium | **High** | **Key Insight:** Market value weights produce a **realistic** cost of capital because they reflect what investors *currently* demand, not what they paid in the past. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Components & Computation Flow of Cost of Capital ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Capital Structure] --> B[Sources of Long-term Finance] B --> C1[Equity Shares] B --> C2[Preference Shares] B --> C3[Debentures or Bonds] B --> C4[Retained Earnings] B --> C5[Term Loans] C1 --> D1[Cost of Equity Ke
DDM or CAPM] C2 --> D2[Cost of Preference Kp
Dp divided by P0] C3 --> D3[Cost of Debt Kd
After tax YTM] C4 --> D4[Cost of Retained Kr
Same as Ke less flotation] C5 --> D5[Cost of Term Loan
Same as Kd] D1 --> E[Apply Weights
Book or Market Value] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E D5 --> E E --> F[Weighted Average Cost of Capital WACC] F --> G{Decision Use} G --> G1[Capital Budgeting Hurdle Rate] G --> G2[Firm Valuation DCF] G --> G3[EVA Calculation] G --> G4[Performance Benchmark] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#ea580c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000 style D2 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000 style D3 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000 style D4 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000 style D5 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000 style E fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: How to Choose the Right Cost of Equity Model ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Need to estimate Ke] --> B{Are dividends
paid regularly?} B -->|Yes| C{Is growth rate
constant?} B -->|No| D{Is market price
and beta available?} C -->|Yes| E[Use DDM or Gordon Model
Ke = D1 by P0 plus g] C -->|No| F[Use Multi-stage DDM
or H Model] D -->|Yes| G[Use CAPM
Ke = Rf plus Beta times Rm minus Rf] D -->|No| H[Use Earning Yield Approach
or Realised Yield] E --> I[Final Ke] F --> I G --> I H --> I style A fill:#0f766e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#facc15,stroke:#000 style G fill:#facc15,stroke:#000 style I fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Architecture Map: WACC Computation as a Functional Block System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK] I1[Capital Structure Data] I2[Dividend and Growth Info] I3[Interest Rate and Maturity] I4[Tax Rate] I5[Market Prices] end subgraph PROCESS[PROCESSING BLOCK] P1[Compute Specific Costs] P2[Compute Weights] P3[Weighted Multiplication] end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT BLOCK] O1[WACC Percentage] O2[Project Hurdle Rate] O3[Valuation Discount Factor] end I1 --> P2 I2 --> P1 I3 --> P1 I4 --> P1 I5 --> P2 P1 --> P3 P2 --> P3 P3 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O1 --> O3 style INPUT fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#000 style PROCESS fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000 style OUTPUT fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Cost of Capital — Conceptual Hierarchy Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cost of Capital Concept] --> B[Specific Cost] A --> C[Composite Cost] A --> D[Marginal Cost] B --> B1[Cost of Equity Ke] B --> B2[Cost of Debt Kd] B --> B3[Cost of Preference Kp] B --> B4[Cost of Retained Earnings Kr] C --> C1[WACC or Ko] C1 --> C2[Used as Discount Rate
in DCF] D --> D1[Cost of Next Rupee] D1 --> D2[Relevant for New Projects] B1 --> B1a[DDM Method] B1 --> B1b[CAPM Method] B1 --> B1c[Realised Yield Method] B2 --> B2a[Irredeemable] B2 --> B2b[Redeemable Approximation] B2 --> B2c[YTM Method] style A fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C1 fill:#15803d,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D1 fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Cost of Capital and state its significance.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Cost of Capital is the minimum rate of return required by the suppliers of long-term funds (equity, preference, and debt) of a firm, such that the market value of the firm remains unchanged. It is the composite discount rate used for evaluating investment proposals and is mathematically expressed as the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC). > > **Significance (1.5 marks):** > 1. **Capital Budgeting Decision:** Serves as the **hurdle rate (cut-off rate)** — projects with IRR > Cost of Capital are accepted. > 2. **Performance Evaluation:** Forms the basis of EVA (Economic Value Added) and Residual Income computations. > 3. **Valuation of Firm:** Used as the discount rate in DCF models: $V = \sum \dfrac{FCF_t}{(1 + WACC)^t}$. > 4. **Capital Structure Decision:** Helps in deciding the optimal mix of debt and equity. --- ### **Question 2: Distinguish between Cost of Equity and Cost of Retained Earnings.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Basis | Cost of Equity ($K_e$) | Cost of Retained Earnings ($K_r$) | |---|---|---| | **Meaning** | Cost of raising funds by issuing new equity shares | Cost of using profits retained in the business | | **Floatation Cost** | **Incurred** — issue expenses reduce net proceeds | **Not incurred** — internal source | | **Personal Tax** | Shareholders may have to pay capital gains tax if sold | No personal tax on retained profits | | **Magnitude** | **Higher** | **Lower** (or equal in theory) | | **Formula** | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g$ | $K_r = K_e - F$ (approx.) | > **Key Takeaway:** $K_r$ is **always less than or equal to** $K_e$ because of savings on floatation costs. --- --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice is provided — you must attempt ONE of the two options.** --- ### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **The following information relates to XCEL Industries Ltd.:** | Source | Book Value (₹) | Specific Cost (%) | |---|---|---| | Equity Shares (₹100 each) | 10,00,000 | 15% | | 9% Preference Shares (₹100 each) | 4,00,000 | 9% | | 8% Debentures (₹1,000 each) | 6,00,000 | 8% (pre-tax) | **Additional Information:** - Tax rate: **30%** - The debentures are **redeemable at par after 10 years**. - The company is considering a project requiring an investment of ₹5,00,000 yielding 12% return. **Required:** - **(a)** Compute the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** using **book value weights**. **(7 Marks)** - **(b)** Advise whether the project should be **accepted or rejected**, with reasons. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** ### Part (a): WACC using Book Value Weights (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Identify Book Values and Specific Costs:** | Source | Book Value (₹) | Cost | |---|---|---| | Equity ($E$) | 10,00,000 | $K_e = 15\%$ | | Preference ($P$) | 4,00,000 | $K_p = 9\%$ | | Debentures ($D$) | 6,00,000 | $K_d = 8\%$ (pre-tax) | **Step 2 — Total Capital (V):** $$ V = 10{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Book Value Weights:** $$ W_e = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 0.50 $$ $$ W_p = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 0.20 $$ $$ W_d = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 0.30 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the After-tax Cost of Debt:** $$ K_d (\text{after-tax}) = 8\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 5.6\% $$ *(Since debentures are redeemable at par at book value, the approximation formula yields the same result — no premium/discount adjustment needed.)* **Step 5 — Compute the WACC:** $$ K_o = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d (1 - t) $$ $$ K_o = (0.50 \times 15) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 5.6) $$ $$ K_o = 7.50 + 1.80 + 1.68 = 10.98\% $$ > ✅ **Answer (Part a):** WACC = **10.98%** --- ### Part (b): Project Acceptance Decision (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Identify the Hurdle Rate:** The relevant discount rate is the **WACC = 10.98%**. **Step 2 — Compare Project Return with WACC:** $$ \text{Project Return} = 12\% \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{WACC} = 10.98\% $$ **Step 3 — Decision Rule Application:** Since $12\% > 10.98\%$, the project **earns more than the cost of capital** — it is a **positive NPV project**. **Step 4 — Calculate Surplus Return (in ₹):** $$ \text{Incremental Return} = (12\% - 10.98\%) \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1.02\% \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}100 $$ **Step 5 — Justification (Board Key Words):** > ✅ **DECISION: ACCEPT the project.** > > **Reasons (write at least 3 for full 7 marks):** > 1. Project's return (12%) **exceeds** the firm's cost of capital (10.98%) → positive NPV → value creation. > 2. The surplus of ₹5,100 over the cost translates into **shareholder wealth maximisation**. > 3. The proposal contributes to a **positive Economic Value Added (EVA)**. > 4. Rejecting it would mean forgoing a profitable opportunity, leading to **opportunity cost loss**. --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **PQR Limited provides the following data:** - Expected dividend per share next year: $D_1 = ₹5$ - Current market price per share: $P_0 = ₹50$ - Constant growth rate: $g = 8\%$ p.a. - Risk-free rate of return: $R_f = 7\%$ - Expected market return: $R_m = 14\%$ - Beta of the share: $\beta = 1.2$ **Required:** - **(a)** Compute the **Cost of Equity** using the **(i) Dividend Discount Model** and **(ii) CAPM**. Comment on which estimate should be preferred. **(7 Marks)** - **(b)** If the firm also has **10% debentures of ₹10,00,000** redeemable at par in 8 years with a current market price of ₹95, and **12% preference shares of ₹5,00,000** with market price of ₹105, calculate the **WACC**. Assume tax rate **30%** and equity market value **₹15,00,000**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** ### Part (a): Cost of Equity — DDM and CAPM (7 Marks) **(i) Dividend Discount Model:** $$ K_e^{DDM} = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{5}{50} + 0.08 = 0.10 + 0.08 = 0.18 = 18\% $$ **(ii) CAPM Model:** $$ K_e^{CAPM} = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 7\% + 1.2 \times (14\% - 7\%) $$ $$ K_e^{CAPM} = 7\% + 1.2 \times 7\% = 7\% + 8.4\% = 15.4\% $$ **Comment / Comparison (3 marks for discussion):** | Aspect | DDM (18%) | CAPM (15.4%) | |---|---|---| | Basis | **Internal** (firm's dividend policy) | **External** (market parameters) | | Assumption | Constant dividend growth | Market efficiency, stable beta | | Sensitivity to share price | **High** | Low | | Use in Practice | When dividends are stable | When market data is reliable | > **Recommendation:** **CAPM is theoretically superior** because it explicitly accounts for **systematic risk** through beta. DDM may overstate cost if the firm retains a large portion of earnings (low payout). The **blended** approach — averaging both — is often used in practice, yielding $K_e \approx (18 + 15.4)/2 = 16.7\%$. --- ### Part (b): WACC Calculation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Market Values:** $$ E = 15{,}00{,}000, \quad D = 95\% \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}50{,}000, \quad P = 105\% \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}25{,}000 $$ $$ V = 15{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}50{,}000 + 5{,}25{,}000 = 29{,}75{,}000 $$ **Step 2 — Specific Costs (using CAPM $K_e = 15.4\%$):** **Cost of Debt (Redeemable, after-tax):** Annual interest $I = 10\% \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000$ Redemption Value $RV = 10{,}00{,}000$ (at par) Net Proceeds $NP = 9{,}50{,}000$ Years $n = 8$ $$ K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\frac{(RV + NP)}{2}} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000(0.70) + \frac{50{,}000}{8}}{\frac{19{,}50{,}000}{2}} $$ $$ K_d = \frac{70{,}000 + 6{,}250}{9{,}75{,}000} = \frac{76{,}250}{9{,}75{,}000} = 0.0782 = 7.82\% $$ **Cost of Preference Shares:** $$ K_p = \frac{12}{105} = 0.1143 = 11.43\% $$ **Step 3 — Market Value Weights:** $$ W_e = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{29{,}75{,}000} = 0.5042 $$ $$ W_d = \frac{9{,}50{,}000}{29{,}75{,}000} = 0.3193 $$ $$ W_p = \frac{5{,}25{,}000}{29{,}75{,}000} = 0.1765 $$ **Step 4 — WACC:** $$ K_o = (0.5042 \times 15.4) + (0.3193 \times 7.82) + (0.1765 \times 11.43) $$ $$ K_o = 7.765 + 2.497 + 2.017 = 12.28\% $$ > ✅ **Answer (Part b):** WACC = **12.28%** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP 5 MARKS-LOSING PITFALLS IN COST OF CAPITAL QUESTIONS:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Tax Shield:** Students frequently quote **$K_d = 8\%$ (pre-tax)** instead of **$5.6\%$ (after-tax)** in WACC. This inflates WACC and may flip accept/reject decisions. **Always multiply by $(1-t)$.** > > 2. **Mixing Up Book Value and Market Value:** A common blunder is using market value for one source and book value for another in the same question. KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for inconsistent treatment. > > 3. **Ignoring Floatation Cost in DDM:** When the question says "issue expenses are ₹2 per share," you **MUST** subtract this from $P_0$ in the denominator: $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g$. > > 4. **Using Nominal vs Real Values:** If growth is given as real (inflation-adjusted), discount rate must also be real. **Mismatch loses 1 full mark.** > > 5. **Cost of Retained Earnings = Cost of Equity:** This is a **FALSE** assumption in most cases. If floatation cost is given, $K_r < K_e$. Examiners **specifically test** this distinction. > > **Pro Tip:** Always show **two decimal places** in intermediate calculations. KTU examiners are partial to clean, **tabular presentation** of the WACC final answer. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > **Save this checklist — covers the entire Module 1 Cost of Capital Estimation syllabus:** - ☐ **Cost of Capital = Minimum required rate of return** that maintains share price. - ☐ Three primary sources: **Equity, Preference, Debt** — compute specific cost for each. - ☐ **$K_e$ (DDM):** $K_e = D_1/P_0 + g$ — for stable dividend payers. - ☐ **$K_e$ (CAPM):** $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — for market-data-rich firms. - ☐ **$K_p$:** $K_p = D_p / P_0$ — simple dividend yield, **not tax-deductible**. - ☐ **$K_d$ (Irredeemable):** $K_d = I(1-t)/NP$ — perpetuity interest. - ☐ **$K_d$ (Redeemable):** Uses **interpolation formula** with $(RV+NP)/2$ in denominator. - ☐ **$K_d$ always AFTER TAX** — interest creates a tax shield under Income Tax Act. - ☐ **$K_r$ (Retained Earnings):** $K_r = K_e - F$ — slightly less than new equity. - ☐ **WACC Master Formula:** $K_o = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d$. - ☐ **Weights must sum to 1** — use Book Value OR Market Value, **never mix**. - ☐ **Market Value Weights** are theoretically superior for investment decisions. - ☐ **Marginal Cost of Capital** is used for **new project evaluation** — not historical. - ☐ WACC serves as the **hurdle rate** — accept project **iff** IRR > WACC. - ☐ WACC is the **discount rate** in **DCF valuation**: $V = \sum FCF_t/(1+WACC)^t$. - ☐ Higher debt → lower WACC (due to tax shield) but **higher financial risk**. - ☐ Cost of capital is **NOT a single number** — it changes with capital structure.

topics.title: Beta Estimation and Project Beta
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Beta Estimation and Project Beta

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Beta (β)** is the quantitative measure of **systematic (non-diversifiable) risk** of a security or a portfolio relative to the overall market. It is formally defined as the slope coefficient of the **Security Characteristic Line (SCL)**, derived from regressing the asset's return against the market return. Mathematically, **Beta equals the covariance between the return of asset *i* and the return of the market, divided by the variance of the market return.**

In the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** framework, Beta is the sole firm-specific input that captures risk and is used to determine the cost of equity:

$$R_i = R_f + \beta_i (R_m - R_f)$$

where $R_i$ is the expected return on asset *i*, $R_f$ is the risk-free rate, $R_m$ is the expected market return, and $(R_m - R_f)$ is the market risk premium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Beta as a Sensitivity Thermometer"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine Beta as a **dance partner's responsiveness** on a dance floor (the market).  
> - A partner with **Beta = 1** moves exactly in sync with the crowd — every twist the crowd makes, they mirror.  
> - A partner with **Beta > 1** (say 1.5) **exaggerates** every move — if the crowd steps right gently, they lunge aggressively. This is an **aggressive/defensive** stock.  
> - A partner with **Beta < 1** (say 0.4) **under-reacts** — barely moves when the crowd dances vigorously. This is a **defensive stock** (e.g., FMCG, utilities).  
> - A partner with **Beta < 0** (negative) dances in the **opposite direction** — a perfect hedge asset (e.g., gold during stock crashes).

**Project Beta** is the extension of this concept to **corporate capital budgeting**. When a firm evaluates a new project (a new product line, an R&D venture, an acquisition), the project's own systematic risk must be quantified. Since the project is not a publicly traded security, we estimate its Beta through proxies — this is the essence of **Project Beta estimation**.

### 1.3 Interpretation of Beta Values — KTU Board Reference Table

| Beta Value | Classification | Investor Behaviour | Example Sector |
|------------|----------------|--------------------|----------------|
| β < 0      | Hedge / Inverse | Moves opposite to market | Gold, defensive bonds |
| β = 0      | Risk-free asset | Independent of market | Government T-Bills |
| 0 < β < 1  | Defensive      | Less volatile than market | FMCG, Pharma, Utilities |
| β = 1      | Neutral        | Mirrors market exactly | Broad Index ETFs |
| β > 1      | Aggressive     | More volatile than market | Tech, Auto, Small-cap |
| β ≥ 2      | Highly Aggressive | Extreme amplification | Speculative penny stocks |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The unit of Beta is **dimensionless** (a pure ratio). It has **no units** because it is a ratio of two identical quantities (returns). Always quote Beta to **two decimal places** in KTU board examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Does Beta Exist?

The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM), developed by **William Sharpe (1964), John Lintner (1965), and Jan Mossin (1966)** independently, postulates that in an efficient market, investors are rewarded only for bearing **systematic risk** — risk that cannot be eliminated through diversification. Beta is the linear sensitivity coefficient that maps an asset's excess return to the market's excess return.

The mathematical foundation is rooted in **linear regression**:

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \varepsilon_i$$

where:
- $R_i$ = return on asset *i*
- $\alpha_i$ = intercept (Jensen's alpha — measures abnormal return)
- $\beta_i$ = slope coefficient = **systematic risk measure**
- $\varepsilon_i$ = residual (unsystematic risk; diversifiable)

### 2.2 Methods of Beta Estimation — The Five Core Approaches

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must be able to **compare and contrast** the following methods and select the most appropriate one for a given scenario.

#### (A) Statistical / Historical Beta (Market Model Regression)
This is the **most widely used method**, employing ordinary least squares (OLS) regression of historical stock returns on market returns over a chosen look-back period (typically **60 months / 5 years**, per KTU convention).

$$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)} = \frac{\sum (R_i - \bar{R_i})(R_m - \bar{R_m})}{\sum (R_m - \bar{R_m})^2}$$

**Limitations:** Sensitive to (i) the time period chosen, (ii) frequency of data (daily vs monthly), (iii) market index selection (BSE Sensex vs NSE Nifty vs S&P 500), and (iv) survivorship bias.

#### (B) Adjusted Beta (Bloomberg / Value Line Adjustment)
Practitioners often "shrink" raw historical betas toward the market mean of 1, using the formula:

$$\beta_{adjusted} = \frac{2}{3} \beta_{historical} + \frac{1}{3} (1.0)$$

This **Bloomberg-adjusted beta** corrects the well-documented **regression-to-the-mean** phenomenon — extreme betas (very high or very low) tend to migrate toward 1 over time.

#### (C) Fundamental Beta (Bottom-Up Beta)
Here, Beta is constructed from **firm-specific fundamental variables** (operating leverage, financial leverage, earnings variability, dividend payout, asset tangibility) using cross-sectional regression. The **Fama-French (1993)** and **Mohan & Chen (2004)** frameworks use accounting ratios to predict betas.

#### (D) Accounting Beta
Uses **accounting earnings** instead of stock returns:

$$\beta_{accounting} = \frac{Cov(\Delta Earnings_i, \Delta Earnings_m)}{Var(\Delta Earnings_m)}$$

Useful for **private firms, subsidiaries, and project evaluation** where market data is unavailable.

#### (E) Comparable / Pure-Play Beta
For a project that resembles a publicly listed firm in a similar business, the **comparable firm's beta** is used directly as the project's beta. This is the **most common practical approach** for Project Beta.

### 2.3 Levered vs Unlevered Beta — The Leverage Adjustment

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Critical Concept:** Beta measured from stock returns is **levered beta** (β_L) — it includes the effects of the firm's debt-equity structure. To compare across firms with different capital structures or to evaluate a project funded with project-specific leverage, we must **unlever** the beta first.

- **Levered Beta (β_L):** Reflects business risk + financial risk. Directly estimated from market data.
- **Unlevered Beta (β_U):** Reflects only business (asset) risk. Assumes the firm is all-equity financed.

#### Hamada's Equation (1972) — The Industry Standard

$$\beta_L = \beta_U \left[ 1 + (1 - T) \frac{D}{E} \right]$$

$$\beta_U = \frac{\beta_L}{1 + (1 - T) \frac{D}{E}}$$

where $T$ is the corporate tax rate, $D$ is market value of debt, and $E$ is market value of equity.

### 2.4 Project Beta — Capital Budgeting Application

When a firm undertakes a new project, the project's cost of capital should reflect **only the project's risk**, not the firm's overall WACC. The methodology:

1. Identify the project's business risk (industry, operating leverage, cyclicality).
2. Find **pure-play comparables** — publicly listed firms whose primary business mirrors the project.
3. Compute the **unlevered beta** of each comparable.
4. **Average the unlevered betas** to obtain the asset beta of the comparable industry.
5. **Re-lever** using the **project's (or firm's) target capital structure** to obtain the project Beta.
6. Plug into CAPM to obtain the **project-specific cost of equity**, then compute **project WACC**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\beta_i = \dfrac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$ | Statistical Beta | OLS regression slope |
| 2 | $\beta_{adj} = \dfrac{2}{3}\beta_{hist} + \dfrac{1}{3}(1.0)$ | Bloomberg Adjusted Beta | Use when $\beta_{hist}$ is extreme |
| 3 | $\beta_L = \beta_U \left[ 1 + (1-T)\dfrac{D}{E} \right]$ | Hamada's Levered Beta | Assumes debt is risk-free & perpetual |
| 4 | $\beta_U = \dfrac{\beta_L}{1 + (1-T)\dfrac{D}{E}}$ | Unlevering Beta | For cross-firm / project comparison |
| 5 | $R_i = R_f + \beta_i (R_m - R_f)$ | CAPM Cost of Equity | Beta is the risk input |
| 6 | $\beta_{project} = \beta_{U, comparable} \left[ 1 + (1-T_{proj})\dfrac{D_{proj}}{E_{proj}} \right]$ | Pure-Play Project Beta | Re-lever to **project's** D/E |
| 7 | $\beta_{portfolio} = \sum w_i \beta_i$ | Portfolio Beta | Weighted average of asset betas |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Units Convention:** All Beta values are **dimensionless ratios** (range typically $-1$ to $+3$ for traded stocks; $\geq 0$ for most equity). The tax rate $T$ is in **decimal form** (e.g., $30\% = 0.30$).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Corporate Finance

- **Capital Budgeting:** Determines the **hurdle rate** for new investments; ensures projects are evaluated at risk-appropriate discount rates.
- **Portfolio Management:** Used to construct **risk-targeted portfolios** (e.g., pension funds, insurance funds).
- **Valuation:** Beta is a critical input in **DCF valuation** via CAPM-derived cost of equity.
- **Performance Evaluation:** The **Sharpe ratio** and **Treynor ratio** both use Beta for risk-adjusted performance measurement.
- **Hedging:** Beta of a portfolio determines the number of futures contracts needed to hedge market exposure: $N = \beta_P \cdot (V_P / V_F)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Beta from First Principles (OLS Regression)

The Single-Index Market Model assumes:

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \varepsilon_i$$

**Step 1 — Setup the OLS objective:** Minimize the sum of squared residuals:

$$\min_{\alpha, \beta} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \varepsilon_t^2 = \min_{\alpha, \beta} \sum_{t=1}^{T} (R_{i,t} - \alpha - \beta R_{m,t})^2$$

**Step 2 — First-order conditions (taking partial derivatives):**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \alpha}: \quad \sum (R_i - \alpha - \beta R_m) = 0 \implies \alpha = \bar{R_i} - \beta \bar{R_m}$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}: \quad \sum R_m (R_i - \alpha - \beta R_m) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute α back into the second equation:**

$$\sum R_m (R_i - \bar{R_i} + \beta \bar{R_m} - \beta R_m) = 0$$

$$\sum R_m (R_i - \bar{R_i}) - \beta \sum R_m (R_m - \bar{R_m}) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Recognize covariance and variance structures:**

By definition:
- $Cov(R_i, R_m) = \dfrac{1}{T} \sum (R_i - \bar{R_i})(R_m - \bar{R_m})$
- $Var(R_m) = \dfrac{1}{T} \sum (R_m - \bar{R_m})^2$

**Step 5 — Solve for β:**

$$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Geometric Interpretation:** Beta is the **slope** of the **Security Characteristic Line (SCL)**, which is the best-fit line through the scatter plot of $R_i$ vs $R_m$. The intercept is Jensen's alpha.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Hamada's Equation

**Assumptions:** (i) Firm has perpetual debt $D$; (ii) debt is risk-free at rate $r_d$; (iii) corporate tax rate $T$ is constant; (iv) no bankruptcy costs (Modigliani-Miller with taxes).

**Step 1 — Unlevered firm value (all-equity):**

$$V_U = \frac{EBIT(1-T)}{R_U}$$

where $R_U$ is the cost of equity of an all-equity firm. Since the firm is unlevered, $R_U$ depends only on **asset (business) risk** $\beta_U$.

**Step 2 — Levered firm value (Modigliani-Miller with taxes):**

$$V_L = V_U + T \cdot D$$

**Step 3 — Levered cost of equity** (MM Proposition II with taxes):

$$R_L = R_U + (R_U - r_d)(1-T)\frac{D}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Convert returns to betas using CAPM** ($R = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$):

$$R_L - R_f = (R_U - R_f) + (R_U - r_d)(1-T)\frac{D}{E}$$

$$\beta_L (R_m - R_f) = \beta_U (R_m - R_f) + [\beta_U (R_m - R_f) - \beta_d (R_m - R_f)](1-T)\frac{D}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Since debt is risk-free, $\beta_d = 0$:**

$$\beta_L (R_m - R_f) = \beta_U (R_m - R_f) \left[ 1 + (1-T)\frac{D}{E} \right]$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $(R_m - R_f)$ from both sides:**

$$\boxed{\beta_L = \beta_U \left[ 1 + (1-T)\frac{D}{E} \right]}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Project Beta Estimation

> [!NOTE]  
> **Problem (Model Question Style):**  
> *Infosys Ltd is evaluating a new "Cloud-AI Services" project. The pure-play comparable firm in the same business is listed with: market value of equity = ₹80,000 cr, market value of debt = ₹20,000 cr, levered beta = 1.45, corporate tax rate = 25%. The project's target capital structure has D/E = 0.30, and the corporate tax rate for the project is 30%. Compute the Project Beta. Given $R_f = 6\%$ and $R_m = 14\%$, also compute the cost of equity for the project.*

#### Step 1 — Unlever the comparable's beta using Hamada's equation:

$$\beta_U = \frac{\beta_L}{1 + (1-T_c)\frac{D_c}{E_c}} = \frac{1.45}{1 + (1 - 0.25)\frac{20000}{80000}}$$

$$\beta_U = \frac{1.45}{1 + 0.75 \times 0.25} = \frac{1.45}{1 + 0.1875} = \frac{1.45}{1.1875}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_U = 1.2211 \text{ (asset beta of comparable)}}$$

#### Step 2 — Re-lever using the PROJECT's target capital structure:

$$\beta_{project} = \beta_U \left[ 1 + (1 - T_{proj})\frac{D_{proj}}{E_{proj}} \right]$$

$$\beta_{project} = 1.2211 \times \left[ 1 + (1 - 0.30)(0.30) \right]$$

$$\beta_{project} = 1.2211 \times \left[ 1 + 0.70 \times 0.30 \right] = 1.2211 \times 1.21$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{project} = 1.4775}$$

#### Step 3 — Project Cost of Equity via CAPM:

$$R_{project} = R_f + \beta_{project}(R_m - R_f) = 0.06 + 1.4775 \times (0.14 - 0.06)$$

$$R_{project} = 0.06 + 1.4775 \times 0.08 = 0.06 + 0.11820$$

$$\boxed{R_{project} = 17.82\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Interpretation:** The project is **riskier than its pure-play comparable (β = 1.48 vs 1.45)** because the project's target leverage is **higher** (D/E = 0.30 vs comparable D/E = 0.25). The cost of equity is therefore higher (17.82% vs approximately 17.6% at comparable's structure). This justifies a **higher discount rate** for the project.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Adjusted Beta

> [!NOTE]  
> **Problem:** A stock's historical (raw) beta is 1.80. Compute the Bloomberg-adjusted beta.

**Solution:**

$$\beta_{adj} = \frac{2}{3}(1.80) + \frac{1}{3}(1.00) = 1.2000 + 0.3333$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{adj} = 1.5333}$$

**Interpretation:** The adjustment "pulls" the extreme raw beta of 1.80 back toward the market mean of 1.00, yielding a more conservative and statistically robust estimate of 1.53.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Portfolio Beta

**Problem:** A portfolio contains four stocks with weights and betas:  
- Stock A: w = 0.30, β = 1.20  
- Stock B: w = 0.25, β = 0.80  
- Stock C: w = 0.20, β = 1.50  
- Stock D: w = 0.25, β = 0.60  

**Solution:**

$$\beta_P = \sum w_i \beta_i = (0.30)(1.20) + (0.25)(0.80) + (0.20)(1.50) + (0.25)(0.60)$$

$$\beta_P = 0.360 + 0.200 + 0.300 + 0.150$$

$$\boxed{\beta_P = 1.010}$$

**Interpretation:** The portfolio's beta of 1.01 means it moves **almost exactly with the market** — a well-diversified, market-neutral portfolio.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Beta Method

| Method | Best Used When | Data Required | Accuracy | KTU Frequency |
|--------|----------------|---------------|----------|---------------|
| Historical Beta | Publicly listed mature firms | 5+ years monthly returns | High | ★★★★★ |
| Adjusted Beta | Any listed firm, especially in volatile periods | Historical beta | Medium-High | ★★★★ |
| Fundamental Beta | Cross-sectional, multi-firm studies | Accounting ratios | Medium | ★★★ |
| Accounting Beta | Private firms, subsidiaries | Earnings data | Low-Medium | ★★★ |
| Pure-Play Comparable | Project Beta, M&A valuation | Listed comparables | High (if comparable exists) | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Beta Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Asset or Project] --> B{Is the Asset Publicly Traded?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Collect 60 Months of Returns Data]
    C --> D[Run OLS Regression Ri vs Rm]
    D --> E[Compute Historical Beta]
    E --> F{Is Beta Extreme beyond 1.5 or below 0.5?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply Bloomberg Adjustment 2 over 3 Beta plus 1 over 3]
    F -- No --> H[Use Historical Beta Directly]
    G --> I[Final Levered Beta]
    H --> I
    B -- No --> J[Identify Pure Play Comparable Firm]
    J --> K[Obtain Comparables Levered Beta and D/E]
    K --> L[Unlever Beta using Hamada Equation]
    L --> M[Re-Lever at Project Target D/E]
    M --> N[Project Beta]
    I --> O[Plug into CAPM]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Compute Cost of Equity and WACC]
```

### 4.2 Beta Estimation Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs [INPUT LAYER]
        inp1[Asset Returns Ri]
        inp2[Market Returns Rm]
        inp3[Debt to Equity Ratio]
        inp4[Tax Rate T]
    end

    subgraph processing [PROCESSING LAYER]
        proc1[Covariance Computation]
        proc2[Variance Computation]
        proc3[Hamada Unlever Formula]
        proc4[Hamada Relever Formula]
        proc5[Bloomberg Adjustment]
    end

    subgraph outputs [OUTPUT LAYER]
        out1[Historical Beta]
        out2[Adjusted Beta]
        out3[Unlevered Beta]
        out4[Project Beta]
        out5[Cost of Equity]
    end

    inp1 --> proc1
    inp2 --> proc1
    inp2 --> proc2
    proc1 --> out1
    inp2 --> proc2
    out1 --> proc5
    proc5 --> out2
    inp3 --> proc3
    inp4 --> proc3
    out1 --> proc3
    proc3 --> out3
    inp3 --> proc4
    inp4 --> proc4
    out3 --> proc4
    proc4 --> out4
    out4 --> out5
    out1 --> out5
```

### 4.3 Project Beta Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Capital Project Initiated] --> q1{Does Project Have a Direct Market Comparable?}
    q1 -- Yes Single Firm --> S1[Use Comparables Levered Beta]
    q1 -- Multiple Comparables --> S2[Average Unlevered Betas then Relever]
    q1 -- No Comparable --> S3[Use Industry Average Beta from Damodaran Data]
    S1 --> S4[Unlever at Comparables Capital Structure]
    S2 --> S4
    S3 --> S5[Adjust for Project Specific Operating Leverage]
    S4 --> S6[Relever at Projects Target Capital Structure]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Apply CAPM with Project Beta]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Project Discount Rate]
    S8 --> S9[Use in NPV and IRR Calculation]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Pedagogical Note:** The above block diagrams are drawn using Mermaid syntax for maximum portability across KTU e-learning platforms, lecture slides, and printed handouts. Each node represents a discrete computational or decision step in the beta estimation pipeline.
