<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]  
> *Format: Two-mark conceptual definition + one-mark applied example. Answers should fit in 80–100 words.*

#### **Question 1.** Define Beta in the context of CAPM. Differentiate between Levered and Unlevered Beta. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Beta (β) is the measure of **systematic risk** of a security relative to the overall market. It is defined as the covariance of the asset's return with the market return divided by the variance of the market return:

$$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$$

In CAPM, $R_i = R_f + \beta_i (R_m - R_f)$, Beta captures the sensitivity of an asset's excess return to the market's excess return.

**Levered Beta (β_L)** is the Beta of a firm that uses debt in its capital structure — it reflects both **business risk and financial risk**.  
**Unlevered Beta (β_U)** is the Beta of a hypothetical all-equity-financed firm — it reflects **only business (asset) risk** and is used for cross-firm comparison and project valuation.

---

#### **Question 2.** What is the "Pure-Play Method" for estimating Project Beta? Why is it preferred over using the firm's existing Beta? **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Pure-Play Method** estimates a project's beta by identifying one or more **publicly traded firms whose business closely matches the project**, computing (or unlevering) their betas, and re-levering at the project's target capital structure.

$$\beta_{project} = \beta_{U, comparable} \left[ 1 + (1 - T_{proj})\frac{D_{proj}}{E_{proj}} \right]$$

It is preferred because a firm's overall beta reflects the **average risk of all its existing divisions and projects**, which may differ significantly from the risk of a new project. Using firm-wide beta would either **over-state or under-state** the required return, leading to incorrect accept/reject decisions in capital budgeting.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]  
> *Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation: Each Part-B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice is provided between Question A and Question B.*

---

#### **Question A. (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **statistical (historical) method** of Beta estimation. Discuss its **assumptions, computational procedure, and limitations**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** XYZ Corporation has a levered Beta of 1.30. The firm has a debt-equity ratio (market value) of 0.50 and a corporate tax rate of 30%. Compute the **Unlevered Beta**. If the firm restructures to a debt-equity ratio of 0.30, what will be the **new levered Beta**? Assume the risk-free rate is 7%, expected market return is 13%. Also, calculate the **cost of equity** before and after restructuring. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Statistical Method of Beta Estimation [7 Marks]

**1. Definition and Conceptual Basis (2 marks):**  
The statistical (historical) method estimates Beta by regressing the historical returns of a security against the historical returns of the market index over a chosen time period. Beta is the slope of the Security Characteristic Line (SCL).

**2. Computational Procedure (3 marks):**

- **Step 1:** Select the market index (BSE Sensex, NSE Nifty, S&P 500).
- **Step 2:** Choose the frequency — monthly returns are preferred (industry standard = 60 months).
- **Step 3:** Compute periodic returns: $R_t = (P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t) / P_{t-1}$.
- **Step 4:** Run OLS regression: $R_i = \alpha + \beta R_m + \varepsilon$.
- **Step 5:** Calculate:

$$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)} = \frac{\sum (R_{i,t} - \bar{R_i})(R_{m,t} - \bar{R_m})}{\sum (R_{m,t} - \bar{R_m})^2}$$

- **Step 6:** Test for statistical significance using t-statistics and R².

**3. Assumptions and Limitations (2 marks):**
- Beta is **stationary** over time (often violated — betas drift).
- **Market return proxy** is correctly specified.
- **Survivorship bias** — delisted firms are excluded.
- Sensitivity to **time period**, **return interval**, and **market index**.
- **Unsystematic risk** in the residual term may be non-zero.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\beta_L = 1.30$, $D/E = 0.50$, $T = 30\%$

**Step 1 — Unlevered Beta using Hamada's Equation:**

$$\beta_U = \frac{\beta_L}{1 + (1 - T)\frac{D}{E}} = \frac{1.30}{1 + (1 - 0.30)(0.50)}$$

$$\beta_U = \frac{1.30}{1 + 0.70 \times 0.50} = \frac{1.30}{1 + 0.35} = \frac{1.30}{1.35}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_U = 0.9630}$$

**Step 2 — New Levered Beta at D/E = 0.30 (using same β_U):**

$$\beta_L^{new} = \beta_U \left[ 1 + (1 - T)\frac{D_{new}}{E_{new}} \right] = 0.9630 \times [1 + 0.70 \times 0.30]$$

$$\beta_L^{new} = 0.9630 \times [1 + 0.21] = 0.9630 \times 1.21$$

$$\boxed{\beta_L^{new} = 1.1652}$$

**Step 3 — Cost of Equity (Before and After Restructuring):**

**Before restructuring:**
$$R_{eq, before} = 0.07 + 1.30 \times (0.13 - 0.07) = 0.07 + 1.30 \times 0.06 = 0.07 + 0.078$$

$$\boxed{R_{eq, before} = 14.80\%}$$

**After restructuring:**
$$R_{eq, after} = 0.07 + 1.1652 \times 0.06 = 0.07 + 0.0699$$

$$\boxed{R_{eq, after} = 13.99\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The restructuring (reduction of leverage from 0.50 to 0.30) reduces financial risk, lowers the levered Beta from 1.30 to 1.1652, and decreases the cost of equity from 14.80% to 13.99%. **Note:** While equity holders benefit, the overall WACC impact depends on the cost of debt — typically the firm's WACC decreases, making the firm more valuable.

---

#### **Question B. (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** What is **Project Beta**? Discuss in detail the **step-by-step methodology** to estimate Project Beta using the Pure-Play method. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A firm is considering launching a new pharmaceutical product line. Two pure-play comparables are identified:  
- **Firm Alpha:** β_L = 1.10, D/E = 0.40, T = 25%  
- **Firm Beta:** β_L = 1.40, D/E = 0.60, T = 30%  

The project will be financed at a target D/E = 0.50 and tax rate of 30%. Compute the **Project Beta**. Also compute the **Cost of Equity** for the project assuming $R_f = 6.5\%$ and $R_m = 12.5\%$. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Concept and Methodology [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**  
**Project Beta** is the systematic risk measure of a specific new project (not the entire firm). It is essential for computing the **project-specific cost of equity** in capital budgeting decisions, ensuring the discount rate reflects only the project's risk.

**Step-by-Step Methodology — Pure-Play Method (5 marks):**

1. **Identify Pure-Play Comparables:** Find publicly listed firms whose **core business closely mirrors** the project's operations.
2. **Obtain Comparables' Financial Data:** Collect levered Beta (β_L), debt-equity ratio (D/E), and tax rate (T) for each comparable.
3. **Unlever the Comparables' Betas:** Apply Hamada's equation for each comparable:

$$\beta_{U,j} = \frac{\beta_{L,j}}{1 + (1 - T_j)(D/E)_j}$$

4. **Average the Unlevered Betas** to obtain the **industry asset beta**:

$$\beta_{U,industry} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_{U,j}$$

5. **Re-Lever at the Project's Target Capital Structure:**

$$\beta_{project} = \beta_{U,industry} \left[ 1 + (1 - T_{proj})(D/E)_{proj} \right]$$

6. **Compute Cost of Equity** using CAPM: $R_{eq} = R_f + \beta_{project}(R_m - R_f)$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Unlever Beta of Firm Alpha:**

$$\beta_{U,Alpha} = \frac{1.10}{1 + (1 - 0.25)(0.40)} = \frac{1.10}{1 + 0.75 \times 0.40} = \frac{1.10}{1.30}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{U,Alpha} = 0.8462}$$

**Step 2 — Unlever Beta of Firm Beta:**

$$\beta_{U,Beta} = \frac{1.40}{1 + (1 - 0.30)(0.60)} = \frac{1.40}{1 + 0.70 \times 0.60} = \frac{1.40}{1.42}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{U,Beta} = 0.9859}$$

**Step 3 — Average Unlevered Beta (Industry Asset Beta):**

$$\beta_{U,industry} = \frac{0.8462 + 0.9859}{2} = \frac{1.8321}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{U,industry} = 0.9160}$$

**Step 4 — Re-Lever at Project's Target Structure (D/E = 0.50, T = 30%):**

$$\beta_{project} = 0.9160 \times [1 + (1 - 0.30)(0.50)] = 0.9160 \times [1 + 0.35]$$

$$\beta_{project} = 0.9160 \times 1.35$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{project} = 1.2366}$$

**Step 5 — Cost of Equity for the Project:**

$$R_{eq, project} = 0.065 + 1.2366 \times (0.125 - 0.065) = 0.065 + 1.2366 \times 0.060$$

$$R_{eq, project} = 0.065 + 0.0742$$

$$\boxed{R_{eq, project} = 13.92\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The pharmaceutical project carries a systematic risk of 1.24, slightly more volatile than the average market. The project-specific cost of equity is 13.92%, which should be used in NPV computation for this project — **not** the firm's overall cost of equity.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to unlever first:** Students often re-lever a comparable's levered beta using the project's leverage — this is **double-counting**. Always unlever first, then re-lever.
> 2. **Using book value D/E instead of market value D/E:** In CAPM and Hamada's equation, always use **market values**. Book values are acceptable only when market values are unavailable.
> 3. **Mixing up T values:** When unlevering, use the **comparable's** tax rate. When re-levering, use the **project's (or firm's)** tax rate. These may differ.
> 4. **Treating Beta as percentage:** Beta is a **dimensionless number**, not a percentage. Don't write "Beta = 130%"; write "Beta = 1.30".
> 5. **Skipping the pure-play adjustment:** For Project Beta, **do not use the firm's own beta** — this is the most common error costing 3–4 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Beta Estimation and Project Beta — Key Takeaways for Last-Minute Revision:**

- 🔹 **Beta (β)** = $Cov(R_i, R_m) / Var(R_m)$ — measures **systematic risk only**.
- 🔹 **CAPM Equation:** $R_i = R_f + \beta_i (R_m - R_f)$ — Beta is the risk input.
- 🔹 **β = 1** → Neutral; **β > 1** → Aggressive; **β < 1** → Defensive; **β < 0** → Hedge.
- 🔹 **Statistical Beta** uses OLS regression on 60 months of returns.
- 🔹 **Bloomberg-Adjusted Beta** = $\frac{2}{3}\beta_{hist} + \frac{1}{3}(1.0)$ — corrects regression-to-mean.
- 🔹 **Levered Beta (β_L)** includes both business + financial risk.
- 🔹 **Unlevered Beta (β_U)** includes only business (asset) risk.
- 🔹 **Hamada's Equation (Unlever):** $\beta_U = \dfrac{\beta_L}{1 + (1-T)(D/E)}$.
- 🔹 **Hamada's Equation (Re-lever):** $\beta_L = \beta_U \left[ 1 + (1-T)(D/E) \right]$.
- 🔹 **Project Beta** uses **Pure-Play comparables** — never the firm's own beta.
- 🔹 **Pure-Play Steps:** Identify comparables → Unlever each → Average → Re-lever at project's D/E.
- 🔹 **Always use market values** of debt and equity (not book values).
- 🔹 **Portfolio Beta** = $\sum w_i \beta_i$ — weighted average.
- 🔹 **Beta is dimensionless** — quote to 2 decimal places in KTU exams.
- 🔹 **Fundamental Beta** uses accounting data; **Accounting Beta** uses earnings; both are useful for private firms.
- 🔹 **Cost of Equity** is computed using CAPM with the appropriate Beta (project, firm, or comparable).
- 🔹 **Hamada's assumptions:** risk-free perpetual debt, constant tax rate, MM framework holds.
- 🔹 **Adjusted Beta** is preferred when historical beta is statistically extreme (|β - 1| > 0.5).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Country Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Country Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1 — Country Risk: Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Country Risk** is the collection of risks associated with investing or doing business in a foreign country. These risks arise from **political instability, macroeconomic volatility, sovereign default, currency volatility, and regulatory/legal uncertainty**, which together can erode the value of cross-border investments and the cash flows repatriated to the parent entity.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET744)** framework, Country Risk is treated as a **non-diversifiable, systematic risk component** that must be quantified and added to the discount rate (or subtracted from expected cash flows) when performing **cross-border capital budgeting, foreign direct investment (FDI) valuation, and international portfolio construction**.

The risk is typically broken down into the following operational layers:

| Sub-Risk Layer | Definition (KTU Terminology) |
|---|---|
| **Political Risk** | Risk of loss due to government action, expropriation, war, or regime change. |
| **Economic Risk** | Risk arising from macroeconomic instability — high inflation, low growth, current account deficits. |
| **Sovereign Risk** | Risk that a national government defaults on its debt obligations. |
| **Currency / FX Risk** | Risk of loss due to adverse movements in the exchange rate between the host and home currencies. |
| **Transfer Risk** | Risk that the host country imposes capital controls, preventing repatriation of profits. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Foreign Tourist's Wallet" Intuition

Imagine you are a tourist travelling from India to a country you have never visited. Before spending money, you worry about:

1. Will my **money actually work** here? *(Currency / FX Risk)*
2. Will the **government suddenly seize** my hotel property? *(Expropriation / Political Risk)*
3. Will the **local economy collapse**, making my hotel worthless? *(Economic Risk)*
4. Can I **take my profits home** without restrictions? *(Transfer Risk)*

> [!NOTE]
> **The Tourist Analogy** — Country risk for an international investor is exactly the same worry, but scaled to **millions of dollars** of capital and **multi-year** horizons. The "host country" is the hotel, and the "tourist" is the multinational corporation (MNC) or foreign portfolio investor.

---

## 1.3 Why Country Risk Matters in Corporate Finance

For a firm evaluating a foreign project (e.g., setting up a plant in Brazil or Vietnam), the **weighted average cost of capital (WACC)** used to discount the foreign cash flows must reflect the elevated risk of operating in that country. Ignoring country risk leads to:

- Over-valuation of foreign projects
- Capital misallocation
- Sudden value destruction during crisis events (e.g., 1997 Asian Crisis, 2001 Argentina Default, 2014 Russia Sanctions)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Country Risk Assessment

### Pillar 1 — Political Risk Analysis

This includes both **macro-political risk** (war, revolution, sanctions) and **micro-political risk** (sector-specific regulations, contract repudiation). Key indicators monitored:

- Government stability index
- Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI)
- Regulatory quality
- Rule of law index
- Political risk services (PRS) ratings

### Pillar 2 — Economic Risk Analysis

This captures **macroeconomic vulnerability**:

- Inflation rate volatility
- GDP growth rate and volatility
- Current account deficit / surplus
- External debt-to-GDP ratio
- Foreign exchange reserves
- Fiscal deficit

### Pillar 3 — Sovereign / Financial Risk

Captures the probability that the **central government** will default on its external debt obligations. This is most commonly measured using:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sovereign Yield Spread** = Yield on Country's 10-Year Government Bond **−** Yield on US (or German) 10-Year Benchmark Bond

This spread represents the **default premium** demanded by global investors for lending to that government.

---

## 2.2 Components of Country Risk — Structured Logic

1. **Identify the Host Country** → Establish the geographic exposure.
2. **Quantify Political Stability** → Use indices like the **International Country Risk Guide (ICRG)**.
3. **Measure Sovereign Default Spread** → Compare the country's 10-year bond yield to the home country's benchmark.
4. **Adjust for Equity Volatility** → Because equities are more volatile than bonds, the raw bond spread **underestimates** the equity country risk premium.
5. **Compute the Adjusted Discount Rate** → Add the Country Risk Premium (CRP) to the standard CAPM-based cost of capital.
6. **Re-value the Project** → Discount the FCFF or FCFE of the foreign project using the adjusted rate.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield reference** for solving any country risk numerical problem in the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sovereign Yield Spread | $\Delta_s = Y_{\text{country}} - Y_{\text{benchmark}}$ | Direct measure of sovereign default risk. |
| 2 | Country Risk Premium (Damodaran Approach) | $CRP = \Delta_s \times \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{equity}}}{\sigma_{\text{bond}}}$ | Adjusts bond spread using equity-to-bond volatility ratio. |
| 3 | Country Risk Premium (Simplified) | $CRP \approx \Delta_s$ | Used when volatility ratio is unavailable. |
| 4 | Adjusted CAPM (Foreign Project) | $k_e = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f) + CRP$ | Cost of equity for a foreign project. |
| 5 | Adjusted WACC (Foreign Project) | $k_{\text{WACC}} = w_e \cdot k_e + w_d \cdot k_d \cdot (1-t) + CRP$ | Foreign project discount rate. |
| 6 | Adjusted Discount Rate Method (DCF) | $PV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+k_{\text{adj}})^t}$ | Present value of foreign cash flows. |
| 7 | Cash Flow Adjustment Method | $CF_{\text{adj},t} = CF_t \times (1 - \text{Probability of Loss})$ | Reduces expected cash flow by expected political loss. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Convention** — All yields, spreads, and premiums must be expressed in **decimal form** (e.g., 8% = **0.08**) before substitution into the formulas.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Finance Utility

Country risk analysis is critical in:

- **FDI appraisal** for Indian conglomerates (Tata, Reliance, Adani) entering Africa, ASEAN, or Latin America.
- **International portfolio management** — deciding equity weights in emerging markets.
- **Export-import finance** — banks pricing Letters of Credit for foreign buyers.
- **Project finance** — lenders requiring political risk insurance (e.g., MIGA cover) for infrastructure in fragile states.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sovereign Yield Spread

The **Sovereign Yield Spread** ($\Delta_s$) is the foundational building block of country risk premium calculation. It is derived as follows.

Let:
- $Y_c$ = Yield to maturity on the **foreign country's** 10-year government bond (denominated in local currency or USD).
- $Y_b$ = Yield to maturity on the **risk-free benchmark** bond (typically US 10-Year Treasury).

> The spread reflects the additional return investors demand for bearing the credit and political risk of lending to the foreign sovereign.

$$
\Delta_s = Y_c - Y_b
$$

**Example:**
If Brazil's 10-Year bond yields 11.5% and the US 10-Year Treasury yields 4.0%:

$$
\Delta_s = 11.5\% - 4.0\% = 7.5\%
$$

This **7.5%** is the **default risk premium** the market demands for Brazilian sovereign exposure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Country Risk Premium (Damodaran's Volatility-Adjusted Method)

A bond spread alone **understates** the true equity country risk because equities have higher volatility than bonds. Damodaran (NYU) proposed scaling the bond spread by the ratio of equity volatility to bond volatility:

Let:
- $\sigma_e$ = Annualized volatility of the country's equity market index.
- $\sigma_b$ = Annualized volatility of the country's sovereign bond index.

$$
CRP = \Delta_s \times \frac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_b}
$$

**Example:**
Using the Brazilian data:
- $\Delta_s = 7.5\%$
- $\sigma_e = 28\%$ (Bovespa annual volatility)
- $\sigma_b = 12\%$ (Brazilian sovereign bond volatility)

$$
CRP = 7.5\% \times \frac{28\%}{12\%} = 7.5\% \times 2.3333 = 17.50\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic behind the scaling:** A 7.5% bond spread represents compensation for default risk on debt. Equity investors face the same default risk plus additional market volatility. Hence, the equity risk premium must be scaled up by the volatility ratio.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Adjusted Cost of Equity (CAPM + CRP)

The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) is extended to incorporate country risk for foreign projects:

Standard domestic CAPM:

$$
k_e^{\text{domestic}} = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f)
$$

For a foreign project, we add the **Country Risk Premium** ($CRP$):

$$
k_e^{\text{foreign}} = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f) + CRP
$$

**Example:**
- $R_f = 4.0\%$ (US 10-Year Treasury)
- $\beta = 1.2$ (project sensitivity to global market)
- $R_m - R_f = 5.5\%$ (equity risk premium)
- $CRP = 17.50\%$ (computed in Derivation 2)

$$
k_e = 4.0\% + 1.2 \times 5.5\% + 17.50\%
$$

$$
k_e = 4.0\% + 6.6\% + 17.50\%
$$

$$
k_e = 28.10\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Full Worked Example (Foreign Project Valuation)

An Indian MNC is evaluating a project in Vietnam with the following data:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Expected FCFF next year ($CF_1$) | ₹100 crore |
| Constant growth rate ($g$) | 4% |
| Domestic Rf | 6% |
| Equity Risk Premium (India) | 6.5% |
| Beta of project | 1.1 |
| Vietnam 10-Year bond yield | 8.5% |
| India 10-Year G-Sec yield | 6.0% |
| Vietnam equity volatility | 24% |
| Vietnam bond volatility | 10% |

**Step 1 — Compute Sovereign Yield Spread:**

$$
\Delta_s = 8.5\% - 6.0\% = 2.5\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Country Risk Premium:**

$$
CRP = 2.5\% \times \frac{24\%}{10\%} = 2.5\% \times 2.4 = 6.0\%
$$

**Step 3 — Adjusted Cost of Equity:**

$$
k_e = 6.0\% + 1.1 \times 6.5\% + 6.0\%
$$

$$
k_e = 6.0\% + 7.15\% + 6.0\%
$$

$$
k_e = 19.15\%
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Gordon Growth Model:**

$$
V_0 = \frac{CF_1}{k_e - g} = \frac{100}{0.1915 - 0.04}
$$

$$
V_0 = \frac{100}{0.1515}
$$

$$
V_0 \approx ₹660.07 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparative Analysis (Domestic Only):**
If the same project were located in India (no CRP):

$$
k_e^{\text{domestic}} = 6.0\% + 1.1 \times 6.5\% = 13.15\%
$$

$$
V_0^{\text{domestic}} = \frac{100}{0.1315 - 0.04} = \frac{100}{0.0915} \approx ₹1,092.90 \text{ crore}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight** — The **country risk premium of 6%** reduced the project value from ₹1,092.90 crore (domestic) to ₹660.07 crore (foreign) — a **39.6% drop in valuation** purely due to country risk. This is why every KTU valuation question on cross-border investment MUST incorporate CRP.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component Architecture of Country Risk

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[COUNTRY RISK] --> B[Political Risk]
    A --> C[Economic Risk]
    A --> D[Financial Sovereign Risk]
    A --> E[Currency FX Risk]
    A --> F[Transfer Risk]
    
    B --> B1[Government Instability]
    B --> B2[Expropriation]
    B --> B3[War and Conflict]
    B --> B4[Regulatory Change]
    
    C --> C1[Inflation Volatility]
    C --> C2[GDP Growth Volatility]
    C --> C3[Fiscal Deficit]
    C --> C4[Current Account Deficit]
    
    D --> D1[Sovereign Default]
    D --> D2[Sovereign Bond Spread]
    D --> D3[Debt to GDP Ratio]
    
    E --> E1[Currency Depreciation]
    E --> E2[Exchange Rate Volatility]
    
    F --> F1[Capital Controls]
    F --> F2[Profit Repatriation Limits]
```

## 4.2 Country Risk Premium Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start Identify Host Country] --> B[Gather Sovereign Bond Yields]
    B --> C[Compute Yield Spread delta]
    C --> D{Is Volatility Data Available?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Compute Volatility Ratio sigmaE over sigmaB]
    D -->|No| F[Use Spread Directly as CRP]
    E --> G[CRP equals delta times Volatility Ratio]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Add CRP to CAPM Cost of Equity]
    H --> I[Apply to Foreign Project Discount Rate]
    I --> J[Re-evaluate Project NPV and IRR]
```

## 4.3 Two Methods of Incorporating Country Risk in DCF

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Foreign Project Cash Flows] --> B{Choose Method}
    B -->|Method 1 Adjusted Discount Rate| C[Add CRP to WACC]
    B -->|Method 2 Adjusted Cash Flows| D[Reduce CF by Probability of Loss]
    C --> E[Discount at Higher Rate]
    D --> F[Discount at Domestic Rate on Lower CF]
    E --> G[Project NPV Foreign]
    F --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Define Country Risk and list its major components.**

**Model Answer:**

Country Risk refers to the risk of financial loss arising from investing or operating in a foreign country due to political, economic, financial, currency, and transfer-related uncertainties that affect the expected return on cross-border investments.

Major Components:
1. **Political Risk** — government instability, expropriation, war, regulatory changes.
2. **Economic Risk** — high inflation, low growth, fiscal imbalances.
3. **Sovereign Risk** — risk of government default on debt.
4. **Currency Risk** — adverse exchange rate movements.
5. **Transfer Risk** — restrictions on repatriation of capital and profits.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Explain the concept of Sovereign Yield Spread and its role in measuring country risk.**

**Model Answer:**

The Sovereign Yield Spread is the difference in yields between a foreign country's 10-year government bond and the home country's (benchmark) 10-year risk-free bond.

$$
\Delta_s = Y_{\text{foreign bond}} - Y_{\text{benchmark bond}}
$$

It serves as a **proxy for default risk** and is the foundational input for computing the **Country Risk Premium (CRP)**. A higher spread indicates greater perceived default risk and political instability.

---

## 📌 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**An Indian firm is evaluating a manufacturing project in Argentina. The following data is provided:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Expected FCFF for Year 1 | ₹150 crore |
| Constant growth rate | 3% |
| Risk-free rate (India 10Y G-Sec) | 6.5% |
| Equity Risk Premium (Indian market) | 6% |
| Beta of project | 1.15 |
| Argentina 10-Year Sovereign Bond Yield | 12.5% |
| India 10-Year Sovereign Bond Yield | 6.5% |
| Argentina Equity Market Volatility (annualized) | 35% |
| Argentina Bond Index Volatility (annualized) | 14% |

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the Sovereign Yield Spread, Country Risk Premium, and the Adjusted Cost of Equity for the Argentine project.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the present value of the project using the Gordon Growth Model and comment on how country risk affects the valuation.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Sovereign Yield Spread:**

$$
\Delta_s = Y_{\text{Argentina}} - Y_{\text{India}} = 12.5\% - 6.5\% = 6.0\%
$$

**Step 2 — Country Risk Premium (Damodaran Method):**

$$
CRP = \Delta_s \times \frac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_b} = 6.0\% \times \frac{35\%}{14\%}
$$

$$
CRP = 6.0\% \times 2.5 = 15.0\%
$$

**Step 3 — Adjusted Cost of Equity:**

$$
k_e = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f) + CRP
$$

$$
k_e = 6.5\% + 1.15 \times 6\% + 15.0\%
$$

$$
k_e = 6.5\% + 6.9\% + 15.0\%
$$

$$
k_e = 28.4\%
$$

**Conclusion of Part (a):** The **CRP of 15%** significantly inflates the cost of equity beyond the standard CAPM estimate (13.4%), reflecting Argentina's elevated country risk profile.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Apply Gordon Growth Model:**

$$
V_0 = \frac{CF_1}{k_e - g} = \frac{150}{0.284 - 0.03}
$$

$$
V_0 = \frac{150}{0.254}
$$

$$
V_0 \approx ₹590.55 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 2 — Domestic Counterfactual (No CRP):**

$$
k_e^{\text{domestic}} = 6.5\% + 1.15 \times 6\% = 13.4\%
$$

$$
V_0^{\text{domestic}} = \frac{150}{0.134 - 0.03} = \frac{150}{0.104} \approx ₹1,442.31 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 3 — Loss in Value Due to Country Risk:**

$$
\Delta V = 1,442.31 - 590.55 = ₹851.76 \text{ crore}
$$

$$
\text{Percentage Loss} = \frac{851.76}{1,442.31} \times 100 \approx 59.05\%
$$

**Comment:**
Country risk has caused a **~59% erosion in project value**, demonstrating that ignoring CRP in cross-border valuation leads to gross over-valuation and potentially catastrophic investment decisions.

---

### ❓ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **two methods** of incorporating country risk in foreign project valuation: (i) Adjusted Discount Rate Method and (ii) Adjusted Cash Flow Method. Use appropriate formulas.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A firm is considering a 5-year project in Country X with the following data:

- Initial Investment: ₹500 crore
- Annual FCFF: ₹120 crore (constant)
- Country X Sovereign Spread: 4.5%
- Volatility Ratio ($\sigma_e / \sigma_b$): 1.8
- Domestic Cost of Equity: 13%
- Probability of Political Loss per year: 3%
- Expected Loss Amount per year: 10% of FCFF

Compute the project NPV under **both methods** and compare.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Method 1 — Adjusted Discount Rate Method:**

The Country Risk Premium (CRP) is added to the standard cost of capital, and the foreign cash flows are discounted at this higher rate.

$$
k_{\text{adjusted}} = k_{\text{domestic}} + CRP
$$

$$
PV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + k_{\text{adjusted}})^t}
$$

**Method 2 — Adjusted Cash Flow Method:**

The expected cash flows are reduced by the probability-weighted political loss, and then discounted at the **domestic** cost of capital.

$$
CF_{\text{adjusted}} = CF_t \times (1 - p_{\text{loss}} \times L)
$$

where $p_{\text{loss}}$ is the probability of political loss and $L$ is the loss severity (fraction of CF lost).

$$
PV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t^{\text{adj}}}{(1 + k_{\text{domestic}})^t}
$$

**Comparison:**
- Method 1 adjusts the **discount rate** (right side of NPV equation).
- Method 2 adjusts the **cash flows** (left side of NPV equation).
- Both should yield the **same NPV** if applied consistently (put-call parity equivalence).
- Method 1 is more common in practice due to data availability on sovereign spreads.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute CRP:**

$$
CRP = \Delta_s \times \frac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_b} = 4.5\% \times 1.8 = 8.1\%
$$

**Step 2 — Adjusted Discount Rate:**

$$
k_{\text{adj}} = 13\% + 8.1\% = 21.1\%
$$

**Step 3 — NPV (Method 1):**

Assuming a constant annuity of ₹120 crore for 5 years:

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = 120 \times \frac{1 - (1 + 0.211)^{-5}}{0.211}
$$

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = 120 \times \frac{1 - (1.211)^{-5}}{0.211}
$$

$$
(1.211)^{-5} = \frac{1}{(1.211)^5}
$$

$$
(1.211)^2 = 1.4665
$$

$$
(1.211)^4 = (1.4665)^2 = 2.1506
$$

$$
(1.211)^5 = 2.1506 \times 1.211 = 2.6044
$$

$$
(1.211)^{-5} = \frac{1}{2.6044} \approx 0.3840
$$

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = 120 \times \frac{1 - 0.3840}{0.211} = 120 \times \frac{0.6160}{0.211}
$$

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = 120 \times 2.9194 \approx 350.33 \text{ crore}
$$

$$
NPV_{\text{Method 1}} = 350.33 - 500 = -149.67 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 4 — Adjusted Cash Flow (Method 2):**

Expected political loss per year = $0.03 \times 0.10 = 0.003$ (i.e., 0.3% reduction)

$$
CF_{\text{adj}} = 120 \times (1 - 0.003) = 120 \times 0.997 = 119.64 \text{ crore}
$$

Discount at **domestic rate** of 13%:

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = 119.64 \times \frac{1 - (1.13)^{-5}}{0.13}
$$

$$
(1.13)^5 \approx 1.8424
$$

$$
(1.13)^{-5} \approx 0.5428
$$

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = 119.64 \times \frac{1 - 0.5428}{0.13} = 119.64 \times \frac{0.4572}{0.13}
$$

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = 119.64 \times 3.5172 \approx 420.81 \text{ crore}
$$

$$
NPV_{\text{Method 2}} = 420.81 - 500 = -79.19 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

| Method | Adjusted Rate | Adjusted CF | NPV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Method 1 — Adjusted Discount Rate | 21.1% | ₹120 cr | **−₹149.67 cr** |
| Method 2 — Adjusted Cash Flow | 13.0% | ₹119.64 cr | **−₹79.19 cr** |

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Students forget to convert percentages into decimals (e.g., writing 21.1 instead of 0.211). This alone causes a **2-mark deduction** in KTU papers.
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Confusing the **Sovereign Spread** with the **Country Risk Premium**. The spread is a *bond* metric; the CRP is the *equity* equivalent (after volatility scaling).
> - **Common Mistake #3**: Forgetting to add the CRP to the *cost of equity*, not the WACC directly (unless the project is fully equity-financed).
> - **Common Mistake #4**: Using the **domestic risk-free rate** in the CAPM formula for a foreign project. Always use the **home-country Rf** for consistency.
> - **Common Mistake #5**: In Gordon Growth Model, students often use the **incorrect denominator** ($k - g$). Ensure both are in **decimal form** and $k > g$.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Country Risk (PEIET744, Module 1)**

- 🔹 **Country Risk** = Political + Economic + Sovereign + Currency + Transfer risk.
- 🔹 **Sovereign Yield Spread** = Foreign 10Y bond yield − Benchmark 10Y bond yield.
- 🔹 **Damodaran CRP Formula**: $CRP = \Delta_s \times \dfrac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_b}$.
- 🔹 **Simplified CRP**: $CRP \approx \Delta_s$ (when volatility data unavailable).
- 🔹 **Adjusted CAPM**: $k_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f) + CRP$.
- 🔹 **Two Methods of Incorporation**: (1) Adjusted Discount Rate, (2) Adjusted Cash Flows.
- 🔹 **Always convert percentages to decimals** before substitution.
- 🔹 **Country risk is non-diversifiable** for MNCs heavily concentrated in one foreign market.
- 🔹 **Political Risk Insurance** (e.g., from MIGA, OPIC) can offset — but not eliminate — country risk.
- 🔹 **Gordon Growth Model with CRP**: $V_0 = \dfrac{CF_1}{k_e + CRP - g}$.
- 🔹 **KTU Examiner expects**: explicit formula, correct substitution, intermediate steps, and a concluding remark on impact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marginal Cost of Capital Schedule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Marginal Cost of Capital Schedule ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marginal Cost of Capital (MCC):** The marginal cost of capital is the **weighted average cost of the next (additional) rupee of capital** that a firm raises. It is not the historical or book-cost of capital already employed, but the cost of *new* financing required to fund the next investment project. > [!NOTE] > **Marginal Cost of Capital Schedule:** It is a graphical and tabular representation (a step function) that depicts the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) at successive levels of total new capital raised**. Each "step" in the schedule corresponds to a financing tier where the WACC remains constant, and the "rises" occur at **break points** — the financing levels at which one source of capital becomes exhausted and a costlier source must be tapped. The schedule is plotted with **Total New Capital Raised (₹)** on the X-axis and **Marginal Cost of Capital (WACC %)** on the Y-axis. The curve is monotonically non-decreasing (typically rising) because, as the firm raises more and more capital, the cheapest sources (retained earnings, existing debt capacity) get exhausted and the firm must shift to progressively costlier sources (new equity, additional debt at higher yields, preferred stock). ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a small shopkeeper filling water in a bucket from three different pots: - **Pot 1 (Free well-water):** First 50 litres are free (analogy: Retained Earnings — no flotation cost, no underpricing). - **Pot 2 (Municipal tap water at ₹5/litre):** The next 100 litres come from the municipal line (analogy: Debt / Low-cost loans). - **Pot 3 (Packaged water at ₹20/litre):** Anything beyond that must be bought as bottles (analogy: New Common Stock with flotation & underwriting costs). The *average cost per litre* keeps rising as the shopkeeper moves from Pot 1 → Pot 2 → Pot 3. The **Marginal Cost of Capital Schedule** is precisely this "average cost per litre" plotted against the *cumulative litres drawn* — and the points where the shopkeeper switches pots are the **Break Points**. > [!TIP] > **Why does the schedule matter in practice?** > In capital budgeting, a project is accepted **only if its Internal Rate of Return (IRR) exceeds the *marginal* cost of capital** at the financing level the project requires — not the historical average cost. Hence, the MCC Schedule is the **hurdle-rate schedule** that determines which projects clear the bar. ### 1.3 Order of Cost of Various Sources (from cheapest to costliest) In a typical firm under normal market conditions: $$\text{Retained Earnings} < \text{After-tax Debt} < \text{Preferred Stock} < \text{New Common Equity}$$ This natural ordering is the *reason* the MCC schedule is a **rising step function** rather than a flat line. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework A firm finances its investment opportunities using a **target (optimal) capital structure** — a pre-specified mix of equity, preference capital, and debt, expressed as weights $W_e$, $W_p$, $W_d$ such that: $$W_e + W_p + W_d = 1$$ The **component costs** ($K$) used in MCC computation are: | Component | Notation | Typical Value Drivers | |---|---|---| | Cost of Retained Earnings | $K_r$ | Dividend yield + growth, adjusted for opportunity cost | | Cost of New Common Stock | $K_n$ | $K_n > K_r$ due to flotation & underpricing | | After-tax Cost of Debt | $K_d (1-t)$ | Coupon rate, tax shield, market yield | | Cost of Preferred Stock | $K_p$ | Fixed dividend, issue price | The **WACC (also called $K_0$ or $K_a$)** is the weighted sum: $$K_0 = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d (1-t)$$ ### 2.2 The Break Point (BP) Concept A **Break Point** is the **total amount of new capital** that can be raised *before* a particular cheaper source is exhausted, given the target weight of that source in the capital structure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The break point is computed on the **total new financing**, not on the specific source. If retained earnings of ₹X are available and equity weight is $W_e$, then the break point is $\frac{X}{W_e}$. $$BP_j = \frac{\text{Amount of capital available from source } j \text{ at the existing cost}}{\text{Weight of source } j \text{ in the capital structure}}$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Weighted Average Cost of Capital | $K_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i K_i$ | Weights sum to 1; $K_i$ in % | | 2 | Cost of Equity (Dividend Growth Model) | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $D_1$ = expected dividend; $P_0$ = market price; $g$ = growth | | 3 | Cost of Retained Earnings | $K_r = K_e \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{brokerage}}{\text{realised price}}\right)$ or simply $K_r \approx K_e$ | No new issue, but opportunity cost exists | | 4 | Cost of New Common Stock | $K_n = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0(1-f)} + g$ | $F$ = flotation cost; $f$ = flotation % | | 5 | After-tax Cost of Debt | $K_d(1-t)$ | $t$ = corporate tax rate | | 6 | Cost of Preferred Stock | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_p - F_p}$ | Perpetual dividend | | 7 | Break Point | $BP_j = \dfrac{A_j}{W_j}$ | $A_j$ = amount of source $j$; $W_j$ = weight | | 8 | MCC Schedule Slope | $\Delta K_0 / \Delta \text{Capital}$ | Zero within a tier, jumps at BP | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility (for Industrial/B.Tech context):** A large infrastructure / manufacturing project requires a *multi-year* capital deployment plan. By mapping the MCC schedule against the **Investment Opportunity Schedule (IOS)**, the firm identifies the **optimal capital budget** — the point where the IOS curve and MCC schedule intersect. This is the cornerstone of capital rationing decisions. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Break Point Formula **Setup:** Suppose the target capital structure is: - Equity weight $= W_e = 0.50$ - Debt weight $= W_d = 0.30$ - Preferred weight $= W_p = 0.20$ Total new capital to be raised $= C$. Then the firm will raise: $$\text{Equity portion} = 0.50 \times C \quad ; \quad \text{Debt portion} = 0.30 \times C \quad ; \quad \text{Preferred} = 0.20 \times C$$ Now suppose **retained earnings** available at cost $K_r$ are limited to $\text{RE}_{\max}$. The break point is the value of $C$ at which the equity portion exactly equals $\text{RE}_{\max}$: $$W_e \times BP_{RE} = \text{RE}_{\max} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; BP_{RE} = \frac{\text{RE}_{\max}}{W_e}$$ This is the **largest total capital** the firm can raise while still using only retained earnings for the equity portion. Beyond this $C$, the firm must switch to *new* common stock with cost $K_n > K_r$, raising the WACC. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Generalised Break Point Formula:** For any source $j$ of amount $A_j$ and weight $W_j$: > $$BP_j = \frac{A_j}{W_j}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Derivation Walk-through) **Problem Data (a typical KTU 14-mark problem):** A firm's target capital structure is: | Source | Weight ($W_i$) | Cost ($K_i$) | Amount available at this cost (₹) | |---|---|---|---| | Retained Earnings | 0.50 | 15% | 1,00,000 | | New Common Stock | (same weight) | 17% | Unlimited | | Debt | 0.30 | 10% (after tax) | 1,00,000 | | Debt (next tranche) | 0.30 | 11% (after tax) | Unlimited | | Preferred Stock | 0.20 | 12% | 80,000 | | Preferred Stock (next tranche) | 0.20 | 13% | Unlimited | Corporate tax rate $t = 0$ (ignored for simplicity in after-tax cost). #### Step 1 — Identify Break Points $$ \begin{aligned} BP_1 \;(\text{RE exhausted}) &= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{0.50} = 2{,}00{,}000 \\ BP_2 \;(\text{low-cost Debt exhausted}) &= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{0.30} = 3{,}33{,}333.33 \\ BP_3 \;(\text{low-cost Preferred exhausted}) &= \frac{80{,}000}{0.20} = 4{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 2 — Define the Financing Tiers | Tier | Range of Total New Capital (₹) | Sources & Costs | |---|---|---| | I | $0 < C \leq 2{,}00{,}000$ | RE @ 15%, Debt @ 10%, Pref @ 12% | | II | $2{,}00{,}000 < C \leq 3{,}33{,}333$ | New CS @ 17%, Debt @ 10%, Pref @ 12% | | III | $3{,}33{,}333 < C \leq 4{,}00{,}000$ | New CS @ 17%, Debt @ 11%, Pref @ 12% | | IV | $C > 4{,}00{,}000$ | New CS @ 17%, Debt @ 11%, Pref @ 13% | #### Step 3 — Compute WACC ($K_0$) in Each Tier **Tier I** ($C \in (0, 2{,}00{,}000]$): $$ \begin{aligned} K_0^I &= 0.50(15\%) + 0.30(10\%) + 0.20(12\%) \\ &= 7.50\% + 3.00\% + 2.40\% \\ &= 12.90\% \end{aligned} $$ **Tier II** ($C \in (2{,}00{,}000, 3{,}33{,}333]$): $$ \begin{aligned} K_0^{II} &= 0.50(17\%) + 0.30(10\%) + 0.20(12\%) \\ &= 8.50\% + 3.00\% + 2.40\% \\ &= 13.90\% \end{aligned} $$ **Tier III** ($C \in (3{,}33{,}333, 4{,}00{,}000]$): $$ \begin{aligned} K_0^{III} &= 0.50(17\%) + 0.30(11\%) + 0.20(12\%) \\ &= 8.50\% + 3.30\% + 2.40\% \\ &= 14.20\% \end{aligned} $$ **Tier IV** ($C > 4{,}00{,}000$): $$ \begin{aligned} K_0^{IV} &= 0.50(17\%) + 0.30(11\%) + 0.20(13\%) \\ &= 8.50\% + 3.30\% + 2.60\% \\ &= 14.40\% \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4 — Assemble the MCC Schedule | Total Capital Raised (₹) | Marginal Cost of Capital (WACC) | |---|---| | Up to 2,00,000 | 12.90% | | 2,00,001 to 3,33,333 | 13.90% | | 3,33,334 to 4,00,000 | 14.20% | | Above 4,00,000 | 14.40% | > [!NOTE] > The schedule is a **right-stepping curve** — flat within each tier, with discrete upward jumps precisely at the break points. The horizontal axis represents cumulative new capital, and the vertical axis represents the WACC for the *next* rupee of capital beyond that point. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU 14-Mark Sub-Questions | Sub-Question Type | Procedure | Marks Weightage | |---|---|---| | Compute WACC at a given financing level | Substitute weights and component costs into $K_0 = \sum W_i K_i$ | 2–3 marks | | Determine a Break Point | Use $BP = A_j / W_j$ with the relevant source amount and its weight | 2 marks | | Identify the cost of new common stock | $K_n = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g$ or $\dfrac{D_1}{P_0(1-f)} + g$ | 2 marks | | Draw / Tabulate the MCC schedule | Tabulate WACC at each tier; plot a step diagram | 3–4 marks | | Combine MCC with IOS for capital budgeting decision | Find intersection → optimal capital budget | 3–4 marks | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capital Cost Hierarchy (Step-Function Logic) ```mermaid flowchart LR Input[/"Total New Capital Required: C"/] --> T1{Tier 1
C ≤ 2,00,000} T1 -- "Yes" --> S1["Sources: RE + Low Debt + Low Pref
K0 = 12.90%"] T1 -- "No" --> T2{Tier 2
C ≤ 3,33,333} T2 -- "Yes" --> S2["Sources: New CS + Low Debt + Low Pref
K0 = 13.90%"] T2 -- "No" --> T3{Tier 3
C ≤ 4,00,000} T3 -- "Yes" --> S3["Sources: New CS + High Debt + Low Pref
K0 = 14.20%"] T3 -- "No" --> S4["Sources: New CS + High Debt + High Pref
K0 = 14.40%"] S1 --> Out[/"MCC Schedule Point"/] S2 --> Out S3 --> Out S4 --> Out ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow of MCC Computation ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Capital Structure Data
Weights and Component Costs"] --> B["Identify Component-wise
Available Amounts"] B --> C["Compute Break Points
BP_j = A_j / W_j"] C --> D["Sort Break Points
in Ascending Order"] D --> E["Define Financing Tiers
between consecutive BPs"] E --> F["Compute WACC in Each Tier
K0 = Sum W_i * K_i"] F --> G["Tabulate K0 vs Total Capital"] G --> H["Plot Step Function
MCC Schedule"] H --> I["Overlay Investment
Opportunity Schedule IOS"] I --> J[/"Optimal Capital Budget
and Hurdle Rate"/] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid XY-Chart — Visualising the Step Function (MCC Schedule) ```mermaid xychart-beta title "Marginal Cost of Capital Schedule" x-axis "Cumulative New Capital (in INR Lakhs)" [0, 1, 2, 2.01, 3, 3.34, 4, 4.5] y-axis "WACC Percent" 12 --> 15 line [12.9, 12.9, 12.9, 13.9, 13.9, 14.2, 14.2, 14.4] ``` > [!TIP] > The horizontal segments in the chart represent **constant marginal cost within a financing tier**. The vertical jumps at $C = 2{,}00{,}000$, $C = 3{,}33{,}333$ and $C = 4{,}00{,}000$ correspond exactly to the break points derived in Section 3.2. ### 4.4 Sequence Flow — Decision Logic During Capital Raising ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Firm participant Market participant FinanceTeam Firm->>FinanceTeam: Initiate capital raise of amount C FinanceTeam->>Firm: Compute total C and weights FinanceTeam->>Market: Check cheapest source availability alt RE available Market-->>FinanceTeam: Use Retained Earnings else RE exhausted Market-->>FinanceTeam: Issue new common stock end alt Low-cost debt available Market-->>FinanceTeam: Issue low-coupon debt else Debt capacity exhausted Market-->>FinanceTeam: Issue higher-yield debt end FinanceTeam->>Firm: Compute WACC for next rupee FinanceTeam->>Firm: Update MCC schedule ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2-Mark & 3-Mark) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 2 Marks]** *Define the term "Marginal Cost of Capital". How is it different from the "Average Cost of Capital"?* **Model Answer:** The **Marginal Cost of Capital (MCC)** is the cost of raising an additional unit of capital — i.e., the weighted average cost of the *next* rupee of financing. The **Average Cost of Capital (ACC / WACC)** is the weighted cost of capital *already raised and employed* by the firm (historical or book-value weighted). **Key differences:** | Aspect | Marginal Cost of Capital | Average Cost of Capital | |---|---|---| | Basis | Future / next rupee | Past / total employed | | Use | Hurdle rate for new projects | Disclosure & valuation | | Behaviour | Step function (rises at break points) | Single weighted average | | Decision role | Accept/Reject new investments | Performance measurement | **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 3 Marks]** *What is a "Break Point" in the context of the Marginal Cost of Capital Schedule? Illustrate with a simple formula.* **Model Answer:** A **Break Point (BP)** is the total amount of new financing that can be raised before a particular source of capital (with a given cost) is exhausted. Beyond the BP, the firm must switch to a costlier source, causing a discrete jump in the WACC. $$BP_j = \frac{\text{Amount available from source } j \text{ at cost } K_j}{\text{Weight of source } j \text{ in capital structure}}$$ **Example:** If retained earnings of ₹5,00,000 are available and equity weight is 0.50, then $BP = 5{,}00{,}000 / 0.50 = 10{,}00{,}000$. Beyond ₹10 lakh of new capital, the equity portion must be funded by *new* common stock. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations for End Semester Evaluation (ESE), Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with internal choice (Choice-A or Choice-B). Each question typically has two sub-parts: (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]* **Stem:** XYZ Industries Ltd. has a target capital structure comprising **50% equity, 30% debt, and 20% preferred stock**. The relevant cost data is tabulated below: | Source | Cost | Amount available at this cost | |---|---|---| | Retained Earnings | 15% | ₹4,00,000 | | New Common Stock | 17% | Unlimited | | Debt (Tranche 1) | 9% (after tax) | ₹3,00,000 | | Debt (Tranche 2) | 11% (after tax) | Unlimited | | Preferred Stock (Tier 1) | 12% | ₹1,50,000 | | Preferred Stock (Tier 2) | 14% | Unlimited | **(a) Compute the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) at each financing level. Identify all break points.** *(7 Marks)* **(b) Prepare the Marginal Cost of Capital Schedule. A project under consideration requires ₹15,00,000 in new financing and is expected to yield an IRR of 13.5%. Advise whether the project should be accepted.** *(7 Marks)* --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Step 1: Break Points** $$ \begin{aligned} BP_1 \;(\text{RE exhausted}) &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{0.50} = 8{,}00{,}000 \\ BP_2 \;(\text{Debt Tranche 1 exhausted}) &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{0.30} = 10{,}00{,}000 \\ BP_3 \;(\text{Preferred Tier 1 exhausted}) &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{0.20} = 7{,}50{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Order the break points in ascending order** $$BP_3 = 7{,}50{,}000 \;<\; BP_1 = 8{,}00{,}000 \;<\; BP_2 = 10{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 3: Define Tiers and Compute WACC** **Tier I** — $0 < C \leq 7{,}50{,}000$ (RE @ 15%, Debt @ 9%, Pref @ 12%) $$K_0^I = 0.50(15) + 0.30(9) + 0.20(12) = 7.50 + 2.70 + 2.40 = 12.60\%$$ **Tier II** — $7{,}50{,}000 < C \leq 8{,}00{,}000$ (RE @ 15%, Debt @ 9%, Pref @ 14%) $$K_0^{II} = 0.50(15) + 0.30(9) + 0.20(14) = 7.50 + 2.70 + 2.80 = 13.00\%$$ **Tier III** — $8{,}00{,}000 < C \leq 10{,}00{,}000$ (New CS @ 17%, Debt @ 9%, Pref @ 14%) $$K_0^{III} = 0.50(17) + 0.30(9) + 0.20(14) = 8.50 + 2.70 + 2.80 = 14.00\%$$ **Tier IV** — $C > 10{,}00{,}000$ (New CS @ 17%, Debt @ 11%, Pref @ 14%) $$K_0^{IV} = 0.50(17) + 0.30(11) + 0.20(14) = 8.50 + 3.30 + 2.80 = 14.60\%$$ --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **Step 1: MCC Schedule Table** | Total Capital Raised (₹) | Marginal Cost (WACC) | |---|---| | Up to 7,50,000 | 12.60% | | 7,50,001 to 8,00,000 | 13.00% | | 8,00,001 to 10,00,000 | 14.00% | | Above 10,00,000 | 14.60% | **Step 2: Marginal Cost Applicable to the Project** The project requires **₹15,00,000**, which lies in **Tier IV** (since ₹15,00,000 > ₹10,00,000). The applicable marginal cost is **$K_0^{IV} = 14.60\%$**. **Step 3: Decision** - Project IRR = **13.50%** - Marginal Cost of Capital = **14.60%** - Since $\text{IRR} = 13.50\% < K_0^{IV} = 14.60\%$, the project **destroys shareholder value** and **must be REJECTED**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Mixing up weights and amounts in break-point computation:** A common error is computing $BP = A_j \times W_j$ instead of $BP = A_j / W_j$. The denominator is the *weight*, not a multiplier. > 2. **Forgetting to recompute WACC after EACH break point:** Students often change only the cost of the exhausted source, but other sources may have multiple tiers — all active tiers must be reflected in each WACC. > 3. **Using historical / book-value weights instead of target (marginal) weights:** The MCC schedule is always based on the **target capital structure weights**, not the existing capital structure. > 4. **Comparing the project IRR to the AVERAGE cost of capital:** The decision must be against the **MARGINAL** cost, not the historical average. This is the single most common conceptual error. > 5. **Ignoring the order of break points:** Always arrange the break points in ascending order before defining the tiers; otherwise, the WACC values will be mis-assigned to wrong capital ranges. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **Stem:** PQR Engineering Ltd. finances its projects using a capital structure of **40% equity and 60% debt**. The firm has **₹2,00,000 of retained earnings** available at a cost of **13%**. Beyond that, the cost of new common stock rises to **15%**. The cost of debt is **10%** (after tax) up to **₹3,00,000**; beyond that, the cost rises to **12%** (after tax). Tax rate is **30%** and the pre-tax cost of the first debt tranche is **14.29%**. **(a) Calculate the break points and the WACC in each financing tier.** *(7 Marks)* **(b) Construct the MCC schedule. If a new project requires ₹9,00,000 and earns an IRR of 11.8%, should it be accepted? Justify using the schedule.** *(7 Marks)* --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Step 1: Compute Break Points** $$ \begin{aligned} BP_{RE} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = 5{,}00{,}000 \\ BP_{Debt} &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{0.60} = 5{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Define Tiers** - **Tier I:** $0 < C \leq 5{,}00{,}000$ — RE @ 13%, Debt @ 10% (after tax) - **Tier II:** $C > 5{,}00{,}000$ — New CS @ 15%, Debt @ 12% (after tax) **Step 3: Compute WACC** $$ \begin{aligned} K_0^I &= 0.40(13\%) + 0.60(10\%) = 5.20\% + 6.00\% = 11.20\% \\ K_0^{II} &= 0.40(15\%) + 0.60(12\%) = 6.00\% + 7.20\% = 13.20\% \end{aligned} $$ --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **MCC Schedule Table** | Total Capital Raised (₹) | Marginal Cost (WACC) | |---|---| | Up to 5,00,000 | 11.20% | | Above 5,00,000 | 13.20% | **Project Decision:** - Project size: ₹9,00,000 (falls in **Tier II**) - Applicable MCC = **13.20%** - Project IRR = **11.80%** - Decision: **REJECT** the project (11.80% < 13.20%). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Quick-Fire Revision — keep these points in mind before every KTU exam:** - **MCC is the cost of the NEXT rupee** — never the historical average. - **WACC formula** is $K_0 = W_e K_e + W_p K_p + W_d K_d(1-t)$. - **Break Point = Amount available ÷ Weight** (NOT amount × weight). - **Order of cost** (typical): Retained Earnings < Debt < Preferred Stock < New Equity. - **Cost of new equity** $K_n > K_e$ because of flotation costs and price-underpricing on new issues. - **The MCC schedule is a step function:** constant within tiers, jumping up at break points. - **Break points must be sorted ascending** before defining tiers; misordering is a frequent 1-mark loss. - **Marginal weights (target structure)** are used, not the existing book-value weights. - **The relevant cost for accept/reject** is the WACC in the tier that contains the project's capital requirement. - **Combining MCC with IOS** (Investment Opportunity Schedule) yields the optimal capital budget — the intersection point. - **A "tier" of WACC is valid only between two consecutive break points**, including both endpoints where source mix remains unchanged. - **For each new tier, recompute WACC from scratch** — do not just add delta to the previous WACC. - **Tax shield:** Only the cost of *debt* is multiplied by $(1-t)$ in WACC; equity and preferred dividend costs are not tax-deductible. - **KTU 2024 NEP alignment:** This topic maps to Course Outcomes involving capital structure decisions and investment evaluation (CO-2 / CO-3 typical of PEIET744).

topics.title: Flotation Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flotation Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Flotation Cost (Flotation Expenses / Issue Costs):**
> Flotation cost refers to the **total expenses incurred by a company when issuing new securities** (equity, debt, or preference shares) to the public. It is the difference between the **gross proceeds** expected from the security issue and the **net proceeds actually received by the issuing firm** after deducting all underwriting, legal, registration, printing, and marketing expenses.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{Flotation Cost (FC)} = \text{Gross Proceeds} - \text{Net Proceeds}$$

Flotation cost as a **percentage** of gross proceeds:

$$f = \frac{\text{Flotation Cost}}{\text{Gross Proceeds}} = \frac{\text{Gross Proceeds} - \text{Net Proceeds}}{\text{Gross Proceeds}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Flotation costs are treated as a **necessary evil of external financing**. They do not create any value for the firm but must be factored into the **cost of capital**, the **weighted average cost of capital (WACC)**, and the **capital budgeting decisions** under the NPV framework. Ignoring flotation costs leads to **systematic underestimation of the true cost of capital**, which may cause the firm to accept unprofitable projects.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a small-scale **tea shop owner in Kerala** who wants to raise ₹5,00,000 by selling 1,000 "ownership coupons" (shares) of your shop at ₹500 each.

You approach a broker to sell these coupons. The broker says:
- "I will charge **8%** as my fee for selling your coupons."
- "You also need to pay **₹10,000** for printing, advertisement, and getting permission from the local authorities."

So although the public pays ₹5,00,000 (1,000 × ₹500), you receive only:

$$\text{Net Proceeds} = 5,00,000 - (0.08 \times 5,00,000) - 10,000$$
$$\text{Net Proceeds} = 5,00,000 - 40,000 - 10,000 = ₹4,50,000$$

The difference of **₹50,000** is your **flotation cost**. You actually get **only ₹450 per coupon** even though it is priced at ₹500.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Flotation cost is the "**hidden tax**" a company pays just for the privilege of raising money from the public. The larger the issue, the smaller the *percentage* flotation cost (due to economies of scale), but the *absolute* amount is higher.

---

## 1.3 Categories of Flotation Costs

| Category | Description | Typical Range |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Underwriting Fees** | Paid to investment bankers for guaranteeing the issue | **2% – 8%** of gross proceeds |
| **Legal Fees** | Paid to lawyers for drafting the prospectus and compliance | Fixed cost component |
| **Registration & Listing Fees** | Paid to SEBI (India) / SEC (USA) / Stock Exchange | **0.5% – 1%** of gross proceeds |
| **Printing & Stationery** | Cost of printing prospectus, share certificates | Fixed cost component |
| **Advertising & Marketing** | Public issue advertisements, road-shows | **1% – 3%** of gross proceeds |
| **Accounting & Auditing Fees** | Verification of financial statements | Fixed cost component |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Flotation costs are broadly classified as **Fixed Flotation Costs** (independent of issue size) and **Variable Flotation Costs** (proportional to issue size). The *percentage* flotation cost therefore **decreases as issue size increases**.

---

## 1.4 Types of Flotation Cost Percentages (Weighted Average)

When a firm raises capital through **multiple sources simultaneously** (e.g., equity + debt + preference), the overall flotation cost is the **weighted average** of individual flotation costs, weighted by the proportion of each source.

$$f_A = \frac{E}{E + D + P} \cdot f_E + \frac{D}{E + D + P} \cdot f_D + \frac{P}{E + D + P} \cdot f_P$$

where:
- $f_A$ = **Weighted average flotation cost**
- $f_E, f_D, f_P$ = Flotation cost of equity, debt, preference shares respectively
- $E, D, P$ = Amount of equity, debt, preference capital raised
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Flotation Cost – Step-by-Step Logic

The total flotation cost incurred by a firm comprises the following **operational layers**:

**Step 1: Underwriting Spreads**
- The investment banker purchases the securities at a discount and resells them to the public at the issue price.
- Spread = Issue Price − Banker's Purchase Price.

**Step 2: Administrative Expenses**
- Registration fees with the regulator (SEBI in India, SEC in USA).
- Legal counsel fees for drafting the offer document.
- Fees of auditors, merchant bankers, registrars.

**Step 3: Marketing & Selling Expenses**
- Advertisement in newspapers, business channels.
- Road-shows, investor meets, brokers' commissions.

**Step 4: Miscellaneous Costs**
- Printing of share certificates, allotment letters, refund orders.
- Stationery, postage, courier, and listing fees on stock exchanges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Flotation Cost exist?**
> Because issuing new securities is an **information-asymmetric transaction**. Investors demand compensation for the risk that they may be overpaying, and intermediaries (underwriters, lawyers) charge for **certifying the issue quality** and bearing the **risk of undersubscription**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | **Basic Flotation Cost (%)** | $f = \frac{FC}{GP} \times 100$ | $FC$ = Flotation Cost in ₹, $GP$ = Gross Proceeds in ₹ |
| 2 | **Net Proceeds per Share** | $NP_0 = P_0 (1 - f)$ | $P_0$ = Issue price, $f$ = flotation % |
| 3 | **Adjusted Cost of Equity (Gordon Model)** | $k_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0(1-f)} + g$ | Always **higher** than unadjusted $k_e$ |
| 4 | **True / Break-even Cost of Capital** | $k_T = \frac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g$ | $F$ = **fixed** ₹ flotation per share |
| 5 | **Weighted Average Flotation Cost** | $f_A = \sum w_i \cdot f_i$ | $w_i$ = weight of each capital source |
| 6 | **Adjusted WACC** | $k_a = \frac{k_0}{1 - f_A}$ | $k_0$ = WACC ignoring flotation |
| 7 | **Number of Shares to Issue** | $n = \frac{\text{Required Capital}}{P_0(1-f)}$ | Accounts for proceeds leakage |
| 8 | **Adjusted Net Present Value** | $NPV_{\text{adj}} = -[\text{Investment} + \text{Flotation}] + PV(\text{Cash Inflows})$ | Use when flotation is significant |
| 9 | **DCF Valuation (Flotation-Adjusted)** | $V_0 = \frac{D_1}{k_e - g} - F$ | $F$ = total fixed flotation in ₹ |
| 10 | **Marginal Cost of Capital (new funds)** | $k_m = \frac{D_1}{P_0(1-f)} + g$ | Used in MCC schedule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Conventions:**
> - $f$ and $f_A$ are expressed as **decimals** in formulas (e.g., 0.08 for 8%).
> - $D_1, P_0, F$ are in **₹ per share**.
> - $k_e, k_T, g$ are expressed as **percentages or decimals** consistently.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Corporate Finance

Flotation costs are practically relevant in the following **engineering-economics and finance applications** taught in KTU:

1. **Capital Budgeting Decisions:** NPV of a project must deduct the *issue expenses* from the initial cash outflow. If ignored, NPV is overstated and bad projects may be accepted.

2. **IPO Pricing Strategy:** Companies often set issue price slightly higher to absorb flotation expenses. SEBI regulations require full disclosure of issue expenses in the prospectus (Red Herring Prospectus in India).

3. **Rights Issue vs. Public Issue:** Rights issues typically have **lower flotation costs** (~2–3%) compared to public issues (~5–8%) because the company already has an existing shareholder base.

4. **Optimal Capital Structure:** Firms balance **bank loans** (low or zero flotation cost) vs. **equity issues** (high flotation cost) to minimize the overall cost of capital.

5. **Venture Capital & Private Equity:** Pre-IPO companies negotiate **flotation cost discounts** with investment bankers using the book-building method.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Analogy for Students:** Just as a civil engineer includes "**contingency charges**" (typically 5–10%) in the bill of quantities for unforeseen expenses, a finance manager must include **flotation costs** in the cost-of-capital ledger to avoid cost underestimation.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Formula

| Formula | Why it exists |
|---------|---------------|
| $k_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0(1-f)} + g$ | Because the firm actually receives only $P_0(1-f)$ per share, not $P_0$. So the effective yield is higher. |
| $k_a = \frac{k_0}{1-f_A}$ | Because every rupee of capital raised yields only $(1-f_A)$ rupees for productive use, raising the marginal cost. |
| $n = \frac{\text{Required}}{P_0(1-f)}$ | Because to raise `Required` rupees, you must issue more shares than the simple `Required / P_0` calculation suggests. |
| Weighted $f_A$ | Because firms raise money from multiple sources, each with its own issue cost. The aggregate cost must reflect the mix. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Flotation-Adjusted Cost of Equity ($k_e$)

### Given:
- $P_0$ = Issue price per share
- $f$ = Flotation cost percentage (as decimal)
- $D_1$ = Expected dividend at end of year 1
- $g$ = Constant growth rate

### To Derive:
The cost of equity when flotation cost is considered.

---

**Step 1:** Start with the **Gordon Growth Model** (without flotation cost):

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{k_e - g}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the **unadjusted cost of equity**:

$$k_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the firm **does not actually receive $P_0$**. For each share sold at $P_0$, the net proceeds are:

$$NP_0 = P_0 - F = P_0(1 - f)$$

where $F = f \cdot P_0$ is the flotation cost per share.

**Step 4:** Substitute $NP_0$ in place of $P_0$ in the cost of equity formula:

$$k_e^{\text{adj}} = \frac{D_1}{NP_0} + g$$

$$k_e^{\text{adj}} = \frac{D_1}{P_0(1-f)} + g$$

$$\boxed{k_e^{\text{adj}} = \frac{D_1}{P_0(1-f)} + g}$$

**Step 5: Numerical Illustration:**

Suppose $D_1 = ₹2$, $P_0 = ₹40$, $g = 5\% = 0.05$, $f = 10\% = 0.10$.

Unadjusted cost:

$$k_e = \frac{2}{40} + 0.05 = 0.05 + 0.05 = 10\%$$

Adjusted cost:

$$k_e^{\text{adj}} = \frac{2}{40(1-0.10)} + 0.05 = \frac{2}{36} + 0.05 = 0.0556 + 0.05 = 10.56\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cost of equity **increases by 0.56 percentage points** due to the 10% flotation cost. This demonstrates that flotation cost is **not a sunk cost** — it must be recovered by the firm through higher future returns.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: True / Break-Even Cost of Capital ($k_T$)

### Concept:
When flotation cost has a **fixed component** (e.g., legal fees are ₹50,000 regardless of issue size), the per-share flotation cost is:

$$F = \text{Total Fixed Flotation Cost} \div \text{Number of Shares Issued}$$

This is a function of issue size, not a constant.

---

**Step 1:** Modified Gordon Model:

$$NP_0 = P_0 - F = \frac{D_1}{k_T - g}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $k_T$:

$$k_T - g = \frac{D_1}{P_0 - F}$$

$$k_T = \frac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g$$

$$\boxed{k_T = \frac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g}$$

**Step 3: Numerical Illustration:**

A company issues 50,000 shares at ₹100 each. Total fixed flotation cost is ₹1,50,000.

Per-share fixed flotation cost:

$$F = \frac{1,50,000}{50,000} = ₹3 \text{ per share}$$

If $D_1 = ₹8$ and $g = 6\%$:

$$k_T = \frac{8}{100 - 3} + 0.06 = \frac{8}{97} + 0.06 = 0.0825 + 0.06 = 14.25\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> When issue size **increases**, $F$ (per share) **decreases**, and $k_T$ **decreases** → approaching $k_e$. This shows the **economies of scale** in flotation costs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Adjusted WACC ($k_a$)

### Given:
- $k_0$ = WACC without considering flotation cost
- $f_A$ = Weighted average flotation cost

### Step 1: Define WACC ignoring flotation:

$$k_0 = w_e k_e + w_d k_d (1-t) + w_p k_p$$

### Step 2: Recognize that for every ₹1 raised, only $₹(1-f_A)$ is available for investment.

### Step 3: Cost per rupee of usable capital:

$$k_a = \frac{\text{Total Cost of Capital Raised}}{\text{Net Proceeds Available}} = \frac{k_0 \cdot \text{Gross}}{Gross(1-f_A)}$$

$$\boxed{k_a = \frac{k_0}{1 - f_A}}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Illustration:

If $k_0 = 12\%$ and $f_A = 5\% = 0.05$:

$$k_a = \frac{0.12}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{0.12}{0.95} = 0.1263 = 12.63\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Adjustment Rule of Thumb:** For small $f_A$, $k_a \approx k_0 \cdot (1 + f_A)$. So a 5% flotation cost increases WACC by approximately 5% of its original value.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Number of Shares to Be Issued

### Given:
- $A$ = Total capital required by firm (in ₹)
- $P_0$ = Issue price per share
- $f$ = Flotation cost %

### Step 1: Simple case (no flotation):

$$n_{\text{simple}} = \frac{A}{P_0}$$

### Step 2: With flotation, each share yields only $P_0(1-f)$:

$$n_{\text{adjusted}} = \frac{A}{P_0(1-f)}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Illustration:

A firm needs ₹10,00,000. Issue price = ₹100, $f = 10\%$.

Without flotation:

$$n_{\text{simple}} = \frac{10,00,000}{100} = 10,000 \text{ shares}$$

With flotation:

$$n_{\text{adjusted}} = \frac{10,00,000}{100(0.90)} = \frac{10,00,000}{90} = 11,112 \text{ shares}$$

The firm must issue **1,112 additional shares** to compensate for the flotation cost.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Valuation with Flotation Cost

The intrinsic value of a share with flotation cost:

$$\boxed{V_0 = \frac{D_1}{k_e - g} - F}$$

### Derivation:

$$V_0 = \frac{D_1}{k_e - g} - F$$

where $F$ is the total fixed flotation cost per share.

**Numerical Illustration:**

$D_1 = ₹5$, $k_e = 12\%$, $g = 6\%$, $F = ₹4$ (per share).

$$V_0 = \frac{5}{0.12 - 0.06} - 4 = \frac{5}{0.06} - 4 = 83.33 - 4 = ₹79.33$$

Without flotation, $V_0$ would have been ₹83.33. The **₹4 dilution** represents the flotation cost burden on the existing shareholder.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Scenario | Flotation Form | Adjustment |
|----------|---------------|------------|
| Equity issue with % flotation | $f$ as decimal | $k_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0(1-f)} + g$ |
| Equity issue with fixed ₹ flotation | $F$ in ₹ | $k_T = \frac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g$ |
| Multiple sources | Weighted $f_A$ | $k_a = \frac{k_0}{1-f_A}$ |
| Rights issue | Lower $f$ (~2-3%) | Smaller adjustment |
| Private placement | Near-zero $f$ | Minimal adjustment |
| Bank loan | Zero $f$ | No adjustment |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component Architecture of Flotation Costs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["GROSS PROCEEDS FROM SECURITY ISSUE"] --> nodeB["TOTAL FLOTATION COST FC"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["NET PROCEEDS AVAILABLE TO FIRM"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["UNDERWRITING FEES 2 TO 8 PERCENT"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["LEGAL AND AUDIT FEES FIXED"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["REGISTRATION AND LISTING FEES 0.5 TO 1 PERCENT"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["PRINTING AND STATIONERY FIXED"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["ADVERTISING AND MARKETING 1 TO 3 PERCENT"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["VARIABLE COMPONENT"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["FIXED COMPONENT"]
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeK["DECREASES WITH ISSUE SIZE ECONOMIES OF SCALE"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["INDEPENDENT OF ISSUE SIZE"]
```

## 4.2 Issue Process Flow with Cost Implications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStep1["STEP 1 FIRM DECIDES CAPITAL REQUIREMENT"] --> nodeStep2["STEP 2 APPOINT MERCHANT BANKER"]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3["STEP 3 PREPARE DRAFT RED HERRING PROSPECTUS"]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4["STEP 4 FILE WITH SEBI FOR APPROVAL"]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5["STEP 5 DETERMINE PRICE BAND BOOK BUILDING"]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6["STEP 6 COLLECT BID APPLICATIONS FROM INVESTORS"]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7["STEP 7 ALLOT SHARES AND REFUND EXCESS"]
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8["STEP 8 LIST ON STOCK EXCHANGE"]
    nodeStep8 --> nodeStep9["STEP 9 DEDUCT ALL FLOTATION EXPENSES"]
    nodeStep9 --> nodeStep10["STEP 10 NET PROCEEDS AVAILABLE FOR PROJECT USE"]
    nodeStep2 -.-> nodeCostA["INCURS UNDERWRITING FEE"]
    nodeStep3 -.-> nodeCostB["INCURS LEGAL AND AUDIT FEE"]
    nodeStep4 -.-> nodeCostC["INCURS REGISTRATION FEE"]
    nodeStep5 -.-> nodeCostD["INCURS MARKETING COST"]
    nodeStep6 -.-> nodeCostE["INCURS BROKERAGE COMMISSION"]
    nodeStep10 --> nodeFinal["FLOTATION COST IS THE GAP BETWEEN STEPS 8 AND 10"]
```

## 4.3 Flotation Cost Impact Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1["NEW PROJECT REQUIRES CAPITAL"] --> nodeQ2{"SOURCE OF FINANCE"}
    nodeQ2 --> nodeR1["EQUITY ISSUE"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeR2["DEBT ISSUE"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeR3["PREFERENCE ISSUE"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeR4["RIGHTS ISSUE"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeS1["HIGH FLOTATION COST 5 TO 8 PERCENT"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeS2["LOW FLOTATION COST 1 TO 3 PERCENT"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeS3["MODERATE FLOTATION COST 3 TO 5 PERCENT"]
    nodeR4 --> nodeS4["VERY LOW FLOTATION COST 2 TO 3 PERCENT"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeT1["ADJUSTED COST OF EQUITY HIGHER"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeT2["ADJUSTED COST OF DEBT MARGINALLY HIGHER"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeT3["ADJUSTED COST OF PREFERENCE HIGHER"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeT4["MINIMAL ADJUSTMENT NEEDED"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeU["COMPUTE ADJUSTED WACC AND RE EVALUATE NPV"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeU
    nodeT3 --> nodeU
    nodeT4 --> nodeU
    nodeU --> nodeV["ACCEPT PROJECT IF ADJUSTED NPV IS POSITIVE"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cost of Capital Adjustment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        inA["ISSUE PRICE P0"]
        inB["EXPECTED DIVIDEND D1"]
        inC["GROWTH RATE G"]
        inD["FLOTATION COST F OR F PERCENT"]
        inE["WEIGHTS W_E W_D W_P"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        procA["COMPUTE NET PROCEEDS NP0 = P0 TIMES 1 MINUS F"]
        procB["COMPUTE ADJUSTED COST OF EQUITY KE ADJ"]
        procC["COMPUTE WEIGHTED AVERAGE FLOTATION COST F_A"]
        procD["COMPUTE ADJUSTED WACC K_A = K0 DIVIDED BY 1 MINUS F_A"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        outA["ADJUSTED COST OF CAPITAL PERCENTAGE"]
        outB["NUMBER OF SHARES TO ISSUE"]
        outC["PROJECT NPV AFTER FLOTATION"]
    end
    inA --> procA
    inD --> procA
    procA --> procB
    inB --> procB
    inC --> procB
    inE --> procC
    inD --> procC
    procC --> procD

    procA --> outB
    outA --> outC
```
