# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define flotation cost. Explain briefly its components.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Flotation cost is the total expense incurred by a company in issuing new securities to the public. It is the difference between the gross proceeds of the issue and the net proceeds actually received by the firm.

**Components (2 Marks):**
1. **Underwriting fees** – Paid to investment bankers (2–8% of gross proceeds).
2. **Legal and audit fees** – For drafting the prospectus and verifying accounts.
3. **Registration and listing fees** – Paid to SEBI and stock exchanges.
4. **Printing and stationery expenses** – Cost of share certificates and offer documents.
5. **Advertising and marketing expenses** – Public issue advertisements and road-shows.

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between fixed and variable flotation costs with examples.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | Fixed Flotation Cost | Variable Flotation Cost |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Nature** | Independent of issue size | Proportional to issue size |
| **Examples** | Legal fees, audit fees, printing cost of certificates | Underwriting commission, brokerage, advertisement cost |
| **Per-share impact** | Decreases as issue size increases | Constant per rupee raised |
| **Behavior** | Shows economies of scale | Linear with issue size |

(2 Marks for distinction table + 1 Mark for examples.)

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Reliance Industries Ltd. plans to raise ₹50 crores to fund a petrochemical expansion. The financing plan is as follows:**

| Source | Amount (₹ Crore) | Flotation Cost (%) |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| Equity Shares | 25 | 8% |
| Preference Shares | 10 | 5% |
| Debentures | 15 | 3% |

**Additional Information:**
- Cost of equity (unadjusted) = 16%
- Cost of preference shares = 12%
- Cost of debentures (post-tax) = 9%
- Tax rate = 30%
- Expected dividend per share at year 1 = ₹4
- Current market price = ₹50

#### Required:
**(a)** Calculate the **weighted average flotation cost** and the **adjusted WACC**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **flotation-adjusted cost of equity** using Gordon's Growth Model, assuming a growth rate of 5%. (7 Marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION:

### Part (a) – Weighted Average Flotation Cost & Adjusted WACC (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the weighted average flotation cost $f_A$ (3 Marks)**

Total capital = 25 + 10 + 15 = ₹50 Crore

Weights:
- $w_E = \frac{25}{50} = 0.50$
- $w_P = \frac{10}{50} = 0.20$
- $w_D = \frac{15}{50} = 0.30$

Weighted average flotation cost:

$$f_A = (0.50 \times 0.08) + (0.20 \times 0.05) + (0.30 \times 0.03)$$
$$f_A = 0.040 + 0.010 + 0.009 = 0.059 = 5.9\%$$

**Step 2: Compute WACC ignoring flotation $k_0$ (2 Marks)**

$$k_0 = w_E k_E + w_P k_P + w_D k_D(1-t)$$
$$k_0 = (0.50 \times 0.16) + (0.20 \times 0.12) + (0.30 \times 0.09)(1 - 0.30)$$
$$k_0 = 0.080 + 0.024 + 0.0189 = 0.1229 = 12.29\%$$

**Step 3: Adjusted WACC $k_a$ (2 Marks)**

$$k_a = \frac{k_0}{1 - f_A} = \frac{0.1229}{1 - 0.059} = \frac{0.1229}{0.941} = 0.1306 = 13.06\%$$

**Result:** Adjusted WACC = **13.06%** (an increase of 0.77% over the unadjusted WACC of 12.29%).

---

### Part (b) – Flotation-Adjusted Cost of Equity using Gordon Model (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Recall the Gordon Growth Model formula with flotation (3 Marks)**

$$k_e^{\text{adj}} = \frac{D_1}{P_0(1 - f_E)} + g$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (2 Marks)**

$$k_e^{\text{adj}} = \frac{4}{50(1 - 0.08)} + 0.05 = \frac{4}{46} + 0.05$$

**Step 3: Final computation (2 Marks)**

$$k_e^{\text{adj}} = 0.0870 + 0.05 = 0.1370 = 13.70\%$$

**Comparison:**
- Unadjusted $k_e$ = 16% (this was given — note: this likely already includes market risk premium; the Gordon-adjusted figure is computed at 13.70% using the given $D_1$ and $P_0$. The "16%" represents the firm's estimate from CAPM or other methods.)

> [!NOTE]
> The **actual cost of equity** the firm must earn on the petrochemical project must be **at least 13.70%** (Gordon-adjusted) or **16%** (firm's estimate) — whichever is higher — to compensate equity shareholders for the flotation cost burden.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Infosys Ltd. is planning an IPO to raise ₹100 crores for a new software development centre. The issue price is ₹500 per share. The expected dividend in year 1 is ₹15 per share, and dividends are expected to grow at 7% per annum indefinitely. The fixed flotation cost is estimated at ₹2,00,000, and the issue will consist of 20,00,000 shares. The variable flotation cost is 6% of issue price.**

#### Required:
**(a)** Compute the **per-share fixed flotation cost** and the **total flotation cost per share**, then calculate the **true (break-even) cost of capital** using the fixed-flotation Gordon model. (7 Marks)

**(b)** If Infosys had instead raised the same amount via a **rights issue** with a flotation cost of only 2%, how many **additional shares** would have been required, and what would be the **net saving in flotation cost**? (7 Marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION:

### Part (a) – True Cost of Capital (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute per-share fixed flotation cost $F$ (2 Marks)**

$$F = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Flotation Cost}}{\text{Number of Shares}} = \frac{2,00,000}{20,00,000} = ₹0.10 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 2: Compute variable flotation cost per share (1 Mark)**

$$F_{\text{var}} = 0.06 \times 500 = ₹30 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 3: Total flotation cost per share (1 Mark)**

$$F_{\text{total}} = 0.10 + 30 = ₹30.10 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 4: Apply fixed-flotation Gordon model (2 Marks)**

$$k_T = \frac{D_1}{P_0 - F_{\text{fixed}}} + g = \frac{15}{500 - 0.10} + 0.07$$

**Step 5: Final value (1 Mark)**

$$k_T = \frac{15}{499.90} + 0.07 = 0.030006 + 0.07 = 0.100006 = 10.00\%$$

**Interpretation:** Because the fixed flotation cost per share (₹0.10) is negligible compared to issue price (₹500), the true cost of capital is essentially **10%**, very close to the unadjusted cost.

---

### Part (b) – Rights Issue Alternative (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute shares needed under IPO (1 Mark)**

$$n_{\text{IPO}} = \frac{10,00,00,000}{500(1 - 0.06)} = \frac{10,00,00,000}{470} = 21,27,660 \text{ shares}$$

**Step 2: Compute shares needed under Rights Issue (1 Mark)**

$$n_{\text{Rights}} = \frac{10,00,00,000}{500(1 - 0.02)} = \frac{10,00,00,000}{490} = 20,40,816 \text{ shares}$$

**Step 3: Additional shares in IPO (1 Mark)**

$$\Delta n = 21,27,660 - 20,40,816 = 86,844 \text{ additional shares}$$

**Step 4: Compute total flotation cost – IPO (1 Mark)**

$$\text{FC}_{\text{IPO}} = 0.06 \times 10,00,00,000 = ₹60,00,000$$

**Step 5: Compute total flotation cost – Rights (1 Mark)**

$$\text{FC}_{\text{Rights}} = 0.02 \times 10,00,00,000 = ₹20,00,000$$

**Step 6: Net saving (2 Marks)**

$$\text{Saving} = 60,00,000 - 20,00,000 = ₹40,00,000$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Management Insight:** A reduction in flotation cost from 6% to 2% saves ₹40 lakh on a ₹100 crore issue — a direct addition to the firm's value. This is why mature firms prefer rights issues over public issues.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing % and ₹:** Students often confuse **percentage flotation cost** ($f$) with **fixed rupee flotation cost** ($F$). Use $P_0(1-f)$ for percentage form and $P_0 - F$ for fixed rupee form. Mixing them yields wrong answers.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Growth Term:** In Gordon's model with flotation, students frequently forget to add $g$ at the end. The full formula is always $k = \frac{D_1}{NP_0} + g$.
>
> 3. **Weights Not Normalised:** In weighted average flotation cost, weights must sum to 1.0. If using $E, D, P$ in the denominator, ensure they are **book values or market values consistently**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Tax Shield:** When computing adjusted WACC for debt, do NOT forget the $(1-t)$ multiplier for the after-tax cost of debt. Flotation does not affect the tax shield calculation but does affect the *amount of debt issued*.
>
> 5. **NPV Adjustment Error:** In capital budgeting, flotation cost must be **added to the initial investment outflow**, not subtracted from cash inflows. This is a 2-mark trap in KTU valuation.
>
> 6. **Zero Flotation for Bank Loans:** Loans from banks and financial institutions carry **zero flotation cost**. Do not apply $f_A$ to them.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Flotation Costs:**
>
> - **Definition:** Cost of issuing new securities = Gross Proceeds − Net Proceeds.
> - **Two Forms:** Percentage form $f$ (use $P_0(1-f)$) and Fixed ₹ form $F$ (use $P_0 - F$).
> - **Adjusted Cost of Equity (Gordon Model):** $k_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0(1-f)} + g$
> - **True Cost of Capital:** $k_T = \frac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g$
> - **Adjusted WACC:** $k_a = \frac{k_0}{1 - f_A}$
> - **Shares to Issue:** $n = \frac{\text{Amount Required}}{P_0(1-f)}$
> - **Weighted Flotation Cost:** $f_A = \sum w_i f_i$ where $w_i$ = weight of each source.
> - **NPV Adjustment:** Add flotation cost to **initial investment** in Year 0.
> - **Economies of Scale:** Larger issues have **lower percentage** flotation costs (fixed costs spread over more shares).
> - **Rights Issue Advantage:** Flotation cost ~2–3% vs. ~6–8% for public issue → significant savings.
> - **Practical Hierarchy (Lowest to Highest Flotation):** Bank Loan (0%) < Rights Issue (2–3%) < Preference (3–5%) < Public Equity (5–8%).
> - **Examination Pattern:** KTU typically tests flotation cost through (i) adjusted WACC, (ii) number of shares to issue, and (iii) NPV impact — usually in a 7+7 sub-part question for 14 marks.
> - **Common Traps:** Forgetting tax shield, misapplying percentage vs. rupee form, ignoring growth rate in Gordon model.
> - **Real-World Application:** SEBI mandates disclosure of all issue expenses in the offer document; firms set issue price slightly above intrinsic value to recover flotation cost.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Business Risk and Financial Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Business Risk and Financial Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Business Risk (Operating Risk)

**Business Risk** is the uncertainty or variability associated with a firm's **operating income (EBIT — Earnings Before Interest and Taxes)** arising from changes in sales, costs, or production conditions, **independent of how the firm is financed**. It is inherent to the nature of the business and reflects the sensitivity of EBIT to fluctuations in revenue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Business Risk is the risk of fluctuations in the firm's Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EBIT) caused by changes in sales volume, sales price, and cost structure (variable and fixed operating costs). It is measured quantitatively using the **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)**.

**Key Drivers of Business Risk:**
- **Sales Volatility** — unpredictability in demand
- **Operating Leverage** — proportion of fixed costs in the cost structure
- **Input Cost Variability** — fluctuations in raw material, labor
- **Competition & Industry Cycles**
- **Product Mix and Pricing Power**

### 1.2 Financial Risk

**Financial Risk** is the additional risk placed on equity shareholders as a result of the firm's **financing decisions**, particularly the use of **debt (and preference share capital)**. It is the risk that the firm will not be able to meet its fixed financial obligations (interest and preferred dividends), leading to fluctuations in **Earnings Per Share (EPS)** and amplified variability of returns to equity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Financial Risk is the risk arising from the use of debt (and preference capital) in the firm's capital structure. It is the variability in EPS (or returns to equity) due to fixed financial charges. It is measured using the **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)**.

**Key Drivers of Financial Risk:**
- **Debt–Equity Ratio**
- **Fixed Interest Burden (I)**
- **Preference Dividend Obligations (Dp)**
- **Tax Shield Availability**
- **Interest Coverage Capability**

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

**Business Risk — The "Boat on a River" Analogy:**
> Imagine a small fishing boat (your firm) sailing on a river. The waves (sales fluctuations, cost variations) come from the river itself — this is **business risk**. A heavier boat (more fixed costs) rocks more violently in small waves. The size and weight of the boat determine how much it tilts — this is the essence of **operating leverage**. The waves are *external* to the boat's design.

**Financial Risk — The "Engine and Fuel" Analogy:**
> Now imagine the boat has a powerful engine (debt) that lets it go faster. But the engine needs fuel (interest payments) regardless of whether you catch fish (earn profit) or not. A stronger engine means more speed (higher EPS on the upside) but also more danger if there's no fuel money (bankruptcy risk on the downside). This extra instability from the engine is **financial risk** — introduced by the *financing choice*, not the business itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Firm Risk = Business Risk + Financial Risk**, measured by the **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)**. Business risk is unavoidable and exists even in an all-equity firm; financial risk only emerges once fixed-cost financing (debt/preference shares) is introduced.

### 1.4 Key Metrics (KTU 2024 High-Yield Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Sales (Revenue) | $S$ | $S = P \times Q$ |
| Variable Cost | $V$ | $V = v \times Q$ |
| Fixed Operating Cost | $F$ | Constant irrespective of output |
| Earnings Before Interest & Tax | $EBIT$ | $EBIT = S - V - F$ |
| Interest | $I$ | Fixed financial charge |
| Preference Dividend | $D_p$ | Fixed obligation to preference shareholders |
| Tax Rate | $t$ | Corporate tax rate |
| Earnings Per Share | $EPS$ | Final residual earnings to equity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) — The Business Risk Multiplier

The **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)** at a given level of sales measures the **percentage change in EBIT** resulting from a **one-percent change in sales (quantity sold)**.

$$DOL = \frac{\% \text{ Change in } EBIT}{\% \text{ Change in Sales (Q)}}$$

**Expanded Formula (Algebraic Form):**

$$DOL = \frac{Q \cdot (S - V)}{Q \cdot (S - V) - F} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}}$$

where:
- $Q$ = Quantity sold (units)
- $S$ = Selling price per unit
- $V$ = Variable cost per unit
- $F$ = Total fixed operating cost
- $Contribution = Q(S - V) = Total\ Sales - Total\ Variable\ Cost$

**Logic Steps (Why and How):**
1. **Step 1 — Identify Fixed Costs:** A higher proportion of fixed costs (F) in the cost structure amplifies the impact of sales changes on EBIT. This is the *cause* of business risk.
2. **Step 2 — Compute Contribution Margin:** $Contribution = Sales - Variable\ Cost$ represents the amount available to cover fixed costs and generate profit.
3. **Step 3 — Form the Ratio:** DOL is Contribution ÷ EBIT. When EBIT is small (firm near break-even), DOL becomes very large → extreme business risk.
4. **Step 4 — Interpretation:** If DOL = 3, then a 10% rise in sales causes a 30% rise in EBIT. Similarly, a 10% fall in sales causes a 30% fall in EBIT. **Leverage works both ways.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A firm closer to its break-even point has a **higher DOL** and therefore higher business risk. Firms with high fixed costs (e.g., airlines, telecom, manufacturing) have higher DOL than service firms.

---

### 2.2 Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL) — The Financial Risk Multiplier

The **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** measures the **percentage change in EPS** for a **one-percent change in EBIT**.

$$DFL = \frac{\% \text{ Change in } EPS}{\% \text{ Change in } EBIT}$$

**Expanded Formulae:**

**Case 1 — Firm has only equity and debt (no preference shares):**

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$$

**Case 2 — Firm has equity, debt, and preference shares (with tax shield adjustment):**

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \frac{D_p}{(1 - t)}}$$

where:
- $EBIT$ = Earnings Before Interest and Tax
- $I$ = Annual Interest Expense
- $D_p$ = Annual Preference Dividend
- $t$ = Tax rate

**Logic Steps (Why and How):**
1. **Step 1 — Identify Fixed Financial Charges:** Interest (I) and Preference Dividend (Dp) are contractual obligations, paid *before* equity holders receive any residual.
2. **Step 2 — Tax Adjustment on Preference Dividend:** Preference dividends are paid from *after-tax* profits, so for the EPS effect to be neutral, we gross up Dp by dividing by $(1 - t)$.
3. **Step 3 — Form the Ratio:** DFL = EBIT ÷ (EBIT − fixed charges). The smaller the denominator (i.e., the larger I and Dp), the higher the DFL.
4. **Step 4 — Interpretation:** DFL = 2 means a 10% change in EBIT produces a 20% change in EPS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Financial risk is **zero** when the firm has no debt and no preference shares. Every rupee of debt added increases DFL and amplifies EPS volatility.

---

### 2.3 Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL) — The Total Risk Multiplier

The **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)** captures the **total risk** of the firm by combining operating and financial leverage. It measures the **percentage change in EPS** for a **one-percent change in sales**.

$$DCL = DOL \times DFL$$

**Expanded Formula (Direct):**

$$DCL = \frac{Q \cdot (S - V)}{Q \cdot (S - V) - F - I - \frac{D_p}{(1 - t)}}$$

$$DCL = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT} - I - \frac{D_p}{(1 - t)}}$$

**Equivalent Verification:**

$$DCL = \frac{\% \text{ Change in } EPS}{\% \text{ Change in Sales}}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always verify the formula using the EBIT/Sales definition as a backup during valuation.** The algebraic form is faster; the percentage form is a safety check.

| # | Leverage Concept | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOL (Algebraic) | $\dfrac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V)-F}$ | % change in EBIT for 1% change in Sales |
| 2 | DOL (Identity) | $\dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}}$ | Same as #1 — useful for sanity check |
| 3 | DFL (No Pref. Shares) | $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT-I}$ | % change in EPS for 1% change in EBIT |
| 4 | DFL (With Pref. Shares) | $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT-I-\frac{D_p}{1-t}}$ | Adjusts Dp for tax shield |
| 5 | DCL (Product) | $DOL \times DFL$ | Total leverage effect |
| 6 | DCL (Direct) | $\dfrac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V)-F-I-\frac{D_p}{1-t}}$ | One-step combined formula |
| 7 | Break-Even Sales | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{(S-V)}$ | Sales level where EBIT = 0 |
| 8 | Indifference EBIT | $Q_I = \dfrac{(I_1-I_2)}{(P-V)}$ | Sales at which two financing plans give same EPS |
| 9 | EPS (No Pref. Shares) | $EPS = \dfrac{(EBIT-I)(1-t)}{N}$ | Net earnings per equity share |
| 10 | EPS (With Pref. Shares) | $EPS = \dfrac{(EBIT-I)(1-t) - D_p}{N}$ | Net earnings per equity share |

where $N$ = number of equity shares outstanding.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Corporate Decision-Making

- **Capital Budgeting:** Firms with high operating leverage (e.g., capital-intensive industries — steel, oil refining, semiconductor fabs) must carefully forecast sales volume because small demand shifts produce large EBIT swings.
- **Capital Structuring:** A startup with predictable cash flows (e.g., SaaS) can safely take on more debt (higher DFL), while a cyclical firm (e.g., construction) should rely more on equity to keep DCL moderate.
- **Break-Even & Risk Assessment:** DOL and DFL are the building blocks of break-even analysis, EPS forecasting, and **indifference-point analysis** for choosing between debt vs. equity financing plans.
- **Investor Signaling:** Public-listed firms disclose leverage in annual reports; analysts track DOL, DFL, and DCL to evaluate earnings stability and bankruptcy risk.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of DOL

**Starting Point — Definition:**
$$DOL = \frac{\% \Delta EBIT}{\% \Delta Sales}$$

**Step 1 — Express percentage changes in differential form:**
$$DOL = \frac{\frac{d(EBIT)}{EBIT}}{\frac{dS}{S}} = \frac{d(EBIT)}{dS} \cdot \frac{S}{EBIT}$$

**Step 2 — Express EBIT as a function of S:**
$$EBIT = S - V_c - F$$

where $V_c$ = total variable cost and $F$ = fixed cost.

**Step 3 — Differentiate EBIT with respect to S:**
$$\frac{d(EBIT)}{dS} = 1 - \frac{dV_c}{dS}$$

Assuming variable cost is proportional to sales, $\frac{dV_c}{dS} = \frac{V_c}{S}$ (i.e., variable cost ratio is constant). Therefore:

$$\frac{d(EBIT)}{dS} = 1 - \frac{V_c}{S} = \frac{S - V_c}{S} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{S}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**
$$DOL = \left(\frac{\text{Contribution}}{S}\right) \cdot \frac{S}{EBIT} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{EBIT} = \frac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V)-F}$$

**Conclusion:** $DOL = \dfrac{Contribution}{EBIT}$ — a clean and elegant result.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of DFL

**Starting Point — Definition:**
$$DFL = \frac{\% \Delta EPS}{\% \Delta EBIT}$$

**Step 1 — Express in differential form (No Preference Shares Case):**
$$EPS = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1 - t)}{N}$$

where $N$ = number of equity shares, $I$ = interest, $t$ = tax rate.

**Step 2 — Compute $\frac{d(EPS)}{d(EBIT)}$:**
$$\frac{d(EPS)}{d(EBIT)} = \frac{(1 - t)}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**
$$DFL = \frac{d(EPS)}{d(EBIT)} \cdot \frac{EBIT}{EPS} = \frac{(1-t)/N \cdot EBIT}{(EBIT-I)(1-t)/N} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT-I}$$

**Step 4 — Extend to Preference Share Case:**

EPS now becomes:
$$EPS = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1 - t) - D_p}{N}$$

Differentiating with respect to EBIT:
$$\frac{d(EPS)}{d(EBIT)} = \frac{(1 - t)}{N}$$

The denominator:
$$EPS = \frac{(1-t)(EBIT - I) - D_p}{N}$$

Forming the ratio:
$$DFL = \frac{(1-t) \cdot EBIT}{(1-t)(EBIT - I) - D_p} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \frac{D_p}{1-t}}$$

This proves the **tax-adjusted preference dividend formula**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
> A company sells a product at ₹100 per unit. Variable cost is ₹60 per unit. Fixed operating costs are ₹80,000. The firm has ₹2,00,000 of 10% debt and 5,000 equity shares. Tax rate is 30%. Compute DOL, DFL, and DCL at a sales volume of 6,000 units. Also, calculate the new EPS if sales rise by 20%.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Sales, Variable Cost, Contribution, EBIT:**

$$Sales = 6000 \times 100 = 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$Variable\ Cost = 6000 \times 60 = 3{,}60{,}000$$

$$Contribution = 6{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}60{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000$$

$$EBIT = 2{,}40{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute Interest:**

$$I = 10\% \times 2{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}000$$

(No preference shares, so $D_p = 0$)

**Step 3 — Compute DOL:**

$$DOL = \frac{Contribution}{EBIT} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{1{,}60{,}000} = 1.50$$

**Step 4 — Compute DFL:**

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I} = \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{1{,}60{,}000 - 20{,}000} = \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{1{,}40{,}000} = 1.1429$$

**Step 5 — Compute DCL:**

$$DCL = DOL \times DFL = 1.50 \times 1.1429 = 1.7143$$

**Step 6 — Verification Using Direct DCL Formula:**

$$DCL = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{1{,}40{,}000} = 1.7143 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Compute New Sales (20% Increase):**

$$New\ Sales = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 1.20 = 7{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 8 — Recompute New EBIT:**

$$New\ Variable\ Cost = 7200 \times 60 = 4{,}32{,}000$$

$$New\ Contribution = 7{,}20{,}000 - 4{,}32{,}000 = 2{,}88{,}000$$

$$New\ EBIT = 2{,}88{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 2{,}08{,}000$$

**Step 9 — Recompute New EPS:**

$$New\ EPS = \frac{(2{,}08{,}000 - 20{,}000)(1 - 0.30)}{5{,}000} = \frac{1{,}88{,}000 \times 0.70}{5{,}000} = \frac{1{,}31{,}600}{5{,}000} = 26.32$$

**Step 10 — Old EPS for Comparison:**

$$Old\ EPS = \frac{(1{,}60{,}000 - 20{,}000)(1 - 0.30)}{5{,}000} = \frac{1{,}40{,}000 \times 0.70}{5{,}000} = 19.60$$

**Step 11 — Percentage Change Verification (DCL Test):**

$$\%\Delta EPS = \frac{26.32 - 19.60}{19.60} \times 100 = 34.29\%$$

$$\%\Delta Sales = 20\%$$

$$\frac{\%\Delta EPS}{\%\Delta Sales} = \frac{34.29}{20} = 1.7145 \approx DCL \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> The percentage verification confirms the theoretical DCL value (1.7143). This dual check is what KTU examiners look for in model answers — **always show the verification step for full marks.**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Business Risk | Financial Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Operating decisions (fixed costs, sales mix) | Financing decisions (debt, preference shares) |
| **Measured by** | DOL | DFL |
| **Exists when** | Always (even all-equity firm) | Only with fixed financial charges |
| **Affects** | EBIT volatility | EPS / ROE volatility |
| **Controllable by** | Managing cost structure, product mix | Adjusting capital structure |
| **Combined effect** | DCL = DOL × DFL | DCL = DOL × DFL |
| **KTU Treatment** | Computed from P/V ratio and fixed cost | Computed from EBIT and interest |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Differentiate between Business Risk and Financial Risk. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Business Risk | Financial Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Risk of variability in EBIT due to operating factors | Risk of variability in EPS due to financing decisions |
| **Source** | Fixed operating costs and sales variability | Fixed financial charges (interest & preference dividend) |
| **Measured by** | Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) | Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL) |
| **Existence** | Exists even in a zero-debt firm | Exists only when debt/preference shares are used |
| **Controllable through** | Operating decisions | Capital structure decisions |

*(Examiner's note: Any 3 valid distinguishing points = full 3 marks. Drawing a comparison table is the cleanest way to secure full marks.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
**Explain the concept of Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL). How is it computed? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)** is a measure of the **total risk** of a firm — it captures the combined effect of both operating and financial leverage. It represents the **percentage change in EPS (Earnings Per Share)** that results from a **one-percent change in sales**.

**Computation:**

**Method 1 — Product of DOL and DFL:**

$$DCL = DOL \times DFL$$

**Method 2 — Direct Formula:**

$$DCL = \frac{Q(S - V)}{Q(S - V) - F - I - \frac{D_p}{(1 - t)}}$$

**Interpretation:** If DCL = 2, a 10% increase in sales causes a 20% increase in EPS. DCL is the most comprehensive risk measure because it integrates both business and financial risk into a single multiplier.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU ESE Pattern):** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following:

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Define Business Risk. Derive the formula for the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) and explain its significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following data relates to M/s. Beta Tech Pvt. Ltd.:

| Particulars | Value |
|---|---|
| Sales (10,000 units @ ₹50/unit) | ₹5,00,000 |
| Variable Cost | ₹3,00,000 |
| Fixed Cost | ₹1,00,000 |
| Interest on 12% Loan | ₹36,000 |
| Tax Rate | 40% |

Compute the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL), Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL), and Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL). If sales increase by 25%, compute the new EPS and verify using DCL. Number of equity shares = 10,000. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Definition & Derivation of DOL (7 Marks):**

**Definition:** Business Risk is the risk associated with the firm's operations that results in variability of operating income (EBIT) due to changes in sales volume, prices, and cost structure. It arises from the use of fixed operating costs in the production process and is measured by the Degree of Operating Leverage.

**Derivation:**

DOL is defined as:

$$DOL = \frac{\% \text{ Change in } EBIT}{\% \text{ Change in Sales}}$$

Expressing in differential form:

$$DOL = \frac{d(EBIT)/EBIT}{dS/S} = \frac{d(EBIT)}{dS} \cdot \frac{S}{EBIT}$$

Since $EBIT = S - V_c - F$ and assuming variable cost is proportional to sales, $\frac{dV_c}{dS} = \frac{V_c}{S}$. Therefore:

$$\frac{d(EBIT)}{dS} = 1 - \frac{V_c}{S} = \frac{S - V_c}{S} = \frac{Contribution}{Sales}$$

Substituting:

$$DOL = \frac{Contribution}{Sales} \cdot \frac{Sales}{EBIT} = \frac{Contribution}{EBIT}$$

Since $Sales = Q \times P$ and $Contribution = Q(S - V)$:

$$DOL = \frac{Q(S - V)}{Q(S - V) - F}$$

**Significance:**
- **Risk Indicator:** Higher DOL implies higher business risk.
- **Forecasting Tool:** Used to predict change in EBIT from a change in sales.
- **Break-Even Sensitivity:** A firm near break-even has very high DOL, making profits highly volatile.
- **Decision Making:** Helps managers decide on cost structure (fixed vs. variable mix).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Contribution:**

$$Contribution = Sales - Variable\ Cost = 5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute EBIT:**

$$EBIT = Contribution - Fixed\ Cost = 2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute DOL:**

$$DOL = \frac{Contribution}{EBIT} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 2.0$$

**Step 4 — Compute DFL:**

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000 - 36{,}000} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{64{,}000} = 1.5625$$

**Step 5 — Compute DCL:**

$$DCL = DOL \times DFL = 2.0 \times 1.5625 = 3.125$$

**Step 6 — Compute Old EPS:**

$$EPS_{old} = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1 - t)}{N} = \frac{(1{,}00{,}000 - 36{,}000)(1 - 0.40)}{10{,}000} = \frac{64{,}000 \times 0.60}{10{,}000} = \frac{38{,}400}{10{,}000} = 3.84$$

**Step 7 — Compute New Sales and EBIT (25% Increase):**

$$New\ Sales = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.25 = 6{,}25{,}000$$

$$New\ Variable\ Cost = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 1.25 = 3{,}75{,}000$$

$$New\ Contribution = 6{,}25{,}000 - 3{,}75{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000$$

$$New\ EBIT = 2{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 8 — Compute New EPS:**

$$EPS_{new} = \frac{(1{,}50{,}000 - 36{,}000)(1 - 0.40)}{10{,}000} = \frac{1{,}14{,}000 \times 0.60}{10{,}000} = \frac{68{,}400}{10{,}000} = 6.84$$

**Step 9 — Verification Using DCL:**

$$\%\Delta EPS = \frac{6.84 - 3.84}{3.84} \times 100 = 78.125\%$$

$$\%\Delta Sales = 25\%$$

$$DCL_{verified} = \frac{78.125}{25} = 3.125 \quad \checkmark$$

The verified DCL matches the theoretical DCL of 3.125, confirming the calculation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`**

**(a)** Define Financial Risk. Explain the Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL) and discuss its role in capital structure decisions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Gamma Industries has the following capital structure proposals under consideration:

| Plan | Equity (₹) | Debt @ 12% (₹) | Total (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plan I | 10,00,000 | 0 | 10,00,000 |
| Plan II | 6,00,000 | 4,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| Plan III | 4,00,000 | 6,00,000 | 10,00,000 |

Additional data:
- Expected EBIT = ₹1,80,000
- Fixed Operating Cost = ₹60,000
- Variable Cost = 50% of Sales
- Tax Rate = 30%
- Number of Equity Shares: Plan I = 10,000; Plan II = 6,000; Plan III = 4,000

Compute EPS, DOL, DFL, and DCL for each plan. Which plan is the least risky and which is the most risky? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Definition & Role of DFL (7 Marks):**

**Definition:** Financial Risk is the risk of variability in EPS arising from the use of debt and preference share capital in the firm's financing mix. It stems from the obligation to pay fixed interest and preference dividends regardless of profit levels. The Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL) measures this risk as the percentage change in EPS for a 1% change in EBIT.

**Formula:**

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \frac{D_p}{(1 - t)}}$$

**Role in Capital Structure Decisions:**

1. **Risk Quantification:** DFL translates the qualitative concern of "too much debt" into a measurable multiplier, helping managers gauge the EPS sensitivity to EBIT fluctuations.
2. **Optimal Capital Structure:** DFL, combined with DOL, helps identify the capital structure that minimizes the firm's total cost of capital and total risk (DCL).
3. **EPS Maximization:** DFL analysis enables management to choose between alternative financing plans by comparing EPS at different expected EBIT levels.
4. **Bankruptcy Risk Indicator:** A high DFL signals a thin interest coverage cushion, raising the probability of financial distress.
5. **Investor Decision Tool:** Equity investors use DFL to assess the volatility of their returns; lenders use it to evaluate debt-servicing capacity.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Common Parameters:**
- $EBIT = 1{,}80{,}000$
- $F = 60{,}000$
- $Contribution = EBIT + F = 1{,}80{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000$
- $DOL = \dfrac{Contribution}{EBIT} = \dfrac{2{,}40{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} = 1.3333$ (same for all plans — depends only on cost structure)

**Plan I — All Equity:**

- $I = 0$, $N = 10{,}000$
- $DFL = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000 - 0} = 1.0$
- $DCL = 1.3333 \times 1.0 = 1.3333$
- $EPS = \dfrac{(1{,}80{,}000 - 0)(1 - 0.30)}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{1{,}26{,}000}{10{,}000} = 12.60$

**Plan II — Moderate Debt:**

- $I = 12\% \times 4{,}00{,}000 = 48{,}000$, $N = 6{,}000$
- $DFL = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000 - 48{,}000} = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}32{,}000} = 1.3636$
- $DCL = 1.3333 \times 1.3636 = 1.8181$
- $EPS = \dfrac{(1{,}80{,}000 - 48{,}000)(1 - 0.30)}{6{,}000} = \dfrac{1{,}32{,}000 \times 0.70}{6{,}000} = \dfrac{92{,}400}{6{,}000} = 15.40$

**Plan III — High Debt:**

- $I = 12\% \times 6{,}00{,}000 = 72{,}000$, $N = 4{,}000$
- $DFL = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000 - 72{,}000} = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}08{,}000} = 1.6667$
- $DCL = 1.3333 \times 1.6667 = 2.2222$
- $EPS = \dfrac{(1{,}80{,}000 - 72{,}000)(1 - 0.30)}{4{,}000} = \dfrac{1{,}08{,}000 \times 0.70}{4{,}000} = \dfrac{75{,}600}{4{,}000} = 18.90$

**Summary Table:**

| Plan | EPS (₹) | DOL | DFL | DCL | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plan I (All Equity) | 12.60 | 1.3333 | 1.0000 | 1.3333 | **Least Risky** |
| Plan II (Moderate) | 15.40 | 1.3333 | 1.3636 | 1.8181 | Medium |
| Plan III (High Debt) | 18.90 | 1.3333 | 1.6667 | 2.2222 | **Most Risky** |

**Conclusion:** **Plan I is least risky** (lowest DCL = 1.3333, lowest financial leverage) and **Plan III is most risky** (highest DCL = 2.2222, highest financial leverage). However, Plan III delivers the highest EPS at the current EBIT level. This illustrates the classic **risk-return trade-off** in capital structure decisions — managers must weigh higher EPS against higher DCL.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Tax Adjustment on Preference Dividend:** Many students write $DFL = \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - D_p}$, omitting the $\frac{1}{1-t}$ adjustment. This costs **2 marks** in Part B numerical questions.
> 2. **Skipping the DCL Verification Step:** KTU examiners award bonus marks when students verify the DCL using the percentage change method ($\%\Delta EPS / \%\Delta Sales$). Always include this step.
> 3. **Confusing DOL with DFL:** DOL is computed from **Contribution and EBIT**; DFL is computed from **EBIT and Interest**. Mixing them up is the single most common error.
> 4. **Not Labelling Units:** In the formula sheet, clearly state whether $S$, $V$, $F$ are per-unit or total. This is checked strictly.
> 5. **Ignoring the 14-Mark Sub-Part Weightage:** Part (a) and Part (b) are each worth 7 marks. Skimping on derivations or skipping definitions loses easy marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Business Risk** = Operating risk → measured by **DOL**.
- [x] **Financial Risk** = Financing risk → measured by **DFL**.
- [x] **Total Risk** = Combined risk → measured by **DCL = DOL × DFL**.
- [x] **DOL Formula:** $\dfrac{Contribution}{EBIT} = \dfrac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V)-F}$.
- [x] **DFL Formula (no preference shares):** $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$.
- [x] **DFL Formula (with preference shares):** $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \frac{D_p}{1-t}}$.
- [x] **DCL Formula:** $\dfrac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V) - F - I - \frac{D_p}{1-t}}$.
- [x] A leverage value **greater than 1** means EPS is *amplified* — both upside and downside.
- [x] A firm **closer to break-even** has higher DOL and therefore higher business risk.
- [x] Adding **debt** or **preference shares** always increases DFL and DCL.
- [x] Always **verify** DCL numerically by computing $\%\Delta EPS / \%\Delta Sales$.
- [x] **Interpretation rule of thumb:** DOL = 2 → 1% sales change → 2% EBIT change; DFL = 1.5 → 1% EBIT change → 1.5% EPS change.
- [x] In KTU exams, **state assumptions** (e.g., "variable cost is proportional to sales") before applying formulas.
- [x] For numerical answers, present a **summary table** at the end — this earns presentation marks.
- [x] Break-even sales: $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$; useful for identifying high-risk regions.
- [x] Indifference-point analysis (when comparing financing plans) uses the same DFL/DFL framework.
- [x] KTU 2024 scheme weights this topic under **CO2 (Capital Structure & Leverage Analysis)** — practice both conceptual and numerical questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Capital Structure?

**Capital Structure** refers to the specific mix of long-term sources of funds (debt + preferred stock + ordinary/equity share capital + retained earnings) that a corporation employs to finance its fixed assets and permanent working capital requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Capital structure is the proportional relationship (the percentage combination) between **debt** and **equity** (including preference share capital) used by a firm to finance its long-term investments and operations. It represents the permanent financing of the firm.

The term **"Capital Structure Decision"** is the strategic financial decision undertaken by a finance manager regarding the optimum mix of debt and equity. This is one of the most critical decisions in financial management because:

- It determines the **overall cost of capital** ($K_o$) of the firm.
- It directly influences the **market value** of the firm ($V_L$).
- It governs the **financial risk** borne by the shareholders.
- It affects the **earnings per share (EPS)** and the **return on equity (ROE)**.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Financial Leverage** | The use of fixed-cost financing (debt + preference capital) in the capital structure. |
| **Trading on Equity** | Using debt to amplify the return to equity shareholders. |
| **Optimal Capital Structure** | The mix of debt and equity that minimizes the WACC and maximizes the firm's value. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between Capital Structure and Financial Structure:**
> - **Capital Structure** = Long-term sources only (Debt + Preference Share + Equity Share + Retained Earnings).
> - **Financial Structure** = Capital Structure + Short-term sources (Bank loans, working capital advances, trade credit, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pizza and Cocktail" Intuition 🎯

Imagine you are hosting a party and want to arrange a great evening for guests:

- **Equity** is like a **full-course meal at home** — costly in time and money upfront, but you retain complete control and ownership. You are not obliged to "pay" anyone a fixed amount.
- **Debt** is like **ordering pizzas on credit** — quick, cheaper upfront, but you *must* pay the fixed bill (interest + principal) on time, irrespective of whether guests enjoyed the pizza or not.
- **Capital Structure Decision** is like deciding the *perfect ratio* of home-cooked food vs. ordered pizzas that satisfies the guests (shareholders), keeps costs reasonable, and does not break the bank during a low-attendance party (recession).

If you borrow too much (too many pizzas on credit), even a slight decline in guests means you cannot pay the bill — this is **financial distress / bankruptcy risk**.
If you borrow too little (all home-cooked), you may run out of variety and lose shareholder appetite — **lost leverage benefits**.

> The *sweet spot* — where the party is grand *and* the bills are manageable — is the **Optimal Capital Structure**.

## 1.3 Why is the Capital Structure Decision Important?

| # | Importance | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Profitability & EPS** | Higher debt → higher EPS due to lower interest cost relative to equity returns (up to a point). |
| 2 | **Cost of Capital** | WACC depends on weights of debt ($K_d$) and equity ($K_e$). |
| 3 | **Financial Risk** | Greater debt → higher fixed obligations → higher bankruptcy risk. |
| 4 | **Firm Valuation** | Optimal mix → maximized $V_L = \frac{\text{EBIT (1-t)}}{K_o}$. |
| 5 | **Control** | Debt holders have no voting rights; equity issuance dilutes control. |
| 6 | **Flexibility & Credit Rating** | Excess debt lowers credit rating and reduces future borrowing capacity. |

## 1.4 Types of Capital Structure

> [!NOTE]
> **Key classification for KTU exam:**
> 1. **Simple Capital Structure** — Only equity shares (no debt, no preference shares). EPS = (EBIT − I)(1−t) / No. of equity shares.
> 2. **Complex Capital Structure** — Has debt, preference shares, and possibly convertible securities. EPS requires adjustment for interest and preference dividends.

# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Affecting Capital Structure Decision

The finance manager selects an appropriate debt-equity mix after weighing internal and external factors.

### Internal Factors
1. **Size of the Firm** — Large firms enjoy stable cash flows and easier access to debt markets.
2. **Nature of Business / Industry** — Asset-heavy industries (e.g., steel, utilities) tolerate more debt.
3. **Growth Rate** — High-growth firms need more equity to avoid the rigidity of fixed interest.
4. **Profitability & Earnings Stability** — Stable, high-EBIT firms can service debt easily.
5. **Asset Structure** — Firms with tangible, marketable collateral (land, building, machinery) can borrow more.
6. **Attitude of Management** — Risk-averse managers prefer equity; aggressive managers prefer debt.

### External Factors
1. **State of the Capital Market** — Bull vs. bear market conditions.
2. **Government Policy / Tax Rates** — Higher corporate tax makes debt attractive (interest is tax-deductible).
3. **Cost of Capital & Market Conditions** — Interest rates in the economy.
4. **Legal Constraints** — SEBI regulations, debt-to-equity norms (e.g., banks require D/E ≤ 15×).
5. **Lender's Attitude / Credit Rating** — Bankers may refuse debt if leverage is excessive.

## 2.2 The Five Classical Theories of Capital Structure

These theories explain the *relationship between capital structure, cost of capital, and firm value*.

### A. Net Income (NI) Approach — **Durand (1952)**
- **Assumptions:** $K_d$ and $K_e$ remain **constant** irrespective of leverage.
- **Conclusion:** More debt → Lower $K_o$ → Higher $V_L$. Hence, **100% debt = Optimal.**
- **Criticism:** Unrealistic; ignores financial risk.

### B. Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach
- **Assumptions:** $K_o$ (overall WACC) and $V_L$ remain **constant**; $K_e$ rises with leverage to exactly offset the benefit of cheaper debt.
- **Conclusion:** Capital structure is **irrelevant** to firm value. $V_L = V_U$.
- **Criticism:** Fails to explain practical behavior.

### C. Traditional Approach (Intermediate)
- **Assumptions:** $K_d$ is constant up to a "reasonable" level of debt, then rises. $K_e$ rises gradually, then steeply.
- **Conclusion:** There exists an **Optimal Capital Structure** at which $K_o$ is minimized and $V_L$ is maximized.

### D. Modigliani–Miller (MM) Approach
- **Without Tax (1958):** Capital structure is **irrelevant**; $V_L = V_U$. (Same as NOI Approach mathematically.)
- **With Corporate Tax (1963):** Debt creates a **tax shield**, so $V_L = V_U + t \cdot D$. Value increases continuously with leverage.

### E. Trade-Off Theory
- Balances the **tax benefits of debt** against the **costs of financial distress**.
- Optimal D/E occurs where: **Marginal tax shield benefit = Marginal distress cost**.

### F. Pecking Order Theory — **Myers & Majluf (1984)**
- Firms prefer financing in this order: **Internal funds → Debt → External Equity.**
- Asymmetric information makes outside equity the costliest.

## 2.3 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Firm Value (Net Income approach) | $V_L = \dfrac{NI}{K_e} = \dfrac{S + I(1-t)}{K_e}$ | $S$ = Total equity, $I$ = Interest |
| 2 | Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) | $K_o = \dfrac{E}{V} K_e + \dfrac{D}{V} K_d (1-t)$ | $V = E + D$; weights from market values |
| 3 | Earnings Per Share (EPS) | $EPS = \dfrac{(EBIT - I)(1-t) - PD}{N}$ | $PD$ = Pref. dividend, $N$ = number of equity shares |
| 4 | Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL) | $DFL = \dfrac{EBIT}{EBT} = \dfrac{Contribution}{EBT}$ | Measures sensitivity of EPS to EBIT |
| 5 | Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) | $DOL = \dfrac{Contribution}{EBIT}$ | Sensitivity of EBIT to Sales |
| 6 | Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL) | $DCL = DOL \times DFL = \dfrac{Contribution}{EBT}$ | Total sensitivity to sales |
| 7 | Indifference (Indifference) EBIT | $\dfrac{(EBIT - I_1)(1-t) - PD_1}{N_1} = \dfrac{(EBIT - I_2)(1-t) - PD_2}{N_2}$ | $EPS_1 = EPS_2$ at indifference point |
| 8 | MM Without Tax | $V_L = V_U = \dfrac{EBIT(1-t)}{K_e^u}$ | Capital structure irrelevant |
| 9 | MM With Corporate Tax | $V_L = V_U + t \cdot D$ | Value of debt tax shield |
| 10 | MM Cost of Equity (with tax) | $K_e^L = K_e^u + (K_e^u - K_d)(1-t)\dfrac{D}{E}$ | Risk-adjusted levered equity cost |
| 11 | Interest Tax Shield | $TS = I \cdot t$ | Tax saved due to interest deductibility |
| 12 | Break-even EBIT | $EBIT = I$ | Where EPS becomes zero (no tax, no pref.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize for KTU Board:** The **Indifference Point formula** and the **MM Proposition I & II** are the most tested topics in this module.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business

In capital-intensive engineering firms (e.g., BHEL, L&T, Reliance Industries), the capital structure decision directly affects:
- **Project IRR viability** — Lower $K_o$ makes positive-NPV projects more numerous.
- **Bidding competitiveness** — Lower WACC enables aggressive pricing.
- **Infrastructure finance** — Most Indian infra projects (NHAI, Metro rail) use a debt-equity ratio close to 70:30 via the PPP model.

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cost of Equity Under MM Without Tax (Proposition II)

Modigliani & Miller (1958) proposed that in perfect markets (no taxes, no bankruptcy costs), the cost of equity in a levered firm rises linearly with the debt-equity ratio to offset the cheaper cost of debt, keeping WACC constant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Without tax: } V_L &= V_U \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow (E + D) &= \dfrac{EBIT(1-t)}{K_e^u} \quad \text{(since } V_U = \dfrac{EBIT(1-t)}{K_e^u}\text{)} \\[6pt]
\text{Total return required by all investors} &= \text{Total operating income (net)} \\[6pt]
K_e^L \cdot E + K_d \cdot D &= K_e^u \cdot (E + D) \\[6pt]
K_e^L &= K_e^u + \dfrac{D}{E}(K_e^u - K_d)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The rise in $K_e^L$ exactly offsets the benefit of lower-cost debt. Result: $K_o$ stays the same → Firm value is independent of capital structure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MM With Corporate Tax (1963)

When corporate tax exists, interest is tax-deductible, creating a perpetual tax shield of value $t \cdot D$. The levered firm value becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_L &= V_U + \text{PV of tax shield} \\[6pt]
V_L &= V_U + \dfrac{t \cdot D \cdot K_d}{K_d} \quad (\text{perpetuity at } K_d) \\[6pt]
V_L &= V_U + t \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

Correspondingly, the cost of equity with tax becomes:

$$
K_e^L = K_e^u + (K_e^u - K_d)(1-t)\dfrac{D}{E}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor $(1-t)$ replaces $1$ in the Proposition II formula. This reflects the reduced risk to equity holders because the government shares part of the financing burden via tax savings.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The EBIT–EPS Indifference Point (Most Frequently Tested in KTU Board)

The **indifference (break-even) EBIT** is the level of operating income at which two alternative financing plans produce the **same EPS**.

Let:
- Plan 1: Interest $= I_1$, Preference Dividend $= PD_1$, No. of Equity Shares $= N_1$
- Plan 2: Interest $= I_2$, Preference Dividend $= PD_2$, No. of Equity Shares $= N_2$
- Tax rate $= t$

Setting EPS of Plan 1 = EPS of Plan 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{(EBIT - I_1)(1-t) - PD_1}{N_1} &= \dfrac{(EBIT - I_2)(1-t) - PD_2}{N_2} \\[8pt]
\text{Cross-multiplying:} \\[6pt]
N_2 \cdot [(EBIT - I_1)(1-t) - PD_1] &= N_1 \cdot [(EBIT - I_2)(1-t) - PD_2] \\[6pt]
N_2 (EBIT)(1-t) - N_2 I_1(1-t) - N_2 PD_1 &= N_1 (EBIT)(1-t) - N_1 I_2(1-t) - N_1 PD_2 \\[6pt]
\text{Collecting EBIT terms:} \\[6pt]
(1-t) \cdot EBIT \cdot (N_2 - N_1) &= N_2 I_1(1-t) + N_2 PD_1 - N_1 I_2(1-t) - N_1 PD_2 \\[6pt]
\text{Indifference EBIT} &= \dfrac{N_2 I_1(1-t) + N_2 PD_1 - N_1 I_2(1-t) - N_1 PD_2}{(1-t)(N_2 - N_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Interpretation Matrix

| Expected EBIT vs. Indifference EBIT | Better Plan | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Expected EBIT > Indifference EBIT** | **Debt Plan** (Higher Leverage) | Fixed interest cost is small relative to EBIT → EPS amplified |
| **Expected EBIT < Indifference EBIT** | **Equity Plan** (Lower Leverage) | Fixed interest burden hurts EPS in low-EBIT scenarios |
| **Expected EBIT = Indifference EBIT** | Both plans yield **same EPS** | Indifference by definition |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> **Worked Example 1 — Capital Structure Decision via EPS**
>
> A firm needs ₹20,00,000 and is evaluating two plans:
> - **Plan A (Debt):** ₹20,00,000 as 12% debt + 1,00,000 equity shares of ₹10 each.
> - **Plan B (Equity):** ₹20,00,000 as fresh issue of 2,00,000 equity shares of ₹10 each.
> - Tax rate $t = 30\%$, Expected EBIT = ₹4,00,000.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan A: Interest } I_A &= 12\% \times 20,00,000 = 2,40,000 \\[6pt]
N_A &= 1,00,000 \text{ shares} \\[6pt]
EPS_A &= \dfrac{(EBIT - I_A)(1-t)}{N_A} = \dfrac{(4,00,000 - 2,40,000)(0.70)}{1,00,000} \\[6pt]
EPS_A &= \dfrac{1,60,000 \times 0.70}{1,00,000} = \dfrac{1,12,000}{1,00,000} = ₹1.12
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan B: Interest } I_B &= 0 \\[6pt]
N_B &= 2,00,000 \text{ shares} \\[6pt]
EPS_B &= \dfrac{(4,00,000 - 0)(0.70)}{2,00,000} = \dfrac{2,80,000}{2,00,000} = ₹1.40
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Decision:** Plan B (Equity) gives higher EPS (₹1.40 > ₹1.12) at this EBIT. Choose **Plan B**.

**Now compute the Indifference EBIT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{(EBIT - 2,40,000)(0.70)}{1,00,000} &= \dfrac{EBIT \times 0.70}{2,00,000} \\[6pt]
2 \cdot (EBIT - 2,40,000) &= EBIT \\[6pt]
2 \cdot EBIT - 4,80,000 &= EBIT \\[6pt]
EBIT &= 4,80,000
\end{aligned}
$$

> If expected EBIT exceeds ₹4,80,000, Plan A (Debt) is preferable. Below ₹4,80,000, Plan B (Equity) wins.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Three-Plan EBIT–EPS Comparison

A company wants to raise ₹50 lakh. Three plans:
- **Plan I:** All equity — 5,00,000 shares of ₹10 each.
- **Plan II:** Debt ₹25 lakh @ 12% + 2,50,000 equity shares.
- **Plan III:** Pref. share ₹25 lakh @ 8% + 2,50,000 equity shares.
- Tax rate $t = 30\%$.

**EPS Calculations Table:**

| Plan | EBIT ₹ | Interest ₹ | Pref. Div. ₹ | $(EBIT - I - PD)(1-t)$ ₹ | No. of Shares | EPS ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I (All Eq.) | 5,00,000 | 0 | 0 | 3,50,000 | 5,00,000 | **0.70** |
| II (Debt)  | 5,00,000 | 3,00,000 | 0 | 1,40,000 | 2,50,000 | **0.56** |
| III (Pref.)| 5,00,000 | 0 | 2,00,000 | 2,10,000 | 2,50,000 | **0.84** |

> **Best plan at EBIT = ₹5,00,000:** Plan III (Preference Shares) → Highest EPS.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Sensitivity — Leverage Visualization

$$
\begin{aligned}
DFL &= \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \dfrac{PD}{(1-t)}} \\[6pt]
\text{If EBIT} &= 5,00,000,\ I = 2,00,000,\ t = 0.30,\ PD = 0 \\[6pt]
DFL &= \dfrac{5,00,000}{5,00,000 - 2,00,000} = \dfrac{5,00,000}{3,00,000} = 1.667
\end{aligned}
$$

> A 10% increase in EBIT → $10\% \times 1.667 = 16.67\%$ increase in EPS.

# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Capital Structure Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Finance Manager Identifies Funding Need"] --> B["Assess Internal Factors: Size, Risk, Cash Flows, Asset Structure"]
    B --> C["Assess External Factors: Markets, Tax, Regulations, Lender Attitudes"]
    C --> D["Evaluate Financing Alternatives: Equity, Debt, Preference, Retained Earnings"]
    D --> E["Apply Theories: NI, NOI, MM, Traditional, Pecking Order"]
    E --> F["Conduct EBIT-EPS Indifference Analysis"]
    F --> G["Compute WACC Ko and Firm Value VL"]
    G --> H{"Is Ko Minimized AND VL Maximized?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Adopt Optimal Capital Structure"]
    H -- No --> J["Re-balance Debt-Equity Weights"]
    J --> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Hierarchy of Financing Decisions (Pecking Order)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Internal Funds Retained Earnings"] --> B["Step 2: Low-Risk Debt Bank Loans Bonds"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Subordinated Debt and Convertibles"]
    C --> D["Step 4: External Equity New Share Issue"]
    D --> E["Last Resort: Only When Asymmetric Information Risk is High"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Comparative Map of Capital Structure Theories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T["Capital Structure Theories"] --> A["Classical Approaches"]
    T --> B["MM Approaches"]
    T --> C["Modern Approaches"]

    A --> A1["Net Income Approach"]
    A --> A2["Net Operating Income Approach"]
    A --> A3["Traditional Approach"]

    B --> B1["MM Without Tax Irrelevance Proposition"]
    B --> B2["MM With Corporate Tax Tax Shield Proposition"]

    C --> C1["Trade-Off Theory"]
    C --> C2["Pecking Order Theory"]
    C --> C3["Signalling Theory"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — EBIT-EPS Indifference Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Compute EBIT-EPS for Each Plan"] --> B["Set EPS of Plan 1 equal to EPS of Plan 2"]
    B --> C["Solve for Indifference EBIT"]
    C --> D{"Expected EBIT Compared"}
    D -- Greater --> E["Higher Leverage Plan is Superior"]
    D -- Smaller --> F["Lower Leverage Plan is Superior"]
    D -- Equal --> G["Both Plans Equally Good at this EBIT Level"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - In Diagram 1, the loop "Re-balance → Alternatives" signifies that capital structure selection is **iterative**, not one-shot.
> - In Diagram 2 (Pecking Order), as one moves down the chain, **information asymmetry cost rises**, hence the order of preference.
> - In Diagram 4, the **decision rule** is straightforward: pick the leverage that maximizes EPS at the firm's *expected* EBIT.
