# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Capital Structure. What is an optimal capital structure?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Capital Structure** is the composition of a firm's long-term financing consisting of debt, preference share capital, equity share capital, and retained earnings.
>
> An **Optimal Capital Structure** is the specific debt-equity mix that minimizes the firm's weighted average cost of capital (WACC) and simultaneously maximizes its market value ($V_L$). Beyond this point, the rise in $K_e$ (cost of equity) and $K_d$ (cost of debt) outweighs the benefits of leverage, causing $K_o$ to rise and $V_L$ to fall. (3 marks)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is an Indifference Point in capital structure planning? What does it indicate?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Indifference Point (or Break-Even EBIT)** is the level of operating profit (EBIT) at which two alternative capital structure plans produce the same Earnings Per Share (EPS).
>
> **Indication:** If the firm's *expected EBIT* exceeds the indifference point, the plan with **higher financial leverage** (more debt) gives higher EPS, and vice versa. It helps the finance manager choose the appropriate financing alternative based on EBIT expectations. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Distinguish between the **Net Income Approach** and the **Net Operating Income Approach** to capital structure. Which one is considered more realistic, and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** XYZ Ltd. needs ₹40 lakh for expansion. Three alternatives are available:
- **Plan X:** All Equity — 4,00,000 equity shares of ₹10 each.
- **Plan Y:** 50% Debt @ 12% — ₹20 lakh debt + 2,00,000 equity shares of ₹10 each.
- **Plan Z:** 50% Preference @ 10% — ₹20 lakh preference + 2,00,000 equity shares.

The expected EBIT is ₹6,00,000 and the corporate tax rate is 30%.
- (i) Calculate EPS under each plan.
- (ii) Determine the indifference EBIT between Plan X and Plan Y.
- (iii) Comment on the best plan. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a):**

| Basis | Net Income Approach | Net Operating Income Approach |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Assumption on $K_d$ | Constant at all levels of leverage | Constant at all levels |
| 2. Assumption on $K_e$ | Constant | Varies; rises with leverage |
| 3. WACC ($K_o$) | Falls with debt → optimal at max debt | Constant irrespective of leverage |
| 4. Firm Value ($V_L$) | Rises continuously with debt | Remains constant |
| 5. Realism | Less realistic; ignores bankruptcy cost | More realistic in MM sense |
| 6. Conclusion | Capital structure affects value | Capital structure is irrelevant |

> **Most realistic:** The **Traditional Approach** (a hybrid) is considered more realistic, as it accepts the existence of an **optimal capital structure** where WACC is minimized. NI and NOI are extreme cases; reality lies in between. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step (i) — EPS Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan X (All Equity):} \quad EPS_X &= \dfrac{(EBIT - 0)(1-t) - 0}{N_X} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{6,00,000 \times 0.70}{4,00,000} = \dfrac{4,20,000}{4,00,000} = ₹1.05
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan Y (Debt):} \quad I_Y &= 12\% \times 20,00,000 = 2,40,000 \\[6pt]
EPS_Y &= \dfrac{(6,00,000 - 2,40,000)(0.70)}{2,00,000} = \dfrac{3,60,000 \times 0.70}{2,00,000} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2,52,000}{2,00,000} = ₹1.26
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan Z (Preference):} \quad PD_Z &= 10\% \times 20,00,000 = 2,00,000 \\[6pt]
EPS_Z &= \dfrac{(6,00,000 - 0)(0.70) - 2,00,000}{2,00,000} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{4,20,000 - 2,00,000}{2,00,000} = \dfrac{2,20,000}{2,00,000} = ₹1.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**EPS Summary Table:**

| Plan | Structure | EPS ₹ |
|---|---|---|
| X | All Equity | 1.05 |
| Y | 50% Debt | **1.26** |
| Z | 50% Preference | 1.10 |

**Step (ii) — Indifference EBIT between Plan X and Plan Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{(EBIT - 0)(0.70)}{4,00,000} &= \dfrac{(EBIT - 2,40,000)(0.70)}{2,00,000} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{EBIT}{4,00,000} &= \dfrac{EBIT - 2,40,000}{2,00,000} \\[6pt]
2,00,000 \cdot EBIT &= 4,00,000 \cdot (EBIT - 2,40,000) \\[6pt]
2,00,000 \cdot EBIT &= 4,00,000 \cdot EBIT - 96,00,00,000 \\[6pt]
2,00,000 \cdot EBIT &= 9,60,00,000 \\[6pt]
EBIT &= ₹4,80,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Decision:**

- Expected EBIT = ₹6,00,000 > Indifference EBIT ₹4,80,000.
- Hence **Plan Y (Debt)** is the **best choice** with highest EPS of ₹1.26. (7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Modigliani-Miller (MM) Approach** to capital structure. How does the introduction of corporate tax modify the MM propositions? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is evaluating two financing alternatives for raising ₹10 crore:
- **Plan A:** Issue 10% Debentures worth ₹10 crore. Existing equity = 50 lakh shares.
- **Plan B:** Issue equity shares worth ₹10 crore at par (₹10 each).

EBIT is expected to be ₹2.5 crore. Tax rate = 30%.
- (i) Compute EPS under both plans.
- (ii) Find the indifference EBIT.
- (iii) If EBIT falls to ₹1.2 crore due to recession, which plan is safer? Show with calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**MM Without Tax (1958):**
$$
V_L = V_U = \frac{EBIT(1-t)}{K_e^u}
$$
- Assumes perfect capital markets, no taxes, no bankruptcy costs.
- Proposition I: Firm value is independent of capital structure.
- Proposition II: $K_e^L = K_e^u + (K_e^u - K_d) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E}$
- Total return to all investors = EBIT(1-t) regardless of mix.

**MM With Corporate Tax (1963):**
- Interest is tax-deductible → creates a perpetual tax shield $t \cdot D$.
- **Proposition I:** $V_L = V_U + t \cdot D$
- **Proposition II:** $K_e^L = K_e^u + (K_e^u - K_d)(1-t) \cdot \dfrac{D}{E}$
- The $(1-t)$ factor in Proposition II reflects the reduced equity risk due to government sharing of cost.

**Conclusion:** With taxes, debt is always favorable, and the firm should ideally use 100% debt (in pure MM model). In reality, financial distress costs prevent this, leading to the **Trade-off Theory**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step (i) — EPS Computation (EBIT = ₹2.5 crore):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan A (Debt):} \quad I_A &= 10\% \times 10 \text{ crore} = 1 \text{ crore} \\[6pt]
EPS_A &= \dfrac{(2.5 - 1)(1 - 0.30)}{50 \text{ lakh}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 0.70}{50 \text{ lakh}} = \dfrac{1.05 \text{ crore}}{50 \text{ lakh}} = ₹2.10
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan B (Equity):} \quad N_B &= 50 \text{ lakh} + \dfrac{10 \text{ crore}}{₹10} = 50 \text{ lakh} + 1 \text{ crore shares} = 1.5 \text{ crore} \\[6pt]
EPS_B &= \dfrac{(2.5 - 0)(0.70)}{1.5 \text{ crore}} = \dfrac{1.75 \text{ crore}}{1.5 \text{ crore}} = ₹1.1667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Indifference EBIT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{(EBIT - 1)(0.70)}{50} &= \dfrac{EBIT(0.70)}{150} \quad (\text{all figures in lakh shares and crore rupees}) \\[6pt]
3(EBIT - 1) &= EBIT \\[6pt]
3 \cdot EBIT - 3 &= EBIT \\[6pt]
2 \cdot EBIT &= 3 \\[6pt]
EBIT &= ₹1.5 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Recession Scenario (EBIT = ₹1.2 crore):**

Since ₹1.2 crore < Indifference EBIT ₹1.5 crore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPS_A &= \dfrac{(1.2 - 1)(0.70)}{50 \text{ lakh}} = \dfrac{0.20 \times 0.70}{50} = \dfrac{0.14}{50} = ₹0.28 \\[6pt]
EPS_B &= \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.70}{150 \text{ lakh}} = \dfrac{0.84}{150} = ₹0.56
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Safer Plan in Recession: Plan B (Equity)** — EPS of ₹0.56 vs Plan A's ₹0.28. Fixed interest burden hurts Plan A when EBIT falls. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to deduct preference dividend *before* tax — preference dividend is paid out of **post-tax profits**, NOT tax-deductible.
> - **Mistake 2:** Using *book value* of debt and equity instead of **market value** in WACC calculation. KTU board exams almost always expect **market values** for valuation purposes.
> - **Mistake 3:** In MM-with-tax, writing $V_L = V_U + t \cdot I$ instead of $V_L = V_U + t \cdot D$. The shield applies to the **principal amount of debt**, not the annual interest.
> - **Mistake 4:** Skipping the units (₹/lakh/crore) in final answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.
> - **Mistake 5:** Computing EPS using *book value* interest instead of *total* interest on **fresh debt** when financing is incremental.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

| # | Key Takeaway | Quick Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capital structure = long-term debt + equity mix | "Permanent financing" |
| 2 | Optimal structure minimizes WACC ($K_o$) | "$K_o$ ↓ → $V_L$ ↑" |
| 3 | NI Approach → $K_o$ decreases with leverage | "Cheap debt always wins" |
| 4 | NOI Approach → $K_o$ constant | "$V_L = V_U$ regardless" |
| 5 | Traditional → Optimal exists in middle | "Sweet spot exists" |
| 6 | MM Without Tax → Irrelevance | "No tax, no gain" |
| 7 | MM With Tax → $V_L = V_U + t \cdot D$ | "Debt = Money saver" |
| 8 | Trade-off Theory → Tax shield vs Distress cost | "Balancing act" |
| 9 | Pecking Order → Internal > Debt > Equity | "Self-financing first" |
| 10 | Indifference EBIT determines plan choice | "EBIT > ID-EBIT → Debt wins" |
| 11 | DFL amplifies EPS changes vs EBIT changes | "Leverage is a magnifier" |
| 12 | Preference dividend is NOT tax-deductible | "PD paid from after-tax" |
| 13 | Interest IS tax-deductible | "$I$ shields from tax" |
| 14 | Use **market values** for weights in WACC | "MV not BV" |
| 15 | Increase in EBIT always benefits leveraged plan more | "More to gain, more to lose" |

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board
- [ ] Define capital structure vs financial structure.
- [ ] State & compare the **five approaches** with diagrams.
- [ ] Memorize the **indifference EBIT formula** (with and without preference dividend).
- [ ] Derive MM-I and MM-II (with & without tax).
- [ ] Practice **3-plan EBIT-EPS tables** (debt vs equity vs preference).
- [ ] Understand **Trade-off Theory** graphical representation.
- [ ] Be ready for **DFL** computation and interpretation questions.
- [ ] Always compute EPS at **two EBIT levels** to justify plan selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mantra for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> "*Indifference EBIT is the switch. Above it = Debt wins. Below it = Equity wins. At it = Both equal.*"
>
> Always justify your plan choice by comparing **expected EBIT** to **indifference EBIT**, then support it with **DFL analysis** and **WACC minimization** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dividends and Share Repurchase: Dividends - Forms, Payment Chronology, Dividend Policy and Company Value, Factors Affecting Dividend Policy, Pay-out Policies, Analysis of Dividend Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dividends and Share Repurchase: Dividends - Forms, Payment Chronology, Dividend Policy and Company Value, Factors Affecting Dividend Policy, Pay-out Policies, Analysis of Dividend Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Foundational Definition of Dividends

A **dividend** is the formal distribution of a portion of a corporation's **after-tax earnings** (net profits) to its equity shareholders, decided by the company's **Board of Directors**. It represents the primary cash return that shareholders receive for the capital they have invested into the business, the other being capital appreciation through share price growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-Aligned):**
> A dividend is a reward, paid to the shareholders, out of the profits of the company, for the risk capital invested by them in the business. Under the Indian Companies Act, 2013 (Sections 123 and 127), dividends can only be paid out of **current profits** OR **accumulated profits of previous years**, OR out of **both**. Dividends cannot be paid out of capital.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Mango Tree"

Imagine a family that owns a large mango tree. Every summer, the tree bears fruits. The family has three choices:
- **Sell all the mangoes** (Pay 100% as dividend)
- **Eat some, sell the rest** (Pay partial dividend, retain some)
- **Plant the seeds and grow more trees** (Retain all earnings, no dividend)

The decision depends on whether the family **needs cash now** or wants to **build a larger orchard for the future**. The mango tree is the **company's profit pool**, and each mango represents a **rupee of profit** that must be split between today's shareholders (dividends) and future growth (retained earnings). The **dividend policy** is precisely this allocation rule.

## 1.3 The Distinction Between Dividends and Share Repurchase

| Aspect | Dividend | Share Repurchase |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Distribution of cash/stock to all shareholders pro-rata | Company buys back its own shares from the market |
| Payment Method | Cash, stock, property, scrip | Cash to selling shareholders |
| Tax Treatment (India) | Taxed in hands of shareholders as income | Buyback taxed as capital gains |
| Effect on Share Count | **Unchanged** | **Reduces** outstanding shares |
| Effect on EPS | No change | EPS increases mechanically |
| Frequency | Quarterly / Annual | Discretionary, ad-hoc |

> [!NOTE]
> **Module Focus:** While the broader module covers share repurchase, this specific topic zeroes in on **dividends** — their forms, timing mechanics, valuation impact, determinants, payout variants, and safety diagnostics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forms of Dividends

The Board of Directors may declare dividends in one of several structural forms. Each form has distinct accounting, tax, and signaling consequences.

### 2.1.1 Cash Dividend (Most Common)
A **cash dividend** is a direct cash payment per share to shareholders. It is the default, universally understood form of dividend and represents a real outflow of cash from the company's bank account.

- **Typical frequency:** Quarterly (US/UK), Annual or Interim (India).
- **Example:** Reliance Industries declaring ₹10 per share as interim dividend.

### 2.1.2 Stock Dividend (Bonus Shares)
A **stock dividend** (called **Bonus Shares** in India) is the issue of additional shares to existing shareholders **free of cost**, in proportion to their existing holdings. No cash leaves the company.

$$
\text{Number of Bonus Shares} = \frac{\text{Bonus Ratio}}{1} \times \text{Shares Held}
$$

- **Why issue them?** To capitalize retained earnings (convert reserves to share capital), reward shareholders without depleting cash, and improve liquidity of the stock in the market.

### 2.1.3 Property Dividend
The company distributes **non-cash assets** (e.g., shares of a subsidiary, inventory, gold) to shareholders. Extremely rare; only used in liquidation or restructuring scenarios.

### 2.1.4 Scrip Dividend
The company issues a **promissory note (scrip)** payable at a future date, instead of immediate cash. Used historically; practically extinct in modern finance. Currently disallowed under Indian Companies Act 2013.

### 2.1.5 Liquidating Dividend
A distribution that represents a **return of capital** rather than a return on capital. It is paid out of the original invested capital (paid-up share capital), not from profits. Common during partial wind-downs of a business.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In valuation questions, **Cash Dividends** and **Bonus Shares** carry 90% of the weightage. Property, Scrip, and Liquidating dividends are theoretical knowledge items only.

## 2.2 The Payment Chronology (Dividend Process Timeline)

The corporate dividend payment follows a strict four-stage chronology. KTU examiners **frequently** ask students to sequence these dates — committing the wrong order results in losing marks.

### Stage 1: Declaration Date
The **Board of Directors meeting** at which the dividend is formally announced. On this date, the company creates a legal liability (dividends payable becomes a current liability on the balance sheet).

### Stage 2: Ex-Dividend Date
A date set by the **stock exchange** (typically one business day before the record date). Shareholders who **buy shares ON or AFTER the ex-dividend date** will **NOT** receive the declared dividend. The stock price typically **drops by approximately the dividend amount** on this date.

### Stage 3: Record Date (Date of Record)
The company identifies its **shareholders of record** as of this date. Only shareholders whose names appear in the company's register on this date are entitled to receive the dividend.

### Stage 4: Payment Date
The actual **cash is disbursed** to eligible shareholders, either via electronic transfer (NEFT/RTGS/IMPS) or dividend warrants/cheques.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Sequencing Rule:** Declaration Date → Ex-Dividend Date → Record Date → Payment Date.
> The **Ex-Dividend Date ALWAYS precedes the Record Date by one business day** under T+1 settlement.

---

## 2.3 Dividend Policy and Company Value — The Great Theoretical Debate

This is the **most heavily tested** sub-topic in KTU examinations. The central question is: **Does the dividend policy of a firm affect its intrinsic value?**

### 2.3.1 Miller-Modigliani (MM) Dividend Irrelevance Theorem (1961)

**Position:** In a **perfect capital market** (no taxes, no transaction costs, no bankruptcy costs, symmetric information, rational investors), the dividend policy of a firm is **irrelevant** to its share value.

$$
V_L = V_U
$$

Where:
- $V_L$ = Value of the levered (dividend-paying) firm
- $V_U$ = Value of the unlevered (no-dividend) firm

**Logic:** The total value of the firm depends on the **profitability of its assets** and the **risk of its cash flows**, not on how these cash flows are split between dividends and retained earnings. If a shareholder receives an unwanted dividend, they can simply use it to **buy more shares** (this is the "homemade dividend" concept).

### 2.3.2 The "Bird-in-the-Hand" Theory (Gordon-Lintner, 1956)

**Position:** Investors prefer **certain current dividends** to **uncertain future capital gains**. Therefore, firms that pay higher dividends have **higher** valuations.

**Logic:** A rupee of dividend in hand today is worth more than a rupee of uncertain capital appreciation tomorrow.

$$
V_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}
$$

This is the **Gordon Dividend Growth Model**. A higher $D_1$ (dividend) directly increases $V_0$ (share price).

### 2.3.3 Tax Preference Theory

**Position:** Because dividends are taxed at higher rates than capital gains in many jurisdictions, investors prefer **capital appreciation over dividends**. Therefore, dividend-paying firms should have **lower** valuations than non-dividend-paying firms with equivalent earnings.

### 2.3.4 Clientele Effect

**Position:** Different investor clienteles prefer different dividend policies:
- **Retirees / Pension funds** → High dividend payers
- **Growth-focused investors** → Zero/low dividend payers (more reinvestment)

Firms attract the clientele whose preferences match their policy; thus policy still doesn't matter once clienteles are sorted.

### 2.3.5 Signaling Theory (Bhattacharya, 1979)

**Position:** Managers possess **private information** about future earnings. A dividend increase is a **costly, credible signal** that management is confident about future profits. Markets interpret dividend hikes positively and dividend cuts negatively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** In a 14-mark question, KTU examiners expect you to discuss **at least three theories** and conclude with the relevance in **real-world imperfect markets** (where MM's assumptions don't hold). A pure theoretical recital without application will cap marks at 7/14.

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Dividend Policy

A firm's dividend policy is shaped by a web of internal and external constraints. KTU examiners often ask students to enumerate these factors.

### 2.4.1 Legal Constraints
- **Section 123 of Companies Act, 2013** mandates that dividends be paid only from profits.
- **Section 127** requires payment within 30 days of declaration; failure invites penal interest.
- Companies must transfer a minimum percentage of profits to **reserves** before declaring dividends (Depreciation → Reserves → Dividend).

### 2.4.2 Contractual Constraints
- **Loan covenants** may prohibit or limit dividend payment if the firm is already highly leveraged.
- **Bond indentures** may contain negative pledge clauses.

### 2.4.3 Internal Constraints
- **Liquidity position:** A firm may have accounting profits but no cash. Dividends require cash, not accrual profits.
- **Earnings stability:** Firms with stable, predictable earnings (FMCG, utilities) pay higher dividends than cyclical firms (auto, commodities).
- **Growth opportunities:** High-growth firms (Startups, IT) retain more to fund expansion.
- **Access to capital markets:** Firms with easy access to equity capital pay higher dividends.

### 2.4.4 External Constraints
- **Taxation:** Differential tax treatment of dividends vs. capital gains.
- **Shareholder expectations and preferences.**
- **Industry norms and peer behavior.**

---

## 2.5 Pay-out Policies

The **payout policy** is the firm's declared rule for translating earnings into dividends. Four standard variants exist:

### 2.5.1 Stable Dividend Policy (Most Common Among Mature Firms)
- **Rule:** Pay a **fixed dividend per share** regardless of earnings fluctuations; raise the dividend only when sustainable earnings have clearly stepped up.
- **Pros:** Conveys stability, attracts risk-averse investors, smoothens share price volatility.
- **Cons:** May force the firm to pay dividends even during losses (using reserves).

### 2.5.2 Constant Payout Ratio Policy
- **Rule:** Pay a **fixed percentage of net earnings** as dividend each year.
- **Pros:** Dividends rise and fall with profits; perfectly aligned with cash flows.
- **Cons:** Highly volatile dividends → signals uncertainty to market → share price volatility.

### 2.5.3 Residual Dividend Policy
- **Rule:** Pay dividends **only after funding all positive-NPV projects** with retained earnings.
- **Pros:** Maximizes firm value; preserves capital for growth.
- **Cons:** Highly volatile dividends; unattractive to dividend-preferring clientele.

### 2.5.4 Hybrid / Staged Dividend Policy
- **Rule:** A **combination** of stable dividend + extra/special dividend in profitable years.
- **Pros:** Combines stability (base) with flexibility (bonus).
- **Cons:** Requires sophisticated investor communication.

---

## 2.6 Analysis of Dividend Safety

**Dividend Safety** is the probability that the company can **sustain its current dividend payment** without compromising operational health, solvency, or future growth capacity.

### Key Diagnostic Metrics

**1. Dividend Payout Ratio (PR)**
$$
\text{Payout Ratio} = \frac{\text{Dividend per Share (DPS)}}{\text{Earnings per Share (EPS)}}
$$

**2. Retention Ratio (b)**
$$
\text{Retention Ratio} = 1 - \text{Payout Ratio}
$$

**3. Dividend Coverage Ratio (DCR)**
$$
\text{DCR} = \frac{\text{Earnings per Share}}{\text{Dividend per Share}} = \frac{1}{\text{Payout Ratio}}
$$

**4. Sustainable Growth Rate (g) — Higgins Formula**
$$
g = \text{ROE} \times b = \text{ROE} \times (1 - \text{Payout Ratio})
$$

**5. Free Cash Flow Coverage**
$$
\text{FCF Coverage} = \frac{\text{Free Cash Flow}}{\text{Total Dividends Paid}}
$$

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table.** Every formula here is a verified, exam-grade equation. Memorize the boundary conditions.

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dividend Per Share | $\text{DPS} = \dfrac{\text{Total Dividend Paid}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares}}$ | Total dividend, shares | Compute cash dividend per share |
| Earnings Per Share | $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit After Tax (NPAT)}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares}}$ | NPAT, shares | Compute profitability per share |
| Payout Ratio | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{\text{DPS}}{\text{EPS}}$ | DPS, EPS | What % of earnings is paid out |
| Retention Ratio | $b = 1 - \text{PR}$ | Payout ratio | What % of earnings is reinvested |
| Dividend Coverage | $\text{DCR} = \dfrac{\text{EPS}}{\text{DPS}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PR}}$ | EPS, DPS | Cushion of safety for dividend |
| Sustainable Growth Rate | $g = \text{ROE} \times b$ | ROE, retention | Max growth achievable without external equity |
| Gordon Growth Model | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g}$ | $D_1$, $K_e$, $g$ | Share price valuation with dividend |
| Expected Dividend Yield | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0}$ | Next year dividend, price | Income return component |
| Walter's Model | $P_0 = \dfrac{D + \dfrac{r}{K_e}(E-D)}{K_e}$ | $D$, $r$ (ROE), $E$, $K_e$ | Optimal dividend for stable firm |
| Gordon's Model | $P_0 = \dfrac{E(1-b)}{K_e - br}$ | $E$, $b$, $r$, $K_e$ | Share price as function of retention |
| FCF Coverage | $\text{FCFC} = \dfrac{\text{FCF}}{\text{Total Dividends}}$ | FCF, dividends | Cash-based safety check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - If $g < K_e$: Gordon model gives a finite, positive share price.
> - If $g \geq K_e$: Gordon model is invalid (formula blows up).
> - If Payout Ratio = 1: Retention = 0, Sustainable Growth = 0.
> - If Payout Ratio = 0: All earnings retained, max sustainable growth.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gordon Dividend Growth Model

The Gordon Growth Model (GGM) is the cornerstone of dividend valuation theory. Below is the exhaustive derivation, starting from first principles.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Basic Present Value Framework

The current price of a share, $P_0$, equals the present value of all future expected dividends:

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots
$$

**Conversion logic:** We are converting an infinite series of future cash flows (dividends in years 1, 2, 3, ...) into a single present-day valuation figure by discounting each cash flow at the cost of equity $K_e$.

### Step 2 — Impose the Constant Growth Assumption

Assume dividends grow at a **constant rate $g$** forever:

$$
D_2 = D_1(1+g), \quad D_3 = D_2(1+g) = D_1(1+g)^2, \quad \dots
$$

Substitute back into the series:

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_1(1+g)}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_1(1+g)^2}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots
$$

### Step 3 — Factor Out $D_1$

$$
P_0 = D_1 \left[ \frac{1}{(1+K_e)} + \frac{(1+g)}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{(1+g)^2}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Infinite Geometric Series Formula

For a geometric series with first term $a$ and common ratio $r$, the sum equals $\dfrac{a}{1-r}$ when $\vert r \vert < 1$.

Here, $a = \dfrac{1}{1+K_e}$ and $r = \dfrac{1+g}{1+K_e}$.

Applying the formula:

$$
P_0 = D_1 \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{1+K_e}}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the Denominator

$$
1 - \frac{1+g}{1+K_e} = \frac{(1+K_e) - (1+g)}{1+K_e} = \frac{K_e - g}{1+K_e}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Back

$$
P_0 = D_1 \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{1+K_e}}{\dfrac{K_e - g}{1+K_e}}
$$

The $(1+K_e)$ terms cancel:

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the final, board-grade Gordon Growth Model equation.** Note the critical constraint: $K_e > g$. If $g \geq K_e$, the model is mathematically invalid because the share price would be infinite or negative.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Valuation Problem

> **Worked Example (KTU-Style):**
> A company expects to pay a dividend of ₹5 next year. The cost of equity is 12%. Dividends are expected to grow at a constant 6% per annum forever. Compute the current intrinsic value of the share.

### Step 1 — Identify Variables
- $D_1 = 5$ (next year's dividend)
- $K_e = 12\% = 0.12$
- $g = 6\% = 0.06$

### Step 2 — Verify the Boundary Condition

$$
K_e - g = 0.12 - 0.06 = 0.06 > 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

Model is valid.

### Step 3 — Apply the Gordon Growth Formula

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g} = \frac{5}{0.12 - 0.06} = \frac{5}{0.06}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Final Value

$$
P_0 = \frac{5}{0.06} = 83.33 \text{ rupees}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Implied Dividend Yield

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{D_1}{P_0} = \frac{5}{83.33} = 6.00\%
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Capital Gains Yield (Sanity Check)

$$
\text{Capital Gains Yield} = K_e - \text{Yield} = 12\% - 6\% = 6\% \quad \checkmark
$$

The total expected return equals $K_e = 12\%$, confirming internal consistency.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Dividend Safety Diagnostic Grid

The table below is the **KTU Board Examiner's master checklist** for evaluating whether a firm's dividend is safe. Use it as a rubric in 14-mark analytical questions.

| Diagnostic Metric | Healthy / Safe Threshold | Warning / Danger Threshold | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Payout Ratio** | Below **50%** | Above **75%** | Higher payout → less reinvestment buffer |
| **Dividend Coverage Ratio** | Above **2.0x** | Below **1.5x** | Higher DCR → earnings easily cover dividend |
| **Earnings Stability (σ of EPS)** | Low standard deviation (σ < 10% of mean EPS) | High variance (σ > 25% of mean EPS) | Stable earnings = sustainable dividends |
| **FCF Coverage** | Above **1.5x** | Below **1.0x** | Dividend must be paid from cash, not accruals |
| **Debt-to-Equity Ratio** | Below **1.0** | Above **2.0** | Overleveraged firms cannot afford dividends |
| **Retention Ratio vs Growth** | $b \times \text{ROE} \approx g_{\text{actual}}$ | $b \times \text{ROE} \ll g_{\text{actual}}$ | Mismatch means firm can't self-fund growth |
| **Dividend Track Record** | 10+ consecutive years of payment | Recent dividend cuts | Track record signals commitment |

### Worked Safety Diagnostic

> **Problem:** XYZ Ltd. reports the following data:
> - Net Profit After Tax (NPAT) = ₹500 crore
> - Number of equity shares = 100 crore
> - Total dividend declared = ₹150 crore
> - Free Cash Flow = ₹120 crore
> - ROE = 18%
> - Long-term Debt = ₹400 crore; Equity = ₹600 crore
>
> **Evaluate dividend safety.**

**Step 1 — Compute EPS**

$$
\text{EPS} = \frac{500}{100} = 5 \text{ rupees}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DPS**

$$
\text{DPS} = \frac{150}{100} = 1.5 \text{ rupees}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Payout Ratio**

$$
\text{PR} = \frac{1.5}{5} = 0.30 = 30\%
$$

Below 50% → **Safe** ✓

**Step 4 — Compute Dividend Coverage Ratio**

$$
\text{DCR} = \frac{5}{1.5} = 3.33
$$

Above 2.0x → **Safe** ✓

**Step 5 — Compute FCF Coverage**

$$
\text{FCFC} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80
$$

Below 1.0x → **Danger** ✗ — The firm is paying **₹30 crore more in dividends than it generates in cash**, indicating **accrual-based, not cash-based** dividend.

**Step 6 — Compute Debt-to-Equity**

$$
\text{D/E} = \frac{400}{600} = 0.67
$$

Below 1.0 → **Healthy** ✓

**Step 7 — Verdict**

> **Composite Verdict:** While accounting metrics (EPS, Payout, DCR, D/E) signal safety, the **FCF coverage of 0.80x reveals an underlying cash-flow weakness**. The dividend is currently sustained but is **unsafe over a multi-year horizon**. The Board should consider freezing the dividend until FCF improves.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Dividend Theories

| Theory | Core Claim | Mathematical Implication | Practical Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MM Irrelevance** | $V_L = V_U$; policy doesn't matter | Value depends on operating cash flows | Holds only in perfect markets |
| **Bird-in-Hand (Gordon-Lintner)** | Dividends are less risky than capital gains | $V_0$ rises with $D_1$ | Investors do value certainty |
| **Tax Preference** | Capital gains taxed lower than dividends | Higher payout lowers $V_0$ | Strong in jurisdictions with dividend tax |
| **Clientele Effect** | Firms attract matching clienteles | Apparent policy effect is selection bias | Empirically robust |
| **Signaling Theory** | Dividends convey insider information | Dividend hike = good news; cut = bad news | Empirically supported |

---

## 3.5 Advanced Derivation — Walter's Dividend Model

**Walter's Model (1956)** answers: *What is the optimal dividend payout ratio?*

$$
P_0 = \frac{D + \dfrac{r}{K_e}(E - D)}{K_e}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Dividend per share
- $E$ = Earnings per share
- $r$ = Return on Investment (ROE)
- $K_e$ = Cost of equity

### Derivation Logic

A share's value is the sum of two parts:
1. **The dividend income** ($D$), capitalized at $K_e$.
2. **The present value of retained earnings reinvested**, which earns $r$ per rupee retained, but the market capitalizes this at $K_e$.

Thus: **Retained earnings' contribution** = $\dfrac{r}{K_e}(E - D)$.

If $r > K_e$: Firm should **retain** more (growth projects earn more than the market demands).
If $r < K_e$: Firm should **pay out** more (retained earnings destroyed value).
If $r = K_e$: Dividend is **irrelevant** (matches MM conclusion).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Payout Policies

| Payout Policy | Stability of Dividend | Growth Funding | Investor Signal | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stable Dividend** | High (fixed DPS) | Must raise external funds | Stability, confidence | Mature, stable firms (FMCG, utilities) |
| **Constant Payout Ratio** | Low (variable DPS) | Automatically funded | Volatility, risk | Cyclical firms (commodities) |
| **Residual Dividend** | Very low (only leftovers) | Internally funded optimally | Weak dividend signal | High-growth firms (startups, IT) |
| **Hybrid / Staged** | High (base) + variable bonus | Mix of internal and external | Stability + flexibility | Diversified corporates |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define "Dividend Policy" and briefly state the Modigliani-Miller hypothesis regarding dividend irrelevance.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Dividend Policy** is the firm's standing decision rule concerning the **proportion of net earnings distributed to shareholders** as dividends versus the proportion retained for reinvestment within the business.

The **Modigliani-Miller (MM) Hypothesis (1961)** states that in a **perfect capital market** — characterized by:
- No taxes
- No transaction costs
- No bankruptcy costs
- Symmetric information
- Rational investor behavior

— the **dividend policy of a firm is irrelevant to its market value**. The firm's value depends solely on its **operating earnings power and investment decisions**, not on how profits are split between dividends and retained earnings. Shareholders who prefer income can manufacture their own dividends by selling shares ("homemade dividends").

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain the concept of "Ex-Dividend Date" with its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Ex-Dividend Date** is the date set by the stock exchange (under T+1 settlement, it falls **one business day before the Record Date**) on and after which any purchaser of the stock will **NOT** be entitled to receive the next declared dividend.

**Significance:**
1. **Price Adjustment:** On the ex-dividend date, the share price typically falls by approximately the amount of the declared dividend, as the stock is now "ex" (without) the dividend entitlement.
2. **Investor Awareness:** It signals the last day to buy shares to receive the upcoming dividend.
3. **Market Efficiency:** It ensures that the dividend right is correctly transferred between the buyer and seller.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a) Discuss in detail the various forms in which dividends can be paid by a company. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A company may distribute profits to its shareholders in one of the following five forms:

**1. Cash Dividend:**
The most common form, where the company pays a specified amount of money per share directly to shareholders. For example, Infosys paying ₹22 per share as interim dividend. Cash dividends reduce the company's cash reserves and create a real outflow.

**2. Stock Dividend (Bonus Shares):**
The company issues additional fully paid-up shares to existing shareholders **free of cost**, in proportion to their existing holdings. For example, a 1:1 bonus issue means one free share for every share held. This capitalizes the reserves, makes the stock more affordable (improves liquidity), and conserves cash.

**3. Property Dividend:**
A rare form where the company distributes non-cash assets (e.g., shares of a subsidiary, inventory, gold bars) to shareholders. Used mainly during demergers or restructuring.

**4. Scrip Dividend:**
The company issues a **promissory note** payable at a future date. Practically obsolete today and disallowed under the Companies Act, 2013.

**5. Liquidating Dividend:**
A distribution that represents a **return of capital** rather than a return on profits. It is paid out of paid-up capital (typically during partial wind-down). It reduces the company's share capital base.

**Conclusion:** Among all five forms, cash and stock dividends dominate corporate practice. The choice depends on liquidity, tax considerations, and signaling intent.

---

**(b) Explain the chronology of dividend payment with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The dividend payment process follows a strict four-stage chronology:

**Stage 1 — Declaration Date:**
The **date on which the Board of Directors** announces the dividend in a formal meeting. The dividend amount per share, record date, and payment date are all decided. **Accounting impact:** The company recognizes a liability — "Dividends Payable" — on the balance sheet.

**Stage 2 — Ex-Dividend Date:**
Set by the **stock exchange**, this date falls **one business day before the Record Date** (under T+1 settlement). Investors who **purchase shares on or after this date will NOT** receive the declared dividend. The share price typically falls by approximately the dividend amount on this day.

**Stage 3 — Record Date (Date of Record):**
On this date, the company **closes its shareholder register** and identifies all shareholders of record. Only those whose names appear in the register on this date are entitled to receive the dividend.

**Stage 4 — Payment Date:**
The actual **cash is disbursed** to eligible shareholders, either through electronic bank transfer (NEFT/RTGS/IMPS) or by issuing physical dividend warrants/cheques. The "Dividends Payable" liability is extinguished.

**Diagram:**

```
Declaration  →  Ex-Dividend  →  Record Date  →  Payment
   (Board)      (Stock Exch.)   (Freeze Reg.)   (Cash Out)
    Day 0         Day T-1          Day T          Day T+15 to 30
```

**Legal Note:** Under **Section 127 of the Companies Act, 2013**, unclaimed dividends must be transferred to the **Investor Education and Protection Fund (IEPF)** after seven years.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a) Critically evaluate the various theories of dividend policy. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The relationship between dividend policy and firm value has been debated extensively. Five major theories exist:

**1. Miller-Modigliani (MM) Irrelevance Theory (1961):**
In a perfect market, dividend policy is irrelevant because the total value of the firm depends only on its operating cash flows. Shareholders indifferent to dividends can create "homemade dividends" by selling shares.

**2. Bird-in-the-Hand Theory (Gordon & Lintner):**
Investors prefer the certainty of current dividends over uncertain future capital gains. Therefore, dividend-paying firms command higher valuations. Mathematically: $V_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g}$.

**3. Tax Preference Theory:**
Because dividend income is taxed at higher rates than capital gains, investors prefer retained earnings (capital appreciation) over dividends. Firms paying higher dividends will have lower valuations ceteris paribus.

**4. Clientele Effect:**
Different investor groups prefer different dividend policies. Firms attract clienteles whose preferences match their declared policy; the policy itself does not affect the overall market value, only the clientele composition.

**5. Signaling Theory:**
Dividends carry credible information about management's confidence in future earnings. A dividend hike is a positive signal; a dividend cut is a negative signal.

**Critical Evaluation:** In **real-world imperfect markets** (with taxes, information asymmetry, and transaction costs), MM's strict irrelevance proposition does not hold. Empirical evidence supports the **Signaling Theory** and the **Clientele Effect** most strongly. The Bird-in-the-Hand and Tax Preference theories explain observed preferences in specific investor classes.

---

**(b) A firm expects earnings per share of ₹10 next year. The cost of equity is 14% and the retention ratio is 40%. Return on equity is 18%. Using the Gordon Growth Model, compute the current price of the share. Also, calculate the dividend yield. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $E_1 = 10$ (EPS next year)
- $K_e = 14\% = 0.14$
- $b = 0.40$ (retention ratio)
- $r = 18\% = 0.18$ (ROE)

**Step 1 — Compute the Payout Ratio**

$$
\text{Payout Ratio} = 1 - b = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Expected Dividend ($D_1$)**

$$
D_1 = E_1 \times \text{Payout Ratio} = 10 \times 0.60 = 6 \text{ rupees}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Growth Rate ($g$)**

$$
g = r \times b = 0.18 \times 0.40 = 0.072 = 7.2\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Boundary Condition**

$$
K_e - g = 0.14 - 0.072 = 0.068 > 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Gordon Growth Model**

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g} = \frac{6}{0.068}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Computation**

$$
P_0 = \frac{6}{0.068} = 88.24 \text{ rupees}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Dividend Yield**

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{D_1}{P_0} = \frac{6}{88.24} = 0.068 = 6.80\%
$$

**Step 8 — Sanity Check (Capital Gains Yield)**

$$
\text{Capital Gains Yield} = K_e - \text{Yield} = 14\% - 6.80\% = 7.20\% \quad \checkmark
$$

The capital gains yield equals the growth rate, confirming internal consistency.

**Final Answer:** **$P_0 = ₹88.24$** and **Dividend Yield = 6.80%**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Chronology Order:** Writing "Record Date → Ex-Dividend Date" instead of the correct "Ex-Dividend → Record." Loses **1 to 2 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Boundary Condition:** Applying the Gordon model without checking $K_e > g$. If violated, the valuation is mathematically meaningless.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Free Cash Flow:** Stating a dividend is safe because Payout Ratio = 30% without checking FCF Coverage. Payout ratio is an accrual metric; FCF is the real cash check.
>
> 4. **Pure Theory Recital:** Writing 14 marks on MM/Gordon theory without applying it to a numerical problem or real-world case. KTU's NEP 2020 framework rewards **application**, not mere definition.
>
> 5. **Mixing Walter and Gordon:** Walter uses $r$ (ROE) and $K_e$ explicitly; Gordon uses $g = br$. Confusing the two formulas costs **3+ marks**.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Section 123/127:** Not citing the Companies Act provisions where legally relevant. NEP 2020 emphasizes **statutory awareness**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Topic: Dividends**

### A. Dividend Basics
- ✓ Dividend = distribution of profit to shareholders (Section 123, Companies Act 2013).
- ✓ Paid only from current or accumulated profits — never from capital.
- ✓ Unclaimed dividends transferred to IEPF after 7 years.

### B. Five Forms of Dividend
- ✓ **Cash** (most common), **Stock/Bonus** (capitalizes reserves), **Property** (rare assets), **Scrip** (obsolete), **Liquidating** (return of capital).

### C. Payment Chronology (Strict Order)
- ✓ Declaration → Ex-Dividend → Record → Payment.
- ✓ Ex-Dividend = T-1 business day before Record Date.

### D. Five Theories of Dividend Policy
- ✓ **MM (Irrelevance)** — perfect markets only.
- ✓ **Bird-in-Hand** — investors value certainty.
- ✓ **Tax Preference** — capital gains favored.
- ✓ **Clientele Effect** — self-selection by investor type.
- ✓ **Signaling** — dividend changes convey insider info.

### E. Five Factor Categories
- ✓ Legal, Contractual, Internal, External, Shareholder Preferences.

### F. Four Payout Policies
- ✓ Stable DPS, Constant Payout Ratio, Residual, Hybrid.

### G. Core Formulas (Memorize)
- ✓ $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g}$ — Gordon Growth Model.
- ✓ $g = r \times b$ — Sustainable Growth Rate (Higgins).
- ✓ $\text{PR} = \dfrac{\text{DPS}}{\text{EPS}}$ — Payout Ratio.
- ✓ $\text{DCR} = \dfrac{\text{EPS}}{\text{DPS}}$ — Dividend Coverage Ratio.
- ✓ $P_0 = \dfrac{D + \dfrac{r}{K_e}(E-D)}{K_e}$ — Walter's Model.

### H. Dividend Safety Decision Rule
- ✓ **Safe if** PR < 50%, DCR > 2x, FCF Coverage > 1.5x, Earnings Stable, Low Leverage.
- ✓ **Unsafe if** PR > 75%, DCR < 1.5x, FCF Coverage < 1x, Earnings Volatile, High Leverage.

### I. Key Boundary Conditions
- ✓ Gordon Model valid only if $K_e > g$.
- ✓ Walter: retain if $r > K_e$; payout if $r < K_e$.
- ✓ Sustainable Growth $\uparrow$ as Retention $\uparrow$ and ROE $\uparrow$.

### J. Exam Day Triggers
- ✓ Numerical questions ALWAYS require formula → variable substitution → boundary check → final answer.
- ✓ Theory questions ALWAYS require **theory + critique + real-world application**.
- ✓ Indian statutory references (Section 123, 127, IEPF) earn **bonus marks**.

---

topics.title: Share Repurchases - Methods, Effects of Repurchases, Valuation Equivalence of Cash Dividends and Share Repurchases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Share Repurchases - Methods, Effects of Repurchases, Valuation Equivalence of Cash Dividends and Share Repurchases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Share Repurchase** (also known as a stock buyback or equity buyback) is a corporate action in which a company uses its excess cash or free cash flows to buy back its own outstanding shares from the open market or from existing shareholders, thereby reducing the total number of shares outstanding in the market. Under the **Companies Act, 2013 (Sections 68–70)** and SEBI (Buyback of Securities) Regulations, 2018, a company can repurchase its own shares either through a tender offer route or through the open market route, subject to a maximum limit of **25%** of paid-up capital and free reserves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> *"Share repurchase is the re-acquisition by a corporation of its own previously issued outstanding shares, financed out of accumulated profits or free reserves, with the objective of returning cash to shareholders, improving Earnings Per Share (EPS), signaling undervaluation, or defending against hostile takeovers."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza Slice Buy-Back"

Imagine you and your friends started a small pizza shop with 100 slices representing 100 shares. The business is now very profitable and has accumulated extra cash of ₹10,000. Instead of distributing this cash as a "pizza-dividend" (i.e., cash dividends), the shop owner decides to **buy back 20 slices from shareholders** at the market price of ₹500 each, paying out ₹10,000.

Now the total number of slices remaining is **80 slices** (shares outstanding is reduced). The remaining 80 shareholders still own 100% of the pizza shop, but **each slice now represents a larger claim** on the company's future profits. This is precisely how a share repurchase works — it concentrates ownership among the remaining shareholders without changing the proportional cash flow rights of those who sell.

## 1.3 Why Do Companies Repurchase Shares?

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Strategic Motives Behind Share Repurchases:**
> 1. **Distribution of excess cash** — An alternative to cash dividends.
> 2. **EPS enhancement** — Reducing share count automatically increases EPS.
> 3. **Signaling undervaluation** — Management's buyback conveys positive private information.
> 4. **Capital structure adjustment** — Increases financial leverage (debt-to-equity ratio).
> 5. **Hostile takeover defense** — Reduces the pool of shares available for hostile acquirers.

## 1.4 Theoretical Foundation

The concept of share repurchases is rooted in the **Dividend Irrelevance Theory** proposed by **Merton Miller and Franco Modigliani (1961)**, which states that in a perfect capital market (no taxes, no transaction costs, no information asymmetry), the method of cash distribution — whether through dividends or share repurchases — **does not affect shareholder wealth** or the firm's total value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Share Repurchase Methods

Share repurchases are classified into **four primary methods** based on the mechanism of execution:

### 2.1.1 Open Market Repurchases (OMR)

In this method, the company repurchases its own shares gradually through the **stock exchange** (e.g., BSE/NSE in India) over an extended period of time, just like any ordinary investor.

**Key Features:**
- **Discretionary** — Company is not legally obligated to complete the full announced amount.
- **No premium** — Bought at prevailing market price.
- **Duration** — Usually 6 to 12 months.
- **Most common method** — Used by approximately **90%** of all repurchasing firms globally.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Board of Directors approves the buyback proposal.
2. SEBI/Stock Exchange approval is obtained.
3. A merchant banker or broker is appointed.
4. Shares are purchased in the secondary market within prescribed limits (max **15%** in any single day under SEBI).
5. Shares are extinguished (canceled) or held as Treasury Stock.

### 2.1.2 Fixed Price Tender Offers

The company announces a **fixed price** at which it is willing to buy back a specified number of shares directly from shareholders. Shareholders submit their tenders at or below the announced price.

**Key Features:**
- **Premium offered** — Usually **20% to 30%** above market price.
- **Conditional** — If oversubscribed, pro-rata allotment is done.
- **Time-bound** — Typically open for 15–30 days.
- **Definite outcome** — The company is committed to buying back the announced quantity.

### 2.1.3 Dutch Auction Repurchases

A hybrid mechanism where the company specifies a **price range** (e.g., ₹500–₶550) within which shareholders can tender their shares. The company then determines the **lowest price** at which the full quantity can be bought, and all shares tendered at or below this price are bought at that price.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Company announces a price range and total quantity.
2. Shareholders submit bids specifying price and quantity.
3. Company constructs a demand schedule (cumulative shares tendered at each price).
4. The lowest clearing price is identified where total demand equals the buyback quantity.
5. All accepted shareholders receive the same clearing price.

### 2.1.4 Privately Negotiated Repurchases

A **block deal** or negotiated transaction with a single large shareholder (typically a promoter or institutional investor), often at a substantial discount or premium.

**Key Features:**
- Used when company wants to **consolidate promoter holding** or remove a hostile shareholder.
- Subject to shareholder approval via special resolution.
- Least common of the four methods.

## 2.2 Effects of Share Repurchases

### 2.2.1 Effect on Earnings Per Share (EPS)

$$\text{New EPS} = \frac{\text{Net Income} - \text{After-tax Interest on New Debt}}{\text{Original Shares} - \text{Repurchased Shares}}$$

### 2.2.2 Effect on Share Price (Theoretical)

If the company's intrinsic value remains unchanged, the share price should **rise** after the buyback because the same value is now divided among fewer shares.

$$P_1 = \frac{V_f}{N - R}$$

Where:
- $P_1$ = New share price after repurchase
- $V_f$ = Total firm value (unchanged in perfect market)
- $N$ = Original number of shares
- $R$ = Number of shares repurchased

### 2.2.3 Effect on Capital Structure

| Aspect | Before Repurchase | After Repurchase |
|---|---|---|
| Equity Capital | ₹100 Cr | ₹80 Cr |
| Debt | ₹50 Cr | ₹50 Cr |
| Debt-to-Equity Ratio | 0.50 | 0.625 |
| Financial Leverage | Lower | Higher |

### 2.2.4 Effect on Cash Flow Statement

- **Financing Activities**: Cash outflow of ₹(P × R)
- **Investing Activities**: No impact (unless treasury stock is later reissued)
- **Operating Activities**: No direct impact

### 2.2.5 Effect on Control and Ownership

Promoters' percentage ownership **automatically increases** since the denominator (total shares) decreases while numerator (shares held by promoters) stays constant.

$$\text{New Promoter \%} = \frac{\text{Promoter Shares}}{\text{Total Shares} - \text{Repurchased Shares}} \times 100$$

### 2.2.6 Tax Effects (Indian Context)

| Method | Tax Treatment for Shareholders |
|---|---|
| Open Market | Capital Gains Tax (STCG 15% / LTCG 10%) |
| Tender Offer | Capital Gains Tax on gains |
| Buyback Tax (India) | **Additional 25% tax** in hands of shareholder (Section 115QA) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** Under Section 115QA of the Income Tax Act, 1961, a **10% tax** is levied on the company distributing buyback proceeds, and shareholders are **exempt** from paying tax on the income received from the buyback. This is a critical Indian-specific provision.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | New EPS after buyback | $EPS_{new} = \dfrac{NI - I(1-t)}{N_o - N_r}$ | $NI$ = Net Income, $I$ = Interest, $t$ = Tax rate, $N_o$ = Original shares, $N_r$ = Repurchased shares |
| 2 | Theoretical new share price | $P_1 = \dfrac{V_f}{N_o - N_r}$ | $V_f$ = Firm value, $N_o$ = Original shares, $N_r$ = Repurchased |
| 3 | Promoter ownership increase | $\Delta\% = \left(\dfrac{P_s}{N_o - N_r} - \dfrac{P_s}{N_o}\right) \times 100$ | $P_s$ = Promoter shares held |
| 4 | Buyback price per share | $P_{bb} = \dfrac{C_{dist}}{N_r}$ | $C_{dist}$ = Total cash distributed |
| 5 | Share price after repurchase (no dividend) | $P_1 = \dfrac{(V_f - C_{bb})(1+g)}{Ke - g}$ | $C_{bb}$ = Buyback cash, $g$ = growth, $Ke$ = cost of equity |
| 6 | M&M Valuation Equivalence | $V_L = V_U + T_c \cdot D$ | $V_L$ = Levered firm value, $V_U$ = Unlevered value, $T_c$ = Corporate tax rate, $D$ = Debt |
| 7 | Cost of Buyback | $\text{Cost} = (P_{buy} - P_{pre}) \times N_r$ | $P_{buy}$ = Buyback price, $P_{pre}$ = Pre-buyback price |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management

In financial engineering, share repurchases are used in:
- **LBO (Leveraged Buyout) Modeling** — Combining debt financing with share repurchase to enhance ROE.
- **Capital Restructuring** in distressed firms.
- **Algorithmic Trading Strategies** — Companies use VWAP (Volume-Weighted Average Price) algorithms to execute OMRs.
- **Event-Study Analysis** — Quantifying abnormal returns around repurchase announcements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effect of Buyback on Share Price (Perfect Market Assumption)

Consider a company with current share price $P_0 = ₹100$, total shares $N = 10,000$, and total firm value $V_f = ₹10,00,000$. The company decides to repurchase $N_r = 2,000$ shares using excess cash of $C_{bb} = ₹2,00,000$.

### Step 1: Compute the Firm Value Before Repurchase

$$V_f = P_0 \times N = 100 \times 10,000 = ₹10,00,000$$

### Step 2: Compute Cash Drained from the Firm

The firm pays $C_{bb} = P_0 \times N_r$ to repurchase shares:

$$C_{bb} = 100 \times 2,000 = ₹2,00,000$$

### Step 3: Compute the New Firm Value

In a perfect market, the value of cash distributed equals the cash paid. So the residual firm value is:

$$V_f^{new} = V_f - C_{bb} = 10,00,000 - 2,00,000 = ₹8,00,000$$

### Step 4: Compute the New Number of Shares

$$N_{new} = N - N_r = 10,000 - 2,000 = 8,000 \text{ shares}$$

### Step 5: Compute the New Theoretical Share Price

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \frac{V_f^{new}}{N_{new}} \\
&= \frac{8,00,000}{8,000} \\
&= ₹100 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** In a perfect market, the share price remains **unchanged** at ₹100. Shareholder wealth is preserved because sellers receive ₹100 per share, which equals the post-buyback price. **Total shareholder wealth is identical** between a dividend payout of ₹2,00,000 and a buyback of ₹2,00,000.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Buyback on EPS

Given:
- Net Income (NI) = ₹5,00,000
- Original shares ($N_o$) = 10,000
- Repurchased shares ($N_r$) = 2,000
- Tax rate ($t$) = 30%
- Interest on debt raised for buyback ($I$) = ₹20,000

### Step 1: Compute Old EPS

$$\begin{aligned}
EPS_{old} &= \frac{NI}{N_o} \\
&= \frac{5,00,000}{10,000} \\
&= ₹50 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Compute New Numerator (Net Income after Interest)

$$\begin{aligned}
NI_{new} &= NI - I(1-t) \\
&= 5,00,000 - 20,000(1 - 0.30) \\
&= 5,00,000 - 14,000 \\
&= ₹4,86,000
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute New Denominator (Shares Outstanding)

$$N_{new} = N_o - N_r = 10,000 - 2,000 = 8,000 \text{ shares}$$

### Step 4: Compute New EPS

$$\begin{aligned}
EPS_{new} &= \frac{4,86,000}{8,000} \\
&= ₹60.75 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Compute Percentage Increase in EPS

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Increase} &= \frac{EPS_{new} - EPS_{old}}{EPS_{old}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{60.75 - 50}{50} \times 100 \\
&= 21.5\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Even though net income drops from ₹5,00,000 to ₹4,86,000 due to after-tax interest cost of ₹14,000, the EPS rises by **21.5%** because the share count is reduced by 20%. This is the **EPS leverage effect** of buybacks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: M&M Valuation Equivalence Proof (Cash Dividend vs. Share Repurchase)

We will prove that under perfect market conditions, paying a cash dividend of $D$ yields the **same shareholder wealth** as repurchasing shares worth $D$.

### Assumptions (M&M World):
- No taxes
- No transaction costs
- No information asymmetry
- Rational investors
- Constant investment policy

### Setup:
- A firm has $N = 1,000$ shares outstanding.
- Current share price $P_0 = ₹100$.
- Firm value $V_f = ₹1,00,000$.
- The firm earns ₹10,000 in perpetuity (zero growth for simplicity).
- Cost of equity $K_e = 10\%$.
- Excess cash $C = ₹20,000$ to be distributed.

---

### **Scenario A: Cash Dividend Distribution**

#### Step 1: Compute Firm Value Before Dividend

$$V_f = \frac{NI}{K_e} = \frac{10,000}{0.10} = ₹1,00,000$$

#### Step 2: Compute Total Dividend Per Share

$$D = \frac{C}{N} = \frac{20,000}{1,000} = ₹20 \text{ per share}$$

#### Step 3: Compute Share Price After Dividend (Ex-Dividend Price)

In a perfect market, the share price falls by exactly the dividend amount:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{ex} &= P_0 - D \\
&= 100 - 20 \\
&= ₹80 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4: Compute Total Shareholder Wealth (per shareholder holding 1 share)

$$\text{Wealth}_A = P_{ex} + D = 80 + 20 = ₹100$$

**Conclusion for Scenario A:** Total wealth per share = ₹100 (unchanged).

---

### **Scenario B: Share Repurchase**

#### Step 1: Compute Number of Shares Repurchased

$$N_r = \frac{C}{P_0} = \frac{20,000}{100} = 200 \text{ shares}$$

#### Step 2: Compute New Number of Outstanding Shares

$$N_{new} = N - N_r = 1,000 - 200 = 800 \text{ shares}$$

#### Step 3: Compute New Firm Value (Cash Paid Reduces Firm Value)

$$V_f^{new} = V_f - C = 1,00,000 - 20,000 = ₹80,000$$

#### Step 4: Compute New Share Price After Repurchase

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \frac{V_f^{new}}{N_{new}} \\
&= \frac{80,000}{800} \\
&= ₹100 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 5: Compute Total Shareholder Wealth (per shareholder holding 1 share)

- A shareholder who **does not sell**: Still owns 1 share worth ₹100 → Wealth = ₹100.
- A shareholder who **sells 1 share** at ₹100: Receives ₹100 cash → Wealth = ₹100.

**Conclusion for Scenario B:** Total wealth per share = ₹100 (unchanged).

---

### Final Equivalence Statement

$$\boxed{\text{Wealth}_A = \text{Wealth}_B = ₹100}$$

Therefore, **cash dividends and share repurchases are valuation-equivalent** in perfect capital markets. The **mode of cash distribution does not create or destroy value** — it only changes the form in which shareholders hold their wealth (cash vs. equity).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Dividend vs. Repurchase Comparison

| Parameter | Cash Dividend | Share Repurchase |
|---|---|---|
| Decision to participate | Mandatory (pro-rata) | Voluntary (opt-in) |
| Tax treatment (India) | Taxed in hands of shareholder | 10% buyback tax on company; exempt for shareholder |
| Flexibility | Low (committed) | High (discretionary) |
| Signaling effect | Weak (recurring) | Strong (positive) |
| Effect on EPS | No effect | Increases EPS |
| Effect on share count | No effect | Reduces |
| Cost to company | Equal to $D \times N$ | Equal to $P_0 \times N_r$ |
| Effect on cash flow | Financing outflow | Financing outflow |
| Regulatory limit (India) | No specific limit | 25% of paid-up capital + free reserves |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define share repurchase. List any four methods of share repurchase.

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Share repurchase, also known as stock buyback, is a corporate action in which a firm uses its surplus cash to buy back its own outstanding shares from the open market or from existing shareholders, thereby reducing the total number of shares outstanding.

**Four Methods (2 Marks):**

1. **Open Market Repurchase (OMR)** — Shares are purchased gradually through the stock exchange.
2. **Fixed Price Tender Offer** — Company offers a fixed premium price to buy back shares directly.
3. **Dutch Auction** — Company announces a price range and determines the lowest clearing price.
4. **Privately Negotiated Repurchase** — A direct block deal with a single large shareholder.

---

### **Question 2:** Explain the signaling effect of share repurchases.

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **signaling effect** of share repurchases refers to the communication of **insider information** from the management to the market through the act of buying back shares. When management uses company funds to repurchase shares, it signals to investors that:

1. Management believes the shares are **undervalued** at the current market price.
2. The company has **strong future cash flows** and limited high-return investment opportunities.
3. Management is **confident** about the firm's financial stability.

**Empirical Evidence:** Studies by Asquith and Mullins (1986) and Vermaelen (1981) document **abnormal returns of 3% to 5%** around the announcement of share repurchase programs, confirming the positive signaling effect.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various methods of share repurchase. Highlight the distinguishing features of each method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company has 10,00,000 shares outstanding at a market price of ₹120 per share. The company earns a Net Income of ₹60,00,000 and has a tax rate of 30%. The company proposes to repurchase 1,00,000 shares by issuing debt at an interest rate of 8% per annum. Calculate:
   (i) The Old EPS and New EPS.
   (ii) The percentage change in EPS.
   (iii) Comment on whether the buyback is beneficial to shareholders. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A (a)**

Various methods of share repurchase are as follows:

**1. Open Market Repurchase (OMR):**
This is the most common method where the company buys back its own shares through the stock exchange over an extended period, typically 6–12 months. The company appoints a merchant banker or broker to execute the buyback.
- **Features:** Discretionary, no premium, gradual execution, max 15% of daily traded volume per day under SEBI norms.
- **Advantages:** Flexibility, no commitment, minimal price impact.
- **Disadvantages:** Risk of price manipulation, gradual process.

**2. Fixed Price Tender Offer:**
The company announces a single fixed price (typically 20–30% above market price) at which it is willing to repurchase a specified number of shares.
- **Features:** Definite commitment, conditional, pro-rata if oversubscribed.
- **Advantages:** Certainty, faster execution.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher cost due to premium, shareholder approval required.

**3. Dutch Auction:**
The company specifies a price range. Shareholders tender shares at various prices. The lowest price that allows the company to buy the full quantity is the clearing price, paid to all successful tenderers.
- **Features:** Market-driven pricing, uniform payout.
- **Advantages:** Lower cost than tender offer, fair pricing.
- **Disadvantages:** Complexity, requires more administrative effort.

**4. Privately Negotiated Repurchase:**
A direct, off-market transaction with a single shareholder, usually a promoter or institutional investor.
- **Features:** Block deal, special resolution required.
- **Advantages:** Quick, suitable for promoter consolidation or hostile shareholder exit.
- **Disadvantages:** Potential for related-party transaction issues.

---

### **Model Answer — Question A (b)**

**Given:**
- Original shares ($N_o$) = 10,00,000
- Market price ($P_0$) = ₹120
- Net Income (NI) = ₹60,00,000
- Tax rate ($t$) = 30%
- Repurchased shares ($N_r$) = 1,00,000
- Interest rate = 8%

**Step 1: Compute Interest Expense**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Buyback Amount} &= P_0 \times N_r = 120 \times 1,00,000 = ₹1,20,00,000 \\
\text{Interest} &= ₹1,20,00,000 \times 0.08 = ₹9,60,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute Old EPS**

$$\begin{aligned}
EPS_{old} &= \frac{NI}{N_o} = \frac{60,00,000}{10,00,000} = ₹6.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute After-Tax Interest**

$$\begin{aligned}
I(1-t) &= 9,60,000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 9,60,000 \times 0.70 = ₹6,72,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute New Net Income**

$$NI_{new} = 60,00,000 - 6,72,000 = ₹53,28,000$$

**Step 5: Compute New Number of Shares**

$$N_{new} = 10,00,000 - 1,00,000 = 9,00,000$$

**Step 6: Compute New EPS**

$$EPS_{new} = \frac{53,28,000}{9,00,000} = ₹5.92$$

**Step 7: Compute Percentage Change**

$$\% \text{ Change} = \frac{5.92 - 6.00}{6.00} \times 100 = -1.33\%$$

**Comment (1 Mark):** The buyback is **not beneficial** in this case. Although 10% of shares were repurchased, the after-tax interest cost (₹6,72,000) outweighed the EPS benefit from share reduction. The EPS actually **declined** from ₹6.00 to ₹5.92. This is a classic case where leverage is detrimental.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to subtract the **after-tax interest** in the numerator and only divide by the reduced share count. This gives an inflated EPS of ₹6.67 (incorrect). Always compute $I(1-t)$ correctly. This is the **most common error** observed in valuation scripts.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Modigliani-Miller (M&M) theory of dividend irrelevance. Using numerical illustration, prove that cash dividends and share repurchases are valuation-equivalent in a perfect capital market. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** XYZ Ltd. has 5,00,000 shares outstanding, trading at ₹200 per share. The firm's total value is ₹10,00,00,000. The firm has excess cash of ₹1,00,00,000 that it wishes to distribute. The firm's cost of equity is 12% and earnings are perpetual at ₹1,20,00,000.

   Compare the shareholder wealth in the following scenarios:
   (i) If the firm pays the entire excess cash as a dividend.
   (ii) If the firm uses the entire excess cash to repurchase shares at ₹200 per share.
   (iii) Comment on the equivalence. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B (a)**

**Modigliani-Miller (M&M) Dividend Irrelevance Theory (3 Marks):**

Proposed by Merton Miller and Franco Modigliani in 1961, the M&M dividend irrelevance theorem states that in a **perfect capital market** (characterized by no taxes, no transaction costs, no information asymmetry, and rational investors), the **dividend policy of a firm is irrelevant** to its market value and shareholder wealth.

The core argument is that any cash distribution through dividends can be replicated by selling a proportional amount of shares in the open market, and vice versa. Since investors can create their own "homemade dividends," the firm's decision to pay or not pay dividends does not affect the **total wealth** of shareholders — only the form in which wealth is held (cash vs. equity) changes.

**Numerical Illustration (4 Marks):**

Consider a firm with:
- 1,000 shares, $P_0 = ₹100$, total value $V_f = ₹1,00,000$
- Excess cash $C = ₹10,000$ to distribute

**Scenario 1: Cash Dividend**
- Dividend per share $D = 10,000 / 1,000 = ₹10$
- Ex-dividend price $P_{ex} = 100 - 10 = ₹90$
- Total wealth per share = $P_{ex} + D = 90 + 10 = ₹100$ ✓

**Scenario 2: Share Repurchase**
- Shares repurchased = $10,000 / 100 = 100$ shares
- Remaining shares = 1,000 − 100 = 900
- New firm value = 1,00,000 − 10,000 = ₹90,000
- New share price = $90,000 / 900 = ₹100$ ✓

**Wealth Equivalence Proven:** Both scenarios yield total shareholder wealth of ₹100 per share, confirming that cash dividends and share repurchases are valuation-equivalent under M&M assumptions.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B (b)**

**Given:**
- Shares outstanding $N = 5,00,000$
- Share price $P_0 = ₹200$
- Total firm value $V_f = ₹10,00,00,000$
- Excess cash $C = ₹1,00,00,000$
- Cost of equity $K_e = 12\%$
- Perpetual earnings $NI = ₹1,20,00,000$

**Scenario 1: Cash Dividend Distribution**

**Step 1:** Compute dividend per share:

$$D = \frac{C}{N} = \frac{1,00,00,000}{5,00,000} = ₹20 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 2:** Compute ex-dividend price:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{ex} &= P_0 - D \\
&= 200 - 20 \\
&= ₹180
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute total wealth per share for an investor holding 1 share:

$$\text{Wealth}_1 = P_{ex} + D = 180 + 20 = ₹200$$

**Scenario 2: Share Repurchase**

**Step 1:** Compute number of shares repurchased:

$$N_r = \frac{C}{P_0} = \frac{1,00,00,000}{200} = 50,000 \text{ shares}$$

**Step 2:** Compute remaining shares outstanding:

$$N_{new} = 5,00,000 - 50,000 = 4,50,000 \text{ shares}$$

**Step 3:** Compute new firm value:

$$V_f^{new} = 10,00,00,000 - 1,00,00,000 = ₹9,00,00,000$$

**Step 4:** Compute new share price:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \frac{V_f^{new}}{N_{new}} \\
&= \frac{9,00,00,000}{4,50,000} \\
&= ₹200
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Compute total wealth per share:

- Non-participating shareholder: Still owns 1 share worth ₹200 → Wealth = ₹200
- Participating shareholder: Sells 1 share at ₹200 → Wealth = ₹200

**Scenario 3: Comment on Equivalence (2 Marks)**

$$\text{Wealth}_1 = \text{Wealth}_2 = ₹200$$

The numerical analysis confirms that **shareholder wealth is identical** under both distribution methods when the firm operates in a **perfect capital market**. The choice between cash dividend and share repurchase is irrelevant from a wealth-maximization perspective. The **method of distribution does not affect the total value** distributed to shareholders — it only changes whether the shareholder receives the value in cash (via dividend or repurchase participation) or in retained equity (by holding the share).

In real-world imperfect markets, however, differences may emerge due to:
- Differential tax treatment (capital gains vs. dividend income).
- Information signaling effects (buybacks signal undervaluation).
- Transaction costs.
- Clientele effects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting that the share price **falls** in the dividend scenario (to ₹180) and **remains at ₹200** in the buyback scenario. Students often write that the share price "increases" in the dividend case, which is **wrong**. The total wealth (cash + share value) is what matters, not the share price alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- ✅ **Share repurchase** = Buyback of own shares to reduce outstanding share count.
- ✅ **Four methods**: Open Market, Fixed Tender, Dutch Auction, Private Negotiation.
- ✅ **Most common method globally**: Open Market Repurchase (~90%).
- ✅ **SEBI limit (India)**: 25% of paid-up capital + free reserves.
- ✅ **Maximum daily purchase under OMR (SEBI)**: 15% of total traded volume.
- ✅ **EPS effect formula**: $EPS_{new} = \dfrac{NI - I(1-t)}{N_o - N_r}$.
- ✅ **Buyback tax in India**: 10% on company distributing buyback proceeds (Section 115QA).
- ✅ **M&M Theorem**: Dividend policy is irrelevant in perfect markets.
- ✅ **Valuation equivalence**: Cash dividend = Share repurchase in perfect markets.
- ✅ **Signaling effect**: Buyback → positive signal → abnormal returns of 3–5%.
- ✅ **Effect on ownership**: Promoters' stake automatically increases.
- ✅ **Effect on leverage**: Debt-to-equity ratio increases.
- ✅ **Cash flow effect**: Outflow under financing activities.
- ✅ **Promoter shareholding formula**: New % = Promoter Shares / Reduced Outstanding Shares.
- ✅ **Real-world deviations from M&M**: Taxes, transaction costs, asymmetric information.
- ✅ **Capital structure adjustment**: Buyback is a tool to alter D/E ratio without new equity issuance.
- ✅ **Dutch auction price**: Lowest price at which full quantity is bid for.
- ✅ **Ex-dividend price = Market Price − Dividend**.
- ✅ **Post-buyback price formula**: $P_1 = \dfrac{V_f - C_{bb}}{N_o - N_r}$.
- ✅ **Tender offer premium**: Typically 20–30% above market price.
- ✅ **Wealth equivalence equation**: $P_0 = P_{ex} + D = P_{post-buyback}$.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Working Capital Management — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Capital Management (WCM)** is the strategic and operational process of planning, organizing, directing, and controlling the firm's **current assets** and **current liabilities** to ensure the business has sufficient liquidity to meet its short-term obligations while minimizing the cost of capital employed.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Working Capital is classified into:

$$WC = \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}$$

| Term | Definition | KTU Mnemonic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gross Working Capital** | Total of all current assets (Cash, AR, Inventory, Marketable Securities) | "Total Current" |
| **Net Working Capital (NWC)** | Current Assets minus Current Liabilities | "Net Health Check" |
| **Permanent Working Capital** | Minimum level of CA always required to fund operations | "Fixed Floor" |
| **Temporary Working Capital** | Extra CA needed during seasonal/sudden demand spikes | "Seasonal Boost" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Every sub-topic under this module maps to the broader objective of **balancing LIQUIDITY (solvency) vs. PROFITABILITY (efficiency)**. This is the central trade-off in WCM.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Household Water Tank"

Imagine your home's water system:
- **Cash & Marketable Securities** = Water in the overhead tank (immediate supply).
- **Accounts Receivable** = Money owed by friends (recoverable in days).
- **Inventory** = Groceries in the fridge (need cash to refill).
- **Accounts Payable** = Pending electricity/water bills (you owe).
- **Short-Term Loans** = Credit card debt (expensive short-term funding).

If the overhead tank is **too full** (excess cash), you are *liquid* but earning **zero interest** — you are being inefficient. If it is **too empty** (cash crunch), you cannot pay the plumber on time — you face a **liquidity crisis**. **Working Capital Management is the art of keeping the tank at an optimal level.**

---

## 1.3 Managing and Measuring Liquidity

**Liquidity** is the firm's ability to convert assets into cash quickly without significant loss in value, and to meet obligations as they come due.

### Key Liquidity Ratios (KTU High-Yield Metrics)

| Ratio | Formula | Standard Benchmark | What It Measures |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Current Ratio** | $\dfrac{CA}{CL}$ | **2 : 1** | Short-term solvency cushion |
| **Quick (Acid-Test) Ratio** | $\dfrac{CA - Inventory}{CL}$ | **1 : 1** | Strict liquid position |
| **Cash Ratio** | $\dfrac{Cash + Marketable\ Sec.}{CL}$ | **0.2 : 1** | Most conservative liquidity |
| **Net Working Capital** | $CA - CL$ | Positive | Absolute liquidity buffer |
| **Defensive Interval** | $\dfrac{CA}{Daily\ Operating\ Costs}$ | 90–180 days | How long firm survives without sales |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "2:1" for Current Ratio?** Empirically, a 2:1 ratio implies that for every ₹1 of short-term debt, the firm has ₹2 of short-term assets — a comfortable cushion. However, this is **industry-dependent**; a supermarket with rapid inventory turnover may operate safely at 1.2:1.

---

## 1.4 The Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) — The Heart of WCM

$$CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO$$

Where:
- **DIO** = Days Inventory Outstanding
- **DSO** = Days Sales Outstanding
- **DPO** = Days Payable Outstanding

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Concept:** The CCC measures the *time gap* between cash outflow (paying suppliers) and cash inflow (collecting from customers). **A shorter CCC = Better liquidity management.**

Conceptual Analogy: A CCC is like a **swimming pool with two pumps**:
- **Inflow Pump** (DSO): Speed of collecting money from customers.
- **Outflow Pump** (DPO + DIO): Speed of paying suppliers and selling stock.
The smaller the pool (CCC), the less water (cash) you need to keep ready.
