# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the weights sum to 1:** When a problem says "₹6,000 in A and ₹4,000 in B", many students forget that $w_A = 0.6$ and $w_B = 0.4$. Always *convert to proportions* before computing.
> 2. **Confusing CML with SML:** A common blunder. CML uses $\sigma$ on X-axis (valid only for efficient portfolios), SML uses $\beta$ on X-axis (valid for *any* asset). Examiners deduct 2 marks for this mix-up.
> 3. **Sharpe vs. Treynor in denominator:** Sharpe uses $\sigma$ (total risk); Treynor uses $\beta$ (systematic risk). Mixing them up costs full credit.
> 4. **Units of return and risk:** Always express returns in **percentage (%)** and risk in **percentage points**. Do not mix decimals and percentages.
> 5. **Forgetting $R_f$:** If a question says "excess return", it means $E[R_p] - R_f$, not just $E[R_p]$.

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Portfolio" in the context of corporate finance. List the three primary objectives of portfolio management.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Portfolio** is a collection of financial assets — such as equities, bonds, commodities, and money market instruments — held by an investor, designed to achieve a specific balance between **risk** and **expected return** through diversification.

The three primary objectives of portfolio management are:
1. **Capital Appreciation** — Maximizing the growth in the value of investments over time.
2. **Income Generation** — Earning regular returns through dividends, interest, and coupons.
3. **Capital Preservation / Risk Minimization** — Protecting the principal from erosion due to market volatility.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three objectives)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define Beta. Explain the meaning of $\beta = 1.5$, $\beta = 0.5$, and $\beta = -0.8$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Beta ($\beta$)** is a statistical measure of an asset's **systematic risk** relative to the overall market. It is mathematically defined as:

$$\beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\beta = 1.5$: The asset is **15% more volatile** than the market. If the Nifty 50 rises 10%, the asset is expected to rise **15%**, and fall 15% if the market falls 10%. Classified as an **aggressive** stock.
- $\beta = 0.5$: The asset is **half as volatile** as the market. It moves 5% for every 10% market move. Classified as a **defensive** stock (e.g., FMCG, utility stocks).
- $\beta = -0.8$: The asset moves in the **opposite direction** to the market. When Nifty rises 10%, this asset falls 8%. It acts as a **hedge** (e.g., gold, put options, inverse ETFs).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for the three interpretations)*

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Part B questions carry **internal choice**. The student must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Both options are presented below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Two stocks, P and Q, have the following return data:**

| State | Probability | Return on P (%) | Return on Q (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bull | 0.25 | 25 | 10 |
| Normal | 0.50 | 15 | 12 |
| Bear | 0.25 | 5 | 14 |

**(a)** Calculate the expected return and standard deviation of stock P and stock Q. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If an investor allocates 60% to P and 40% to Q, calculate the expected return, variance, and standard deviation of the portfolio. Comment on whether the portfolio is efficient. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Expected Returns and Standard Deviations

**Expected Return of P:**

$$E[R_P] = (0.25 \cdot 25) + (0.50 \cdot 15) + (0.25 \cdot 5)$$
$$E[R_P] = 6.25 + 7.50 + 1.25 = 15.00\%$$

**Expected Return of Q:**

$$E[R_Q] = (0.25 \cdot 10) + (0.50 \cdot 12) + (0.25 \cdot 14)$$
$$E[R_Q] = 2.50 + 6.00 + 3.50 = 12.00\%$$

**Variance of P:**

$$\sigma_P^2 = 0.25(25-15)^2 + 0.50(15-15)^2 + 0.25(5-15)^2$$
$$\sigma_P^2 = 0.25(100) + 0.50(0) + 0.25(100) = 50.00$$
$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{50.00} \approx 7.07\%$$

**Variance of Q:**

$$\sigma_Q^2 = 0.25(10-12)^2 + 0.50(12-12)^2 + 0.25(14-12)^2$$
$$\sigma_Q^2 = 0.25(4) + 0.50(0) + 0.25(4) = 2.00$$
$$\sigma_Q = \sqrt{2.00} \approx 1.41\%$$

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 for $E[R_P]$, 1.5 for $E[R_Q]$, 2 for $\sigma_P$, 2 for $\sigma_Q$)*

#### Part (b) — Portfolio Metrics and Efficiency Comment

**Portfolio Expected Return:**

$$E[R_p] = (0.60 \cdot 15.00) + (0.40 \cdot 12.00) = 9.00 + 4.80 = 13.80\%$$

**Covariance between P and Q:**

$$Cov(P,Q) = 0.25(25-15)(10-12) + 0.50(15-15)(12-12) + 0.25(5-15)(14-12)$$
$$Cov(P,Q) = 0.25(10)(-2) + 0.50(0)(0) + 0.25(-10)(2)$$
$$Cov(P,Q) = -5.00 + 0 + (-5.00) = -10.00$$

**Correlation Coefficient:**

$$\rho_{P,Q} = \frac{-10.00}{7.07 \cdot 1.41} = \frac{-10.00}{9.97} \approx -1.00$$

(The negative correlation of nearly $-1$ indicates P and Q are **near-perfect hedges** — an ideal diversification candidate.)

**Portfolio Variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.60)^2(50) + (0.40)^2(2) + 2(0.60)(0.40)(-10.00)$$
$$\sigma_p^2 = 18.00 + 0.32 + (-4.80) = 13.52$$

**Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{13.52} \approx 3.68\%$$

**Efficiency Comment:**

The portfolio offers a return of **13.80%** at a risk of **3.68%**, which is *higher return and lower risk* than holding Q alone (12.00% return at 1.41% risk, but P alone offers 15% at 7.07%). While the portfolio has a *lower return* than P alone (13.80% vs 15.00%), it has a **dramatically lower risk** (3.68% vs 7.07%). The Sharpe-equivalent trade-off is favorable.

> For a **truly efficient** portfolio, the investor would need to compare this combination against the **full efficient frontier** constructed by varying the weights. The current (60/40) combination is a **low-risk defensive** portfolio due to the near-perfect negative correlation, making it an excellent **capital preservation** strategy.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 for $E[R_p]$, 2 for covariance, 1 for correlation, 1.5 for $\sigma_p$, 1 for the efficiency comment)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Define the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). Derive the Security Market Line (SML) equation and explain the meaning of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A portfolio has an expected return of 18%, a beta of 1.2, and a standard deviation of 22%. The risk-free rate is 6%, and the market return is 14%. Calculate the **Sharpe Ratio**, **Treynor Ratio**, and **Jensen's Alpha** of the portfolio. Comment on the manager's performance. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — CAPM Definition and SML Derivation

**Definition:**

The **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**, developed by William Sharpe (1964), John Lintner, and Jan Mossin, is a single-period equilibrium model that describes the relationship between the **systematic risk** of a security and its **expected return** in a competitive capital market.

**The CAPM Equation:**

The expected return on any asset $i$ is the risk-free rate plus a premium for bearing systematic risk:

$$E[R_i] = R_f + \beta_i \cdot (E[R_M] - R_f)$$

Where:
- $E[R_i]$ = Expected return on asset $i$
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate (e.g., 10-year Indian Government Security yield)
- $\beta_i$ = Beta of asset $i$ (systematic risk)
- $E[R_M]$ = Expected return on the market portfolio
- $E[R_M] - R_f$ = **Market Risk Premium (MRP)**

**The Security Market Line (SML):**

Plotting the CAPM equation in the $(E[R], \beta)$ space produces the **SML**, a straight line with intercept $R_f$ and slope $(E[R_M] - R_f)$.

**Interpretation of SML:**
- Assets **on** the SML: **Fairly priced** by the market.
- Assets **above** the SML: **Underpriced** (offer higher return for given risk) → **Buy**.
- Assets **below** the SML: **Overpriced** (offer lower return for given risk) → **Sell**.

The SML holds for **all** assets and portfolios, efficient or not.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for definition, 2 for the equation, 1.5 for component explanation, 1.5 for the SML interpretation)*

#### Part (b) — Performance Metric Calculation

**Given:**
- $E[R_p] = 18\%$, $\beta_p = 1.2$, $\sigma_p = 22\%$, $R_f = 6\%$, $E[R_M] = 14\%$

**Step 1 — Sharpe Ratio:**

$$S_p = \frac{E[R_p] - R_f}{\sigma_p} = \frac{18 - 6}{22} = \frac{12}{22} \approx 0.5455$$

**Step 2 — Treynor Ratio:**

$$T_p = \frac{E[R_p] - R_f}{\beta_p} = \frac{18 - 6}{1.2} = \frac{12}{1.2} = 10.00$$

**Step 3 — Jensen's Alpha:**

$$\alpha_J = E[R_p] - [R_f + \beta_p \cdot (E[R_M] - R_f)]$$
$$\alpha_J = 18 - [6 + 1.2 \cdot (14 - 6)]$$
$$\alpha_J = 18 - [6 + 1.2 \cdot 8]$$
$$\alpha_J = 18 - [6 + 9.6]$$
$$\alpha_J = 18 - 15.6 = +2.4\%$$

**Performance Comment:**

- The **positive Jensen's Alpha (+2.4%)** indicates the manager has **outperformed** the CAPM-predicted return by 2.4 percentage points. This is **strong evidence of stock-picking skill**.
- The **Sharpe Ratio of 0.5455** is decent — the portfolio generates 0.55% excess return per 1% of total risk.
- The **Treynor Ratio of 10.00** is excellent — the portfolio generates 10% excess return per unit of systematic (market) risk.
- **Conclusion:** The manager has **consistently added value** above the passive market benchmark, justifying active management fees.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 for Sharpe, 1.5 for Treynor, 2 for Jensen's Alpha, 2 for the interpretation comment)*

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam**

**1. Core Definitions:**
- **Portfolio** = collection of financial assets held to optimize the risk-return trade-off.
- **Expected Return** = probability-weighted mean return.
- **Risk** = standard deviation of returns (or variance).
- **Covariance** = direction of co-movement; **Correlation** = standardized covariance in $[-1, +1]$.
- **Beta** = systematic risk relative to the market; $\beta = Cov/\sigma_M^2$.

**2. Asset Pricing Models:**
- **CAPM:** $E[R] = R_f + \beta (E[R_M] - R_f)$ — single factor.
- **APT:** $E[R] = R_f + \sum \beta_k \cdot RP_k$ — multi-factor.
- **SML** uses $\beta$ on X-axis (any asset).
- **CML** uses $\sigma$ on X-axis (efficient portfolios only).

**3. Performance Ratios — Know the Denominator:**
- **Sharpe** → Total risk ($\sigma$) in denominator.
- **Treynor** → Systematic risk ($\beta$) in denominator.
- **Jensen's Alpha** → No denominator; measures abnormal return intercept.

**4. The Diversification Theorem:**
- Diversification reduces **unsystematic** risk to **zero** as $N \to \infty$.
- Diversification **cannot** eliminate systematic (market) risk.

**5. Key Formulas to Memorize Verbatim:**
- $E[R_p] = \sum w_i E[R_i]$
- $\sigma_p^2 = \sum w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2 \sum_{i<j} w_i w_j \rho_{ij} \sigma_i \sigma_j$
- $\beta_p = \sum w_i \beta_i$

**6. Examiner's Hot Buttons:**
- Always state $w_1 + w_2 = 1$ when using weights.
- Always mention "**excess return**" when discussing Sharpe/Treynor.
- Always interpret the *sign* of Jensen's Alpha (positive = outperformance).
- Always mention "**on, above, or below the SML**" when commenting on asset pricing.
- Always show units: returns in **%**, beta and correlation as **dimensionless numbers**.

**7. KTU 2024 Scheme Format Reminder:**
- Part A: 3-mark definition or short computation (answer in 1-2 paragraphs).
- Part B: 14-mark problem with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.
- Module 3 specifically emphasizes **portfolio construction and evaluation**, so expect 14-mark questions on Sharpe/Treynor/Jensen's Alpha or CAPM-based calculations.

# Corporate Governance Considerations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Corporate Governance (CG)** is the system of rules, practices, processes, and institutions by which a company is directed and controlled. It encompasses the mechanisms through which a corporation's objectives are established, monitored, and achieved, while balancing the interests of all stakeholders.

According to the **Cadbury Report (1992)**, corporate governance is *"the system by which companies are directed and controlled."* The **OECD Principles of Corporate Governance (2015)** define it as *"a set of relationships between a company's management, its board, its shareholders, and other stakeholders."*

### 1.2 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Corporate Governance (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Accountability** – Of the board to shareholders and stakeholders
> 2. **Transparency** – In disclosure of financial and non-financial information
> 3. **Fairness** – In treatment of all stakeholders, including minority shareholders

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – The Family Joint-Hindu System

Imagine a large joint family business in Kerala with multiple branches, where the **Karta (Managing Director)** runs operations, the **senior members (Board of Directors)** take strategic decisions, and the **younger generation (Shareholders)** has a stake in the family wealth. To prevent disputes over property, succession, and management, the family writes a **"family constitution"** (Articles of Association, Board Charter) that defines:
- Who takes what decision (Roles & Responsibilities)
- How disagreements are resolved (Dispute Resolution Mechanisms)
- How wealth is distributed and audited (Disclosure & Audit)
- What happens if a member misbehaves (Compliance & Penalties)

**Corporate Governance is that "family constitution" for a publicly listed company.** It protects the **Principal (Shareholders)** from opportunistic behavior of the **Agent (Management)**, and ensures the long-term survival of the business.

### 1.4 Why It Matters in Corporate Finance

| Dimension | Impact on Corporate Finance |
|---|---|
| **Cost of Capital** | Strong CG lowers the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** by 50–100 basis points |
| **Firm Valuation** | Better-rated CG firms command a **Tobin's Q premium** of 0.2–0.5 |
| **Capital Access** | Strong governance unlocks **FII, DII, and ESG fund flows** |
| **Risk Premium** | Weak CG adds a **country risk premium** of 2–5% to discount rates |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall:** CG is the *invisible balance sheet* of a company. While financial statements show the *what*, governance shows the *why* and *how* the numbers were produced.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Corporate Governance

### A. Agency Theory (Jensen & Meckling, 1976)

> [!NOTE]
> **Agency Theory** is the dominant lens for understanding CG. It views the corporation as a *"nexus of contracts"* between Principals (shareholders) and Agents (managers).

**Core Assumptions:**
- **Rational Self-Interest** – Agents maximize their own utility, not necessarily shareholder wealth
- **Information Asymmetry** – Managers know more than owners about true firm value
- **Moral Hazard** – Managers may shirk or pursue empire-building
- **Adverse Selection** – Hidden characteristics of agents (e.g., competence)

**The Agency Cost Equation** (Jensen & Meckling, 1976):

$$
AC_{total} = MC + BC + RC
$$

Where:
- $AC_{total}$ = Total Agency Costs
- $MC$ = Monitoring Costs (audit, board oversight, reporting)
- $BC$ = Bonding Costs (compensation contracts, non-compete clauses)
- $RC$ = Residual Loss (the leftover welfare loss despite controls)

**Why It Matters:** Every governance mechanism exists to **reduce one of these three costs**.

### B. Stakeholder Theory (Freeman, 1984)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stakeholder Theory** broadens the scope of CG beyond shareholders to include **all parties affected by corporate decisions**: employees, creditors, customers, suppliers, communities, and the environment.

**Primary vs. Secondary Stakeholders (Mitchell, Agle & Wood, 1997):**

| Attribute | Primary Stakeholders | Secondary Stakeholders |
|---|---|---|
| **Examples** | Shareholders, Employees, Customers | NGOs, Media, Community |
| **Contractual Link** | Direct (formal) | Indirect (informal) |
| **Influence on Firm** | High | Medium-Low |
| **Firm's Dependence** | Critical | Peripheral |

### C. Stewardship Theory (Donaldson & Davis, 1991)

A **counter-theory to Agency Theory**. Stewards are intrinsically motivated to act in the organization's best interest. Implications:
- Less need for monitoring
- Empowerment-based governance
- Trust-based board relationships

### D. Resource Dependency Theory (Pfeffer & Salancik, 1978)

Boards exist not only to monitor but also to **provide critical resources** (expertise, contacts, legitimacy, capital). This justifies having directors from diverse industries.

## 2.2 Models of Corporate Governance Worldwide

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table** – This is a frequently asked 14-mark question.

| Feature | **Anglo-Saxon Model** (USA, UK) | **German Model** (Germany) | **Japanese Model** (Japan) | **Indian Model** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Board Type** | Single (Unitary) | Two-Tier (Vorstand + Aufsichtsrat) | Single (with Keiretsu links) | Single (with regulatory overlay) |
| **Ownership** | Dispersed | Concentrated, banks | Cross-shareholding (Keiretsu) | Concentrated, promoter-driven |
| **Main Bank Role** | Limited | High (Hausbank) | Main Bank system | Lender-of-last-resort |
| **Shareholder Power** | High (Proxy fights, activism) | Low (codetermination) | Low (stable holders) | Promoter-dominant |
| **Key Law** | Sarbanes-Oxley (2002) | Aktiengesetz (1937) | Companies Act (Japan) | Companies Act 2013, SEBI LODR |
| **Worker Voice** | Weak | Strong (50% Supervisory Board) | Lifetime employment norm | Limited |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for solving numerical and analytical questions on CG in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Engineering / Finance Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Agency Cost | $AC = MC + BC + RC$ | Quantifies the *cost of poor governance* in valuation models |
| 2 | Tobin's Q (Governance Proxy) | $Q = \dfrac{MV_{equity} + MV_{debt}}{BV_{assets}}$ | $Q > 1$ indicates intangible value (good governance) |
| 3 | Governance Risk Premium | $k_e^{adj} = k_e^{rf} + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f) + GRP$ | GRP = 2% to 5% for weak-governance firms |
| 4 | Board Independence Ratio | $BIR = \dfrac{N_{independent}}{N_{total\;directors}}$ | SEBI mandates $\geq 50\%$ for top-500 listed firms |
| 5 | Board Diversity Index (Blau) | $B = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ | Measures gender/professional diversity |
| 6 | Audit Committee Effectiveness | $ACE = \dfrac{M_{attendance} \times I_{independence} \times F_{expertise}}{N_{meetings}}$ | SEBI scoring parameter |
| 7 | Related Party Transaction Ratio | $RPTR = \dfrac{\sum RPT_{value}}{Total\;Assets}$ | Threshold: $< 10\%$ (SEBI Materiality Rule) |
| 8 | ESG-Adjusted WACC | $WACC_{ESG} = WACC_{base} \times (1 - \delta_{ESG})$ | $\delta_{ESG}$ = ESG discount of 0.5–1.5% |
| 9 | Governance Score (G-Score) | $GS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ | Weighted score from 0 to 100 (ISS Governance) |
| 10 | Residual Loss (Jensen-Meckling) | $RL = E[V_{no\;control}] - E[V_{with\;control}]$ | The "unrecoverable" welfare loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Substitution Rule:** For absolute value or conditional expressions, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ (NOT bare pipes) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance Fields

> [!TIP]
> Corporate Governance is not just "management theory" — it has **direct financial engineering applications**:

- **Valuation Models:** DCF discounts must be adjusted using Governance Risk Premium (Formula 3 above)
- **Portfolio Construction:** Governance scores feed into **Smart Beta** and **ESG-tilted** indices
- **Capital Budgeting:** Firms with poor CG face **higher bond yields** (1–2% credit spread penalty)
- **M&A:** Target due-diligence now includes **Governance Quality Score (GQS)** audit
- **Risk Management:** CG is the **Tier-1 control** in Basel III's Operational Risk framework for banks

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Agency Cost Impact on Firm Value

**Objective:** To derive the reduction in firm value due to agency costs and express the **Net Firm Value** under a governance regime.

### Step 1: Define the Unconstrained Firm Value

Let $V_0$ be the firm's value if the manager were the sole owner (no agency conflict):

$$
V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{FCF_t}{(1 + k_e)^t}
$$

### Step 2: Introduce the Agency Cost

The manager, acting as agent, consumes **perquisites** (perks) and engages in **empire-building** investments. Let $\alpha$ be the fraction of value destroyed annually:

$$
V_{agency} = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{FCF_t \cdot (1 - \alpha)}{(1 + k_e)^t} = V_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha)
$$

### Step 3: Add Monitoring Costs

Let $MC$ be the annual monitoring cost (audit, board, compliance). Reducing $\alpha$ by an amount $\Delta \alpha$ costs:

$$
MC = c \cdot \Delta \alpha
$$

where $c$ is the **marginal cost of monitoring** (in $/\Delta \alpha$).

### Step 4: Optimize the Governance Investment

The optimal $\alpha^*$ is found by equating marginal benefit of monitoring to marginal cost:

$$
\dfrac{\partial V_{agency}}{\partial \alpha} = -\dfrac{\partial MC}{\partial \alpha}
$$

$$
- V_0 = -c \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = V_0
$$

This gives a clean **rule of thumb:** *Invest in monitoring up to the point where the marginal cost equals the firm value lost per unit of agency cost.*

### Step 5: Net Value Under Governance

$$
V_{net} = V_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha^*) - MC(\alpha^*)
$$

**Interpretation:** The optimal governance structure **maximizes** $V_{net}$ — it does not eliminate agency costs entirely (residual loss remains), but it sets them at a level where the marginal cost of further control exceeds the marginal benefit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Governance-Adjusted Discount Rate

**Objective:** To incorporate a Governance Risk Premium (GRP) into the CAPM cost of equity.

### Step 1: Standard CAPM

$$
k_e = R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f)
$$

### Step 2: Add Country Risk Premium (CRP)

For an emerging market firm (e.g., India):

$$
k_e^{EM} = R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f) + CRP
$$

### Step 3: Add Governance Risk Premium (GRP)

Damodaran (NYU) framework — the GRP captures firm-specific governance weakness:

$$
k_e^{final} = R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f) + CRP + GRP
$$

### Step 4: Quantify GRP via Governance Score

Damodaran's empirical finding: GRP decreases roughly linearly from 5% (worst-governed decile) to 0% (best-governed decile).

$$
GRP = 5\% \times \left(1 - \dfrac{GS}{100}\right)
$$

Where $GS$ is the Governance Score (0 to 100). Example:
- $GS = 80 \Rightarrow GRP = 5\% \times 0.20 = 1.0\%$
- $GS = 50 \Rightarrow GRP = 5\% \times 0.50 = 2.5\%$

### Step 5: Impact on Valuation

A 1% increase in $k_e$ reduces firm value by approximately:

$$
\Delta V \approx -V \cdot \dfrac{\Delta k_e}{k_e}
$$

For a perpetual cash flow, $\Delta V / V = -\Delta k_e / k_e$. So a **2% GRP** on a 12% cost of equity = **~16.7% reduction in firm value**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Governance Mechanisms to Agency Problems

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **classic KTU 14-mark answer framework**. Memorize the structure.

| Governance Mechanism | Agency Problem Addressed | Stakeholder Group Protected | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Independent Directors** | Managerial opportunism | Shareholders | SEBI: ≥ 50% independent |
| **Audit Committee** | Financial misstatement | All stakeholders | Section 177, Companies Act 2013 |
| **Auditor Rotation** | Auditor-management collusion | Minority shareholders | 5-year rotation rule |
| **Related Party Transaction (RPT) Policy** | Tunneling by promoters | Minority shareholders | Material RPTs need shareholder approval |
| **Whistleblower Policy** | Fraud concealment | Employees, public at large | Section 177(9)–(10) |
| **CSR Spending (2% of net profit)** | Externalities | Community | Section 135, Companies Act 2013 |
| **Executive Compensation Clawback** | Short-termism | Shareholders | SEBI LODR Reg. 26 |
| **Shareholder Voting (E-voting)** | Entrenchment of management | All shareholders | Section 108, Companies Act 2013 |
| **Risk Management Committee** | Undisclosed risks | Lenders, investors | SEBI for top-500 |
| **Code of Conduct** | Ethical lapses | All stakeholders | SEBI LODR Schedule II |

## 3.4 Detailed Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

**Problem:** A firm has the following data:
- Risk-free rate ($R_f$) = **6%**
- Market risk premium ($R_m - R_f$) = **7%**
- Beta ($\beta$) = **1.2**
- Country Risk Premium (CRP) = **1%**
- Governance Score ($GS$) = **60**

**Find:** Cost of equity $k_e$ using Damodaran's Governance-Adjusted CAPM.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
k_e = R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f) + CRP + GRP
$$

First, compute the standard CAPM component:

$$
CAPM = 6\% + 1.2 \times 7\% = 6\% + 8.4\% = 14.4\%
$$

Next, compute the GRP:

$$
GRP = 5\% \times \left(1 - \dfrac{60}{100}\right) = 5\% \times 0.40 = 2.0\%
$$

Add CRP and GRP:

$$
k_e = 14.4\% + 1\% + 2\% = 17.4\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Weak governance ($GS = 60$) adds 2 percentage points to the cost of equity, which translates to a **~13% valuation penalty** relative to a perfectly-governed peer.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Tip (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - 2 marks: Correctly identifying the formula
> - 2 marks: Each step (CAPM, GRP, Final)
> - 1 mark: Economic interpretation

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define Corporate Governance. Explain the Cadbury Report's definition and its significance in modern CG framework.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Corporate Governance is the system by which companies are directed and controlled, encompassing the rules, practices, and processes through which corporate objectives are set, achieved, and monitored. *(1 mark)*

The **Cadbury Report (1992, UK)** defined it as *"the system by which companies are directed and controlled."* Issued in the wake of BCCI, Polly Peck, and Maxwell scandals, it became the **global template** for modern CG codes. *(1 mark)*

**Significance:**
- Introduced the **"comply or explain"** principle (now standard in India via SEBI LODR)
- Recommended separation of **Chairman and CEO roles**
- Mandated **independent directors** and **audit committee** structures
- Forms the basis of the **OECD Principles (1999, revised 2015)** *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the "Comply or Explain" principle? How is it implemented under the Indian regulatory regime?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **"Comply or Explain"** principle is a regulatory approach where companies must either **comply with** the prescribed corporate governance code or **explain** in their annual report why they deviated from a particular provision. *(1.5 marks)*

**Indian Implementation:**
- Introduced by **SEBI** through the **Clauses 49** of the Listing Agreement (2003, revised 2014)
- Now codified in **SEBI LODR Regulations, 2015 (Chapter IV, Schedule II)**
- Annual **Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR)** strengthens the "explain" mandate with quantified ESG metrics *(1.5 marks)*

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Module 3, Q.7.a/7.b pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Agency Theory** of corporate governance. Discuss the **three types of agency costs** identified by Jensen and Meckling (1976) with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **Anglo-Saxon, German, and Japanese models** of corporate governance. Which model does India follow and why? *(7 marks)*

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the **major corporate governance failures** globally (Enron, WorldCom, Satyam) and the regulatory responses (Sarbanes-Oxley Act, Companies Act 2013). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **role, composition, and functions of the Board of Directors** as prescribed by the **Companies Act 2013 and SEBI LODR Regulations 2015**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Complete Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Agency Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**
Agency Theory, formalized by Jensen & Meckling (1976), views the corporation as a **"nexus of contracts"** between Principals (shareholders/owners) and Agents (managers/directors). The relationship is marked by:
- **Divergent objectives** – Principals want wealth maximization; Agents may pursue perks, leisure, or empire-building
- **Information asymmetry** – Agents have superior knowledge of operations
- **Monitoring difficulty** – Principals cannot fully observe agent effort

**Step 2: The Three Agency Costs (3 marks)**

$$
AC_{total} = MC + BC + RC
$$

| Cost | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Monitoring Costs (MC)** | Expenditure by Principals to oversee Agents | Statutory audit fees, board expenses, SEBI compliance costs |
| **Bonding Costs (BC)** | Expenditure by Agents to assure Principals | Non-compete clauses, performance bonds, stock options |
| **Residual Loss (RC)** | The welfare loss remaining despite controls | Diversion of corporate opportunities, soft loans to related parties |

**Step 3: Application to Indian Context (2 marks)**
In promoter-driven Indian firms, the **principal-agent** relationship is further complicated by the **principal-principal** conflict (promoter vs. minority shareholders). Tools like the **SEBI Takeover Code**, **RPT disclosure norms**, and **majority-of-minority shareholder approval** address this.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Models (7 marks)

**Step 1: Anglo-Saxon Model (2 marks)**
- **Origin:** USA, UK, Australia
- **Features:** Dispersed ownership, strong shareholder rights, active proxy contests, hostile takeovers
- **Board:** Single (Unitary) board with independent directors
- **Weakness:** Short-termism, shareholder primacy ignores other stakeholders

**Step 2: German Model (2 marks)**
- **Origin:** Germany, Northern Europe
- **Features:** Two-tier board (Vorstand = Management; Aufsichtsrat = Supervisory with 50% worker representation)
- **Concentrated ownership** with **Hausbank** (house bank) providing long-term capital
- **Weakness:** Slow decision-making, limited shareholder voice

**Step 3: Japanese Model (1.5 marks)**
- **Origin:** Japan
- **Features:** **Keiretsu** (industrial and banking groups) with cross-shareholding
- Lifetime employment, main bank system, consensual decision-making (**ringi-sho**)
- **Weakness:** Stagnation, lack of accountability in crisis (lost decades)

**Step 4: Indian Hybrid Model (1.5 marks)**
India follows a **modified Anglo-Saxon model with German and Indian features:**
- **Unitary board** (Anglo-Saxon)
- **Mandatory 50% independent directors** for top-500 (SEBI LODR)
- **Stakeholder representation** through CSR (2% of net profit) and Worker Directors in specific companies
- **Concentrated promoter ownership** (similar to German banking influence)
- Codified in **Companies Act 2013** + **SEBI LODR 2015** + **Industry-specific regulators** (RBI, IRDAI, PFRDA)

---

### Complete Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — CG Failures and Regulatory Responses (7 marks)

**Case 1: Enron (USA, 2001) (1.5 marks)**
- **Failure:** Off-balance-sheet Special Purpose Entities (SPEs) hid $1 billion in debt; auditor Arthur Andersen signed off
- **Response:** **Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) 2002** — Section 302 (CEO/CFO certification), Section 404 (Internal Controls over Financial Reporting), Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB)

**Case 2: WorldCom (USA, 2002) (1.5 marks)**
- **Failure:** $11 billion accounting fraud by capitalizing operating expenses
- **Response:** Strengthened SOX provisions, mandatory **audit partner rotation**

**Case 3: Satyam Computer Services (India, 2009) (2 marks)**
- **Failure:** Founder-chairman **Ramalinga Raju** confessed to $1.47 billion cash-inflated balances
- **Response:**
  - **Companies Act 2013** introduced **National Financial Reporting Authority (NFRA)** with powers to penalize auditors
  - **SEBI** strengthened **Clause 49 / LODR** norms
  - **Class Action Suits** (Section 245) introduced for the first time
  - **Rotational audit** and **joint audit** norms formalized
  - **Whistleblower mechanism** strengthened (Section 177(9)–(10))

**Step 4: Comparative Impact (2 marks)**

| Aspect | USA (Post-Enron) | India (Post-Satyam) |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Law** | SOX 2002 | Companies Act 2013 + SEBI LODR |
| **Audit Oversight** | PCAOB | NFRA |
| **Whistleblower** | Dodd-Frank 2010 | Section 177, Companies Act |
| **Punishment** | Criminal (Sarbanes-Oxley §802: 20 years jail) | Criminal (Section 447, 2013 Act) |

---

#### Part (b) — Board of Directors: Role, Composition, Functions (7 marks)

**Step 1: Legal Framework (1 mark)**
The Board of Directors is the **highest decision-making body** of a company, governed by:
- **Companies Act 2013** (Sections 149–169)
- **SEBI LODR Regulations 2015** (Chapter IV, Schedule II)

**Step 2: Composition (2 marks)**

| Category | Requirement | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Directors** | Min 3 (public), Max 15 (can be increased by special resolution) | Sec 149(1) |
| **Independent Directors** | ≥ 50% for top-500; ≥ 33% for top-1501+ | SEBI LODR Reg 17 |
| **Woman Director** | At least 1 in top-500 prescribed companies | Sec 149(1) |
| **Non-Executive Chairman** | For top-500 (separating Chairman from MD/CEO) | SEBI LODR Reg 17(1B) |
| **Resident Director** | At least 1 who stayed in India ≥ 182 days | Sec 149(3) |

**Step 3: Key Functions (2 marks)**
1. **Strategic** – Approve budgets, business plans, M&A, expansion
2. **Oversight** – Monitor management performance, risk management
3. **Compliance** – Ensure adherence to laws, regulations, and ethical standards
4. **Fiduciary** – Act in the best interest of the company (Duty of Care, Duty of Loyalty)
5. **Disclosure** – Approve financial statements and ensure fair presentation

**Step 4: Mandatory Committees (2 marks)**
- **Audit Committee** (Sec 177) – Min 3 directors, 2/3 independent, chaired by independent director
- **Nomination and Remuneration Committee (NRC)** – Independent director chair
- **Stakeholders Relationship Committee (SRC)** – For shareholder grievance redressal
- **Risk Management Committee** – For top-500 listed entities
- **CSR Committee** – Sec 135, for companies meeting CSR thresholds

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "corporate governance" with "corporate management"** – Governance is about *oversight and direction*; management is about *execution*. (–2 marks common deduction)
> 2. **Writing SOX as a UK law** – SOX is the **US** Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, *not* a UK regulation. The UK equivalent is the **UK Companies Act 2006 + UK Corporate Governance Code 2018**.
> 3. **Misattributing the Cadbury Report** – The 1992 Cadbury Report is **UK**, not Indian. India has the **Kumar Mangalam Birla Committee (2000)** and **Naresh Chandra Committee (2002)**.
> 4. **Forgetting the Cadbury "comply or explain"** – Many students write definitions but skip this *pivotal* contribution. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Mixing up Sections** – Section 177 is **Audit Committee**, Section 135 is **CSR Committee**, Section 149 is **Board composition**. Do not interchange.
> 6. **Numerical mistakes in GRP calculation** – The Damodaran base is **5%**, not 10% or 2%. Verify before writing.
> 7. **Mermaid syntax in exams** – If asked to draw, students often confuse node IDs with reserved words. Avoid `end`, `subgraph` as node names.

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- **Definition:** Cadbury (1992) – *"the system by which companies are directed and controlled."*
- **Core Theories:** Agency Theory (Jensen-Meckling), Stakeholder Theory (Freeman), Stewardship Theory (Donaldson-Davis), Resource Dependency (Pfeffer-Salancik)
- **Agency Cost Equation:** $AC = MC + BC + RC$ — must know all three components
- **Three Pillars:** Accountability, Transparency, Fairness
- **Three Models:** Anglo-Saxon, German (Two-Tier), Japanese (Keiretsu) — India is a **modified Anglo-Saxon hybrid**
- **Cadbury Contribution:** Comply or Explain principle, separation of Chairman & CEO, independent directors
- **Key Indian Committees:** Kumar Mangalam Birla (2000), Naresh Chandra (2002), Narayana Murthy (2003), Uday Kotak (2017)
- **Companies Act 2013 — Must-Know Sections:**
  - Sec 135 → CSR
  - Sec 149 → Board composition
  - Sec 166 → Director duties
  - Sec 177 → Audit Committee + Whistleblower
  - Sec 245 → Class Action Suits
  - Sec 447 → Corporate Fraud (Punishment)
- **SEBI LODR 2015:** Chapter IV is the **CG Bible** for listed companies
- **Global Scandals to Remember:** Enron (2001), WorldCom (2002), Satyam (2009), IL&FS (2018), DHFL (2019)
- **Key Laws to Remember:** Sarbanes-Oxley (USA, 2002), Dodd-Frank (USA, 2010), UK Companies Act 2006, Companies Act 2013 (India)
- **Quantitative Tools:** Tobin's Q, GRP, Board Independence Ratio, Blau Diversity Index, ISS Governance Score
- **WACC Adjustment:** $WACC_{ESG} = WACC_{base} \times (1 - \delta_{ESG})$ with $\delta_{ESG} = 0.5\% \text{ to } 1.5\%$
- **Mnemonic for CG Models:** **"A**nglo = **A**ggressive shareholders; **G**erman = **G**roupthink + **G**ewerkschaft; **J**apanese = **J**oint families (Keiretsu)"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Board
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Board # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board of Directors (Corporate Board):** A formally constituted, elected body of individuals, duly appointed by the shareholders of a company under the provisions of the Companies Act and the Articles of Association, who collectively hold the **legal authority, fiduciary responsibility, and strategic oversight** to govern the corporation, supervise its executive management, safeguard stakeholder interests, and ratify critical financial and operational decisions. In the discipline of **Corporate Finance and Portfolio Management**, the *Board* is not merely an administrative fixture — it is the **apex governing mechanism** that bridges ownership (shareholders) and management (executives). It is the institutional safeguard that ensures managerial decisions align with shareholder wealth maximization, risk-adjusted return objectives, and statutory compliance. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Ship Captain Analogy:** Imagine a massive cargo ship (the company) loaded with valuable goods (capital). The shareholders are the owners of the goods standing on the dock. The executives (CEO, CFO) are the sailors steering the ship daily. The **Board of Directors is the Council of Senior Navigators** seated in the chart room. They don't steer the wheel daily, but they: > - Set the **destination** (corporate strategy) > - Approve the **route map** (capital budgeting decisions) > - Verify the **cargo manifest** (financial statements) > - Hire/fire the **Captain** (CEO appointment and removal) > - Sound the alarm during **storms** (risk oversight) The Board is the **collective wisdom layer** that prevents the ship from running aground due to short-term greed, incompetence, or negligence. ## 1.3 Core Composition Parameters - **Minimum Directors:** As per the Companies Act, 2013 — **3** for a public company, **2** for a private company, **1** for a One Person Company. - **Maximum Directors:** As prescribed by the Articles of Association (typically **15**, extendable by special resolution). - **Independent Directors:** At least **1/3rd** of total directors for listed companies (as per **SEBI LODR Regulations, 2015**). - **Women Director:** At least **1** woman director for the top **500** listed companies by market capitalization (SEBI mandate). - **Key Managerial Personnel (KMP):** Managing Director (MD), Whole-Time Director (WTD), Chief Executive Officer (CEO), Chief Financial Officer (CFO), Company Secretary (CS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Definition Box:** > **Fiduciary Duty** = A legal and ethical obligation of the directors to act in *utmost good faith* and in the *best interest of the company* (not personal interest), placing the interests of the company above their own. ## 1.4 Why the Board Matters in Corporate Finance | Dimension | Board's Role | |---|---| | Capital Structure | Approves debt-equity mix, leverage limits | | Dividend Policy | Recommends dividend rate, buyback decisions | | M&A Decisions | Authorizes mergers, acquisitions, divestitures | | Risk Management | Sets risk appetite, oversees risk frameworks | | Investor Relations | Approves financial disclosures, earnings reports | | Audit Oversight | Ensures integrity of financial statements | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of the Board The Board operates as a **tiered governance architecture**: ### Tier 1: The Full Board - Apex body, meets periodically (minimum **4** board meetings per year for listed companies, with a **maximum gap of 120 days** between two meetings). - Reviews strategic plans, approves budgets, ratifies major financial commitments. ### Tier 2: Board Committees Statutory and non-statutory committees that handle specialized oversight: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statutory Committees (Mandatory under SEBI LODR / Companies Act):** > 1. **Audit Committee** — Minimum **3** directors, with **2/3rd** being independent; chaired by an independent director. Oversees financial reporting, internal controls, audit processes, and related-party transactions. > 2. **Nomination and Remuneration Committee (NRC)** — All non-executive directors; at least **1/2** independent. Recommends appointment, remuneration, and performance evaluation of directors and KMP. > 3. **Stakeholders Relationship Committee** — Addresses shareholder/investor grievances. > 4. **Risk Management Committee** — Mandatory for top **500** listed companies; oversees enterprise-wide risk management framework. > 5. **CSR Committee** — For companies meeting the CSR applicability thresholds under Section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013. ### Tier 3: Management Committees - Executive Committee, Finance Committee, Investment Committee — facilitate rapid operational decisions between full Board meetings. ## 2.2 Classification of Directors | Category | Definition | Key Role | |---|---|---| | **Executive Director** | Whole-time director involved in day-to-day management | Operational leadership | | **Non-Executive Director (NED)** | Not involved in daily operations; provides oversight | Strategic guidance | | **Independent Director (ID)** | NED with no material relationship with the company | Objective scrutiny, conflict resolution | | **Managing Director (MD)** | Director entrusted with substantial powers of management | CEO-equivalent role | | **Chairman** | Head of the Board (can be executive or non-executive) | Conducts Board meetings, sets agenda | | **Nominee Director** | Appointed by a specific stakeholder (e.g., financial institution, government) | Represents specific interest | | **Woman Director** | Mandatory for top listed companies | Diversity, gender perspective | | **Small Shareholders' Director** | Appointed if requested by **1/10th** of small shareholders | Minority protection | ## 2.3 Core Functions of the Board (Cadbury Framework + SEBI) 1. **Strategic Function:** Review and guide corporate strategy, major plans of action, risk policy, annual budgets, and business plans. 2. **Oversight Function:** Monitor implementation of strategy, oversee major capital expenditure, acquisitions, and divestitures. 3. **Financial Stewardship:** Approve quarterly/annual financial statements, declare dividends, authorize share issuances and buybacks. 4. **Compliance Function:** Ensure compliance with legal/regulatory requirements, accounting standards, and ethical standards. 5. **Risk Function:** Identify principal risks, set risk appetite, ensure risk management systems are robust. 6. **People Function:** Select, compensate, monitor, and — when necessary — replace key executives; oversee succession planning. 7. **Stakeholder Function:** Ensure effective communication with shareholders and other stakeholders; protect their interests. 8. **Self-Governance Function:** Review Board's own performance, composition, and governance practices. ## 2.4 Fiduciary Duties of Directors (Section 166, Companies Act 2013) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every director of a company shall: > 1. Act in accordance with the **Articles of Association**. > 2. Act in **good faith** to promote the **objects of the company** for the benefit of its **members as a whole**, and in the **best interests of the company**, its employees, shareholders, community, and environment. > 3. Exercise duties with **due and reasonable care, skill, and diligence** and exercise **independent judgment**. > 4. **Not involve in a situation** in which he may have a direct or indirect interest that conflicts with the interest of the company. > 5. **Not achieve or attempt to achieve** any undue gain or advantage for himself or his relatives, partners, or associates. > 6. **Not assign his office** — directorship is a **personal** role, not transferable. > 7. Act in compliance with the provisions of the Companies Act and other applicable laws. ## 2.5 Board's Role in Portfolio Management Context In the **portfolio management** dimension of this course, the Board (especially through its committees) influences: - **Investment Policy Decisions:** Approves asset allocation frameworks, risk-return trade-offs. - **Performance Benchmarking:** Sets benchmarks (e.g., **Nifty 50**, **BSE Sensex**, or risk-free rate like the **10-year G-Sec yield**). - **Fund Manager Oversight:** Reviews performance of internal/external fund managers, assesses fee structures. - **Treasury Operations:** Approves treasury investment limits, counter-party exposure limits. - **Disclosure Standards:** Ensures transparency in NAV reporting, portfolio disclosure, and risk metrics. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Governance Sheet > [!NOTE] > The "formulas" in Board governance are largely **numerical compliance thresholds** and **decision rules** rather than algebraic equations. These are **direct exam-fetchers** in KTU examinations. | Parameter | Rule / Threshold | Statutory Source | |---|---|---| | Minimum Directors (Public Co.) | **3** | Companies Act, 2013 | | Minimum Directors (Private Co.) | **2** | Companies Act, 2013 | | Minimum Directors (OPC) | **1** | Companies Act, 2013 | | Maximum Directors (default) | **15** | Companies Act, 2013 | | Independent Directors (Listed Co.) | $\geq \frac{1}{3}$ of total | SEBI LODR | | Women Director (Top 500 by m-cap) | $\geq 1$ | SEBI LODR | | Board Meetings (per year) | $\geq 4$ | SEBI LODR | | Maximum Gap Between Meetings | $\leq 120$ days | SEBI LODR | | Audit Committee Size | $\geq 3$ directors | SEBI LODR | | Audit Committee Independence | $\geq \frac{2}{3}$ independent | SEBI LODR | | NRC Composition | All NEDs, $\geq \frac{1}{2}$ independent | SEBI LODR | | Director's Liability Limit (Negligence) | Unlimited (personal) | Case law | | Quorum for Board Meeting | $\geq \frac{1}{3}$ of total directors OR 2 (whichever is higher) | Companies Act, 2013 | | Special Resolution Threshold | $\geq 75\%$ votes cast | Companies Act, 2013 | | Ordinary Resolution Threshold | Simple majority ($> 50\%$) | Companies Act, 2013 | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Finance & Engineering - **Corporate Restructuring:** Board's approval is mandatory for schemes of arrangement, mergers, demergers, and reduction of capital. - **IPO / FPO Decisions:** Board's resolution is the foundational document for SEBI filings and DRHP/RHP preparation. - **Project Finance Approvals:** For large infrastructure or capital projects, the Board's sanction authorizes the company's borrowing limits and project viability certifications. - **Tech Companies:** Boards of tech firms (e.g., Infosys, TCS) drive R&D budget allocation, IP strategy, and global expansion finance. - **Banking Sector:** Banks have additional RBI-mandated Board norms (e.g., **Fit and Proper** criteria, Risk Management Committee under RBI Master Directions). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Optimal Board Size and Composition While board size is largely a compliance parameter, the **economic theory** behind it is a high-yield analytical concept. Let's derive the conditions. ### Step 1: Define the Trade-off Function The Board's effectiveness $E(B)$ can be modeled as a function of its size $n$ (number of directors): $$E(B) = B(n) - C(n)$$ Where: - $B(n)$ = **Benefit function** (diversity of expertise, broader oversight, stakeholder representation) - $C(n)$ = **Cost function** (coordination costs, decision delays, free-rider problem, agency costs) ### Step 2: Expand the Benefit Function $$B(n) = \alpha \cdot \ln(n)$$ Logarithmic benefits: Each additional director adds value, but with **diminishing returns** (concave benefit curve). Where $\alpha$ is the **synergy coefficient** representing the marginal value of additional expertise. ### Step 3: Expand the Cost Function $$C(n) = \beta \cdot n^2$$ Quadratic costs: Coordination costs and decision-making complexity grow **faster than linearly** as board size increases. Where $\beta$ is the **coordination friction coefficient**. ### Step 4: Formulate the Net Effectiveness Equation $$E(B) = \alpha \cdot \ln(n) - \beta \cdot n^2$$ ### Step 5: Take the First Derivative and Set to Zero To find the **optimal board size** $n^{*}$, we maximize $E(B)$: $$\frac{dE}{dn} = \frac{\alpha}{n} - 2\beta \cdot n = 0$$ ### Step 6: Solve for the Optimal Board Size $$\frac{\alpha}{n} = 2\beta \cdot n$$ $$2\beta \cdot n^2 = \alpha$$ $$n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{2\beta}}$$ ### Step 7: Verify Second-Order Condition (Maximum) $$\frac{d^2E}{dn^2} = -\frac{\alpha}{n^2} - 2\beta < 0$$ The second derivative is **negative for all valid $n$**, confirming that $n^{*}$ is indeed a **maximum** (not a minimum). ### Step 8: Interpret the Result > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The optimal board size $n^{*} = \sqrt{\alpha / 2\beta}$ depends on: > - **Higher synergy ($\alpha \uparrow$)** → larger board is optimal (more diverse skills needed). > - **Higher coordination friction ($\beta \uparrow$)** → smaller board is optimal (avoid decision paralysis). > > Empirical research (e.g., **Jensen, 1993**) suggests that for most public companies, the optimal size lies in the range of **7 to 9 directors** — large enough for diverse expertise, small enough for effective decision-making. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Board Independence Ratio Let us derive the minimum number of independent directors required for a given board size. ### Given: - Total Board Size = $N$ - Minimum Independent Director Proportion = $p = \frac{1}{3}$ ### Find: - Minimum Independent Directors $I_{min}$ = ? ### Step-by-Step: $$I_{min} = p \cdot N$$ $$I_{min} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot N$$ $$I_{min} = \frac{N}{3}$$ **Worked Examples:** | Total Directors $N$ | Calculation | $I_{min}$ (rounded up) | |---|---|---| | 6 | $\frac{6}{3}$ | 2 | | 7 | $\frac{7}{3}$ | 3 (rounded up to nearest integer) | | 9 | $\frac{9}{3}$ | 3 | | 10 | $\frac{10}{3}$ | 4 (rounded up) | | 12 | $\frac{12}{3}$ | 4 | | 15 | $\frac{15}{3}$ | 5 | > [!NOTE] > The rule is to **round up** to the next integer since you cannot have a fractional director. This ensures compliance is never borderline. ## 3.3 Quorum Calculation Derivation ### Quorum for Board Meeting: $$Q = \max\left(\frac{N}{3}, \; 2\right)$$ ### Step-by-Step Application: - For $N = 9$: $Q = \max(3, 2) = 3$ - For $N = 5$: $Q = \max(1.67, 2) = 2$ - For $N = 3$: $Q = \max(1, 2) = 2$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Board's Authority Hierarchy in Financial Decisions | Decision Category | Authority Level | Example | |---|---|---| | Routine operational | Management | Vendor payments, daily treasury | | Significant financial commitment | Board approval | Capital expenditure > threshold | | Major strategic | Board + Shareholders | M&A, diversification | | Structural changes | Special Resolution | Alter Articles, change object clause | | Statutory compliance | Board | Financial statement approval, dividend declaration | ## 3.5 Board Composition and Agency Cost Minimization **Agency Cost** ($AC$) — the cost of conflicts between principals (shareholders) and agents (management) — is a core corporate finance concept where the Board plays a central role. $$AC = AC_{monitoring} + AC_{bonding} + AC_{residual}$$ Where: - $AC_{monitoring}$ = Costs incurred by the principal to monitor the agent (e.g., audit fees, Board expenses). - $AC_{bonding}$ = Costs incurred by the agent to assure the principal (e.g., compliance certifications). - $AC_{residual}$ = The welfare loss that remains even after monitoring and bonding (e.g., suboptimal decisions). **Board's role in reducing $AC$:** - Effective Audit Committee $\rightarrow$ reduces $AC_{monitoring}$ inaccuracy. - Independent Directors $\rightarrow$ reduce $AC_{residual}$ by curbing managerial entrenchment. - NRC oversight $\rightarrow$ aligns manager incentives, reducing $AC_{bonding}$ needs. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Corporate Board Governance Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB SH["SHAREHOLDERS
OWNERS"] -->|"Elect / Remove"| BRD["BOARD OF DIRECTORS
APEX GOVERNING BODY"] SH -->|"Special Resolution"| SR["STRATEGIC DECISIONS
M&A, CAPITAL, ARTICLES"] BRD -->|"Appoints / Evaluates"| CEO["CEO / MANAGING DIRECTOR"] BRD -->|"Approves Strategy"| STR["STRATEGIC PLAN
AND BUDGET"] BRD -->|"Approves Financials"| FIN["QUARTERLY / ANNUAL
FINANCIAL STATEMENTS"] BRD -->|"Approves Dividend"| DIV["DIVIDEND DECLARATION
AND BUYBACK"] BRD --> AC["AUDIT COMMITTEE

SECTION 135"] AC -->|"Oversees"| IA["INTERNAL AUDIT"] AC -->|"Recommends"| EA["EXTERNAL AUDITOR"] NRC -->|"Sets Pay"| KMP["KMP REMUNERATION
CEO, CFO, CS"] RMC -->|"Implements"| ERM["ENTERPRISE RISK

Single security %, Sector %"] P2 --> KPI["KPI TRACKING

Bank, Broker Limits"] P3 --> FX["FX RISK MANAGEMENT

OF DIRECTORS"] FD --> D1["DUTY OF LOYALTY
Act in company interest
Avoid conflicts"] FD --> D2["DUTY OF CARE
Reasonable diligence
