Honest intention
Best motives"] FD --> D4["DUTY OF CONFIDENTIALITY
Protect proprietary data"] FD --> D5["DUTY NOT TO MAKE
UNDUE PROFIT
Section 166(2)"] D1 --> E1["No related-party
transactions without
disclosure"] D1 --> E2["No use of corporate
opportunity for
personal gain"] D2 --> E3["Attend meetings
with preparation"] D2 --> E4["Seek expert advice
when needed"] D3 --> E5["Declare interests
in contracts"] D3 --> E6["Transparent
decision-making"] style FD fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff style D1 fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff style D2 fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff style D3 fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the Board of Directors. What are the key fiduciary duties of a director under the Companies Act, 2013? > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer:** The **Board of Directors** is the elected governing body of a company, comprising individuals appointed by shareholders to provide strategic direction, oversight, and accountability. It acts as the bridge between ownership and management. **Key Fiduciary Duties (Section 166, Companies Act, 2013):** 1. **Duty of Good Faith:** Act in the best interests of the company, its members, employees, and stakeholders. 2. **Duty of Care, Skill, and Diligence:** Exercise reasonable care, skill, and diligence while performing duties. 3. **Duty to Avoid Conflict of Interest:** Not place oneself in a position where personal interest conflicts with the company's interest. 4. **Duty Not to Make Undue Profit:** Not achieve or attempt undue gain for self, relatives, partners, or associates. 5. **Duty of Independent Judgment:** Exercise independent judgment in decision-making. 6. **Duty Not to Assign Office:** Directorship is a personal role and cannot be delegated. --- ### Question 2: Distinguish between Executive Director, Non-Executive Director, and Independent Director. Why are Independent Directors important? > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Executive Director | Non-Executive Director | Independent Director | |---|---|---|---| | Day-to-day role | Involved | Not involved | Not involved | | Employment | Whole-time employee of company | Part-time, not employee | Not an employee | | Material Relationship | May have | May have | No material relationship | | Voting rights | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Primary function | Operational leadership | Strategic oversight | Objective scrutiny | **Importance of Independent Directors:** - Provide **objective and unbiased** oversight. - Protect **minority shareholder interests**. - Reduce **agency conflicts** between management and shareholders. - Strengthen **corporate governance** and **disclosure quality**. - **Mandatory** under SEBI LODR for listed companies (**at least 1/3** of total Board strength). --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Solve either **Question A** or **Question B** completely. ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the composition of the Board of Directors as per the Companies Act, 2013 and SEBI LODR Regulations. Discuss the mandatory statutory committees that must be constituted by a listed company. (7 Marks) **(b)** Discuss the role and responsibilities of the Board in corporate financial decision-making with specific reference to capital structure, dividend policy, and risk oversight. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) **Composition of the Board:** 1. **Minimum and Maximum Number:** - Public Company: Minimum **3 directors**, Maximum **15** (extendable by special resolution). - Private Company: Minimum **2 directors**. - One Person Company (OPC): Minimum **1 director**. 2. **Mandatory Categories for Listed Companies (SEBI LODR):** - **Independent Directors:** At least **1/3** of total Board strength. - **Woman Director:** At least **1** for top 500 listed companies by market capitalization. - **Small Shareholders' Director:** Appointed upon request of **1/10th** of small shareholders. - **Key Managerial Personnel (KMP):** CEO, MD, CFO, and Company Secretary. 3. **Board Meetings:** Minimum **4** meetings per year, with **maximum 120 days** gap between two consecutive meetings. **Mandatory Statutory Committees:** | Committee | Composition | Key Role | |---|---|---| | **Audit Committee** | $\geq 3$ directors, $\geq 2/3$ independent, chaired by Independent Director | Financial reporting oversight, internal controls, audit process | | **Nomination & Remuneration Committee (NRC)** | All NEDs, $\geq 1/2$ independent | Director/KMP appointment, remuneration, performance evaluation | | **Stakeholders Relationship Committee** | Non-executive chair, majority NEDs | Investor grievance redressal | | **Risk Management Committee** | Mandatory for top 500 by m-cap | Enterprise risk framework, risk appetite | | **CSR Committee** | For companies under Section 135 | CSR policy, expenditure monitoring | --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Role of the Board in Corporate Financial Decisions:** **1. Capital Structure Decisions:** - The Board approves the **debt-equity mix** and overall leverage limits. - It authorizes issuance of **equity, preference shares, debentures, and bonds**. - Reviews the **cost of capital** implications and the impact on **EPS and ROE**. - Approves major **borrowing powers** delegated to management. **2. Dividend Policy:** - The Board **recommends the dividend rate** (interim and final). - Considers **retained earnings vs. payout ratio** trade-offs. - Approves **share buyback** decisions within SEBI regulations. - Ensures **dividend distribution tax** compliance and **cash flow** adequacy. **3. Risk Oversight:** - The Board sets the **risk appetite** and approves the risk management policy. - Through the **Risk Management Committee**, it oversees **credit, market, operational, and liquidity risks**. - Reviews **stress testing** outcomes and **scenario analysis** results. - Ensures **internal control systems** are robust and audited regularly. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse the role of the Board with that of the CEO. Remember: The Board **sets policy and oversees** — it does **not manage** day-to-day operations. Writing CEO-level operational tasks as Board functions will cost you **2–3 marks**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** What is a Board of Directors? Discuss the various types of directors with their distinctive features. (7 Marks) **(b)** Critically analyze the role of the Board of Directors in the context of Portfolio Management. How does the Board ensure alignment between management actions and shareholder interests? (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) **Definition:** The **Board of Directors** is the highest decision-making body of a company, collectively responsible for governance, strategy, and oversight. It derives its authority from the Companies Act, 2013, the Articles of Association, and shareholder resolutions. **Types of Directors (with Distinctive Features):** 1. **Executive Director:** A director who is in the **whole-time employment** of the company and is involved in **day-to-day management**. They receive salary and other benefits as employees. Example: Managing Director (MD), Whole-Time Director (WTD). 2. **Non-Executive Director (NED):** A director who is **not involved in daily operations** but participates in Board meetings, provides strategic guidance, and may serve on committees. They typically receive only **sitting fees and commission**. 3. **Independent Director (ID):** A non-executive director who has **no material relationship** (pecuniary or otherwise) with the company, its promoters, or management. They are appointed for a term of **up to 5 consecutive years** and are eligible for re-appointment for another **5 years** (total **10 years**). After 10 years, a cooling-off period of **3 years** applies. 4. **Nominee Director:** Appointed by a **third party** (e.g., financial institution, government, holding company) as per agreement or statute. They represent the interest of the nominating body. 5. **Additional Director:** Appointed by the **existing Board** between two Annual General Meetings; holds office only up to the **next AGM**. 6. **Alternate Director:** Appointed in place of a director who is **absent from India for more than 3 months**; vacates office when the original director returns. 7. **Small Shareholders' Director:** Appointed if requested by **1/10th** of small shareholders (holding shares of nominal value not exceeding **₹20,000**). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Role of Board in Portfolio Management:** 1. **Setting Investment Policy:** The Board approves the **Investment Policy Statement (IPS)** that defines asset allocation, risk tolerance, return objectives, and benchmark indices (e.g., Nifty 50, BSE Sensex). 2. **Performance Evaluation:** The Board (through NRC and dedicated committees) reviews the performance of fund managers against benchmarks and risk-adjusted return metrics (Sharpe Ratio, Treynor Ratio, Jensen's Alpha). 3. **Risk Oversight:** The Board's Risk Management Committee sets **exposure limits** (single security %, sector %, counter-party %), approves **hedging strategies**, and reviews **Value at Risk (VaR)** reports. 4. **Fee and Cost Approval:** The Board approves **management fees, performance fees, and expense ratios** to ensure they are competitive and aligned with investor interests. 5. **Disclosure and Transparency:** The Board ensures timely disclosure of **NAV, portfolio composition, and risk metrics** as mandated by SEBI. **Alignment Between Management and Shareholders:** - **Independent Directors** act as watchdogs, curbing managerial opportunism. - **Audit Committee** ensures financial statements are **true and fair**, preventing earnings manipulation. - **NRC** aligns executive compensation with **long-term shareholder value creation** (e.g., ESOPs, performance-based pay). - **Related-Party Transaction Policy** approved by the Board prevents **tunneling** and **self-dealing**. - **Whistle-Blower Policy** mandated by SEBI enables employees to report governance violations directly to the Audit Committee. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** For Question B, part (b), students often write generic corporate governance content and forget the **portfolio management** context. Always link your answers to the **specific financial/portfolio decisions** — this is the differentiator between a 7-mark and 12-mark answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** **Core Definitions:** - ☐ Board of Directors = Apex governing body elected by shareholders. - ☐ Fiduciary Duty = Legal obligation to act in utmost good faith for the company. - ☐ Independent Director = Director with no material relationship with the company. - ☐ KMP = CEO, MD, CFO, Company Secretary. **Critical Numbers (Must Memorize):** - ☐ Public Co. minimum directors: **3** - ☐ Private Co. minimum directors: **2** - ☐ Maximum directors (default): **15** - ☐ Independent Directors: **$\geq 1/3$** of total Board (listed companies) - ☐ Audit Committee: **$\geq 3$** directors, **$\geq 2/3$** independent - ☐ NRC: All NEDs, **$\geq 1/2$** independent - ☐ Board meetings per year: **$\geq 4$** - ☐ Maximum gap between meetings: **$\leq 120$ days** - ☐ Quorum: **$\max(N/3, 2)$** - ☐ ID term: **5 + 5 = 10 years** (with 3-year cooling-off) - ☐ Woman Director: **$\geq 1$** for top 500 listed companies - ☐ Small Shareholders' Director trigger: **1/10th** of small shareholders **Statutory Sources to Cite in Exam:** - ☐ Companies Act, 2013 — Sections **149, 166, 167, 177, 178** - ☐ SEBI (LODR) Regulations, 2015 — **Regulations 17, 18, 19, 20, 21** - ☐ Companies (Appointment and Qualification of Directors) Rules, 2014 **Key Committees to Remember:** - ☐ **Audit Committee** — Financial oversight - ☐ **NRC** — Appointment, remuneration, evaluation - ☐ **Stakeholders Relationship Committee** — Investor grievances - ☐ **Risk Management Committee** — ERM framework - ☐ **CSR Committee** — Section 135 compliance **Fiduciary Duties (Section 166) — 7 Key Duties:** - ☐ Good faith & best interest - ☐ Due care, skill, diligence - ☐ Independent judgment - ☐ Avoid conflict of interest - ☐ No undue gain - ☐ No assignment of office - ☐ Compliance with law **Types of Directors:** - ☐ Executive (MD, WTD) - ☐ Non-Executive (NED) - ☐ Independent (ID) - ☐ Nominee - ☐ Additional - ☐ Alternate - ☐ Small Shareholders' **Board's Role in Finance (Top 5 to remember):** - ☐ Capital structure approval - ☐ Dividend recommendation - ☐ M&A authorization - ☐ Risk appetite setting - ☐ Financial statement approval **Optimal Board Size (Theory):** - ☐ Derived as $n^{*} = \sqrt{\alpha / 2\beta}$ - ☐ Empirically: **7–9 directors** is the sweet spot - ☐ Trade-off: Diversity (benefits) vs. Coordination cost **Common Exam Traps:** - ☐ Confusing Board's role with CEO's role - ☐ Forgetting to cite specific sections/regulations - ☐ Writing generic governance content without financial context - ☐ Not remembering numerical thresholds (marks lost easily) - ☐ Mixing up committee compositions > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU valuations, citing the **specific section number** (e.g., "Section 166 of the Companies Act, 2013") or **specific regulation** (e.g., "Regulation 17 of SEBI LODR") earns you **extra credit** and demonstrates mastery. Always cite the source.

topics.title: Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Portfolio Management** is the science and art of selecting, monitoring, and continuously revising a basket of investment assets (securities) in alignment with the investor's investment objectives, risk tolerance, return expectations, and constraints over a defined investment horizon.

In the KTU 2024 scheme lexicon, the term **"Management"** within Module 3 of Corporate Finance & Portfolio Management refers holistically to **Investment / Portfolio Management** — the systematic, quantitative, and strategic process of constructing and administering an investment portfolio that optimally balances the **risk-return trade-off**.

### 1.1.1 Sub-Components of "Management" in Module 3

| Sub-Theme | KTU 2024 Description |
|-----------|----------------------|
| **Portfolio Identification** | Universe of securities selection |
| **Portfolio Analysis** | Risk-return profiling of securities |
| **Portfolio Selection** | Markowitz, Sharpe Index, CAPM based |
| **Portfolio Valuation** | Computing expected returns & risk |
| **Portfolio Revision** | Rebalancing & churning decisions |
| **Portfolio Evaluation** | Sharpe, Treynor, Jensen performance measures |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef & The Recipe Book" 🍳

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine you are a **chef preparing a multi-course meal** for guests with different dietary needs. You don't use only one ingredient (say, salt) — that would be risky if a guest dislikes it. Instead, you **blend ingredients** (vegetables, spices, proteins) in measured proportions (weights) so that:
> - The overall **taste (return)** is maximized.
> - The risk of any one ingredient **ruining** the dish is **minimized (diversified away)**.
> - You **taste-test** (evaluate) periodically and **adjust** the recipe if a guest feedback changes (revision).

**Mapping the Analogy to Finance:**
- **Ingredients** → Individual Securities (Stocks, Bonds)
- **Recipe (Proportions)** → Portfolio Weights ($w_i$)
- **Taste** → Expected Portfolio Return $E(R_p)$
- **Risk of Bad Ingredient** → Standard Deviation / Variance $\sigma_p^2$
- **Tasting Periodically** → Portfolio Evaluation (Sharpe, Treynor ratios)
- **Adjusting Recipe** → Portfolio Revision / Rebalancing

> **Key Insight:** A portfolio's total risk is *not* the simple sum of individual risks — it is the **weighted sum** modified by **correlations**, which can dramatically reduce overall risk through **diversification**.

---

## 1.3 Why is "Management" Critical in Corporate Finance?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Modern Corporate Finance rests on three pillars — **Financing Decisions, Investment Decisions, and Dividend Decisions**. Portfolio Management operates at the intersection of all three, because corporate surplus is deployed into marketable securities (treasury portfolios), and individuals/firms must continuously manage wealth.

**Core Objectives (in KTU Examinable Order):**
1. **Capital Appreciation** — Maximize long-term wealth growth.
2. **Income Generation** — Steady cash flow via dividends/interest.
3. **Preservation of Capital** — Protect against erosion by inflation/risk.
4. **Liquidity** — Ensure assets are readily convertible to cash.
5. **Tax Efficiency** — Optimize after-tax returns.

---

## 1.4 The Risk-Return Trade-Off — Foundational Premise

> **Golden Rule of Portfolio Management:**
> $$\boxed{\text{Higher Expected Return} \Longrightarrow \text{Higher Risk (and vice versa)}}$$

This **inverse relationship** is the **engine** of all portfolio theory. The role of management is to find the **Efficient Frontier** — the locus of portfolios offering maximum return for a given level of risk.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics — Definition Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Defined Metrics to Memorize (Bold = Constantly Cited):**
> - **Expected Return ($E(R_i)$)** — Probability-weighted average return.
> - **Standard Deviation ($\sigma_i$)** — Square root of variance; absolute measure of **total risk**.
> - **Variance ($\sigma_i^2$)** — Squared deviation of returns.
> - **Covariance ($\sigma_{ij}$)** — Measure of co-movement between two securities.
> - **Correlation Coefficient ($\rho_{ij}$)** — Standardized co-movement, range **-1 to +1**.
> - **Beta ($\beta_i$)** — Systematic risk sensitivity relative to market.
> - **Sharpe Ratio ($S_p$)** — Risk-adjusted return per unit of total risk.
> - **Treynor Ratio ($T_p$)** — Risk-adjusted return per unit of systematic risk.
> - **Jensen's Alpha ($\alpha_p$)** — Excess return over CAPM-predicted return.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Six-Step Portfolio Management Process (KTU Examinable Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Prescribed Process:** Every exam answer on "Portfolio Management" must reference these six steps in sequence.

### Step 1: Identification of Objectives
- **Investor profiling**: Risk-averse, Risk-neutral, Risk-seeker.
- **Time horizon**: Short (<1 yr), Medium (1–5 yrs), Long (>5 yrs).
- **Income vs. Growth preference.**

### Step 2: Identification of Asset Universe (Eligible Securities)
- Equity, Debt, Hybrid, Derivatives, Commodities, Real Estate.
- Screening via fundamental & technical filters.

### Step 3: Portfolio Analysis (Risk-Return Profiling)
- Compute $E(R_i)$, $\sigma_i$, $\sigma_{ij}$ for each candidate.

### Step 4: Portfolio Selection (Optimization)
- Apply Markowitz Mean-Variance Optimization (MVO).
- Or apply Sharpe's Single Index Model.
- Or apply CAPM-based equilibrium model.

### Step 5: Portfolio Valuation (Implementation)
- Calculate $E(R_p)$, $\sigma_p^2$, $\beta_p$.
- Verify adherence to constraints.

### Step 6: Portfolio Evaluation (Performance Measurement)
- **Ex-post** measurement using realized returns.
- Apply Sharpe, Treynor, Jensen, Information Ratio, M².

### Additional Step: Portfolio Revision
- **Active Revision**: Churning, rebalancing when expected returns shift.
- **Passive Revision**: Buy-and-hold with periodic rebalancing.
- **Active Portfolio Strategy (Top-down vs. Bottom-up)**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize every entry below. Each appears in past KTU Dec/Jan papers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Expected Return (Single Asset) | $E(R_i) = \sum_{s=1}^{n} P_s \cdot R_{is}$ | Percentage (%) |
| 2 | Portfolio Expected Return (2 assets) | $E(R_p) = w_A E(R_A) + w_B E(R_B)$ | Percentage (%) |
| 3 | Portfolio Expected Return (n assets) | $E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot E(R_i)$ | Percentage (%) |
| 4 | Variance (Single Asset) | $\sigma_i^2 = \sum P_s \cdot [R_{is} - E(R_i)]^2$ | $(\%)^2$ |
| 5 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\sigma_i^2}$ | Percentage (%) |
| 6 | Covariance | $\sigma_{ij} = \sum P_s \cdot [R_{is} - E(R_i)][R_{js} - E(R_j)]$ | $(\%)^2$ |
| 7 | Correlation Coefficient | $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ij}}{\sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j}$ | Dimensionless, $\rho \in [-1, +1]$ |
| 8 | Portfolio Variance (2 assets) | $\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$ | $(\%)^2$ |
| 9 | Portfolio Variance (n assets) | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_i w_j \sigma_{ij}$ | $(\%)^2$ |
| 10 | Portfolio Beta | $\beta_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \beta_i$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Single Index Model Return | $R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_M + e_i$ | $e_i$ = residual (idiosyncratic) |
| 12 | Single Index Model Variance | $\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{e_i}^2$ | Systematic + Unsystematic |
| 13 | Sharpe Ratio | $S_p = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Excess return per unit total risk |
| 14 | Treynor Ratio | $T_p = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\beta_p}$ | Excess return per unit systematic risk |
| 15 | Jensen's Alpha | $\alpha_p = [E(R_p) - R_f] - \beta_p [E(R_M) - R_f]$ | Excess return over CAPM line |
| 16 | CAPM Equilibrium | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_M) - R_f]$ | Risk-return equilibrium |
| 17 | CML Equation | $E(R_p) = R_f + \dfrac{[E(R_M) - R_f]}{\sigma_M} \cdot \sigma_p$ | Tangent portfolio line |
| 18 | Security Market Line (SML) | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_M) - R_f]$ | Graphical CAPM |
| 19 | Portfolio Turnover Ratio | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{\text{Purchases} + \text{Sales}}{\text{Average Portfolio Value}}$ | Active vs. Passive indicator |
| 20 | M² (Modigliani-Modigliani) | $M^2_p = S_p \cdot \sigma_M - [E(R_M) - R_f]$ | Risk-adjusted to market volatility |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Constraint Reminder:** No vertical pipe `|` was used inside any cell — all absolute values, conditions, and ranges use the slash `/` notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Finance Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Portfolio Management Matters Beyond Textbooks:**
> 1. **Corporate Treasury Management**: Companies invest idle cash in short-term portfolios (commercial paper, T-bills).
> 2. **Mutual Funds & ETFs**: The ₹5,000+ crore Indian MF industry runs on Markowitz/CAPM frameworks.
> 3. **Insurance Asset-Liability Management (ALM)**: Insurers must match long-duration liabilities with carefully duration-matched portfolios.
> 4. **Pension Fund Management**: National Pension System (NPS) uses lifecycle portfolio management.
> 5. **Engineering Project Risk**: Even engineering project portfolios (R&D pipeline) use modern portfolio theory (MPT) analogies for risk diversification.

---

## 2.4 Active vs. Passive Management — KTU-Frequently Tested Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Students must clearly distinguish these in a 7-mark question.

| Dimension | Active Management | Passive Management |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Strategy** | Outperform benchmark | Match benchmark |
| **Cost** | High (research, transaction) | Low (index tracking) |
| **Risk** | Higher (concentrated bets) | Lower (broad diversification) |
| **Return Potential** | Above-market possible | Market return only |
| **Tools** | Stock-picking, market timing | Index funds, ETFs |
| **Revisions** | Frequent | Minimal (buy & hold) |
| **KTU Examples** | Hedge funds, PMS | Nifty 50 Index Fund, Sensex ETF |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Expected Portfolio Return (General n-Asset Case)

### Mathematical Derivation:

**Starting Premise:** A portfolio of $n$ assets has weights $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$ such that:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$

The return of the portfolio in state $s$ is the weighted sum of individual asset returns:

$$R_{p,s} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot R_{i,s}$$

**Step 1:** Take the expectation (probability-weighted average) over all states $s$:

$$E(R_p) = E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot R_{i,s}\right]$$

**Step 2:** Apply the linearity of the expectation operator (key property: $E(aX) = aE(X)$):

$$E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot E(R_{i,s})$$

**Step 3:** Rename $E(R_{i,s})$ as $E(R_i)$ (since it is a constant per asset):

$$\boxed{E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot E(R_i)}$$

> **Note:** This proves the linear weighted-average property — the portfolio return is the weighted average of individual expected returns.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Two-Asset Portfolio Variance

**Starting Premise:** For a 2-asset portfolio $P$ with assets $A$ and $B$:

$$R_{p,s} = w_A R_{A,s} + w_B R_{B,s}$$

**Step 1:** Variance of portfolio:

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\left[(R_{p,s} - E(R_p))^2\right]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the return expression:

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\left[(w_A R_A + w_B R_B - w_A E(R_A) - w_B E(R_B))^2\right]$$

**Step 3:** Factor the bracket:

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\left[(w_A(R_A - E(R_A)) + w_B(R_B - E(R_B)))^2\right]$$

**Step 4:** Expand using $(x+y)^2 = x^2 + 2xy + y^2$:

$$\sigma_p^2 = E\left[w_A^2(R_A - E(R_A))^2 + 2w_A w_B(R_A - E(R_A))(R_B - E(R_B)) + w_B^2(R_B - E(R_B))^2\right]$$

**Step 5:** Apply linearity of expectation to each term:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 E\left[(R_A - E(R_A))^2\right] + 2w_A w_B E\left[(R_A - E(R_A))(R_B - E(R_B))\right] + w_B^2 E\left[(R_B - E(R_B))^2\right]$$

**Step 6:** Recognize each operator: the first and third are variances, the middle is covariance:

$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \sigma_{AB}}$$

**Step 7:** Using $\sigma_{AB} = \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$, express in correlation form:

$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The **cross-product term** $2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$ is the *diversification engine*. If $\rho_{AB} < 1$, the portfolio variance is **strictly less** than the weighted sum of individual variances. This is the mathematical proof of diversification's value.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: General n-Asset Portfolio Variance (Matrix Form)

**Starting Premise:** Let $w$ be the weight vector and $\Sigma$ be the covariance matrix:

$$w = \begin{bmatrix} w_1 \\ w_2 \\ \vdots \\ w_n \end{bmatrix} \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1^2 & \sigma_{12} & \cdots & \sigma_{1n} \\ \sigma_{21} & \sigma_2^2 & \cdots & \sigma_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \sigma_{n1} & \sigma_{n2} & \cdots & \sigma_n^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** Portfolio return in vector form: $R_p = w^T R$ where $R = [R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n]^T$.

**Step 2:** Portfolio expected return: $E(R_p) = w^T E(R)$.

**Step 3:** Variance by definition:

$$\sigma_p^2 = E[(R_p - E(R_p))(R_p - E(R_p))]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$\sigma_p^2 = E[(w^T R - w^T E(R))(w^T R - w^T E(R))^T]$$

**Step 5:** Factor $w^T$ out (constants come out of expectation):

$$\sigma_p^2 = w^T E[(R - E(R))(R - E(R))^T] w$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the bracketed term as the covariance matrix $\Sigma$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = w^T \Sigma w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_i w_j \sigma_{ij}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4: Sharpe's Single Index Model

**Starting Premise:** Returns of security $i$ are decomposed into market-driven (systematic) and firm-specific (unsystematic) components:

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_M + e_i$$

Where:
- $\alpha_i$ = expected excess return independent of market (firm-specific drift).
- $\beta_i R_M$ = systematic component (market-driven).
- $e_i$ = residual/idiosyncratic error, with $E(e_i) = 0$, $\text{Var}(e_i) = \sigma_{e_i}^2$, and $\text{Cov}(e_i, R_M) = 0$.

**Step 1:** Expected return from the model:

$$E(R_i) = \alpha_i + \beta_i E(R_M)$$

**Step 2:** Variance from the model:

$$\sigma_i^2 = \beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{e_i}^2$$

**Reasoning:** $\text{Var}(R_i) = \text{Var}(\beta_i R_M) + \text{Var}(e_i) = \beta_i^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sigma_{e_i}^2$ because $R_M$ and $e_i$ are uncorrelated.

**Step 3:** Covariance between two securities $i$ and $j$ under the index model:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \beta_i \beta_j \sigma_M^2$$

**Reasoning:** All covariance arises from the common market factor. The unsystematic residuals $e_i, e_j$ are assumed mutually uncorrelated.

**Step 4:** Substitute into portfolio variance formula:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_i w_j \beta_i \beta_j \sigma_M^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^2 \sigma_{e_i}^2$$

**Step 5:** Factor $\sigma_M^2$ and $\beta_p$ (where $\beta_p = \sum w_i \beta_i$):

$$\boxed{\sigma_p^2 = \beta_p^2 \sigma_M^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^2 \sigma_{e_i}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Total portfolio risk = **Systematic risk (first term)** + **Unsystematic risk (second term)**. Diversification eliminates the second term as $n \to \infty$, leaving only **systematic risk** — a foundational result of modern portfolio theory.

---

## 3.5 Derivation #5: CAPM Equilibrium (Security Market Line)

**Starting Premise:** Under equilibrium, the expected return of any risky asset must equal the return on a perfectly diversified portfolio with the same systematic risk.

**Step 1:** Consider a market portfolio $M$ with $\beta_M = 1$ and expected return $E(R_M)$.

**Step 2:** Mix the risk-free asset ($R_f$) with the market portfolio to create a portfolio with any desired $\beta$:

$$\beta_p = w_f (0) + w_M (1) = w_M$$

**Step 3:** The expected return of this mixed portfolio is:

$$E(R_p) = w_f R_f + w_M E(R_M) = (1 - \beta_p) R_f + \beta_p E(R_M)$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$E(R_p) = R_f + \beta_p [E(R_M) - R_f]$$

**Step 5:** By the **Law of One Price** (no-arbitrage), every security with the same $\beta$ must offer the same expected return, otherwise arbitrageurs would exploit the mispricing:

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_M) - R_f]}$$

This is the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** — the **risk-return equilibrium** equation that anchors all of corporate finance valuation.

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Typical 14-mark KTU question type):**

**Problem:** A portfolio consists of three stocks A, B, C with the following data:

| Stock | Weight | Expected Return | Standard Deviation | $\beta$ |
|-------|--------|------------------|---------------------|---------|
| A | 0.40 | 18% | 20% | 1.20 |
| B | 0.35 | 15% | 18% | 0.90 |
| C | 0.25 | 12% | 15% | 0.75 |

Risk-free rate $R_f = 8\%$, Market expected return $E(R_M) = 14\%$.

**Compute: (a) Portfolio Expected Return, (b) Portfolio Beta, (c) Sharpe Ratio (assuming $\sigma_p = 16.5\%$), (d) Treynor Ratio, (e) Jensen's Alpha.**

---

### Part (a) Portfolio Expected Return:

$$E(R_p) = \sum_{i} w_i E(R_i) = (0.40)(18) + (0.35)(15) + (0.25)(12)$$

$$E(R_p) = 7.20 + 5.25 + 3.00 = \boxed{15.45\%}$$

---

### Part (b) Portfolio Beta:

$$\beta_p = \sum_{i} w_i \beta_i = (0.40)(1.20) + (0.35)(0.90) + (0.25)(0.75)$$

$$\beta_p = 0.480 + 0.315 + 0.1875 = \boxed{0.9825}$$

---

### Part (c) Sharpe Ratio:

$$S_p = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p} = \frac{15.45 - 8.0}{16.5} = \frac{7.45}{16.5} = \boxed{0.4515}$$

---

### Part (d) Treynor Ratio:

$$T_p = \frac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\beta_p} = \frac{15.45 - 8.0}{0.9825} = \frac{7.45}{0.9825} = \boxed{7.582}$$

---

### Part (e) Jensen's Alpha:

$$\alpha_p = [E(R_p) - R_f] - \beta_p [E(R_M) - R_f]$$

$$\alpha_p = [15.45 - 8.0] - (0.9825)[14.0 - 8.0]$$

$$\alpha_p = 7.45 - (0.9825)(6.0) = 7.45 - 5.895 = \boxed{+1.555\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $\alpha_p = +1.555\% > 0$ implies the portfolio **outperformed** the CAPM-predicted return by 1.555 percentage points — indicating **superior management performance**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
