### B. By Legal Structure (Statutory Form)

| Type | KTU Board Definition |
|---|---|
| **Statutory Merger** | Target dissolves; acquirer survives; governed by Companies Act 2013 Sections 230–232 |
| **Subsidiary Merger** | Target becomes a wholly-owned subsidiary; survives as separate legal entity |
| **Consolidation** | Both old firms dissolve; a **brand-new entity** is formed |
| **Triangular Merger** | Acquirer creates a subsidiary, which then absorbs the target (forward/reverse triangular) |

### C. By Mode of Acquisition (Tactical Form)

| Type | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Friendly / Negotiated Acquisition** | Both boards consent; negotiated exchange ratio |
| **Hostile / Unfriendly Takeover** | Acquirer bypasses target board, approaches shareholders directly (open offer) |
| **Tender Offer** | Public offer to shareholders at a stipulated price (regulated by SEBI Takeover Regulations, 2011) |
| **Leveraged Buyout (LBO)** | Acquisition financed predominantly by debt; the target's own assets serve as collateral |

### D. By Geographic Scope

- **Domestic M&A** — within the same country (e.g., within India)
- **Cross-Border M&A** — across national borders (subject to FEMA, 1999 and RBI approvals)

### E. By Payment Mode

- **Cash Deal** — Pure cash consideration to target shareholders
- **Stock-for-Stock (Equity Swap)** — Acquirer issues its own shares to target shareholders
- **Mixed Consideration** — Combination of cash + stock + earn-outs + convertible instruments

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Sheet — Examinable Numerical Constructs**

| # | Formula / Construct | Expression | Board Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Exchange Ratio (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{P_{offer}}{P_{acquirer}}$ | Number of acquirer shares per target share |
| 2 | **Weighted Avg ER** | $ER_w = \dfrac{N_T \cdot ER}{N_A + N_T \cdot ER}$ | Post-merger shareholding % |
| 3 | **Synergy Value** | $V_{synergy} = V_{combined} - (V_A + V_T)$ | Net benefit of merger |
| 4 | **Cost of Cash Deal** | $C_{cash} = N_T \cdot P_{offer} - V_T$ | Premium paid over intrinsic value |
| 5 | **EPS Accretion / Dilution** | $\Delta EPS = \dfrac{(E_A + E_T + S - I)(1-t) - (E_A)(1-t)}{N_A + N_T \cdot ER}$ | Post-merger EPS change |
| 6 | **Post-Merger EPS** | $EPS_{PM} = \dfrac{EPS_A + EPS_T \cdot ER}{1 + ER}$ | Quick estimation in stock deals |
| 7 | **Maximum Exchange Ratio** | $ER_{max} = \dfrac{EPS_A}{EPS_T}$ | Break-even ratio (no dilution) |
| 8 | **Realized Synergy (PV)** | $PV(S) = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+WACC)^t}$ | Discounted synergy capture |
| 9 | **Break-even Premium** | $P_{BE} = \dfrac{(N_A + N_T \cdot ER) \cdot EPS_A - N_A \cdot EPS_A}{N_T}$ | Premium at which EPS is preserved |

Where:
- $N_A, N_T$ = Number of shares of acquirer / target
- $E_A, E_T$ = EBIT of acquirer / target
- $P_{offer}, P_{acquirer}$ = Offer price / Acquirer market price
- $S$ = Annual synergy, $I$ = Incremental interest, $t$ = Tax rate

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Maximum Exchange Ratio (No-Dilution Break-Even)

### Problem Setup
A KTU Board-style question typically asks: *"At what exchange ratio will the post-merger EPS exactly equal the pre-merger EPS of the acquirer?"*

Let:
- $EPS_A$ = Earnings per share of acquirer *before* merger
- $EPS_T$ = Earnings per share of target
- $ER$ = Exchange ratio (acquirer shares per target share)
- $N_A, N_T$ = Number of shares outstanding (acquirer, target)
- Tax rate and synergy assumed zero for the break-even case.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Write pre-merger EPS of the acquirer:
$$EPS_A = \dfrac{E_A}{N_A}$$

**Step 2** — Write total post-merger earnings (assuming no synergy, no incremental interest, no tax differential):
$$E_{PM} = E_A + E_T$$

**Step 3** — Write total post-merger number of shares. The acquirer issues $ER \cdot N_T$ new shares to the target shareholders:
$$N_{PM} = N_A + ER \cdot N_T$$

**Step 4** — Write post-merger EPS:
$$EPS_{PM} = \dfrac{E_A + E_T}{N_A + ER \cdot N_T}$$

**Step 5** — Apply the **break-even condition** $EPS_{PM} = EPS_A$:
$$\dfrac{E_A + E_T}{N_A + ER \cdot N_T} = \dfrac{E_A}{N_A}$$

**Step 6** — Cross-multiply:
$$(E_A + E_T) \cdot N_A = E_A \cdot (N_A + ER \cdot N_T)$$

**Step 7** — Expand the RHS:
$$E_A \cdot N_A + E_A \cdot ER \cdot N_T = E_A \cdot N_A + E_T \cdot N_A$$

**Step 8** — Cancel $E_A \cdot N_A$ from both sides:
$$E_A \cdot ER \cdot N_T = E_T \cdot N_A$$

**Step 9** — Solve for ER. Note that $EPS_A = E_A/N_A$ and $EPS_T = E_T/N_T$, so $E_A = EPS_A \cdot N_A$ and $E_T = EPS_T \cdot N_T$:
$$ER \cdot EPS_A \cdot N_A \cdot N_T = EPS_T \cdot N_T \cdot N_A$$

**Step 10** — Cancel $N_A \cdot N_T$ (assuming non-zero):
$$ER \cdot EPS_A = EPS_T$$

**Step 11** — Final closed-form result:
$$\boxed{ER_{max} = \dfrac{EPS_T}{EPS_A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip**: Any ER *below* $EPS_T / EPS_A$ causes **EPS dilution**; any ER *above* causes **EPS accretion** for the acquirer. This is a 14-mark favourite.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Post-Merger EPS with Synergy & Tax

### Problem Setup
A more advanced KTU 14-mark question introduces **synergy $S$**, **incremental interest $I$** on cash debt, and **tax rate $t$**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Combine pre-tax earnings:
$$EBIT_{PM} = EBIT_A + EBIT_T + S$$

**Step 2** — Subtract incremental interest:
$$EBT_{PM} = EBIT_A + EBIT_T + S - I$$

**Step 3** — Apply tax:
$$E_{PM} = (EBIT_A + EBIT_T + S - I)(1 - t)$$

**Step 4** — Total post-merger shares:
$$N_{PM} = N_A + ER \cdot N_T$$

**Step 5** — Post-merger EPS:
$$EPS_{PM} = \dfrac{(EBIT_A + EBIT_T + S - I)(1 - t)}{N_A + ER \cdot N_T}$$

**Step 6** — Compare against pre-merger $EPS_A$:
$$\Delta EPS = EPS_{PM} - EPS_A$$

> The merger is **accretive** if $\Delta EPS > 0$, **dilutive** if $\Delta EPS < 0$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU Typical Style)

**Question (KTU-typical 14-mark adaptation)**:
Acquirer Co. has $N_A = 10{,}000$ shares, $EBIT_A = \text{Rs. } 2{,}00{,}000$, tax rate $t = 30\%$.
Target Co. has $N_T = 5{,}000$ shares, $EBIT_T = \text{Rs. } 1{,}00{,}000$.
Expected synergy $S = \text{Rs. } 20{,}000$ per year, no incremental debt ($I = 0$).
Offered exchange ratio $ER = 0.6$. Find post-merger EPS and verify accretion/dilution.

### Solution

**Step 1** — Pre-merger EPS of Acquirer:
$$EPS_A = \dfrac{E_A}{N_A} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.30)}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{1{,}40{,}000}{10{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 14.00$$

**Step 2** — Combined post-tax earnings:
$$E_{PM} = (2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 - 0)(1 - 0.30)$$

$$E_{PM} = (3{,}20{,}000)(0.70) = \text{Rs. } 2{,}24{,}000$$

**Step 3** — Post-merger share count:
$$N_{PM} = 10{,}000 + (0.6 \times 5{,}000) = 10{,}000 + 3{,}000 = 13{,}000$$

**Step 4** — Post-merger EPS:
$$EPS_{PM} = \dfrac{2{,}24{,}000}{13{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 17.23$$

**Step 5** — Accretion:
$$\Delta EPS = 17.23 - 14.00 = +\text{Rs. } 3.23 \quad (\text{accretive})$$

**Step 6** — Accretion %:
$$\text{Accretion \%} = \dfrac{3.23}{14.00} \times 100 = 23.07\%$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of M&A (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["MERGERS AND ACQUISITIONS"]
    
    rootA --> nodeB["BY NATURE OF COMBINATION"]
    rootA --> nodeC["BY LEGAL STRUCTURE"]
    rootA --> nodeD["BY MODE OF ACQUISITION"]
    rootA --> nodeE["BY GEOGRAPHIC SCOPE"]
    rootA --> nodeF["BY PAYMENT MODE"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["Horizontal Merger"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Vertical Merger"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Conglomerate Merger"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Congeneric Merger"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["Statutory Merger"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Subsidiary Merger"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Consolidation"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["Triangular Merger"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["Friendly Acquisition"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Hostile Takeover"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Tender Offer"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["Leveraged Buyout"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["Domestic M&A"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Cross-Border M&A"]

    nodeF --> nodeF1["Cash Deal"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["Stock-for-Stock Swap"]
    nodeF --> nodeF3["Mixed Consideration"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — M&A Decision Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["Strategic Motivation"]
    stepB["Target Identification"]
    stepC["Due Diligence"]
    stepD["Valuation Modeling"]
    stepE["Exchange Ratio Determination"]
    stepF["Scheme Drafting"]
    stepG["NCLT / CCI Approval"]
    stepH["Post-Merger Integration"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: Every M&A transaction flows sequentially through *Motivation → Identification → Due Diligence → Valuation → Exchange Ratio → Scheme Drafting → Regulatory Approval → Integration*. KTU 2024 expects students to identify *which classification* applies at each step (e.g., a hostile acquisition skips Step F's friendly negotiation).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**Distinguish between a Merger and an Acquisition with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Basis | Merger | Acquisition |
|---|---|---|
| Legal Status | Target ceases to exist; new/surviving entity emerges | Target continues as separate legal entity |
| Control | Mutual, via share swap | One-sided, by acquirer |
| Mode | Share-for-share exchange | Cash / open offer |
| Example | Two firms combining into a new entity | Holding company buying 51% stake |

*Mark split*: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for distinguishing feature/example.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**What is a Conglomerate Merger? How does it differ from a Horizontal Merger?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- A **Conglomerate Merger** combines firms in **unrelated lines of business** (e.g., an FMCG firm merging with a hotel chain). Its purpose is **diversification and risk reduction**.
- A **Horizontal Merger** combines firms in the **same industry and same product line** (e.g., two banks). Its purpose is **market-share expansion and synergy in core operations**.
- Key difference: *industry overlap vs. non-overlap*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain the **various classifications of Mergers** with examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** ABC Ltd. proposes to acquire XYZ Ltd. in a stock-for-stock deal. Given:
- ABC Ltd.: $N_A = 20{,}000$ shares, $EBIT_A = \text{Rs. } 5{,}00{,}000$
- XYZ Ltd.: $N_T = 10{,}000$ shares, $EBIT_T = \text{Rs. } 2{,}00{,}000$
- Tax rate $t = 30\%$, Synergy $S = \text{Rs. } 50{,}000$, $I = 0$
- Proposed $ER = 0.5$

Calculate the **post-merger EPS**, **accretion / dilution**, and the **maximum exchange ratio** at which the deal remains non-dilutive. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Pre-merger EPS of ABC**:
$$EPS_A = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.30)}{20{,}000} = \dfrac{3{,}50{,}000}{20{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 17.50$$

**Step 2 — Combined post-tax earnings**:
$$E_{PM} = (5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000)(0.70) = (7{,}50{,}000)(0.70) = \text{Rs. } 5{,}25{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Post-merger shares**:
$$N_{PM} = 20{,}000 + (0.5 \times 10{,}000) = 25{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Post-merger EPS**:
$$EPS_{PM} = \dfrac{5{,}25{,}000}{25{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 21.00$$

**Step 5 — Accretion**:
$$\Delta EPS = 21.00 - 17.50 = +\text{Rs. } 3.50$$

$$\text{Accretion \%} = \dfrac{3.50}{17.50} \times 100 = 20\%$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Exchange Ratio (no dilution)**:
$$ER_{max} = \dfrac{EPS_T}{EPS_A} = \dfrac{(2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 / 10{,}000)}{17.50} = \dfrac{14.00}{17.50} = 0.80$$

Since proposed $ER = 0.5 < 0.80$, the deal is **accretive**. ✔

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Alternative Module Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the **motivations and rationale** behind Mergers and Acquisitions. Differentiate between **friendly** and **hostile** takeovers. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A target firm has $N_T = 4{,}000$ shares and EPS of Rs. 20. The acquirer has $N_A = 16{,}000$ shares and EPS of Rs. 10. The merger is expected to generate synergy of Rs. 80,000 per year (pre-tax) and the tax rate is 40%. No incremental debt. **Determine the range of exchange ratios** for which the merger is EPS-accretive to the acquirer. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Step 1 — Acquirer's pre-merger post-tax earnings**:
$$E_A = EPS_A \times N_A = 10 \times 16{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Target's pre-merger post-tax earnings**:
$$E_T = EPS_T \times N_T = 20 \times 4{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 80{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Synergy post-tax**:
$$S_{after-tax} = 80{,}000 \times (1 - 0.40) = \text{Rs. } 48{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Total post-merger earnings**:
$$E_{PM} = 1{,}60{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 48{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}88{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Accretive condition**:
$$\dfrac{2{,}88{,}000}{16{,}000 + 4{,}000 \cdot ER} \geq 10$$

**Step 6 — Cross-multiply**:
$$2{,}88{,}000 \geq 10 \times (16{,}000 + 4{,}000 \cdot ER)$$

$$2{,}88{,}000 \geq 1{,}60{,}000 + 40{,}000 \cdot ER$$

**Step 7 — Solve**:
$$40{,}000 \cdot ER \leq 1{,}28{,}000$$

$$ER \leq \dfrac{1{,}28{,}000}{40{,}000} = 3.20$$

**Result**: The merger is **EPS-accretive for $ER \leq 3.20$**. ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Confusing EPS with EBT**: Always tax-adjust EBIT before computing EPS. Many students forget `(1 - t)` and lose 2 marks instantly.
> - **Missing the synergy term $S$**: In stock-for-stock friendly deals, examiners explicitly include synergy; omitting it drops the result by Rs. 1–3 per share.
> - **Using pre-merger instead of combined EPS**: The condition is $EPS_{PM} \geq EPS_A$, *not* $EPS_{PM} \geq EPS_T$.
> - **Exchange ratio direction**: $ER = EPS_T / EPS_A$ is for *break-even*; for accretion in the *opposite* direction (lower ER), always re-derive explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Definitions & Classifications**

- ✔ A **merger** dissolves legal individuality; an **acquisition** transfers control while the target survives.
- ✔ Five classification axes: **Nature, Legal, Mode, Geography, Payment**.
- ✔ **Horizontal** = same industry; **Vertical** = supply-chain link; **Conglomerate** = unrelated.
- ✔ **Friendly** = board consent; **Hostile** = direct shareholder approach (open offer under SEBI 2011).
- ✔ **Statutory merger** is governed by **Sections 230–232, Companies Act 2013**.
- ✔ **Exchange Ratio (ER)** is the heart of every stock-deal valuation question.
- ✔ **Break-even condition**: $ER_{max} = EPS_T / EPS_A$ (ignoring synergy).
- ✔ **Accretive deal** ⟹ post-merger EPS > pre-merger EPS of acquirer.
- ✔ **Synergy** = $V_{combined} - (V_A + V_T)$; positive synergy is the *raison d'être* of M&A.
- ✔ **NCLT** = National Company Law Tribunal (Indian approval authority).
- ✔ **CCI** = Competition Commission of India (anti-trust clearance for combinations above threshold).
- ✔ In exam answers, always **state the assumption** (no synergy, no tax, etc.) before writing the formula.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motives for Merger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motives for Merger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: MOTIVES FOR MERGER — CORE DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **MERGER (Statutory Definition):**
> A merger is a strategic corporate restructuring transaction in which two or more independent firms combine their operations, assets, liabilities, and equity ownership to operate as a single unified legal and economic entity, with the objective of creating value greater than the sum of their individual standalone values.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET744)** framework, a merger is studied as a **capital budgeting decision** of the highest order — a firm either chooses to *grow internally* (organic expansion via capital expenditure) or *grow externally* (inorganic expansion via M&A). The decision rule follows standard NPV logic:

$$\text{NPV of Merger} = V_{AB} - (V_A + V_B) - C_M$$

Where:
- $V_{AB}$ = Value of the combined (merged) firm
- $V_A, V_B$ = Standalone values of the merging firms
- $C_M$ = Total cost of the merger (transaction costs, integration costs, premium paid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 GOLDEN RULE OF MERGER:**
> A merger is *financially justified* **if and only if** the **synergy value** $\Delta V = V_{AB} - (V_A + V_B)$ is **strictly greater than** the premium paid to the target plus transaction costs. Otherwise, the acquisition destroys shareholder wealth, regardless of strategic narrative.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Story)

Imagine two neighbouring tea stalls in Kerala — *Chai Kada A* run by **Ramesh** and *Chai Kada B* run by **Suresh**. Both sell tea at ₹15 a cup. Their costs, customers, and supply chains are nearly identical.

### Scenario 1 — Stay Separate
Each earns ₹30,000/month. Combined, their family income is **₹60,000/month**. They compete, sometimes undercut each other, and pay rent on two shops.

### Scenario 2 — Merge
Ramesh and Suresh combine into one stall. They:
1. **Share one shop** → rent halves → savings of ₹8,000/month *(Economies of Scale)*
2. **Buy milk in bulk** → unit cost drops 15% → savings of ₹5,000/month *(Operational Synergy)*
3. **Stop competing** → can raise price to ₹18 → extra revenue ₹10,000/month *(Market Power)*
4. **Pool savings** → can now buy an auto for delivery → new revenue ₹15,000/month *(Growth Motive)*

**Combined new income ≈ ₹98,000/month**, far exceeding the ₹60,000 they had separately.

This *extra value created* (₹98,000 − ₹60,000 = **₹38,000**) is the **SYNERGY** — the cornerstone motive of every merger. KTU examiners expect students to identify synergy in *every* merger valuation problem.

---

## 1.3 Comprehensive Classification of Merger Motives (Syllabus Map)

For the KTU board exam, merger motives are classified into **four broad categories**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE FOUR PILLARS OF MERGER MOTIVES:**
>
> | S.No. | Motive Category | Core Question Answered | KTU Priority |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Synergy & Operational** | Will combined cost/revenue exceed the sum? | ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH |
> | 2 | **Growth & Strategic** | Is this the fastest/cheapest path to scale? | ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH |
> | 3 | **Financial & Tax** | Does it lower WACC, boost EPS, save tax? | ⭐⭐ MEDIUM |
> | 4 | **Managerial / Hubris** | Are managers acting in shareholders' interest? | ⭐⭐ MEDIUM |

The detailed taxonomy of each motive is unfolded in **SECTION 2**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — SYNERGY MOTIVES (Operational, Financial, Market)

Synergy is the **mother of all merger motives**. Without synergy, a merger is mathematically unjustifiable.

### 2.1.1 Operational Synergy (Cost-Side)

Operational synergy arises when the merged firm can **produce the combined output at a total cost lower than the sum of standalone costs**.

$$C_{AB}(Q_A + Q_B) < C_A(Q_A) + C_B(Q_B)$$

**Sources:**
- **Economies of Scale** — Fixed costs (R&D, plant, headquarters) spread over larger output.
- **Economies of Scope** — One input (brand, technology, distribution channel) used to produce multiple products.
- **Elimination of Duplication** — Layoffs, closure of overlapping branches/plants.

### 2.1.2 Financial Synergy (Capital-Side)

Financial synergy comes from a **stronger combined balance sheet**:

$$k_{AB} < w \cdot k_A + (1-w) \cdot k_B$$

i.e., the **WACC of the combined firm is lower** than the weighted average of standalone WACCs. Causes:

- **Lower cost of debt** (larger firms have higher credit ratings, get cheaper loans).
- **Lower cost of equity** (diversification reduces non-systematic risk → lower $\beta$ → lower $k_e$).
- **Higher debt capacity** (more stable combined cash flows support more leverage).
- **Better access to capital markets** (larger firms tap IPOs, FPOs, ADRs more easily).

### 2.1.3 Market / Revenue Synergy (Top-Line)

Revenue synergy occurs when the combined firm generates **higher combined revenue than the sum of individual revenues**:

$$R_{AB} > R_A + R_B$$

Mechanisms:
- **Cross-selling** (banking + insurance bundling — classic HDFC example).
- **Improved market power / pricing power**.
- **Enhanced brand reach and distribution**.
- **Block entry of competitors** (defensive merger).

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — GROWTH & STRATEGIC MOTIVES

### 2.2.1 Faster Growth than Organic Expansion

A firm wanting to grow by 30% may need 5–7 years via internal capex. A merger achieves this in **one transaction**.

$$\text{Growth Rate}_{\text{Inorganic}} \gg \text{Growth Rate}_{\text{Organic}}$$

### 2.2.2 Diversification (Risk Reduction)

If firm A is in cyclical *steel* and firm B is in defensive *FMCG*, the merged firm has **lower variance of earnings**.

$$\sigma_{AB}^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

If $\rho_{AB} < 1$ (low correlation), portfolio variance falls. This is the **MM Diversification Theorem** applied at the corporate level — though MM argued that shareholders can diversify *personally*, the **bankruptcy cost reduction** and **managerial risk reduction** still justify corporate diversification.

### 2.2.3 Vertical Integration Motive

Merging with a **supplier** (backward integration) or a **distributor** (forward integration) eliminates double marginalization.

$$\text{Double Margin Elimination} \Rightarrow \text{Lower Total Cost to Channel}$$

Examples: Tata Steel + Tata Mining (backward), Amazon + Whole Foods (forward).

### 2.2.4 Horizontal vs Vertical vs Conglomerate Merger

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU EXAM FAVOURITE — CLASSIFICATION TABLE:**
>
> | Type | Parties Involved | Strategic Logic | Example |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Horizontal** | Competitors in same industry | Market power, scale | HDFC + HDFC Bank (reverse) |
> | **Vertical** | Firms in same supply chain | Control, eliminate margin | Tata Power + coal mine |
> | **Conglomerate** | Unrelated businesses | Diversification, capital allocation | ITC (FMCG + Hotels + Paper) |
> | **Congeneric** | Related industries, different products | Scope economies | P&G acquiring Gillette |

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — FINANCIAL & TAX MOTIVES

### 2.3.1 EPS Accretion / Dilution Motive

Managers love mergers that **accretive to EPS** in Year 1, because the market often reacts positively to EPS growth (even if value-destructive).

$$EPS_{AB} = \frac{NI_A + NI_B + \Delta NI_{\text{synergy}}}{N_A + N_{\text{new shares}}}$$

If $EPS_{AB} > EPS_A$, the merger is **accretive**. But KTU examiners will mark this as **insufficient** — accretion is an accounting effect, not value creation.

### 2.3.2 Tax Shield / Loss Carry-Forward

A profitable firm acquires a loss-making firm to **set off target's losses against its own profits**, reducing tax outflow.

$$\text{Tax Saved} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (L_t \times \tau)$$

Where $L_t$ = loss of target firm in year $t$, $\tau$ = corporate tax rate, $T$ = number of years losses can be carried forward (typically **8 years** under Indian Income Tax Act).

### 2.3.3 Undervaluation Hypothesis

If the target firm's market price is **less than its intrinsic value** ($P_B < V_B$), the acquirer gets a "bargain purchase":

$$\text{Bargain Value} = V_B - P_B \quad (\text{added to synergy})$$

This is the classic **Tobin’s Q** based motive:

$$q = \frac{\text{Market Value}}{\text{Replacement Value}}$$

If $q_B < 1$, the target is cheap to acquire — classic motive for takeover.

### 2.3.4 Surplus Cash Hypothesis (Jensen, 1986)

Firms with **large free cash flows** and few positive-NPV projects are tempted to deploy cash via acquisitions (often value-destructive) rather than pay dividends. This is **agency cost** manifesting as a motive.

---

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — MANAGERIAL & BEHAVIOURAL MOTIVES

### 2.4.1 Hubris Hypothesis (Richard Roll, 1986)

> [!NOTE]
> **HUBRIS THEORY (Roll, 1986):**
> "Takeovers are the result of mistakes caused by over-confident managers who overestimate their ability to create value." — *Richard Roll, Journal of Finance*

In Roll’s view, **every merger premium paid is an over-payment due to managerial overconfidence**, and synergies are largely imagined. This is the **sceptical academic view**.

### 2.4.2 Empire Building

Managers derive private benefits (status, salary, perquisites) from managing a **larger firm**, even if the merger is NPV-negative. This is the classic **Agency Theory** critique.

$$\text{Manager's Utility} = f(\text{Firm Size}, \text{Salary}, \text{Power})$$

### 2.4.3 Defence Against Takeover

Managers may initiate a **"Pac-Man" or "Poison Pill" merger** to make the firm too large or too complex to be acquired.

### 2.4.4 Window Dressing

Managers may merge just before year-end to **boost consolidated revenue/asset figures** for short-term market signalling — this is **NOT a value motive**.

---

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THESE 8 FORMULAS BEFORE THE EXAM — DIRECTLY ASKED IN KTU 2024 ESE**

| S.No. | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | $\Delta V = V_{AB} - V_A - V_B$ | Synergy Value | Deciding if merger is justified |
| **F2** | $\text{NPV}_{\text{merger}} = \Delta V - C_M$ | Merger Net Present Value | Accept/Reject decision |
| **F3** | $ER = \frac{P_{\text{offer}}}{P_B}$ | Exchange Ratio | Determining share-swap ratio |
| **F4** | $ER_{\max} = \frac{V_B + \Delta V - C_M}{N_A \cdot P_A}$ | Maximum Exchange Ratio | Cap on how much acquirer can offer |
| **F5** | $EPS_{AB} = \frac{NI_A + NI_B + \Delta NI}{N_A + N_{\text{new}}}$ | Merged EPS | Accretion/Dilution test |
| **F6** | $k_{AB} < w_A k_A + w_B k_B$ | Financial Synergy Condition | Capital cost reduction |
| **F7** | $q_B = \frac{MV_B}{RV_B} < 1$ | Tobin's Q test | Identifying undervalued targets |
| **F8** | $\text{Tax Savings} = \sum_{t=1}^{8} L_t \cdot \tau$ | Loss Set-off Benefit | Tax-motivated merger valuation |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** In all KTU merger numericals, students must **always deduct transaction costs** ($C_M$) from synergy value before concluding that the merger creates wealth. Skipping $C_M$ costs **2 to 3 marks** — a common valuation trap.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Industry Applications

| Industry | Real Merger | Dominant Motive | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indian Banking | HDFC + HDFC Bank (2023) | Cross-selling synergy | Created India's largest private bank by m-cap |
| Indian Telecom | Vodafone + Idea (2018) | Market power, survival | Reduced from 4-player to 3-player market |
| Pharma | Sun Pharma + Ranbaxy (2014) | Market reach, scale | Became India's largest pharma + global top 5 |
| Cement | UltraTech + various (2014–22) | Market share leadership | Now >25% of Indian capacity |
| E-commerce | Flipkart + PhonePe (2022) | Vertical separation, focus | Unlocked value via separate listings |
| IT (Failed) | Wipro + Spectramind (2002) | Service diversification | Disappointing — clients lost confidence |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Synergy Value Formula

**Starting Premise:** Two firms $A$ (acquirer) and $B$ (target) exist as separate entities with standalone values $V_A$ and $V_B$. After merger, they operate as a combined firm $AB$.

### Step 1: Define Standalone Values

By the discounted cash flow model, the standalone value of each firm is the present value of its expected future free cash flows.

$$V_A = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{FCF_{A,t}}{(1+k_A)^t}$$

$$V_B = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{FCF_{B,t}}{(1+k_B)^t}$$

### Step 2: Define Combined Firm Value

After merger, the combined firm generates $FCF_{AB,t}$, which is **not equal** to $FCF_{A,t} + FCF_{B,t}$ because of synergy.

$$V_{AB} = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{FCF_{AB,t}}{(1+k_{AB})^t}$$

Where $FCF_{AB,t} = FCF_{A,t} + FCF_{B,t} + \Delta FCF_{\text{synergy},t}$

### Step 3: Expand the Synergy Term

The synergy cash flow has two components:

$$\Delta FCF_{\text{synergy},t} = \Delta FCF_{\text{operational},t} + \Delta FCF_{\text{financial},t} + \Delta FCF_{\text{revenue},t}$$

### Step 4: Subtract to Get Pure Synergy Value

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{\text{synergy}} &= V_{AB} - (V_A + V_B) \\
&= \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{\Delta FCF_{\text{synergy},t}}{(1+k_{AB})^t} + \text{NPV effect of lower } k_{AB}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **gross synergy** before subtracting the cost of achieving it.

### Step 5: Net Synergy to the Acquirer

The acquirer (firm $A$) captures only a portion of total synergy — the rest goes to firm $B$ as **merger premium**.

$$\text{Net Synergy}_A = \Delta V_{\text{synergy}} - \text{Premium Paid} - C_M$$

$$\text{Premium Paid} = P_{\text{offer}} - V_B$$

Where $P_{\text{offer}}$ is the actual price paid per share of $B$ (in cash or stock value).

### Step 6: Final Accept/Reject Rule

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{\text{merger to A}} &= V_{AB} - V_A - P_{\text{offer}} - C_M \\
&= \Delta V_{\text{synergy}} - \text{Premium Paid} - C_M
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision Rule:**

$$\begin{cases}
\text{NPV} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPT merger (creates value for A's shareholders)} \\
\text{NPV} \leq 0 \Rightarrow \text{REJECT merger (destroys value)}
\end{cases}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Exchange Ratio (KTU Numerical Favourite)

**Scenario:** Acquirer $A$ issues its own shares to acquire target $B$ in a **stock-for-stock merger**. The exchange ratio (ER) determines how many of $A$'s shares are given per share of $B$.

### Step 1: Define Exchange Ratio

$$ER = \frac{\text{Number of } A\text{'s shares issued}}{\text{Number of } B\text{'s shares outstanding}}$$

### Step 2: Value of Shares Issued to B's Shareholders

$$\text{Total Value Given} = ER \times N_B \times P_A$$

Where $N_B$ = number of $B$'s shares, $P_A$ = current market price of $A$'s share.

### Step 3: Apply the Value Conservation Constraint

For A's existing shareholders to be **no worse off**, the value given must not exceed the value received plus synergy minus costs:

$$ER \times N_B \times P_A \leq V_B + \Delta V - C_M$$

### Step 4: Solve for Maximum ER

$$\begin{aligned}
ER \times N_B \times P_A &= V_B + \Delta V - C_M \\
ER_{\max} &= \frac{V_B + \Delta V - C_M}{N_B \times P_A}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU EXAM TRAP:** Many students forget to subtract $C_M$ (transaction/integration costs) in the numerator. This inflates $ER_{\max}$ and leads to overpaying. Examiner's comment will be: *"Failed to account for cost of merger — answer is incorrect."*

### Step 5: Refine Using Per-Share Terms

Since $V_B / N_B = V_B^*$ (value per share of $B$) and $P_A$ is value per share of $A$:

$$ER_{\max} = \frac{V_B^* + (\Delta V - C_M)/N_B}{P_A}$$

This is the **theoretical ceiling** — any $ER > ER_{\max}$ destroys value for acquirer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — EPS Accretion / Dilution Test

### Step 1: Pre-Merger EPS of Acquirer

$$EPS_A = \frac{NI_A}{N_A}$$

### Step 2: Post-Merger Net Income

$$NI_{AB} = NI_A + NI_B + \Delta NI_{\text{synergy}}$$

$\Delta NI_{\text{synergy}}$ = After-tax synergy gain (operating cost savings + revenue gains − incremental depreciation − incremental interest on new debt).

### Step 3: Post-Merger Share Count

In a stock-for-stock merger, new shares issued = $ER \times N_B$:

$$N_{AB} = N_A + (ER \times N_B)$$

### Step 4: Merged EPS

$$EPS_{AB} = \frac{NI_A + NI_B + \Delta NI_{\text{synergy}}}{N_A + ER \times N_B}$$

### Step 5: Test for Accretion

$$\text{Accretive if } EPS_{AB} > EPS_A$$

$$\frac{NI_A + NI_B + \Delta NI}{N_A + ER \cdot N_B} > \frac{NI_A}{N_A}$$

### Step 6: Cross-Multiply (assuming all terms positive)

$$N_A (NI_A + NI_B + \Delta NI) > NI_A (N_A + ER \cdot N_B)$$

$$N_A \cdot NI_B + N_A \cdot \Delta NI > NI_A \cdot ER \cdot N_B$$

$$\frac{NI_B + \Delta NI}{ER \cdot N_B} > \frac{NI_A}{N_A}$$

$$\frac{EPS_B^{*} + \Delta NI/N_B}{ER} > EPS_A$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER WARNING:** Accretion in **Year 1** does **NOT** guarantee value creation. The proper test is the **NPV of merger**, not EPS accretion. A merger can be EPS-accretive but value-destructive (because the acquirer paid too high a price). Always cross-check with NPV.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Motive vs Evidence Required

For each motive, the KTU examiner expects students to state **what evidence would prove/disprove** it. This is a **high-yield answer framework** for 14-mark questions:

| Motive Claimed | Evidence Required to Verify | KPI to Measure |
|---|---|---|
| Operational Synergy | Cost saving breakdown | % reduction in unit cost, headcount, plant closures |
| Financial Synergy | Combined WACC fall | $k_{AB}$ vs $w_A k_A + w_B k_B$ |
| Revenue Synergy | Cross-sell pipeline | Cross-sell revenue / total revenue |
| Market Power | Post-merger HHI index | Δ in market concentration |
| Diversification | Correlation of cash flows | $\rho_{AB}$ between $A$ and $B$ |
| Tax Motive | Target's losses usable | Tax shield PV |
| EPS Accretion | Year 1 + Year 3 EPS | Δ EPS % |
| Undervaluation | Tobin's Q of target | $q_B = MV / RV$ |
| Managerial Hubris | Premium paid vs synergy | Premium / $\Delta V$ ratio |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:**
Firm $A$ (NI = ₹500 cr, Shares = 100 cr) is acquiring Firm $B$ (NI = ₹150 cr, Shares = 50 cr) via stock swap at $ER = 0.6$. Annual synergy gain = ₹80 cr, integration cost = ₹20 cr (one-time), tax rate = 30%.

**(i) Calculate pre-merger EPS of A and B.**

$$EPS_A = \frac{500}{100} = ₹5.00 \quad ; \quad EPS_B = \frac{150}{50} = ₹3.00$$

**(ii) Calculate post-merger share count.**

$$N_{AB} = 100 + (0.6 \times 50) = 100 + 30 = 130 \text{ cr shares}$$

**(iii) Calculate post-merger net income.**

$$NI_{AB} = 500 + 150 + (80 \times 0.7) = 500 + 150 + 56 = ₹706 \text{ cr}$$

*(Synergy gain is after-tax = $80 \times (1 - 0.30) = 56$ cr)*

*(Integration cost ignored for steady-state EPS — it would reduce Year 1 only)*

**(iv) Calculate merged EPS.**

$$EPS_{AB} = \frac{706}{130} = ₹5.43$$

**(v) Accretion/Dilution Test.**

$$\text{Accretion} = \frac{5.43 - 5.00}{5.00} \times 100\% = +8.6\% \text{ Accretive ✓}$$

**(vi) Verification via NPV logic.**

Assuming perpetual synergy cash flow after tax = ₹56 cr, and acquirer's cost of capital = 12%:

$$PV_{\text{synergy}} = \frac{56}{0.12} = ₹467 \text{ cr}$$

Value given to B = $30 \text{ cr shares} \times ₹5 = ₹150$ cr (assuming A's price stays ₹5).

$$NPV_{\text{merger}} = 467 - 150 = ₹317 \text{ cr} \quad (\text{Strongly value-creative})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU BOARD TIP:** Always conclude with an explicit "NPV > 0, hence merger is recommended" sentence. Examiners award the final **decision mark** only when this explicit verdict is given.

# MODULE 3: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

> **"Distinguish between Horizontal, Vertical, and Conglomerate mergers with one example each. Which type is most likely to face antitrust scrutiny and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Parties Involved | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal** | Competitors in the same product/geography | Vodafone + Idea Cellular (telecom, 2018) |
| **Vertical** | Firms in the same supply chain (supplier or buyer) | Tata Steel acquiring coal mines (backward) |
| **Conglomerate** | Unrelated businesses | ITC's diversification into FMCG, paper, hotels |

**Antitrust scrutiny:** **Horizontal mergers** are most likely to face antitrust scrutiny because they reduce the number of competitors in the same market, increase concentration (HHI), and may lead to monopolistic pricing. Example: Competition Commission of India (CCI) scrutinizing the Vodafone–Idea merger for market share.

*(Examiner's distribution: 1 mark for each definition + example; 1 mark for antitrust reasoning.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **"Explain the Hubris Hypothesis of mergers as proposed by Richard Roll (1986). Why is it considered a managerial motive rather than a value motive?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

According to **Richard Roll's Hubris Hypothesis (Journal of Finance, 1986)**, mergers are largely the result of **managerial overconfidence** — managers systematically overestimate their ability to create synergy and underestimate the price they are paying for the target. Roll argued that the **entire merger premium** paid by acquirers is essentially an over-payment caused by hubris, not justified by real synergy.

**Why it is a managerial, not value motive:** Hubris benefits the manager (reputation, empire size, bonuses tied to firm scale) rather than the acquirer's shareholders. It violates the fundamental rule that **NPV of merger > 0** is required. Thus, hubris-driven mergers are often **value-destructive** despite management's genuine belief that they are creating value.

*(Examiner's distribution: 1.5 marks for explaining Roll's theory; 1.5 marks for distinguishing managerial vs value motive.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 REGULATION:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **sub-parts (a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part requires full working/steps.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted from Dec 2022]**

#### **(a) Explain any seven motives for merger with one real-world example for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Synergy Creation (Operational)** — Combined firm has lower costs; *HDFC + HDFC Bank* reduced overlapping branches.
2. **Market Power / Increased Market Share** — *Vodafone + Idea* gave the merged entity ~35% Indian telecom market share.
3. **Diversification of Risk** — *ITC* diversified into FMCG, hotels, paper to reduce tobacco dependence.
4. **Vertical Integration** — *Reliance* acquired its own retail and petrochemical chain to control supply.
5. **Tax Benefits** — Acquisition of loss-making firms to set off losses; *Aditya Birla Group* acquired Australian coal mines for tax-efficient structure.
6. **Faster Growth than Organic Expansion** — *Flipkart* grew faster than would have been possible by building warehouses organically.
7. **Resource and Talent Acquisition** — *Microsoft + LinkedIn (2016)* to acquire talent and data assets.
8. **Undervaluation of Target** — Acquired firms with Tobin's Q < 1; classic Buffett strategy.
9. **Surplus Cash Utilization (Jensen, 1986)** — Companies with excess FCF use M&A instead of paying dividends.
10. **Defence Against Hostile Takeover** — Becoming large reduces probability of being a target.

*(Examiner's distribution: 7 motives × 0.5 mark for explanation + 0.5 mark for example = 7 marks.)*

#### **(b) Firm X is considering the acquisition of Firm Y. The following data is given:**

> - Standalone values: $V_X = ₹2{,}000$ cr, $V_Y = ₹800$ cr
> - Combined firm value: $V_{XY} = ₹3{,}100$ cr
> - Premium offered to Y's shareholders: ₹300 cr
> - Transaction and integration costs: $C_M = ₹150$ cr
>
> **Calculate: (i) Gross Synergy, (ii) Net Synergy to Firm X, (iii) Whether the merger is value-accretive, and (iv) Maximum premium Firm X should have offered to remain value-neutral. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer with Full Steps:**

**(i) Gross Synergy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{\text{synergy}} &= V_{XY} - V_X - V_Y \\
&= 3{,}100 - 2{,}000 - 800 \\
&= ₹300 \text{ cr}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Net Synergy to Firm X:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Synergy}_X &= \Delta V_{\text{synergy}} - \text{Premium} - C_M \\
&= 300 - 300 - 150 \\
&= -₹150 \text{ cr}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Decision:**

Since **Net Synergy is negative (₹−150 cr)**, the merger is **NOT value-accretive** to Firm X's shareholders. It should be **REJECTED** or the **premium renegotiated downward**.

*(Alternative equivalent: $\text{NPV}_{\text{merger}} = V_{XY} - V_X - \text{Premium} - C_M = 3{,}100 - 2{,}000 - 300 - 150 = ₹650$ cr. Note: this is the value created **in total**, of which Y's shareholders get ₹300 cr premium, leaving ₹650 − 300 − 150 = ₹200 cr for X. Wait, let me re-check:)*

**Reconciliation (Correct Approach):**

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{merger to X}} = V_{XY} - V_X - (\text{Premium paid to Y}) - C_M$$

$$= 3{,}100 - 2{,}000 - 300 - 150 = ₹650 \text{ cr} \quad (\text{Value-accretive})$$

**Total synergy to be split:** ₹300 cr gross synergy + ₹100 cr extra combined value (since $V_{XY} = 3{,}100$ vs $V_X + V_Y = 2{,}800$ → real synergy = ₹300 cr).

Wait — by definition, synergy = $V_{XY} - V_X - V_Y = ₹300$ cr. So total value created = ₹300 cr.

Value captured by **Y's shareholders** = ₹300 cr premium (they get paid ₹1,100 instead of ₹800 standalone → gain = ₹300 cr).

Value captured by **X's shareholders** = ₹0 cr synergy − ₹150 cr cost = **−₹150 cr** (value destroyed).

Therefore, **the merger is value-destructive for X**. X should renegotiate.

**(iv) Maximum Premium for Value-Neutrality:**

For value-neutrality, X's net synergy = 0:

$$\text{Premium}_{\max} = \Delta V_{\text{synergy}} - C_M = 300 - 150 = ₹150 \text{ cr}$$

So the **maximum premium X should offer** is **₹150 cr**, not ₹300 cr.

**Final Verdict (1 mark):** Merger as proposed **destroys value** by ₹150 cr; renegotiate premium to ≤₹150 cr or abandon.

*(Examiner's distribution: 1.5 marks each for parts (i)–(iv). Final verdict: 1 mark.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted from June 2023]**

#### **(a) Explain the three types of synergy (Operational, Financial, Revenue) with examples. How is each quantified in a merger valuation model? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Operational Synergy:**
- **Definition:** Cost savings from combining operations of two firms.
- **Sources:** Plant rationalization, headcount reduction, procurement scale, R&D consolidation.
- **Example:** *HDFC + HDFC Bank* shared branches and back-office, saving ~₹500 cr annually.
- **Quantification:** Estimate annual cost savings (ΔCOGS + ΔOpEx) → tax-adjust → discount at $k_{AB}$ over the synergy horizon (typically 5–10 years).

**Financial Synergy:**
- **Definition:** Reduction in the combined firm's cost of capital due to larger size, lower risk, and better credit.
- **Sources:** Higher credit rating → lower $k_d$; lower $\beta$ → lower $k_e$; greater debt capacity → more tax shield.
- **Example:** *Reliance Jio's* size enabled it to raise capital at sub-7% rates vs 12% for smaller telcos.
- **Quantification:** Compute $k_{AB}$ post-merger and apply to combined cash flows. The differential vs weighted pre-merger WACC, multiplied by capital base, gives the value impact.

**Revenue Synergy:**
- **Definition:** Higher combined revenue from cross-selling, pricing power, and channel access.
- **Sources:** Bundling products, accessing new geographies, eliminating duplicate channels.
- **Example:** *Disney + Hulu + ESPN* (planned) for content bundling.
- **Quantification:** Estimate incremental revenue per year → apply post-tax operating margin → discount at $k_{AB}$. Revenue synergy is **riskier** than cost synergy, so often discounted at a higher rate or haircut applied.

**Comparison Table (Bonus 1 mark):**

| Aspect | Operational | Financial | Revenue |
|---|---|---|---|
| Source | Cost side | Capital side | Revenue side |
| Confidence | High | High | Lower |
| Time to realize | 1–3 years | Immediate | 3–7 years |
| Typical share of value | 60–70% | 10–20% | 15–25% |

*(Examiner's distribution: 2 marks each synergy + 1 mark for quantification summary.)*

#### **(b) Firm P has 200 cr shares at ₹50 each. It acquires Firm Q (60 cr shares at ₹40 each) in an all-stock deal. Pre-merger NI: P = ₹1,000 cr, Q = ₹240 cr. After-tax synergy = ₹70 cr/year. Transaction cost = ₹30 cr. Calculate: (i) Exchange Ratio assuming P pays a 20% premium, (ii) New shares issued, (iii) Merged EPS, (iv) Accretion/Dilution %, (v) NPV of merger assuming perpetual synergy and cost of capital = 12%. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer with Full Working:**

**(i) Exchange Ratio (with 20% premium):**

Premium-adjusted offer price per Q share = $40 \times 1.20 = ₹48$

$$\begin{aligned}
ER &= \frac{P_{\text{offer}}}{P_P} = \frac{48}{50} = 0.96
\end{aligned}$$

*(Meaning: 0.96 shares of P for every 1 share of Q.)*

**(ii) New Shares Issued:**

$$N_{\text{new}} = ER \times N_Q = 0.96 \times 60 = 57.6 \text{ cr shares}$$

**(iii) Merged Net Income:**

$$NI_{PQ} = NI_P + NI_Q + \text{Synergy}_{\text{after-tax}} = 1{,}000 + 240 + 70 = ₹1{,}310 \text{ cr}$$

**Merged Share Count:**

$$N_{PQ} = 200 + 57.6 = 257.6 \text{ cr shares}$$

**Merged EPS:**

$$EPS_{PQ} = \frac{1{,}310}{257.6} = ₹5.085$$

**(iv) Accretion / Dilution:**

Pre-merger EPS of P: $\frac{1{,}000}{200} = ₹5.00$

$$\text{Accretion \%} = \frac{5.085 - 5.00}{5.00} \times 100\% = +1.70\% \quad (\text{Accretive ✓})$$

**(v) NPV of Merger:**

PV of perpetual after-tax synergy = $\frac{70}{0.12} = ₹583.33$ cr

Value given to Q's shareholders = $57.6 \times ₹50 = ₹2{,}880$ cr (assuming P's price stays at ₹50)

Value received = $V_Q = 60 \times ₹40 = ₹2{,}400$ cr (but with premium, the implicit offer = 2,880)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{\text{merger}} &= V_{PQ} - V_P - \text{Premium} - C_M \\
&= \text{Synergy PV} + (V_P + V_Q) - V_P - \text{Premium} - C_M \\
&= 583.33 + 2{,}400 - 2{,}400 - (2{,}880 - 2{,}400) - 30 \\
&= 583.33 - 480 - 30 \\
&= ₹73.33 \text{ cr}
\end{aligned}$$

Since NPV > 0, **the merger creates value for P's shareholders**, even though it is only marginally EPS-accretive.

*(Examiner's distribution: 1.5 marks each for parts (i)–(iv); 1 mark for (v) NPV.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & PITFALL CALLOUTS

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 WAYS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN MOTIVES-FOR-MERGER QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Transaction Cost ($C_M$):** In EPS accretion problems, many students compute the merged EPS without deducting one-time integration cost for Year 1. Examiner deducts **2 marks** for this omission.
>
> 2. **Confusing EPS Accretion with Value Creation:** A merger can be EPS-accretive in Year 1 yet value-destructive over 10 years. Always cross-check with NPV. Marking: −2 marks if NPV is not computed.
>
> 3. **Not Differentiating Between Gross and Net Synergy:** Examiner expects **both** values: $\Delta V_{\text{gross}} = V_{AB} - V_A - V_B$ **AND** $\Delta V_{\text{net}} = \Delta V_{\text{gross}} - \text{Premium} - C_M$. Conflating them costs **1.5 marks**.
>
> 4. **Mixing Up Hubris with Synergy:** In motive-classification questions, students often list "growth" or "synergy" without giving a **specific quantitative example**. Examiner expects a number or a real-world deal. Generic answers get **half marks**.
>
> 5. **Wrong Exchange Ratio Formula:** Using $ER = P_B / P_A$ (without premium) instead of $ER = P_{\text{offer}} / P_A$. Examiner specifically tests whether you understand that the offer price includes premium. **−1.5 marks** for the wrong formula.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY LAST-MINUTE REVISION — RE-READ BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL**

- ✅ A merger is **value-justified** if and only if $NPV_{\text{merger}} = \Delta V_{\text{synergy}} - \text{Premium} - C_M > 0$.
- ✅ Synergy has **three flavours**: Operational (cost), Financial (WACC), Revenue (top-line).
- ✅ Operational synergy is the **most reliable** and accounts for **60–70%** of deal value.
- ✅ Revenue synergy is the **most over-stated** and frequently fails to materialize.
- ✅ Tax motive is valid but **secondary**; it is the *primary* motive in only loss-firm takeovers.
- ✅ Diversification via merger is **NOT always shareholder-friendly** (MM theorem) — only bankruptcy-cost reduction justifies it.
- ✅ **EPS accretion ≠ Value creation.** The market often mistakes one for the other.
- ✅ **Hubris (Roll, 1986)** says managers systematically overpay; the entire premium is over-payment.
- ✅ **Jensen's Free Cash Flow Hypothesis (1986)** says surplus cash tempts managers into value-destroying acquisitions.
- ✅ **Exchange Ratio** $ER = P_{\text{offer}} / P_A$ — premium-inclusive.
- ✅ **Maximum ER** for value-neutrality = $(V_B + \Delta V - C_M) / (N_B \times P_A)$.
- ✅ Always mention **Tobin's Q** when discussing the undervaluation motive ($q = MV/RV$).
- ✅ Four broad motive categories: **(1) Synergy, (2) Growth/Strategic, (3) Financial/Tax, (4) Managerial.**
- ✅ Horizontal mergers face **antitrust scrutiny** (CCI in India, FTC in USA).
- ✅ Vertical integration eliminates **double marginalization**.
- ✅ After computing synergy, **always subtract premium and transaction cost** before concluding value creation.
- ✅ Use **$k_{AB}$** (combined WACC) to discount combined cash flows, not $k_A$ alone.
- ✅ Remember the **Indian Income Tax Act loss carry-forward period = 8 years** for tax-motivated mergers.
- ✅ The final verdict sentence **("Merger recommended / Not recommended")** carries **1 mark** — never omit it.
- ✅ In KTU Part B, **always show full working** for numerical; partial marks are awarded for correct intermediate steps.
- ✅ A 14-mark answer must have **at least 1 diagram or table** for full marks — use the taxonomy table or the motive classification matrix.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transaction Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transaction Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Corporate Finance and Portfolio Management**, **Transaction Characteristics** refer to the measurable attributes and qualitative features that describe how a financial transaction (buying or selling of a security, asset, or financial instrument) is conducted in a market. These characteristics collectively determine the **efficiency, cost, risk, and execution quality** of the transaction for the investor, trader, or corporate entity.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, transaction characteristics encompass the structural, economic, and behavioral properties of a trade, including but not limited to:

- **Transaction Cost** (both explicit and implicit)
- **Bid–Ask Spread**
- **Market Liquidity**
- **Market Depth and Breadth**
- **Price Volatility**
- **Order Size and Frequency**
- **Speed of Execution / Settlement**
- **Information Asymmetry and Market Impact**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Transaction Characteristics form the foundation of **trade execution strategy**, **transaction cost analysis (TCA)**, and **portfolio rebalancing decisions**. Every buy/sell decision in portfolio management is governed by these characteristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a cup of coffee from a street vendor versus buying the same coffee from a five-star hotel:

- **Street Vendor (Highly Liquid Market):** Instant delivery, very low cost, fixed price, no negotiation, no waiting — just like a **liquid stock** traded in high volume.
- **Five-Star Hotel (Illiquid Market):** Higher cost, possible waiting, negotiable price, custom preparation, additional service charges — just like a **thinly traded stock** or a **large block trade** in a less liquid market.

The **differences in how these transactions occur** — cost, time, certainty, and quality — are essentially what we call **Transaction Characteristics**. In financial markets, these characteristics decide whether a portfolio manager gets the **best execution** or suffers an **execution shortfall**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used (Bold for Constants)

The following standard market microstructure metrics are universally used to characterize a transaction:

- **Bid Price** = The maximum price a buyer is willing to pay.
- **Ask Price** = The minimum price a seller is willing to accept.
- **Midpoint** = (Bid + Ask) / 2
- **Bid-Ask Spread** = Ask - Bid
- **Effective Half-Spread** = Trade Price - Midpoint
- **Tick Size** = Minimum price increment allowed by the exchange.
- **Round Trip Cost** = Total cost of buying and then selling a security.
- **Standard Trading Unit** = Lot size as defined by the exchange.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> **Transaction Characteristics** = The set of measurable and qualitative properties of a financial trade that influence its **cost**, **execution quality**, **timing**, and **market impact**. They are the lens through which portfolio managers evaluate trade efficiency.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Transaction Characteristics

Transaction characteristics are broadly classified into **three categories**:

| # | Category | Examples |
|---|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Cost-Based Characteristics** | Commissions, fees, taxes, bid-ask spread, market impact |
| 2 | **Time-Based Characteristics** | Speed of execution, settlement cycle, trade frequency |
| 3 | **Market-Based Characteristics** | Liquidity, volatility, depth, breadth, information flow |

---

## 1.5 Why Transaction Characteristics Matter in Corporate Finance

In a corporate setting, the firm may transact in:

- **Equity shares** (issuing or buying back)
- **Bonds / Debentures**
- **Foreign Exchange (Forex)**
- **Derivatives** (Futures, Options, Swaps)
- **Real Assets** (M&A transactions)

Each of these transactions carries distinct characteristics that affect the **firm's cost of capital**, **earnings volatility**, and **shareholder value**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Major Transaction Characteristics

### A. Transaction Cost

Transaction cost is the **total cost incurred by a buyer or seller to execute a trade**. It has two major components:

1. **Explicit Costs (Direct Costs):**
   - Brokerage commissions
   - Exchange fees
   - Securities transaction taxes (STT, GST)
   - Stamp duty
   - Clearing and settlement charges

2. **Implicit Costs (Indirect Costs):**
   - **Bid-Ask Spread** — the difference between ask and bid price
   - **Market Impact** — the price movement caused by the trade itself
   - **Timing Cost / Opportunity Cost** — cost of delay in execution
   - **Information Leakage Cost** — movement of price due to signaling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Cost Warning:** In KTU exam problems, explicit costs are usually given numerically, while implicit costs must be **calculated** using the bid-ask spread and execution price — students often forget this and lose marks.

### B. Bid-Ask Spread

The **Bid-Ask Spread** is the difference between the lowest ask price and the highest bid price in the order book. It is the **primary compensation for market makers** who provide liquidity.

**Components of the Spread:**
- **Order Processing Cost** — cost of clearing, settlement, record-keeping
- **Inventory Holding Cost** — risk market makers bear by holding inventory
- **Adverse Selection Cost** — loss to informed traders

### C. Market Liquidity

Liquidity refers to **the ease with which a security can be bought or sold without affecting its price**. The four dimensions of liquidity (as defined by Kyle, 1985) are:

- **Tightness** — low bid-ask spread
- **Depth** — large order book size
- **Resiliency** — speed of price recovery after a trade
- **Immediacy** — speed of execution

### D. Market Depth and Breadth

- **Depth** = Volume of orders available at prices near the current market price.
- **Breadth** = Number of distinct buyers and sellers in the market.

### E. Volatility

Volatility is the **degree of variation of a trading price series over time**. Higher volatility increases transaction risk and may widen the bid-ask spread.

### F. Order Size and Frequency

- **Block Trade** = A large-sized transaction (typically > 10,000 shares or > ₹5 crore in India).
- **High-Frequency Trade (HFT)** = Trades executed in microseconds using algorithms.

### G. Speed of Execution and Settlement

- **T+1 Settlement Cycle** (current standard in India since Jan 2023) — Trade day + 1 working day.
- **Real-time Gross Settlement (RTGS)** — for high-value transactions.

### H. Information Asymmetry

Occurs when one party in a transaction has **more or better information** than the other. It causes **adverse selection**, leading to wider spreads in less transparent markets.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Bid-Ask Spread (Quoted Spread) | $S = P_{ask} - P_{bid}$ | $P_{ask}$ = ask price, $P_{bid}$ = bid price |
| 2 | Quoted Half-Spread | $S/2 = (P_{ask} - P_{bid})/2$ | Per-side cost to a market maker |
| 3 | Midpoint | $M = (P_{ask} + P_{bid})/2$ | Reference "fair" price |
| 4 | Effective Spread | $ES = 2 \cdot \vert P_{trade} - M \vert$ | Measures actual execution cost |
| 5 | Realized Spread | $RS = 2 \cdot \vert P_{trade} - M_{t+\Delta} \vert$ | $M_{t+\Delta}$ = midpoint after $\Delta$ time |
| 6 | Price Impact | $PI = P_{trade} - P_{mid \text{ before trade}}$ | Caused by order size |
| 7 | Total Transaction Cost | $TC = C_{broker} + S/2 + PI + T_{cost}$ | $C_{broker}$ = commission, $T_{cost}$ = timing cost |
| 8 | Round-Trip Cost | $RTC = 2 \cdot (S/2 + PI) + 2 \cdot C_{broker}$ | Cost of buy + sell |
| 9 | Proportional Spread | $\%S = S / M \times 100$ | Spread as % of midpoint |
| 10 | Volatility (Annualized) | $\sigma_{annual} = \sigma_{daily} \cdot \sqrt{252}$ | Trading days in a year |
| 11 | Turnover Ratio | $TR = \text{Volume} / \text{Shares Outstanding}$ | Indicator of liquidity |
| 12 | Amihud Illiquidity Ratio | $ILLIQ = \vert R_t \vert / V_t$ | $R_t$ = return, $V_t$ = volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Convention:** In KTU answer sheets, the **vertical bar** `|` symbol for absolute value may break the markdown tables. Always write absolute value as `\vert` in markdown tables and `|x|` in standalone math mode.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

While this is a finance course, transaction characteristics are extensively used in:

- **Algorithmic Trading Systems** — Software Engineering graduates design systems that optimize transaction cost.
- **High-Frequency Trading (HFT) Platforms** — Require microsecond-level execution speed.
- **Risk Management Software** — Uses volatility and spread data for real-time risk assessment.
- **Corporate Treasury Systems** — ERP modules (SAP, Oracle) use these metrics for cash management.
- **Forex Hedging Platforms** — Use spread and liquidity to determine hedging cost.
- **Robo-Advisors** — Optimize portfolio rebalancing using transaction cost minimization.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** In your final exam, always state the **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming T+1 settlement", "assuming a liquid market") before applying a formula. Examiners award marks for stated assumptions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Effective Spread from a Trade

**Problem Setup:** A trader executes a buy order. The market bid-ask quote before the trade is:

$$P_{bid} = \text{₹} 99.50, \quad P_{ask} = \text{₹} 100.00$$

The actual trade executes at $P_{trade} = \text{₹} 100.00$ (at the ask).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the Midpoint**

$$M = \frac{P_{bid} + P_{ask}}{2} = \frac{99.50 + 100.00}{2} = \frac{199.50}{2}$$

$$M = \text{₹} 99.75$$

This represents the theoretical fair value before the trade.

**Step 2: Compute the Quoted Spread**

$$S = P_{ask} - P_{bid} = 100.00 - 99.50 = \text{₹} 0.50$$

The quoted spread is ₹0.50 per share.

**Step 3: Compute the Effective Spread**

The effective spread captures the **real cost paid by the trader**, measured from the midpoint:

$$ES = 2 \cdot \vert P_{trade} - M \vert = 2 \cdot \vert 100.00 - 99.75 \vert$$

$$ES = 2 \cdot 0.25 = \text{₹} 0.50$$

Since the trade executed exactly at the ask, the effective spread equals the quoted spread. This is the **maximum possible cost** for a market buy order.

**Step 4: Interpretation**

- The trader paid ₹0.25 above the midpoint (half-spread).
- The seller received ₹0.25 below the midpoint.
- Total cost of liquidity = ₹0.50 per share.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Realized Spread and Market Impact

**Setup:** Consider the same trade as above, but **5 minutes after the trade**, the new midpoint is:

$$M_{t+5} = \text{₹} 99.85$$

The trade size was $V = 10{,}000$ shares.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the Realized Spread (profit to the market maker)**

$$RS = 2 \cdot \vert P_{trade} - M_{t+5} \vert = 2 \cdot \vert 100.00 - 99.85 \vert$$

$$RS = 2 \cdot 0.15 = \text{₹} 0.30$$

The market maker profits ₹0.30 per share after the price reverts.

**Step 2: Compute the Market Impact / Price Impact**

The market impact is the **non-reverting component** of the spread:

$$PI = ES - RS = 0.50 - 0.30 = \text{₹} 0.20$$

This ₹0.20 represents **permanent price impact** — the price moved and stayed moved.

**Step 3: Total Cost of Trade (in ₹)**

$$TC_{total} = ES \times V = 0.50 \times 10{,}000 = \text{₹} 5{,}000$$

**Step 4: Decompose Total Cost into Reversible and Permanent**

$$TC_{reversible} = RS \times V = 0.30 \times 10{,}000 = \text{₹} 3{,}000 \quad \text{(profit to dealer)}$$

$$TC_{permanent} = PI \times V = 0.20 \times 10{,}000 = \text{₹} 2{,}000 \quad \text{(cost of information)}$$

**Step 5: Final Analytical Insight**

> **Why this matters for KTU:** The split between realized spread and price impact tells the portfolio manager **why** a transaction cost was incurred — was it due to **dealer compensation** (reversible) or **information leakage** (permanent)?

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Amihud Illiquidity Measure

The **Amihud (2002) Illiquidity Ratio** measures the price impact per unit of trading volume:

$$ILLIQ_t = \frac{\vert R_t \vert}{V_t}$$

where $R_t$ is the daily return and $V_t$ is the daily trading volume (in ₹ crores).

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose Stock A has:
- Daily return: $R_t = 0.02$ (i.e., 2%)
- Daily volume: $V_t = \text{₹} 5 \text{ crore}$

Then:

$$ILLIQ_t = \frac{\vert 0.02 \vert}{5} = 0.004$$

A higher ILLIQ means **greater price impact per rupee of volume** — i.e., the stock is **less liquid**.

**Annualized ILLIQ (over N days):**

$$\overline{ILLIQ} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\vert R_t \vert}{V_t}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Liquidity Measures

| Measure | Formula | Strength | Weakness |
|---------|---------|----------|----------|
| **Bid-Ask Spread** | $P_{ask} - P_{bid}$ | Simple, real-time | Snapshot only |
| **Effective Spread** | $2 \cdot \vert P_{trade} - M \vert$ | Captures actual cost | Needs trade data |
| **Amihud Ratio** | $\vert R \vert / V$ | Daily-frequency | Backward-looking |
| **Turnover Ratio** | Volume / Shares Out | Indicates activity | Ignores price impact |
| **Kyle's Lambda** | $\Delta P / \sqrt{Volume}$ | Captures depth | Hard to estimate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
