<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the term "Bid-Ask Spread" and explain its significance in measuring transaction cost.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bid-Ask Spread** is the difference between the **lowest ask price** (minimum price at which a seller is willing to sell) and the **highest bid price** (maximum price at which a buyer is willing to buy) in the market order book.

$$S = P_{ask} - P_{bid}$$

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
1. **Direct measure of transaction cost** — A wider spread indicates a higher cost of executing a trade.
2. **Indicator of liquidity** — A narrow spread indicates a liquid market; a wide spread indicates illiquidity.
3. **Compensation to market makers** — The spread represents the dealer's earnings for providing immediacy and bearing inventory risk.

> The **half-spread** ($S/2$) is often used to represent the per-side cost to either a buyer or seller.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List and briefly explain any four dimensions of market liquidity as defined by Kyle (1985).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

According to **Kyle (1985)**, market liquidity has four dimensions:

1. **Tightness** — Refers to a low bid-ask spread, indicating low cost of immediate execution.
2. **Depth** — The volume of orders available at prices close to the midpoint; indicates the market's ability to absorb large orders.
3. **Resiliency** — The speed with which prices return to equilibrium after a large trade causes a temporary disturbance.
4. **Immediacy** — The speed with which orders can be executed; a highly immediate market has very low latency.

A market is considered **liquid** when all four dimensions are favorable.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various **components of transaction cost** with suitable examples. Distinguish clearly between **explicit** and **implicit** costs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data is available for a buy order executed by a portfolio manager:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Best Bid Price | ₹ 198.00 |
| Best Ask Price | ₹ 200.00 |
| Trade Execution Price | ₹ 199.50 |
| Order Size | 5,000 shares |
| Brokerage Commission | 0.10% of trade value |
| Midpoint 5 minutes after trade | ₹ 199.20 |

Compute the following:
(i) Quoted Spread
(ii) Effective Spread
(iii) Realized Spread
(iv) Market Impact per share
(v) Total Transaction Cost (including brokerage) **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Components of Transaction Cost (7 marks):**

Transaction cost is the total expense incurred to execute a trade. It is classified into two broad categories:

**1. Explicit Costs (Direct, Visible Costs):**
- **Brokerage Commission** — Fee paid to the broker for executing the trade. E.g., ₹20 per trade or 0.5% of trade value.
- **Exchange Fees** — Charges levied by the stock exchange (transaction charges, SEBI fees).
- **Securities Transaction Tax (STT)** — A government tax on securities transactions.
- **GST** — Goods and Services Tax on brokerage.
- **Stamp Duty** — State-level duty on trade value.
- **Clearing & Settlement Charges** — Paid to clearing houses (e.g., NSDL, CDSL).

**2. Implicit Costs (Indirect, Hidden Costs):**
- **Bid-Ask Spread Cost** — Cost of crossing the spread. For a buy at the ask, the cost is at least the half-spread.
- **Market Impact Cost** — Price movement caused by the order itself. Larger orders cause greater impact.
- **Timing Cost / Opportunity Cost** — Cost of delaying execution in anticipation of a better price. If the price moves adversely, the cost is realized.
- **Information Leakage Cost** — When the market infers information from a large order, prices may move against the trader.

> **Example:** Suppose you buy 1,000 shares of Reliance at ₹2,500 (ask price) when the bid is ₹2,498. The half-spread cost is ₹2 × 1,000 = ₹2,000. The brokerage may be 0.1% × ₹25,00,000 = ₹2,500. Total explicit + implicit cost = ₹4,500.

**Distinction:**
| Feature | Explicit Cost | Implicit Cost |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Visibility | Directly visible in trade contract | Hidden, must be estimated |
| Timing | Known before trade | Known only after trade |
| Examples | Brokerage, STT, GST | Spread, market impact, timing |
| Magnitude | Usually small (% of trade) | Can be large for big orders |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$P_{bid} = 198.00$, $P_{ask} = 200.00$, $P_{trade} = 199.50$, $V = 5{,}000$, $M_{t+5} = 199.20$.

**(i) Quoted Spread:**

$$S = P_{ask} - P_{bid} = 200.00 - 198.00 = \text{₹} 2.00$$

Quoted spread is **₹2.00 per share**.

**(ii) Effective Spread:**

First compute midpoint before trade:

$$M = \frac{P_{bid} + P_{ask}}{2} = \frac{198.00 + 200.00}{2} = \text{₹} 199.00$$

Effective spread:

$$ES = 2 \cdot \vert P_{trade} - M \vert = 2 \cdot \vert 199.50 - 199.00 \vert$$

$$ES = 2 \cdot 0.50 = \text{₹} 1.00$$

Effective spread is **₹1.00 per share**.

**(iii) Realized Spread:**

$$RS = 2 \cdot \vert P_{trade} - M_{t+5} \vert = 2 \cdot \vert 199.50 - 199.20 \vert$$

$$RS = 2 \cdot 0.30 = \text{₹} 0.60$$

Realized spread is **₹0.60 per share** — this is the market maker's profit after price reverts.

**(iv) Market Impact per share:**

$$PI = ES - RS = 1.00 - 0.60 = \text{₹} 0.40$$

Market impact is **₹0.40 per share** — this is the permanent price movement caused by the order.

**(v) Total Transaction Cost (including brokerage):**

Per-share implicit cost = Effective Spread = ₹1.00

Total implicit cost for 5,000 shares:

$$TC_{implicit} = 1.00 \times 5{,}000 = \text{₹} 5{,}000$$

Brokerage commission:

$$C_{broker} = 0.10\% \times (199.50 \times 5{,}000) = 0.001 \times 9{,}97{,}500$$

$$C_{broker} = \text{₹} 997.50$$

Total transaction cost:

$$TC_{total} = TC_{implicit} + C_{broker} = 5{,}000 + 997.50 = \text{₹} 5{,}997.50$$

**Total transaction cost = ₹5,997.50**

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **concept of market liquidity** and describe the **four dimensions of liquidity** (Tightness, Depth, Resiliency, Immediacy) as proposed by Kyle (1985). Discuss how these dimensions impact the transaction cost faced by a portfolio manager. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider two stocks, A and B, with the following daily trading data over 5 days:

| Day | Stock A Return | Stock A Volume (₹ Cr) | Stock B Return | Stock B Volume (₹ Cr) |
|-----|---------------|----------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1   | 0.01          | 10                   | 0.005         | 2                    |
| 2   | 0.02          | 12                   | 0.008         | 1.5                  |
| 3   | -0.015        | 8                    | -0.003        | 1.8                  |
| 4   | 0.025         | 15                   | 0.010         | 2.2                  |
| 5   | -0.010        | 9                    | -0.006        | 1.6                  |

Compute the **Amihud Illiquidity Ratio** for both stocks. Which stock is more liquid? Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Market Liquidity & Four Dimensions (7 marks):**

**Concept of Market Liquidity:**

Market liquidity refers to **the ability of a market to facilitate the purchase or sale of an asset without causing a significant change in its price**. A liquid market is characterized by the presence of many buyers and sellers, tight spreads, and rapid order execution.

Liquidity is a **multi-dimensional concept** — it cannot be measured by a single number. According to **Kyle (1985)**, a market is liquid if it satisfies four conditions:

**1. Tightness:**
- Refers to a **low bid-ask spread**.
- A tight market has a small difference between bid and ask prices.
- Impact on cost: Lower tightness means **lower transaction cost per share** for both buyers and sellers.
- Example: Reliance Industries has a spread of ₹0.50; a small-cap stock may have a spread of ₹5.

**2. Depth:**
- The **volume of orders** available at prices close to the current market price.
- A deep market can absorb large orders without significantly moving the price.
- Impact on cost: Deeper markets **reduce market impact cost** for large trades.
- Example: NSE has high depth in index stocks; small-cap stocks have limited depth.

**3. Resiliency:**
- The **speed at which prices return to fair value** after a temporary disturbance caused by a large trade.
- A resilient market recovers quickly, allowing new trades at fair prices.
- Impact on cost: High resiliency reduces **timing cost** — traders can be more patient with execution.
- Example: After a large institutional sale of a banking stock, the price recovers within minutes in a resilient market.

**4. Immediacy:**
- The **speed of order execution** — the time between order placement and trade confirmation.
- A high-immediacy market has low latency (milliseconds or microseconds).
- Impact on cost: High immediacy **reduces slippage cost** and allows traders to capture short-lived opportunities.

> **Synthesis:** A portfolio manager prefers markets with **all four dimensions favorable**. If a market has high tightness but low depth, large orders will still incur high market impact. If a market has high immediacy but low resiliency, the trader may face **adverse post-trade price movement**.

---

**Part (b) — Amihud Illiquidity Calculation (7 marks):**

The **Amihud Illiquidity Ratio** is:

$$ILLIQ_t = \frac{\vert R_t \vert}{V_t}$$

**For Stock A:**

| Day | $\vert R \vert$ | $V$ (₹ Cr) | $\vert R \vert / V$ |
|-----|---------|--------|-------------|
| 1   | 0.010   | 10     | 0.001000    |
| 2   | 0.020   | 12     | 0.001667    |
| 3   | 0.015   | 8      | 0.001875    |
| 4   | 0.025   | 15     | 0.001667    |
| 5   | 0.010   | 9      | 0.001111    |

**Sum for Stock A:**

$$\sum ILLIQ_A = 0.001000 + 0.001667 + 0.001875 + 0.001667 + 0.001111$$

$$\sum ILLIQ_A = 0.007320$$

**Average ILLIQ for Stock A:**

$$\overline{ILLIQ}_A = \frac{0.007320}{5} = 0.001464 \text{ per ₹ crore}$$

**For Stock B:**

| Day | $\vert R \vert$ | $V$ (₹ Cr) | $\vert R \vert / V$ |
|-----|---------|--------|-------------|
| 1   | 0.005   | 2.0    | 0.002500    |
| 2   | 0.008   | 1.5    | 0.005333    |
| 3   | 0.003   | 1.8    | 0.001667    |
| 4   | 0.010   | 2.2    | 0.004545    |
| 5   | 0.006   | 1.6    | 0.003750    |

**Sum for Stock B:**

$$\sum ILLIQ_B = 0.002500 + 0.005333 + 0.001667 + 0.004545 + 0.003750$$

$$\sum ILLIQ_B = 0.017795$$

**Average ILLIQ for Stock B:**

$$\overline{ILLIQ}_B = \frac{0.017795}{5} = 0.003559 \text{ per ₹ crore}$$

**Comparison and Conclusion:**

$$\overline{ILLIQ}_A = 0.001464 < \overline{ILLIQ}_B = 0.003559$$

> **Conclusion (2 marks):** Stock A is **more liquid** than Stock B. A unit of trading volume in Stock B causes a **2.43 times larger price impact** than in Stock A. Portfolio managers should prefer Stock A for large transactions because the market impact cost per rupee traded is significantly lower.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU 2024 Exam (Module 3 - Transaction Characteristics):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Bid-Ask Spread with Effective Spread** — The quoted spread is the *displayed* difference; the effective spread is *twice the absolute deviation of trade price from midpoint*. Students often compute only the half-spread and lose 2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Brokerage in Total Transaction Cost** — The question may give the spread impact but expect you to add commission separately. Marks allocated: typically 1 mark for the addition step.
>
> 3. **Not Converting Volume Units** — Amihud ratio is sensitive to the unit of volume. If volume is in ₹ crore, the ratio is in per-crore units; if in ₹ lakh, the ratio is in per-lakh units. **Always state the unit explicitly.**
>
> 4. **Ignoring Settlement Cycle in Real Questions** — KTU 2024 emphasizes T+1 settlement. If a question asks about settlement, mentioning the **wrong cycle** (T+2, T+3) is a direct loss of 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Not Defining the Trade Side** — For a *buy* order, the trade price is closer to ask; for a *sell* order, closer to bid. Effective spread is always positive, but the **sign convention** matters for interpretation. State explicitly: "The effective spread is ₹X (positive indicates cost to the trader)."
>
> 6. **Skipping the Midpoint Calculation** — In numericals, the midpoint is the most important intermediate step. Skipping it is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transaction Characteristics (Module 3)**

- ⭐ **Transaction Characteristics** describe the cost, time, and market-related properties of a financial trade.
- ⭐ **Bid-Ask Spread** = $P_{ask} - P_{bid}$ — primary measure of explicit trading cost.
- ⭐ **Effective Spread** = $2 \cdot \vert P_{trade} - M \vert$ — actual cost paid by the trader.
- ⭐ **Realized Spread** = $2 \cdot \vert P_{trade} - M_{t+\Delta} \vert$ — market maker's profit after price reverts.
- ⭐ **Price Impact** = Effective Spread − Realized Spread — permanent information-driven price move.
- ⭐ **Kyle's 4 Dimensions of Liquidity:** Tightness, Depth, Resiliency, Immediacy.
- ⭐ **Amihud Illiquidity Ratio** = $\vert R \vert / V$ — higher value = less liquid stock.
- ⭐ **Transaction Cost Components:** Brokerage + Spread + Market Impact + Timing Cost + Information Leakage.
- ⭐ **Settlement Standard (India, 2024):** T+1 for equities; T+0 for select forex and government securities.
- ⭐ **Block Trade:** Large transaction typically > ₹5 crore, often negotiated off-exchange or in dark pools.
- ⭐ **Information Asymmetry:** Drives adverse selection; increases spread in opaque markets.
- ⭐ **High-Frequency Trading (HFT):** Uses algorithmic execution to minimize spread cost; benefits from immediacy.
- ⭐ **Round-Trip Cost:** Total cost of buying and selling a security — must be considered before any trade.
- ⭐ **Volatility Impact:** Higher volatility → Wider spreads → Higher transaction cost.
- ⭐ **Market Microstructure:** The study of how transaction characteristics arise from the trading mechanism (auction, dealer, electronic order book).
- ⭐ **For KTU 2024 Numerical:** Always (1) state the trade side, (2) compute midpoint, (3) show the formula, (4) substitute values, (5) interpret the result in one sentence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Takeovers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Takeovers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Takeover** is a corporate transaction in which one entity (the **acquirer**) obtains control over another entity (the **target**) by purchasing a significant portion of its equity shares or assets. Control is conventionally established when the acquirer secures more than **50%** of the voting stock, although effective operational control in practice is often achieved with lower thresholds (typically **30%–51%**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A takeover is the transfer of control of a company from one group of shareholders to another. It is a fundamental corporate restructuring strategy pursued to achieve synergies, market expansion, diversification, or value capture. Takeovers may be **friendly (negotiated)** or **hostile (unnegotiated)**.

### 1.2 Types of Takeovers – KTU Classification Framework

| Category | Sub-Type | Core Characteristic |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| **By Mode of Acquisition** | Share Takeover | Acquirer purchases equity shares of the target |
| | Asset Takeover | Acquirer purchases specific assets/divisions |
| **By Consent Level** | Friendly Takeover | Board of target consents; negotiated premium |
| | Hostile Takeover | Board opposes; acquirer bypasses management via tender offer |
| **By Geographic Reach** | Domestic Takeover | Both firms in same country |
| | Cross-Border Takeover | Firms in different jurisdictions |
| **By Structure** | Horizontal Takeover | Same industry, competing firms |
| | Vertical Takeover | Buyer and supplier in supply chain |
| | Conglomerate Takeover | Unrelated business diversification |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cricket Team Captaincy":**
> Imagine a cricket club where one influential business group gradually buys up voting rights (shares) from existing members. When they own more than half, they become the "captain" of the team — they decide the playing XI (board composition), strategy (business direction), and prize money distribution (dividend policy). 
> 
> If the existing captain (target's board) **welcomes** the new group, it is a *friendly takeover*. If the captain **resists** and the new group directly approaches the players (shareholders) through public offers, it becomes a *hostile takeover*. The extra money paid above the current share price is the **acquisition premium** — essentially a "transfer fee" the new owner pays to convince shareholders to switch allegiance.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Control Threshold:** **>50%** of voting equity (statutory majority)
- **Creeping Acquisition Trigger:** **5% / 10% / 15%** disclosure thresholds (SEBI Takeover Regulations 2011 / India context)
- **Open Offer Trigger:** **25%** acquisition of voting rights (under SEBI SAST Regulations)
- **Mandatory Open Offer Size:** Minimum **26%** of shares from public shareholders
- **Tender Offer Period:** Typically **10 working days** minimum (in India); **20 business days** (US)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The "Why" and "How"

#### A. Motives Behind Takeovers (The Strategic "Why")

1. **Synergy Gains:** Combined entity value exceeds the sum of individual values.
2. **Market Power:** Eliminate competition, increase pricing ability.
3. **Diversification:** Reduce firm-specific risk for the acquirer.
4. **Vertical Integration:** Reduce transaction costs in the supply chain.
5. **Managerial Hubris:** Overconfident managers pursue empire-building.
6. **Bootstrapping Effect:** Use overvalued acquirer stock to acquire undervalued targets.
7. **Tax Benefits:** Utilize target's loss carry-forwards (subject to regulations).

#### B. Mechanism of Takeovers (The Operational "How")

**Step 1: Target Identification & Valuation**
The acquirer's investment banker identifies undervalued firms and conducts fundamental analysis using DCF, comparable companies, and precedent transactions.

**Step 2: Preliminary Approach (Friendly Route)**
Negotiation begins with the target's board; confidentiality agreements (NDAs) are signed.

**Step 3: Due Diligence**
Comprehensive audit of target's financial, legal, and operational health.

**Step 4: Offer Finalization**
Offer price = Standalone Value + Acquisition Premium

**Step 5: Communication to Shareholders**
- *Friendly:* Board recommends acceptance.
- *Hostile:* Direct tender offer to public shareholders, bypassing the board.

**Step 6: Regulatory Approvals**
Competition Commission (CCI in India), SEBI (Takeover Code), and other statutory bodies.

**Step 7: Post-Merger Integration**
Combining operations, systems, and culture.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Examination**

| Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions |
|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Synergy Value** | $\Delta V = V_{AB} - (V_A + V_B)$ | $V_{AB}$ = Combined firm value, $V_A, V_B$ = Standalone values |
| **Acquisition Premium** | $P = \frac{(P_{offer} - P_{market})}{P_{market}} \times 100$ | $P_{offer}$ = Offer price, $P_{market}$ = Pre-announcement market price |
| **EPS Accretion / Dilution** | $\Delta EPS = EPS_{new} - EPS_{acquirer}$ | Tests if merger enhances earnings per share |
| **Post-Merger EPS** | $EPS_{new} = \frac{(NI_A + NI_B - I(1-t))}{S_A + S_B}$ | $NI$ = Net Income, $I$ = Interest, $t$ = Tax, $S$ = Shares |
| **Maximum Offer Price (DCF)** | $P_{max} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{FCF_t}{(1+WACC)^t} + \frac{TV}{(1+WACC)^n}$ | Present value of free cash flows + Terminal value |
| **Exchange Ratio (Stock Swap)** | $ER = \frac{P_{offer}}{P_{acquirer}}$ | Number of acquirer shares given per target share |
| **Break-Even Premium** | $P_{BE} = \frac{Synergy}{Value\ of\ Target\ Equity}$ | Minimum premium justified by synergy capture |
| **Ratchet Effect** | $R = \frac{\Delta P_{target}}{\Delta P_{acquirer}}$ | Measures target shareholder's percentage gain |
| **Net Present Value of Takeover** | $NPV = V_{AB} - V_A - P_{offer}$ | Should be positive for acceptance |
| **Bootstrap Effect EPS** | $EPS_{B} = \frac{NI_A + NI_B}{S_A + (P_{offer}/P_A)\cdot S_B}$ | For stock-financed acquisitions |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

While takeovers are primarily a **finance/management** concept, the valuation techniques used (DCF, risk-adjusted returns) directly derive from engineering economics principles. The **WACC computation** in the Maximum Offer Price formula mirrors the **capital recovery** logic from engineering project appraisal. **Synergy quantification** parallels **system efficiency gains** in integrated engineering systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** In PEIET744 (an interdisciplinary course), examiners frequently test whether students can **apply the DCF formula correctly** with a takeover context, **compute acquisition premium**, and perform **EPS accretion/dilution analysis** — all heavily formula-driven questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Justifiable Offer Price (DCF Approach)

The acquirer must ensure that the offer price does not exceed the **Net Present Value** of synergies the combined entity will generate, less the standalone value of the target.

$$
NPV_{takeover} = PV(Synergies) - Premium\ Paid
$$

**Logic for maximum acceptable offer:**

$$
P_{max}^{offer} = V_T^{standalone} + PV(Synergies) \times (\alpha)
$$

Where $\alpha$ represents the **acquirer's share of the synergy capture** (target shareholders typically receive 50%–70% of synergy value in negotiated deals).

**Step-by-step solution to a typical KTU problem:**

**Problem:** Acquirer Co. has WACC = 10%. Target Co. generates Free Cash Flows of ₹500 Cr (Year 1) growing at 6% perpetual. Synergies are valued at ₹800 Cr (PV). Acquirer claims 50% of synergies. Market price of Target = ₹1,200 Cr. Find maximum offer price and premium justified.

**Step 1: Compute Standalone Value of Target (Gordon Growth Model)**

$$
V_T = \frac{FCF_1}{WACC - g} = \frac{500}{0.10 - 0.06}
$$

$$
V_T = \frac{500}{0.04} = 12{,}500\ \text{Crore}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Synergy Share Retained by Acquirer**

$$
\alpha = 0.50 \times 800 = 400\ \text{Crore}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Offer Price (acquirer's break-even)**

$$
P_{max} = V_T + \alpha \cdot PV(Synergies) = 12{,}500 + 400 = 12{,}900\ \text{Crore}
$$

**Step 4: Premium Justified vs Market Price**

$$
Premium = \frac{12{,}900 - 1{,}200}{1{,}200} \times 100 = 975\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note:** The above premium seems extreme because the market price is depressed compared to intrinsic value — a classic *undervalued target* scenario. KTU problems typically ensure the market price reflects fundamentals more closely.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: EPS Accretion / Dilution Analysis

**Given Data:**
- Acquirer: $NI_A = 200$ Cr, Shares $S_A = 100$ Cr, $EPS_A = ₹2.00$
- Target: $NI_B = 50$ Cr, Shares $S_B = 25$ Cr, $EPS_B = ₹2.00$
- Offer: 1 share of Acquirer for every 2 shares of Target (all-stock)
- Interest on debt: $I = 30$ Cr, Tax rate $t = 30\%$

**Step 1: Calculate New Shares Issued**

$$
\Delta S = \frac{S_B}{2} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5\ \text{Cr shares}
$$

**Step 2: Total New Shares Outstanding**

$$
S_{new} = S_A + \Delta S = 100 + 12.5 = 112.5\ \text{Cr}
$$

**Step 3: Combined Net Income (After Tax Cost of Debt)**

$$
NI_{combined} = NI_A + NI_B - I(1-t) = 200 + 50 - 30(1-0.30)
$$

$$
NI_{combined} = 250 - 21 = 229\ \text{Cr}
$$

**Step 4: New EPS**

$$
EPS_{new} = \frac{229}{112.5} = ₹2.0356
$$

**Step 5: Accretion / Dilution Test**

$$
\Delta EPS = 2.0356 - 2.00 = +0.0356
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Accretive} \Rightarrow \text{Merger is EPS-enhancing}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Accretive** = New EPS > Old EPS (merger increases shareholder wealth).
> **Dilutive** = New EPS < Old EPS (merger reduces shareholder wealth).

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Exchange Ratio Calculation

**Stock-for-Stock Acquisition Formula:**

$$
ER = \frac{P_{offer}}{P_{acquirer}} = \frac{\text{Offer Price per Target Share}}{\text{Current Market Price per Acquirer Share}}
$$

**Alternative: Fixed Exchange Ratio (negotiated)**

If 1 share of Target = 0.6 shares of Acquirer, and Acquirer market price = ₹150, then implied offer price:

$$
P_{offer} = 0.6 \times 150 = ₹90
$$

If Target's pre-announcement price = ₹75, then premium:

$$
Premium = \frac{90 - 75}{75} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Friendly vs. Hostile Takeover

| Parameter | Friendly Takeover | Hostile Takeover |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| Board Consent | Required | Not required; bypassed |
| Speed | Slower (negotiated) | Faster (direct tender) |
| Premium Paid | Lower (10%–30%) | Higher (30%–50%+) |
| Synergy Realization | Higher (cooperation) | Lower (resistance) |
| Legal/Advisory Costs | Lower | Higher |
| Success Rate | ~70%–80% | ~30%–50% |
| Post-Merger Integration | Smoother | Riskier, attrition possible |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Takeover Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ACQUIRER IDENTIFIES TARGET"] --> B["PRELIMINARY VALUATION DCF AND COMPARABLES"]
    B --> C{"FRIENDLY OR HOSTILE?"}
    C -->|"FRIENDLY PATH"| D["NEGOTIATE WITH BOARD"]
    C -->|"HOSTILE PATH"| E["SECRETE STAKE BUILDING"]
    D --> F["DUE DILIGENCE PHASE"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["FINALIZE OFFER PRICE WITH PREMIUM"]
    G --> H["REGULATORY APPROVALS SEBI AND CCI"]
    H --> I["COMMUNICATION TO SHAREHOLDERS"]
    I --> J["ACCEPTANCE PERIOD OPEN OFFER"]
    J --> K{"ACQUISITION SUCCESSFUL?"}
    K -->|"YES"| L["POST MERGER INTEGRATION"]
    K -->|"NO"| M["WITHDRAWAL OR COUNTER BID"]
    L --> N["SYNERGY REALIZATION AND VALUE CREATION"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Types of Takeovers — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TAKEOVERS CLASSIFICATION"] --> B["BY CONSENT"]
    A --> C["BY STRUCTURE"]
    A --> D["BY GEOGRAPHY"]
    B --> B1["FRIENDLY NEGOTIATED"]
    B --> B2["HOSTILE TENDER OFFER"]
    C --> C1["HORIZONTAL SAME INDUSTRY"]
    C --> C2["VERTICAL SUPPLY CHAIN"]
    C --> C3["CONGLOMERATE UNRELATED"]
    D --> D1["DOMESTIC SAME COUNTRY"]
    D --> D2["CROSS BORDER INTERNATIONAL"]
    B1 --> B1A["LOWER PREMIUM 10 TO 30 PERCENT"]
    B1 --> B1B["HIGHER SYNERGY CAPTURE"]
    B2 --> B2A["HIGHER PREMIUM 30 TO 50 PERCENT"]
    B2 --> B2B["LEGAL COMPLEXITY HIGH"]
    C1 --> C1A["MARKET POWER GAIN"]
    C1 --> C1B["ANTITRUST SCRUTINY"]
    C2 --> C2A["COST REDUCTION"]
    C2 --> C2B["SUPPLY SECURITY"]
    C3 --> C3A["RISK DIVERSIFICATION"]
    C3 --> C3B["CAPITAL ALLOCATION BENEFIT"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Valuation in Takeovers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VALUATION_BLOCK["VALUATION METHODOLOGY STACK"]
        A1["INTRINSIC DCF MODEL"] --> A2["WACC COMPUTATION"]
        A2 --> A3["FREE CASH FLOW PROJECTION"]
        A3 --> A4["TERMINAL VALUE GORDON"]
        A4 --> A5["PRESENT VALUE DISCOUNTING"]
    end
    subgraph RELATIVE_BLOCK["RELATIVE VALUATION BLOCK"]
        B1["PE MULTIPLE PEER FIRMS"] --> B2["EV TO EBITDA COMPARABLE"]
        B2 --> B3["PRECEDENT TRANSACTION MULTIPLES"]
    end
    subgraph PREMIUM_BLOCK["PREMIUM AND SYNERGY BLOCK"]
        C1["STANDALONE TARGET VALUE"] --> C2["PLUS SYNERGY NPV"]
        C2 --> C3["EQUALS MAX OFFER PRICE"]
        C3 --> C4["MINUS MARKET PRICE EQUALS PREMIUM"]
    end
    VALUATION_BLOCK --> FINAL["FINAL OFFER PRICE DETERMINATION"]
    RELATIVE_BLOCK --> FINAL
    PREMIUM_BLOCK --> FINAL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

**Q1. Define a 'Hostile Takeover'. How does it differ from a 'Friendly Takeover'? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **hostile takeover** is an acquisition in which the acquiring company bypasses the target company's board of directors and makes a direct offer to its shareholders — typically via a **tender offer** — despite opposition from the incumbent management.

| Aspect | Friendly | Hostile |
|--------|----------|---------|
| Board Support | Yes | No |
| Mechanism | Negotiation | Public Tender |
| Premium | 10%–30% | 30%–50%+ |

The key difference lies in **board consent** and the **mechanism of approaching shareholders**.

---

**Q2. What is an 'Acquisition Premium' and why is it paid? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

An **acquisition premium** is the amount paid by the acquirer over and above the prevailing market price of the target's shares, expressed as a percentage.

$$
Premium = \frac{(Offer\ Price - Market\ Price)}{Market\ Price} \times 100
$$

**Reasons for paying premium:**
1. To compensate target shareholders for **control transfer**.
2. To share the **synergy gains** expected from the merger.
3. To **overcome resistance** from shareholders and incumbent management.
4. To reflect the **strategic value** of the target to the acquirer.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern — ESE Module 3 Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the various **motives and theories** behind corporate takeovers. Discuss the **Synergy Theory** and **Hubris Hypothesis** in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Acquirer Ltd. is evaluating the acquisition of Target Ltd. The following data is available:
- Target's standalone equity value: ₹3,000 Crore
- Synergy benefits (PV): ₹1,200 Crore
- Acquirer will retain 60% of synergy value
- Market price of Target: ₹2,400 Crore
- Acquirer's WACC: 12%, Target's perpetual growth: 4%

**Compute:**
(i) The maximum offer price the acquirer should pay.
(ii) The acquisition premium justified over the market price.
(iii) Comment on whether the deal is **accretive or dilutive** if executed at the maximum price. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Motives and Theories:**

1. **Synergy Theory:** Combined value > sum of standalone values; revenue + cost synergies.
2. **Hubris Hypothesis (Roll, 1986):** Overconfident managers overpay due to ego.
3. **Agency Theory:** Managers pursue growth for personal empire-building.
4. **Market Power Theory:** Acquire competitors to gain pricing dominance.
5. **Bootstrap Effect:** Use overvalued stock to acquire undervalued assets.
6. **Tax Motive:** Utilize tax shields, loss carry-forwards.
7. **Information Asymmetry:** Acquirer has superior information about target's value.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Offer Price:**

$$
P_{max} = V_T^{standalone} + (\alpha \times PV_{synergy})
$$

$$
P_{max} = 3{,}000 + (0.60 \times 1{,}200) = 3{,}000 + 720
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{max} = ₹3{,}720\ \text{Crore}}
$$

**(ii) Acquisition Premium over Market Price:**

$$
Premium = \frac{(3{,}720 - 2{,}400)}{2{,}400} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{Premium = 55\%}
$$

**(iii) Accretion/Dilution Comment:**

Since the maximum offer price (₹3,720 Cr) is below the intrinsic value of the target plus retained synergies, the deal creates **positive NPV** for the acquirer. The deal is **value-accretive** provided synergies are realized as projected.

$$
NPV = 720 - 720 = 0\ \text{(at break-even)}
$$

Any execution below ₹3,720 Cr generates positive NPV ⇒ **Accretive deal**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanism of a hostile takeover**. Explain the role of **tender offers, proxy fights, and greenmail** in such transactions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** XYZ Corp. proposes to acquire ABC Ltd. through an **all-stock deal**. The following data applies:

| Parameter | XYZ (Acquirer) | ABC (Target) |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| Net Income | ₹400 Cr | ₹120 Cr |
| Shares Outstanding | 200 Cr | 60 Cr |
| Market Price | ₹100 | ₹180 |

**Offer:** 2 shares of XYZ for every 3 shares of ABC. Additionally, XYZ will issue ₹50 Cr in new debt at 10% interest, tax rate 25%.

**Compute:**
(i) New EPS of combined entity.
(ii) EPS accretion or dilution (vs XYZ's pre-merger EPS).
(iii) Implied premium per ABC share. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hostile Takeover Mechanism:**

1. **Tender Offer:** Direct public offer to shareholders to buy shares at a specified price within a stipulated period.
2. **Proxy Fight:** Acquiring sufficient proxies from shareholders to vote out incumbent board at AGM.
3. **Greenmail:** Target's management repurchases its own shares from the hostile bidder at a premium to make them go away.
4. **Toehold Purchases:** Stealthy accumulation of <5% stake in open market before announcement.
5. **Crown Jewel Defense:** Selling target's most valuable assets to make acquisition unattractive.
6. **Poison Pill:** Issuing new shares to existing shareholders, diluting the bidder's stake.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) New Shares Issued by XYZ:**

$$
\Delta S = \frac{60 \times 2}{3} = 40\ \text{Cr new shares}
$$

$$
S_{new} = 200 + 40 = 240\ \text{Cr}
$$

**Net Income (post-tax interest cost):**

$$
I_{after\ tax} = 50 \times 0.10 \times (1 - 0.25) = 3.75\ \text{Cr}
$$

$$
NI_{combined} = 400 + 120 - 3.75 = 516.25\ \text{Cr}
$$

**New EPS:**

$$
EPS_{new} = \frac{516.25}{240} = ₹2.151
$$

**(ii) Pre-merger EPS of XYZ:**

$$
EPS_{XYZ} = \frac{400}{200} = ₹2.00
$$

$$
\Delta EPS = 2.151 - 2.00 = +0.151
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Accretive Deal} — EPS\ increases\ by\ ₹0.151\ (7.55\%)}
$$

**(iii) Implied Premium per ABC Share:**

Offer price per ABC share:

$$
P_{offer} = \frac{2}{3} \times 100 = ₹66.67
$$

Premium:

$$
Premium = \frac{(66.67 - 180)}{180} \times 100
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{-62.96\%}$ — a **massive discount, not a premium**. This indicates a poorly structured offer; XYZ should revise the exchange ratio upward, or ABC shareholders will reject the bid outright.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add back synergy share**: Many students compute $P_{max} = V_T^{standalone}$ only, missing the synergy component (3–4 marks lost).
> 2. **Confusing accretion with dilution direction**: Always compare New EPS to **Acquirer's pre-merger EPS**, not Target's.
> 3. **Exchange ratio errors**: Misreading "2 shares of XYZ for 3 of ABC" — multiply the ratio correctly against the target's share count.
> 4. **Ignoring tax shield on debt**: In Part B numericals, $I(1-t)$ must be deducted; full marks require this.
> 5. **Premium vs market price ≠ Premium vs intrinsic value**: Always clarify the **denominator** in the premium formula.
> 6. **Not stating "Accretive" or "Dilutive" explicitly**: The conclusion word is mandatory for full marks in KTU valuation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ High-Density Revision Notes — Takeovers (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Takeover = acquisition of **>50% voting control** of a company.
- ✅ **Two Broad Types:** Friendly (negotiated, board-approved) and Hostile (tender offer, board opposed).
- ✅ **Three Structural Types:** Horizontal, Vertical, Conglomerate.
- ✅ **Core Valuation Formula:** $P_{max} = V_T^{standalone} + \alpha \cdot PV(Synergies)$.
- ✅ **Premium Formula:** $Premium = \frac{P_{offer} - P_{market}}{P_{market}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Synergy Value:** $\Delta V = V_{AB} - (V_A + V_B)$ — must be **positive** for a value-creating deal.
- ✅ **EPS Accretion Test:** Compute $EPS_{new}$ vs $EPS_{acquirer}$ — positive = accretive.
- ✅ **Exchange Ratio:** $ER = P_{offer} / P_{acquirer}$ for stock-swap deals.
- ✅ **Bootstrap Effect:** Useful when acquirer's P/E is high relative to target's.
- ✅ **Hostile Defense Mechanisms:** Poison pill, crown jewel, greenmail, white knight, golden parachute, staggered board.
- ✅ **Regulatory Triggers (India):** SEBI SAST Regulations — open offer mandatory at **25%** acquisition, minimum offer size **26%**.
- ✅ **Typical Premium Range:** 10%–30% (friendly), 30%–50%+ (hostile).
- ✅ **NPV Rule:** $NPV_{takeover} = PV(Synergies) - Premium\ Paid > 0$ for acceptance.
- ✅ **Memorize the Three Drivers of Value:** Revenue Synergy + Cost Synergy + Financial Synergy.
- ✅ **Hubris Warning:** Managers often overpay; always run NPV test, not just strategic-fit test.
- ✅ **Integration Risk:** Post-merger integration is where **30%–50%** of deals fail to deliver projected synergies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Regulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Financial Regulation** in the context of corporate finance and portfolio management refers to the **systematic framework of laws, rules, supervisory bodies, and enforcement mechanisms** that govern the issuance, trading, distribution, and management of securities, as well as the conduct of market intermediaries, listed companies, and institutional investors.

In the Indian context, this regulatory architecture is primarily anchored by the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)**, established under the **SEBI Act, 1992**, alongside complementary statutes such as the **Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956 (SCRA)**, the **Companies Act, 2013**, the **Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999 (FEMA)**, and the **Depositories Act, 1996**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> Regulation in corporate finance encompasses the statutory and quasi-judicial framework that ensures **market integrity, investor protection, transparency, disclosure, and fair play** in the primary (issuance) and secondary (trading) capital markets, while also governing portfolio management services, mutual funds, collective investment schemes, and alternative investment funds.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏦 The Traffic Police & Highway Analogy**
> Imagine the Indian capital market as a massive, multi-lane national highway where **companies** are vehicles launching onto the road (IPO), **investors** are drivers, **stockbrokers** are driving instructors, and **portfolio managers** are professional chauffeurs. Without traffic signals, lane markings, speed limits, and police checkpoints, the highway would be a deadly chaos of collisions, frauds, and accidents.
>
> **SEBI** is essentially the "Traffic Police HQ" — setting the speed limits (price bands, margin rules), issuing driving licenses (registration of intermediaries), inspecting vehicles (corporate disclosures, audits), and prosecuting reckless drivers (insider traders, manipulators). The **Acts of Parliament** (SEBI Act, SCRA) are the foundational Highway Code.

This analogy makes it intuitive why regulation exists — **to prevent information asymmetry, moral hazard, and market failure** that would otherwise erode investor confidence and dry up capital formation.

---

## 1.3 Core Pillars of Securities Regulation

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes regulation along **five foundational pillars**:

| Pillar | Core Objective | KTU-Relevant Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Disclosure-Based Regime** | Full, true, and fair disclosure of material information | Prospectus, Annual Reports, Insider Trading Code |
| **Registration of Intermediaries** | Only qualified entities can operate | SEBI registration of brokers, PMS, MFs, AIFs |
| **Investor Protection** | Grievance redressal & compensation | SEBI Complaints Redressal System (SCORES), Investor Protection Fund |
| **Market Integrity** | Prevention of fraud & manipulation | Insider Trading Regulations, PFUTP Regulations |
| **Corporate Governance** | Accountability of management | LODR Regulations, 2015, Board composition norms |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Trick:** The five pillars spell **"DRI-MC"** — *Disclosure, Registration, Investor, Market, Corporate*. These are the most frequently tested 2-mark concepts in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 Key Regulatory Bodies — A Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU 2024 List of Regulators:**

1. **SEBI** (Securities and Exchange Board of India) — Apex securities market regulator.
2. **RBI** (Reserve Bank of India) — Regulates banks, NBFCs, forex, and monetary policy.
3. **IRDAI** (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India) — Regulates insurance companies and their investment portfolios.
4. **PFRDA** (Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority) — Regulates the National Pension System (NPS) and pension fund managers.
5. **MCA** (Ministry of Corporate Affairs) — Administers the Companies Act, 2013.
6. **SROs** (Self-Regulatory Organizations) — Stock exchanges (BSE, NSE), clearing corporations (NSCCL, ICCL).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The SEBI Act, 1992 — Backbone of Indian Securities Regulation

The **SEBI Act, 1992** was enacted to provide SEBI with **statutory powers** to:
- Regulate the securities market.
- Protect the interests of investors in securities.
- Promote the development of the securities market.
- Regulate substantial acquisition of shares and takeovers.

### 2.1.1 Powers of SEBI (Section 11 & 11B of the SEBI Act)

SEBI's powers are categorized as:

| Power Type | Description | Statutory Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Quasi-Legislative** | Frame regulations, guidelines, and circulars | Section 11(1) |
| **Quasi-Executive** | Investigate, inspect, and enforce | Section 11(2), 11(4) |
| **Quasi-Judicial** | Pass orders, impose penalties, adjudicate disputes | Section 11(3), 11(4A), 11B, 15 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks "Discuss the nature of SEBI's powers," always enumerate these **three quasi-powers** in order — this is a guaranteed 5–7 mark question in the ESE.

---

## 2.2 The Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956 (SCRA)

The SCRA is the **mother legislation** for the regulation of securities trading in India. Its core objectives are:

- To prevent undesirable speculation in securities.
- To regulate the recognition, functioning, and trading of stock exchanges.
- To provide for **listing of securities** and related matters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definitions under SCRA, Section 2:**
> - **"Securities"** (Section 2(h)) — Includes shares, scrips, stocks, bonds, debentures, debenture stock, and marketable securities of like nature.
> - **"Stock Exchange"** (Section 2(j)) — Any body of individuals, whether incorporated or not, constituted for the purpose of assisting, regulating, or controlling the business of buying, selling, or dealing in securities.
> - **"Recognised Stock Exchange"** — A stock exchange recognized by the Central Government under Section 4 of SCRA.

---

## 2.3 SEBI (Issue and Listing of Debt Securities) Regulations, 2008

These regulations govern the **issuance, listing, and trading of debt securities** (corporate bonds, NCDs, commercial papers) on Indian stock exchanges.

### 2.3.1 Eligibility Conditions for Issuance

- The issuer must be a **body corporate, registered society, trust, or a public financial institution**.
- Minimum subscription of **₹100 crore** for public issues.
- Credit rating from a registered Credit Rating Agency (CRISIL, ICRA, CARE, India Ratings).

> [!TIP]
> **Formula for Minimum Application Size:**
> $$\text{Minimum Application Size} = \text{Face Value} \times \text{Minimum Lot Size}$$
>
> For most NCD issues, the minimum lot is **₹10,000** face value.

---

## 2.4 SEBI (Portfolio Managers) Regulations, 2020

Replacing the older 1993 regulations, the **Portfolio Managers Regulations, 2020** govern entities managing individual investor portfolios under **discretionary, non-discretionary, or advisory** modes.

### 2.4.1 Net Worth Requirement

$$\text{Net Worth of Portfolio Manager} \geq \text{₹ 5 Crore}$$

This is a key compliance threshold for registration.

### 2.4.2 Investment Restrictions for PMS Funds

| Restriction | Threshold |
|---|---|
| **Minimum investment in a single scrip** | 25% of the total AUM of the scheme (for equity) |
| **Maximum exposure to a single sector** | 25% of the portfolio |
| **Cap on unlisted securities** | 25% of the portfolio |
| **Minimum investment per client** | **₹50 lakh** (for discretionary & non-discretionary) / **₹25 lakh** (advisory) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **₹50 lakh** PMS minimum with the **₹10 lakh** minimum for Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs). Remember: **PMS = ₹50L (discretionary) / ₹25L (advisory)**.

---

## 2.5 SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015

Insider trading is the **illegal practice of trading in securities based on Unpublished Price Sensitive Information (UPSI)**.

### 2.5.1 Definition of "Insider"

An **insider** is any person who is or was **connected** or **deemed connected** with the company, and who has or has had access to UPSI.

**Connected Person** (Regulation 2(1)(d)) includes:
- Directors, officers, employees.
- Auditors, legal advisors, consultants.
- Persons who have a professional or business relationship (whether temporary or permanent).
- Immediate relatives of the above.

### 2.5.2 Definition of UPSI

> Unpublished Price Sensitive Information means any information relating to a company or its securities, directly or indirectly, that is **not generally available** and which upon becoming generally available, is likely to **materially affect the price** of the securities.

**Examples of UPSI:**
- Financial results (quarterly/annual).
- Dividends declaration.
- Change in capital structure (bonus, rights, splits).
- Mergers, demergers, acquisitions, disposals.
- Changes in Key Managerial Personnel (KMP).
- Major contracts or orders.

### 2.5.3 The Two Main Prohibitions

1. **Regulation 3 — Communication of UPSI:** No insider shall communicate UPSI to any other person except for legitimate purposes, performance of duties, or discharge of legal obligations.
2. **Regulation 4 — Trading when in possession of UPSI:** No insider shall trade in securities that are listed or proposed to be listed, when in possession of UPSI.

### 2.5.4 Code of Conduct

The 2015 Regulations prescribe a **Code of Conduct** for:
- **Insiders** (Reg. 9) — Trading window closure, pre-clearance, disclosure of holdings.
- **Board of Directors** (Reg. 9) — Obligations on designated persons.
- **Initial Disclosure** (Reg. 7(1)) — Within **30 days** of becoming a director/KMP.
- **Continual Disclosure** (Reg. 7(2)) — Within **2 trading days** of any trade exceeding **₹10 lakh** in a quarter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet (Markdown Table Format):**

| Regulation / Topic | Key Threshold / Rule | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **PMS Net Worth** | ≥ **₹5 crore** | 2-mark |
| **PMS Min. Investment (Discretionary)** | **₹50 lakh** | 3-mark |
| **AIF Min. Investment (Angel/Category I)** | **₹25 lakh** | 3-mark |
| **Insider Trading Initial Disclosure** | Within **30 days** | 2-mark |
| **Insider Trading Continual Disclosure** | Within **2 trading days** | 2-mark |
| **Continual Disclosure Trade Threshold** | **₹10 lakh per quarter** | 2-mark |
| **PMS Single Stock Cap (Equity)** | **25% of AUM** | 3-mark |
| **SEBI Foreign Ownership Cap (single FPI)** | **10%** of paid-up capital | 2-mark |
| **Takeover Trigger Threshold** | **10% + change** in control | 3-mark |
| **Open Offer Minimum Size** | **26%** of shares | 3-mark |
| **Minimum Public Shareholding (MPS)** | **25%** for listed cos. | 2-mark |
| **Minimum Issue Size (NCD public)** | **₹100 crore** | 2-mark |
| **Listing Time (IPO on main board)** | Within **3 working days** | 2-mark |
| **Audit Committee (LODR)** | Min. **3 directors**, with 2/3rds independent | 3-mark |
| **STT on Equity Delivery** | **0.1%** (both buy & sell) | 2-mark |

---

## 2.6 SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 (Takeover Code)

The **Takeover Code** regulates the acquisition of substantial shares, voting rights, and control in listed companies.

### 2.6.1 Trigger Points

$$\text{Initial Trigger} = 25\% \text{ of shares or voting rights (with intention to acquire control)}$$

$$\text{Creeping Acquisition Limit} = 5\% \text{ per financial year (up to 75\%)}$$

### 2.6.2 Open Offer Obligation

When an acquirer crosses the **25% threshold** (or 5% creeping beyond 25% to 75%), they must make an **open offer** to buy at least **26%** of shares from the public shareholders.

> **Example Calculation (Frequently asked):**
> If a listed company has 10 crore shares, and an acquirer crosses 25% (2.5 crore shares), they must make an open offer for:
> $$\text{Open Offer Shares} = 26\% \times 10 \text{ Cr} = 2.6 \text{ crore shares}$$

---

## 2.7 SEBI (Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2015 (LODR)

The **LODR Regulations** consolidate listing norms for all listed entities on recognized stock exchanges.

### 2.7.1 Key Disclosure Requirements

| Event | Disclosure Timeline |
|---|---|
| Financial Results (Quarterly) | Within **45 days** of quarter end |
| Annual Audited Results | Within **60 days** of FY end |
| Outcome of Board Meeting | Within **30 minutes** of closure |
| Material events (any of 30+ categories) | Within **24 hours** |
| Shareholding Pattern | Within **21 days** from end of quarter |
| Investor Grievance Report | Quarterly |

### 2.7.2 Corporate Governance Norms (Regulation 17–27)

- **Board of Directors:** Minimum **6 directors** (or as per Companies Act, whichever is higher); at least **1/3rd** must be independent.
- **Audit Committee:** Minimum **3 directors**; **2/3rds** must be independent; Chair must be an independent director.
- **Nomination & Remuneration Committee:** All independent directors.
- **Stakeholders Relationship Committee:** To resolve investor grievances.
- **Risk Management Committee:** Mandatory for top **500 listed companies by market cap**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** LODR is the most tested regulation in Part B questions — questions like "Explain the role of the Audit Committee under LODR" appear almost every KTU exam cycle.

---

## 2.8 The Companies Act, 2013 — Key Regulatory Provisions for Corporate Finance

### 2.8.1 Prospectus & Allotment (Section 23–39)

- A public offer must be made through a **prospectus** containing all prescribed disclosures (Schedule II & III).
- A **Shelf Prospectus** (Section 31) is permitted for a single or multiple issues within **1 year**.
- **Red Herring Prospectus** (Section 32) is used in book-building issues.

### 2.8.2 Prohibition of Fraudulent Issuance (Section 34)

> A person who **fraudulently** induces any person to subscribe for shares shall be liable for **imprisonment up to 10 years** and a **fine up to ₹25 lakh**.

### 2.8.3 Buyback of Shares (Section 68–70)

A company may buy back its own shares subject to:
- **Maximum 25%** of paid-up capital and free reserves.
- **Debt-to-Equity** ratio not exceeding **2:1**.
- Prior approval of shareholders by special resolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula for Maximum Buyback Quantum:**
> $$\text{Maximum Buyback} = \min\{25\% \text{ of (Paid-up Capital + Free Reserves)}\}$$
>
> **Verification by Board Resolution** vs. **Shareholder Special Resolution**:
> $$\text{If buyback} \leq 10\% \text{ of total paid-up capital and free reserves} \rightarrow \text{Board Resolution suffices}$$

---

## 2.9 FEMA, 1999 — Foreign Exchange Regulation

The **Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999** replaced the FERA, 1979, and is the principal legislation regulating foreign exchange transactions in India.

### 2.9.1 Key Definitions

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Authorised Person** | An authorised dealer, money changer, or offshore banking unit. |
| **Capital Account Transaction** | Transaction which alters the assets/liabilities outside India. |
| **Current Account Transaction** | Payment not related to capital account (e.g., trade in goods/services). |
| **Person Resident in India** | An individual who has stayed in India ≥ **182 days** in the preceding FY, or any Indian company/HUF. |

### 2.9.2 Foreign Portfolio Investor (FPI) Limits

A single FPI or FPI group can hold **less than 10%** of the paid-up capital of an Indian listed company. The aggregate FPI limit can go up to the **sectoral cap / 100%** of paid-up capital, subject to RBI/SEBI conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick to Remember:**
> - **FPI** = Foreign **Portfolio** Investor (passive holding)
> - **FDI** = Foreign **Direct** Investor (control/management)
> - **NRI** = Non-Resident Indian (individual)
> - **OCI** = Overseas Citizen of India

---

## 2.10 Regulatory Framework for Mutual Funds

Mutual funds are regulated by **SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 1996**.

### 2.10.1 Structure of a Mutual Fund

A mutual fund has a **three-tier structure**:

1. **Sponsor** (Promotes the MF, must contribute ≥ 40% to the AMC's net worth).
2. **Trustee** (Board of Trustees — protects unit-holders' interest; min. 4 trustees, 2/3rds independent).
3. **Asset Management Company (AMC)** (Manages the investments; min. net worth ₹50 crore).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "STA":** **S**ponsor → **T**rustee → **A**MC. The flow of capital is *Sponsor → Trustee → AMC → Investor's money*.

### 2.10.2 Investment Restrictions (Regulation 25 & Schedule 7)

| Restriction | Limit |
|---|---|
| **Single stock exposure** | **10%** of NAV |
| **Single group exposure** | **20%** of NAV (relaxed to 25% for financials, 35% for debt-only) |
| **Sector exposure** | Sector-specific caps |
| **Unrated debt exposure** | **5%** of debt portfolio |
| **Cash & money market** | Not exceeding **25%** of liquid/equity scheme |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Formula:**
> $$\text{Single Stock Cap} = 10\% \text{ of the Scheme's NAV}$$
> $$\text{Unlisted Stocks Cap (MF)} = 10\% \text{ of NAV}$$

### 2.10.3 Expense Ratio Cap (SEBI Circular 2023)

The **Total Expense Ratio (TER)** is capped based on the **Average AUM** of the scheme:

$$\text{TER Ceiling} = \text{Basis Points} \times \sqrt{\text{AUM}}$$

> **Example:** For an equity scheme with AUM of ₹10,000 crore:
> $$\text{TER} = 1.65\% \text{ on first ₹500 cr} \rightarrow \text{sliding scale down to } 1.05\%$$

---

## 2.11 Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs) — SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012

**AIFs** are privately pooled investment vehicles (other than MFs) registered with SEBI. They are categorized into:

| Category | Investment Focus | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Category I** | Startups, SMEs, infrastructure, social ventures | Venture Capital Funds |
| **Category II** | Private equity, debt funds, fund of funds | PE Funds, Real Estate Funds |
| **Category III** | Complex strategies — hedge funds, PIPE | Hedge Funds |

**Minimum Investment:**
- **Category I & II:** **₹1 crore** (with a minimum of **₹25 lakh** initial).
- **Category III:** **₹1 crore** (with a minimum of **₹25 lakh** initial).

**Continuing Interest (Sponsor):** Sponsor must maintain a **continuing interest** of not less than **2.5%** of the corpus, or **₹5 crore**, whichever is lower.

---

## 2.12 Prevention of Fraud and Unfair Trade Practices (PFUTP) Regulations, 2003

These regulations prohibit:
- **Market manipulation** (price rigging, pump-and-dump).
- **Mis-statement to SEBI** — false disclosure in documents.
- **Insider trading** (now subsumed under 2015 Regulations).
- **Front-running** — trading ahead of a known large institutional order.
- **Mis-selling** — selling products inconsistent with the investor's risk profile.

> Penalties under PFUTP include **monetary penalties up to ₹25 crore** (or 3 times the profit made) and **imprisonment up to 10 years** for serious violations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: How to Calculate Open Offer Size Under the Takeover Code

> **Worked-Out Numerical Example for KTU 14-Mark Questions**

### Problem Statement

X Ltd. is a listed company with a total paid-up capital of **12 crore equity shares** of face value ₹10 each, fully subscribed. An acquirer, ABC Corp., currently holds **20%** of X Ltd. They propose to acquire an additional **8%** from the open market, which will push their total holding to **28%**.

> **Questions:**
> 1. Is ABC Corp. required to make an open offer? If yes, calculate the size of the open offer.
> 2. What is the minimum offer price formula under the Takeover Code, 2011?
> 3. If the audited book value per share is ₹150 and the highest traded price in the 52 weeks preceding the public announcement is ₹480, what will be the open offer price?

### Step 1: Determine if the Takeover Code is Triggered

The acquirer currently holds **20%** (below the 25% threshold). After acquiring an additional **8%**, total holding will be:

$$20\% + 8\% = 28\%$$

Since this is a **direct jump from 20% to 28%**, it is a **single acquisition crossing 25%**. Therefore, the **Takeover Code is triggered**.

### Step 2: Identify the Triggered Regulation

Under **Regulation 3(1) of the SAST Regulations, 2011**, any acquirer acquiring shares (along with PAC) such that their aggregate holding exceeds **25%** of the voting rights in a target company must make an open offer.

### Step 3: Calculate the Minimum Open Offer Size

As per **Regulation 13(1) and 14(1)**, the open offer must be for **at least 26%** of the total shares of the target company.

$$\text{Total Shares of X Ltd.} = 12{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares}$$

$$\text{Minimum Open Offer Shares} = 26\% \times 12{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Minimum Open Offer Shares} = \frac{26}{100} \times 12{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Minimum Open Offer Shares} = 3{,}12{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Minimum Offer Price

As per **Regulation 8(2)** of the SAST Regulations, 2011, the open offer price is the **highest of the following three**:

| Parameter | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Negotiated Price | Not applicable (no private agreement) | Where a SPA exists |
| Highest Traded Price (52 weeks) | ₹480 | Preceding public announcement |
| VWAMP (Volume-Weighted Average Market Price) | Not given (assumed ₹420) | 60 trading days preceding the announcement |

For this case (no negotiated price), the **minimum offer price** is the **highest of** the remaining two:

$$\text{Offer Price} = \max(\text{HTP 52-week}, \text{VWAMP 60-day})$$

$$\text{Offer Price} = \max(₹480, ₹420) = ₹480 \text{ per share}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Total Open Offer Value

$$\text{Total Offer Value} = \text{Open Offer Shares} \times \text{Offer Price}$$

$$\text{Total Offer Value} = 3{,}12{,}00{,}000 \times ₹480$$

$$\text{Total Offer Value} = ₹1{,}497{,}60{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Total Offer Value} = ₹1{,}497.60 \text{ crore} \approx ₹1,498 \text{ crore}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-Wise Recap for Exam:**
> 1. Identify the threshold crossing (25% or creeping 5%).
> 2. Apply Regulation 3(1) or Regulation 4(1).
> 3. Compute 26% of total share capital.
> 4. Compute offer price = max(Negotiated Price, HTP 52-week, VWAMP 60-day).
> 5. Multiply for total offer value.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of PMS vs. AIF vs. MF

> **A frequently tested KTU comparison table (assemble in your answer for full marks)**

| Parameter | Portfolio Management Service (PMS) | Alternative Investment Fund (AIF) | Mutual Fund (MF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Regulator** | SEBI | SEBI | SEBI |
| **Governing Regulation** | PMS Regulations, 2020 | AIF Regulations, 2012 | MF Regulations, 1996 |
| **Minimum Investment** | ₹50 lakh (disc./non-disc.) / ₹25 lakh (advisory) | ₹1 crore (min. ₹25 lakh) | ₹500 (Systematic Investment Plan) |
| **Investor Type** | HNIs (High Net-worth Individuals) | Ultra HNIs / Institutional | Retail to HNI |
| **Structure** | Direct contractual relationship | Trust (with Trustee) | Trust (Sponsor + Trustee + AMC) |
| **Disclosure Frequency** | Monthly statement | Quarterly (NAV disclosure) | Daily NAV |
| **Lock-in Period** | None | 1 year (Category I & II); 1 year for III (with extension) | None |
| **Number of Investors** | Max 200 (per fund) | 1,000 (per scheme) | Unlimited |
| **Cap on Single Stock** | 25% of portfolio (equity) | 25% (Cat. I); 15% (Cat. III) | 10% of NAV |
| **Tax Treatment** | Pass-through (segregated portfolio) | Pass-through (Cat. I & II) | Dividend taxed; capital gains taxed |
| **Customization** | Fully customized | Limited customization | Standardized scheme |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Question Type:**
> *"Compare and contrast the regulatory framework of PMS and AIF. Which is more suitable for an HNI with ₹2 crore investable surplus?"* — A guaranteed 14-marker.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Mutual Fund Expense Ratio Sliding Scale

> **Frequently asked in KTU numerical questions — the TER calculation.**

### Problem Statement

The "G-Equity Growth Fund" has the following average AUM at the end of each quarter:
- **Q1:** ₹250 crore
- **Q2:** ₹300 crore
- **Q3:** ₹340 crore
- **Q4:** ₹410 crore

Compute the **Total Expense Ratio (TER)** applicable for the entire financial year and the **maximum allowable expense** for the fund.

### Step 1: Compute the Average AUM

$$\text{Average AUM} = \frac{250 + 300 + 340 + 410}{4}$$

$$\text{Average AUM} = \frac{1300}{4} = 325 \text{ crore}$$

### Step 2: Identify the Applicable TER Slab

Per SEBI's TER circular, the TER is a **sliding scale** based on Average AUM:

| AUM Slab | Applicable TER (Equity) |
|---|---|
| First ₹500 crore | 2.00% (or 1.65% post-2023 revision) |
| Next ₹250 crore (₹500cr – ₹750cr) | 1.75% |
| Next ₹1,250 crore (₹750cr – ₹2,000cr) | 1.50% |
| Above ₹2,000 crore | 1.35% |

For this problem, average AUM = ₹325 crore — falls entirely in the first slab.

### Step 3: Compute the Maximum Allowable Expense

$$\text{Maximum Expense} = \text{Average AUM} \times \text{TER \%}$$

$$\text{Maximum Expense} = ₹325 \text{ crore} \times 1.65\%$$

$$\text{Maximum Expense} = \frac{325 \times 1.65}{100} = ₹5.3625 \text{ crore}$$

### Step 4: Allocate Expenses Across Daily NAV

Since the TER is recovered from the **Scheme's NAV daily**, the AMC computes a daily TER:

$$\text{Daily Expense Ratio} = \frac{1.65\%}{365} = 0.00452\% \text{ per day}$$

This small daily figure is multiplied by the daily AUM and debited to the scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-step deduction summary (for full KTU marks):**
> 1. Sum quarterly AUMs, divide by 4 → average AUM.
> 2. Identify TER slab from SEBI circular.
> 3. Apply TER % to average AUM → max expense.
> 4. Cross-verify with AMFI database.
> 5. Note that additional GST (18%) is charged over and above TER.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Insider Trading Disclosure Threshold

> **Worked Example for Part A 3-Mark Questions**

### Problem Statement

Mr. X is a KMP of Y Ltd. (a listed company). He transacted in Y Ltd. shares on the following dates:
- **March 5:** Purchased 8,000 shares @ ₹520
- **March 15:** Sold 6,000 shares @ ₹550
- **March 22:** Purchased 12,000 shares @ ₹510

**Quarterly aggregate trade value:**

$$\text{Q1 Total} = (8{,}000 \times 520) + (6{,}000 \times 550) + (12{,}000 \times 510)$$

$$= 41{,}60{,}000 + 33{,}00{,}000 + 61{,}20{,}000$$

$$= ₹1{,}35{,}80{,}000$$

$$\text{Threshold for Continual Disclosure} = ₹10 \text{ lakh} \text{ (per quarter)}$$

Since the **aggregate trade value exceeds ₹10 lakh**, Mr. X must make a **continual disclosure** within **2 trading days** of the last trade (or end of the quarter) under **Regulation 7(2)** of the Insider Trading Regulations, 2015.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Logic Recap:**
> $$\text{If Aggregate Quarterly Trade Value} \geq ₹10 \text{ lakh} \rightarrow \text{Disclose within 2 trading days}$$

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Compute Buyback Quantum Under Companies Act, 2013

> **Frequently tested numerical problem in KTU Module 3.**

### Problem Statement

Z Ltd. has the following balance sheet extract:
- **Paid-up Equity Share Capital:** ₹100 crore (10 crore shares @ ₹10 each)
- **Free Reserves:** ₹80 crore
- **Securities Premium:** ₹40 crore
- **Outstanding Debt:** ₹90 crore

The Board of Directors proposes to buy back **up to 18%** of paid-up capital and free reserves.

### Step 1: Calculate the Maximum Buyback Quantum (Section 68(1))

$$\text{Max Buyback} = 25\% \text{ of (Paid-up Capital + Free Reserves)}$$

$$\text{Max Buyback} = 25\% \times (100 + 80) = 25\% \times 180 = ₹45 \text{ crore}$$

### Step 2: Verify the Proposed Quantum

The Board proposes **18%**, which is less than the **25% cap**.

$$\text{Proposed Buyback} = 18\% \times 180 = ₹32.40 \text{ crore}$$

✓ This is **within the Section 68 limit**.

### Step 3: Verify Debt-to-Equity Ratio (Section 68(2)(d))

$$\text{Post-Buyback Equity} = 180 - 32.40 = 147.60 \text{ crore (approximately)}$$

$$\text{Debt-to-Equity Ratio} = \frac{90}{147.60} = 0.61 : 1$$

Since the limit is **2:1**, the condition is **satisfied** (0.61 < 2.0).

### Step 4: Determine the Approval Mechanism (Section 68(2)(b))

> **Rule:**
> - Buyback **≤ 10%** of total paid-up capital + free reserves → **Board Resolution** suffices.
> - Buyback **> 10%** → **Special Resolution** of shareholders required.

Since **18% > 10%**, Z Ltd. must pass a **Special Resolution** of shareholders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Summary for KTU:**
> 1. Maximum permissible buyback = ₹45 crore (25% of 180).
> 2. Proposed buyback of ₹32.40 crore is within the cap.
> 3. Post-buyback D/E ratio = 0.61:1, within the 2:1 statutory limit.
> 4. A **Special Resolution** (Section 68(2)(b)) is mandatory since the buyback exceeds 10%.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> *"What are the quasi-powers of SEBI? Why is each of them essential for effective securities market regulation?"*

**Model Answer:**

SEBI possesses three distinct categories of powers, all derived from the **SEBI Act, 1992** (especially Sections 11, 11B, and 15):

**1. Quasi-Legislative Power (Section 11(1)):** SEBI can frame **regulations, guidelines, and circulars** that have the force of law on intermediaries, listed companies, and market participants.

> *Why essential:* Enables SEBI to dynamically respond to evolving market practices and crises (e.g., COVID-19 circulars, T+1 settlement circular) **without waiting for parliamentary amendments**.

**2. Quasi-Executive Power (Section 11(2), 11(4)):** SEBI can **investigate, inspect books of accounts, conduct enquiries, and direct intermediaries** to comply.

> *Why essential:* Provides **deterrent enforcement capability** — the threat of investigation is often enough to ensure compliance.

**3. Quasi-Judicial Power (Section 11B, 15):** SEBI can **pass orders, adjudicate disputes, and impose penalties** (up to ₹25 crore or 3x the profit made).

> *Why essential:* Provides a **specialized, fast-track forum** for dispute resolution without burdening civil courts.

Together, these three powers transform SEBI from a mere advisory body into a **fully functional regulatory authority**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

> *"Explain the concept of Unpublished Price Sensitive Information (UPSI) under the SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015. Give any four examples."*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** As per **Regulation 2(1)(n)** of the Insider Trading Regulations, 2015, **UPSI** is any information relating to a company or its securities, directly or indirectly, that is **not generally available** and which upon becoming generally available, is likely to **materially affect the price** of the securities.

The two essential ingredients are:
1. **Information must be specific and not generally available** (i.e., the public does not have access to it).
2. **It must have the potential to materially affect the price** of the company's securities upon becoming public.

**Four Examples of UPSI:**
1. **Financial results** — quarterly/annual unaudited or audited results.
2. **Dividends declaration** — change in dividend rate or declaration of a special dividend.
3. **Capital structure changes** — bonus shares, rights issues, stock splits, buyback announcements.
4. **Mergers & Acquisitions** — any M&A deal, joint venture, or significant asset acquisition/disposal.
5. (Bonus) Changes in **Key Managerial Personnel** (CEO, CFO, Managing Director).
6. (Bonus) Material events in **litigation, fraud, or force majeure**.

> Any insider in possession of UPSI is **prohibited from trading** (Regulation 4) and from **communicating** it (Regulation 3) except for legitimate purposes.

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)
