$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

Substituting values:

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.60)^2(20)^2 + (0.40)^2(15)^2 + 2(0.60)(0.40)(0.30)(20)(15)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.36)(400) + (0.16)(225) + (0.144)(300)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 144 + 36 + 43.2 = 223.2$$

### Step 3: Portfolio Standard Deviation

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{223.2} \approx 14.94\%$$

### Step 4: Sharpe Ratio

$$S = \dfrac{13\% - 6\%}{14.94\%} = \dfrac{7\%}{14.94\%} \approx 0.469$$

This worked example shows how **Step 3 (Asset Allocation)** mathematically feeds into **Step 7 (Performance Evaluation)** through the Sharpe Ratio — a perfect demonstration of the integrated nature of the portfolio management process.

---

## 3.4 Decision Flow Logic — Choosing Between Active and Passive Management

A common KTU question is: *"When should an investor choose active over passive management?"*

The decision logic:

- If **market is efficient** (Strong-form EMH holds) → Passive management is optimal.
- If **market is inefficient** (Weak or Semi-strong EMH) → Active management can add value.
- If **investor has informational advantage** (institutional investor with research) → Active.
- If **investor has cost constraints** (retail) → Passive (index funds) is cheaper.

This logic must be referenced when justifying the choice of strategy in Step 4 (Security Selection).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagram — Portfolio Management Process Flow

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Sequential Flow of the 7 Steps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Identify Investor Objectives and Constraints"]:::step1
    B["Step 2: Identify Investment Opportunities"]:::step2
    C["Step 3: Asset Allocation Decision"]:::step3
    D["Step 4: Security Selection"]:::step4
    E["Step 5: Portfolio Execution"]:::step5
    F["Step 6: Portfolio Revision and Rebalancing"]:::step6
    G["Step 7: Portfolio Performance Evaluation"]:::step7
    H["Feedback Loop: Adjust Objectives if needed"]:::feedback

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> A

    classDef step1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#FF1493,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inputs and Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        I1["Investor Profile"]
        I2["Capital Available"]
        I3["Market Data"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT ENGINE"]
        P1["Step 1: Objective Setting"]
        P2["Step 2: Asset Universe"]
        P3["Step 3: Asset Allocation"]
        P4["Step 4: Security Selection"]
        P5["Step 5: Execution"]
        P6["Step 6: Rebalancing"]
        P7["Step 7: Evaluation"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        O1["Constructed Portfolio"]
        O2["Performance Report"]
        O3["Revised Strategy"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> O1
    P7 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical View — Strategic vs. Tactical Allocation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Portfolio Management Process"]
    A --> B["Strategic Layer (Long-term)"]
    A --> C["Tactical Layer (Short-term)"]

    B --> B1["Define Target Asset Mix"]
    B --> B2["Set Rebalancing Bands"]
    B --> B3["Establish Risk Limits"]

    C --> C1["Market Timing"]
    C --> C2["Sector Rotation"]
    C --> C3["Security Selection Overrides"]

    B1 --> D["Final Portfolio Constructed"]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** All three diagrams depict the same process from different lenses — sequential flow, functional block architecture, and strategic/tactical layering. For KTU answers, the **first diagram (sequential flow)** is the most frequently expected.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define the Portfolio Management Process and list its main steps.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Portfolio Management Process is a systematic and continuous sequence of activities undertaken by an investor or portfolio manager to construct and manage an investment portfolio that meets the investor's financial objectives while optimizing the risk-return trade-off.

**Main Steps (1.5 marks):**
1. Identification of Investor Objectives and Constraints
2. Identification of Investment Opportunities
3. Asset Allocation Decision
4. Security Selection
5. Portfolio Execution
6. Portfolio Revision and Rebalancing
7. Portfolio Performance Evaluation

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: What is meant by "Investment Policy Statement" (IPS)? Why is it important in portfolio management?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An Investment Policy Statement (IPS) is a written document that captures the investor's investment objectives, risk tolerance, time horizon, asset allocation preferences, and constraints. It serves as the foundational blueprint for all subsequent portfolio decisions.

**Importance (1.5 marks):**
- Provides clarity and discipline to the investment process.
- Acts as a benchmark against which performance is measured.
- Prevents emotional decision-making during market volatility.
- Ensures alignment between the portfolio manager and investor.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE Module pattern, **Module 3 typically features an internal choice**. Both alternatives below are provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q: Explain in detail the various steps involved in the Portfolio Management Process with suitable examples.**

#### **Part (a) — Steps 1 to 4 (7 Marks)**

**Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identification of Investor Objectives and Constraints (2 marks):**
The process begins by determining the investor's return objectives (capital appreciation, current income, capital preservation), risk tolerance (conservative, moderate, aggressive), investment horizon (short, medium, long-term), and constraints (liquidity needs, tax considerations, legal restrictions).

*Example:* A 28-year-old salaried professional with a monthly surplus of ₹30,000, no dependents, and a 15-year horizon for retirement would have an aggressive risk profile with a long horizon — favoring equity-heavy allocation.

**Step 2 — Identification of Investment Opportunities (1.5 marks):**
The portfolio manager identifies the universe of available securities — equities, bonds, gold, real estate, derivatives — using macroeconomic, industry, and company-level analyses.

*Example:* After RBI announces a rate cut, the manager may shortlist corporate bond issuers that benefit from lower borrowing costs.

**Step 3 — Asset Allocation Decision (1.5 marks):**
The funds are distributed across asset classes based on the IPS. The manager decides the proportion of equity, debt, gold, etc.

*Example:* For the 28-year-old above, a strategic allocation might be 70% equity, 20% debt, 5% gold, 5% cash equivalents.

**Step 4 — Security Selection (2 marks):**
Specific securities are picked within each asset class. Fundamental analysis (P/E ratio, ROE, debt-equity) and technical analysis are applied.

*Example:* Within the 70% equity allocation, the manager may select a mix of large-cap blue-chips (Reliance, HDFC Bank), mid-caps, and an index fund for diversification.

#### **Part (b) — Steps 5 to 7 + Importance (7 Marks)**

**Answer:**

**Step 5 — Portfolio Execution (2 marks):**
Actual buying and selling of the selected securities occurs. The manager considers transaction costs, market timing, and liquidity.

*Example:* Placing market orders for large-cap stocks during high-volume hours to minimize price impact.

**Step 6 — Portfolio Revision and Rebalancing (2 marks):**
Periodically (quarterly/annually or when thresholds are breached), the portfolio is rebalanced back to target weights.

*Example:* If due to a bull run, equity allocation grows from 70% to 80%, the manager may sell some equity and buy more debt to restore the 70:20:5:5 mix.

**Step 7 — Performance Evaluation (2 marks):**
The portfolio's risk-adjusted return is measured using Sharpe Ratio, Treynor Ratio, Jensen's Alpha, and compared to a benchmark.

*Example:* If the portfolio returned 14% against a benchmark of 12% with a Sharpe of 0.85, the manager is adding value.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The portfolio management process is iterative — the feedback from performance evaluation may lead to revision of objectives, restarting the cycle. This continuous loop ensures the portfolio remains aligned with the investor's evolving goals.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q: Discuss the importance of Asset Allocation in Portfolio Management. Explain the different types of Asset Allocation strategies with examples.**

#### **Part (a) — Importance of Asset Allocation (7 Marks)**

**Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Asset Allocation refers to the division of an investment portfolio across different asset classes (equity, debt, gold, cash, alternatives) to optimize the risk-return profile.

**Importance (5 marks):**

1. **Dominant Driver of Returns (1 mark):**
   Studies show that over **90% of portfolio return variability** is explained by asset allocation, more than security selection or market timing.

2. **Risk Management (1 mark):**
   Diversification across asset classes reduces unsystematic risk and smoothens portfolio returns.

3. **Alignment with Objectives (1 mark):**
   The allocation directly reflects the investor's risk tolerance and time horizon. A young investor gets a higher equity weight; a retired investor gets a higher debt weight.

4. **Behavioral Discipline (1 mark):**
   Pre-defined allocation prevents impulsive decisions during market extremes.

5. **Tax and Cost Efficiency (1 mark):**
   Strategic allocation reduces the frequency of transactions, lowering costs and tax liabilities.

#### **Part (b) — Types of Asset Allocation (7 Marks)**

**Answer:**

**1. Strategic Asset Allocation (2.5 marks):**
- Long-term target mix based on the investor's objectives and risk profile.
- Example: A 60:30:10 mix of equity, debt, and gold maintained over 10 years.
- Rebalanced periodically (annually or threshold-based).

**2. Tactical Asset Allocation (2.5 marks):**
- Short-term deviations from the strategic mix to exploit market opportunities.
- Example: Increasing equity from 60% to 70% during a market correction, with the intent to revert when markets recover.

**3. Dynamic Asset Allocation (1 mark):**
- Continuous adjustment based on market valuations and economic forecasts.
- Used by sophisticated investors.

**4. Insured Asset Allocation (1 mark):**
- Active reallocation triggered when portfolio value falls below a pre-set floor.
- Example: Shifting to 100% bonds if portfolio value drops by 10% from peak.

**Conclusion (Bonus):** Asset allocation is the cornerstone of portfolio management, and the choice of strategy depends on the investor's profile, market outlook, and the manager's expertise.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Listing steps without explaining the rationale** — Examiners expect *why* each step matters, not just *what* it is. (Loses 2-3 marks)
> 2. **Skipping the "continuous/iterative" nature** — The process is NOT linear; it loops back. Failing to mention this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Asset Allocation with Security Selection** — Many students merge these two steps. They are distinct: allocation = asset class %, selection = specific securities within class.
> 4. **Omitting examples** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards application-oriented answers. Always include at least one example per step.
> 5. **Ignoring constraints** — In Step 1, students often mention only objectives, forgetting liquidity, tax, and legal constraints.
> 6. **Memorizing without understanding the formulas** — Sharpe/Treynor/Jensen derivations may be asked in Part B; rote answers fail.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Boards):**
>
> ✅ Portfolio Management Process = **Systematic + Continuous + Goal-Oriented** set of activities.
>
> ✅ **7 Steps:** (1) Objectives & Constraints → (2) Investment Opportunities → (3) Asset Allocation → (4) Security Selection → (5) Execution → (6) Rebalancing → (7) Performance Evaluation.
>
> ✅ **Asset Allocation** is the most critical decision — accounts for >90% of return variability.
>
> ✅ **Strategic vs. Tactical:** Strategic = long-term baseline; Tactical = short-term opportunistic deviations.
>
> ✅ **Sharpe Ratio** uses total risk ($\sigma_p$); **Treynor Ratio** uses systematic risk ($\beta_p$).
>
> ✅ **Jensen's Alpha** measures abnormal return above CAPM-predicted return.
>
> ✅ **Rebalancing** can be calendar-based, threshold-based, or via CPPI.
>
> ✅ The process is **iterative** — feedback from Step 7 loops back to refine Step 1.
>
> ✅ **IPS (Investment Policy Statement)** is the written document emerging from Step 1.
>
> ✅ **Active vs. Passive** selection depends on market efficiency and investor's informational advantage.
>
> ✅ Always conclude answers with the **iterative nature** of the process for an extra mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pooled Investments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pooled Investments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pooled Investments** are collective investment vehicles that aggregate capital from numerous investors to acquire a diversified portfolio of securities, real assets, or financial instruments. A professional fund manager administers the pooled capital, and each investor receives units or shares proportionate to their contribution, with returns distributed according to the performance of the underlying asset pool.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (aligned with **NEP 2020** and **Outcome-Based Education**), pooled investments are studied under the broader umbrella of portfolio construction techniques. The fundamental economic premise rests on **risk diversification**, **economies of scale**, and **access to institutional-grade investment opportunities** that would otherwise be unavailable to individual retail investors.

### Categories of Pooled Investment Vehicles (KTU Syllabus Scope)

| Vehicle | Asset Class | Liquidity | Management Style |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mutual Funds** | Equities, Bonds, Hybrid | Daily NAV | Active / Passive |
| **Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs)** | Index-based / Sector | Intraday | Mostly Passive |
| **Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs)** | Commercial Property | Moderate | Active |
| **Unit Investment Trusts (UITs)** | Fixed Portfolio | Limited | Fixed |
| **Hedge Funds** | Multi-Asset / Derivatives | Lock-in Period | Active / Aggressive |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places special emphasis on **Mutual Funds** and **ETFs**, including the computation of **Net Asset Value (NAV)**, **expense ratios**, and the **front-end vs. back-end load** structures. Mastery of these is mandatory for ESE and continuous assessment.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a group of 100 office colleagues wanting to invest in the stock market, but each has only ₹5,000. Individually, ₹5,000 can barely buy one share of a quality company, leaving them exposed to **idiosyncratic (company-specific) risk**. 

Now imagine all 100 colleagues contribute their money into a common "kitty" (pool) of **₹5,00,000**. An experienced colleague (the **fund manager**) uses this corpus to buy 50 different stocks across 8 sectors. Each colleague now owns a "slice" of this diversified pie. 

**The magic?** Even if one company underperforms, the other 49 cushion the loss. The fund manager handles research, transactions, and compliance — services that no individual could afford alone.

This "kitty" is precisely what pooled investments formalize through legal, regulatory, and accounting structures.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of pooled investments as a "financial co-operative supermarket"**: you shop from a pre-built, professionally managed basket instead of buying every ingredient yourself.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary — Must-Know Terms

- **Net Asset Value (NAV):** The per-unit market value of the fund's assets minus liabilities.
- **Expense Ratio:** Annual fee charged by the fund house as a percentage of AUM (Assets Under Management).
- **Load:** A sales charge — **front-end load** (at purchase) or **back-end load** (at redemption).
- **AUM (Assets Under Management):** Total market value of all assets managed by the fund.
- **Unit / Share:** The minimum denomination representing proportional ownership.
- **Benchmark Index:** The reference index (e.g., Nifty 50, BSE Sensex) against which fund performance is measured.
- **Alpha:** Excess return of a fund over its benchmark.
- **Beta:** Sensitivity of fund returns to market movements.

---

## 1.4 Why Pooled Investments Matter — KTU 2024 Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corporate Finance Connection:** Pooled investments form the institutional bedrock of capital markets. Corporations raise capital from pooled vehicles (e.g., equity mutual funds are major IPO subscribers). For portfolio managers, pooled vehicles represent **off-the-shelf diversification tools** that satisfy **Course Outcome CO2** (Analyze investment avenues) and **CO3** (Construct and evaluate portfolios) under the 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Pooled Investment

A pooled investment entity operates through a **trust structure** typically involving four parties:

1. **Investor (Unit Holder):** Provides capital; receives units and proportional returns.
2. **Asset Management Company (AMC):** Manages the portfolio; makes buy/sell decisions.
3. **Trustee:** Independent fiduciary safeguarding investor interests (in India, often a bank).
4. **Custodian:** Holds the actual securities in segregated accounts (regulatory requirement).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Examiners frequently test the **fiduciary relationship** and the **segregation of duties** principle. Remember the formula: *Capital + Trust + Diversification = Pooled Investment*.

---

## 2.2 Mutual Funds — The Flagship Pooled Vehicle

### 2.2.1 Classification by Structure

- **Open-Ended Funds:** Units can be issued and redeemed continuously at NAV-based prices. No fixed maturity.
- **Closed-Ended Funds:** Fixed number of units issued via IPO; traded on stock exchanges; price may deviate from NAV (trades at premium/discount).

### 2.2.2 Classification by Investment Objective

| Category | Primary Goal | Typical Holdings |
|---|---|---|
| Growth Fund | Capital appreciation | High P/E equities |
| Value Fund | Buying undervalued stocks | Low P/B, high dividend |
| Income Fund | Regular income | Bonds, dividend stocks |
| Balanced / Hybrid Fund | Mix of growth + income | 60% equity / 40% debt |
| Index Fund | Match benchmark return | Replica of Nifty/Sensex |
| Money Market Fund | Capital preservation + liquidity | T-bills, commercial paper |

### 2.2.3 Load Structure Economics

$$ \text{Effective Investment} = \text{Gross Investment} \times (1 - \text{Front-End Load}) $$

$$ \text{Redemption Proceeds} = \text{Units Held} \times \text{NAV at Redemption} \times (1 - \text{Back-End Load}) $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to **annualize** returns when comparing load vs. no-load funds over different holding periods. Always compute the **APR (Annual Percentage Rate)** before drawing conclusions.

---

## 2.3 Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) — The Hybrid Innovation

ETFs combine features of **mutual funds** (diversification, professional management) and **stocks** (intraday trading, limit orders). They typically track an index passively.

### 2.3.1 ETF Creation / Redemption Mechanism (In-Kind)

Authorized Participants (APs) deliver a basket of securities matching the index and receive ETF shares (creation), or vice versa (redemption). This arbitrage keeps market price ≈ NAV.

---

## 2.4 REITs — Real Estate Pooling

Real Estate Investment Trusts allow investors to pool capital for commercial real estate. Mandatory distribution of **≥ 90% of taxable income** as dividends makes REITs attractive for income-seeking investors.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Comprehensive

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $NAV = \dfrac{A - L}{N}$ | Net Asset Value per unit | Pricing of fund units |
| 2 | $A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times Q_i)$ | Total market value of assets | Portfolio valuation |
| 3 | $\text{Expense Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Fund Expenses}}{\text{Average AUM}}$ | Cost of fund management | Comparing fund costs |
| 4 | $\text{Total Return} = \dfrac{(NAV_1 - NAV_0) + D}{NAV_0}$ | Return including distributions | Performance measurement |
| 5 | $CAGR = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Compound Annual Growth Rate | Multi-year comparisons |
| 6 | $\alpha = R_p - [R_f + \beta_p (R_m - R_f)]$ | Jensen's Alpha | Risk-adjusted performance |
| 7 | $\beta_p = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_p, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$ | Portfolio Beta | Systematic risk measure |
| 8 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Var}(e_p)}{\text{Var}(R_p)}$ | Coefficient of determination | Diversification effectiveness |
| 9 | $\text{Sharpe Ratio} = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Risk-adjusted return per unit risk | Comparing funds |
| 10 | $\text{Treynor Ratio} = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\beta_p}$ | Excess return per unit systematic risk | Portfolio vs. market |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention:** $A$ = Total Assets at market value, $L$ = Total Liabilities, $N$ = Number of outstanding units, $P_i$ = Price of asset $i$, $Q_i$ = Quantity of asset $i$, $D$ = Distributions per unit, $R_p$ = Portfolio return, $R_f$ = Risk-free rate, $R_m$ = Market return, $\sigma_p$ = Portfolio standard deviation, $n$ = Number of years.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Business Utility

- **Corporate Treasury Operations:** Firms park short-term surplus in **liquid pooled funds** (money market funds) to optimize idle cash.
- **Pension Fund Management:** Retirement schemes deploy pooled vehicles for low-cost, diversified exposure.
- **Wealth Management:** HNIs (High Net-worth Individuals) access **AIFs (Alternative Investment Funds)** — pooled structures for private equity, venture capital.
- **Market Stability:** Pooled vehicles amplify market liquidity and price discovery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Asset Value (NAV) Computation

### Formal Statement

The NAV represents the per-unit intrinsic value of a mutual fund. It is calculated at the close of each trading day using market prices.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify Total Assets at Market Value**

Sum the market value of all securities held by the fund:

$$ A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times Q_i) $$

Where $P_i$ is the closing market price of security $i$ and $Q_i$ is the quantity held.

**Step 2: Identify Total Liabilities**

Sum all outstanding obligations of the fund:

$$ L = \sum_{j=1}^{m} L_j $$

These include management fees accrued, pending redemptions, borrowings, and expenses payable.

**Step 3: Compute Net Assets**

Subtract liabilities from assets:

$$ \text{Net Assets} = A - L $$

**Step 4: Determine Number of Outstanding Units**

$$ N = \text{Total Units Issued} - \text{Units Redeemed} $$

**Step 5: Compute NAV per Unit**

$$ NAV = \dfrac{A - L}{N} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is **foundational** for KTU numerical questions. Memorize the logic, not just the formula.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — NAV Calculation

### Problem
A mutual fund holds the following portfolio at day's end:

| Security | Quantity | Closing Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Reliance Industries | 10,000 | 2,800 |
| TCS | 5,000 | 3,600 |
| HDFC Bank | 8,000 | 1,650 |
| Government Securities | 20,000 | 105 |
| Cash & Equivalents | — | 5,00,000 |

**Liabilities:** Accrued management fee ₹1,20,000; pending redemptions ₹3,80,000.  
**Outstanding Units:** 5,00,000

### Solution

**Step 1: Total Assets**

Reliance: $10{,}000 \times 2{,}800 = 2{,}80{,}00{,}000$

TCS: $5{,}000 \times 3{,}600 = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000$

HDFC Bank: $8{,}000 \times 1{,}650 = 1{,}32{,}00{,}000$

Govt Securities: $20{,}000 \times 105 = 21{,}00{,}000$

Cash: $5{,}00{,}000$

$$ A = 2{,}80{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}32{,}00{,}000 + 21{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 $$

$$ A = 6{,}18{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹6.18 Crore)} $$

**Step 2: Total Liabilities**

$$ L = 1{,}20{,}000 + 3{,}80{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 3: Net Assets**

$$ A - L = 6{,}18{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}13{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 4: NAV**

$$ NAV = \dfrac{6{,}13{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = \dfrac{613}{5} = 122.60 $$

**Result:** $NAV = ₹122.60 \text{ per unit}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Expense Ratio Impact on Investor Returns

### Formal Setup

The expense ratio $E$ represents the fraction of AUM consumed annually by the fund. Over $T$ years, the drag on returns compounds.

### Derivation

Gross annual return of the fund before expenses: $r_g$

Net annual return to investor:

$$ r_n = r_g - E $$

If the fund value compounds over $T$ years:

$$ V_f^{gross} = V_i \times (1 + r_g)^T $$

$$ V_f^{net} = V_i \times (1 + r_g - E)^T $$

**Dollar Cost of Expenses over T years:**

$$ \text{Cost} = V_i \times \left[(1 + r_g)^T - (1 + r_g - E)^T\right] $$

### Numerical Illustration

Suppose $V_i = ₹1{,}00{,}000$, $r_g = 12\%$, $E = 1.5\%$, $T = 10$ years.

$$ V_f^{gross} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.12)^{10} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 3.1058 = 3{,}10{,}580 $$

$$ V_f^{net} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (0.12 - 0.015 + 1)^{10} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.105)^{10} $$

$$ (1.105)^{10} \approx 2.694 $$

$$ V_f^{net} \approx 2{,}69{,}400 $$

$$ \text{Expense Drag} = 3{,}10{,}580 - 2{,}69{,}400 = ₹41{,}180 $$

> [!WARNING]
> A seemingly small **1.5% expense ratio** can erode over **₹41,000** of a ₹1 lakh investment over a decade. This is a frequent KTU viva question on investor awareness.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Sharpe Ratio of a Pooled Fund

The Sharpe Ratio measures **excess return per unit of total risk**.

### Derivation

Excess return over risk-free rate:

$$ \text{Excess Return} = \bar{R_p} - R_f $$

Standard deviation of fund returns (total risk):

$$ \sigma_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T-1} \sum_{t=1}^{T} (R_{p,t} - \bar{R_p})^2} $$

Sharpe Ratio:

$$ S = \dfrac{\bar{R_p} - R_f}{\sigma_p} $$

### Interpretation Matrix

| Sharpe Ratio Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $S < 0$ | Fund underperforms risk-free asset |
| $0 \leq S < 1$ | Sub-optimal risk-adjusted return |
| $1 \leq S < 2$ | Good performance |
| $2 \leq S < 3$ | Very good performance |
| $S \geq 3$ | Exceptional (often leverage-driven) |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Pooled Vehicle

| Investor Profile | Recommended Vehicle | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Young professional (high risk appetite, long horizon) | Equity Growth Fund / Sector ETF | Wealth accumulation through equities |
| Pre-retiree (capital preservation) | Balanced Fund / Debt Fund | Stability with moderate growth |
| Retiree (regular income) | REIT / Dividend Yield Fund / Income Fund | Cash flow via distributions |
| Conservative investor (liquidity priority) | Liquid Fund / Money Market Fund | Daily liquidity, low volatility |
| Tax-conscious HNI | Index ETF (low turnover) | Tax efficiency via minimal distributions |
| Speculative investor (high risk, lock-in tolerance) | Hedge Fund / AIF Category III | Aggressive strategies, alternative assets |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pooled Investment Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multiple Investors Contribute Capital] --> B[Pooled Corpus Formed]
    B --> C[AMC Receives Mandate]
    C --> D[Fund Manager Constructs Portfolio]
    D --> E[Buy Securities Across Asset Classes]
    E --> F[Daily NAV Computation]
    F --> G{Investor Action}
    G -->|Subscribe| H[Issue New Units at NAV]
    G -->|Redeem| I[Cancel Units at NAV]
    H --> J[Proportional Ownership Allocated]
    I --> K[Proceeds Disbursed]
    J --> L[Periodic Distributions / NAV Growth]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Reinvestment or Withdrawal]
```

---

## 4.2 Mutual Fund Structure — Four-Party Trust Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inv1[Investor A] --> amc
    inv2[Investor B] --> amc
    inv3[Investor C] --> amc
    amc[Asset Management Company]
    amc --> fm[Fund Manager]
    fm --> pf[Investment Portfolio]
    trustee[Independent Trustee] -.->|Oversight| amc
    custodian[Custodian Bank] -.->|Safekeeping| pf
    sebi[SEBI Regulator] -.->|Compliance| amc
    sebi -.->|Compliance| trustee
    sebi -.->|Compliance| custodian
```

---

## 4.3 NAV Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Step 1: List All Securities Held] --> s2[Step 2: Multiply Quantity x Market Price]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3: Sum to Get Total Assets]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4: Sum All Liabilities]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5: Net Assets = Assets - Liabilities]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6: Count Outstanding Units]
    s6 --> s7[Step 7: NAV = Net Assets / Units]
    s7 --> s8[Publish NAV to Investors]
```

---

## 4.4 Classification Tree of Pooled Investments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Pooled Investments]
    root --> mf[Mutual Funds]
    root --> etf[Exchange Traded Funds]
    root --> reit[REITs]
    root --> uit[Unit Investment Trusts]
    root --> hf[Hedge Funds]
    mf --> mf1[Open Ended]
    mf --> mf2[Closed Ended]
    mf1 --> mf1a[Growth Fund]
    mf1 --> mf1b[Value Fund]
    mf1 --> mf1c[Income Fund]
    mf1 --> mf1d[Index Fund]
    mf1 --> mf1e[Money Market Fund]
    mf2 --> mf2a[Listed Fixed Maturity]
    etf --> etf1[Equity ETF]
    etf --> etf2[Bond ETF]
    etf --> etf3[Commodity ETF]
    etf --> etf4[International ETF]
    reit --> reit1[Equity REIT]
    reit --> reit2[Mortgage REIT]
    reit --> reit3[Hybrid REIT]
    hf --> hf1[Long Short]
    hf --> hf2[Event Driven]
    hf --> hf3[Global Macro]
    hf --> hf4[Quantitative]
```

---

## 4.5 Cost-of-Ownership Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Investor Capital Input] --> blockB[Gross Return Generation]
    blockB --> blockC[Expense Ratio Deduction]
    blockC --> blockD[Load Fee Deduction if Applicable]
    blockD --> blockE[Tax on Distributions]
    blockE --> blockF[Net Investor Return]
    blockB -.-> blockG[Compounding Effect Multiplier]
    blockG -.-> blockF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define "Pooled Investment Vehicle." List any three types with one distinguishing feature each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Pooled Investment Vehicle (PIV)** is a financial structure that aggregates capital from multiple investors for collective investment in securities or assets, with each investor holding proportional units.

**Three types:**

1. **Mutual Fund:** Pooled capital managed by an AMC; NAV-based pricing; daily subscription/redemption (open-ended).
2. **Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF):** Trades on stock exchanges like a stock; tracks an index passively; intraday liquidity.
3. **Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT):** Invests in income-generating real estate; mandated ≥ 90% distribution of taxable income as dividends.

*(Each valid type with distinguishing feature: 1 mark. Definition: 1 mark. Total: 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of "Expense Ratio" in a mutual fund. Why is it important for investors?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Expense Ratio** is the annual fee expressed as a percentage of Average Assets Under Management (AUM) that a mutual fund charges to cover operating costs — including management fees, administrative expenses, marketing (12b-1 fees), and custodial charges.

$$ \text{Expense Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Operating Expenses}}{\text{Average AUM}} $$

**Importance for Investors:**
- **Direct drag on returns:** Every percentage point of expense reduces net returns.
- **Compounding impact:** Over long horizons, even 1% difference can cost lakhs.
- **Benchmarking tool:** Allows comparison of cost-efficiency across similar funds.
- **Indicator of management style:** Index funds typically have ratios < 0.5%, while actively managed funds may have 1–2%.

*(Definition with formula: 1 mark. Importance with at least 2 valid points: 2 marks. Total: 3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the structure and operational mechanism of a Mutual Fund. Discuss the role of AMC, Trustee, and Custodian in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Structure of a Mutual Fund:**

A mutual fund is constituted as a **trust** under the Indian Trusts Act, 1882, and is regulated by SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 1996. It has a four-tier structure:

**1. Sponsor (Setter):**
- The promoter entity that establishes the trust.
- Must contribute a minimum of 40% of net worth of the AMC.

**2. Asset Management Company (AMC):**
- Manages the fund's portfolio on behalf of unit holders.
- Makes investment decisions, executes trades, and handles investor servicing.
- Earns management fees (typically 0.5%–2% of AUM).
- Required to be registered with SEBI; minimum net worth ₹50 crore.

**3. Trustee:**
- Independent fiduciary that protects investor interests.
- Ensures AMC operates within the trust deed and regulations.
- Cannot be associated with the AMC.
- Provides oversight and grievance redressal mechanism.

**4. Custodian:**
- Holds securities in segregated accounts to prevent misuse.
- Settles trades, collects dividends/interest, and reports to AMC.
- Provides safekeeping and operational integrity.

**Operational Mechanism:**
1. Investor submits application with KYC compliance.
2. AMC allots units at prevailing NAV.
3. Capital pooled in a common fund corpus.
4. Fund manager deploys capital across securities.
5. Daily NAV computed and published.
6. Periodic distributions (dividend) or NAV appreciation yields returns.
7. Redemption requests honored at applicable NAV (T+1/T+2 settlement).

*(Structure with parties: 4 marks. Operational mechanism: 3 marks. Total: 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Given the following portfolio of an equity mutual fund, calculate the NAV per unit. (7 marks)**

| Stock | Quantity | Closing Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Infosys | 20,000 | 1,450 |
| Wipro | 30,000 | 425 |
| ICICI Bank | 15,000 | 1,025 |
| Tata Steel | 25,000 | 130 |
| Cash | — | 8,50,000 |

- Accrued expenses: ₹2,75,000
- Outstanding units: 6,00,000

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total Asset Value**

Infosys: $20{,}000 \times 1{,}450 = 2{,}90{,}00{,}000$

Wipro: $30{,}000 \times 425 = 1{,}27{,}50{,}000$

ICICI Bank: $15{,}000 \times 1{,}025 = 1{,}53{,}75{,}000$

Tata Steel: $25{,}000 \times 130 = 32{,}50{,}000$

Cash: $8{,}50{,}000$

$$ A = 2{,}90{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}27{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}53{,}75{,}000 + 32{,}50{,}000 + 8{,}50{,}000 = 6{,}12{,}25{,}000 $$

**Step 2: Total Liabilities**

$$ L = ₹2{,}75{,}000 $$

**Step 3: Net Assets**

$$ A - L = 6{,}12{,}25{,}000 - 2{,}75{,}000 = 6{,}09{,}50{,}000 $$

**Step 4: NAV**

$$ NAV = \dfrac{6{,}09{,}50{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 101.58 $$

**Answer:** $NAV = ₹101.58 \text{ per unit}$

*(Asset calculation: 3 marks. Liability + net assets: 2 marks. NAV calculation: 2 marks. Total: 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Differentiate between Open-Ended and Closed-Ended Mutual Funds. Compare Mutual Funds with Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Open-Ended vs. Closed-Ended Mutual Funds:**

| Feature | Open-Ended | Closed-Ended |
|---|---|---|
| Unit Availability | Continuous issue/redemption | Fixed (IPO only) |
| Pricing | NAV-based | Market-driven (may trade at premium/discount to NAV) |
| Liquidity | Daily at NAV | Exchange-based trading |
| Maturity | No fixed maturity | Fixed maturity (often 3–5 years) |
| Fund Size | Variable (expands/contracts) | Fixed at IPO |
| Listing | Not listed | Listed on stock exchange |

**Mutual Funds vs. ETFs:**

| Feature | Mutual Fund | ETF |
|---|---|---|
| Trading | End-of-day NAV | Intraday, live market price |
| Management Style | Often active | Mostly passive (index-tracking) |
| Expense Ratio | Higher (1–2%) | Lower (0.1–0.5%) |
| Minimum Investment | Often ₹500–₹1,000 | 1 unit (market price) |
| Tax Efficiency | Year-end capital gains distribution | In-kind redemption minimizes tax events |
| Transparency | Monthly portfolio disclosure | Daily disclosure |

*(Open vs. closed comparison: 3 marks. MF vs. ETF: 4 marks. Total: 7 marks)*

---

**(b) An investor holds 10,000 units of a mutual fund. The NAV at purchase was ₹100. After 3 years, NAV is ₹148 with dividends of ₹8 per unit received annually. The fund has a 1.2% expense ratio and a 1% exit load. Calculate the net CAGR earned by the investor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Initial Investment**

$$ V_i = 10{,}000 \times 100 = ₹10{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 2: Total Dividends Received (3 years)**

$$ D_{total} = 10{,}000 \times 8 \times 3 = ₹2{,}40{,}000 $$

**Step 3: Value Before Exit Load**

$$ V_{gross} = 10{,}000 \times 148 = ₹14{,}80{,}000 $$

**Step 4: Apply Exit Load (1%)**

$$ V_{after\_load} = 14{,}80{,}000 \times (1 - 0.01) = ₹14{,}65{,}200 $$

**Step 5: Total Value to Investor**

$$ V_{total} = 14{,}65{,}200 + 2{,}40{,}000 = ₹17{,}05{,}200 $$

**Step 6: Compound Annual Growth Rate**

$$ CAGR = \left(\dfrac{V_{total}}{V_i}\right)^{1/n} - 1 = \left(\dfrac{17{,}05{,}200}{10{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/3} - 1 $$

$$ CAGR = (1.7052)^{1/3} - 1 $$

$$ CAGR = 1.1948 - 1 = 0.1948 $$

$$ CAGR \approx 19.48\% $$

**Step 7: Note on Expense Ratio**

The CAGR calculated (19.48%) is the **gross of expense ratio**. The AMC deducts 1.2% annually from the fund's assets before computing NAV, so the published NAV already reflects expense drag. The investor's effective CAGR is the 19.48% realized above.

*(Investment identification: 1 mark. Dividend calculation: 1 mark. Exit load application: 1 mark. Total proceeds: 1 mark. CAGR formula application: 2 marks. Final answer: 1 mark. Total: 7 marks)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Pooled Investments Questions:**
>
> 1. **NAV Calculation Errors:** Students frequently forget to **subtract liabilities** before dividing by units. Examiner specifically tests: $NAV = (A - L)/N$, **NOT** $NAV = A/N$.
>
> 2. **Closed-End Fund Misconception:** Students assume closed-end funds trade at NAV. They trade at **market price**, which can differ from NAV due to supply-demand.
>
> 3. **Expense Ratio Application:** Don't deduct expense ratio from investor's gross return — it is **already netted** in the NAV. This confuses nearly 40% of candidates.
>
> 4. **ETF vs. Mutual Fund Tax:** Students often incorrectly state ETFs are tax-free. They are tax-efficient due to in-kind creation, but capital gains tax still applies on sale.
>
> 5. **CAGR Formula:** Confusing simple annualized return with CAGR. Always use the geometric mean: $(V_f/V_i)^{1/n} - 1$.
>
> 6. **Unit Confusion:** Failing to multiply dividends by number of units held, or forgetting to add dividends to final value.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pooled Investments (KTU Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Pooled investments aggregate capital from multiple investors into a professionally managed, diversified portfolio.
- **Core Categories:** Mutual Funds, ETFs, REITs, UITs, Hedge Funds, AIFs.
- **Mutual Fund Types:** Open-ended (continuous NAV pricing) vs. Closed-ended (fixed units, exchange-traded).
- **Investment Objectives:** Growth, Value, Income, Balanced, Index, Money Market.
- **NAV Formula:** $NAV = (A - L)/N$ — apply daily; core to all numerical questions.
- **Expense Ratio:** Annualized fee as % of AUM; reduces investor returns.
- **Load Fees:** Front-end (purchase) vs. Back-end (redemption); affects net returns.
- **ETF Mechanics:** Trade intraday, low expense ratios, in-kind creation/redemption, tax-efficient.
- **REIT Specifics:** Invest in real estate, mandated high payout ratios.
- **Risk Metrics:** Beta (systematic risk), Alpha (excess return), Sharpe (risk-adjusted), R² (diversification).
- **Performance Evaluation:** Compare against benchmark; evaluate risk-adjusted returns, not just absolute returns.
- **Regulatory Framework:** In India, SEBI governs mutual funds; in USA, SEC and FINRA.
- **Diversification Principle:** Pooling reduces idiosyncratic risk but **not** systematic (market) risk.
- **Investor Suitability:** Match fund type to risk appetite, time horizon, and liquidity needs.
- **Practical Caution:** Past performance ≠ future results; expense ratios compound significantly over time.
- **KTU Hot Spots:** NAV computation, AMC-Trustee-Custodian roles, ETF vs. MF comparison, CAGR derivation, Sharpe Ratio interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Portfolio Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Portfolio Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 3: PORTFOLIO CONCEPTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Portfolio** is defined as a collection of financial assets—such as stocks, bonds, commodities, cash equivalents, and alternative investments—held jointly by an individual or institutional investor with the objective of generating returns while managing exposure to risk. **Portfolio Management** is the art and science of selecting, monitoring, and revising a group of investments to achieve a specified investment objective, balancing the **risk-return trade-off** in alignment with the investor's risk tolerance, time horizon, and financial goals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Core Theme of Module 3**
> The module centres on the foundational vocabulary, principles, and strategic framework that an investor employs *before* mathematical optimization (Markowitz Modern Portfolio Theory, CAPM, etc., covered later). The emphasis is on **concepts**, **process**, and **policy framework**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hosting a grand Indian wedding feast (a *sadya* on a banana leaf). You wouldn't serve only *payasam* (sweet) or only *avial*—that would be either too sweet or too bitter. A wise host curates **24 different dishes** so that the strengths of one item mask the weaknesses of another.

A **Portfolio** is the investor's *sadya*. Each dish is a **security** (a stock, a bond, a gold ETF). Some dishes are spicy (high-risk, high-return), some are bland (low-risk, low-return). Combined intelligently, the overall meal is **palatable** (acceptable risk) and **satisfying** (good expected return). Mixing is **Diversification**.

---

### 1.1.1 The Two Pillars: Risk and Return

| Term | Standard Metric | Plain English |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Return** | $\mu$ or $\bar{R}$ (expected return, in %) | The *reward* an investor earns; money made from capital gains + dividends/interest. |
| **Risk** | $\sigma$ (standard deviation) or **Variance** $\sigma^2$ | The *uncertainty* about actual returns; the probability that expected returns may not materialize. |
| **Risk Tolerance** | Qualitative scale (Conservative → Aggressive) | The investor's psychological + financial capacity to absorb losses without panic-selling. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Axiom of Finance**
> "There is no such thing as a free lunch." Higher expected returns **always** come packaged with higher risk. Portfolio Management is essentially about obtaining the **best possible return for each unit of risk** assumed—mathematically, maximising the **Sharpe Ratio** (covered in Module 4).

---

### 1.1.2 Classification of Investors (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Investor Type | Risk Tolerance | Primary Goal | Typical Time Horizon |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conservative** | Low | Capital preservation | Short (1–3 years) |
| **Moderate / Balanced** | Medium | Income + growth | Medium (3–7 years) |
| **Aggressive / Growth** | High | Wealth accumulation | Long (> 7 years) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Portfolio Management Process (5-Stage Cycle)

KTU examiners frequently ask students to *"List and explain the stages of the portfolio management process"*—this is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in every End Semester Exam (ESE). Memorise the five stages below as a **closed loop**:

### Stage 1 — Identification of Objectives
- Determine the investor's **return objectives** (target capital growth, current income, capital preservation).
- Determine the **risk tolerance** (ability and willingness to take risk).
- Identify **constraints**: liquidity needs, tax bracket, time horizon, regulatory restrictions (e.g., Shariah-compliant funds, PF regulations).

### Stage 2 — Selection of Asset Mix (Strategic Asset Allocation)
- Decide the broad categories: e.g., **60% equity, 30% debt, 10% gold**.
- This is the **single most important** decision—strategic asset allocation accounts for roughly **90% of portfolio return variability** (a celebrated finding by Brinson, Hood & Beebower, 1986).

### Stage 3 — Security Selection (Active Decision)
- Within each asset class, pick specific securities (e.g., Infosys vs TCS, Reliance vs ONGC).
- Active managers use **fundamental analysis**, **technical analysis**, or **quantitative screens**.

### Stage 4 — Portfolio Execution (Trading)
- Place orders on the exchange; consider **transaction costs**, **bid-ask spread**, **market impact**, and **timing**.

### Stage 5 — Portfolio Monitoring & Rebalancing
- Track performance vs. benchmark.
- **Rebalance** when actual weights drift from target weights by a pre-defined tolerance band (e.g., ±5%).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the 5 Stages: "I-S-S-E-M"**
> **I**dentify → **S**et asset mix → **S**elect securities → **E**xecute → **M**onitor & Rebalance.

---

## 2.2 Types of Portfolio Risk (Decomposition)

The total risk of a portfolio, measured by the variance $\sigma_P^2$, can be decomposed as:

$$\sigma_P^2 = \underbrace{\beta_P^2 \, \sigma_M^2}_{\text{Systematic Risk}} + \underbrace{\sigma_{eP}^2}_{\text{Unsystematic Risk}}$$

### 2.2.1 Systematic Risk (Market / Non-Diversifiable Risk)

- Affects the **entire market** (e.g., RBI rate hike, recession, war, oil shock).
- Cannot be eliminated through diversification.
- Measured by **Beta** ($\beta$).
- Examples: **Interest rate risk**, **inflation risk**, **market risk**, **currency risk**, **political risk**.

### 2.2.2 Unsystematic Risk (Firm-Specific / Diversifiable Risk)

- Affects a **specific company or industry**.
- **Can be reduced** by holding a well-diversified portfolio.
- Examples: **Management failure**, **product recall**, **strike at a factory**, **lawsuits**, **earnings miss**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight**
> By holding approximately **20–30 randomly selected stocks** across industries, an investor can virtually **eliminate** unsystematic risk. Systematic risk remains, which is why even a perfectly diversified Nifty 50 index fund still moves when the market crashes.

---

## 2.3 Diversification — The "Only Free Lunch in Finance"

Diversification is the practice of spreading investments across different assets to reduce unsystematic risk. The principle rests on **correlation**:

$$\rho_{ij} = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_i \, \sigma_j} \quad ; \quad -1 \leq \rho_{ij} \leq +1$$

- $\rho = +1$: Perfectly positive correlation → **no diversification benefit**.
- $\rho = 0$: Uncorrelated → **moderate diversification benefit**.
- $\rho = -1$: Perfectly negatively correlated → **maximum diversification benefit** (theoretically risk can be eliminated).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for KTU**: Numerical problems on portfolio variance with 2 assets are routine. The formula you must memorise is in Section 3.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Expected Return of a Single Asset | $\bar{R} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \, R_i$ | $P_i$ = probability, $R_i$ = return in state $i$ |
| 2 | Expected Return of Portfolio (2 assets) | $E(R_P) = w_A \, E(R_A) + w_B \, E(R_B)$ | $w_A + w_B = 1$ |
| 3 | Portfolio Variance (2 assets) | $\sigma_P^2 = w_A^2\sigma_A^2 + w_B^2\sigma_B^2 + 2w_Aw_B\rho_{AB}\sigma_A\sigma_B$ | $\rho_{AB}$ = correlation |
| 4 | Portfolio Standard Deviation | $\sigma_P = \sqrt{\sigma_P^2}$ | Always positive |
| 5 | Covariance | $\text{Cov}(A,B) = \rho_{AB}\sigma_A\sigma_B$ | Measure of co-movement |
| 6 | Correlation | $\rho_{AB} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(A,B)}{\sigma_A\sigma_B}$ | Bounded: $-1 \leq \rho \leq +1$ |
| 7 | Beta | $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_M)}{\sigma_M^2}$ | Sensitivity to market |
| 8 | Sharpe Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{R_P - R_f}{\sigma_P}$ | Reward per unit of total risk |
| 9 | Treynor Ratio | $T = \dfrac{R_P - R_f}{\beta_P}$ | Reward per unit of systematic risk |
| 10 | Jensen's Alpha | $\alpha_P = R_P - [R_f + \beta_P(R_M - R_f)]$ | Excess return over CAPM |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Formatting Rule**: All absolute-value / correlation notations use $\vert$ or $\rho$—**never** the literal pipe character `|`—to keep the markdown table parser happy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Return of a 2-Asset Portfolio

We start with two assets A and B. Let $w_A$ be the proportion of wealth invested in A and $w_B$ be the proportion invested in B.

Since the entire capital must be deployed, the **budget constraint** is:

$$w_A + w_B = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_B = 1 - w_A$$

The dollar return from asset A on an investment of $I$ is $I \cdot w_A \cdot R_A$, where $R_A$ is the rate of return (in decimal form). Similarly for asset B. The **total dollar return** of the portfolio is:

$$\text{Total Return} = I \cdot w_A \cdot R_A + I \cdot w_B \cdot R_B$$

Dividing both sides by the original investment $I$ to get the portfolio return $R_P$:

$$R_P = w_A \cdot R_A + w_B \cdot R_B$$

Taking the **expectation** operator on both sides, and noting that weights are constants:

$$\mathbb{E}(R_P) = w_A \cdot \mathbb{E}(R_A) + w_B \cdot \mathbb{E}(R_B)$$

$$\boxed{\,E(R_P) = w_A \, E(R_A) + w_B \, E(R_B)\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Portfolio Variance of 2 Assets

Starting from the definition of variance for a random variable:

$$\sigma_P^2 = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(R_P - \mathbb{E}(R_P)\right)^2\right]$$

Substituting $R_P = w_A R_A + w_B R_B$:

$$\sigma_P^2 = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(w_A R_A + w_B R_B - w_A E(R_A) - w_B E(R_B)\right)^2\right]$$

Grouping terms:

$$\sigma_P^2 = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(w_A (R_A - E(R_A)) + w_B (R_B - E(R_B))\right)^2\right]$$

Expanding the square using $(x+y)^2 = x^2 + 2xy + y^2$:

$$\sigma_P^2 = w_A^2 \mathbb{E}\left[(R_A - E(R_A))^2\right] + w_B^2 \mathbb{E}\left[(R_B - E(R_B))^2\right] + 2 w_A w_B \mathbb{E}\left[(R_A - E(R_A))(R_B - E(R_B))\right]$$

Recognising each term:

$$\sigma_P^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \, \text{Cov}(A,B)$$

Substituting the identity $\text{Cov}(A,B) = \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$:

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_P^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **[Past KTU Pattern]** "Mr. Krishna is constructing a 2-asset portfolio with the following data: Asset X has expected return 18% and standard deviation 22%. Asset Y has expected return 12% and standard deviation 14%. The correlation coefficient is 0.30. If Krishna invests 60% in X and 40% in Y, calculate the expected return and standard deviation of the portfolio."

### Step 1 — Extract Given Data

$$E(R_X) = 18\%, \quad \sigma_X = 22\%$$

$$E(R_Y) = 12\%, \quad \sigma_Y = 14\%$$

$$\rho_{XY} = 0.30, \quad w_X = 0.60, \quad w_Y = 0.40$$

### Step 2 — Expected Return of Portfolio

$$E(R_P) = w_X \cdot E(R_X) + w_Y \cdot E(R_Y)$$

$$E(R_P) = (0.60)(18) + (0.40)(12)$$

$$E(R_P) = 10.80 + 4.80$$

$$\boxed{\,E(R_P) = 15.60\%\,}$$

### Step 3 — Covariance Term

$$\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \rho_{XY} \cdot \sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y$$

$$\text{Cov}(X,Y) = (0.30)(22)(14)$$

$$\text{Cov}(X,Y) = 0.30 \times 308 = 92.40$$

### Step 4 — Portfolio Variance

$$\sigma_P^2 = w_X^2 \sigma_X^2 + w_Y^2 \sigma_Y^2 + 2 w_X w_Y \text{Cov}(X,Y)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.60)^2 (22)^2 + (0.40)^2 (14)^2 + 2(0.60)(0.40)(92.40)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.36)(484) + (0.16)(196) + (0.48)(92.40)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = 174.24 + 31.36 + 44.352$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = 249.952$$

### Step 5 — Portfolio Standard Deviation

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{249.952}$$

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_P \approx 15.81\%\,}$$

### Interpretation

The portfolio's expected return of **15.60%** is a weighted average of the individual returns, while the standard deviation of **15.81%** is *lower* than the simple weighted average of individual standard deviations—which is exactly the diversification benefit quantified.

$$\text{Naive avg. SD} = (0.60)(22) + (0.40)(14) = 18.80\%$$

$$\text{Diversification gain} = 18.80 - 15.81 = 2.99 \text{ percentage points}$$

---

## 3.4 Extension: Correlation's Power — A Limiting Case Study

| Case | $\rho_{XY}$ | $\sigma_P$ (from above data) | Insight |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $+1.0$ | $18.80\%$ | **No benefit**—portfolio SD = weighted average SD. |
| 2 | $+0.3$ | $15.81\%$ | **Moderate benefit**—realistic market case. |
| 3 | $0.0$ | $14.34\%$ | **Higher benefit**—uncorrelated assets. |
| 4 | $-1.0$ | $5.60\%$ | **Maximum benefit**—risk could be hedged fully. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut**: When $\rho = +1$, you can solve this **in your head**—no need to compute covariance. KTU valuators award full marks for stating this property explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Portfolio Management Lifecycle (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STAGE 1: IDENTIFY OBJECTIVES"] --> nodeB["STAGE 2: STRATEGIC ASSET ALLOCATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STAGE 3: SECURITY SELECTION"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STAGE 4: PORTFOLIO EXECUTION"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STAGE 5: MONITOR AND REBALANCE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"DEVIATION GREATER THAN TOLERANCE BAND?"}
    nodeF -->|"YES"| nodeB
    nodeF -->|"NO"| nodeE
```

> **Interpretation**: Note the feedback loop from Stage 5 back to Stage 2 — rebalancing is *not* a one-time event but a continuous discipline.

---

## 4.2 Risk Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["TOTAL PORTFOLIO RISK SIGMA P SQUARED"] --> node2["SYSTEMATIC RISK MARKET WIDE"]
    node1 --> node3["UNSYSTEMATIC RISK FIRM SPECIFIC"]
    node2 --> node4["INTEREST RATE"]
    node2 --> node5["INFLATION"]
    node2 --> node6["POLITICAL"]
    node2 --> node7["CURRENCY"]
    node3 --> node8["MANAGEMENT FAILURE"]
    node3 --> node9["PRODUCT RECALL"]
    node3 --> node10["LAWSUIT"]
    node3 --> node11["DIVERSIFICATION REDUCES TO ZERO"]
```

> **Interpretation**: Systematic risk is the *unavoidable* floor; unsystematic risk is what diversification knocks out.

---

## 4.3 Investor Decision Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["INVESTOR PROFILE"] --> stepB["RISK TOLERANCE TEST"]
    stepB --> stepC["TIME HORIZON"]
    stepC --> stepD{"CONSERVATIVE MODERATE AGGRESSIVE"}
    stepD -->|"LOW RISK"| stepE["70 PERCENT DEBT 25 PERCENT EQUITY 5 PERCENT GOLD"]
    stepD -->|"MEDIUM RISK"| stepF["40 PERCENT DEBT 50 PERCENT EQUITY 10 PERCENT GOLD"]
    stepD -->|"HIGH RISK"| stepG["10 PERCENT DEBT 80 PERCENT EQUITY 10 PERCENT ALTERNATIVES"]
```

---

## 4.4 Active vs. Passive Portfolio Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    choiceA["PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT APPROACH"]
    choiceA --> choiceB["ACTIVE MANAGEMENT"]
    choiceA --> choiceC["PASSIVE MANAGEMENT"]
    choiceB --> detailB["BEAT BENCHMARK VIA STOCK PICKING AND MARKET TIMING HIGHER FEES HIGHER TAX BURNOUT"]
    choiceC --> detailC["REPLICATE BENCHMARK INDEX LOW FEES LOW TAX DISCIPLINED"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a portfolio. What is meant by diversification?**

**Model Answer**:
A **portfolio** is a collection of financial assets held by an investor—such as stocks, bonds, gold, and cash equivalents—with the aim of generating returns while managing risk. **Diversification** is the strategy of combining assets that are not perfectly positively correlated, so that the unsystematic risk of individual securities gets partially or fully cancelled out. Mathematically, when $\rho_{AB} < +1$, the portfolio variance is **less** than the weighted average of individual variances, giving the investor a "free lunch" of reduced risk without sacrificing expected return. *(Word limit ideal: ~80 words.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between systematic risk and unsystematic risk.**

**Model Answer**:

| Basis | Systematic Risk | Unsystematic Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Source | Market-wide (macro) | Firm / industry-specific |
| Diversifiable? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Measured by | Beta ($\beta$) | Residual standard deviation ($\sigma_e$) |
| Examples | Interest rate, inflation, war | Strike, lawsuit, fraud |

*(Tabular answer in ~70 words fetches full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Module 3 Internal Choice Rule**: Two alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are offered. **Attempt any ONE.**

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five stages of the portfolio management process**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Stock A has expected return **20%** and standard deviation **25%**. Stock B has expected return **14%** and standard deviation **18%**. The correlation between them is **0.40**. If an investor allocates **70% to A** and **30% to B**, compute the **expected return** and **standard deviation** of the portfolio. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

1. **Identification of Objectives (1 mark)**: Determine the investor's return goal (capital appreciation, income, preservation) and risk tolerance. Also identify constraints—liquidity, tax, time horizon.

2. **Selection of Asset Mix (1 mark)**: Allocate capital across broad asset classes (equity, debt, gold, cash). This is strategic asset allocation—the most critical decision.

3. **Security Selection (1 mark)**: Within each asset class, pick specific instruments using fundamental analysis, technical analysis, or quant screens.

4. **Portfolio Execution (1 mark)**: Place buy/sell orders; consider transaction costs, bid-ask spread, market timing.

5. **Portfolio Monitoring & Rebalancing (2 marks)**: Continuously compare actual weights with target weights. Rebalance when deviation exceeds the pre-defined tolerance band (commonly ±5%). Document the loop-back to Stage 2.

6. **Conclusion (1 mark)**: The process is a closed feedback loop, ensuring the portfolio adapts to changing market conditions and investor circumstances.

---

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given**: $E(R_A) = 20\%$, $\sigma_A = 25\%$, $E(R_B) = 14\%$, $\sigma_B = 18\%$, $\rho_{AB} = 0.40$, $w_A = 0.70$, $w_B = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — Expected Return**:

$$E(R_P) = (0.70)(20) + (0.30)(14) = 14.00 + 4.20 = \boxed{18.20\%}$$

**Step 2 — Covariance**:

$$\text{Cov}(A,B) = (0.40)(25)(18) = 180$$

**Step 3 — Portfolio Variance**:

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.70)^2(25)^2 + (0.30)^2(18)^2 + 2(0.70)(0.30)(180)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = (0.49)(625) + (0.09)(324) + (0.42)(180)$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = 306.25 + 29.16 + 75.60 = 411.01$$

**Step 4 — Portfolio Standard Deviation**:

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{411.01} \approx \boxed{20.27\%}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark)**: The weighted-average SD would be $(0.70)(25)+(0.30)(18) = 22.90\%$. The actual SD of $20.27\%$ is lower — confirming diversification benefit of **2.63 percentage points**.

---

### 📘 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the meaning and importance of an **Investment Policy Statement (IPS)**. What are its key components? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A portfolio consists of **three securities** X, Y, Z with the following weights and expected returns: $w_X = 0.50$, $E(R_X) = 15\%$; $w_Y = 0.30$, $E(R_Y) = 12\%$; $w_Z = 0.20$, $E(R_Z) = 10\%$. Compute the portfolio's expected return. If the correlation between X and Y is **0.50**, $\sigma_X = 20\%$, $\sigma_Y = 15\%$, calculate the variance contribution of just these two assets (treat as a sub-portfolio with 70% combined weight). *(7 marks)*

---

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

An **Investment Policy Statement (IPS)** is a written document drafted jointly by the investor and the portfolio manager that codifies the investor's objectives, constraints, and the strategic approach to portfolio management. It acts as a **rulebook** that prevents emotional decision-making during market turbulence.

**Key Components (1 mark each)**:

1. **Investment Objectives** — Return target (nominal & real) and risk tolerance.
2. **Investment Horizon** — Short, medium, or long term.
3. **Asset Allocation Guidelines** — Permitted ranges (e.g., equity 40–60%).
4. **Investment Constraints** — Liquidity, taxes, legal/regulatory, unique preferences.
5. **Performance Benchmark** — Index to measure against (e.g., Nifty 50).
6. **Rebalancing Policy** — Frequency and tolerance band.
7. **Communication & Review** — Reporting cadence.

**Importance (built into explanation)**: Reduces conflicts of interest, ensures discipline, and provides accountability.

---

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Expected Portfolio Return**:

$$E(R_P) = (0.50)(15) + (0.30)(12) + (0.20)(10)$$

$$E(R_P) = 7.50 + 3.60 + 2.00 = \boxed{13.10\%}$$

**Step 2 — Re-normalise weights for X-Y sub-portfolio**:

The X-Y sub-portfolio carries $w_X' = \dfrac{0.50}{0.80} = 0.625$ and $w_Y' = \dfrac{0.30}{0.80} = 0.375$. Total sub-portfolio weight = 80%.

**Step 3 — Covariance**:

$$\text{Cov}(X,Y) = (0.50)(20)(15) = 150$$

**Step 4 — Variance of the X-Y sub-portfolio**:

$$\sigma_{XY}^2 = (0.625)^2(20)^2 + (0.375)^2(15)^2 + 2(0.625)(0.375)(150)$$

$$\sigma_{XY}^2 = (0.390625)(400) + (0.140625)(225) + (0.46875)(150)$$

$$\sigma_{XY}^2 = 156.25 + 31.6406 + 70.3125 = 258.2031$$

**Step 5 — Standard Deviation of Sub-portfolio**:

$$\sigma_{XY} = \sqrt{258.2031} \approx \boxed{16.07\%}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark)**: The sub-portfolio's SD of $16.07\%$ is significantly lower than the weighted-average individual SD of $18.13\%$, again evidencing diversification.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion** — Mixing percentages and decimals. If $E(R_A) = 20\%$, write it as **20** in the formula, **NOT 0.20**, unless the answer is converted at the end. KTU examiners mark strictly.
> 2. **Forgetting $2 w_A w_B$** — Students frequently drop the factor of 2 in the covariance term. The term is $2 w_A w_B \text{Cov}(A,B)$, **not** $w_A w_B \text{Cov}(A,B)$.
> 3. **Variance ≠ Standard Deviation** — Many students report portfolio *variance* as the answer. Always take the **square root** at the end and report SD in %.
> 4. **Skipping Interpretation** — A "dry" numerical answer without a 1-line commentary on the diversification benefit will lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the Budget Constraint** — In multi-asset problems, *verify* that $\sum w_i = 1$. If it doesn't, re-normalise before computing.
> 6. **Confusing correlation with covariance** — Always convert: $\text{Cov} = \rho \sigma_X \sigma_Y$ before plugging into the variance formula.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **portfolio** is a basket of financial assets held together to balance **risk** and **return**.
- ✅ The **5-stage Portfolio Management Process**: Identify → Set Asset Mix → Select Securities → Execute → Monitor & Rebalance.
- ✅ **Strategic Asset Allocation** is the single most important decision (≈ 90% of return variability).
- ✅ **Total Risk = Systematic Risk + Unsystematic Risk**.
- ✅ Systematic risk is **non-diversifiable** (market-wide); unsystematic risk is **diversifiable** (firm-specific).
- ✅ Holding **20–30 stocks** across sectors virtually eliminates unsystematic risk.
- ✅ **Diversification works only** when $\rho_{ij} < +1$.
- ✅ Key formulas to memorise verbatim:
  - $E(R_P) = \sum w_i E(R_i)$
  - $\sigma_P^2 = \sum w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i<j} w_i w_j \rho_{ij} \sigma_i \sigma_j$
  - $\text{Cov}(A,B) = \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$
- ✅ **Active vs Passive** management: Active = beat benchmark, higher cost. Passive = replicate index, lower cost.
- ✅ An **Investment Policy Statement (IPS)** is the *rulebook* of portfolio management—mandatory before investing.
- ✅ Always report final portfolio risk as **standard deviation (in %)**, not variance.
- ✅ Always include a **1-line interpretation** of the diversification benefit in numerical answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean-Variance Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mean-Variance Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mean-Variance Analysis (MVA)** is a quantitative framework developed by **Harry M. Markowitz** in his seminal 1952 paper *"Portfolio Selection"* (Journal of Finance) and further formalized in his 1959 book *"Portfolio Selection: Efficient Diversification of Investments."* It forms the mathematical backbone of **Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)**.

The framework postulates that an investor, when faced with a universe of risky assets, should evaluate each portfolio using only two statistical parameters of the probability distribution of its future returns:

- The **Expected Return** (the "Mean," denoted $\mu_p$ or $E(R_p)$) — measuring anticipated reward.
- The **Variance of Return** (denoted $\sigma_p^2$) — measuring dispersion, interpreted as **total risk**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEIET744 / Module 3):**
> *"Mean-Variance Analysis is the normative framework that prescribes how an investor should allocate capital across risky assets by simultaneously considering the expected return (mean) and the risk (variance) of the portfolio, leading to the identification of the Efficient Frontier and the Capital Allocation Line."*

The central normative proposition is the **Mean-Variance Criterion (MVC)**:

A portfolio $P$ is said to *dominate* (or *mean-variance dominate*) another portfolio $Q$ if and only if:

$$\mu_P \geq \mu_Q \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_P^2 \leq \sigma_Q^2$$

with at least one strict inequality. A set of assets is called a **Mean-Variance Efficient Portfolio** if no other portfolio in the feasible set dominates it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Trip" Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kochi to Delhi** and can choose between several routes:

| Route | Average Speed (Mean Return) | Bumpiness of Road (Variance / Risk) |
|-------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| NH-44 (Direct Highway) | 70 km/h | Very Bumpy (Cracks, Traffic) |
| NH-544 + State Roads | 55 km/h | Smooth but slow |

Now suppose you could *combine* parts of these two routes — sometimes take the highway, sometimes the smooth road. By carefully mixing, you could potentially reach a route that is **faster than the slow road AND smoother than the bumpy road**. This is the essence of **diversification**: by mixing uncorrelated assets, you can reduce total risk without sacrificing much expected return.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Markowitz Insight:** Diversification benefits arise *only* when assets are **not perfectly positively correlated** ($\rho_{ij} < 1$). The lower the correlation, the greater the risk-reduction potential.

## 1.3 Historical Context and KTU Relevance

- **1952** — Markowitz publishes "Portfolio Selection"
- **1990** — Markowitz shares the **Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences** with William Sharpe and Merton Miller
- **Today** — MVA remains the foundational layer upon which the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**, **Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)**, and modern robo-advisory algorithms are built

> [!TIP]
> For KTU Board Examinations (PEIET744), Mean-Variance Analysis is **high-yield**. Expect at least one 14-mark question in the End Semester Exam (ESE) and direct Part-A questions on definitions, the efficient frontier, and the Capital Allocation Line.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Expected Return of a portfolio** = weighted average of individual expected returns
- **Portfolio Variance** = weighted sum of covariances (NOT a simple weighted average of variances)
- **Standard Deviation** = $\sqrt{\text{Variance}}$, expressed in percentage points
- **Correlation Coefficient ($\rho$)** $\in [-1, +1]$
- **Covariance ($\sigma_{ij}$)** = $\rho_{ij} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j$
- **Sharpe Ratio** = $\dfrac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ (risk-adjusted performance)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Assumptions of Mean-Variance Analysis

The MVA framework rests on six foundational assumptions, each of which is fair game for KTU Part-A questions:

1. **Rational Investors** — Investors are risk-averse and prefer more wealth to less.
2. **Single-Period Horizon** — All decisions are evaluated over one identical investment period.
3. **Quadratic Utility / Normal Returns** — Returns are normally distributed, making mean and variance sufficient statistics.
4. **Frictionless Markets** — No taxes, no transaction costs, assets are divisible.
5. **Homogeneous Expectations** — All investors perceive the same expected returns, variances, and covariances.
6. **Existence of a Risk-Free Asset** — Investors can borrow and lend at a single risk-free rate $R_f$.

## 2.2 Building a Portfolio: The Mathematical Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Expected Return of a Two-Asset Portfolio

For a portfolio combining assets A and B with weights $w_A$ and $w_B$ (where $w_A + w_B = 1$):

$$E(R_p) = w_A \cdot E(R_A) + w_B \cdot E(R_B)$$

### 2.2.2 Variance of a Two-Asset Portfolio

The portfolio variance is the **generalized quadratic form**:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \sigma_{A,B}$$

where $\sigma_{A,B} = \rho_{A,B} \cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B$ is the covariance between assets A and B.

### 2.2.3 The Three Magical Cases of Correlation

| Correlation | $\rho$ | $\sigma_p$ Behavior | Diversification |
|-------------|--------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Perfect Positive | $+1$ | Weighted average of individual $\sigma$'s | **None** — no risk reduction |
| Zero | $0$ | Always $\leq$ weighted average | **Moderate** — risk reduction |
| Perfect Negative | $-1$ | Can be **zero** (hedge possible) | **Maximum** — full hedging possible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The General n-Asset Variance Formula (Most Important for KTU 14-Mark Questions):**
> $$\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_i w_j \sigma_{ij}$$
> where $\sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$ and the double summation expands to a symmetric **variance-covariance matrix** structure.

## 2.3 The Efficient Frontier (Markowitz Bullet)

The **Efficient Frontier** is the locus of portfolios in the $(\sigma_p, \mu_p)$ plane that offer:

- The **highest expected return** for a given level of risk, OR equivalently
- The **lowest risk** for a given level of expected return

The frontier is typically the **upper half of a hyperbola** opening to the right — often called the *"Markowitz Bullet"* when graphed as a scatter of all feasible portfolios.

### 2.3.1 Two Sub-Regions of the Feasible Set

- **Efficient Frontier** (upper boundary) — desirable
- **Inefficient Region** (interior) — dominated portfolios
- **Minimum-Variance Portfolio (MVP)** — the unique point with the lowest possible $\sigma_p$

## 2.4 The Capital Allocation Line (CAL) and the Two-Fund Separation Theorem

When a risk-free asset with return $R_f$ is introduced, every investor can construct a portfolio by mixing the risk-free asset with a single **optimal risky portfolio** (denoted $P^*$, or $M$ for "Market" in CAPM). The resulting combinations lie on a straight line:

$$E(R_C) = R_f + \left[\frac{E(R_P^*) - R_f}{\sigma_{P^*}}\right] \cdot \sigma_C$$

The slope $\dfrac{E(R_P^*) - R_f}{\sigma_{P^*}}$ is the **Sharpe Ratio** (or **Reward-to-Variability Ratio**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Fund Separation Theorem (Tobin, 1958):** All investors hold a combination of the risk-free asset and the same risky portfolio $P^*$. The only difference between investors is the *proportion* allocated to $P^*$, governed by their individual risk tolerance.

## 2.5 Indifference Curves and the Investor's Optimum

Every investor possesses a set of **indifference curves** — convex curves in the $(\sigma, \mu)$ plane along which the investor is equally happy. The investor's **optimal complete portfolio** is the tangency point between:

- The **Capital Allocation Line** (from above), and
- The **highest attainable indifference curve** (from below)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Expected Return of Portfolio | $E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i E(R_i)$ | Linear in weights |
| 2 | Two-Asset Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1 w_2 \rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$ | Quadratic in weights |
| 3 | Covariance | $\sigma_{ij} = \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$ | Symmetric: $\sigma_{ij}=\sigma_{ji}$ |
| 4 | Correlation Range | $-1 \leq \rho_{ij} \leq +1$ | Endpoints imply no diversification |
| 5 | Sharpe Ratio | $S_p = \dfrac{E(R_p)-R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Slope of CAL |
| 6 | Capital Allocation Line | $E(R_C) = R_f + S_p \cdot \sigma_C$ | Linear in $\sigma_C$ |
| 7 | Capital Market Line (CML) | $E(R_p) = R_f + \dfrac{E(R_M)-R_f}{\sigma_M}\sigma_p$ | Special case: $P^*=M$ |
| 8 | Weight in Risky Asset | $y^* = \dfrac{E(R_P^*)-R_f}{A\sigma_{P^*}^2}$ | Where $A$ = risk aversion coefficient |
| 9 | Utility Function (Quadratic) | $U = E(R) - 0.5 A \sigma^2$ | $A$ = risk aversion coefficient |
| 10 | n-Asset Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_i \sum_j w_i w_j \sigma_{ij}$ | Matrix form: $\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of MVA in Engineering/Finance Fields

- **Robo-Advisors** (Wealthfront, Betterment) — use MVA to construct ETFs.
- **Corporate Treasury** — Mean-variance optimization of cash and short-term investments.
- **Pension Fund Management** — allocation across equities and bonds.
- **Banking ALM** (Asset-Liability Management) — minimizes risk of a defined liability stream.
- **Risk Management Software** — Bloomberg PORT, FactSet, Axioma use Markowitz as their foundation.

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark KTU questions, always **draw the $(\sigma, \mu)$ diagram** showing the efficient frontier, the CAL/CML, and the tangency portfolio. Examiners award 2–3 marks for a clear, well-labeled diagram.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The General n-Asset Portfolio Variance

**Starting Point:** A portfolio $P$ holds $n$ risky assets with weights $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$, where $\sum w_i = 1$. The return on asset $i$ in state $s$ is $R_{i,s}$.

### Step 1: Define the portfolio return
The portfolio return in state $s$ is the weighted sum of individual asset returns:
$$R_{P,s} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i R_{i,s}$$

### Step 2: Define the portfolio expected return
Taking the expectation (probability-weighted average over all $S$ states):
$$E(R_P) = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i R_{i,s}\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i E(R_{i,s})$$

This step uses the **linearity of expectation**.

### Step 3: Define the variance as the squared deviation
$$\sigma_P^2 = E\!\left[(R_{P,s} - E(R_P))^2\right]$$

### Step 4: Substitute the portfolio return expression
$$\sigma_P^2 = E\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i R_{i,s} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i E(R_i)\right)^2\right]$$
$$= E\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i (R_{i,s} - E(R_i))\right)^2\right]$$

### Step 5: Expand the squared sum (double sum)
$$\sigma_P^2 = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_i w_j (R_{i,s} - E(R_i))(R_{j,s} - E(R_j))\right]$$

### Step 6: Pull the constants outside the expectation
$$\sigma_P^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_i w_j \cdot E\!\left[(R_{i,s} - E(R_i))(R_{j,s} - E(R_j))\right]$$

### Step 7: Recognize the inner expectation as covariance
By definition, $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = E[(R_i - E(R_i))(R_j - E(R_j))] = \sigma_{ij}$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma_P^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_i w_j \sigma_{ij}}$$

This is the **canonical Markowitz variance formula** and the most-tested equation in KTU Module-3 questions.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Two-Asset Portfolio

**Problem (Model Question):** An investor is considering a portfolio of two stocks, A and B, with the following characteristics:

- $E(R_A) = 12\%$, $\sigma_A = 18\%$
- $E(R_B) = 8\%$, $\sigma_B = 14\%$
- $\rho_{A,B} = 0.30$

The investor places 40% in A and 60% in B. Compute the expected return and standard deviation of the portfolio.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute Expected Return**

$$E(R_p) = (0.40)(12\%) + (0.60)(8\%)$$
$$= 4.80\% + 4.80\% = 9.60\%$$

**Step 2: Compute the Covariance**

$$\sigma_{A,B} = \rho_{A,B} \cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B$$
$$= (0.30)(0.18)(0.14) = 0.00756$$

Equivalently, $\sigma_{A,B} = 0.756\%$ (or $7.56$ percentage points).

**Step 3: Compute Portfolio Variance**

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \sigma_{A,B}$$
