# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the Capital Market Line (CML) and the Security Market Line (SML).**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | CML | SML |
|-------|-----|-----|
| Risk measure | Total risk ($\sigma_p$) | Systematic risk ($\beta_i$) |
| Applicability | Only efficient portfolios | All assets and portfolios |
| Slope | Sharpe Ratio of market $\dfrac{E(R_m)-R_f}{\sigma_m}$ | Market Risk Premium $E(R_m)-R_f$ |
| Equilibrium | Portfolios only | Individual assets also |

The CML is a *special case* of the SML — for efficient portfolios, $\sigma_p$ and $\beta_p$ are linearly related, making the two lines coincide at the Market Portfolio.

*(3 marks: 2 for distinction table + 1 for special case remark)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Market Portfolio? Why is it unique in CAPM equilibrium?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Market Portfolio (M)** is a theoretical portfolio containing *every* risky asset in the economy held in proportion to its market capitalization. It is the **tangency portfolio** on the Markowitz efficient frontier when a risk-free asset is introduced.

**Uniqueness arises because:**
1. All rational investors with homogeneous expectations will identify the same tangency point.
2. It has the **highest possible Sharpe Ratio** in the economy.
3. In equilibrium, the demand for every asset equals its supply *only* when all investors hold **M** combined with $R_f$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for uniqueness reasoning)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Using $\sigma_i$ (total risk) instead of $\beta_i$ in CAPM equations → **−3 marks**
> 2. Forgetting to square/convert when computing Cov and Var → **−2 marks**
> 3. Mixing up Sharpe Ratio (denominator $\sigma_p$) and Treynor Ratio (denominator $\beta_p$) → **−2 marks**
> 4. Failing to verify whether the portfolio lies on the CML before applying $\sigma_p = w_m \sigma_m$ → **−3 marks**
> 5. Not stating assumptions of CAPM before applying the formula → **−2 marks**

---

### Question B (A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** State and explain the assumptions of the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**. Derive the CAPM equation: $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider two securities A and B with the following data:

| Security | $E(R)$ | $\sigma$ | $\beta$ |
|----------|--------|----------|---------|
| A | 18% | 22% | 1.30 |
| B | 13% | 15% | 0.85 |

The risk-free rate is $R_f = 6\%$ and the expected market return is $E(R_m) = 12\%$.

**Compute:**
(i) Required return using CAPM for both A and B.
(ii) Jensen's Alpha for both securities. Identify which is undervalued.
(iii) Sharpe Ratio and Treynor Ratio for both securities. Identify which is superior under CML and SML frameworks.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (A)

**Part (a) — Assumptions and Derivation:**

**Assumptions of CAPM:**
1. Investors are rational mean-variance optimizers.
2. Single-period investment horizon.
3. Homogeneous expectations about returns, variances, and covariances.
4. Existence of a risk-free asset with zero default risk.
5. No taxes, transaction costs, or restrictions on borrowing/lending at $R_f$.
6. Markets are perfectly competitive — no investor can influence prices.
7. All assets are infinitely divisible and marketable.

**Derivation:**

Let asset $i$'s return be:
$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \varepsilon_i$$

Taking expectations:
$$E(R_i) = \alpha_i + \beta_i E(R_m) + E(\varepsilon_i) = \alpha_i + \beta_i E(R_m)$$

For an efficient well-diversified portfolio, $\alpha_i = (1 - \beta_i) R_f$. Substituting:
$$E(R_i) = (1 - \beta_i) R_f + \beta_i E(R_m) = R_f + \beta_i \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]$$

Equivalently, using no-arbitrage (Sharpe Ratio equalization) gives the same result. ✔️

*(Award: 4 marks for assumptions + 3 marks for derivation)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solutions:**

**(i) Required Return via CAPM:**

For Security A:
$$E(R_A) = 6\% + 1.30 \times (12\% - 6\%) = 6\% + 1.30 \times 6\%$$
$$\boxed{E(R_A) = 6\% + 7.8\% = 13.8\%}$$

For Security B:
$$E(R_B) = 6\% + 0.85 \times (12\% - 6\%) = 6\% + 0.85 \times 6\%$$
$$\boxed{E(R_B) = 6\% + 5.1\% = 11.1\%}$$

**(ii) Jensen's Alpha:**

$$\alpha_A = 18\% - 13.8\% = \boxed{+4.2\%}$$
$$\alpha_B = 13\% - 11.1\% = \boxed{+1.9\%}$$

**Both have positive alpha**, meaning both lie *above* the SML. Security **A** has higher alpha → **more undervalued** (greater abnormal return potential).

**(iii) Sharpe and Treynor Ratios:**

**Sharpe Ratio** (assumes portfolio is efficient — denominator = total risk):
$$S_A = \dfrac{18\% - 6\%}{22\%} = \dfrac{12}{22} \approx 0.5455$$
$$S_B = \dfrac{13\% - 6\%}{15\%} = \dfrac{7}{15} \approx 0.4667$$

**Security A is superior on Sharpe basis** (higher reward per unit total risk).

**Treynor Ratio** (denominator = systematic risk $\beta$):
$$T_A = \dfrac{18\% - 6\%}{1.30} = \dfrac{12}{1.30} \approx 9.2308$$
$$T_B = \dfrac{13\% - 6\%}{0.85} = \dfrac{7}{0.85} \approx 8.2353$$

**Security A is also superior on Treynor basis** (higher reward per unit systematic risk).

**Conclusion:** Security **A dominates** Security **B** on every performance metric.

*(Award: 2 + 2 + 3 marks distribution)*

---

### Question B (B) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **Capital Market Line (CML)** with a neat sketch. Show that the introduction of a risk-free asset converts the parabolic efficient frontier into a straight line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given: $R_f = 5\%$, $E(R_m) = 11\%$, $\sigma_m = 16\%$. A portfolio manager constructs a portfolio **P** with $\sigma_p = 12\%$ and $w_m = 0.75$.

**Compute:**
(i) The expected return of P using the **CML formula**.
(ii) The expected return of P using the **weighted-average method**.
(iii) The Sharpe Ratio of P and the Market Portfolio. Verify they lie on the same CML.
(iv) Suppose the manager's portfolio **Q** has $\beta_Q = 1.25$ but the same expected return as P. Calculate its required return using CAPM and comment on whether it is fairly priced. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (B)

**Part (a) — CML Explanation:**

The **Capital Market Line** is the locus of all efficient portfolios formed by combining the risk-free asset $R_f$ with the Market Portfolio **M**. In the $\sigma_p$–$E(R_p)$ plane, it is a straight line passing through the vertical axis at $R_f$ and tangent to the Markowitz efficient frontier at the point **M**.

**Geometric Proof:**

A two-asset portfolio of $R_f$ and **M** has expected return:
$$E(R_p) = w_f R_f + (1 - w_f) E(R_m)$$

and standard deviation (since $\sigma_f = 0$):
$$\sigma_p = (1 - w_f) \, \sigma_m$$

Eliminating $(1 - w_f)$:
$$(1 - w_f) = \dfrac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_m}$$

Substituting back:
$$E(R_p) = R_f + \dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \, \sigma_p$$

This is linear in $\sigma_p$ → the **efficient frontier becomes a straight line (the CML)** passing through $(0, R_f)$ and tangent to the parabolic frontier at **M**.

**Sketch Description:**
- Y-axis: $E(R_p)$
- X-axis: $\sigma_p$
- Parabola: Markowitz efficient frontier (without $R_f$)
- Straight line from $R_f$ tangent at **M**: the **CML**
- The slope of the CML = **Sharpe Ratio of M**

*(Award: 4 marks for explanation + 3 marks for derivation/sketch)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solutions:**

**(i) Expected Return via CML:**
$$E(R_p) = R_f + \dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \, \sigma_p$$
$$E(R_p) = 5\% + \dfrac{11\% - 5\%}{16\%} \times 12\%$$
$$E(R_p) = 5\% + \dfrac{6}{16} \times 12\% = 5\% + 4.5\%$$
$$\boxed{E(R_p) = 9.5\%}$$

**(ii) Expected Return via Weighted Average:**
$$E(R_p) = w_f R_f + w_m E(R_m) = 0.25 \times 5\% + 0.75 \times 11\%$$
$$E(R_p) = 1.25\% + 8.25\%$$
$$\boxed{E(R_p) = 9.5\%}$$ ✔️ (Matches CML)

**(iii) Sharpe Ratios:**

For portfolio P:
$$S_p = \dfrac{9.5\% - 5\%}{12\%} = \dfrac{4.5}{12} = 0.3750$$

For Market M:
$$S_m = \dfrac{11\% - 5\%}{16\%} = \dfrac{6}{16} = 0.3750$$ ✔️

**Verification:** $S_p = S_m$ confirms P lies on the CML. ✔️

**(iv) Required Return of Q via CAPM:**

$$E(R_Q^{\text{required}}) = R_f + \beta_Q \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr]$$
$$E(R_Q^{\text{required}}) = 5\% + 1.25 \times (11\% - 5\%)$$
$$E(R_Q^{\text{required}}) = 5\% + 1.25 \times 6\% = 5\% + 7.5\%$$
$$\boxed{E(R_Q^{\text{required}}) = 12.5\%}$$

**Comparison with P:** $E(R_P) = 9.5\%$.

Since Q's required return ($12.5\%$) exceeds P's realized return ($9.5\%$), but assuming Q actually *earns* the same $9.5\%$, then:

**Jensen's Alpha of Q:**
$$\alpha_Q = 9.5\% - 12.5\% = -3.0\%$$

A **negative alpha** means **Q lies below the SML** → **Q is overvalued** (offers insufficient return for its systematic risk). The market price is too high; investor should sell/avoid.

*(Award: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 marks distribution)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **CMT** bridges MPT (Module 3) and CAPM (Asset Pricing).
- 🔹 **Risk-free asset** transforms the parabolic efficient frontier into the **straight CML**.
- 🔹 **Market Portfolio (M)** is the unique tangency portfolio with the **maximum Sharpe Ratio**.
- 🔹 **Two-Fund Separation Theorem:** All investors hold combinations of $R_f$ and **M** only.
- 🔹 **CAPM / SML:** $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ — valid for *any* asset.
- 🔹 **CML:** $E(R_p) = R_f + S_m \cdot \sigma_p$ — valid only for *efficient* portfolios.
- 🔹 **Beta** = $\dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)} = \rho_{im} \dfrac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$ (dimensionless).
- 🔹 **Market Risk Premium** = $E(R_m) - R_f$ — the price of systematic risk.
- 🔹 **Jensen's Alpha** > 0 → undervalued / outperforming; < 0 → overvalued / underperforming.
- 🔹 **Sharpe Ratio** uses $\sigma_p$ (total risk); **Treynor Ratio** uses $\beta_p$ (systematic risk).
- 🔹 **CAPM Assumptions:** Rational mean-variance investors, homogeneous expectations, single period, no taxes/transaction costs, perfect markets.
- 🔹 Total risk = Systematic (priced) + Unsystematic (diversifiable away, NOT priced).
- 🔹 **Slope of CML** = Sharpe Ratio of Market = $\dfrac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}$.
- 🔹 **Slope of SML** = Market Risk Premium = $E(R_m) - R_f$.
- 🔹 Numerical sanity check: CML formula and weighted-average formula MUST give identical answers.
- 🔹 In KTU numericals, always show the **CML vs Weighted Average dual verification** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pricing of Risk and Computation of Expected Return
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pricing of Risk and Computation of Expected Return

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pricing of Risk — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pricing of Risk** is the quantitative process of determining the additional return (called the **risk premium**) that an investor demands for bearing an additional unit of risk in an investment. In modern portfolio theory, risk is *priced* through mathematical models such as the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** and the **Security Market Line (SML)**.

In a competitive financial market, every risky asset must offer a return high enough to compensate the investor for:
1. The **time value of money** (reward for postponing consumption → Risk-Free Rate, $R_f$).
2. The **systematic risk** assumed (reward for bearing non-diversifiable market risk → Risk Premium).

Mathematically, the expected return of any asset $i$ in equilibrium is:

$$\text{Pricing of Risk} \;\Rightarrow\; E(R_i) = R_f + \text{Risk Premium for asset } i$$

The **risk premium** is therefore the "price" the market pays for risk. The higher the risk, the higher the price (premium).

---

## 1.2 Expected Return — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected Return** is the probability-weighted average of all possible returns that an investment may produce. It represents the *mean* (first central moment) of the return distribution and is a forward-looking measure, not a guarantee of realized outcomes.

For a single asset with $n$ possible states of the world:

$$E(R_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \cdot R_{ij}$$

Where:
- $P_j$ = Probability of state $j$ occurring.
- $R_{ij}$ = Return of asset $i$ in state $j$.
- $\sum P_j = 1$.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber

Imagine two mountain climbing expeditions:

| Feature | Expedition A (Trek) | Expedition B (Everest) |
|---|---|---|
| Risk | Low — well-marked trail, mild slopes | Extreme — avalanches, crevasses, altitude sickness |
| Reward | Small — a scenic valley view | Huge — the world's highest summit, global fame, sponsorships |

The **expected reward (return)** for Everest must be **much larger** than for the trek. This *extra* reward is the **risk premium** — the "price of risk." The market behaves exactly this way: investors will only hold risky assets if the **expected return is high enough** to justify the danger.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** In finance, *pricing of risk* answers the question — *"How much extra return must a risky asset offer to be attractive?"* — while *expected return* answers — *"What is the average outcome I can anticipate from this asset?"*

---

## 1.4 The Two Faces of Risk

> [!WARNING]
> Risk is **not** uniform. It has two distinct components that must be priced differently.

- **Systematic Risk (Market Risk)**: Cannot be eliminated by diversification (e.g., interest rate changes, recessions, wars). Measured by **Beta ($\beta$)**.
- **Unsystematic Risk (Idiosyncratic / Firm-Specific Risk)**: Can be reduced to near zero through diversification (e.g., a CEO resigning, a product recall).

In equilibrium, **only systematic risk is priced**. Unsystematic risk earns no premium because investors can avoid it for free by holding a diversified portfolio.

---

## 1.5 Risk Metrics Used in KTU Syllabus

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Variance | $\sigma_i^2$ | Average squared deviation of returns from mean |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma_i$ | Square root of variance — measures **total risk** |
| Covariance | $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)$ | Co-movement of asset return with market return |
| Correlation | $\rho_{i,m}$ | Standardized co-movement (ranges from $-1$ to $+1$) |
| Beta | $\beta_i$ | Systematic risk relative to the market |
| Risk-Free Rate | $R_f$ | Return on a default-free asset (e.g., 10-yr T-bond) |
| Market Risk Premium | $[E(R_m) - R_f]$ | Extra return demanded for holding the market portfolio |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Expected Return — Probability Distribution Framework

When an investor estimates returns, each possible outcome $R_j$ is assigned a probability $P_j$. The **expected return** is the probability-weighted average:

$$E(R_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \cdot R_{ij}$$

**Why this works:** Each probability $P_j$ acts as a "weight" indicating how likely that return is. Summing all such weighted returns gives the *mean* of the distribution.

**Variance of returns** (measures dispersion — total risk):

$$\sigma_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \cdot [R_{ij} - E(R_i)]^2$$

**Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_i = \sqrt{\sigma_i^2}$$

**Coefficient of Variation (CV)** — risk per unit of expected return, useful for comparing assets with different return scales:

$$CV_i = \frac{\sigma_i}{E(R_i)}$$

---

## 2.2 Portfolio Expected Return

For a portfolio of $N$ assets with weights $w_i$ (where $\sum w_i = 1$):

$$E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot E(R_i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The expected return of a portfolio is simply the weighted average of expected returns of its component assets. Risk, however, does **NOT** combine so simply because of correlations.

---

## 2.3 Portfolio Risk — Two-Asset Case

For a two-asset portfolio with weights $w_1, w_2$:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$$

Where $\rho_{12}$ is the correlation coefficient between the two assets. Notice:
- If $\rho = +1$ → no diversification benefit.
- If $\rho = -1$ → perfect hedging; risk can be eliminated entirely.
- In general, **the lower the correlation, the greater the diversification benefit**.

---

## 2.4 The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) — Core of Risk Pricing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAPM** is the foundational model of modern finance that explicitly prices systematic risk. It was developed independently by **William Sharpe (1964)**, **John Lintner (1965)**, and **Jan Mossin (1966)**, building on Harry Markowitz's portfolio theory (1952). The 1990 Nobel Prize in Economics was awarded to Sharpe, Markowitz, and Miller for this body of work.

The CAPM equilibrium equation:

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

**Reading the formula left to right:**
- $E(R_i)$ = Expected return on asset $i$.
- $R_f$ = Compensation for time value of money (risk-free rate).
- $\beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$ = Compensation for systematic risk.
- $[E(R_m) - R_f]$ = **Market Risk Premium** (extra return for holding the whole market).

---

## 2.5 Beta — The Measure of Systematic Risk

$$\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\sigma_m^2}$$

Equivalent forms:

$$\beta_i = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$$

**Interpretation of $\beta$:**

| Beta Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $\beta = 1$ | Asset moves *with* the market (same systematic risk) |
| $\beta > 1$ | Asset is **aggressive** — amplifies market movements |
| $\beta < 1$ | Asset is **defensive** — dampens market movements |
| $\beta = 0$ | Asset has no systematic risk (e.g., risk-free asset) |
| $\beta < 0$ | Asset moves *against* the market (hedge) |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E(R_i) = \sum P_j R_{ij}$ | Expected return of single asset |
| 2 | $\sigma_i^2 = \sum P_j [R_{ij} - E(R_i)]^2$ | Variance (total risk) |
| 3 | $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\sigma_i^2}$ | Standard deviation |
| 4 | $CV_i = \sigma_i / E(R_i)$ | Coefficient of variation |
| 5 | $E(R_p) = \sum w_i E(R_i)$ | Portfolio expected return |
| 6 | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + \sum\sum w_i w_j \text{Cov}(R_i,R_j)$ | Portfolio variance (N-asset) |
| 7 | $\beta_i = \text{Cov}(R_i,R_m)/\sigma_m^2$ | Beta of asset $i$ |
| 8 | $\beta_i = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \sigma_i/\sigma_m$ | Beta using correlation |
| 9 | $\beta_p = \sum w_i \beta_i$ | Beta of portfolio |
| 10 | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ | **CAPM — central to KTU** |
| 11 | $SML = R_f + \beta [E(R_m) - R_f]$ | Security Market Line equation |
| 12 | $\alpha_i = R_i - [R_f + \beta_i(E(R_m)-R_f)]$ | Jensen's Alpha |
| 13 | $\text{Total Risk} = \sigma_i^2 = \sigma_{sys}^2 + \sigma_{unsys}^2$ | Risk decomposition |

---

## 2.7 The Security Market Line (SML)

> [!NOTE]
> The **SML** is the graphical representation of the CAPM. It plots **expected return on the Y-axis** and **beta on the X-axis**.

- The Y-intercept is $R_f$ (when $\beta = 0$).
- The slope is $[E(R_m) - R_f]$, the market risk premium.
- **All correctly priced assets lie on the SML** in equilibrium.
- Assets **above** the SML are **underpriced** (offer too much return for their risk).
- Assets **below** the SML are **overpriced** (offer too little return for their risk).

---

## 2.8 Utility of CAPM in Engineering Economics

For engineers and managers, CAPM is used in:
- **Capital Budgeting**: Computing the discount rate for project evaluation.
- **Cost of Equity**: A core input in WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital).
- **Performance Evaluation**: Comparing actual returns to required (CAPM) returns.
- **Risk-Adjusted Valuation**: Bridging engineering decisions with shareholder value.

---

## 2.9 Assumptions of CAPM (Required for Exam)

1. Investors are **rational, risk-averse**, and use mean-variance optimization.
2. **Single-period** investment horizon.
3. **Homogeneous expectations** among investors.
4. **No taxes** or **transaction costs**.
5. All assets are **divisible** and **marketable**.
6. Investors can **borrow/lend at the risk-free rate**.
7. Markets are in **equilibrium** (everyone holds the market portfolio).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Expected Return from Probability Distribution

**Given:** Asset $A$ has three possible economic scenarios.

| State | Probability $P_j$ | Return $R_j$ (%) |
|---|---|---|
| Boom | 0.30 | +25 |
| Normal | 0.50 | +12 |
| Recession | 0.20 | −08 |

**Step 1** — Verify probabilities sum to 1.

$$\sum_{j=1}^{3} P_j = 0.30 + 0.50 + 0.20 = 1.00 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 2** — Apply the expected return formula.

$$E(R_A) = \sum_{j=1}^{3} P_j \cdot R_{j}$$

$$E(R_A) = (0.30)(25) + (0.50)(12) + (0.20)(-8)$$

$$E(R_A) = 7.5 + 6.0 + (-1.6)$$

$$\boxed{E(R_A) = 11.9\%}$$

**Step 3** — Compute variance.

$$\sigma_A^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{3} P_j \cdot [R_j - E(R_A)]^2$$

Deviations from the mean:
- Boom: $25 - 11.9 = 13.1$
- Normal: $12 - 11.9 = 0.1$
- Recession: $-8 - 11.9 = -19.9$

Squared deviations:
- Boom: $13.1^2 = 171.61$
- Normal: $0.1^2 = 0.01$
- Recession: $(-19.9)^2 = 396.01$

$$\sigma_A^2 = (0.30)(171.61) + (0.50)(0.01) + (0.20)(396.01)$$

$$\sigma_A^2 = 51.483 + 0.005 + 79.202$$

$$\sigma_A^2 = 130.69 \;(\%^2)$$

**Step 4** — Standard deviation.

$$\sigma_A = \sqrt{130.69} = 11.43\%$$

**Step 5** — Coefficient of variation.

$$CV_A = \frac{11.43}{11.9} = 0.9605$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Expected Return for a Portfolio

**Given:** A portfolio $P$ has weights $w_A = 0.40, w_B = 0.60$ in two assets.
$E(R_A) = 14\%$, $E(R_B) = 9\%$.

**Step 1** — Apply the weighted average formula.

$$E(R_p) = w_A \cdot E(R_A) + w_B \cdot E(R_B)$$

$$E(R_p) = (0.40)(14) + (0.60)(9)$$

$$E(R_p) = 5.6 + 5.4$$

$$\boxed{E(R_p) = 11.0\%}$$

---

## 3.3 Beta Derivation from Data

**Given:** Market data over 12 months.

| Statistic | Value |
|---|---|
| $\text{Cov}(R_A, R_m)$ | 0.0025 |
| $\sigma_m^2$ (market variance) | 0.0040 |
| $\sigma_A$ (asset std. dev.) | 0.18 |
| $\sigma_m$ (market std. dev.) | 0.20 |

**Step 1** — Use covariance method.

$$\beta_A = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_A, R_m)}{\sigma_m^2} = \frac{0.0025}{0.0040} = 0.625$$

**Step 2** — Cross-verify with correlation method.

$$\rho_{A,m} = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_A, R_m)}{\sigma_A \cdot \sigma_m} = \frac{0.0025}{(0.18)(0.20)} = \frac{0.0025}{0.036} = 0.0694$$

$$\beta_A = \rho_{A,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_A}{\sigma_m} = 0.0694 \cdot \frac{0.18}{0.20} = 0.0694 \cdot 0.90 = 0.0625$$

Wait — let me recompute carefully:

$$\beta_A = 0.0694 \cdot \frac{0.18}{0.20} = 0.0694 \cdot 0.90 = 0.0625$$

There is a discrepancy. Let me correct step 1 with proper numbers. The covariance method gives $\beta_A = 0.625$, so let's recheck the correlation. The correlation must satisfy:

$$\rho_{A,m} = \beta_A \cdot \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_A} = 0.625 \cdot \frac{0.20}{0.18} = 0.6944$$

So the correct correlation is $0.6944$, not $0.0694$. Let me restate the verification properly:

**Step 2 — Cross-verify (corrected).**

$$\beta_A = \rho_{A,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_A}{\sigma_m} = 0.6944 \cdot \frac{0.18}{0.20} = 0.625 \;\;\checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\beta_A = 0.625}$$

**Interpretation:** Asset $A$ has 62.5% of the systematic risk of the market — it is a defensive stock.

---

## 3.4 Full CAPM Expected Return Calculation

**Given:** Risk-free rate $R_f = 6\%$, market expected return $E(R_m) = 14\%$, asset beta $\beta = 1.25$.

**Step 1** — Identify market risk premium.

$$E(R_m) - R_f = 14\% - 6\% = 8\%$$

**Step 2** — Apply CAPM.

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

$$E(R_i) = 6\% + (1.25)(8\%)$$

$$E(R_i) = 6\% + 10\%$$

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = 16\%}$$

**Interpretation:** This aggressive stock ($\beta > 1$) demands 16% expected return to compensate for its above-average systematic risk.

---

## 3.5 CAPM with Portfolio Beta

**Given:** Portfolio with three stocks.
- Stock 1: $w_1 = 0.50$, $\beta_1 = 0.80$
- Stock 2: $w_2 = 0.30$, $\beta_2 = 1.40$
- Stock 3: $w_3 = 0.20$, $\beta_3 = 0.60$

Market data: $R_f = 5\%$, $E(R_m) - R_f = 7\%$.

**Step 1** — Compute portfolio beta.

$$\beta_p = \sum w_i \beta_i = (0.50)(0.80) + (0.30)(1.40) + (0.20)(0.60)$$

$$\beta_p = 0.40 + 0.42 + 0.12 = 0.94$$

**Step 2** — Apply CAPM to portfolio.

$$E(R_p) = 5\% + (0.94)(7\%) = 5\% + 6.58\%$$

$$\boxed{E(R_p) = 11.58\%}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Pricing of Risk Flow

| Step | Input | Process | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify risk-free rate | Read from govt. bond yield | $R_f$ |
| 2 | Identify market expected return | Estimate using index returns | $E(R_m)$ |
| 3 | Compute Market Risk Premium | Subtract: $E(R_m) - R_f$ | MRP |
| 4 | Estimate asset beta | Regress asset on market index | $\beta_i$ |
| 5 | Apply CAPM | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot \text{MRP}$ | Required return |
| 6 | Compare with actual return | $R_i - E(R_i)$ | Jensen's $\alpha$ |
| 7 | Decision | If $\alpha > 0$ → underpriced; $\alpha < 0$ → overpriced | Action |

> [!TIP]
> **Mastery Tip for KTU:** Always compute the **market risk premium** explicitly before plugging into CAPM. Examiners often award partial marks just for correctly identifying $E(R_m) - R_f$.

---

## 3.7 The CAPM Equilibrium Logic

The CAPM can be derived intuitively by noting two boundary conditions:

**Condition 1:** A risk-free asset must earn exactly $R_f$ (no risk premium).

$$E(R_f) = R_f + \beta_f \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

Since $\beta_f = 0$ (no systematic risk):

$$E(R_f) = R_f \;\;\checkmark$$

**Condition 2:** The market portfolio itself earns $E(R_m)$ and has $\beta_m = 1$.

$$E(R_m) = R_f + (1) \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f] = R_f + E(R_m) - R_f = E(R_m) \;\;\checkmark$$

These two boundary conditions uniquely pin down a straight line in $(\beta, E(R))$ space — that line is the **Security Market Line (SML)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Risk Pricing Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Risk sigma squared] --> B[Systematic Risk]
    A --> C[Unsystematic Risk]
    B --> D[Priced by CAPM]
    C --> E[Eliminated by Diversification]
    D --> F[Measured by Beta]
    F --> G["Risk Premium = beta times MRP"]
    G --> H["Expected Return E_R = Rf + Risk Premium"]
    E --> I[No Premium Earned]
    H --> J[Investor Compensation]
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 CAPM Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Identify Rf] --> B[Step 2: Identify E_Rm]
    B --> C[Step 3: MRP = E_Rm minus Rf]
    C --> D[Step 4: Compute Beta]
    D --> E[Step 5: Apply CAPM Formula]
    E --> F[Output: Required Expected Return]
    F --> G{Compare with Actual Return}
    G --> H[Alpha greater than 0: Underpriced]
    G --> I[Alpha less than 0: Overpriced]
```

## 4.3 Security Market Line (SML) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SML [Security Market Line in Beta-Return Space]
        direction LR
        YAxis[Y axis: Expected Return percent] --- XAxis[X axis: Beta]
    end
    P1[Intercept at Beta=0: Rf] --> P2[Slope: Market Risk Premium]
    P2 --> P3[Point at Beta=1: E_Rm]
    P3 --> P4[Asset above SML: Underpriced]
    P3 --> P5[Asset on SML: Fairly Priced]
    P3 --> P6[Asset below SML: Overpriced]
```

## 4.4 Portfolio Expected Return Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Asset Returns and Weights] --> W1[Weight w1]
    Input --> W2[Weight w2]
    Input --> W3[Weight wN]
    W1 --> ER1[Expected Return R1]
    W2 --> ER2[Expected Return R2]
    W3 --> ERN[Expected Return RN]
    ER1 --> Sum[Weighted Sum]
    ER2 --> Sum
    ERN --> Sum
    Sum --> Out["Portfolio Expected Return E_Rp"]
```

## 4.5 Beta Interpretation Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B[Beta Value] --> B1[Beta equals 0: Risk-free]
    B --> B2[Beta between 0 and 1: Defensive]
    B --> B3[Beta equals 1: Market Tracker]
    B --> B4[Beta greater than 1: Aggressive]
    B --> B5[Beta less than 0: Hedge]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define Beta. Explain the significance of a beta coefficient greater than 1.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Beta ($\beta$)** is a measure of the **systematic risk** of a security or portfolio in relation to the overall market. Mathematically, $\beta_i = \text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)/\sigma_m^2$.

A **beta greater than 1** signifies that the security is **aggressive** or **high-risk**. Such an asset amplifies market movements — if the market rises by 10%, the asset is expected to rise by more than 10%; if the market falls, it falls by more. Investors demand a higher risk premium, leading to higher expected returns under CAPM.

**Mark distribution (3 marks):**
- Definition of Beta: 1 mark
- Formula: 1 mark
- Interpretation of $\beta > 1$: 1 mark

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Differentiate between systematic and unsystematic risk.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Systematic Risk | Unsystematic Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Market-wide (macro) | Firm-specific (micro) |
| Diversifiable? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Measured by | Beta ($\beta$) | Residual variance |
| Examples | Interest rates, inflation, recession | Managerial change, product recall |
| Priced? | **Yes** (via CAPM) | **No** (eliminated for free) |

**Mark distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark each for source/diversification/pricing.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** An asset has the following return distribution:

| State | Probability | Return |
|---|---|---|
| Bull market | 0.25 | 30% |
| Stable growth | 0.45 | 14% |
| Slowdown | 0.20 | 5% |
| Recession | 0.10 | −15% |

Calculate the **expected return**, **variance**, and **standard deviation** of the asset.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the CAPM, compute the required return on a stock with $\beta = 1.45$ if $R_f = 7\%$ and $E(R_m) = 15\%$. Comment on the risk classification.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Verify probabilities.

$$\sum P_j = 0.25 + 0.45 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Compute $E(R)$.

$$E(R) = (0.25)(30) + (0.45)(14) + (0.20)(5) + (0.10)(-15)$$

$$E(R) = 7.5 + 6.3 + 1.0 - 1.5$$

$$\boxed{E(R) = 13.3\%}$$

**Step 3:** Compute deviations and squared deviations.

| State | $R_j$ | $R_j - E(R)$ | $[R_j - E(R)]^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bull | 30 | 16.7 | 278.89 |
| Stable | 14 | 0.7 | 0.49 |
| Slowdown | 5 | −8.3 | 68.89 |
| Recession | −15 | −28.3 | 800.89 |

**Step 4:** Compute variance.

$$\sigma^2 = (0.25)(278.89) + (0.45)(0.49) + (0.20)(68.89) + (0.10)(800.89)$$

$$\sigma^2 = 69.7225 + 0.2205 + 13.778 + 80.089$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = 163.81 \;(\%^2)}$$

**Step 5:** Standard deviation.

$$\sigma = \sqrt{163.81} = 12.80\%$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = 12.80\%}$$

**Mark allocation (7 marks):**
- Expected return: 2 marks
- Deviations table: 2 marks
- Variance: 2 marks
- Standard deviation: 1 mark

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Identify inputs.
- $R_f = 7\%$
- $E(R_m) = 15\%$
- $\beta = 1.45$

**Step 2:** Compute Market Risk Premium.

$$E(R_m) - R_f = 15\% - 7\% = 8\%$$

**Step 3:** Apply CAPM.

$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

$$E(R_i) = 7\% + (1.45)(8\%)$$

$$E(R_i) = 7\% + 11.6\%$$

$$\boxed{E(R_i) = 18.6\%}$$

**Step 4:** Comment.

Since $\beta = 1.45 > 1$, the stock is **aggressive**. It amplifies market movements by 45% more than the market. Investors demand 18.6% expected return — significantly higher than the market's 15% — to compensate for this elevated systematic risk.

**Mark allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct inputs: 1 mark
- MRP calculation: 2 marks
- CAPM application: 2 marks
- Risk interpretation: 2 marks

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A portfolio consists of three assets with the following data:

| Asset | Weight | Expected Return | Beta |
|---|---|---|---|
| X | 0.40 | 18% | 1.30 |
| Y | 0.35 | 12% | 0.90 |
| Z | 0.25 | 9% | 0.60 |

Calculate the **portfolio's expected return** and **portfolio beta**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the risk-free rate is $6\%$ and the market risk premium is $8.5\%$, determine whether each asset is **fairly priced, underpriced, or overpriced** using CAPM. Justify.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Compute Portfolio Expected Return.

$$E(R_p) = w_X E(R_X) + w_Y E(R_Y) + w_Z E(R_Z)$$

$$E(R_p) = (0.40)(18) + (0.35)(12) + (0.25)(9)$$

$$E(R_p) = 7.2 + 4.2 + 2.25$$

$$\boxed{E(R_p) = 13.65\%}$$

**Step 2:** Compute Portfolio Beta.

$$\beta_p = w_X \beta_X + w_Y \beta_Y + w_Z \beta_Z$$

$$\beta_p = (0.40)(1.30) + (0.35)(0.90) + (0.25)(0.60)$$

$$\beta_p = 0.520 + 0.315 + 0.150$$

$$\boxed{\beta_p = 0.985}$$

**Mark allocation (7 marks):** 3.5 marks each for portfolio return and beta.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Required return per asset using CAPM.

$$E(R_i)^{CAPM} = R_f + \beta_i \cdot \text{MRP}$$

$$\text{MRP} = E(R_m) - R_f = 8.5\%$$

**Asset X:**
$$E(R_X)^{CAPM} = 6\% + (1.30)(8.5\%) = 6\% + 11.05\% = 17.05\%$$
- Actual $E(R_X) = 18\%$ → **Underpriced** (offers more return than required).

**Asset Y:**
$$E(R_Y)^{CAPM} = 6\% + (0.90)(8.5\%) = 6\% + 7.65\% = 13.65\%$$
- Actual $E(R_Y) = 12\%$ → **Overpriced** (offers less than required).

**Asset Z:**
$$E(R_Z)^{CAPM} = 6\% + (0.60)(8.5\%) = 6\% + 5.10\% = 11.10\%$$
- Actual $E(R_Z) = 9\%$ → **Overpriced**.

**Step 2:** Decision Summary.

| Asset | Required Return | Actual Return | $\alpha$ | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| X | 17.05% | 18% | +0.95% | Underpriced |
| Y | 13.65% | 12% | −1.65% | Overpriced |
| Z | 11.10% | 9% | −2.10% | Overpriced |

**Mark allocation (7 marks):**
- CAPM required return for each asset: 3 marks (1 each)
- Correct classification: 3 marks (1 each)
- Justification comment: 1 mark

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square deviations** — variance requires $(R_j - E(R))^2$, not $|R_j - E(R)|$. Partial mark loss.
> 2. **Mixing up correlation and covariance** in beta computation — remember $\beta = \rho \cdot (\sigma_i/\sigma_m)$, not $\rho \cdot (\sigma_m/\sigma_i)$.
> 3. **Using wrong slope for SML** — slope is the Market Risk Premium $E(R_m) - R_f$, NOT $E(R_m)$ alone.
> 4. **Forgetting $\sum w_i = 1$** — examiners deduct marks if portfolio weights don't sum to 1; always verify.
> 5. **Reporting variance instead of standard deviation** when asked for risk — read the question carefully.
> 6. **Confusing "expected return" (mean) with "required return" (CAPM)** — these are conceptually different; expected return is the probability-weighted forecast, while required return is the market's compensation for risk.
> 7. **Sign errors in Jensen's alpha** — $\alpha = R_i - [R_f + \beta(E(R_m)-R_f)]$, not the reverse.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Must-Remember Points before the KTU Exam:**

- [x] Expected return = **probability-weighted mean** of outcomes.
- [x] Variance = probability-weighted mean of **squared deviations** from the mean.
- [x] Standard deviation = **square root of variance** (total risk measure).
- [x] Beta = **systematic risk** measure = $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m) / \sigma_m^2$.
- [x] Portfolio expected return = **weighted average** of individual expected returns.
- [x] Portfolio beta = **weighted average** of individual betas.
- [x] **CAPM**: $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ — *the heart of risk pricing.*
- [x] **SML** is the graphical depiction of CAPM with slope = Market Risk Premium.
- [x] Only **systematic risk** is priced; unsystematic risk is **diversifiable away**.
- [x] $\beta = 1$ → market tracker; $\beta > 1$ → aggressive; $\beta < 1$ → defensive; $\beta = 0$ → risk-free.
- [x] **Jensen's Alpha** $\alpha_i = R_i - E(R_i)^{CAPM}$ — positive alpha = underpriced asset.
- [x] **Market Risk Premium (MRP)** = $E(R_m) - R_f$ — the *price per unit of systematic risk*.
- [x] CAPM was developed by **Sharpe (1964), Lintner (1965), Mossin (1966)**; Nobel 1990.
- [x] Always verify $\sum P_j = 1$ and $\sum w_i = 1$ before any calculation.

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Pricing of Risk and Computation of Expected Return**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) --- # 🟦 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** is an equilibrium asset-pricing theory, developed independently by **William Sharpe (1964)**, **John Lintner (1965)**, and **Jan Mossin (1966)**, building upon Harry Markowitz's Modern Portfolio Theory (1952). It mathematically describes the relationship between the **systematic risk** of a security (or portfolio) and its **expected return** in a competitive, frictionless financial market. $$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$ Where: - $E(R_i)$ = Expected return on the individual asset / portfolio - $R_f$ = Risk-free rate of return (e.g., 10-year G-Sec yield) - $\beta_i$ (Beta) = Sensitivity measure of the asset's systematic risk relative to the overall market - $E(R_m)$ = Expected return on the market portfolio (e.g., Nifty 50 / S&P 500 / BSE Sensex) - $[E(R_m) - R_f]$ = **Market Risk Premium (MRP)** — the extra return demanded by investors for holding market risk over a risk-free asset > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** CAPM is the cornerstone of Module 4 — "Asset Pricing Models." It is essential for computing the **cost of equity (Ke)**, which directly feeds into the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** and is used in **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** valuations. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are deciding between two jobs: | Job Choice | Salary (Return) | Stress / Risk Level | |---|---|---| | Government Clerk | Fixed ₹50,000/month | Zero (Risk-free) | | Stock Trader | ₹50,000 + Variable Bonus | High volatility (Risky) | You would naturally demand the **Trader job pays MORE** than the Clerk because you are bearing **uncertainty**. CAPM answers precisely **how much more** you should demand for taking on incremental risk. ### The "Two Pieces" Logic: 1. **Piece 1 — Base Pay ($R_f$):** The guaranteed return for doing nothing risky (e.g., lending to the Government). This is your "time value of money." 2. **Piece 2 — Risk Premium ($\beta_i \times [E(R_m) - R_f]$):** Extra compensation proportional to how much the asset "moves with the market." A stock with $\beta = 2$ is twice as volatile as the market, so it demands roughly double the risk premium. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy Extension:** Think of CAPM like a salary structure for a corporate employee. The "Basic Pay" is the risk-free rate, and the "Variable Pay" depends on $\beta$ (the role's exposure to business risk) and the Market Risk Premium (how well the overall company/industry performs). --- ## 1.3 Why CAPM Matters in Engineering/Corporate Finance For B.Tech students, CAPM bridges **Finance Theory** and **Practical Valuation**: - **Capital Budgeting:** When an engineering firm evaluates a new project, the discount rate (cost of equity) is derived via CAPM. - **Start-up Valuation:** Angel investors and VCs use CAPM-derived discount rates for early-stage tech firms. - **Infrastructure Projects:** Public-Private Partnership (PPP) bids in India (NHAI, Power, Metro) use CAPM to determine the cost of equity for long-tenure projects. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Market Benchmark in India:** The BSE Sensex or Nifty 50 serves as the proxy for the **Market Portfolio ($R_m$)**. The Indian 10-Year Government Bond Yield serves as $R_f$. --- # 🟦 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of CAPM For CAPM to hold perfectly in equilibrium, the following stringent assumptions must be satisfied: ### A. Investor Behaviour - **Rational, risk-averse investors** who maximize expected utility (Markowitz framework). - All investors have a **single-period investment horizon** (e.g., 1 year). - Investors are **price-takers** — no single investor can influence security prices. ### B. Market Structure - **Frictionless markets:** No taxes, no transaction costs, no brokerage. - **Divisibility:** Securities can be bought in fractional units (e.g., 0.5 shares). - **Perfect information:** All investors have free, simultaneous access to all relevant data. ### C. Asset Universe - All risky assets (stocks, bonds, real estate) are **marketable** and tradable. - There exists a **risk-free asset** ($R_f$) at which investors can lend or borrow unlimited amounts at the same rate. - The supply of all risky assets is **fixed and given** (fixed composition of the market portfolio). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Perspective:** In Part B questions, students are often asked to *"List and explain the assumptions of CAPM."* Memorize the above under three clean buckets: **Investor Behaviour, Market Structure, Asset Universe**. --- ## 2.2 The CAPM Equation — Component Breakdown $$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$ ### Component 1: Risk-Free Rate ($R_f$) - Theoretical return on a zero-default-risk security. - In practice: **Indian 10-Year G-Sec yield** or **91-day T-Bill yield** for short horizons. - Represents the **time value of money** — the opportunity cost of capital. ### Component 2: Beta ($\beta_i$) - Measures **systematic (non-diversifiable) risk** — the risk that cannot be eliminated through portfolio diversification. - Beta of the market portfolio = **1.0** (by definition). - Beta of a risk-free asset = **0**. - Interpretation: - $\beta > 1$ → Aggressive security (more volatile than market). - $\beta < 1$ → Defensive security (less volatile than market). - $\beta < 0$ → Negatively correlated with market (e.g., gold, defensive bonds). $$\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)} = \frac{\rho_{i,m} \cdot \sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$$ Where: - $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)$ = Covariance between asset return and market return - $\text{Var}(R_m)$ = Variance of market returns - $\rho_{i,m}$ = Correlation coefficient between asset and market - $\sigma_i, \sigma_m$ = Standard deviations of asset and market ### Component 3: Market Risk Premium ($E(R_m) - R_f$) - The **excess return** investors demand for moving from a risk-free asset to the broad market portfolio. - Empirical estimates (India): historically between **5% to 8%**. - It is the "price of risk" per unit of beta. --- ## 2.3 Key Theoretical Constructs ### The Capital Market Line (CML) - Applies **only to efficient portfolios** (those on the Markowitz efficient frontier). - Equation: $E(R_p) = R_f + \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \cdot \sigma_p$ - Slope = **Sharpe Ratio** of the market portfolio. - Plot: Expected Return vs. **Total Risk (Standard Deviation, $\sigma$)**. ### The Security Market Line (SML) - Applies to **all securities** (efficient or not — individual stocks also plot on it). - Equation: Identical to CAPM formula. - Plot: Expected Return vs. **Systematic Risk (Beta, $\beta$)**. - Slope = **Market Risk Premium**. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the axes. CML uses **$\sigma$ (total risk)**, SML uses **$\beta$ (systematic risk)**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark question in KTU exams! --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Description** | **Use Case in KTU Exam** | |---|---|---| | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i (E(R_m) - R_f)$ | CAPM core equation | Compute expected return / cost of equity | | $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$ | Beta from covariance | Given covariance & variance data | | $\beta_i = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$ | Beta from correlation | When correlation & SDs are given | | $\beta_p = \sum w_i \cdot \beta_i$ | Portfolio beta | Weighted average beta of a multi-asset portfolio | | $E(R_p) = R_f + \beta_p (E(R_m) - R_f)$ | CAPM for portfolio | Portfolio expected return | | $K_e = R_f + \beta \cdot \text{MRP}$ | Cost of equity | Direct valuation & WACC calculations | | $\alpha_i = E(R_i) - [R_f + \beta_i (E(R_m) - R_f)]$ | Jensen's Alpha | Stock outperformance/underperformance | | $S = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Sharpe Ratio | Portfolio ranking (CML slope) | | $T = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\beta_p}$ | Treynor Ratio | Portfolio ranking (SML slope) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **LaTeX Note for tables:** Vertical bar symbols inside math are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering/Corporate Utility | **Application Domain** | **Role of CAPM** | |---|---| | **Corporate Valuation (DCF)** | Determines the discount rate for projecting future cash flows. | | **Project Appraisal (PPP)** | NHAI, Metro, Smart City bids use CAPM for cost of equity. | | **Initial Public Offering (IPO)** | Valuation of unlisted tech / engineering start-ups. | | **Capital Budgeting** | Evaluating new R&D and product lines in manufacturing firms. | | **Performance Evaluation** | Jensen's Alpha measures fund manager outperformance. | --- # 🟦 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Markowitz Frontier to CML to SML → CAPM ### Logical Step-by-Step Build-Up **Step 1:** Start with Markowitz Mean-Variance Optimization. For a two-fund portfolio of a risky asset $A$ and the risk-free asset $R_f$: $$E(R_p) = w_A \cdot E(R_A) + (1 - w_A) \cdot R_f$$ $$\sigma_p = w_A \cdot \sigma_A \quad \text{(since } R_f \text{ has zero SD)}$$ **Step 2:** Solve for $w_A$ from the standard deviation equation: $$w_A = \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_A}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute back into the expected return equation: $$E(R_p) = \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_A} \cdot E(R_A) + \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_A}\right) \cdot R_f$$ **Step 4:** Rearrange to obtain the **Capital Market Line (CML)** form: $$E(R_p) = R_f + \frac{E(R_A) - R_f}{\sigma_A} \cdot \sigma_p$$ > The slope $\dfrac{E(R_A) - R_f}{\sigma_A}$ is the **Sharpe Ratio** of asset $A$. **Step 5:** Introduce the **Market Portfolio ($M$)** as the optimal risky portfolio (Tobin's Separation Theorem). For the market portfolio: $$\text{Sharpe Ratio of Market} = \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m}$$ **Step 6:** Replace asset $A$ with the market portfolio $M$ in the CML equation: $$E(R_p) = R_f + \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \cdot \sigma_p$$ **Step 7:** Now consider an individual stock $i$ (which is NOT necessarily on the CML). Define $\beta_i$ as the contribution of stock $i$ to the risk of the market portfolio: $$\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\sigma_m^2}$$ **Step 8:** For an efficient portfolio $p$, the volatility contribution from asset $i$ is proportional to its beta: $$\sigma_p^{\text{market-risk}} = \beta_p \cdot \sigma_m$$ **Step 9:** Substitute this relationship into the CML equation: $$E(R_p) = R_f + \frac{E(R_m) - R_f}{\sigma_m} \cdot (\beta_p \cdot \sigma_m)$$ **Step 10:** Simplify by canceling $\sigma_m$: $$\boxed{E(R_p) = R_f + \beta_p \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]}$$ This is the **CAPM / Security Market Line (SML)** equation. 🎯 --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Beta of a Portfolio For a portfolio with $n$ assets having weights $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$: $$\beta_p = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_p, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$$ Expanding the covariance: $$\text{Cov}(R_p, R_m) = \text{Cov}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i R_i, R_m\right)$$ $$= \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)$$ Dividing by $\text{Var}(R_m)$: $$\beta_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \beta_i$$ > **Key Insight:** Portfolio beta is simply the **weighted average** of individual betas. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Examples (KTU Exam Style) ### 📘 Example 1: Computing Expected Return via CAPM **Given Data:** - Risk-free rate, $R_f = 6\%$ - Expected market return, $E(R_m) = 14\%$ - Beta of stock, $\beta_i = 1.5$ **Find:** Expected return on stock $i$ using CAPM. **Solution:** Step 1: Identify Market Risk Premium. $$\text{MRP} = E(R_m) - R_f = 14\% - 6\% = 8\%$$ Step 2: Apply CAPM. $$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot \text{MRP}$$ $$E(R_i) = 6\% + 1.5 \times 8\%$$ $$E(R_i) = 6\% + 12\% = \boxed{18\%}$$ --- ### 📘 Example 2: Computing Beta from Correlation **Given Data:** - Correlation between stock and market, $\rho_{i,m} = 0.8$ - Standard deviation of stock, $\sigma_i = 20\%$ - Standard deviation of market, $\sigma_m = 16\%$ **Find:** Beta of stock $i$. **Solution:** Step 1: Apply the correlation-based beta formula. $$\beta_i = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$$ Step 2: Substitute values. $$\beta_i = 0.8 \times \frac{20\%}{16\%}$$ $$\beta_i = 0.8 \times 1.25$$ $$\beta_i = \boxed{1.00}$$ --- ### 📘 Example 3: Portfolio Beta & Expected Return **Given Data:** | Asset | Weight ($w_i$) | Beta ($\beta_i$) | |---|---|---| | Stock A | 0.40 | 0.80 | | Stock B | 0.30 | 1.20 | | Stock C | 0.30 | 1.50 | Given: $R_f = 5\%$, $E(R_m) = 12\%$ **Find:** Portfolio Beta and Expected Return. **Solution:** **Step 1: Portfolio Beta.** $$\beta_p = (0.40 \times 0.80) + (0.30 \times 1.20) + (0.30 \times 1.50)$$ $$\beta_p = 0.32 + 0.36 + 0.45$$ $$\beta_p = \boxed{1.13}$$ **Step 2: Market Risk Premium.** $$\text{MRP} = 12\% - 5\% = 7\%$$ **Step 3: Apply CAPM for Portfolio.** $$E(R_p) = R_f + \beta_p \cdot \text{MRP}$$ $$E(R_p) = 5\% + 1.13 \times 7\%$$ $$E(R_p) = 5\% + 7.91\%$$ $$E(R_p) = \boxed{12.91\%}$$ --- ### 📘 Example 4: Cost of Equity for an Engineering Firm (WACC Application) **Scenario:** An Indian infrastructure firm is evaluating a 10-year highway project. The following data is available: - Risk-free rate (10-Year G-Sec): $R_f = 7\%$ - Expected market return (Nifty 50): $E(R_m) = 13\%$ - Project Beta: $\beta = 1.25$ - Target Debt-Equity Ratio: 60:40 - Pre-tax Cost of Debt: $K_d = 9\%$ - Tax Rate: $T = 30\%$ **Find:** WACC of the project. **Solution:** **Step 1: Compute Cost of Equity ($K_e$) via CAPM.** $$K_e = R_f + \beta \cdot (E(R_m) - R_f)$$ $$K_e = 7\% + 1.25 \times (13\% - 7\%)$$ $$K_e = 7\% + 1.25 \times 6\%$$ $$K_e = 7\% + 7.5\% = \boxed{14.5\%}$$ **Step 2: Compute After-Tax Cost of Debt.** $$K_d(\text{after-tax}) = K_d \times (1 - T) = 9\% \times (1 - 0.30) = \boxed{6.3\%}$$ **Step 3: Compute Weights.** $$W_e = \frac{E}{D + E} = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40$$ $$W_d = \frac{D}{D + E} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.60$$ **Step 4: Compute WACC.** $$\text{WACC} = (W_e \times K_e) + (W_d \times K_d \text{ after-tax})$$ $$\text{WACC} = (0.40 \times 14.5\%) + (0.60 \times 6.3\%)$$ $$\text{WACC} = 5.8\% + 3.78\%$$ $$\text{WACC} = \boxed{9.58\%}$$ This WACC becomes the **discount rate** for evaluating the highway project's NPV. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CAPM in Equilibrium | **Asset Type** | **Beta Value** | **Risk Premium** | **Interpretation** | |---|---|---|---| | Risk-free asset | $\beta = 0$ | Zero | Plots at $(0, R_f)$ on SML | | Market portfolio | $\beta = 1$ | Full MRP | Anchors the SML | | Defensive stock | $0 < \beta < 1$ | Below MRP | Less volatile than market | | Aggressive stock | $\beta > 1$ | Above MRP | More volatile than market | | Negative beta asset | $\beta < 0$ | Negative risk premium | Hedge against market (e.g., gold) | | Overpriced stock | $\beta$ normal, return low | Plots below SML | Negative Jensen's Alpha | | Underpriced stock | $\beta$ normal, return high | Plots above SML | Positive Jensen's Alpha | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** In equilibrium, ALL securities must plot ON the SML. Securities ABOVE the SML are underpriced (buy); BELOW the SML are overpriced (sell). This is the foundational arbitrage argument. --- # 🟦 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CAPM Inputs → Outputs ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Layer
