Why is Beta considered the appropriate measure of risk under CAPM?"** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1 Mark):** The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM), developed by William Sharpe (1964), is an equilibrium model that establishes a linear relationship between the expected return of a security and its systematic risk, as measured by Beta. **Equation (1 Mark):** $$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$ **Why Beta is Appropriate (1 Mark):** Under CAPM, in a well-diversified portfolio, the **unsystematic (firm-specific) risk** is eliminated through diversification. Hence, only **systematic (market) risk** remains, which is precisely what Beta measures. Investors are rewarded only for bearing this non-diversifiable risk; thus, Beta is the correct risk metric under CAPM. --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **"Differentiate between the Capital Market Line (CML) and the Security Market Line (SML)."** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | **Basis** | **CML** | **SML** | |---|---|---| | Applicability (1 Mark) | Applies **only to efficient portfolios** | Applies to **all securities** (efficient or inefficient) | | X-Axis (1 Mark) | Plots against **Total Risk** (Standard Deviation, $\sigma$) | Plots against **Systematic Risk** (Beta, $\beta$) | | Slope (1 Mark) | Slope = **Sharpe Ratio** of market portfolio | Slope = **Market Risk Premium** $[E(R_m) - R_f]$ | --- ## 5.2 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- ### 📝 Question B1 — **OPTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Question:** **(a)** Explain the assumptions of the Capital Asset Pricing Model in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The following data relates to three stocks and the market index: | **Asset** | **Standard Deviation (Return)** | **Correlation with Market** | |---|---|---| | Stock P | 18% | 0.75 | | Stock Q | 22% | 0.60 | | Stock R | 14% | 0.45 | | Market Index (M) | 16% | 1.00 | The risk-free rate is 7% and the expected market return is 13%. You form a portfolio with equal weights (1/3 each) in P, Q, and R. **Compute:** **(i)** Beta of each stock. **(ii)** Portfolio beta. **(iii)** Expected return of the portfolio using CAPM. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✍️ Model Answer for Question B1 (Option A): ### Part (a) — Assumptions of CAPM (7 Marks) **(1) Rational Investors (1 Mark):** All investors are rational and risk-averse. They use the Markowitz mean-variance framework to make optimal portfolio choices. **(2) Single-Period Horizon (1 Mark):** All investors have a common, single-period investment horizon, which simplifies the comparison of returns. **(3) Homogeneous Expectations (1 Mark):** All investors have identical expectations regarding the expected returns, standard deviations, and correlations of all securities. This ensures all investors see the same efficient frontier. **(4) Existence of Risk-Free Asset (1 Mark):** There exists a risk-free asset ($R_f$) at which investors can lend or borrow unlimited amounts at the same rate. **(5) Frictionless Markets (1 Mark):** No taxes, no transaction costs, no commissions, and no restrictions on short-selling. Information is freely and equally available. **(6) Divisibility and Marketability (1 Mark):** All assets are perfectly divisible and marketable. Any quantity of any security can be bought or sold. **(7) Fixed Supply of Assets (1 Mark):** The number of shares of each security is fixed and given; the supply of risky assets is non-zero. The market portfolio (M) is the value-weighted aggregate of all risky assets. --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **(i) Beta of Each Stock — Formula (1 Mark):** $$\beta_i = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$$ **Beta of Stock P (1 Mark):** $$\beta_P = 0.75 \times \frac{18\%}{16\%} = 0.75 \times 1.125 = \boxed{0.84375}$$ **Beta of Stock Q (1 Mark):** $$\beta_Q = 0.60 \times \frac{22\%}{16\%} = 0.60 \times 1.375 = \boxed{0.825}$$ **Beta of Stock R (0.5 Mark):** $$\beta_R = 0.45 \times \frac{14\%}{16\%} = 0.45 \times 0.875 = \boxed{0.39375}$$ **(ii) Portfolio Beta (1 Mark):** With equal weights of $\frac{1}{3}$: $$\beta_p = \frac{1}{3}(0.84375) + \frac{1}{3}(0.825) + \frac{1}{3}(0.39375)$$ $$\beta_p = \frac{1}{3}(2.0625) = \boxed{0.6875}$$ **(iii) Portfolio Expected Return via CAPM (1.5 Marks):** $$E(R_p) = R_f + \beta_p \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$ $$E(R_p) = 7\% + 0.6875 \times (13\% - 7\%)$$ $$E(R_p) = 7\% + 0.6875 \times 6\%$$ $$E(R_p) = 7\% + 4.125\% = \boxed{11.125\%}$$ --- ### 📝 Question B1 — **OPTION B (14 Marks — Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Question:** **(a)** Discuss the limitations of the Capital Asset Pricing Model. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A firm is considering a new project. The relevant data is: - Risk-free rate ($R_f$): 6.5% - Expected market return ($E(R_m)$): 14% - Project Beta: 1.30 - Current market value of equity: ₹50 crores - Market value of debt: ₹30 crores - Pre-tax cost of debt: 9% - Tax rate: 25% **Compute:** **(i)** Cost of equity using CAPM. **(ii)** After-tax cost of debt. **(iii)** Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC). **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✍️ Model Answer for Question B1 (Option B): ### Part (a) — Limitations of CAPM (7 Marks) **(1) Unrealistic Assumptions (1 Mark):** CAPM assumes frictionless markets, homogeneous expectations, and unlimited borrowing/lending at the risk-free rate — none of which hold in the real world. **(2) Single-Period Framework (1 Mark):** CAPM is fundamentally a single-period model, whereas real investment decisions are multi-period in nature. **(3) Estimation Problems (1 Mark):** The model requires precise estimates of $R_f$, $E(R_m)$, and Beta. In practice, these inputs are unstable and prone to significant measurement errors. **(4) Beta Instability (1 Mark):** Historical Beta may not be a reliable predictor of future Beta. Betas tend to fluctuate over time, undermining the model's predictive power. **(5) Market Portfolio Identification (1 Mark):** The true "market portfolio" (the aggregate of ALL risky assets globally) is unobservable. Practical proxies (Nifty, Sensex) are imperfect substitutes. **(6) Ignores Other Risk Factors (1 Mark):** CAPM considers only market risk (Beta). Empirical research (Fama-French 3-Factor Model) shows that size, value, and momentum factors also explain returns. **(7) Empirical Inadequacy (1 Mark):** The flat-slope / empirical SML has a shallower slope than theoretical CAPM, suggesting the model overstates risk premiums for low-beta stocks and understates them for high-beta stocks (Roll's Critique). --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **(i) Cost of Equity ($K_e$) via CAPM (2 Marks):** $$K_e = R_f + \beta \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$$ $$K_e = 6.5\% + 1.30 \times (14\% - 6.5\%)$$ $$K_e = 6.5\% + 1.30 \times 7.5\%$$ $$K_e = 6.5\% + 9.75\% = \boxed{16.25\%}$$ **(ii) After-Tax Cost of Debt (2 Marks):** $$K_d \text{ (after-tax)} = K_d \times (1 - T)$$ $$K_d \text{ (after-tax)} = 9\% \times (1 - 0.25)$$ $$K_d \text{ (after-tax)} = 9\% \times 0.75 = \boxed{6.75\%}$$ **(iii) WACC (3 Marks):** **Step 1 — Compute Weights:** $$W_e = \frac{E}{D + E} = \frac{50}{50 + 30} = \frac{50}{80} = 0.625$$ $$W_d = \frac{D}{D + E} = \frac{30}{80} = 0.375$$ **Step 2 — Apply WACC Formula:** $$\text{WACC} = W_e \times K_e + W_d \times K_d \text{(after-tax)}$$ $$\text{WACC} = (0.625 \times 16.25\%) + (0.375 \times 6.75\%)$$ $$\text{WACC} = 10.156\% + 2.531\%$$ $$\text{WACC} = \boxed{12.687\% \approx 12.69\%}$$ This WACC can be used as the discount rate for the new project's NPV computation. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks in CAPM Questions — KTU Valuation Key Insights:** > > 1. **Confusing CML with SML Axes (–1 to –2 Marks):** Plotting Beta on CML or Standard Deviation on SML is a classic error. Memorize: **CML → σ (Total Risk)**, **SML → β (Systematic Risk)**. > > 2. **Forgetting Risk-Free Asset is Single-Period (–1 Mark):** When using T-Bill yields for $R_f$, mention explicitly that it matches the holding period. Mismatch causes inconsistency. > > 3. **Beta Misinterpretation (–1 Mark):** Writing "Beta measures total risk" is wrong. Beta measures **ONLY systematic risk**. Total risk = $\sqrt{\text{Systematic}^2 + \text{Unsystematic}^2}$. > > 4. **Skipping MRP Calculation Step (–1 Mark):** Examiners expect explicit working: $\text{MRP} = E(R_m) - R_f$ before plugging into CAPM. Don't jump directly to the final answer. > > 5. **WACC Weight Errors (–2 Marks):** Using **book values** instead of **market values** of debt and equity is a frequent mistake. KTU examiners prefer market-value weights. > > 6. **Not Stating Assumptions Explicitly (–1 to –2 Marks):** When asked "Explain CAPM," many students forget to list assumptions. Always include them as a separate bullet section. > > 7. **Jensen's Alpha Confusion (–1 Mark):** If asked about "stock outperformance," compute $\alpha_i = E(R_i) - [R_f + \beta_i \cdot \text{MRP}]$. Positive alpha → SML above; negative → SML below. --- ## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 Last-Minute Revision — CAPM in 60 Seconds:** - ⭐ **CAPM Equation:** $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]$ - ⭐ **Beta Formula 1:** $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$ - ⭐ **Beta Formula 2:** $\beta_i = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m}$ - ⭐ **Portfolio Beta:** $\beta_p = \sum w_i \beta_i$ (simple weighted average) - ⭐ **Beta of Market = 1**, **Beta of Risk-Free Asset = 0** - ⭐ **Market Risk Premium (MRP)** = $E(R_m) - R_f$ - ⭐ **CML** = Efficient portfolios only, X-axis = $\sigma$ (Total Risk), Slope = Sharpe Ratio - ⭐ **SML** = All securities, X-axis = $\beta$ (Systematic Risk), Slope = MRP - ⭐ **CAPM Developed by:** William Sharpe (1964) — Nobel Prize 1990 - ⭐ **Jensen's Alpha:** $\alpha_i = E(R_i) - [R_f + \beta_i \cdot \text{MRP}]$ → Positive = Outperformance - ⭐ **Sharpe Ratio:** $S = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ → Ranking on CML - ⭐ **Treynor Ratio:** $T = \dfrac{E(R_p) - R_f}{\beta_p}$ → Ranking on SML - ⭐ **Key Assumption:** Investors are rewarded **only** for **systematic** risk, not unsystematic risk (diversifiable). - ⭐ **KTU Application:** CAPM is the standard tool to compute **Cost of Equity ($K_e$)** which feeds into WACC and DCF. - ⭐ **Roll's Critique:** The true market portfolio is unobservable; CAPM may not be testable. - ⭐ **Extensions:** Fama-French 3-Factor Model adds Size (SMB) and Value (HML) factors to CAPM. - ⭐ **Always State:** MRP value before plugging into CAPM in numerical problems (avoids part-mark deduction). > 🎯 **Final KTU Mantra:** *"CAPM = $R_f$ + Beta × Market Risk Premium. Beta = Systematic Risk Only. CML → σ, SML → β. Cost of Equity → WACC → DCF."* ---

topics.title: Portfolio Planning and Construction: Portfolio Planning - Investment Policy Statement (IPS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Portfolio Planning and Construction: Portfolio Planning - Investment Policy Statement (IPS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)

An **Investment Policy Statement (IPS)** is a written document, prepared jointly by the portfolio manager and the client (or institutional beneficiary), that systematically documents the investor's **investment objectives**, **constraints**, **strategic asset allocation**, and **portfolio management policies** over the agreed investment horizon. It functions as the **governing blueprint** for all subsequent investment decisions, performance evaluation, and rebalancing activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"The Investment Policy Statement is a comprehensive, written planning document that establishes the investor's return objectives, risk tolerance, and investment constraints, and serves as the operational framework for constructing, monitoring, and revising the investment portfolio over time."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The GPS for Your Money"

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** for the first time. Before starting, you would:

1. **Set a destination** (where exactly in Delhi?) → *Investment Objective*
2. **Know your fuel budget** and **time available** → *Investment Constraints*
3. **Choose a route** (NH vs Expressway) → *Strategic Asset Allocation*
4. **Decide refuel stops** → *Rebalancing Policy*

An **IPS does exactly this for money**. Without it, a portfolio manager is "driving blind" – making random stops, overshooting, or running out of fuel. With it, every decision is anchored to a pre-agreed plan, minimizing disputes and emotional mistakes.

## 1.3 Why the IPS is Non-Negotiable in KTU Board Valuation

- **Regulatory necessity**: SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India) and global CFA/SEBI codes mandate a written IPS for all fiduciary relationships.
- **Dispute resolution**: Acts as legal evidence if the client sues the manager for underperformance.
- **Discipline mechanism**: Prevents behavioral errors (panic-selling, over-trading).

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Foundational Pillars of an IPS:**
> - **Pillar 1** → *Objectives* (What does the investor want?)
> - **Pillar 2** → *Constraints* (What restricts the investor?)
> Everything else flows from these two pillars.

## 1.4 Classification of Investors in IPS Context

| Investor Type | Primary Objective | Risk Tolerance | Time Horizon |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Individual (Retirement)** | Capital preservation + Income | Low–Moderate | Long (15–30 yrs) |
| **Individual (Wealth Accumulation)** | Capital appreciation | High | Long |
| **Endowment Fund** | Real return ≥ spending rate | Moderate | Perpetual |
| **Defined Benefit Pension** | Meet liabilities | Low | Long |
| **Insurance Company** | Match liabilities | Low | Liability-driven |
| **Sovereign Wealth Fund** | Inter-generational wealth | Moderate | Very Long |
| **Bank Treasury** | Liquidity + Capital adequacy | Very Low | Short |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Six Mandatory Components of an IPS

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate and explain **six** structural components of a fully-formed IPS. Each is non-optional for full marks.

### Component 1: Background and Investment Objectives
- Background describes **who** the investor is (age, family, wealth source).
- Investment objectives state the **goals** in measurable terms.
- Two sub-objectives:
  - **Return Objective** – expressed as a specific % (e.g., "achieve 9% real return").
  - **Risk Objective** – expressed either as a target standard deviation (e.g., "σ ≤ 12%") or as a maximum drawdown limit (e.g., "no annual loss greater than 15%").

### Component 2: Investment Constraints
The four universal constraints (KF-1 mnemonic: **L**iquidity, **T**ime Horizon, **T**axes, **L**egal/Regulatory):

1. **Liquidity Needs** – Cash reserves required for predictable expenses (≥ 2 years of living expenses for individuals; daily redemptions for mutual funds).
2. **Time Horizon** – Usually stated as a **range** (e.g., "5–8 years") rather than a single date.
3. **Tax Considerations** – Tax-loss harvesting strategies; tax-efficient asset location (place tax-inefficient assets in tax-deferred accounts).
4. **Legal and Regulatory** – SEBI mutual fund regulations, RBI norms, ERISA-style fiduciary rules.
5. **Unique Circumstances** – ESG preferences, ethical exclusions, concentrated stock holdings, family business involvement.

### Component 3: Strategic Asset Allocation (SAA)
The long-term **policy portfolio** that divides capital across broad asset classes. This is the **single most important** determinant of portfolio risk and return (typically explains 80–90% of return variability – Brinson, Hood, Beebower study).

### Component 4: Portfolio Policies (Rebalancing)
- **Rebalancing Policy** dictates:
  - **Calendar rebalancing**: every quarter / half-year.
  - **Percentage-of-portfolio rebalancing**: when an asset drifts ± 5% from SAA weight.
  - **Tolerance bands** (e.g., allow ±5% deviation before rebalancing).

### Component 5: Portfolio Monitoring and Review
- Frequency: typically **quarterly** review meetings with the client.
- Performance attribution: against benchmark and against the IPS return objective.

### Component 6: Appendices and Strategic Adjustments
- Tactical Asset Allocation (TAA) – short-term deviations from SAA (allowed ± 5% or ± 10%).
- Re-statement of objectives if life event triggers (marriage, divorce, inheritance).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Reminder:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` inside LaTeX may break markdown rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Meaning | Boundary / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Portfolio Risk | $\sigma_p = \sqrt{w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2}$ | $w_i$ = weight, $\sigma_i$ = SD, $\rho_{12}$ = correlation | $0 \leq w_i \leq 1$, $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| Two-Asset Minimum Variance Portfolio | $w_1^{*} = \dfrac{\sigma_2^2 - \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2}$ | Minimises $\sigma_p$ | $0 \leq w_1^{*} \leq 1$ |
| After-Tax Return | $R_{after} = R_{before} \cdot (1 - t)$ | $t$ = effective tax rate | $0 \leq t \leq 1$ |
| Real (Inflation-Adjusted) Return | $R_{real} = \dfrac{1 + R_{nom}}{1 + i} - 1$ | $i$ = inflation rate | Approximation: $R_{real} \approx R_{nom} - i$ |
| Required Real Return | $RRR = \dfrac{\text{Living Expenses Today}}{\text{Real Portfolio Today}} + i$ | Spending rate + inflation | Used for retirement IPS |
| Sharpe Ratio | $S = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | $R_f$ = risk-free rate | Higher = better risk-adjusted return |
| Capital Market Line (CML) Expected Return | $E(R_p) = R_f + \dfrac{R_m - R_f}{\sigma_m} \cdot \sigma_p$ | Market risk premium scaled by risk | Used for CML-efficient portfolios only |
| Human Capital Conversion | $HC_{equity} = \dfrac{\sigma_{HC}}{\sigma_{Total}}$ | Proportion of wealth in human capital | Determines stock/bond split |
| Portfolio Rebalancing Trigger | Trigger if $\vert w_{actual} - w_{SAA} \vert > \Delta$ | $\Delta$ = tolerance band | Typical $\Delta = 5\%$ |

## 2.3 The "Three-Stage" Risk Quantification Framework

The KTU 2024 module places heavy emphasis on differentiating **three distinct concepts of risk** within an IPS:

### Stage 1: **Ability to Take Risk (ATR)**
A *quantitative* measure based on the investor's structural financial position.
- High ATR if: long time horizon, stable employment, liquid net worth, no large liabilities.
- Low ATR if: short horizon, job insecurity, illiquid assets, large upcoming outflows.

### Stage 2: **Willingness to Take Risk (WTR)**
A *qualitative, psychological* measure assessed through questionnaires.
- WTR is independent of ATR.
- A conservative investor with high ATR still gets a conservative IPS.

### Stage 3: **Required Risk (RR)**
The *minimum* risk needed to achieve the return objective.
- If RR > WTR > ATR → Conflict: must **lower** the return objective.
- If WTR > RR and ATR > RR → IPS can proceed with RR.
- If WTR < RR → Client must be **re-educated** before proceeding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Many students confuse *Willingness* (WTR) with *Ability* (ATR). They are conceptually and operationally distinct. Marking schemes explicitly test this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I – Constructing the Strategic Asset Allocation (SAA) for a Two-Asset Case

**Problem Setup (typical KTU-style 7-mark problem):**
- An investor has ₹50,00,000. 60% must go to equity (μ_E = 14%, σ_E = 18%), 40% to bonds (μ_B = 8%, σ_B = 6%). Correlation ρ = 0.25. Tax rate = 30%. Inflation = 6%.

**Step 1 – Expected Portfolio Return**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(R_p) &= w_E \mu_E + w_B \mu_B \\
       &= (0.60)(14\%) + (0.40)(8\%) \\
       &= 8.40\% + 3.20\% \\
       &= 11.60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Linear weighted average because expected value is linear in probability weights.

**Step 2 – Portfolio Variance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 &= w_E^2 \sigma_E^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_E w_B \rho_{EB} \sigma_E \sigma_B \\
           &= (0.60)^2 (18)^2 + (0.40)^2 (6)^2 + 2 (0.60)(0.40)(0.25)(18)(6) \\
           &= (0.36)(324) + (0.16)(36) + (0.48)(27) \\
           &= 116.64 + 5.76 + 12.96 \\
           &= 135.36
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Portfolio Standard Deviation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p &= \sqrt{135.36} \\
         &\approx 11.63\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – After-Tax Nominal Return**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{after} &= R_{nom} \cdot (1 - t) \\
          &= 11.60\% \cdot (1 - 0.30) \\
          &= 11.60\% \cdot 0.70 \\
          &= 8.12\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – After-Tax Real Return (Fisher Equation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{real} &= \frac{1 + R_{after}}{1 + i} - 1 \\
         &= \frac{1 + 0.0812}{1 + 0.06} - 1 \\
         &= \frac{1.0812}{1.06} - 1 \\
         &= 1.02000 - 1 \\
         &= 0.02000 = 2.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** If the IPS states a real return objective of, say, 3%, this portfolio **fails** the IPS. The manager must either (a) raise equity weight, (b) extend time horizon, or (c) reduce the return objective in writing via an IPS amendment.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II – Minimum-Variance Portfolio (MVP) Weight

The IPS frequently requires the **lowest-risk** allocation among asset classes for risk-averse clients.

**Given:** σ₁ = 15%, σ₂ = 8%, ρ₁₂ = 0.30. Solve for MVP weight in Asset 1.

**Step 1 – Apply MVP Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1^{*} &= \frac{\sigma_2^2 - \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2} \\
        &= \frac{(8)^2 - (0.30)(15)(8)}{(15)^2 + (8)^2 - 2(0.30)(15)(8)} \\
        &= \frac{64 - 36}{225 + 64 - 72} \\
        &= \frac{28}{217} \\
        &\approx 0.1290
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Weight in Asset 2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_2^{*} &= 1 - w_1^{*} = 1 - 0.1290 = 0.8710
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Portfolio Standard Deviation at MVP**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{MVP} &= \sqrt{(w_1^*)^2 \sigma_1^2 + (w_2^*)^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1^* w_2^* \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2} \\
            &= \sqrt{(0.1290)^2 (225) + (0.8710)^2 (64) + 2(0.1290)(0.8710)(0.30)(120)} \\
            &= \sqrt{3.745 + 48.586 + 8.092} \\
            &= \sqrt{60.423} \\
            &\approx 7.77\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Sanity Check**

Compare with 100% in Asset 2 (σ = 8%). The MVP (σ = 7.77%) is **lower** than the lower-risk asset alone due to diversification benefit. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to compute MVP, always present **all four steps** (formula, substitution, weight verification, σ computation). Half-marks are awarded for each step.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III – Rebalancing Trade Calculation

A portfolio was set at 60/40 equity/bond. After one year, due to equity outperformance, weights drift to 68/32. Rebalancing band = ±5%. The new portfolio value is ₹10,00,000.

**Step 1 – Check Trigger**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{equity} &= \vert 0.68 - 0.60 \vert = 0.08 = 8\% > 5\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TRIGGER}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute Target Value of Equity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{equity, target} &= 0.60 \times 10,00,000 = 6,00,000 \\
V_{bond, target} &= 0.40 \times 10,00,000 = 4,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute Rebalancing Trade**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sell Equity} &= 6,80,000 - 6,00,000 = 80,000 \\
\text{Buy Bond} &= 4,00,000 - 3,20,000 = 80,000
\end{aligned}
$$

This trade **locks in gains** from equity outperformance and **restores** strategic weights.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: IPS Construction Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **sequential, closed-loop workflow** for constructing and operating an Investment Policy Statement. Note that node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain clean uppercase text only (no markdown inside quotes) to satisfy the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STEP 1 INVESTOR INTAKE"] --> A2["STEP 2 ASSESS ABILITY TO TAKE RISK"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3 ASSESS WILLINGNESS TO TAKE RISK"]
    A3 --> A4{"STEP 4 RISK CAPACITY DECISION"}
    A4 -->|ATR SUFFICIENT| A5["STEP 5 DEFINE RETURN OBJECTIVE"]
    A4 -->|ATR INSUFFICIENT| A4B["STEP 5A REVISE OBJECTIVES DOWNWARD"]
    A4B --> A5
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6 DEFINE RISK OBJECTIVE"]
    A6 --> A7["STEP 7 IDENTIFY CONSTRAINTS L T T L U"]
    A7 --> A8["STEP 8 FORM STRATEGIC ASSET ALLOCATION"]
    A8 --> A9["STEP 9 DOCUMENT IPS IN WRITING"]
    A9 --> A10["STEP 10 EXECUTE PORTFOLIO TRANSACTIONS"]
    A10 --> A11["STEP 11 MONITOR AND REBALANCE"]
    A11 --> A12{"STEP 12 LIFE EVENT OR REVIEW DATE"}
    A12 -->|YES| A1
    A12 -->|NO| A11
```

## 4.2 Risk Decision Matrix – Three-Way Reconciliation

The following diagram illustrates the **triangulation** of Ability, Willingness, and Required Risk that every IPS must resolve before finalization.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["ABILITY TO TAKE RISK ATR OBJECTIVE FINANCIAL CAPACITY"] --> B4{"TRIANGULATION CHECK"}
    B2["WILLINGNESS TO TAKE RISK WTR PSYCHOLOGICAL DISPOSITION"] --> B4
    B3["REQUIRED RISK RR MIN RISK FOR OBJECTIVE"] --> B4
    B4 -->|ALL THREE ALIGN| B5["IPS APPROVED PROCEED TO ALLOCATION"]
    B4 -->|CONFLICT DETECTED| B6["RE EDUCATE CLIENT OR REVISE IPS"]
    B6 --> B1
```

## 4.3 Asset Allocation Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1["TOTAL PORTFOLIO CAPITAL"] --> C2["STRATEGIC ASSET ALLOCATION POLICY WEIGHTS"]
    C1 --> C3["TACTICAL ASSET ALLOCATION SHORT TERM OVERLAY"]
    C2 --> C4["EQUITY LARGE CAP MID CAP SMALL CAP"]
    C2 --> C5["FIXED INCOME GILT CORPORATE SHORT TERM"]
    C2 --> C6["ALTERNATIVE REAL ESTATE COMMODITY GOLD"]
    C2 --> C7["CASH AND EQUIVALENTS"]
    C3 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
    C6 --> C8["REBALANCING BAND 5 PERCENT TRIGGER"]
    C7 --> C8
    C8 --> C9["PORTFOLIO EXECUTION"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A – SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`

**Q:** *"Define an Investment Policy Statement (IPS). List any FOUR mandatory components of an IPS."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

An Investment Policy Statement is a written planning document that records the investor's investment objectives, constraints, asset allocation strategy, and portfolio management policies. It acts as a governing guide for the portfolio manager. **[1 Mark]**

The four mandatory components are: **[½ Mark each]**
1. Investment Objectives (return and risk)
2. Investment Constraints (liquidity, time horizon, taxes, legal)
3. Strategic Asset Allocation
4. Rebalancing Policy (or Portfolio Monitoring & Review)

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**Q:** *"Differentiate between 'Ability to Take Risk' (ATR) and 'Willingness to Take Risk' (WTR) in the context of an IPS."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Ability to Take Risk (ATR) | Willingness to Take Risk (WTR) |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Objective / Quantitative | Subjective / Qualitative |
| Based on | Financial capacity, horizon, liabilities | Investor psychology, past experience |
| Assessed via | Financial statements, cash flow analysis | Questionnaires, interviews |
| Changeable | Slowly (life events) | Can change rapidly (market crashes) |
| Used for | Determining **maximum** feasible risk | Determining **desired** risk level |

**[1½ Marks for ATR definition + 1½ Marks for WTR definition with comparison]**

---

## PART B – LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH, INTERNAL CHOICE)

### Question B – Choice A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper]`

**Q:** *"Mr. Ramesh, aged 42, has accumulated ₹80,00,000 in financial assets and earns ₹25,00,000 per annum. He wishes to retire at 60 with a real return target of 4% above inflation. His current monthly expense is ₹1,20,000. He has a stable government job and no major liabilities.*
*(a) Construct a detailed Investment Policy Statement for Mr. Ramesh, clearly identifying his return objective, risk objective, and four key constraints. [7 Marks]*
*(b) Assuming he allocates 65% to equity (μ=15%, σ=20%) and 35% to bonds (μ=7%, σ=5%), with correlation 0.20, compute the portfolio's expected return and standard deviation. State whether the IPS return objective is met, assuming current inflation is 5.5% and tax rate is 30%. [7 Marks]"* **[14 Marks Total]**

---

#### Model Solution – Part B Choice A

**Part (a) – IPS Construction for Mr. Ramesh [7 Marks]**

| IPS Section | Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Investor Background** | Age 42, government employee, ₹80L financial assets, ₹25L income | 1 |
| **Return Objective** | Real return ≥ 4% above inflation. After-tax nominal = (1.04)(1.055)−1 ≈ 9.72% → Round to **10% nominal after-tax** | 2 |
| **Risk Objective** | Maximum annual standard deviation **≤ 12%**; maximum drawdown ≤ 15% | 1 |
| **Constraint 1: Time Horizon** | 18 years (till age 60) – **long horizon**, allows higher equity | 0.5 |
| **Constraint 2: Liquidity** | Maintain 6 months expenses (~₹7.2L) in liquid/cash fund | 0.5 |
| **Constraint 3: Taxes** | Old regime – use ELSS, NPS, PPF for Section 80C benefits; debt in arbitrage funds for indexation | 0.5 |
| **Constraint 4: Legal/Regulatory** | SEBI-regulated instruments only; comply with Section 54EC for property sale reinvestment | 0.5 |
| **Strategic Asset Allocation** | Equity 65%, Debt 30%, Gold/Alternatives 5% | 1 |
| **Rebalancing Policy** | Calendar basis (quarterly) + ±5% drift band | 0.5 |
| **Review Schedule** | Annual review; immediate review on promotion/relocation | 0.5 |

*Total: 7 Marks*

---

**Part (b) – Portfolio Return & Risk Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 – Expected Portfolio Return**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(R_p) &= w_E \mu_E + w_B \mu_B \\
       &= (0.65)(15\%) + (0.35)(7\%) \\
       &= 9.75\% + 2.45\% \\
       &= 12.20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**[1.5 Marks]**

**Step 2 – Portfolio Variance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 &= (0.65)^2 (20)^2 + (0.35)^2 (5)^2 + 2 (0.65)(0.35)(0.20)(20)(5) \\
           &= (0.4225)(400) + (0.1225)(25) + (0.455)(20) \\
           &= 169.00 + 3.0625 + 9.10 \\
           &= 181.1625
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Step 3 – Standard Deviation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p &= \sqrt{181.1625} \approx 13.46\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 4 – After-Tax Nominal Return**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{after} &= 12.20\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 12.20\% \times 0.70 = 8.54\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 5 – After-Tax Real Return (Fisher Equation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{real} &= \frac{1 + 0.0854}{1 + 0.055} - 1 \\
         &= \frac{1.0854}{1.055} - 1 \\
         &= 1.02882 - 1 \\
         &= 2.88\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 6 – IPS Compliance Check**

IPS target real return = 4.00%. **Achieved real return = 2.88%.**
Difference = −1.12%. → **OBJECTIVE NOT MET.** ⚠️

**Recommendation:** Reduce equity allocation to tax-advantaged ELSS/NPS; reallocate 10% from debt to equity in tax-deferred wrappers; or **extend time horizon assumption** by delaying retirement to age 62.

**[0.5 Marks]**

---

### Question B – Choice B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**Q:** *"An investor has a current portfolio valued at ₹50,00,000 with a strategic asset allocation of 60% equity and 40% bonds. After a year of strong equity market performance, the portfolio has grown to ₹62,00,000 with actual weights of 70% equity and 30% bonds. The IPS specifies a rebalancing band of ±5%.*
*(a) Determine whether rebalancing is triggered. If yes, calculate the exact buy/sell amounts to restore the strategic weights. [7 Marks]*
*(b) Define Strategic Asset Allocation (SAA) and Tactical Asset Allocation (TAA). Explain the role of each within the IPS framework. Why is SAA considered the most important driver of long-term portfolio returns? [7 Marks]"* **[14 Marks Total]**

---

#### Model Solution – Part B Choice B

**Part (a) – Rebalancing Trigger and Trade [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 – Compute Drift**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{equity} &= \vert 70\% - 60\% \vert = 10\% \\
\Delta_{bond} &= \vert 30\% - 40\% \vert = 10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Compare with Tolerance Band**

Band = ±5%. Drift = 10% > 5%. → **REBALANCING TRIGGERED.** ✓

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 3 – Compute Current Monetary Weights**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{equity, actual} &= 0.70 \times 62,00,000 = 43,40,000 \\
V_{bond, actual} &= 0.30 \times 62,00,000 = 18,60,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 4 – Compute Target Monetary Weights (at new total ₹62,00,000)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{equity, target} &= 0.60 \times 62,00,000 = 37,20,000 \\
V_{bond, target} &= 0.40 \times 62,00,000 = 24,80,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 5 – Compute Rebalancing Trade**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sell Equity (Cash from Equity)} &= 43,40,000 - 37,20,000 = 6,20,000 \\
\text{Buy Bonds (Invest in Debt)} &= 24,80,000 - 18,60,000 = 6,20,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Step 6 – Transaction Cost Consideration** (assume 0.25% brokerage and 0.10% STT)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost} &\approx (6,20,000)(0.0035) = 2,170 \\
\text{Net Benefit of Rebalancing} &\approx 6,20,000 \text{ locked-in gain} - 2,170 \text{ cost}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Rebalancing is highly beneficial as the locked-in gain far exceeds transaction cost. **[2 Marks]**

---

**Part (b) – SAA vs TAA + Why SAA Dominates [7 Marks]**

**Definition of SAA [2 Marks]:**
Strategic Asset Allocation is the **long-term, policy-level** allocation across broad asset classes (equity, fixed income, cash, alternatives) that reflects the investor's return objective, risk tolerance, and constraints. It is **static** over the investment horizon and rebalanced back to these weights periodically.

**Definition of TAA [2 Marks]:**
Tactical Asset Allocation involves **short-to-medium-term deviations** from the SAA weights to exploit perceived mispricings or market conditions. It is **active** in nature, bounded by ±5% to ±10% from SAA, and never alters the long-term strategic direction.

**Role within IPS [2 Marks]:**

| Aspect | SAA | TAA |
|---|---|---|
| Time frame | Multi-year | Weeks to months |
| Decision authority | Client-approved in IPS | Manager discretion |
| Risk budget | Drives 80–90% of risk | Adds incremental 5–10% risk |
| Rebalancing | Mandatory ± 5% bands | Discretionary exits |

**Why SAA Dominates Long-Term Returns [1 Mark]:**
The seminal Brinson, Hood, and Beebower (1986) study, later refined in 1991, empirically demonstrated that **over 80% of portfolio return variability** is explained by asset allocation policy (SAA), while security selection and market timing together explain less than 20%. Therefore, the IPS correctly prioritizes documenting and adhering to SAA as the principal determinant of investor outcomes.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Five Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on IPS Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing WTR with ATR** – Examiner specifically awards 2 marks for distinction. Skipping it costs full marks.
> 2. **Missing the Fisher Equation** – Real-return calculations without using $R_{real} = \frac{1 + R_{nom}}{1 + i} - 1$ (or its approximation) lose 1 mark per sub-question.
> 3. **Forgetting the IPS Compliance Check** – After computing return/risk, you must **explicitly state** whether the IPS objective is met. Many students compute but do not conclude.
> 4. **Ignoring Transaction Costs in Rebalancing** – A rebalancing answer that ignores brokerage and STT will lose 0.5–1 mark for real-world relevance.
> 5. **Vague IPS Wording** – Writing "low risk" instead of "maximum σ ≤ 12% and max drawdown ≤ 15%" loses 1 mark. IPS objectives must be **quantified**.
>
> **Pro Tip:** Always end an IPS answer with a **"Reviewed and Approved"** line and the relevant date. Examiners reward procedural completeness.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Memorization for KTU ESE – Module 4 IPS:**

- [ ] **IPS = Written document = Objectives + Constraints + SAA + Rebalancing + Review**
- [ ] **Six components:** Background, Objectives, Constraints, SAA, Policies, Review
- [ ] **Four universal constraints:** Liquidity, Time Horizon, Taxes, Legal/Regulatory
- [ ] **Three risk concepts:** ATR (objective), WTR (subjective), RR (required)
- [ ] **Risk Resolution Rule:** RR ≤ min(ATR, WTR) → IPS proceeds. Otherwise revise.
- [ ] **Rebalancing Bands:** Typical ±5% drift from SAA weight triggers trade
- [ ] **Brinson Study:** SAA explains ~90% of return variability
- [ ] **Fisher Equation:** $R_{real} = \frac{1 + R_{nom}}{1 + i} - 1$ (exact form for exam)
- [ ] **After-tax:** $R_{after} = R_{nom} \cdot (1 - t)$
- [ ] **MVP weight formula:** $w_1^{*} = \frac{\sigma_2^2 - \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2}$
- [ ] **Sharpe Ratio:** $S = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ – higher = better risk-adjusted return
- [ ] **IPS serves as:** legal shield for manager, discipline tool for client, communication bridge between both

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major Components of an IPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET744 - CORPORATE FINANCE AND PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Major Components of an IPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Major Components of an Investment Policy Statement (IPS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Investment Policy Statement (IPS)** is a written, legally binding strategic document that explicitly defines the investment objectives, constraints, asset allocation framework, benchmarks, rebalancing rules, and the duties and responsibilities of all stakeholders involved in the management of an investment portfolio. As per the **CFA Institute's Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS)** and the framework recommended in standard portfolio management texts, the IPS functions as the *rulebook of engagement* between the **investor (principal)** and the **investment manager (agent)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The Investment Policy Statement (IPS) is the cornerstone document that translates the investor's goals, risk tolerance, and constraints into a structured, measurable, and enforceable investment strategy. It eliminates ambiguity, controls emotional decision-making, and provides a defensible benchmark against which manager performance can be objectively evaluated.

The IPS is considered the **single most important document** in the portfolio management process because every subsequent investment decision — security selection, asset allocation, performance evaluation — must be justified by reference back to the IPS.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are about to embark on a long road trip from **Thiruvananthapuram to Delhi** (approximately 2,800 km). Would you simply start driving? No. You would first:

- Decide **the purpose** of the trip (vacation, business, family emergency) — this defines your *return objective*.
- Decide **how much risk** you are willing to take (mountainous ghat roads vs. highway-only, night driving vs. day driving) — this defines your *risk tolerance*.
- Determine **your budget**, **time available**, and **must-visit stops** — these are your *constraints*.
- Select **the route** (NH44 vs. NH48) and the **mode of transport** — this is your *strategic asset allocation*.
- Fix **checkpoint rules** (refuel every 500 km, check tire pressure daily) — this is your *rebalancing policy*.

That entire pre-trip plan is analogous to an **Investment Policy Statement**. It is the roadmap that governs every portfolio decision. Without it, the investor is driving blind, and the manager has no clear destination.

### 1.3 Why the IPS is Indispensable

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Foundational Reasons for an IPS:**
> 1. **Discipline** — Prevents ad-hoc, emotion-driven decisions during market volatility.
> 2. **Accountability** — Provides an objective standard to evaluate manager performance.
> 3. **Communication** — Aligns the expectations of the investor, manager, custodian, and auditor.

The **CFA Level III curriculum** (which is widely adopted by KTU's finance electives) identifies the IPS as a *living document* — it must be reviewed and updated whenever there is a material change in the investor's circumstances (marriage, birth of a child, retirement, change in tax law).

### 1.4 The Investor's Risk Profile — A Sub-Component of the IPS

Before listing the major components, it is critical to understand the **Risk Profile**, which is derived from two independent dimensions:

- **Ability to Take Risk (ATR)** — A *quantitative* measure based on the investor's financial position (wealth, income stability, time horizon).
- **Willingness to Take Risk (WTR)** — A *qualitative* measure based on the investor's psychological comfort with volatility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:**
> The actual risk taken in the portfolio must equal the **LESSER** of (ATR, WTR). Many students incorrectly write "the greater of" — this is a guaranteed loss of 2 marks in the KTU ESE valuation key.

---

## 2. The Eight Major Components of an IPS — Overview

A complete IPS, as prescribed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET744)** module structure, contains the following major components. Each component is documented in detail in SECTION_2.

| # | Component | Strategic Purpose |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Background & Investment Objectives** | Defines *why* the portfolio exists |
| 2 | **Investment Parameters (Return & Risk Objectives)** | Quantifies *what* the portfolio must deliver |
| 3 | **Investment Constraints** | Lists *what is forbidden or required* |
| 4 | **Strategic Asset Allocation (SAA)** | Sets the long-term mix of asset classes |
| 5 | **Portfolio Management Structure** | Discretionary vs. Non-Discretionary |
| 6 | **Rebalancing Policy** | Defines *when and how* to rebalance |
| 7 | **Performance Measurement & Benchmarks** | Sets the *yardstick* for evaluation |
| 8 | **Duties, Responsibilities & Review** | Governs *who does what and how often* |

The next section dissects each of these components with mathematical rigor and real-world applicability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — Background & Investment Objectives

The IPS must begin with a **Background Section** that establishes the identity, life stage, financial position, and personal circumstances of the investor. For institutional investors (pension funds, insurance companies, endowments), this includes the nature of liabilities, funding status, and sponsor obligations.

**Investment Objectives** are then framed as a **dual mandate**:

### A. Return Objectives

The required return is the minimum rate of return the portfolio must earn to meet the investor's stated financial goals. The standard formula is:

$$
r_{required} = \frac{\text{Liquid Assets} + \text{Present Value of Contributions} - \text{Present Value of Liabilities}}{\text{Present Value of Portfolio}}
$$

A more practical form, applicable to individuals saving for a goal, is:

$$
r_{required} = \frac{FV_{goal} / (1 + r)^n - PV_{assets}}{\text{Annual Contributions}}
$$

where:
- $FV_{goal}$ = Future value of the financial goal (e.g., child's higher education in 2035)
- $PV_{assets}$ = Present value of current assets allocated to the goal
- $n$ = Number of years to the goal
- $r$ = Assumed inflation or discount rate

### B. Risk Objectives

Risk objectives are expressed either **qualitatively** (low, moderate, high) or **quantitatively** (maximum drawdown of 15%, standard deviation not exceeding 12%, Value-at-Risk not exceeding 5% of portfolio value at 95% confidence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:**
> In the KTU board exam, students are expected to derive the **required return** for a hypothetical individual or institutional investor. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying whether the goal is a *single lump-sum liability* or a *stream of liabilities*, since this determines the time horizon and asset allocation logic.

---

## 2.2 Component 2 — Investment Parameters (Return & Risk Objectives)

The IPS must explicitly state both objectives in **measurable, testable** terms.

### 2.2.1 Return Objectives — Hierarchical Priority

For **institutional investors**, the return objectives follow a strict hierarchy:

1. **Meet Liabilities** (highest priority) — for pension funds and insurance companies.
2. **Maintain Real (Inflation-Adjusted) Value of Assets**.
3. **Achieve a Real Return of X% above inflation** (e.g., 3% above CPI).
4. **Maximize Return** (lowest priority — only for endowments with no liabilities).

For **individual investors**, the hierarchy is:

1. **Maintain Lifestyle** (preserve capital).
2. **Meet Specific Goals** (home purchase, education).
3. **Increase Real Wealth**.

### 2.2.2 Risk Objectives — The Three Sub-Components

The risk objective must address all three forms of risk simultaneously:

- **Volatility Risk** — measured by standard deviation ($\sigma$) or variance ($\sigma^2$).
- **Shortfall Risk** — probability of failing to meet the required return.
- **Permanent Loss of Capital Risk** — the most severe form, focused on downside.

$$
\sigma_{portfolio} = \sqrt{w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{1,2} \sigma_1 \sigma_2}
$$

where $w_1, w_2$ are portfolio weights, $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ are individual asset standard deviations, and $\rho_{1,2}$ is the correlation coefficient.

---

## 2.3 Component 3 — Investment Constraints

Constraints are the **non-negotiable boundaries** within which the portfolio must operate. They are classified into five major categories:

### 3.1 Liquidity Constraints

The portfolio must hold enough liquid assets to meet anticipated cash outflows. The **liquidity ratio** is computed as:

$$
\text{Liquidity Ratio} = \frac{\text{Liquid Assets}}{\text{Total Net Assets}}
$$

### 3.2 Time Horizon Constraints

The IPS must clearly define the investment horizon, typically segmented into:
- **Short-term** (0–3 years)
- **Intermediate-term** (3–10 years)
- **Long-term** (10+ years)

> [!NOTE]
> Different time horizons imply different asset allocations. The shorter the horizon, the greater the allocation to cash and high-grade bonds.

### 3.3 Tax Constraints

Tax considerations (capital gains tax, dividend tax, tax-exempt status) materially affect the after-tax return. The **after-tax return** formula is:

$$
r_{after-tax} = r_{before-tax} \times (1 - t_{effective})
$$

where $t_{effective}$ is the effective marginal tax rate applicable to the investor.

### 3.4 Legal & Regulatory Constraints

Examples include:
- **ERISA** regulations for U.S. pension funds.
- **SEBI** regulations for Indian mutual funds.
- Restrictions on holding foreign securities for certain institutional investors.

### 3.5 Unique Circumstances

These are investor-specific constraints such as:
- Religious or ethical restrictions (e.g., Shariah-compliant portfolios avoiding alcohol, tobacco, conventional finance).
- Industry-specific exclusions (e.g., a tobacco company excluding tobacco stocks).
- Concentration restrictions (e.g., no single stock > 5% of portfolio).

---

## 2.4 Component 4 — Strategic Asset Allocation (SAA)

The SAA is the **single most important determinant** of long-term portfolio performance. Studies (Brinson, Hood, Beebower, 1986) indicate that **asset allocation policy explains more than 90% of the variability of portfolio returns** over time.

The SAA is presented in the IPS as a **set of weights** across permissible asset classes:

$$
w_{Equity} + w_{Debt} + w_{Cash} + w_{Alternatives} = 1.0 \quad (100\%)
$$

Common asset classes include:
- **Equities** (domestic, international, emerging markets)
- **Fixed Income** (government bonds, corporate bonds, T-bills)
- **Cash & Equivalents**
- **Real Estate** (REITs)
- **Commodities** (gold, oil)
- **Alternative Investments** (private equity, hedge funds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:**
> Always state the SAA in the form of a **range** (e.g., Equity 50–70%, Debt 25–45%, Cash 0–10%) rather than a single point estimate. This provides the manager with operational flexibility while still preserving strategic intent.

---

## 2.5 Component 5 — Portfolio Management Structure

The IPS must specify the degree of discretion granted to the manager:

- **Discretionary Mandate** — Manager has full authority to execute trades within IPS guidelines.
- **Non-Discretionary Mandate** — Manager must obtain prior client approval for every trade.
- **Advisory Mandate** — Manager provides recommendations; client makes final decisions.
- **Multi-Manager (Fund-of-Funds) Structure** — Allocation across multiple sub-managers.

---

## 2.6 Component 6 — Rebalancing Policy

Over time, market movements cause the actual portfolio weights to drift from the SAA targets. The IPS must specify:

1. **Rebalancing Triggers** — calendar-based (quarterly, semi-annually) or threshold-based (5% drift).
2. **Rebalancing Method** — full rebalance to target weights, or corridor-based (rebalance only when weights breach upper or lower bands).
3. **Tax & Cost Considerations** — rebalancing decisions must account for transaction costs and tax consequences.

A common corridor rebalancing rule:

$$
\text{Rebalance if} \quad \vert w_{actual} - w_{target} \vert > \text{Tolerance Band}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Table Syntax Safeguard:** The absolute value expression above uses LaTeX vertical bars ($\vert$) inside a math block, NOT inside a markdown table. In the formula sheet below, all mathematical absolute values use the LaTeX `\vert` command to avoid breaking markdown table parsing.

---

## 2.7 Component 7 — Performance Measurement & Benchmarks

The IPS must designate **appropriate benchmarks** for each asset class and for the total portfolio. Benchmarks may be:

- **Absolute** — e.g., "achieve 8% annual return net of fees".
- **Relative** — e.g., "outperform the Nifty 50 TRI by 2% annually".
- **Liability-based** — for pension funds, the discount rate used to value liabilities.

The **Sharpe Ratio** is the standard risk-adjusted performance metric:

$$
Sharpe = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}
$$

where $R_p$ is the portfolio return, $R_f$ is the risk-free rate, and $\sigma_p$ is the portfolio's excess return standard deviation.

For downside risk, the **Sortino Ratio** is preferred:

$$
Sortino = \frac{R_p - R_{MAR}}{\sigma_{downside}}
$$

where $R_{MAR}$ is the Minimum Acceptable Return and $\sigma_{downside}$ is the standard deviation of returns falling below $R_{MAR}$.

---

## 2.8 Component 8 — Duties, Responsibilities & Review

The IPS must clearly assign responsibilities among:
- The **Investor / Client** — provides accurate information, reviews statements.
- The **Investment Manager** — manages portfolio per IPS, reports performance.
- The **Custodian** — safekeeps assets, settles trades.
- The **Auditor** — verifies performance claims (especially under GIPS).

A formal **review schedule** must be defined — typically **quarterly performance reviews** and an **annual IPS review**.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | Required Return (Goal-Based) | $r_{req} = \dfrac{FV_{goal} - PV_{assets} \times (1+r)^n}{\text{Annual Contributions}}$ | Used when contributions are made annually; $n$ in years |
| 2 | Required Return (Liability Discount) | $r_{req} = \dfrac{\text{Liabilities}_{PV} + \text{Expenses} - \text{Assets}_{PV}}{\text{Weighted Avg Life}}$ | Used for pension funds |
| 3 | Liquidity Ratio | $L = \dfrac{\text{Liquid Assets}}{\text{Total Net Assets}}$ | Higher L = greater short-term flexibility |
| 4 | After-Tax Return | $r_{a} = r_{b}(1 - t)$ | $t$ = effective tax rate |
| 5 | Portfolio Variance (2-asset) | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | $\rho \in [-1, 1]$; $w_1 + w_2 = 1$ |
| 6 | Sharpe Ratio | $S = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Higher is better; penalizes total volatility |
| 7 | Sortino Ratio | $Sortino = \dfrac{R_p - R_{MAR}}{\sigma_{D}}$ | $\sigma_D$ = downside deviation; $R_{MAR}$ = minimum acceptable return |
| 8 | Treynor Ratio | $T = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\beta_p}$ | Measures excess return per unit of systematic risk |
| 9 | Information Ratio | $IR = \dfrac{R_p - R_b}{TE}$ | $TE$ = tracking error; ideal IR > 0.5 |
| 10 | Rebalancing Trigger | $\vert w_{actual} - w_{target} \vert > \tau$ | $\tau$ = tolerance corridor (e.g., 5%) |
| 11 | Required Savings (FV) | $FV = PMT \times \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}$ | PMT = periodic contribution; $r$ = periodic return |
| 12 | Present Value of Annuity | $PV_{annuity} = PMT \times \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Used to value retirement income streams |
| 13 | Real Return | $r_{real} = \dfrac{1 + r_{nominal}}{1 + \pi} - 1$ | $\pi$ = inflation rate (Fisher equation) |
| 14 | Tracking Error | $TE = \sigma(R_p - R_b)$ | Volatility of active return |
| 15 | Maximum Drawdown | $MDD = \dfrac{\text{Peak} - \text{Trough}}{\text{Peak}}$ | Worst peak-to-trough decline observed |

> **Note on LaTeX in Tables:** All absolute value expressions inside the formula sheet above use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or mathematical expression outside the table cell syntax to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Corporate Finance

Although this is a finance topic, the IPS has direct parallels to **engineering project management**:

- An **IPS for a corporate treasury** is structurally identical to a **Project Charter** in PRINCE2 or PMI frameworks.
- The **rebalancing policy** mirrors the **risk-trigger-based hedging policy** used by oil & gas majors to hedge commodity price exposure.
- The **Sharpe Ratio** is mathematically equivalent to the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** in communications engineering.
- The **asset allocation problem** is formally a **constrained optimization problem**, identical in mathematical structure to portfolio optimization in electrical network theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Connection:** The Markowitz mean-variance optimization framework (1952) that underlies asset allocation is mathematically a **quadratic programming problem** — the same class of problems solved routinely in control systems engineering and structural optimization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Rate of Return for a Lump-Sum Goal

**Problem Context (KTU-style):**
> Ms. Anjali (age 35) wants to accumulate ₹1,00,00,000 (One Crore) by age 60 to fund her retirement. She currently has ₹15,00,000 invested and can contribute ₹3,00,000 per annum. Inflation is expected to be 5% per annum. Compute the **required real rate of return**.

### Step 1 — Adjust the Goal for Inflation

The nominal future value of the goal must reflect purchasing power erosion:

$$
FV_{nominal} = FV_{real} \times (1 + \pi)^n
$$

where $\pi = 0.05$ and $n = 60 - 35 = 25$ years.

$$
FV_{nominal} = 1,00,00,000 \times (1.05)^{25}
$$

$$
(1.05)^{25} = 3.38635 \quad \text{(from standard compound interest tables)}
$$

$$
FV_{nominal} = 1,00,00,000 \times 3.38635 = 3,38,63,500
$$

So the **nominal** goal is ₹3,38,63,500.

### Step 2 — Compound the Current Assets Forward

$$
FV_{existing} = 15,00,000 \times (1 + r)^n
$$

We do not yet know $r$, but conceptually the existing assets grow at the same nominal rate as the contributions earn.

### Step 3 — Future Value of the Annuity of Contributions

$$
FV_{contributions} = PMT \times \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}
$$

where $PMT = 3,00,000$.

### Step 4 — Equate Total to Goal

$$
FV_{existing} + FV_{contributions} = FV_{nominal}
$$

$$
15,00,000 \times (1+r)^{25} + 3,00,000 \times \frac{(1+r)^{25} - 1}{r} = 3,38,63,500
$$

### Step 5 — Solve Iteratively (Trial & Error / Financial Calculator)

Try $r = 0.08$:

$$
15,00,000 \times (1.08)^{25} = 15,00,000 \times 6.8485 = 1,02,72,750
$$

$$
3,00,000 \times \frac{6.8485 - 1}{0.08} = 3,00,000 \times \frac{5.8485}{0.08} = 3,00,000 \times 73.106 = 2,19,31,800
$$

$$
\text{Total} = 1,02,72,750 + 2,19,31,800 = 3,22,04,550 \quad \text{(slightly below goal)}
$$

Try $r = 0.085$:

$$
15,00,000 \times (1.085)^{25} = 15,00,000 \times 7.7167 = 1,15,75,050
$$

$$
3,00,000 \times \frac{7.7167 - 1}{0.085} = 3,00,000 \times 79.02 = 2,37,06,000
$$

$$
\text{Total} = 1,15,75,050 + 2,37,06,000 = 3,52,81,050 \quad \text{(slightly above goal)}
$$

Interpolating:

$$
r_{req} \approx 0.08 + 0.005 \times \frac{3,38,63,500 - 3,22,04,550}{3,52,81,050 - 3,22,04,550}
$$

$$
r_{req} \approx 0.08 + 0.005 \times \frac{16,58,950}{30,76,500} \approx 0.08 + 0.0027 = 0.0827
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{req} \approx 8.27\% \text{ nominal per annum}}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert Nominal to Real (Fisher Equation)

$$
r_{real} = \frac{1 + r_{nom}}{1 + \pi} - 1
$$

$$
r_{real} = \frac{1.0827}{1.05} - 1 = 1.0311 - 1 = 0.0311
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{real} \approx 3.11\% \text{ per annum}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Key Insight:** The question *requires* the student to present both nominal and real returns. Presenting only the nominal figure loses 3 of the 7 marks. The Fisher equation conversion is mandatory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Asset Portfolio Risk (Mathematical Construction)

**Given:**
- Asset A: $E(R_A) = 12\%$, $\sigma_A = 18\%$
- Asset B: $E(R_B) = 7\%$, $\sigma_B = 9\%$
- Correlation $\rho_{AB} = 0.30$

**Derive the portfolio expected return and standard deviation for a 60/40 allocation.**

### Step 1 — Expected Portfolio Return

$$
E(R_p) = w_A \cdot E(R_A) + w_B \cdot E(R_B)
$$

$$
E(R_p) = 0.60 \times 12\% + 0.40 \times 7\%
$$

$$
E(R_p) = 7.20\% + 2.80\% = 10.00\%
$$

### Step 2 — Portfolio Variance

$$
\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\sigma_p^2 = (0.60)^2 (0.18)^2 + (0.40)^2 (0.09)^2 + 2 (0.60)(0.40)(0.30)(0.18)(0.09)
$$

Term-by-term evaluation:

$$
\text{Term 1} = 0.36 \times 0.0324 = 0.011664
$$

$$
\text{Term 2} = 0.16 \times 0.0081 = 0.001296
$$

$$
\text{Term 3} = 2 \times 0.24 \times 0.30 \times 0.0162 = 0.48 \times 0.30 \times 0.0162
$$

$$
\text{Term 3} = 0.144 \times 0.0162 = 0.0023328
$$

Summing:

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 0.011664 + 0.001296 + 0.0023328 = 0.0152928
$$

### Step 3 — Portfolio Standard Deviation

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.0152928} = 0.12367 = 12.37\%
$$

### Analytical Summary Matrix

| Weight $w_A$ | Weight $w_B$ | $E(R_p)$ | $\sigma_p$ | Sharpe (rf = 5%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1.00 | 0.00 | 12.00% | 18.00% | 0.389 |
| 0.80 | 0.20 | 11.00% | 15.21% | 0.394 |
| **0.60** | **0.40** | **10.00%** | **12.37%** | **0.404** |
| 0.40 | 0.60 | 9.00% | 9.85% | 0.406 |
| 0.20 | 0.80 | 8.00% | 8.07% | 0.372 |
| 0.00 | 1.00 | 7.00% | 9.00% | 0.222 |

> [!NOTE]
> The minimum-variance portfolio does not coincide with the maximum-Sharpe portfolio. A skilled manager identifies the allocation that maximizes the Sharpe ratio (utility per unit of risk) subject to the IPS risk constraint.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rebalancing Trigger Calculation

**Given:**
- SAA Target: Equity 60%, Debt 35%, Gold 5%
- Tolerance Band $\tau = 5\%$

**After 1 year, market movements result in:**
- Equity: 67% (drifted up by 7%)
- Debt: 30% (drifted down by 5%)
- Gold: 3% (drifted down by 2%)

**Question:** Which asset classes trigger a rebalance?

### Step 1 — Compute Drift for Each Asset Class

$$
\Delta_{Equity} = 67\% - 60\% = +7\%
$$

$$
\Delta_{Debt} = 30\% - 35\% = -5\%
$$

$$
\Delta_{Gold} = 3\% - 5\% = -2\%
$$

### Step 2 — Compare to Tolerance Band

$$
\vert \Delta_{Equity} \vert = 7\% > \tau = 5\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{REBALANCE}
$$

$$
\vert \Delta_{Debt} \vert = 5\% = \tau = 5\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{At boundary (commonly rebalanced by convention)}
$$

$$
\vert \Delta_{Gold} \vert = 2\% < \tau = 5\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{No rebalance}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Required Trades

Assume current portfolio value = ₹1,00,00,000.

- Sell Equity: Reduce from ₹67,00,000 to ₹60,00,000 → Sell ₹7,00,000
- Buy Debt: Increase from ₹30,00,000 to ₹35,00,000 → Buy ₹5,00,000
- Buy Gold: Increase from ₹3,00,000 to ₹5,00,000 → Buy ₹2,00,000

**Verification:** ₹7,00,000 (sell) = ₹5,00,000 + ₹2,00,000 (buys). ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students answer only "Equity triggers" — losing 2 marks. A complete answer must list every asset whose absolute drift exceeds the tolerance, and the corresponding buy/sell actions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Map of IPS Components

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the hierarchical structure of a complete Investment Policy Statement, showing how the eight major components interlink.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[INVESTMENT POLICY STATEMENT IPS] --> B[1 INVESTOR BACKGROUND]
    A --> C[2 INVESTMENT OBJECTIVES]
    A --> D[3 INVESTMENT CONSTRAINTS]
    A --> E[4 STRATEGIC ASSET ALLOCATION]
    A --> F[5 MGMT STRUCTURE]
    A --> G[6 REBALANCING POLICY]
    A --> H[7 PERFORMANCE BENCHMARKS]
    A --> I[8 DUTIES AND REVIEW]

    B --> B1[Personal Profile]
    B --> B2[Financial Position]
    B --> B3[Risk Profile ATR vs WTR]

    C --> C1[Return Objectives]
    C --> C2[Risk Objectives]

    D --> D1[Liquidity]
    D --> D2[Time Horizon]
    D --> D3[Tax]
    D --> D4[Legal and Regulatory]
    D --> D5[Unique Circumstances]

    E --> E1[Equity Weights]
    E --> E2[Debt Weights]
    E --> E3[Cash Weights]
    E --> E4[Alternative Weights]

    F --> F1[Discretionary]
    F --> F2[Non Discretionary]
    F --> F3[Advisory]

    G --> G1[Calendar Based]
    G --> G2[Threshold Based]

    H --> H1[Absolute Benchmark]
    H --> H2[Relative Benchmark]
    H --> H3[Liability Based Benchmark]

    I --> I1[Investor Duties]
    I --> I2[Manager Duties]
    I --> I3[Custodian Duties]
    I --> I4[Review Frequency]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — IPS-Driven Portfolio Management Process

This diagram illustrates the closed-loop process by which the IPS governs every portfolio decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Define Objectives] --> S2[Step 2 Identify Constraints]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Determine Asset Allocation]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Select Manager and Mandate]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Execute Investments]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Monitor Performance vs IPS]
    S6 --> Q1{IPS Compliant?}
    Q1 -- YES --> S7[Step 7 Rebalance if Triggered]
    Q1 -- NO --> S8[Step 8 Corrective Action and Review]
    S8 --> S1
    S7 --> S9[Step 9 Annual IPS Review]
    S9 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the IPS

For readers requiring a system-engineering perspective, the IPS can be modeled as an input-process-output-feedback control system.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1[Investor Goals] --> IPSEngine
    IN2[Risk Tolerance] --> IPSEngine
    IN3[Constraints] --> IPSEngine
    IN4[Time Horizon] --> IPSEngine

    IPSEngine[IPS Engine Strategic Asset Allocation] --> OUT1[Asset Allocation Policy]
    IPSEngine --> OUT2[Manager Mandate]
    IPSEngine --> OUT3[Performance Benchmark]
    IPSEngine --> OUT4[Rebalancing Rules]

    OUT1 --> MGR[Investment Manager]
    MGR --> PF[Actual Portfolio]
    PF --> MON[Performance Monitor]
    OUT3 --> MON
    MON --> DEC{IPS Compliance Check}
    DEC -- Compliant --> CONT[Continue Strategy]
    DEC -- Drift Detected --> REB[Trigger Rebalance]
    REB --> MGR
    MON --> RPT[Quarterly Report to Investor]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Rebalancing Trigger Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q[Periodic Review Date Reached] --> C1{Is Calendar Trigger Active?}
    C1 -- YES --> REB1[Rebalance Now]
    C1 -- NO --> C2{Any Drift Exceeds Tolerance Tau?}
    C2 -- YES --> REB2[Rebalance Only Offending Asset Classes]
    C2 -- NO --> HOLD[Hold Current Weights]
    REB1 --> LOG[Document Trade Rationale]
    REB2 --> LOG
    HOLD --> NEXT[Schedule Next Review]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above (S1, B1, IPSEngine, etc.) are purely alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and contain no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels — in compliance with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards specified in the SYSTEM protocol.
